any corrupt observance, ^sn2-8-6-2

or suspicious spiritual life, ^sn2-8-6-3

is not very fruitful.” ^sn2-8-6-4

That’s what the godling Tāyana said. ^sn2-8-7-1

Then he bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^sn2-8-7-2

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened. ^sn2-8-8-1

“Mendicants, tonight, the glorious godling Tāyana, formerly a religious founder, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, ^sn2-8-8-2

and recited these verses in my presence.” The Buddha repeated the verses in full, adding: ^sn2-8-8-3

“That’s what the godling Tāyana said. Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^sn2-8-14-1

Mendicants, learn the verses of Tāyana! ^sn2-8-14-2

Memorize the verses of Tāyana! ^sn2-8-14-3

Remember the verses of Tāyana! ^sn2-8-14-4

These verses are beneficial and relate to the fundamentals of the spiritual life.” ^sn2-8-14-5

### The Moon (Linked Discourses 2.9) ^sn2-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn2-9-1-1

Now at that time the Moon God had been seized by Rāhu, lord of titans. ^sn2-9-1-2

Then the Moon God, recollecting the Buddha, at that time spoke this verse: ^sn2-9-1-3

“Homage to you, Buddha, hero! ^sn2-9-2-1

You’re free in every way. ^sn2-9-2-2

I’ve wandered into confinement: ^sn2-9-2-3

be my refuge!” ^sn2-9-2-4

Then the Buddha addressed Rāhu in verse concerning the Moon God: ^sn2-9-3-1

“The Moon God has gone for refuge ^sn2-9-4-1

to the Realized One, the perfected one. ^sn2-9-4-2

The Buddhas have compassion for the world— ^sn2-9-4-3

so Rāhu, release the Moon!” ^sn2-9-4-4

Then Rāhu, having released the Moon, rushed to see Vepacitti, lord of titans and stood to one side, shocked and awestruck. ^sn2-9-5-1

Vepacitti addressed him in verse: ^sn2-9-5-2

“Why the rush? ^sn2-9-6-1

Rāhu, you released the Moon ^sn2-9-6-2

and came here looking like you’re in shock: ^sn2-9-6-3

why do you stand there so scared?” ^sn2-9-6-4

“My head would have exploded in seven pieces, ^sn2-9-7-1

I would have found no happiness in life, ^sn2-9-7-2

if, when enchanted by the Buddha’s verse, ^sn2-9-7-3

I had not released the Moon.” ^sn2-9-7-4

### The Sun (Linked Discourses 2.10) ^sn2-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn2-10-1-1

Now at that time the Sun God had been seized by Rāhu, lord of titans. ^sn2-10-1-2

Then the Sun God, recollecting the Buddha, at that time spoke this verse: ^sn2-10-1-3

“Homage to you, Buddha, hero! ^sn2-10-2-1

You’re free in every way. ^sn2-10-2-2

I’ve wandered into confinement: ^sn2-10-2-3

be my refuge!” ^sn2-10-2-4

Then the Buddha addressed Rāhu in verse concerning the Sun God: ^sn2-10-3-1

“The Sun God has gone for refuge ^sn2-10-4-1

to the Realized One, the perfected one. ^sn2-10-4-2

The Buddhas have compassion for the world— ^sn2-10-4-3

so Rāhu, release the Sun! ^sn2-10-4-4

He is a beacon in the darkness, ^sn2-10-5-1

the blazing sun, circle of awesome might. ^sn2-10-5-2

Rāhu, do not swallow him as he traverses the sky. ^sn2-10-5-3

Rāhu, release my offspring, the Sun!” ^sn2-10-5-4

Then Rāhu, having released the Sun, rushed to see Vepacitti, lord of titans and stood to one side, shocked and awestruck. ^sn2-10-6-1

Vepacitti addressed him in verse: ^sn2-10-6-2

“Why the rush? ^sn2-10-7-1

Rāhu, you released the Sun ^sn2-10-7-2

and came here looking like you’re in shock: ^sn2-10-7-3

why do you stand there so scared?” ^sn2-10-7-4

“My head would have exploded in seven pieces, ^sn2-10-8-1

I would have found no joy in life, ^sn2-10-8-2

if, when enchanted by the Buddha’s verse, ^sn2-10-8-3

I had not released the Sun.” ^sn2-10-8-4

### With Candimasa (Linked Discourses 2.11) ^sn2-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn2-11-1-1

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Candimasa, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, ^sn2-11-1-2

and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-11-1-3

“Like deer in a mosquito-free marsh, ^sn2-11-2-1

they will reach a safe place ^sn2-11-2-2

having entered the absorptions, ^sn2-11-2-3

unified, alert, and mindful.” ^sn2-11-2-4

“Like fish when the net is cut, ^sn2-11-3-1

they will reach the far shore ^sn2-11-3-2

having entered the absorptions, ^sn2-11-3-3

diligent, with strife discarded.” ^sn2-11-3-4

### With Vishnu (Linked Discourses 2.12) ^sn2-12-0

Standing to one side, the godling Vishnu spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-12-1-1

“Happy are the men ^sn2-12-2-1

who pay homage to the Holy One! ^sn2-12-2-2

They apply themselves to Gotama’s instructions, ^sn2-12-2-3

diligently training.” ^sn2-12-2-4

“Those who practice absorption <j>in accord with the training”, ^sn2-12-3-1

said the Buddha to Vishnu, ^sn2-12-3-2

“in the way of teaching I’ve proclaimed, ^sn2-12-3-3

diligent while there is still time; ^sn2-12-3-4

they won’t fall under the sway of Death.” ^sn2-12-3-5

### With Dīghalaṭṭhi (Linked Discourses 2.13) ^sn2-13-0

So I have heard. ^sn2-13-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn2-13-1-2

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Dīghalaṭṭhi, lighting up the entire Bamboo Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, ^sn2-13-1-3

and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-13-1-4

“If a mendicant is a meditator, freed in mind, ^sn2-13-2-1

and if they desire the heart’s fulfillment— ^sn2-13-2-2

having known the arising and passing of the world, ^sn2-13-2-3

good-hearted, independent, that is their reward.” ^sn2-13-2-4

### With Nandana (Linked Discourses 2.14) ^sn2-14-0

Standing to one side, the godling Nandana addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn2-14-1-1

“I ask you, Gotama, whose wisdom is vast, ^sn2-14-2-1

the Blessed One <j>of unhindered knowledge and vision. ^sn2-14-2-2

What sort do they call ethical? ^sn2-14-2-3

What sort do they call wise? ^sn2-14-2-4

What sort lives on after transcending suffering? ^sn2-14-2-5

What sort is worshiped by the deities?” ^sn2-14-2-6

“Someone who is ethical, wise, evolved, ^sn2-14-3-1

becomes serene, loving absorption, mindful, ^sn2-14-3-2

who’s gotten rid of and given up all sorrows, ^sn2-14-3-3

with defilements ended, they bear their final body. ^sn2-14-3-4

That sort they call ethical. ^sn2-14-4-1

That sort they call wise. ^sn2-14-4-2

That sort lives on after transcending suffering. ^sn2-14-4-3

That sort is worshiped by the deities.” ^sn2-14-4-4

### With Candana (Linked Discourses 2.15) ^sn2-15-0

Standing to one side, the godling Candana addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn2-15-1-1

“Who here crosses the flood, ^sn2-15-2-1

tireless all day and night? ^sn2-15-2-2

Who, not standing and unsupported, ^sn2-15-2-3

does not sink in the deep?” ^sn2-15-2-4

“Someone who is always endowed with ethics, ^sn2-15-3-1

wise and serene, ^sn2-15-3-2

energetic and resolute, ^sn2-15-3-3

crosses the flood so hard to cross. ^sn2-15-3-4

Someone who desists from sensual perception, ^sn2-15-4-1

has moved past the fetter of form, ^sn2-15-4-2

and has finished with relishing and greed ^sn2-15-4-3

does not sink in the deep.” ^sn2-15-4-4

### With Vāsudatta (Linked Discourses 2.16) ^sn2-16-0

Standing to one side, the godling Vāsudatta spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-16-1-1

“Like they’re struck by a sword, ^sn2-16-2-1

like their head was on fire, ^sn2-16-2-2

a mendicant should wander mindful, ^sn2-16-2-3

to give up sensual desire.” ^sn2-16-2-4

“Like they’re struck by a sword, ^sn2-16-3-1

like their head was on fire, ^sn2-16-3-2

a mendicant should wander mindful, ^sn2-16-3-3

to give up substantialist view.” ^sn2-16-3-4

### With Subrahmā (Linked Discourses 2.17) ^sn2-17-0

Standing to one side, the godling Subrahmā addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn2-17-1-1

“This mind is always anxious, ^sn2-17-2-1

this mind is always stressed ^sn2-17-2-2

about stresses that haven’t arisen ^sn2-17-2-3

and those that have. ^sn2-17-2-4

If there is a state free of anxiety, ^sn2-17-2-5

please answer my question.” ^sn2-17-2-6

“Not without understanding and fervor, ^sn2-17-3-1

not without restraining the sense faculties, ^sn2-17-3-2

not without letting go of everything, ^sn2-17-3-3

do I see safety for living creatures.” ^sn2-17-3-4

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn2-17-4-1

The god vanished right there. ^sn2-17-4-2

### With Kakudha (Linked Discourses 2.18) ^sn2-18-0

So I have heard. ^sn2-18-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāketa in the deer park at the Añjana Wood. ^sn2-18-1-2

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Kakudha, lighting up the entire Añjana Wood, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^sn2-18-1-3

“Do you delight, ascetic?” ^sn2-18-1-4

“What have I gained, sir?” ^sn2-18-1-5

“Well then, ascetic, do you sorrow?” ^sn2-18-1-6

“What have I lost, sir?” ^sn2-18-1-7

“Well then, ascetic, do you neither delight nor sorrow?” ^sn2-18-1-8

“Yes, sir.” ^sn2-18-1-9

“I hope you’re not gloomy, mendicant, ^sn2-18-2-1

I hope that delight isn’t found in you. ^sn2-18-2-2

I hope that discontent doesn’t ^sn2-18-2-3

overwhelm you as you sit alone.” ^sn2-18-2-4

“I’m genuinely not gloomy, spirit, ^sn2-18-3-1

and no delight is found in me. ^sn2-18-3-2

And also discontent doesn’t ^sn2-18-3-3

overwhelm me as I sit alone.” ^sn2-18-3-4

“How are you not gloomy, mendicant? ^sn2-18-4-1

How is delight not found in you? ^sn2-18-4-2

How does discontent not ^sn2-18-4-3

overwhelm you as you sit alone?” ^sn2-18-4-4

“Delight is born from gloom, ^sn2-18-5-1

gloom is born from delight; ^sn2-18-5-2

sir, you should know me as ^sn2-18-5-3

a mendicant free of delight and gloom.” ^sn2-18-5-4

“After a long time I see ^sn2-18-6-1

a brahmin fully quenched. ^sn2-18-6-2

A mendicant free of delight and gloom, ^sn2-18-6-3

he has crossed over clinging to the world.” ^sn2-18-6-4

### With Uttara (Linked Discourses 2.19) ^sn2-19-0

At Rājagaha. ^sn2-19-1-1

Standing to one side, the godling Uttara spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-19-1-2

“This life, so very short, is led onward. ^sn2-19-2-1

One led on to old age has no shelter. ^sn2-19-2-2

Seeing this peril in death, ^sn2-19-2-3

do good deeds that bring you joy.” ^sn2-19-2-4

“This life, so very short, is led onward. ^sn2-19-3-1

One led on to old age has no shelter. ^sn2-19-3-2

Seeing this peril in death, ^sn2-19-3-3

a seeker of peace would drop the world’s bait.” ^sn2-19-3-4

### With Anāthapiṇḍika (Linked Discourses 2.20) ^sn2-20-0

Standing to one side, the godling Anāthapiṇḍika recited these verses in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-20-1-1

“This is indeed that Jeta’s Grove, ^sn2-20-2-1

frequented by the Saṅgha of seers, ^sn2-20-2-2

where the King of Dhamma stayed: ^sn2-20-2-3

it brings me joy! ^sn2-20-2-4

Deeds, knowledge, and principle; ^sn2-20-3-1

ethical conduct, an excellent livelihood; ^sn2-20-3-2

by these are mortals purified, ^sn2-20-3-3

not by clan or wealth. ^sn2-20-3-4

That’s why an astute person, ^sn2-20-4-1

seeing what’s good for themselves, ^sn2-20-4-2

would examine the teaching rationally, ^sn2-20-4-3

that’s how they become purified in that. ^sn2-20-4-4

Sāriputta is full of wisdom, ^sn2-20-5-1

ethics, and peace. ^sn2-20-5-2

Even a mendicant who has crossed over ^sn2-20-5-3

might at best equal him.” ^sn2-20-5-4

This is what the godling Anāthapiṇḍika said. ^sn2-20-6-1

Then he bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^sn2-20-6-2

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn2-20-7-1

“Mendicants, tonight, a certain glorious godling, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, ^sn2-20-7-2

and recited these verses in my presence.” The Buddha then repeated the verses in full. ^sn2-20-7-3

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^sn2-20-13-1

“Sir, that godling must surely have been Anāthapiṇḍika. ^sn2-20-13-2

For the householder Anāthapiṇḍika was devoted to Venerable Sāriputta.” ^sn2-20-13-3

“Good, good, Ānanda. You’ve reached the logical conclusion, as far as logic goes. ^sn2-20-13-4

For that was indeed the godling Anāthapiṇḍika.” ^sn2-20-13-5

### With Shiva (Linked Discourses 2.21) ^sn2-21-0

So I have heard. ^sn2-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn2-21-1-2

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Shiva, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, ^sn2-21-1-3

and recited these verses in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-21-1-4

“Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-2-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-2-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-2-3

things get better, not worse. ^sn2-21-2-4

Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-3-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-3-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-3-3

wisdom is gained—but not from anyone else. ^sn2-21-3-4

Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-4-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-4-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-4-3

you grieve not among the grieving. ^sn2-21-4-4

Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-5-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-5-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-5-3

you shine among your relatives. ^sn2-21-5-4

Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-6-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-6-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-6-3

sentient beings go to a good place. ^sn2-21-6-4

Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-7-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-7-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-7-3

sentient beings live happily.” ^sn2-21-7-4

Then the Buddha replied to Shiva in verse: ^sn2-21-8-1

“Associate only with the virtuous! ^sn2-21-9-1

Try to get close to the virtuous! ^sn2-21-9-2

Understanding the true teaching of the good, ^sn2-21-9-3

you’re released from all suffering.” ^sn2-21-9-4

### With Khema (Linked Discourses 2.22) ^sn2-22-0

Standing to one side, the godling Khema recited these verses in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-22-1-1

“Fools and simpletons behave ^sn2-22-2-1

like their own worst enemies, ^sn2-22-2-2

doing wicked deeds ^sn2-22-2-3

that ripen as bitter fruit. ^sn2-22-2-4

It’s not good to do a deed ^sn2-22-3-1

that plagues you later on, ^sn2-22-3-2

for which you weep and wail, ^sn2-22-3-3

as its effect stays with you. ^sn2-22-3-4

It is good to do a deed ^sn2-22-4-1

that doesn’t plague you later on, ^sn2-22-4-2

that gladdens and cheers, ^sn2-22-4-3

as its effect stays with you.” ^sn2-22-4-4

“As a precaution, you should do ^sn2-22-5-1

what you know is for your own welfare. ^sn2-22-5-2

A thinker, a wise one would not proceed ^sn2-22-5-3

thinking like the cart driver. ^sn2-22-5-4

Suppose a cart driver leaves the highway, ^sn2-22-6-1

so even and well compacted. ^sn2-22-6-2

They enter upon a rough road, ^sn2-22-6-3

and fret as soon as their axle is broken. ^sn2-22-6-4

So too, a dullard leaves the good ^sn2-22-7-1

to follow what’s against the good. ^sn2-22-7-2

Fallen in the jaws of death, ^sn2-22-7-3

they fret like one whose axle is broken.” ^sn2-22-7-4

### With Serī (Linked Discourses 2.23) ^sn2-23-0

Standing to one side, the godling Serī addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn2-23-1-1

“Both gods and humans ^sn2-23-2-1

enjoy their food. ^sn2-23-2-2

So what’s the name of the spirit ^sn2-23-2-3

who doesn’t like food?” ^sn2-23-2-4

“Those who give with faith ^sn2-23-3-1

and a clear and confident heart, ^sn2-23-3-2

partake of food ^sn2-23-3-3

in this world and the next. ^sn2-23-3-4

So you should dispel stinginess, ^sn2-23-4-1

overcoming that stain, and give a gift. ^sn2-23-4-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^sn2-23-4-3

support them in the next world.” ^sn2-23-4-4

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn2-23-5-1

how well said this was by the Buddha.” He repeated the Buddha’s verses, and said: ^sn2-23-5-2

“Once upon a time, sir, I was a king named Serī, a giver, a donor, who praised giving. ^sn2-23-8-1

I gave gifts at the four gates to ascetics and brahmins, to paupers, vagrants, supplicants, and beggars. ^sn2-23-8-2

Then the ladies of my harem approached me and said, ^sn2-23-8-3

‘Your Majesty gives gifts, but we don’t. ^sn2-23-8-4

Your Majesty, please support us to give gifts and make merit.’ ^sn2-23-8-5

Then it occurred to me, ^sn2-23-8-6

‘I’m a giver, a donor, who praises giving. ^sn2-23-8-7

When they say, “We would give gifts”, what am I to say?’ ^sn2-23-8-8

And so I gave the first gate to the ladies of my harem. ^sn2-23-8-9

There they gave gifts, while my own giving dwindled. ^sn2-23-8-10

Then my aristocrat vassals approached me and said, ^sn2-23-9-1

‘Your Majesty gives gifts, the ladies of your harem give gifts, but we don’t. ^sn2-23-9-2

Your Majesty, please support us to give gifts and make merit.’ ^sn2-23-9-3

Then it occurred to me, ^sn2-23-9-4

‘I’m a giver, a donor, who praises giving. ^sn2-23-9-5

When they say, “We would give gifts”, what am I to say?’ ^sn2-23-9-6

And so I gave the second gate to my aristocrat vassals. ^sn2-23-9-7

There they gave gifts, while my own giving dwindled. ^sn2-23-9-8

Then my troops approached me and said, ^sn2-23-10-1

‘Your Majesty gives gifts, the ladies of your harem give gifts, your aristocrat vassals give gifts, but we don’t. ^sn2-23-10-2

Your Majesty, please support us to give gifts and make merit.’ ^sn2-23-10-3

Then it occurred to me, ^sn2-23-10-4

‘I’m a giver, a donor, who praises giving. ^sn2-23-10-5

When they say, “We would give gifts”, what am I to say?’ ^sn2-23-10-6

And so I gave the third gate to my troops. ^sn2-23-10-7

There they gave gifts, while my own giving dwindled. ^sn2-23-10-8

Then my brahmins and householders approached me and said, ^sn2-23-11-1

‘Your Majesty gives gifts, the ladies of your harem give gifts, your aristocrat vassals give gifts, your troops give gifts, but we don’t. ^sn2-23-11-2

Your Majesty, please support us to give gifts and make merit.’ ^sn2-23-11-3

Then it occurred to me, ^sn2-23-11-4

‘I’m a giver, a donor, who praises giving. ^sn2-23-11-5

When they say, “We would give gifts”, what am I to say?’ ^sn2-23-11-6

And so I gave the fourth gate to my brahmins and householders. ^sn2-23-11-7

There they gave gifts, while my own giving dwindled. ^sn2-23-11-8

Then my men approached me and said, ^sn2-23-12-1

‘Now Your Majesty is not giving gifts anywhere!’ ^sn2-23-12-2

When they said this, I said to those men, ^sn2-23-12-3

‘Well then, lads, deposit half the revenue generated in the outer districts in the royal compound. Give the other half as charity to ascetics and brahmins, to paupers, vagrants, supplicants, and beggars in those districts.’ ^sn2-23-12-4

Sir, for a long time I made so much merit and did so many skillful deeds. I never reached any limit so as to say ^sn2-23-12-5

‘there’s this much merit’ or ‘there’s this much result of merit’ or ‘for so long I’ll remain in heaven’. ^sn2-23-12-6

It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn2-23-12-7

how well said this was by the Buddha. ^sn2-23-12-8

‘Those who give with faith ^sn2-23-13-1

and a clear and confident heart, ^sn2-23-13-2

partake of food ^sn2-23-13-3

in this world and the next. ^sn2-23-13-4

So you should dispel stinginess, ^sn2-23-14-1

overcoming that stain, and give a gift. ^sn2-23-14-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^sn2-23-14-3

support them in the next world.’” ^sn2-23-14-4

### With Ghaṭīkāra (Linked Discourses 2.24) ^sn2-24-0

Standing to one side, the godling Ghaṭīkāra spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-24-1-1

“Seven mendicants reborn in Aviha ^sn2-24-2-1

have been freed. ^sn2-24-2-2

With the complete ending of greed and hate, ^sn2-24-2-3

they’ve crossed over clinging to the world.” ^sn2-24-2-4

“Who are those who’ve crossed the bog, ^sn2-24-3-1

Death’s dominion so hard to pass? ^sn2-24-3-2

Who, after leaving behind the human body, ^sn2-24-3-3

have risen above celestial yokes?” ^sn2-24-3-4

“Upaka and Palagaṇḍa, ^sn2-24-4-1

and Pukkusāti, these three; ^sn2-24-4-2

Bhaddiya and Bhaddadeva, ^sn2-24-4-3

and Bāhudantī and Piṅgiya. ^sn2-24-4-4

They, after leaving behind the human body, ^sn2-24-4-5

have risen above celestial yokes.” ^sn2-24-4-6

“You speak well of them, ^sn2-24-5-1

who have let go the snares of Māra. ^sn2-24-5-2

Whose teaching did they understand ^sn2-24-5-3

to cut the bonds of rebirth?” ^sn2-24-5-4

“None other than the Blessed One! ^sn2-24-6-1

None other than your instruction! ^sn2-24-6-2

It was your teaching that they understood ^sn2-24-6-3

to cut the bonds of rebirth. ^sn2-24-6-4

Where name and form ^sn2-24-7-1

cease with no residue left behind; ^sn2-24-7-2

understanding this teaching, ^sn2-24-7-3

they cut the bonds of rebirth.” ^sn2-24-7-4

“The words you say are deep, ^sn2-24-8-1

hard to understand, so very hard to wake up to. ^sn2-24-8-2

Whose teaching did you understand ^sn2-24-8-3

to be able to say such things?” ^sn2-24-8-4

“In the past I was a potter ^sn2-24-9-1

in Vebhaliṅga called Ghaṭīkāra. ^sn2-24-9-2

I took care of my parents ^sn2-24-9-3

as a lay follower of Buddha Kassapa. ^sn2-24-9-4

I refrained from sexual intercourse, ^sn2-24-10-1

I was chaste, spiritual. ^sn2-24-10-2

We lived in the same village; ^sn2-24-10-3

in the past I was your comrade. ^sn2-24-10-4

I am the one who understands ^sn2-24-11-1

that these seven mendicants have been freed. ^sn2-24-11-2

With the complete ending of greed and hate, ^sn2-24-11-3

they’ve crossed over clinging to the world.” ^sn2-24-11-4

“That’s exactly how it was, ^sn2-24-12-1

just as you say, Bhaggava. ^sn2-24-12-2

In the past you were a potter ^sn2-24-12-3

in Vebhaliṅga called Ghaṭīkāra. ^sn2-24-12-4

You took care of your parents ^sn2-24-13-1

as a lay follower of Buddha Kassapa. ^sn2-24-13-2

You refrained from sexual intercourse, ^sn2-24-13-3

you were chaste, spiritual. ^sn2-24-13-4

We lived in the same village; ^sn2-24-13-5

in the past you were my comrade.” ^sn2-24-13-6

“That’s how it was ^sn2-24-14-1

when those friends of old met again. ^sn2-24-14-2

Both of them are evolved, ^sn2-24-14-3

and bear their final body.” ^sn2-24-14-4

### With Jantu (Linked Discourses 2.25) ^sn2-25-0

So I have heard. ^sn2-25-1-1

At one time several mendicants were staying in the Kosalan lands, in a wilderness hut on the slopes of the Himalayas. They were restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties. ^sn2-25-1-2

Then on the fifteenth day sabbath the godling Jantu went up to those mendicants and addressed them in verse: ^sn2-25-2-1

“The mendicants used to live happily, ^sn2-25-3-1

as disciples of Gotama. ^sn2-25-3-2

Desireless they sought alms; ^sn2-25-3-3

desireless they used their lodgings. ^sn2-25-3-4

Knowing that the world was impermanent ^sn2-25-3-5

they made an end of suffering. ^sn2-25-3-6

But now they’ve made themselves hard to look after, ^sn2-25-4-1

like chiefs in a village. ^sn2-25-4-2

They eat and eat and then lie down, ^sn2-25-4-3

passed out in the homes of others. ^sn2-25-4-4

Having raised my cupped palms to the Saṅgha, ^sn2-25-5-1

I speak here only about certain people. ^sn2-25-5-2

They’re dregs, with no protector, ^sn2-25-5-3

just like those who have passed away. ^sn2-25-5-4

I’m speaking about ^sn2-25-6-1

those who live negligently. ^sn2-25-6-2

To those who live diligently ^sn2-25-6-3

I pay homage.” ^sn2-25-6-4

### With Rohitassa (Linked Discourses 2.26) ^sn2-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn2-26-1-1

Standing to one side, the godling Rohitassa said to the Buddha: ^sn2-26-1-2

“Sir, is it possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn?” ^sn2-26-1-3

“Reverend, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn.” ^sn2-26-1-4

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn2-26-2-1

how well said this was by the Buddha. ^sn2-26-2-2

Once upon a time, I was a seer called Rohitassa, son of the benefactors. I was a sky-walker with psychic powers. ^sn2-26-3-1

I was as fast as a light arrow easily shot across the shadow of a palm tree by a well-trained expert archer with a strong bow. ^sn2-26-3-2

My stride was such that it could span from the eastern ocean to the western ocean. ^sn2-26-3-3

This wish came to me: ^sn2-26-3-4

‘I will reach the end of the world by traveling.’ ^sn2-26-3-5

Having such speed and stride, I traveled for my whole lifespan of a hundred years—pausing only to eat and drink, go to the toilet, and sleep to dispel weariness—and I passed away along the way, never reaching the end of the world. ^sn2-26-3-6

It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn2-26-4-1

how well said this was by the Buddha. ^sn2-26-4-2

‘Reverend, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn.’” ^sn2-26-4-3

“But Reverend, I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world. ^sn2-26-5-1

For it is in this fathom-long carcass with its perception and mind that I describe the world, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn2-26-5-2

The end of the world can never ^sn2-26-6-1

be reached by traveling. ^sn2-26-6-2

But without reaching the end of the world, ^sn2-26-6-3

there’s no release from suffering. ^sn2-26-6-4

So an intelligent person, understanding the world, ^sn2-26-7-1

has completed the spiritual journey <j>and gone to the end of the world. ^sn2-26-7-2

Assuaged, knowing the end of the world, ^sn2-26-7-3

they do not hope for this world or the next.” ^sn2-26-7-4

### With Nanda (Linked Discourses 2.27) ^sn2-27-0

Standing to one side, the godling Nanda spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-27-1-1

“Time flies, nights pass by, ^sn2-27-2-1

the stages of life leave us one by one. ^sn2-27-2-2

Seeing this peril in death, ^sn2-27-2-3

do good deeds that bring you joy.” ^sn2-27-2-4

“Time flies, nights pass by, ^sn2-27-3-1

the stages of life leave us one by one. ^sn2-27-3-2

Seeing this peril in death, ^sn2-27-3-3

a seeker of peace would drop the world’s bait.” ^sn2-27-3-4

### With Nandivisāla (Linked Discourses 2.28) ^sn2-28-0

Standing to one side, the godling Nandivisāla addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn2-28-1-1

“Four are its wheels, and nine its doors; ^sn2-28-2-1

it’s stuffed full, bound with greed, ^sn2-28-2-2

and born from a bog. ^sn2-28-2-3

Great hero, how will I keep going?” ^sn2-28-2-4

“Having cut the strap and harness— ^sn2-28-3-1

wicked desire and greed— ^sn2-28-3-2

and having plucked out craving, root and all: ^sn2-28-3-3

that’s how you will keep going.” ^sn2-28-3-4

### With Susīma (Linked Discourses 2.29) ^sn2-29-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn2-29-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^sn2-29-1-2

“Ānanda, do you endorse Sāriputta?” ^sn2-29-1-3

“Sir, who on earth would not endorse Venerable Sāriputta unless they’re a fool, a hater, delusional, or mentally deranged? ^sn2-29-2-1

Venerable Sāriputta is astute, ^sn2-29-2-2

he has great wisdom, ^sn2-29-2-3

widespread wisdom, ^sn2-29-2-4

laughing wisdom, ^sn2-29-2-5

swift wisdom, ^sn2-29-2-6

sharp wisdom, ^sn2-29-2-7

and penetrating wisdom. ^sn2-29-2-8

He has few wishes, ^sn2-29-2-9

he’s content, ^sn2-29-2-10

secluded, ^sn2-29-2-11

aloof, ^sn2-29-2-12

and energetic. ^sn2-29-2-13

He gives advice ^sn2-29-2-14

and accepts advice; ^sn2-29-2-15

he accuses ^sn2-29-2-16

and criticizes wickedness. ^sn2-29-2-17

Who on earth would not endorse Venerable Sāriputta unless they’re a fool, a hater, delusional, or mentally deranged?” ^sn2-29-2-18

“That’s so true, Ānanda! That’s so true! ^sn2-29-3-1

Who on earth would not endorse Venerable Sāriputta unless they’re a fool, a hater, delusional, or mentally deranged?” ^sn2-29-3-2

And the Buddha repeated all of Ānanda’s terms of praise. ^sn2-29-3-3

While this praise of Sāriputta was being spoken, the godling Susīma approached the Buddha, escorted by a large assembly of godlings. He bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^sn2-29-4-1

“That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One! ^sn2-29-5-1

Who on earth would not endorse Venerable Sāriputta unless they’re a fool, a hater, delusional, or mentally deranged?” ^sn2-29-5-2

And he too repeated all the terms of praise of Sāriputta, adding, ^sn2-29-5-3

“For I too, sir, whenever I go to an assembly of godlings, frequently hear the same terms of praise.” ^sn2-29-6-1

While this praise of Sāriputta was being spoken, the godlings of Susīma’s assembly—uplifted and overjoyed, full of rapture and happiness—displayed a rainbow of bright colors. ^sn2-29-7-1

Suppose there was a beryl gem that was naturally lustrous, eight-faceted, well-worked. When placed on a cream rug it would shine and glow and radiate. ^sn2-29-8-1

In the same way, the godlings of Susīma’s assembly displayed a rainbow of bright colors. ^sn2-29-8-2

Suppose there was a pendant of Black Plum River gold, fashioned by a deft smith, well wrought in the forge. When placed on a cream rug it would shine and glow and radiate. ^sn2-29-9-1

In the same way, the godlings of Susīma’s assembly displayed a rainbow of bright colors. ^sn2-29-9-2

Suppose that  in the autumn the heavens were clear and cloudless. At the crack of dawn, the Morning Star shines and glows and radiates. ^sn2-29-10-1

In the same way, the godlings of Susīma’s assembly displayed a rainbow of bright colors. ^sn2-29-10-2

Suppose that in the autumn the heavens were clear and cloudless. As the sun is rising to the firmament, having dispelled all the darkness of space, shines and glows and radiates. ^sn2-29-11-1

In the same way, the godlings of Susīma’s assembly displayed a rainbow of bright colors. ^sn2-29-11-2

Then the godling Susīma spoke this verse about Venerable Sāriputta in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-29-12-1

“He’s considered astute, ^sn2-29-13-1

Sāriputta, free of anger. ^sn2-29-13-2

Few in wishes, gentle, tamed, ^sn2-29-13-3

the seer shines in the Teacher’s praise!” ^sn2-29-13-4

Then the Buddha replied to Susīma with this verse about Venerable Sāriputta: ^sn2-29-14-1

“He’s considered astute, ^sn2-29-15-1

Sāriputta, free of anger. ^sn2-29-15-2

Few in wishes, gentle, tamed; ^sn2-29-15-3

developed and well-tamed, he awaits his time.” ^sn2-29-15-4

### The Disciples of Various Monastics of Other Religions (Linked Discourses 2.30) ^sn2-30-0

So I have heard. ^sn2-30-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn2-30-1-2

Then, late at night, several glorious godlings lit up the entire Bamboo Grove. They were Asama, Sahalī, Niṅka, Ākoṭaka, Veṭambarī, and Māṇavagāmiya, and all of them were disciples of various monastics of other religions. They went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. ^sn2-30-1-3

Standing to one side, the godling Asama spoke this verse about Pūraṇa Kassapa in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-30-1-4

“In injuring and killing here, ^sn2-30-2-1

in beating and extortion, ^sn2-30-2-2

Kassapa saw no evil, ^sn2-30-2-3

nor any merit for oneself. ^sn2-30-2-4

What he taught should truly be trusted, ^sn2-30-2-5

he’s worthy of esteem as Teacher.” ^sn2-30-2-6

Then the godling Sahalī spoke this verse about the bamboo-staffed ascetic Gosāla in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-30-3-1

“Through fervent mortification in disgust of sin he became well restrained. ^sn2-30-4-1

He gave up quarreling with people. ^sn2-30-4-2

Refraining from false speech, he spoke the truth. ^sn2-30-4-3

Surely such a man does no wrong!” ^sn2-30-4-4

Then the godling Niṅka spoke this verse about the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-30-5-1

“Disgusted at sin, an alert mendicant, ^sn2-30-6-1

well restrained in the fourfold constraint; ^sn2-30-6-2

explaining what is seen and heard: ^sn2-30-6-3

surely he can be no sinner!” ^sn2-30-6-4

Then the godling Ākoṭaka spoke this verse about various sectarian teachers in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-30-7-1

“Pakudhaka Kātiyāna and the Jain ascetic, ^sn2-30-8-1

as well as this bamboo-staffed ascetic and Pūraṇa: ^sn2-30-8-2

Teachers of communities, attained ascetics, ^sn2-30-8-3

surely they weren’t far from truly good men!” ^sn2-30-8-4

Then the godling Veṭambarī replied to the godling Ākoṭaka in verse: ^sn2-30-9-1

“Though the wretched jackal howls along, ^sn2-30-10-1

it never equals the lion. ^sn2-30-10-2

A nude liar with suspicious conduct, ^sn2-30-10-3

though they teach a community, is not like the good.” ^sn2-30-10-4

Then Māra the Wicked took possession of the godling Veṭambarī and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn2-30-11-1

“Those dedicated to mortification in disgust of sin, ^sn2-30-12-1

safeguarding their seclusion, ^sn2-30-12-2

entrenched in form, ^sn2-30-12-3

they rejoice in the heavenly realm. ^sn2-30-12-4

Indeed, those mortals give correct instructions ^sn2-30-12-5

regarding the next world.” ^sn2-30-12-6

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn2-30-13-1

“Whatever forms there are in this life or the next, ^sn2-30-14-1

and those of shining beauty in the sky, ^sn2-30-14-2

all of these you praise, Namuci, ^sn2-30-14-3

like bait tossed out for catching fish.” ^sn2-30-14-4

Then the godling Māṇavagāmiya spoke this verse about the Buddha in his presence: ^sn2-30-15-1

“Of all the mountains of Rājagaha, ^sn2-30-16-1

Vipula’s said to be the best. ^sn2-30-16-2

Seta is the best of the Himalayan peaks, ^sn2-30-16-3

and the sun, of travelers in space. ^sn2-30-16-4

The ocean is the best of seas, ^sn2-30-17-1

and the moon, of lights that shine at night. ^sn2-30-17-2

But in all the world with its gods, ^sn2-30-17-3

the Buddha is declared foremost.” ^sn2-30-17-4

The Linked Discourses on Godlings are complete. ^sn2-30-18-5

## Saṁyutta 3 ^sn3-0

### Young (Linked Discourses 3.1) ^sn3-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn3-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn3-1-1-2

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn3-1-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha, ^sn3-1-1-4

“Does the worthy Gotama claim to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening?” ^sn3-1-1-5

“If anyone should rightly be said to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening, it’s me. ^sn3-1-1-6

For, great king, I have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening.” ^sn3-1-1-7

“Well, there are those ascetics and brahmins who lead an order and a community, and tutor a community. They’re well-known and famous religious founders, deemed holy by many people. That is, ^sn3-1-2-1

Pūraṇa Kassapa, the bamboo-staffed ascetic Gosāla, the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan, Sañjaya Belaṭṭhiputta, Pakudha Kaccāyana, and Ajita of the hair blanket. ^sn3-1-2-2

I also asked them whether they claimed to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening, but they made no such claim. ^sn3-1-2-3

So why do you, given that you’re so young in age and newly gone forth?” ^sn3-1-2-4

“Great king, these four things should not be looked down upon or disparaged because they are young. ^sn3-1-3-1

What four? ^sn3-1-3-2

An aristocrat, ^sn3-1-3-3

a serpent, ^sn3-1-3-4

a fire, ^sn3-1-3-5

and a mendicant. ^sn3-1-3-6

These four things should not be looked down upon or disparaged because they are young.” ^sn3-1-3-7

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn3-1-4-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn3-1-4-2

“A man should not despise ^sn3-1-5-1

an aristocrat of impeccable lineage, ^sn3-1-5-2

high-born and famous, ^sn3-1-5-3

just because they’re young. ^sn3-1-5-4

For it’s possible that that lord of mankind, ^sn3-1-6-1

as aristocrat, will gain the throne. ^sn3-1-6-2

And in his anger he’ll execute a royal punishment, ^sn3-1-6-3

and have you violently beaten. ^sn3-1-6-4

Hence you should avoid him ^sn3-1-6-5

for the sake of your own life. ^sn3-1-6-6

Whether in village or wilderness, ^sn3-1-7-1

wherever a serpent is seen, ^sn3-1-7-2

a man should not look down on it ^sn3-1-7-3

or despise it for its youth. ^sn3-1-7-4

With its rainbow of colors, ^sn3-1-8-1

the serpent of fiery breath glides along. ^sn3-1-8-2

It lashes out and bites the fool, ^sn3-1-8-3

both men and women alike. ^sn3-1-8-4

Hence you should avoid it ^sn3-1-8-5

for the sake of your own life. ^sn3-1-8-6

A fire devours a huge amount, ^sn3-1-9-1

a conflagration with a blackened trail. ^sn3-1-9-2

A man should not look down on it ^sn3-1-9-3

just because it’s young. ^sn3-1-9-4

For once it gets fuel ^sn3-1-10-1

it’ll become a huge conflagration. ^sn3-1-10-2

It’ll lash out and burn the fool, ^sn3-1-10-3

both men and women alike. ^sn3-1-10-4

Hence you should avoid it ^sn3-1-10-5

for the sake of your own life. ^sn3-1-10-6

When a forest is burned by fire, ^sn3-1-11-1

a conflagration with a blackened trail, ^sn3-1-11-2

the shoots will spring up there again, ^sn3-1-11-3

with the passing of the days and nights. ^sn3-1-11-4

But if a mendicant endowed with ethics ^sn3-1-12-1

burns you with their power, ^sn3-1-12-2

you’ll have no children or livestock, ^sn3-1-12-3

nor will your heirs find wealth. ^sn3-1-12-4

Childless and heirless you become, ^sn3-1-12-5

like a palm-tree stump. ^sn3-1-12-6

That’s why an astute person, ^sn3-1-13-1

seeing what’s good for themselves, ^sn3-1-13-2

would always treat these properly: ^sn3-1-13-3

a snake, a conflagration, ^sn3-1-13-4

a famous aristocrat, ^sn3-1-13-5

and a mendicant endowed with ethics.” ^sn3-1-13-6

When this was said, King Pasenadi of Kosala said to the Buddha, ^sn3-1-14-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^sn3-1-14-2

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn3-1-14-3

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn3-1-14-4

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn3-1-14-5

### A Person (Linked Discourses 3.2) ^sn3-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-2-1-1

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha, ^sn3-2-1-2

“Sir, how many things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort?” ^sn3-2-1-3

“Great king, three things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort. ^sn3-2-2-1

What three? ^sn3-2-2-2

Greed, ^sn3-2-2-3

hate, ^sn3-2-2-4

and delusion. ^sn3-2-2-5

These three things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort.” ^sn3-2-2-6

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-2-2-7

“When greed, hate, and delusion, ^sn3-2-3-1

originate inside oneself, ^sn3-2-3-2

they harm a person of wicked heart, ^sn3-2-3-3

as a bamboo is destroyed by its own fruit.” ^sn3-2-3-4

### Old Age and Death (Linked Discourses 3.3) ^sn3-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-3-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-3-1-2

“Sir, for someone who has been reborn, is there any exemption from old age and death?” ^sn3-3-1-3

“Great king, for someone who has been reborn, there’s no exemption from old age and death. ^sn3-3-1-4

Even for well-to-do aristocrats, ^sn3-3-1-5

brahmins, ^sn3-3-1-6

or householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain—when they’re born, there’s no exemption from old age and death. ^sn3-3-1-7

Even for mendicants who are perfected—who have ended the defilements, completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and are rightly freed through enlightenment—their bodies are liable to break up and be laid to rest.” ^sn3-3-1-8

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-3-1-9

“Fancy chariots of kings wear out, ^sn3-3-2-1

and even this body gets old. ^sn3-3-2-2

But the truth of the good never gets old— ^sn3-3-2-3

so the good proclaim to the good.” ^sn3-3-2-4

### Loved (Linked Discourses 3.4) ^sn3-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-4-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-4-1-2

“Just now, sir, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind. ^sn3-4-1-3

‘Who are those who love themselves? And who are those who don’t love themselves?’ ^sn3-4-1-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn3-4-1-5

‘Those who do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind ^sn3-4-1-6

don’t love themselves. ^sn3-4-1-7

Even though they may say: ^sn3-4-1-8

“I love myself”, they don’t really. ^sn3-4-1-9

Why is that? ^sn3-4-1-10

It’s because they treat themselves like an enemy. ^sn3-4-1-11

That’s why they don’t love themselves. ^sn3-4-1-12

Those who do good things by way of body, speech, and mind ^sn3-4-1-13

do love themselves. ^sn3-4-1-14

Even though they may say: ^sn3-4-1-15

“I don’t love myself”, they do really. ^sn3-4-1-16

Why is that? ^sn3-4-1-17

It’s because they treat themselves like a loved one. ^sn3-4-1-18

That’s why they do love themselves.’” ^sn3-4-1-19

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!” said the Buddha. And he repeated the king’s statement, adding: ^sn3-4-2-1

“If you knew your self as beloved, ^sn3-4-3-1

you’d not yoke yourself to wickedness. ^sn3-4-3-2

For happiness is not easy to find ^sn3-4-3-3

by someone who does bad deeds. ^sn3-4-3-4

When you’re seized by the terminator ^sn3-4-4-1

as you give up your human life, ^sn3-4-4-2

what can you call your own? ^sn3-4-4-3

What do you take when you go? ^sn3-4-4-4

What goes with you, ^sn3-4-4-5

like a shadow that never leaves? ^sn3-4-4-6

Both the good and the bad ^sn3-4-5-1

that a mortal does in this life ^sn3-4-5-2

is what they can call their own. ^sn3-4-5-3

That’s what they take when they go. ^sn3-4-5-4

That’s what goes with them, ^sn3-4-5-5

like a shadow that never leaves. ^sn3-4-5-6

That’s why you should do good, ^sn3-4-6-1

investing in the future life. ^sn3-4-6-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^sn3-4-6-3

support them in the next world.” ^sn3-4-6-4

### Self-Protected (Linked Discourses 3.5) ^sn3-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-5-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-5-1-2

“Just now, sir, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind. ^sn3-5-1-3

‘Who are those who protect themselves? And who are those who don’t protect themselves?’ ^sn3-5-1-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn3-5-1-5

‘Those who do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind ^sn3-5-1-6

don’t protect themselves. ^sn3-5-1-7

Even if they’re protected by a company of elephants, cavalry, chariots, or infantry, ^sn3-5-1-8

they still don’t protect themselves. ^sn3-5-1-9

Why is that? ^sn3-5-1-10

Because such protection is exterior, not interior. ^sn3-5-1-11

That’s why they don’t protect themselves. ^sn3-5-1-12

Those who do good things by way of body, speech, and mind ^sn3-5-1-13

do protect themselves. ^sn3-5-1-14

Even if they’re not protected by a company of elephants, cavalry, chariots, or infantry, ^sn3-5-1-15

they still protect themselves. ^sn3-5-1-16

Why is that? ^sn3-5-1-17

Because such protection is interior, not exterior. ^sn3-5-1-18

That’s why they do protect themselves.’” ^sn3-5-1-19

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!” said the Buddha. And he repeated the king’s statement, adding: ^sn3-5-2-1

“Restraint of the body is good; ^sn3-5-3-1

restraint of speech is good; ^sn3-5-3-2

restraint of mind is good; ^sn3-5-3-3

everywhere, restraint is good. ^sn3-5-3-4

A sincere person, restrained everywhere, ^sn3-5-3-5

is said to be ‘protected’.” ^sn3-5-3-6

### Few (Linked Discourses 3.6) ^sn3-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-6-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-6-1-2

“Just now, sir, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind: ^sn3-6-1-3

‘Few are the sentient beings in the world who, when they obtain luxury possessions, don’t grow indulgent and negligent, giving in to greed for sensual pleasures, and doing the wrong thing by others. ^sn3-6-1-4

There are many more who, when they obtain luxury possessions, do grow indulgent and negligent, giving in to greed for sensual pleasures, and doing the wrong thing by others.’” ^sn3-6-1-5

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!” said the Buddha. And he repeated the king’s statement, adding: ^sn3-6-2-1

“Full of desire for possessions and pleasures, ^sn3-6-3-1

greedy, infatuated by sensual pleasures; ^sn3-6-3-2

they don’t notice that they’ve gone too far, ^sn3-6-3-3

like deer ensnared in a trap set out. ^sn3-6-3-4

It’ll be bitter later on; ^sn3-6-3-5

for the result will be bad for them.” ^sn3-6-3-6

### Judgment (Linked Discourses 3.7) ^sn3-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-7-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-7-1-2

“Sir, when I’m sitting in court I see well-to-do aristocrats, brahmins, and householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. But they tell deliberate lies for the sake of sensual pleasures. ^sn3-7-1-3

Then it occurred to me: ^sn3-7-1-4

‘Enough with passing judgment today. Now my dearest will be known by the judgments he makes.’” ^sn3-7-1-5

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true! ^sn3-7-2-1

Those who are well-to-do aristocrats, brahmins, and householders tell deliberate lies for the sake of sensual pleasures. ^sn3-7-2-2

That is for their lasting harm and suffering.” ^sn3-7-2-3

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-7-2-4

“Full of desire for possessions and pleasures, ^sn3-7-3-1

greedy, infatuated by sensual pleasures; ^sn3-7-3-2

they don’t notice that they’ve gone too far, ^sn3-7-3-3

like fish swiftly snared. ^sn3-7-3-4

It’ll be bitter later on; ^sn3-7-3-5

for the result will be bad for them.” ^sn3-7-3-6

### With Queen Mallikā (Linked Discourses 3.8) ^sn3-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-8-1-1

Now at that time King Pasenadi of Kosala was upstairs in the royal longhouse together with Queen Mallikā. ^sn3-8-1-2

Then the king said to the queen, ^sn3-8-1-3

“Mallikā, is there anyone more dear to you than yourself?” ^sn3-8-1-4

“No, great king, there isn’t. ^sn3-8-1-5

But is there anyone more dear to you than yourself?” ^sn3-8-1-6

“For me also, Mallikā, there’s no-one.” ^sn3-8-1-7

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala came downstairs from the stilt longhouse, went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn3-8-2-1

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha spoke this verse: ^sn3-8-3-1

“Having explored every quarter with the mind, ^sn3-8-4-1

one finds no-one dearer than oneself. ^sn3-8-4-2

Likewise for others, each holds themselves dear; ^sn3-8-4-3

so one who desires self-knowledge would harm no other.” ^sn3-8-4-4

### Sacrifice (Linked Discourses 3.9) ^sn3-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-9-1-1

Now at that time a big sacrifice had been set up for King Pasenadi of Kosala. Bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats and rams—five hundred of each—had been led to the pillar for the sacrifice. ^sn3-9-1-2

His bondservants, servants, and workers did their jobs under threat of punishment and danger, weeping with tearful faces. ^sn3-9-1-3

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn3-9-2-1

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what was happening. ^sn3-9-2-2

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha recited these verses: ^sn3-9-3-1

“Horse sacrifice, human sacrifice, ^sn3-9-4-1

the ‘casting of the yoke-pin’, <j>the ‘royal soma drinking’, ^sn3-9-4-2

and the ‘unimpeded’— ^sn3-9-4-3

these huge violent sacrifices yield no great fruit. ^sn3-9-4-4

The great sages of right comportment ^sn3-9-5-1

don’t attend sacrifices ^sn3-9-5-2

where goats, sheep, and cattle ^sn3-9-5-3

and various creatures are killed. ^sn3-9-5-4

But the great sages of right comportment ^sn3-9-6-1

do attend those sacrifices ^sn3-9-6-2

that are non-violent and propitious, ^sn3-9-6-3

where goats, sheep, and cattle, ^sn3-9-6-4

and various creatures are not killed. ^sn3-9-6-5

A clever person should sacrifice like this, ^sn3-9-7-1

for this sacrifice is very fruitful. ^sn3-9-7-2

For a sponsor of sacrifices like this, ^sn3-9-7-3

things get better, not worse. ^sn3-9-7-4

Such a sacrifice is truly abundant, ^sn3-9-7-5

and even the deities are pleased.” ^sn3-9-7-6

### Shackles (Linked Discourses 3.10) ^sn3-10-0

Now at that time a large group of people had been put in shackles by King Pasenadi of Kosala—some in ropes, some in manacles, some in chains. ^sn3-10-1-1

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn3-10-2-1

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what was happening. ^sn3-10-2-2

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha recited these verses: ^sn3-10-3-1

“The attentive say that shackle is not strong ^sn3-10-4-1

that’s made of iron, wood, or knots. ^sn3-10-4-2

But obsession with jeweled earrings, ^sn3-10-4-3

concern for your partners and children: ^sn3-10-4-4

this, say the attentive, is a strong shackle ^sn3-10-5-1

dragging the indulgent down, hard to escape. ^sn3-10-5-2

Having cut this one too they go forth, ^sn3-10-5-3

unconcerned, having given up sensual pleasures.” ^sn3-10-5-4

### Seven Matted-Hair Ascetics (Linked Discourses 3.11) ^sn3-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^sn3-11-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and sat outside the gate. ^sn3-11-1-2

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^sn3-11-1-3

Now at that time seven matted-hair ascetics, seven Jain ascetics, seven naked ascetics, seven one-cloth ascetics, and seven wanderers passed by not far from the Buddha. Their armpits and bodies were hairy, and their nails were long; and they carried their pack with shoulder-poles. ^sn3-11-2-1

Then King Pasenadi rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, knelt with his right knee on the ground, raised his cupped palms toward those various ascetics, and pronounced his name three times: ^sn3-11-2-2

“Sirs, I am Pasenadi, king of Kosala! … ^sn3-11-2-3

I am Pasenadi, king of Kosala!” ^sn3-11-2-4

Then, soon after those ascetics had left, King Pasenadi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn3-11-3-1

“Sir, these are among those in the world who are perfected or who are on the path to perfection!” ^sn3-11-3-2

“Great king, as a layman enjoying sensual pleasures, living at home with your children, using sandalwood imported from Kāsi, wearing garlands, fragrance, and makeup, and accepting gold and currency, it’s hard for you to know who is perfected or on the path to perfection. ^sn3-11-4-1

You can get to know a person’s ethics by living with them. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless. ^sn3-11-5-1

You can get to know a person’s purity by dealing with them. … ^sn3-11-5-2

You can get to know a person’s resilience in times of trouble. … ^sn3-11-5-3

You can get to know a person’s wisdom by discussion. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless.” ^sn3-11-5-4

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn3-11-6-1

how well said this was by the Buddha. … ^sn3-11-6-2

Sir, these are my spies, my undercover agents returning after spying on the country. ^sn3-11-7-1

I shall later make use of what they first spied out. ^sn3-11-7-2

Now—when they have rinsed off the dust and dirt, and are nicely bathed and anointed, with hair and beard dressed, and dressed in white—they will amuse themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation.” ^sn3-11-7-3

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha recited these verses: ^sn3-11-8-1

“It’s not easy to know a man by his appearance. ^sn3-11-9-1

You shouldn’t trust them at first sight. ^sn3-11-9-2

For undisciplined men live in this world ^sn3-11-9-3

disguised as the disciplined. ^sn3-11-9-4

Like a fake earring made of clay, ^sn3-11-10-1

like a copper halfpenny covered with gold, ^sn3-11-10-2

they live hidden in the world, ^sn3-11-10-3

corrupt inside but impressive outside.” ^sn3-11-10-4

### Five Kings (Linked Discourses 3.12) ^sn3-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-12-1-1

Now at that time five kings headed by Pasenadi were amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation, and this discussion came up among them: ^sn3-12-1-2

“What’s the best of sensual pleasures?” ^sn3-12-1-3

Some of them said, ^sn3-12-1-4

“Sights are the best of sensual pleasures!” ^sn3-12-1-5

Others said, ^sn3-12-1-6

“Sounds are best!” ^sn3-12-1-7

Others said, ^sn3-12-1-8

“Smells are best!” ^sn3-12-1-9

Others said, ^sn3-12-1-10

“Tastes are best!” ^sn3-12-1-11

Others said, ^sn3-12-1-12

“Touches are best!” ^sn3-12-1-13

Since those kings were unable to persuade each other, ^sn3-12-1-14

King Pasenadi said to them, ^sn3-12-2-1

“Come, good fellows, let’s go to the Buddha and ask him about this. ^sn3-12-2-2

As he answers, so we’ll remember it.” ^sn3-12-2-3

“Yes, good fellows,” replied those kings. ^sn3-12-2-4

Then those five kings headed by Pasenadi went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. King Pasenadi reported their conversation to the Buddha, and said, ^sn3-12-3-1

“Sir, what’s the best of sensual pleasures?” ^sn3-12-3-14

“Great king, which kind of sensual stimulation is best is defined by which is most agreeable, I say. ^sn3-12-4-1

The very same sights that are agreeable to some are disagreeable to others. ^sn3-12-4-2

When you’re happy with certain sights, as you’ve got all you wished for, you don’t want any other sight that’s better or finer. ^sn3-12-4-3

For you, those sights are perfect ^sn3-12-4-4

and supreme. ^sn3-12-4-5

The very same sounds … ^sn3-12-5-1

smells … ^sn3-12-6-1

tastes … ^sn3-12-7-1

touches that are agreeable to some are disagreeable to others. ^sn3-12-8-1

When you’re happy with certain touches, as you’ve got all you wished for, you don’t want any other touch that’s better or finer. ^sn3-12-8-2

For you, those touches are perfect ^sn3-12-8-3

and supreme.” ^sn3-12-8-4

Now at that time the lay follower Candanaṅgalika was sitting in that assembly. ^sn3-12-9-1

Then he rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn3-12-9-2

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn3-12-9-3

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn3-12-9-4

Then the lay follower Candanaṅgalika extolled the Buddha in his presence with an appropriate verse: ^sn3-12-10-1

“Like a fragrant pink lotus ^sn3-12-11-1

that blooms at daybreak, its fragrance unfaded— ^sn3-12-11-2

see Aṅgīrasa shine, ^sn3-12-11-3

bright as the sun in the sky!” ^sn3-12-11-4

Then those five kings clothed Candanaṅgalika with five upper robes. ^sn3-12-12-1

And Candanaṅgalika in turn endowed the Buddha with those robes. ^sn3-12-12-2

### A Bucketful of Rice (Linked Discourses 3.13) ^sn3-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-13-1-1

Now at that time King Pasenadi of Kosala used to eat boiled rice by the bucketful. ^sn3-13-1-2

Then after eating King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, huffing and puffing. He bowed and sat down to one side. ^sn3-13-1-3

Then, knowing that King Pasenadi was huffing and puffing after eating, on that occasion the Buddha spoke this verse: ^sn3-13-2-1

“When a man is always mindful, ^sn3-13-3-1

knowing moderation in eating, ^sn3-13-3-2

his discomfort fades, ^sn3-13-3-3

and he ages slowly, taking care of his life.” ^sn3-13-3-4

Now at that time the student Sudassana was standing behind the king. ^sn3-13-4-1

Then King Pasenadi addressed him, ^sn3-13-4-2

“Please, dear Sudassana, memorize this verse in the Buddha’s presence and recite it to me whenever I am served a meal. ^sn3-13-4-3

I’ll set up a regular daily allowance of a hundred dollars for you.” ^sn3-13-4-4

“Yes, Your Majesty,” replied Sudassana. He memorized that verse in the Buddha’s presence, and then whenever the king was served a meal he would repeat it: ^sn3-13-4-5

“When a man is always mindful, ^sn3-13-5-1

knowing moderation in eating, ^sn3-13-5-2

his discomfort fades, ^sn3-13-5-3

and he ages slowly, taking care of his life.” ^sn3-13-5-4

Then the king gradually got used to having at most a cup of rice. ^sn3-13-6-1

After some time King Pasenadi’s body slimmed right down. Stroking his limbs with his hands, at that time he expressed this heartfelt sentiment: ^sn3-13-6-2

“In both ways the Buddha has sympathy for me: ^sn3-13-6-3

in the good of this life and the good of the next.” ^sn3-13-6-4

### Battle (1st) (Linked Discourses 3.14) ^sn3-14-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-14-1-1

Then King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, mobilized an army of four divisions and marched to Kāsi to attack King Pasenadi of Kosala. ^sn3-14-1-2

When King Pasenadi heard of this, ^sn3-14-1-3

he mobilized an army of four divisions and marched to Kāsi to defend it against Ajātasattu. ^sn3-14-1-5

Then the two kings met in battle. ^sn3-14-1-6

And in that battle Ajātasattu defeated Pasenadi, ^sn3-14-1-7

who withdrew to his own capital at Sāvatthī. ^sn3-14-1-8

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn3-14-2-1

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. Then the Buddha said: ^sn3-14-2-2

“Mendicants, King Ajātasattu has bad friends, companions, and associates. ^sn3-14-4-1

But King Pasenadi has good friends, companions, and associates. ^sn3-14-4-2

Yet on this day King Pasenadi will have a bad night’s sleep as one defeated.” ^sn3-14-4-3

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-14-4-4

“Victory breeds enmity; ^sn3-14-5-1

the defeated sleep badly. ^sn3-14-5-2

The peaceful sleep at ease, ^sn3-14-5-3

having left victory and defeat behind.” ^sn3-14-5-4

### Battle (2nd) (Linked Discourses 3.15) ^sn3-15-0

Then King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, mobilized an army of four divisions and marched to Kāsi to attack King Pasenadi of Kosala. ^sn3-15-1-1

When King Pasenadi heard of this, ^sn3-15-1-2

he mobilized an army of four divisions and marched to Kāsi to defend it against Ajātasattu. ^sn3-15-1-4

Then the two kings met in battle. ^sn3-15-1-5

And in that battle Pasenadi defeated Ajātasattu and captured him alive. ^sn3-15-1-6

Then King Pasenadi thought, ^sn3-15-1-7

“Even though I’ve never betrayed this King Ajātasattu, he betrayed me. Still, he is my nephew. ^sn3-15-1-8

Now that I’ve vanquished all of Ajātasattu’s elephant troops, cavalry, chariots, and infantry, why don’t I let him loose with just his life?” ^sn3-15-1-9

And that’s what he did. ^sn3-15-2-1

Then several mendicants … ^sn3-15-3-1

told the Buddha what had happened. ^sn3-15-3-2

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha recited these verses: ^sn3-15-6-1

“A man goes on plundering ^sn3-15-7-1

as long as it serves his ends. ^sn3-15-7-2

But as soon as others plunder him, ^sn3-15-7-3

the plunderer is plundered. ^sn3-15-7-4

For the fool thinks they’re on solid ground, ^sn3-15-8-1

so long as their wickedness has not ripened. ^sn3-15-8-2

But as soon as that wickedness ripens, ^sn3-15-8-3

they fall into suffering. ^sn3-15-8-4

A killer creates a killer; ^sn3-15-9-1

a conqueror creates a conqueror; ^sn3-15-9-2

an abuser creates abuse, ^sn3-15-9-3

and a bully creates a bully. ^sn3-15-9-4

And so as deeds unfold ^sn3-15-9-5

the plunderer is plundered.” ^sn3-15-9-6

### A Daughter (Linked Discourses 3.16) ^sn3-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-16-1-1

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^sn3-16-1-2

Then a man went up to the king and whispered in his ear, ^sn3-16-1-3

“Your Majesty, Queen Mallikā has given birth to a daughter.” ^sn3-16-1-4

When this was said, King Pasenadi was disappointed. ^sn3-16-1-5

Then, knowing that King Pasenadi was disappointed, on that occasion the Buddha recited these verses: ^sn3-16-2-1

“Well, some women are better than men, ^sn3-16-3-1

O ruler of the people. ^sn3-16-3-2

Wise and virtuous, a devoted wife ^sn3-16-3-3

whose mother-in-law is her queen. ^sn3-16-3-4

And when she has a son, ^sn3-16-4-1

he becomes a hero, O lord of the land. ^sn3-16-4-2

The son of such a blessed lady ^sn3-16-4-3

may even rule the realm.” ^sn3-16-4-4

### Diligence (Linked Discourses 3.17) ^sn3-17-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-17-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-17-1-3

“Sir, is there one thing that secures benefits for both ^sn3-17-1-4

this life and lives to come?” ^sn3-17-1-5

“There is, great king.” ^sn3-17-2-1

“So what is that one thing?” ^sn3-17-3-1

“Diligence, great king, is one thing that secures benefits for both ^sn3-17-4-1

this life and lives to come. ^sn3-17-4-2

The footprints of all creatures that walk can fit inside an elephant’s footprint. So an elephant’s footprint is said to be the biggest of them all. ^sn3-17-4-3

In the same way, diligence is one thing that secures benefits for both ^sn3-17-4-4

this life and lives to come.” ^sn3-17-4-5

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-17-4-6

“For one who desires high delights ^sn3-17-5-1

again and again— ^sn3-17-5-2

long life, beauty, and health, ^sn3-17-5-3

heaven, and an eminent family— ^sn3-17-5-4

the astute praise diligence ^sn3-17-6-1

in making merit. ^sn3-17-6-2

Being diligent, an astute person ^sn3-17-6-3

secures both benefits: ^sn3-17-6-4

the benefit in this life, ^sn3-17-7-1

and in lives to come. ^sn3-17-7-2

An attentive one, comprehending the meaning, ^sn3-17-7-3

is said to be astute.” ^sn3-17-7-4

### Good Friends (Linked Discourses 3.18) ^sn3-18-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-18-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-18-1-2

“Just now, sir, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind. ^sn3-18-1-3

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha. But it’s for someone with good friends, companions, and associates, not for someone with bad friends, companions, and associates.’” ^sn3-18-1-4

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!” said the Buddha. And he repeated the king’s statement, adding: ^sn3-18-2-1

“Great king, this one time I was staying in the land of the Sakyans where they have a town named Townsville. ^sn3-18-3-1

Then the mendicant Ānanda came to me, bowed, sat down to one side, and said: ^sn3-18-3-2

‘Sir, good friends, companions, and associates are half the spiritual life.’ ^sn3-18-3-3

When he had spoken, I said to him: ^sn3-18-4-1

‘Not so, Ānanda! Not so, Ānanda! ^sn3-18-4-2

Good friends, companions, and associates are the whole of the spiritual life. ^sn3-18-4-3

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path. ^sn3-18-4-4

And how does a mendicant with good friends develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path? ^sn3-18-5-1

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn3-18-5-2

That’s how a mendicant with good friends develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path. ^sn3-18-5-3

And here’s another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life. ^sn3-18-5-4

For, by relying on me as a good friend, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things. ^sn3-18-6-1

This is another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.’ ^sn3-18-6-2

So, great king, you should train like this: ^sn3-18-7-1

‘I will have good friends, companions, and associates.’ ^sn3-18-7-2

That’s how you should train. ^sn3-18-7-3

When you have good friends, companions, and associates, you should live supported by one thing: ^sn3-18-8-1

diligence in skillful qualities. ^sn3-18-8-2

When you’re diligent, supported by diligence, your ladies of the harem, ^sn3-18-9-1

aristocrat vassals, ^sn3-18-10-1

troops, ^sn3-18-11-1

and people of town and country will think: ^sn3-18-12-1

‘The king lives diligently, supported by diligence. ^sn3-18-12-2

We’d better live diligently, supported by diligence!’ ^sn3-18-12-3

When you’re diligent, supported by diligence, then not only you yourself, ^sn3-18-13-1

but your ladies of the harem, and your treasury and storehouses will be guarded and protected.” ^sn3-18-13-2

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-18-13-3

“For one who desires high wealth ^sn3-18-14-1

again and again, ^sn3-18-14-2

the astute praise diligence ^sn3-18-14-3

in making merit. ^sn3-18-14-4

Being diligent, an astute person ^sn3-18-14-5

secures both benefits: ^sn3-18-14-6

the benefit in this life, ^sn3-18-15-1

and in lives to come. ^sn3-18-15-2

Attentive, comprehending the meaning, ^sn3-18-15-3

they are said to be astute.” ^sn3-18-15-4

### Childless (1st) (Linked Discourses 3.19) ^sn3-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-19-1-1

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha in the middle of the day, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^sn3-19-1-2

“So, great king, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^sn3-19-1-3

“Sir, here in Sāvatthī a financier householder has passed away. ^sn3-19-2-1

Since he died childless, I have come after transferring his fortune to the royal compound. ^sn3-19-2-2

There was eight million in gold coin, not to mention the silver coin. ^sn3-19-2-3

And yet that financier ate meals of ^sn3-19-2-4

rough gruel with false black pepper. ^sn3-19-2-5

He wore clothes consisting of ^sn3-19-2-6

three patches of sunn hemp. ^sn3-19-2-7

He traveled around in a vehicle that was ^sn3-19-2-8

a dilapidated little cart, holding a leaf as parasol.” ^sn3-19-2-9

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true! ^sn3-19-3-1

When an untrue person has acquired notable wealth they don’t make themselves happy and pleased. Nor do they make their mother and father, partners and children, bondservants, workers, and staff, and friends and colleagues happy and pleased. And they don’t establish an uplifting religious donation for ascetics and brahmins that’s conducive to heaven, ripens in happiness, and leads to heaven. ^sn3-19-3-2

Because they haven’t made proper use of that wealth, rulers or bandits take it, or fire consumes it, or flood sweeps it away, or unloved heirs take it. ^sn3-19-3-3

Since that wealth is not properly utilized, it’s wasted, not used. ^sn3-19-3-4

Suppose there was a lotus pond in an uninhabited region with clear, sweet, cool water, clean, with smooth banks, delightful. ^sn3-19-4-1

But people don’t collect it or drink it or bathe in it or use it for any purpose. ^sn3-19-4-2

Since that water is not properly utilized, it’s wasted, not used. ^sn3-19-4-3

In the same way, when an untrue person has acquired notable wealth … ^sn3-19-4-4

it’s wasted, not used. ^sn3-19-4-6

When a true person has acquired notable wealth they make themselves happy and pleased. And they make their mother and father, partners and children, bondservants, workers, and staff, and friends and colleagues happy and pleased. And they establish an uplifting religious donation for ascetics and brahmins that’s conducive to heaven, ripens in happiness, and leads to heaven. ^sn3-19-5-1

Because they make proper use of that wealth, rulers or bandits don’t take it, fire doesn’t consume it, flood doesn’t sweep it away, and unloved heirs don’t take it. ^sn3-19-5-2

Since that wealth is properly utilized, it’s used, not wasted. ^sn3-19-5-3

Suppose there was a lotus pond not far from a town or village with clear, sweet, cool water, clean, with smooth banks, delightful. ^sn3-19-6-1

And people collected it and drank it and bathed in it and used it for their own purpose. ^sn3-19-6-2

Since that water is properly utilized, it’s used, not wasted. ^sn3-19-6-3

In the same way, when a true person has acquired notable wealth … ^sn3-19-6-4

it’s used, not wasted. ^sn3-19-6-6

As cool water in an uninhabited region ^sn3-19-7-1

evaporates when not drunk; ^sn3-19-7-2

so too when a reprobate acquires wealth, ^sn3-19-7-3

they neither use it themselves nor give it away. ^sn3-19-7-4

When the attentive and sensible acquire wealth, ^sn3-19-8-1

they use it, being dutiful. ^sn3-19-8-2

That boss, having supported the family unit, ^sn3-19-8-3

blameless, goes to a heavenly place.” ^sn3-19-8-4

### Childless (2nd) (Linked Discourses 3.20) ^sn3-20-0

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha in the middle of the day … The Buddha said to him, ^sn3-20-1-1

“So, great king, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^sn3-20-1-2

“Sir, here in Sāvatthī a financier householder has passed away. ^sn3-20-2-1

Since he died childless, I have come after transferring his fortune to the royal compound. ^sn3-20-2-2

There was ten million in gold coin, not to mention the silver coin. ^sn3-20-2-3

And yet that financier ate meals of ^sn3-20-2-4

rough gruel with false black pepper. ^sn3-20-2-5

He wore clothes consisting of ^sn3-20-2-6

three patches of sunn hemp. ^sn3-20-2-7

He traveled around in a vehicle that was ^sn3-20-2-8

a dilapidated little cart, holding a leaf as parasol.” ^sn3-20-2-9

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true! ^sn3-20-3-1

Once upon a time, great king, that financier householder provided almsfood on behalf of an Independent Buddha named Tagarasikhī. ^sn3-20-3-2

He instructed: ‘Give alms to that ascetic,’ before getting up from his seat and leaving. ^sn3-20-3-3

But after giving he regretted it: ^sn3-20-3-4

‘It would have been better to feed the bondservants or workers with that almsfood.’ ^sn3-20-3-5

What’s more, he murdered his brother’s only child for the sake of his fortune. ^sn3-20-3-6

Because that financier provided Tagarasikhī with almsfood, as a result of that deed he was reborn seven times in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn3-20-4-1

And as a residual result of that same deed he held the position of financier seven times right here in Sāvatthī. ^sn3-20-4-2

But because that financier regretted giving alms, ^sn3-20-4-3

as a result of that deed his mind didn’t tend to enjoy nice food, clothes, vehicles, or the five refined kinds of sensual stimulation. ^sn3-20-4-4

And because that financier murdered his brother’s only child for the sake of his fortune, as a result of that deed he burned in hell for many years, for many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands of years. ^sn3-20-4-5

And as a residual result of that same deed, he is childless for the seventh time, his fortune ending up in the royal treasury. ^sn3-20-4-6

Now the old merit of that financier has been used up, and he hasn’t accumulated new merit. ^sn3-20-4-7

Today, great king, that financier burns in the Great Hell of Screams.” ^sn3-20-4-8

“Really, sir, that financier has been reborn in the Great Hell of Screams?” ^sn3-20-4-9

“Yes he has, great king.” ^sn3-20-4-10

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-20-4-11

“Grain, money, silver, and gold, ^sn3-20-5-1

or whatever other possessions there are; ^sn3-20-5-2

bondservants, workers, servants, ^sn3-20-5-3

and those dependent for their livelihood: ^sn3-20-5-4

you must go on without taking these; ^sn3-20-6-1

all of them are left behind. ^sn3-20-6-2

But the deeds you do ^sn3-20-6-3

by body, speech, and mind— ^sn3-20-6-4

that’s what you can call your own. ^sn3-20-7-1

That’s what you take when you go. ^sn3-20-7-2

That’s what goes with you, ^sn3-20-7-3

like a shadow that never leaves. ^sn3-20-7-4

That’s why you should do good, ^sn3-20-8-1

investing in the future life. ^sn3-20-8-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^sn3-20-8-3

support them in the next world.” ^sn3-20-8-4

### Individuals (Linked Discourses 3.21) ^sn3-21-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-21-1-1

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn3-21-1-2

“Great king, these four individuals are found in the world. ^sn3-21-1-3

What four? ^sn3-21-1-4

The dark bound for darkness, ^sn3-21-1-5

the dark bound for light, ^sn3-21-1-6

the light bound for darkness, ^sn3-21-1-7

and the light bound for light. ^sn3-21-1-8

And how is an individual dark and bound for darkness? ^sn3-21-2-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in a low family—a family of corpse-workers, bamboo-workers, hunters, chariot-makers, or scavengers—poor, with little to eat or drink, where life is tough, and food and shelter are hard to find. ^sn3-21-2-2

And they’re ugly, unsightly, deformed, sickly—one-eyed, crippled, lame, or half-paralyzed. They don’t get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; or bed, house, and lighting. ^sn3-21-2-3

And they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn3-21-2-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn3-21-2-5

This individual is like someone who goes from darkness to darkness, from blackness to blackness, from bloodstain to bloodstain. ^sn3-21-3-1

That’s how an individual is dark and bound for darkness. ^sn3-21-3-3

And how is an individual dark and bound for light? ^sn3-21-4-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in a low family—a family of corpse-workers, bamboo-workers, hunters, chariot-makers, or scavengers—poor, with little to eat or drink, where life is tough, and food and shelter are hard to find. ^sn3-21-4-2

And they’re ugly, unsightly, deformed, sickly—one-eyed, crippled, lame, or half-paralyzed. They don’t get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; or bed, house, and lighting. ^sn3-21-4-3

But they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn3-21-4-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn3-21-4-5

This individual is like someone who ascends from the ground to a couch; from a couch to horseback; from horseback to an elephant; and from an elephant to a stilt longhouse. ^sn3-21-5-1

That’s how an individual is dark and bound for light. ^sn3-21-5-3

And how is an individual light and bound for darkness? ^sn3-21-6-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in an eminent family—a well-to-do family of aristocrats, brahmins, or householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. ^sn3-21-6-2

And they’re attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. They get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^sn3-21-6-3

But they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn3-21-6-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn3-21-6-5

This individual is like someone who descends from a stilt longhouse to an elephant; from an elephant to horseback; from horseback to a couch; and from a couch to the ground; and from the ground they enter darkness. ^sn3-21-7-1

That’s how an individual is light and bound for darkness. ^sn3-21-7-3

And how is an individual light and bound for light? ^sn3-21-8-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in an eminent family—a well-to-do family of aristocrats, brahmins, or householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. ^sn3-21-8-2

And they’re attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. They get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^sn3-21-8-3

And they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn3-21-8-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn3-21-8-5

This individual is like someone who shifts from one couch to another; from the back of one horse to another; from one elephant to another; or from one stilt longhouse to another. ^sn3-21-9-1

That’s how an individual is light and bound for light. ^sn3-21-9-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^sn3-21-9-4

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-21-9-5

“O king, some people are poor, ^sn3-21-10-1

faithless and stingy. ^sn3-21-10-2

Miserly, with bad intentions, ^sn3-21-10-3

they lack regard, they have wrong view. ^sn3-21-10-4

They abuse and insult ^sn3-21-11-1

ascetics and brahmins ^sn3-21-11-2

and other supplicants. ^sn3-21-11-3

They’re nihilists and bullies, ^sn3-21-11-4

who prevent others from giving ^sn3-21-12-1

food to beggars. ^sn3-21-12-2

O king, ruler of the people: ^sn3-21-12-3

when such people die ^sn3-21-12-4

they fall into the terrible hell— ^sn3-21-12-5

from darkness they’re bound for darkness. ^sn3-21-12-6

O king, some people are poor, ^sn3-21-13-1

but faithful and not stingy. ^sn3-21-13-2

They give with best of intentions, ^sn3-21-13-3

that peaceful-hearted person. ^sn3-21-13-4

They rise for and bow to ^sn3-21-14-1

ascetics and brahmins ^sn3-21-14-2

and other supplicants. ^sn3-21-14-3

Training in moral conduct, ^sn3-21-14-4

they don’t prevent others from giving ^sn3-21-15-1

food to beggars. ^sn3-21-15-2

O king, ruler of the people: ^sn3-21-15-3

when such people die ^sn3-21-15-4

they go to the Third Heaven— ^sn3-21-15-5

from darkness they’re bound for light. ^sn3-21-15-6

O king, some people are rich, ^sn3-21-16-1

but faithless and stingy. ^sn3-21-16-2

Miserly, with bad intentions, ^sn3-21-16-3

they lack regard, they have wrong view. ^sn3-21-16-4

They abuse and insult ^sn3-21-17-1

ascetics and brahmins ^sn3-21-17-2

and other supplicants ^sn3-21-17-3

They’re nihilists and bullies, ^sn3-21-17-4

who prevent others from giving ^sn3-21-18-1

food to beggars. ^sn3-21-18-2

O king, ruler of the people: ^sn3-21-18-3

when such people die ^sn3-21-18-4

they fall into the terrible hell— ^sn3-21-18-5

from light they’re bound for darkness. ^sn3-21-18-6

O king, some people are rich, ^sn3-21-19-1

faithful and not stingy. ^sn3-21-19-2

They give with best of intentions, ^sn3-21-19-3

that peaceful-hearted person. ^sn3-21-19-4

They rise for and bow to ^sn3-21-20-1

ascetics and brahmins ^sn3-21-20-2

and other supplicants. ^sn3-21-20-3

Training in moral conduct, ^sn3-21-20-4

they don’t prevent others from giving ^sn3-21-21-1

food to beggars. ^sn3-21-21-2

O king, ruler of the people: ^sn3-21-21-3

when such people die ^sn3-21-21-4

they go to the Third Heaven— ^sn3-21-21-5

from light they’re bound for light.” ^sn3-21-21-6

### Grandmother (Linked Discourses 3.22) ^sn3-22-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-22-1-1

King Pasenadi of Kosala sat to one side, and the Buddha said to him, ^sn3-22-1-2
