“So, great king, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^sn3-22-1-3

“Sir, my grandmother has passed away. She was old, elderly and senior. She was advanced in years and had reached the final stage of life; she was a hundred and twenty years old. ^sn3-22-2-1

But I loved my grandmother; she was dear to me. ^sn3-22-2-2

If by giving away the elephant-treasure I could get my grandmother back, I’d do it. ^sn3-22-2-3

If by giving away the horse-treasure I could get my grandmother back, I’d do it. ^sn3-22-2-5

If by giving away a prize village I could get my grandmother back, I’d do it. ^sn3-22-2-7

If by giving away the whole country I could get my grandmother back, I’d do it.” ^sn3-22-2-9

“Great king, all sentient beings are liable to die. Death is their end; they’re not exempt from death.” ^sn3-22-2-11

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn3-22-2-12

how well said this was by the Buddha: ^sn3-22-2-13

‘All sentient beings are liable to die. Death is their end; they’re not exempt from death.’” ^sn3-22-2-14

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true! ^sn3-22-3-1

All sentient beings are liable to die. Death is their end; they’re not exempt from death. ^sn3-22-3-2

It’s like the vessels made by potters. Whatever kind they are, whether baked or unbaked, all of them are liable to break apart. Breaking is their end; they’re not exempt from breakage. ^sn3-22-3-3

In the same way, all sentient beings are liable to die. Death is their end; they’re not exempt from death.” ^sn3-22-3-4

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-22-3-5

“All beings will die, ^sn3-22-4-1

for life ends in death. ^sn3-22-4-2

They pass on according to their deeds, ^sn3-22-4-3

reaping the fruits of good and bad. ^sn3-22-4-4

Those who do bad go to hell, ^sn3-22-4-5

and if you do good you go to heaven. ^sn3-22-4-6

That’s why you should do good, ^sn3-22-5-1

investing in the future life. ^sn3-22-5-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^sn3-22-5-3

support them in the next world.” ^sn3-22-5-4

### The World (Linked Discourses 3.23) ^sn3-23-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-23-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-23-1-2

“Sir, how many things arise in the world for its harm, suffering, and discomfort?” ^sn3-23-1-3

“Great king, three things arise in the world for its harm, suffering, and discomfort. ^sn3-23-1-4

What three? ^sn3-23-1-5

Greed, ^sn3-23-1-6

hate, ^sn3-23-1-7

and delusion. ^sn3-23-1-8

These three things arise in the world for its harm, suffering, and discomfort.” ^sn3-23-1-9

That is what the Buddha said. … ^sn3-23-1-10

“When greed, hate, and delusion, ^sn3-23-2-1

originate inside oneself, ^sn3-23-2-2

they harm a person of wicked heart, ^sn3-23-2-3

as a bamboo is destroyed by its own fruit.” ^sn3-23-2-4

### Archery (Linked Discourses 3.24) ^sn3-24-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-24-1-1

Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha, ^sn3-24-1-2

“Sir, where should a gift be given?” ^sn3-24-1-3

“Wherever your heart feels inspired, great king.” ^sn3-24-1-4

“But sir, where is a gift very fruitful?” ^sn3-24-1-5

“Where a gift should be given is one thing, great king, but where a gift is very fruitful is another. ^sn3-24-1-6

A gift is very fruitful when it’s given to an ethical person, not so much to an unethical person. ^sn3-24-1-7

Well then, great king, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^sn3-24-1-8

What do you think, great king? ^sn3-24-1-9

Suppose you were at war, ready to fight a battle. ^sn3-24-1-10

Then along comes an aristocrat youth who is untrained, inexpert, unfit, inexperienced. And he’s fearful, scared, nervous, quick to flee. ^sn3-24-1-11

Would you employ such a man? Would he be of any use to you?” ^sn3-24-1-12

“No, sir, I would have no use for such a man.” ^sn3-24-1-13

“What about a brahmin youth, ^sn3-24-1-14

a peasant youth, ^sn3-24-1-15

or a menial youth who was similar?” ^sn3-24-1-16

“No, sir, I would have no use for such a man.” ^sn3-24-1-17

“What do you think, great king? ^sn3-24-2-1

Suppose you were at war, ready to fight a battle. ^sn3-24-2-2

Then along comes an aristocrat youth who is trained, expert, fit, experienced. And he’s fearless, brave, bold, standing his ground. ^sn3-24-2-3

Would you employ such a man? Would he be of any use to you?” ^sn3-24-2-4

“Yes, sir, I would have a use for such a man.” ^sn3-24-2-5

“What about a brahmin youth, ^sn3-24-2-6

a peasant youth, ^sn3-24-2-7

or a menial youth who was similar? ^sn3-24-2-8

Would you employ such a man? Would he be of any use to you?” ^sn3-24-2-9

“Yes, sir, I would have a use for such a man.” ^sn3-24-2-10

“In the same way, a gift to anyone who has given up five factors and possesses five factors is very fruitful, no matter what family they’ve gone forth from. ^sn3-24-3-1

What are the five factors they’ve given up? ^sn3-24-3-2

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^sn3-24-3-3

These are the five factors they’ve given up. ^sn3-24-3-4

What are the five factors they possess? ^sn3-24-3-5

The entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and knowledge and vision of freedom. ^sn3-24-3-6

These are the five factors they possess. ^sn3-24-3-7

I say that a gift to anyone who has given up these five factors and possesses these five factors is very fruitful.” ^sn3-24-3-8

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn3-24-3-9

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn3-24-3-10

“Any youth skilled at archery, ^sn3-24-4-1

powerful and vigorous, ^sn3-24-4-2

would be employed by a king going to war— ^sn3-24-4-3

one is not a coward by reason of birth. ^sn3-24-4-4

Just so, whoever is settled ^sn3-24-5-1

in the qualities of patience and gentleness, ^sn3-24-5-2

a clever person with noble conduct, ^sn3-24-5-3

should be venerated even if they’re low born. ^sn3-24-5-4

You should build lovely hermitages ^sn3-24-6-1

and settle learned people in them. ^sn3-24-6-2

You should set up water supplies in barren regions ^sn3-24-6-3

and passages in places hard to travel. ^sn3-24-6-4

Food, drink, edibles, ^sn3-24-7-1

clothes, and lodgings ^sn3-24-7-2

should be given to the upright ones, ^sn3-24-7-3

with a clear and confident heart. ^sn3-24-7-4

The thundering rain cloud, ^sn3-24-8-1

its hundred peaks wreathed in lightning, ^sn3-24-8-2

pours down over the rich earth, ^sn3-24-8-3

soaking the uplands and valleys. ^sn3-24-8-4

So too an astute person, ^sn3-24-9-1

faithful and learned, ^sn3-24-9-2

should prepare a meal to satisfy ^sn3-24-9-3

supplicants with food and drink. ^sn3-24-9-4

Rejoicing, they strew gifts about, ^sn3-24-10-1

crying ‘Give! give!’ ^sn3-24-10-2

For that is their thunder, ^sn3-24-10-3

like rain sent from the heavens. ^sn3-24-10-4

That stream of merit so abundant ^sn3-24-10-5

showers down on the giver.” ^sn3-24-10-6

### The Simile of the Mountain (Linked Discourses 3.25) ^sn3-25-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn3-25-1-1

King Pasenadi of Kosala sat to one side, and the Buddha said to him, ^sn3-25-1-2

“So, great king, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^sn3-25-1-3

“Sir, there are anointed aristocratic kings who are infatuated with authority, and obsessed with greed for sensual pleasures. They have attained stability in the country, occupying a vast conquered territory. Today I have been busy fulfilling the duties of such kings.” ^sn3-25-1-4

“What do you think, great king? ^sn3-25-2-1

Suppose a trustworthy and dependable man were to come from the east. ^sn3-25-2-2

He’d approach you and say: ^sn3-25-2-3

‘Please great king, you should know this. I come from the east. ^sn3-25-2-4

There I saw a huge mountain that reached the clouds. And it was coming this way, crushing all creatures. ^sn3-25-2-5

So then, great king, do what you must!’ ^sn3-25-2-6

Then a second trustworthy and dependable man were to come from the west … ^sn3-25-2-7

a third from the north … ^sn3-25-2-8

and a fourth from the south. ^sn3-25-2-9

He’d approach you and say: ^sn3-25-2-10

‘Please great king, you should know this. I come from the south. ^sn3-25-2-11

There I saw a huge mountain that reached the clouds. And it was coming this way, crushing all creatures. ^sn3-25-2-12

So then, great king, do what you must!’ ^sn3-25-2-13

Should such a dire threat arise—a grim loss of human life, when human birth is so rare—what would you do?” ^sn3-25-2-14

“Sir, what could I do but practice the teachings, practice morality, doing skillful and good actions?” ^sn3-25-3-1

“I tell you, great king, I announce to you: old age and death are advancing upon you. ^sn3-25-4-1

Since old age and death are advancing upon you, what would you do?” ^sn3-25-4-2

“Sir, what can I do but practice the teachings, practice morality, doing skillful and good actions? ^sn3-25-4-3

Sir, there are anointed aristocratic kings who are infatuated with authority, and obsessed with greed for sensual pleasures. They have attained stability in the country, occupying a vast conquered territory. Such kings engage in battles of elephants, ^sn3-25-4-4

cavalry, ^sn3-25-4-7

chariots, ^sn3-25-4-8

or infantry. ^sn3-25-4-9

But there is no place, no domain for such battles when old age and death are advancing. ^sn3-25-4-10

In this royal court there are ministers of wise counsel who are capable of dividing an approaching enemy by wise counsel. ^sn3-25-4-11

But there is no place, no domain for such diplomatic battles when old age and death are advancing. ^sn3-25-4-12

In this royal court there is abundant gold, both coined and uncoined, stored above and below ground. Using this wealth we can bribe an approaching enemy. ^sn3-25-4-13

But there is no place, no domain for such monetary battles when old age and death are advancing. ^sn3-25-4-14

When old age and death are advancing, what can I do but practice the teachings, practice morality, doing skillful and good actions?” ^sn3-25-4-15

“That’s so true, great king! That’s so true! ^sn3-25-5-1

When old age and death are advancing, what can you do but practice the teachings, practice morality, doing skillful and good actions?” ^sn3-25-5-2

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn3-25-5-3

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn3-25-5-4

“Suppose there were vast mountains ^sn3-25-6-1

of solid rock pressing against the sky ^sn3-25-6-2

drawing in from all sides ^sn3-25-6-3

and crushing the four quarters. ^sn3-25-6-4

So too old age and death ^sn3-25-7-1

advance upon all living creatures— ^sn3-25-7-2

aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, ^sn3-25-7-3

menials, corpse-workers, and scavengers. ^sn3-25-7-4

They spare nothing. ^sn3-25-7-5

They crush all beneath them. ^sn3-25-7-6

There’s nowhere for elephants to take a stand, ^sn3-25-8-1

nor chariots nor infantry. ^sn3-25-8-2

They can’t be defeated ^sn3-25-8-3

by diplomatic battles or by wealth. ^sn3-25-8-4

That’s why an astute person, ^sn3-25-9-1

seeing what’s good for themselves, ^sn3-25-9-2

being wise, would place faith ^sn3-25-9-3

in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^sn3-25-9-4

Whoever lives by the teaching ^sn3-25-10-1

in body, speech, and mind, ^sn3-25-10-2

is praised in this life ^sn3-25-10-3

and departs to rejoice in heaven.” ^sn3-25-10-4

The Linked Discourses with the Kosalan are complete. ^sn3-25-11-5

## Saṁyutta 4 ^sn4-0

### Mortification (Linked Discourses 4.1) ^sn4-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-1-1-1

At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn4-1-1-2

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn4-1-1-3

“I am truly freed from that grueling work! ^sn4-1-1-4

Thank goodness I’m freed from that pointless grueling work. ^sn4-1-1-5

Thank goodness I’m freed and have attained awakening.” ^sn4-1-1-6

And then Māra the Wicked, knowing the Buddha’s train of thought, went up to him and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-1-2-1

“You’ve left the practice of mortification ^sn4-1-3-1

by which sons of men purify themselves. ^sn4-1-3-2

You’re impure, but think yourself pure; ^sn4-1-3-3

you’ve strayed from the path of purity.” ^sn4-1-3-4

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn4-1-4-1

“I realized that it’s pointless; ^sn4-1-5-1

all that endless mortification ^sn4-1-5-2

is as futile ^sn4-1-5-3

as oars and rudder on dry land. ^sn4-1-5-4

Ethics, immersion, and wisdom: ^sn4-1-6-1

by developing this path to awakening ^sn4-1-6-2

I attained ultimate purity. ^sn4-1-6-3

You’re beaten, terminator!” ^sn4-1-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-1-7-1

### In the Form of an Elephant King (Linked Discourses 4.2) ^sn4-2-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-2-1-1

At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn4-2-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha was meditating in the open during the dark of night, while a gentle rain drizzled down. ^sn4-2-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the Buddha feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, manifested in the form of a huge elephant king and approached him. ^sn4-2-1-4

Its head was like a huge block of soapstone. ^sn4-2-1-5

Its tusks were like pure silver. ^sn4-2-1-7

Its trunk was like a long plough pole. ^sn4-2-1-9

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse: ^sn4-2-1-11

“Transmigrating for such a long time, ^sn4-2-2-1

you’ve made forms beautiful and ugly. ^sn4-2-2-2

Enough of this, Wicked One! ^sn4-2-2-3

You’re beaten, terminator!” ^sn4-2-2-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-2-3-1

### Beautiful (Linked Discourses 4.3) ^sn4-3-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-3-1-1

At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn4-3-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha was meditating in the open during the dark of night, while a gentle rain drizzled down. ^sn4-3-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the Buddha feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, approached him, and while not far away, displayed a rainbow of bright colors, both beautiful and ugly. ^sn4-3-1-4

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn4-3-1-5

“Transmigrating for such a long time, ^sn4-3-2-1

you’ve made forms beautiful and ugly. ^sn4-3-2-2

Enough of this, Wicked One! ^sn4-3-2-3

You’re beaten, terminator. ^sn4-3-2-4

Those who are well restrained ^sn4-3-3-1

in body, speech, and mind ^sn4-3-3-2

don’t fall under Māra’s sway, ^sn4-3-3-3

nor are they your footmen.” ^sn4-3-3-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-3-4-1

### Māra’s Snares (1st) (Linked Discourses 4.4) ^sn4-4-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-4-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn4-4-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn4-4-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn4-4-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn4-4-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn4-4-1-6

“Mendicants, I have attained and realized supreme freedom through rational application of mind and rational right effort. ^sn4-4-2-1

You too should attain and realize supreme freedom through rational application of mind and rational right effort.” ^sn4-4-2-2

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-4-2-3

“You’re bound by Māra’s snares, ^sn4-4-3-1

both human and heavenly. ^sn4-4-3-2

You’re bound by Māra’s bonds: ^sn4-4-3-3

you won’t escape me, ascetic!” ^sn4-4-3-4

“I’m freed from Māra’s snares, ^sn4-4-4-1

both human and heavenly. ^sn4-4-4-2

I’m freed from Māra’s bonds. ^sn4-4-4-3

You’re beaten, terminator!” ^sn4-4-4-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-4-5-1

### Māra’s Snares (2nd) (Linked Discourses 4.5) ^sn4-5-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn4-5-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn4-5-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn4-5-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn4-5-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn4-5-1-5

“Mendicants, I am freed from all snares, both human and heavenly. ^sn4-5-2-1

You are also freed from all snares, both human and heavenly. ^sn4-5-2-2

Wander forth, mendicants, for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^sn4-5-2-3

Let not two go by one road. ^sn4-5-2-4

Teach the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn4-5-2-5

There are beings with little dust in their eyes. They’re in decline because they haven’t heard the teaching. ^sn4-5-2-6

There will be those who understand the teaching! ^sn4-5-2-7

I will travel to the town of Senā in Uruvelā in order to teach the Dhamma.” ^sn4-5-2-8

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-5-2-9

“You’re bound by all snares, ^sn4-5-3-1

both human and heavenly. ^sn4-5-3-2

You’re bound by the great bond: ^sn4-5-3-3

you won’t escape me, ascetic!” ^sn4-5-3-4

“I’m freed from all snares, ^sn4-5-4-1

both human and heavenly. ^sn4-5-4-2

I’m freed from the great bonds; ^sn4-5-4-3

You’re beaten, terminator!” ^sn4-5-4-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-5-5-1

### A Serpent (Linked Discourses 4.6) ^sn4-6-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-6-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn4-6-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha was meditating in the open during the dark of night, while a gentle rain drizzled down. ^sn4-6-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the Buddha feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, manifested in the form of a huge serpent king and approached him. ^sn4-6-2-1

Its body was like a huge canoe carved from a single tree. ^sn4-6-2-2

Its hood was like a large brewer’s sieve. ^sn4-6-2-4

Its eyes were like big bronze cups from Kosala. ^sn4-6-2-6

Its tongue flickered from its mouth like lightning flashes in a thunderstorm. ^sn4-6-2-8

The sound of its breathing was like the puffing of a blacksmith’s bellows. ^sn4-6-2-10

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn4-6-3-1

“A self-controlled sage frequents ^sn4-6-4-1

empty buildings for lodging. ^sn4-6-4-2

It’s appropriate for that sort ^sn4-6-4-3

to live there after relinquishing. ^sn4-6-4-4

Though there are lots of creepy crawlies, ^sn4-6-5-1

and lots of flies and reptiles, ^sn4-6-5-2

they wouldn’t stir a hair ^sn4-6-5-3

of a great sage in that empty hut. ^sn4-6-5-4

Though the firmament may split <j>and the earth may quake, ^sn4-6-6-1

and all creatures be stricken with fear; ^sn4-6-6-2

and even if an arrow’s aimed at their breast, ^sn4-6-6-3

the Buddhas take no shelter in attachments.” ^sn4-6-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-6-7-1

### Sleeping (Linked Discourses 4.7) ^sn4-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn4-7-1-1

He spent much of the night walking mindfully in the open. At the crack of dawn he washed his feet and entered his dwelling. ^sn4-7-1-2

He laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^sn4-7-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-7-1-4

“What, you’re asleep? <j>Really, you’re asleep? ^sn4-7-2-1

You sleep like a loser—<j>what’s up with that? ^sn4-7-2-2

You sleep, thinking that the hut is empty. ^sn4-7-2-3

You sleep when the sun has come up—<j>what’s up with that?” ^sn4-7-2-4

“For them there is no craving— ^sn4-7-3-1

the weaver, the clinger—<j>to track them anywhere. ^sn4-7-3-2

With the ending of all attachments <j>the awakened Buddha sleeps. ^sn4-7-3-3

What’s that got to do with you, Māra?” ^sn4-7-3-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-7-4-1

### Delighting (Linked Discourses 4.8) ^sn4-8-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-8-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn4-8-1-2

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn4-8-1-3

“Children bring you delight! ^sn4-8-2-1

Cattle also bring you delight! ^sn4-8-2-2

For attachments are a man’s delight; ^sn4-8-2-3

without attachments there’s no delight.” ^sn4-8-2-4

“Your children bring you sorrow. ^sn4-8-3-1

Your cattle also bring you sorrow. ^sn4-8-3-2

For attachments are a man’s sorrow; ^sn4-8-3-3

without attachments there are no sorrows.” ^sn4-8-3-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-8-4-1

### Lifespan (1st) (Linked Discourses 4.9) ^sn4-9-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-9-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn4-9-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn4-9-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn4-9-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn4-9-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn4-9-1-6

“Mendicants, the lifespan of humans is short. ^sn4-9-2-1

You must go to the next life. So you should do what is skillful, you should practice the spiritual life. ^sn4-9-2-2

No-one born is immortal. ^sn4-9-2-3

A long life is a hundred years or a little more.” ^sn4-9-2-4

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-9-3-1

“The life of humans is long! ^sn4-9-4-1

A true person wouldn’t scorn it. ^sn4-9-4-2

Live like a suckling babe, ^sn4-9-4-3

for Death has not come for you.” ^sn4-9-4-4

“The life of humans is short, ^sn4-9-5-1

and a true person scorns it. ^sn4-9-5-2

They should live as though their head was on fire, ^sn4-9-5-3

for Death comes for everyone.” ^sn4-9-5-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-9-6-1

### Lifespan (2nd) (Linked Discourses 4.10) ^sn4-10-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-10-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn4-10-1-2

There the Buddha … said: ^sn4-10-1-3

“Mendicants, the lifespan of humans is short. ^sn4-10-2-1

You must go to the next life. So you should do what is skillful, you should practice the spiritual life. ^sn4-10-2-2

No-one born is immortal. ^sn4-10-2-3

A long life is a hundred years or a little more.” ^sn4-10-2-4

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-10-3-1

“The days and nights don’t rush by, ^sn4-10-4-1

and life isn’t cut short. ^sn4-10-4-2

The life of mortals keeps rolling on, ^sn4-10-4-3

like a chariot’s rim around the hub.” ^sn4-10-4-4

“The days and nights rush by, ^sn4-10-5-1

and then life is cut short. ^sn4-10-5-2

The life of mortals wastes away, ^sn4-10-5-3

like the water in tiny streams.” ^sn4-10-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-10-6-1

### Boulders (Linked Discourses 4.11) ^sn4-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn4-11-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was meditating in the open during the dark of night, while a gentle rain drizzled down. ^sn4-11-1-2

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the Buddha feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, approached him, and crushed some large boulders close by him. ^sn4-11-1-3

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse: ^sn4-11-2-1

“Even if you shake ^sn4-11-3-1

this entire Vulture’s Peak, ^sn4-11-3-2

the rightly released, ^sn4-11-3-3

the awakened, are unshaken.” ^sn4-11-3-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-11-4-1

### Lion (Linked Discourses 4.12) ^sn4-12-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn4-12-1-1

Now, at that time the Buddha was teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly. ^sn4-12-1-2

Then Māra thought, ^sn4-12-2-1

“The ascetic Gotama is teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly. ^sn4-12-2-2

Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?” ^sn4-12-2-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-12-2-4

“Why now do you roar like a lion? ^sn4-12-3-1

You’re so self-assured in the assembly! ^sn4-12-3-2

For there is someone who’ll wrestle with you, ^sn4-12-3-3

so why do you imagine you’re the victor?” ^sn4-12-3-4

“The great heroes they roar, ^sn4-12-4-1

self-assured in the assemblies. ^sn4-12-4-2

The Realized One, attained to power, ^sn4-12-4-3

has crossed over clinging to the world.” ^sn4-12-4-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-12-5-1

### A Splinter (Linked Discourses 4.13) ^sn4-13-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-13-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha in the Maddakucchi deer park. ^sn4-13-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha’s foot had been cut by a wooden splinter. ^sn4-13-1-3

The Buddha was stricken by harrowing pains; physical feelings that were painful, sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, and disagreeable. ^sn4-13-1-4

But he endured unbothered, with mindfulness and situational awareness. ^sn4-13-1-5

And then he spread out his outer robe folded in four and laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware. ^sn4-13-1-6

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-13-1-7

“Are you feeble that you lie down? <j>Or are you drunk on poetry? ^sn4-13-2-1

Are your goals not amply satisfied? ^sn4-13-2-2

Alone in a secluded lodging, ^sn4-13-2-3

why this sleeping, sleepyhead?” ^sn4-13-2-4

“I’m not feeble that I lie down, <j>nor am I drunk on poetry. ^sn4-13-3-1

Having reached the goal, I’m rid of sorrow. ^sn4-13-3-2

Alone in a secluded lodging, ^sn4-13-3-3

I lie down full of sympathy for all living creatures. ^sn4-13-3-4

Even those with a dart stuck in the breast, ^sn4-13-4-1

piercing the heart moment by moment, ^sn4-13-4-2

are able to get some sleep. ^sn4-13-4-3

So why not I, whose dart is drawn out? ^sn4-13-4-4

I don’t lie awake tense, nor do I fear to sleep. ^sn4-13-5-1

The days and nights don’t disturb me, ^sn4-13-5-2

as I see no decline for myself in the world. ^sn4-13-5-3

That’s why I lie down full of sympathy for all living creatures.” ^sn4-13-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-13-6-1

### Appropriate (Linked Discourses 4.14) ^sn4-14-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kosalans near the brahmin village of Ekasālā. ^sn4-14-1-1

Now, at that time the Buddha was teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople. ^sn4-14-1-2

Then Māra thought, ^sn4-14-2-1

“The ascetic Gotama is teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople. ^sn4-14-2-2

Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?” ^sn4-14-2-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-14-2-4

“It’s not appropriate for you ^sn4-14-3-1

to instruct others. ^sn4-14-3-2

As you engage in this, ^sn4-14-3-3

don’t get caught up in favoring and opposing.” ^sn4-14-3-4

“The Buddha instructs others ^sn4-14-4-1

out of compassion for their welfare. ^sn4-14-4-2

The Realized One is liberated ^sn4-14-4-3

from favoring and opposing.” ^sn4-14-4-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-14-5-1

### A Mental Snare (Linked Discourses 4.15) ^sn4-15-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-15-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn4-15-1-2

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-15-1-3

“There’s a mental snare ^sn4-15-2-1

wandering in midair. ^sn4-15-2-2

I’ll bind you with it— ^sn4-15-2-3

you won’t escape me, ascetic!” ^sn4-15-2-4

“Sights, sounds, tastes, smells, ^sn4-15-3-1

and touches so delightful: ^sn4-15-3-2

desire for these is gone from me. ^sn4-15-3-3

You’re beaten, terminator!” ^sn4-15-3-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-15-4-1

### The Alms Bowls (Linked Discourses 4.16) ^sn4-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn4-16-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the five grasping aggregates. ^sn4-16-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn4-16-1-3

Then Māra thought, ^sn4-16-2-1

“This ascetic Gotama is educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the five grasping aggregates. ^sn4-16-2-2

And the mendicants are paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn4-16-2-3

Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?” ^sn4-16-2-4

At that time several alms bowls were placed in the open air. ^sn4-16-3-1

Then Māra the Wicked manifested in the form of an ox and approached those bowls. ^sn4-16-3-2

One of the mendicants said to another, ^sn4-16-3-3

“Mendicant, mendicant, that ox will break the bowls.” ^sn4-16-3-4

When this was said, the Buddha said to that mendicant, ^sn4-16-3-5

“Mendicant, that’s no ox. ^sn4-16-3-6

That’s Māra the Wicked come to pull the wool over your eyes!” ^sn4-16-3-7

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse: ^sn4-16-3-8

“Form, what is felt, and perception, ^sn4-16-4-1

consciousness, and what is chosen: ^sn4-16-4-2

‘I am not this’ and ‘this is not mine’; ^sn4-16-4-3

that’s how they become free of desire for them. ^sn4-16-4-4

When you’re detached, secure, ^sn4-16-5-1

all fetters transcended, ^sn4-16-5-2

though Māra and his army chase everywhere ^sn4-16-5-3

they never find you.” ^sn4-16-5-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-16-6-1

### The Six Fields of Contact (Linked Discourses 4.17) ^sn4-17-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn4-17-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the six fields of contact. ^sn4-17-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn4-17-1-3

Then Māra thought, ^sn4-17-2-1

“This ascetic Gotama is educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the six fields of contact. ^sn4-17-2-2

And those mendicants are paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn4-17-2-3

Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?” ^sn4-17-2-4

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and made a terrifyingly loud noise close by him. It seemed as if the earth were shattering, so that one of the mendicants said to another, ^sn4-17-2-5

“Mendicant, mendicant, it seems like the earth is shattering!” ^sn4-17-2-6

When this was said, the Buddha said to that mendicant, ^sn4-17-2-7

“Mendicant, that’s not the earth shattering. ^sn4-17-2-8

That’s Māra the Wicked come to pull the wool over your eyes!” ^sn4-17-2-9

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse: ^sn4-17-2-10

“Sights, sounds, tastes, smells, ^sn4-17-3-1

touches, and ideas, the lot of them— ^sn4-17-3-2

this is the terrible bait ^sn4-17-3-3

that the world’s infatuated by. ^sn4-17-3-4

But a mindful disciple of the Buddha ^sn4-17-4-1

has transcended all that. ^sn4-17-4-2

Having slipped free of Māra’s dominion, ^sn4-17-4-3

they shine like the sun.” ^sn4-17-4-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-17-5-1

### Alms Food (Linked Discourses 4.18) ^sn4-18-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Magadhans near the brahmin village of Pañcasālā. ^sn4-18-1-1

Now at that time in Pañcasālā the young maidens were occupied with guests. ^sn4-18-1-2

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Pañcasālā for alms. ^sn4-18-1-3

Now at that time Māra had possessed the brahmins and householders of Pañcasālā, so that they thought, ^sn4-18-1-4

“Don’t let the ascetic Gotama get any alms!” ^sn4-18-1-5

Then the Buddha left the village with his bowl as clean-washed as it was when he entered for alms. ^sn4-18-2-1

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and said to him, ^sn4-18-2-2

“Well, ascetic, did you get any alms?” ^sn4-18-2-3

“Wicked One, did you make sure I didn’t get any alms?” ^sn4-18-2-4

“Well then, sir, let the Buddha enter Pañcasālā a second time for alms. ^sn4-18-2-5

I’ll make sure you get alms.” ^sn4-18-2-6

“Māra has produced wickedness ^sn4-18-3-1

in attacking the Realized One. ^sn4-18-3-2

Wicked One, do you imagine that ^sn4-18-3-3

your wickedness won’t bear fruit? ^sn4-18-3-4

Let us live so very happily, ^sn4-18-4-1

we who have nothing. ^sn4-18-4-2

We shall feed on rapture, ^sn4-18-4-3

like the gods of streaming radiance.” ^sn4-18-4-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-18-5-1

### A Farmer (Linked Discourses 4.19) ^sn4-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn4-19-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about extinguishment. ^sn4-19-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn4-19-1-3

Then Māra thought, ^sn4-19-2-1

“The ascetic Gotama is giving a Dhamma talk about extinguishment … and the mendicants are listening well. ^sn4-19-2-2

Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?” ^sn4-19-2-3

Then Māra the Wicked manifested in the form of a farmer carrying a large plough on his shoulder. He held a long goad, his hair was messy, he was clad in sunn hemp, and his feet were muddy. He went up to the Buddha and said to him, ^sn4-19-2-4

“So, ascetic, did you happen to see any oxen?” ^sn4-19-2-5

“But what have you to do with oxen, Wicked One?” ^sn4-19-2-6

“Mine alone, ascetic, is the eye, mine are sights, mine is the field of eye contact consciousness. ^sn4-19-2-7

Where can you escape me, ascetic? ^sn4-19-2-8

Mine alone is the ear … ^sn4-19-2-9

nose … ^sn4-19-2-10

tongue … ^sn4-19-2-11

body … ^sn4-19-2-12

mind, mine are ideas, mine is the field of mind contact consciousness. ^sn4-19-2-13

Where can you escape me, ascetic?” ^sn4-19-2-14

“Yours alone, Wicked One, is the eye, yours are sights, yours is the field of eye contact consciousness. ^sn4-19-3-1

Where there is no eye, no sights, no eye contact consciousness—you have no place there, Wicked One! ^sn4-19-3-2

Yours alone is the ear … ^sn4-19-3-3

nose … ^sn4-19-3-5

tongue … ^sn4-19-3-7

body … ^sn4-19-3-8

mind, yours are ideas, yours is the field of mind contact consciousness. ^sn4-19-3-9

Where there is no mind, no ideas, no mind contact consciousness—you have no place there, Wicked One!” ^sn4-19-3-10

“The things they call ‘mine’, ^sn4-19-4-1

and those who say ‘it’s mine’: ^sn4-19-4-2

if your mind remains there, ^sn4-19-4-3

you won’t escape me, ascetic!” ^sn4-19-4-4

“The things they speak of aren’t mine; ^sn4-19-5-1

I’m not someone who speaks like that. ^sn4-19-5-2

So know this, Wicked One: ^sn4-19-5-3

you won’t even see the path I take.” ^sn4-19-5-4

Then Māra … vanished right there. ^sn4-19-6-1

### Ruling (Linked Discourses 4.20) ^sn4-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kosalans, in a wilderness hut on the slopes of the Himalayas. ^sn4-20-1-1

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn4-20-1-2

“I wonder if it’s possible to rule legitimately, without killing or having someone kill for you; without conquering or having someone conquer for you; without sorrowing or causing sorrow?” ^sn4-20-1-3

And then Māra the Wicked, knowing the Buddha’s train of thought, went up to him and said, ^sn4-20-2-1

“Rule, Blessed One! Rule, Holy One! Rule legitimately, without killing or having someone kill for you; without conquering or having someone conquer for you; without sorrowing or causing sorrow!” ^sn4-20-2-2

“But what do you see, Wicked One, that you say this to me?” ^sn4-20-2-3

“The Blessed One, sir, has developed and cultivated the four bases for psychic power, made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them. ^sn4-20-2-5

If he wished, the Blessed One need only determine that the Himalaya, king of mountains, was gold, and it would turn into gold.” ^sn4-20-2-6

“Take a golden mountain, ^sn4-20-3-1

entirely of native gold, and double it— ^sn4-20-3-2

it’s still not enough for one! ^sn4-20-3-3

Knowing this, live a moral life. ^sn4-20-3-4

When a personage has seen where suffering comes from ^sn4-20-4-1

how could they incline towards sensual pleasures? ^sn4-20-4-2

Realizing that attachment is a chain in the world, ^sn4-20-4-3

a personage would train to remove it.” ^sn4-20-4-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-20-5-1

### Several (Linked Discourses 4.21) ^sn4-21-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans near Silāvatī. ^sn4-21-1-2

Now at that time several mendicants were meditating not far from the Buddha, diligent, keen, and resolute. ^sn4-21-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked manifested in the form of a brahmin with a large matted dreadlock, wearing an antelope hide. He was old, bent double, wheezing, and held a staff made of cluster fig tree wood. He went up to those mendicants and said, ^sn4-21-1-4

“You’ve gone forth while young, reverends.  With pristine black hair, you’re blessed with youth, in the prime of life, and you’ve never flirted with sensual pleasures. ^sn4-21-1-5

Enjoy human sensual pleasures. ^sn4-21-1-6

Don’t give up what is apparent in the present to chase after what takes effect over time.” ^sn4-21-1-7

“Brahmin, that’s not what we’re doing. ^sn4-21-1-8

We’re giving up what takes effect over time to chase after what is apparent in the present. ^sn4-21-1-9

For the Buddha says that sensual pleasures take effect over time; they give much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. ^sn4-21-1-10

But this teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^sn4-21-1-11

When they had spoken, Māra the Wicked shook his head, waggled his tongue, raised his eyebrows until his brow puckered in three furrows, and left leaning on his staff. ^sn4-21-1-12

Then those mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said, ^sn4-21-2-1

“Mendicants, that was no brahmin. ^sn4-21-3-1

That was Māra the Wicked who came to pull the wool over your eyes!” ^sn4-21-3-2

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha spoke this verse: ^sn4-21-3-3

“When a personage has seen where suffering comes from ^sn4-21-4-1

how could they incline towards sensual pleasures? ^sn4-21-4-2

Realizing that attachment is a chain in the world, ^sn4-21-4-3

a personage would train to remove it.” ^sn4-21-4-4

### With Samiddhi (Linked Discourses 4.22) ^sn4-22-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans near Silāvatī. ^sn4-22-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Samiddhi was meditating not far from the Buddha, diligent, keen, and resolute. ^sn4-22-1-2

Then as Venerable Samiddhi was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn4-22-1-3

“I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate, ^sn4-22-1-4

to have a teacher who is a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha! ^sn4-22-1-5

I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate, ^sn4-22-1-6

to have gone forth in a teaching and training so well explained! ^sn4-22-1-7

I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate, ^sn4-22-1-8

to have spiritual companions who are ethical and of good character.” ^sn4-22-1-9

And then Māra the Wicked, knowing Samiddhi’s train of thought, went up to him and made a terrifyingly loud noise close by him. It seemed as if the earth was shattering. ^sn4-22-1-10

Then Samiddhi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said, ^sn4-22-2-1

“Samiddhi, that’s not the earth shattering. ^sn4-22-3-1

That’s Māra the Wicked come to pull the wool over your eyes! ^sn4-22-3-2

Go back to that same place, Samiddhi, and meditate, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn4-22-3-3

“Yes, sir,” replied Samiddhi. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn4-22-3-4

And for a second time Samiddhi was meditating in that same place, diligent, ardent, and resolute. ^sn4-22-3-5

And for a second time he had the same thought … ^sn4-22-3-6

and Māra made an earth-shattering noise. ^sn4-22-3-7

Then Samiddhi addressed Māra the Wicked One in verse: ^sn4-22-3-9

“I went forth out of faith ^sn4-22-4-1

from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn4-22-4-2

My mindfulness and wisdom are mature, ^sn4-22-4-3

my mind is serene in immersion. ^sn4-22-4-4

Make whatever illusions you want, ^sn4-22-4-5

it won’t bother me.” ^sn4-22-4-6

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The mendicant Samiddhi knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn4-22-5-1

### With Godhika (Linked Discourses 4.23) ^sn4-23-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-23-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn4-23-1-2

Now at that time Venerable Godhika was staying on the slopes of Isigili at the Black Rock. ^sn4-23-1-3

Then Venerable Godhika, meditating diligent, keen, and resolute, experienced temporary freedom of heart. ^sn4-23-1-4

But then he fell away from that temporary freedom of heart. ^sn4-23-1-5

For a second … ^sn4-23-1-6

third … ^sn4-23-1-8

fourth … ^sn4-23-1-10

fifth … ^sn4-23-1-12

sixth time Godhika experienced temporary freedom of heart. ^sn4-23-1-14

But for a sixth time he fell away from it. ^sn4-23-1-15

For a seventh time Godhika, meditating diligent, keen, and resolute, experienced temporary freedom of heart. ^sn4-23-1-16

Then he thought, ^sn4-23-2-1

“I’ve fallen away from this temporary freedom of heart no less than six times. ^sn4-23-2-2

Why don’t I commit suicide?” ^sn4-23-2-3

And then Māra the Wicked, knowing Godhika’s train of thought, went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-23-2-4

“O great hero, O greatly wise! ^sn4-23-3-1

Shining with power and glory. ^sn4-23-3-2

You’ve gone beyond all threats and perils, ^sn4-23-3-3

I bow to your feet, O Clear-eyed One! ^sn4-23-3-4

Great hero, master of death, ^sn4-23-4-1

your disciple longs for death, ^sn4-23-4-2

he’s planning for it. ^sn4-23-4-3

Stop him, O light-bringer! ^sn4-23-4-4

For Blessed One, renowned among men, ^sn4-23-5-1

your disciple who loves your teaching, ^sn4-23-5-2

a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire— ^sn4-23-5-3

how can they kill themselves?” ^sn4-23-5-4

Now at that time Venerable Godhika had already committed suicide. ^sn4-23-6-1

Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse: ^sn4-23-6-2

“This is how the attentive act, ^sn4-23-7-1

for they don’t long for life. ^sn4-23-7-2

Having plucked out craving, root and all, ^sn4-23-7-3

Godhika is quenched.” ^sn4-23-7-4

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn4-23-8-1

“Come, mendicants, let’s go to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili where Godhika, who came from a good family, committed suicide.” ^sn4-23-8-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn4-23-8-3

Then the Buddha together with several mendicants went to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili. ^sn4-23-9-1

The Buddha saw Godhika off in the distance on his cot, lying with aggregates cast off. ^sn4-23-9-2

Now at that time a cloud of black smoke was moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between. ^sn4-23-9-3

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn4-23-10-1

“Mendicants, do you see that cloud of black smoke moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between?” ^sn4-23-10-2

“Yes, sir.” ^sn4-23-10-3

“That’s Māra the Wicked searching for Godhika’s consciousness, wondering: ^sn4-23-10-4

‘Where is Godhika’s consciousness established?’ ^sn4-23-10-5

But since his consciousness is not established, Godhika is quenched.” ^sn4-23-10-6

Then Māra, carrying his arched harp made from the pale timber of wood-apple, went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-23-10-7

“Above, below, all round, ^sn4-23-11-1

in the four quarters and in-between, ^sn4-23-11-2

I’ve been searching without success: ^sn4-23-11-3

where has that Godhika got to?” ^sn4-23-11-4

“He was an attentive and steadfast sage, ^sn4-23-12-1

a meditator who loved absorption. ^sn4-23-12-2

By day and by night he applied himself, ^sn4-23-12-3

without concern for his life. ^sn4-23-12-4

He defeated the army of death, ^sn4-23-13-1

and won’t return for any future life. ^sn4-23-13-2

Having plucked out craving, root and all ^sn4-23-13-3

Godhika is quenched.” ^sn4-23-13-4

So stricken with sorrow ^sn4-23-14-1

that his harp dropped from his armpit, ^sn4-23-14-2

that spirit, downcast, ^sn4-23-14-3

vanished right there. ^sn4-23-14-4

### Seven Years of Pursuit (Linked Discourses 4.24) ^sn4-24-0

So I have heard. ^sn4-24-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn4-24-1-2

Now at that time Māra the Wicked had been pursuing the Buddha for seven years hoping to find a vulnerability without success. ^sn4-24-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-24-1-4

“Are you swamped by sorrow <j>that you meditate in the forest? ^sn4-24-2-1

Have you lost a fortune, or do you long for one? ^sn4-24-2-2

Or perhaps you’ve committed <j>some crime in the village? ^sn4-24-2-3

Why don’t you get too close to people? ^sn4-24-2-4

And why does no-one get close to you?” ^sn4-24-2-5

“I’ve dug out the root of sorrow completely. ^sn4-24-3-1

I practice absorption free of guilt or sorrow. ^sn4-24-3-2

I’ve cut off all greed and muttered prayer for future lives. ^sn4-24-3-3

Undefiled, I practice absorption, <j>O kinsman of the negligent!” ^sn4-24-3-4

“The things they call ‘mine’, ^sn4-24-4-1

and those who say ‘it’s mine’: ^sn4-24-4-2

if your mind remains there, ^sn4-24-4-3

you won’t escape me, ascetic!” ^sn4-24-4-4

“The things they speak of aren’t mine; ^sn4-24-5-1

I’m not someone who speaks like that. ^sn4-24-5-2

So know this, Wicked One: ^sn4-24-5-3

you won’t even see the path I take.” ^sn4-24-5-4

“If you’ve discovered the path ^sn4-24-6-1

that’s safe, and leads to freedom from death, ^sn4-24-6-2

go and walk that path alone— ^sn4-24-6-3

why teach it to anyone else?” ^sn4-24-6-4

“Those crossing to the far shore ^sn4-24-7-1

ask what’s beyond the domain of Death. ^sn4-24-7-2

When I’m asked, I explain to them ^sn4-24-7-3

the truth without attachments.” ^sn4-24-7-4

“Sir, suppose there was a lotus pond not far from a town or village, ^sn4-24-8-1

and a crab lived there. ^sn4-24-8-2

Then several boys or girls would leave the town or village and go to the pond, where they’d pull out the crab and put it on solid ground. ^sn4-24-8-3

Whenever that crab extended a claw, those boys or girls would snap, crack, and break it off with a stick or a stone. ^sn4-24-8-4

And when that crab’s claws had all been snapped, cracked, and broken off it wouldn’t be able to return down into that lotus pond. ^sn4-24-8-5

In the same way, sir, the Buddha has snapped, cracked, and broken off all my twists, ducks, and dodges. ^sn4-24-8-6

Now I’m not able to approach the Buddha again in hopes of finding a vulnerability.” ^sn4-24-8-7

Then Māra the Wicked recited these verses of disappointment in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn4-24-8-8

“A crow once circled a stone ^sn4-24-9-1

that looked like a lump of fat. ^sn4-24-9-2

‘Perhaps I’ll find something tender,’ it thought, ^sn4-24-9-3

‘perhaps there’s something tasty.’ ^sn4-24-9-4

But finding nothing tasty, ^sn4-24-10-1

the crow left that place. ^sn4-24-10-2

Like the crow that pecked the stone, ^sn4-24-10-3

I leave Gotama disappointed.” ^sn4-24-10-4

### Māra’s Daughters (Linked Discourses 4.25) ^sn4-25-0

And then Māra the Wicked, after reciting these verses of disillusionment in the Buddha’s presence, left that place. He sat cross-legged on the ground not far from the Buddha, silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say, scratching the ground with a stick. ^sn4-25-1-1

Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to Māra the Wicked, and addressed him in verse: ^sn4-25-1-2

“Why so downhearted, dad? ^sn4-25-2-1

What man are you upset about? ^sn4-25-2-2

We’ll catch him with the snare of lust, ^sn4-25-2-3

like an elephant in the wild. ^sn4-25-2-4

We’ll tie him up and bring him back— ^sn4-25-2-5

he’ll fall under your sway!” ^sn4-25-2-6

“In this world he is the perfected one, the Holy One. ^sn4-25-3-1

He’s not easily seduced by lust. ^sn4-25-3-2

He has slipped free of Māra’s dominion; ^sn4-25-3-3

that’s why I’m so upset.” ^sn4-25-3-4

Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to the Buddha, and said to him, ^sn4-25-4-1

“We serve at your feet, ascetic.” ^sn4-25-4-2

But the Buddha ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^sn4-25-4-3

Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side to think up a plan. ^sn4-25-5-1

“Men have a diverse spectrum of tastes. ^sn4-25-5-2

Why don’t we each manifest in the form of a hundred young maidens?” ^sn4-25-5-3

So that’s what they did. Then they went up to the Buddha and said to him, ^sn4-25-5-4

“We serve at your feet, ascetic.” ^sn4-25-5-5

But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^sn4-25-5-6

Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side to think up a plan. ^sn4-25-6-1

“Men have a diverse spectrum of tastes. ^sn4-25-6-2

Why don’t we each manifest in the form of a hundred women who have never given birth?” ^sn4-25-6-3

So that’s what they did. Then they went up to the Buddha and said to him, ^sn4-25-6-4

“We serve at your feet, ascetic.” ^sn4-25-6-5

But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^sn4-25-6-6

Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust … ^sn4-25-7-1

each manifested in the form of a hundred women who have given birth once … ^sn4-25-7-4

women who have given birth twice … ^sn4-25-8-4

middle-aged women … ^sn4-25-8-9

old women … ^sn4-25-9-4

But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^sn4-25-9-5

Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side and said, ^sn4-25-9-6

“What our father said is true: ^sn4-25-9-7

‘In this world he is the perfected one, the Holy One. ^sn4-25-10-1

He’s not easily seduced by lust. ^sn4-25-10-2

He has slipped free of Māra’s dominion; ^sn4-25-10-3

that’s why I’m so upset.’ ^sn4-25-10-4

For if we had come on to any ascetic or brahmin like this who was not free of lust, his heart would explode, or he’d spew hot blood from his mouth, or he’d go mad or lose his mind. ^sn4-25-11-1

He’d dry up, wither away, and shrivel up like a green reed that was mowed down.” ^sn4-25-11-2

Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to the Buddha, and stood to one side. ^sn4-25-12-1

Māra’s daughter Craving addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn4-25-12-2

“Are you swamped by sorrow <j>that you meditate in the forest? ^sn4-25-13-1

Have you lost a fortune, or do you long for one? ^sn4-25-13-2

Or perhaps you’ve committed <j>some crime in the village? ^sn4-25-13-3

Why don’t you get too close to people? ^sn4-25-13-4

And why does no-one get close to you?” ^sn4-25-13-5

“I’ve reached the goal, peace of heart. ^sn4-25-14-1

Having conquered the army <j>of the likable and pleasant, ^sn4-25-14-2

alone, practicing absorption, I awakened to bliss. ^sn4-25-14-3

That’s why I don’t get too close to people, ^sn4-25-14-4

and no-one gets too close to me.” ^sn4-25-14-5

Then Māra’s daughter Malignancy addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn4-25-15-1

“How does a mendicant who has crossed five floods ^sn4-25-16-1

usually meditate here while crossing the sixth? ^sn4-25-16-2

How do they usually practice absorption <j>so that sensual perceptions ^sn4-25-16-3

are blocked out and don’t get hold of them?” ^sn4-25-16-4

“With tranquil body and mind well freed, ^sn4-25-17-1

without making choices, mindful, homeless; ^sn4-25-17-2

understanding the teaching, <j>they practice absorption without placing the mind; ^sn4-25-17-3

they’re not shaking or drifting or rigid. ^sn4-25-17-4

That’s how a mendicant who has crossed five floods ^sn4-25-18-1

usually meditates here while crossing the sixth. ^sn4-25-18-2

That’s how they usually practice absorption <j>so that sensual perceptions ^sn4-25-18-3

are blocked out and don’t get hold of them.” ^sn4-25-18-4

Then Māra’s daughter Lust addressed the Buddha in verse: ^sn4-25-19-1

“He lives with his community after cutting off craving, ^sn4-25-20-1

and many of the faithful will cross over for sure. ^sn4-25-20-2

Alas, this homeless one will snatch many men away, ^sn4-25-20-3

and lead them past the King of Death!” ^sn4-25-20-4

“The great heroes they lead ^sn4-25-21-1

by means of the true teaching. ^sn4-25-21-2

When the Realized Ones are leading by the teaching, ^sn4-25-21-3

how could anyone who knows be jealous?” ^sn4-25-21-4

Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to Māra the Wicked. ^sn4-25-22-1

Māra the Wicked saw them coming off in the distance, ^sn4-25-22-2

and addressed them in verse: ^sn4-25-22-3

“Fools! You drill into a mountain ^sn4-25-23-1

with lotus stalks! ^sn4-25-23-2

You dig up a hill with your nails! ^sn4-25-23-3

You chew iron with your teeth! ^sn4-25-23-4

You seek a footing in the abyss, as it were, ^sn4-25-24-1

while lifting a rock with your head! ^sn4-25-24-2

After attacking a stump with your breast, as it were, ^sn4-25-24-3

you leave Gotama disappointed.” ^sn4-25-24-4

They came dazzling— ^sn4-25-25-1

Craving, Malignancy, and Lust. ^sn4-25-25-2

But the Teacher brushed them off right there, ^sn4-25-25-3

like the gale, a fallen tuft. ^sn4-25-25-4

The Linked Discourses with Māra are complete. ^sn4-25-26-5

## Saṁyutta 5 ^sn5-0

### With Āḷavikā (Linked Discourses 5.1) ^sn5-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn5-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn5-1-1-2

Then the nun Āḷavikā robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn5-1-1-3

She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest seeking seclusion. ^sn5-1-1-4

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Āḷavikā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from seclusion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-1-1-5

“There’s no escape in the world, ^sn5-1-2-1

so what will seclusion do for you? ^sn5-1-2-2

Enjoy erotic delights; ^sn5-1-2-3

don’t regret it later.” ^sn5-1-2-4

Then the nun Āḷavikā thought, ^sn5-1-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-1-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-1-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from seclusion!” ^sn5-1-3-4

Then Āḷavikā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-1-3-5

“There is an escape in the world, ^sn5-1-4-1

and I’ve personally experienced it with wisdom. ^sn5-1-4-2

O Wicked One, kinsman of the negligent, ^sn5-1-4-3

you don’t know that state. ^sn5-1-4-4

Sensual pleasures are like swords and spears; ^sn5-1-5-1

the aggregates are their chopping block. ^sn5-1-5-2

What you call erotic delight ^sn5-1-5-3

is now no delight for me.” ^sn5-1-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Āḷavikā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-1-6-1

### With Somā (Linked Discourses 5.2) ^sn5-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-2-1-1

Then the nun Somā robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn5-2-1-2

She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation, ^sn5-2-1-3

plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a tree to meditate. ^sn5-2-1-4

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Somā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-2-1-5

“That state’s very challenging; ^sn5-2-2-1

it’s for the sages to attain. ^sn5-2-2-2

With her two-inch wisdom, ^sn5-2-2-3

it’s not possible for a woman.” ^sn5-2-2-4

Then the nun Somā thought, ^sn5-2-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-2-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-2-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-2-3-4

Then Somā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-2-3-5

“What difference does womanhood make ^sn5-2-4-1

when the mind is serene, ^sn5-2-4-2

and knowledge is present ^sn5-2-4-3

as you rightly discern the Dhamma. ^sn5-2-4-4

Surely someone who might think: ^sn5-2-5-1

‘I am woman’, or ‘I am man’, ^sn5-2-5-2

or ‘I am’ anything at all, ^sn5-2-5-3

is fit for Māra to address.” ^sn5-2-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Somā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-2-6-1

### With Kisāgotamī (Linked Discourses 5.3) ^sn5-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-3-1-1

Then the nun Kisāgotamī robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn5-3-1-2

She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation, ^sn5-3-1-3

plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a tree to meditate. ^sn5-3-1-4

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Kisāgotamī feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-3-1-5

“Why do you sit alone and cry ^sn5-3-2-1

as if your children have died? ^sn5-3-2-2

You’ve come to the woods all alone— ^sn5-3-2-3

are you looking for a man?” ^sn5-3-2-4

Then the nun Kisāgotamī thought, ^sn5-3-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-3-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-3-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-3-3-4

Then Kisāgotamī, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-3-4-1

“I’ve got over the death of children, ^sn5-3-5-1

and I’m finished with men. ^sn5-3-5-2

I don’t grieve or lament, ^sn5-3-5-3

and I’m not afraid of you, sir! ^sn5-3-5-4

Relishing is banished in every respect, ^sn5-3-6-1

and the mass of darkness is shattered. ^sn5-3-6-2

I’ve defeated the army of death, ^sn5-3-6-3

and live without defilements.” ^sn5-3-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Kisāgotamī knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-3-7-1

### With Vijayā (Linked Discourses 5.4) ^sn5-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-4-1-1

Then the nun Vijayā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-4-1-2

and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^sn5-4-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Vijayā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-4-1-4

“You’re so young and beautiful, ^sn5-4-2-1

and I’m a youth in my prime. ^sn5-4-2-2

Come, my lady, let us enjoy ^sn5-4-2-3

the music of a five-piece band.” ^sn5-4-2-4

Then the nun Vijayā thought, ^sn5-4-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-4-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-4-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-4-3-4

Then Vijayā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-4-3-5

“Sights, sounds, tastes, smells, ^sn5-4-4-1

and touches so delightful. ^sn5-4-4-2

I hand them right back to you, Māra, ^sn5-4-4-3

for I have no use for them. ^sn5-4-4-4

This body is rotting, ^sn5-4-5-1

decaying and frail. ^sn5-4-5-2

I’m horrified and repelled by it, ^sn5-4-5-3

and I’ve eradicated sensual craving. ^sn5-4-5-4

There are beings in the realm of luminous form, ^sn5-4-6-1

and others stuck in the formless. ^sn5-4-6-2

I’ve destroyed the darkness ^sn5-4-6-3

regarding all such peaceful attainments.” ^sn5-4-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Vijayā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-4-7-1

### With Uppalavaṇṇā (Linked Discourses 5.5) ^sn5-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-5-1-1

Then the nun Uppalavaṇṇā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-5-1-2

and stood at the root of a sal tree in full flower. ^sn5-5-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Uppalavaṇṇā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-5-1-4

“You’ve come to this sal tree all crowned with flowers, ^sn5-5-2-1

and stand at its root all alone, O nun. ^sn5-5-2-2

Your beauty is second to none— ^sn5-5-2-3

silly girl, aren’t you afraid of rascals?” ^sn5-5-2-4

Then the nun Uppalavaṇṇā thought, ^sn5-5-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-5-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-5-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-5-3-4

Then Uppalavaṇṇā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-5-3-5

“Even if 100,000 rascals like you ^sn5-5-4-1

were to come here, ^sn5-5-4-2

I’d stir not a hair nor panic. ^sn5-5-4-3

I’m not scared of you, Māra, even alone. ^sn5-5-4-4

I’ll vanish, ^sn5-5-5-1

or I’ll enter your belly; ^sn5-5-5-2

I could stand between your eyebrows ^sn5-5-5-3

and you still wouldn’t see me. ^sn5-5-5-4

I’m the master of my own mind, ^sn5-5-6-1

I’ve developed the bases of psychic power well. ^sn5-5-6-2

I’m free from all bonds, ^sn5-5-6-3

and I’m not afraid of you, sir!” ^sn5-5-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Uppalavaṇṇā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-5-7-1

### With Cālā (Linked Discourses 5.6) ^sn5-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-6-1-1

Then the nun Cālā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-6-1-2

and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^sn5-6-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to Cālā and said to her, ^sn5-6-1-4

“Nun, what don’t you approve of?” ^sn5-6-1-5

“I don’t approve of rebirth, sir.” ^sn5-6-1-6

“Why don’t you approve of rebirth? ^sn5-6-2-1

When you’re born, you get to enjoy sensual pleasures. ^sn5-6-2-2

Who put this idea in your head: ^sn5-6-2-3

‘Nun, don’t approve of rebirth’?” ^sn5-6-2-4

“Death comes to those who are born, ^sn5-6-3-1

when you’re born you undergo sufferings— ^sn5-6-3-2

killing, caging, misery— ^sn5-6-3-3

that’s why you shouldn’t approve of rebirth. ^sn5-6-3-4

The Buddha taught me the Dhamma ^sn5-6-4-1

for transcending rebirth, ^sn5-6-4-2

for giving up all suffering; ^sn5-6-4-3

he settled me in the truth. ^sn5-6-4-4

There are beings in the realm of luminous form, ^sn5-6-5-1

and others stuck in the formless. ^sn5-6-5-2

Not understanding cessation, ^sn5-6-5-3

they return in future lives.” ^sn5-6-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Cālā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-6-6-1

### With Upacālā (Linked Discourses 5.7) ^sn5-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-7-1-1

Then the nun Upacālā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-7-1-2

and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^sn5-7-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to Upacālā and said to her, ^sn5-7-1-4

“Nun, where do you want to be reborn?” ^sn5-7-1-5

“I don’t want to be reborn anywhere, sir.” ^sn5-7-1-6

“There are the gods of the thirty-three, <j>and those of Yama; ^sn5-7-2-1

also the Joyful Deities, ^sn5-7-2-2

the gods who love to create, ^sn5-7-2-3

and the gods who control that imagined by others. ^sn5-7-2-4

Set your heart on such places, ^sn5-7-2-5

and you’ll undergo delight.” ^sn5-7-2-6

“The gods of the thirty-three, <j>and those of Yama; ^sn5-7-3-1

also the Joyful Deities, ^sn5-7-3-2

the gods who love to create, ^sn5-7-3-3

and the gods who control that imagined by others— ^sn5-7-3-4

they’re bound with the bonds of sensuality; ^sn5-7-3-5

they fall under your sway again. ^sn5-7-3-6

All the world is on fire, ^sn5-7-4-1

all the world is smoldering, ^sn5-7-4-2

all the world is ablaze, ^sn5-7-4-3

all the world is rocking. ^sn5-7-4-4

My mind adores that place ^sn5-7-5-1

where Māra cannot go; ^sn5-7-5-2

it’s not shaking or burning, ^sn5-7-5-3

and not frequented by ordinary people.” ^sn5-7-5-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Upacālā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-7-6-1

### With Sīsupacālā (Linked Discourses 5.8) ^sn5-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-8-1-1

Then the nun Sīsupacālā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-8-1-2

and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^sn5-8-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked went up to Sīsupacālā and said to her, ^sn5-8-1-4

“Nun, whose creed do you approve of?” ^sn5-8-1-5

“I don’t approve anyone’s creed, sir.” ^sn5-8-1-6

“In whose name did you shave your head? ^sn5-8-2-1

You look like an ascetic, ^sn5-8-2-2

but you don’t approve any creed. ^sn5-8-2-3

Why do you wander as if lost?” ^sn5-8-2-4

“Followers of other creeds ^sn5-8-3-1

are confident in their views. ^sn5-8-3-2

But I don’t approve their teaching, ^sn5-8-3-3

for they’re no experts in the Dhamma. ^sn5-8-3-4

But there is one born in the Sakyan clan, ^sn5-8-4-1

the unrivaled Buddha, ^sn5-8-4-2

vanquisher of all, dispeller of Māra, ^sn5-8-4-3

everywhere undefeated, ^sn5-8-4-4

everywhere freed, and unattached, ^sn5-8-5-1

the Clear-eyed One sees all. ^sn5-8-5-2

He has attained the end of all deeds, ^sn5-8-5-3

freed with the ending of attachments. ^sn5-8-5-4

That Blessed One is my Teacher, ^sn5-8-5-5

and I approve his instruction.” ^sn5-8-5-6

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Sīsupacālā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-8-6-1

### With Selā (Linked Discourses 5.9) ^sn5-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-9-1-1

Then the nun Selā robed up in the morning … ^sn5-9-1-2

and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^sn5-9-1-3

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Selā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps … ^sn5-9-1-4

addressed her in verse: ^sn5-9-1-5

“Who created this puppet? ^sn5-9-2-1

Where is its maker? ^sn5-9-2-2

Where has the puppet arisen? ^sn5-9-2-3

And where does it cease?” ^sn5-9-2-4

Then the nun Selā thought, ^sn5-9-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-9-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-9-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-9-3-4

Then Selā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-9-3-5

“This puppet isn’t self-made, ^sn5-9-4-1

nor is this gloom made by another. ^sn5-9-4-2

It comes to be because of a cause, ^sn5-9-4-3

and ceases when the cause breaks up. ^sn5-9-4-4

It’s like a seed that’s sown ^sn5-9-5-1

in a field; it grows ^sn5-9-5-2

relying on both the soil’s nutrients ^sn5-9-5-3

as well as moisture. ^sn5-9-5-4

In the same way the aggregates and elements ^sn5-9-6-1

and these six sense fields ^sn5-9-6-2

come to be because of a cause, ^sn5-9-6-3

and cease when the cause breaks up.” ^sn5-9-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Selā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-9-7-1

### With Vajirā (Linked Discourses 5.10) ^sn5-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn5-10-1-1

Then the nun Vajirā robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn5-10-1-2

She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation, ^sn5-10-1-3

plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a tree to meditate. ^sn5-10-1-4

Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Vajirā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse: ^sn5-10-1-5

“Who created this sentient being? ^sn5-10-2-1

Where is its maker? ^sn5-10-2-2

Where has the being arisen? ^sn5-10-2-3

And where does it cease?” ^sn5-10-2-4

Then the nun Vajirā thought, ^sn5-10-3-1

“Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?” ^sn5-10-3-2

Then she thought, ^sn5-10-3-3

“This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!” ^sn5-10-3-4

Then Vajirā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse: ^sn5-10-3-5

“Why do you believe there’s such a thing as a <j>‘sentient being’? ^sn5-10-4-1

Māra, is this your theory? ^sn5-10-4-2

This is just a pile of conditions, ^sn5-10-4-3

you won’t find a sentient being here. ^sn5-10-4-4

When the parts are assembled ^sn5-10-5-1

we use the word ‘chariot’. ^sn5-10-5-2

So too, when the aggregates are present ^sn5-10-5-3

‘sentient being’ is the convention we use. ^sn5-10-5-4

But it’s only suffering that comes to be, ^sn5-10-6-1

lasts a while, then expires. ^sn5-10-6-2

Naught but suffering comes to be, ^sn5-10-6-3

naught but suffering ceases.” ^sn5-10-6-4

Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Vajirā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there. ^sn5-10-7-1

The Linked Discourses on Nuns are complete. ^sn5-10-8-6

## Saṁyutta 6 ^sn6-0

### The Appeal of the Divinity (Linked Discourses 6.1) ^sn6-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn6-1-1-1

At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn6-1-1-2

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn6-1-1-3

“This principle I have discovered is deep, hard to see, hard to understand, peaceful, sublime, beyond the scope of logic, subtle, comprehensible to the astute. ^sn6-1-1-4

But people like clinging, they love it and enjoy it. ^sn6-1-1-5

It’s hard for them to see this topic; that is, specific conditionality, dependent origination. ^sn6-1-1-6

It’s also hard for them to see this topic; that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^sn6-1-1-7

And if I were to teach this principle, others might not understand me, which would be wearying and troublesome for me.” ^sn6-1-1-8

And then these verses, which were neither supernaturally inspired, nor learned before in the past, occurred to the Buddha: ^sn6-1-1-9

“I’ve struggled hard to realize this, ^sn6-1-2-1

enough with trying to explain it! ^sn6-1-2-2

Those mired in greed and hate ^sn6-1-2-3

can’t really understand this teaching. ^sn6-1-2-4

It goes against the stream, subtle, ^sn6-1-3-1

deep, obscure, and very fine. ^sn6-1-3-2

Those besotted by greed cannot see, ^sn6-1-3-3

for they’re cloaked in a mass of darkness.” ^sn6-1-3-4

And as the Buddha reflected like this, his mind inclined to remaining passive, not to teaching the Dhamma. ^sn6-1-4-1

Then, knowing the Buddha’s train of thought, it occurred to the divinity Sahampati, ^sn6-1-5-1

“Oh lord! The world will be lost, the world will perish! For the mind of the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, inclines to remaining passive, not to teaching the Dhamma.” ^sn6-1-5-2

Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of the Buddha. ^sn6-1-5-3

He arranged his robe over one shoulder, knelt with his right knee on the ground, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said: ^sn6-1-5-4

“Sir, let the Blessed One teach the Dhamma! Let the Holy One teach the Dhamma! ^sn6-1-5-5

There are beings with little dust in their eyes. They’re in decline because they haven’t heard the teaching. ^sn6-1-5-6

There will be those who understand the teaching!” ^sn6-1-5-7

This is what the divinity Sahampati said. Then he went on to say: ^sn6-1-5-8

“Among the Magadhans there appeared in the past ^sn6-1-6-1

an impure teaching thought up by the stained. ^sn6-1-6-2

Fling open the door to freedom from death! ^sn6-1-6-3

Let them hear the teaching <j>the immaculate one discovered. ^sn6-1-6-4

Standing high on a rocky mountain, ^sn6-1-7-1

you can see the people all around. ^sn6-1-7-2

In just the same way, All-seer, so intelligent, ^sn6-1-7-3

having ascended the Temple of Truth, ^sn6-1-7-4

rid of sorrow, look upon the people ^sn6-1-7-5

swamped with sorrow,  <j>oppressed by rebirth and old age. ^sn6-1-7-6

Rise, hero! Victor in battle, caravan leader, ^sn6-1-8-1

wander the world cleared of debt. ^sn6-1-8-2

Let the Blessed One teach the Dhamma! ^sn6-1-8-3

There will be those who understand!” ^sn6-1-8-4

Then the Buddha, understanding the Divinity’s invitation, surveyed the world with the eye of a Buddha, out of his compassion for sentient beings. ^sn6-1-9-1

And the Buddha saw sentient beings with little dust in their eyes, and some with much dust in their eyes; with keen faculties and with weak faculties, with good qualities and with bad qualities, easy to teach and hard to teach. And some of them lived seeing the danger in the fault to do with the next world, while others did not. ^sn6-1-9-2

It’s like a pool with blue water lilies, or pink or white lotuses. Some of them sprout and grow in the water without rising above it, thriving underwater. Some of them sprout and grow in the water reaching the water’s surface. And some of them sprout and grow in the water but rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to them. ^sn6-1-9-3

In the same way, the Buddha saw sentient beings with little dust in their eyes, and some with much dust in their eyes; with keen faculties and with weak faculties, with good qualities and with bad qualities, easy to teach and hard to teach. And some of them lived seeing the danger in the fault to do with the next world, while others did not. ^sn6-1-9-4

When he had seen this he replied in verse to the divinity Sahampati: ^sn6-1-9-5

“Flung open are the doors to freedom from death! ^sn6-1-10-1

Let those with ears to hear commit to faith. ^sn6-1-10-2

Thinking it would be troublesome, Divinity, <j>I did not teach ^sn6-1-10-3

the sophisticated, sublime Dhamma among mankind.” ^sn6-1-10-4

Then the divinity Sahampati, knowing that his request for the Buddha to teach the Dhamma had been granted, bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^sn6-1-11-1

### Respect (Linked Discourses 6.2) ^sn6-2-0

So I have heard. ^sn6-2-1-1

At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^sn6-2-1-2

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn6-2-1-3

“One without respect and reverence lives in suffering. What ascetic or brahmin should I honor and respect and rely on?” ^sn6-2-1-4

Then it occurred to him: ^sn6-2-2-1

“I’d honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of ethics, if it were incomplete. ^sn6-2-2-2

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who is more accomplished than myself in ethics, who I should honor and respect and rely on. ^sn6-2-2-3

I’d honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of immersion, if it were incomplete. ^sn6-2-3-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin … who is more accomplished than myself in immersion … ^sn6-2-3-2

I’d honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of wisdom, if it were incomplete. ^sn6-2-4-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin … who is more accomplished than myself in wisdom … ^sn6-2-4-2

I’d honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of freedom, if it were incomplete. ^sn6-2-5-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin … who is more accomplished than myself in freedom … ^sn6-2-5-2

I’d honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of the knowledge and vision of freedom, if it were incomplete. ^sn6-2-6-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who is more accomplished than myself in the knowledge and vision of freedom, who I should honor and respect and rely on. ^sn6-2-6-2

Why don’t I honor and respect and rely on the same Dhamma to which I was awakened?” ^sn6-2-6-3

Then the divinity Sahampati knew the Buddha’s train of thought. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of the Buddha. ^sn6-2-7-1

He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said: ^sn6-2-7-2

“That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One! ^sn6-2-7-3

All the perfected ones, the fully awakened Buddhas who lived in the past honored and respected and relied on this same teaching. ^sn6-2-7-4

All the perfected ones, the fully awakened Buddhas who will live in the future will honor and respect and rely on this same teaching. ^sn6-2-7-5

May the Blessed One, who is the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha at present, also honor and respect and rely on this same teaching.” ^sn6-2-7-6

This is what the divinity Sahampati said. Then he went on to say: ^sn6-2-7-7

“All Buddhas in the past, ^sn6-2-8-1

the Buddhas of the future, ^sn6-2-8-2

and the Buddha at present— ^sn6-2-8-3

destroyer of the sorrows of many— ^sn6-2-8-4

respecting the true teaching ^sn6-2-9-1

they did live, they do live, ^sn6-2-9-2

and they also will live. ^sn6-2-9-3

This is the nature of the Buddhas. ^sn6-2-9-4

Therefore someone who desires self-knowledge, ^sn6-2-10-1

and aspires to greatness themselves, ^sn6-2-10-2

should respect the true teaching, ^sn6-2-10-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^sn6-2-10-4

### With Brahmadeva (Linked Discourses 6.3) ^sn6-3-0

So I have heard. ^sn6-3-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn6-3-1-2

Now at that time a certain brahmin lady had a son called Brahmadeva, who had gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in the presence of the Buddha. ^sn6-3-1-3
