Then he thought, ^sn8-1-1-6

“It’s my loss, my misfortune, ^sn8-1-1-7

that I’ve become dissatisfied, with lust infecting my mind. How is it possible for someone else to dispel my discontent and give rise to satisfaction? ^sn8-1-1-8

Why don’t I do it myself?” ^sn8-1-1-9

Then, on the occasion of dispelling his own discontent and giving rise to satisfaction, he recited these verses: ^sn8-1-1-10

“Now that I’ve renounced ^sn8-1-2-1

the home life for homelessness ^sn8-1-2-2

I’m overrun ^sn8-1-2-3

by the rude thoughts of the Dark One. ^sn8-1-2-4

Even if a thousand warrior chiefs and great archers, ^sn8-1-3-1

well trained, with strong bows, ^sn8-1-3-2

were to completely surround me; ^sn8-1-3-3

I would never flee. ^sn8-1-3-4

And even if women come, ^sn8-1-4-1

many more than that, ^sn8-1-4-2

they won’t bother me, ^sn8-1-4-3

for I stand firm in the teaching. ^sn8-1-4-4

I heard with my own ears ^sn8-1-5-1

from the Buddha, Kinsman of the Sun, ^sn8-1-5-2

about the path going to extinguishment; ^sn8-1-5-3

that’s what delights my mind. ^sn8-1-5-4

Wicked One, if you come near me ^sn8-1-6-1

as I meditate like this, ^sn8-1-6-2

I’ll make sure that you, Death, ^sn8-1-6-3

won’t even see the path I take.” ^sn8-1-6-4

### Dissatisfaction (Linked Discourses 8.2) ^sn8-2-0

At one time Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine, together with his mentor, Venerable Nigrodhakappa. ^sn8-2-1-1

Now at that time after Venerable Nigrodhakappa had finished his meal, on his return from almsround, he would enter his dwelling and not emerge for the rest of that day, or the next. ^sn8-2-1-2

And at that time Venerable Vaṅgīsa became dissatisfied, as lust infected his mind. ^sn8-2-1-3

Then he thought, ^sn8-2-1-4

“It’s my loss, my misfortune, ^sn8-2-1-5

that I’ve become dissatisfied, with lust infecting my mind. How is it possible for someone else to dispel my discontent and give rise to satisfaction? ^sn8-2-1-6

Why don’t I do it myself?” ^sn8-2-1-7

Then, on the occasion of dispelling his own discontent and giving rise to satisfaction, he recited these verses: ^sn8-2-1-8

“Giving up desire and discontent, ^sn8-2-2-1

along with all thoughts of domestic life, ^sn8-2-2-2

they wouldn’t get snarled in anything; ^sn8-2-2-3

unsnarled, undesiring: that’s a real mendicant. ^sn8-2-2-4

Whatever here is included in form— ^sn8-2-3-1

on earth, in the sky, or in planet’s deep— ^sn8-2-3-2

wears out, it is all impermanent: ^sn8-2-3-3

the self-examined live having comprehended this. ^sn8-2-3-4

People are bound to their attachments, ^sn8-2-4-1

to impingement of the seen and heard, <j>and to what is thought. ^sn8-2-4-2

Unstirred, dispel desire for these things; ^sn8-2-4-3

for one called ‘a sage’ does not cling to them. ^sn8-2-4-4

Trapped in the sixty wrong views, <j>full of their own opinions, ^sn8-2-5-1

ordinary people are fixed in wrong principles. ^sn8-2-5-2

But that mendicant wouldn’t join a sect, ^sn8-2-5-3

still less would they utter lewd speech. ^sn8-2-5-4

Clever, serene for a long time, ^sn8-2-6-1

free of deceit, alert, without envy, ^sn8-2-6-2

the sage has reached the state of peace; ^sn8-2-6-3

quenched, he awaits his time.” ^sn8-2-6-4

### Good-Hearted (Linked Discourses 8.3) ^sn8-3-0

At one time Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine, together with his mentor, Venerable Nigrodhakappa. ^sn8-3-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Vaṅgīsa looked down upon other good-hearted mendicants because of his own eloquence. ^sn8-3-1-2

Then he thought, ^sn8-3-1-3

“It’s my loss, my misfortune, ^sn8-3-1-4

that I look down on other good-hearted mendicants because of my own eloquence.” ^sn8-3-1-5

Then, on the occasion of arousing remorse in himself, he recited these verses: ^sn8-3-1-6

“Give up conceit, Gotama! ^sn8-3-2-1

Completely abandon the different kinds of conceit! ^sn8-3-2-2

Besotted with the different kinds of conceit, ^sn8-3-2-3

you’ve had regrets for a long time. ^sn8-3-2-4

Smeared by smears and slain by conceit, ^sn8-3-3-1

people fall into hell. ^sn8-3-3-2

When people slain by conceit are reborn in hell, ^sn8-3-3-3

they grieve for a long time. ^sn8-3-3-4

But a mendicant who practices rightly, ^sn8-3-4-1

a path-victor, never grieves. ^sn8-3-4-2

They enjoy happiness and a good reputation, ^sn8-3-4-3

and they rightly call him a ‘Seer of Truth’. ^sn8-3-4-4

So being not hard-hearted, but energetic, ^sn8-3-5-1

with hindrances given up, pure; ^sn8-3-5-2

and having completely given up conceit, ^sn8-3-5-3

the one who makes an end with knowledge is assuaged.” ^sn8-3-5-4

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 8.4) ^sn8-4-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn8-4-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms with Venerable Vaṅgīsa as his second monk. ^sn8-4-1-2

And at that time Venerable Vaṅgīsa became dissatisfied, as lust infected his mind. ^sn8-4-1-3

Then he addressed Ānanda in verse: ^sn8-4-1-4

“I’ve got a burning desire for pleasure; ^sn8-4-2-1

My mind is on fire! ^sn8-4-2-2

Please, out of compassion, Gotama, ^sn8-4-2-3

tell me how to quench the flames.” ^sn8-4-2-4

“Your mind is on fire ^sn8-4-3-1

because of a perversion of perception. ^sn8-4-3-2

Turn away from the feature of things ^sn8-4-3-3

that’s attractive, provoking lust. ^sn8-4-3-4

See all conditioned phenomena as alien, ^sn8-4-4-1

as suffering and not-self. ^sn8-4-4-2

Extinguish the great fire of lust, ^sn8-4-4-3

don’t burn up again and again. ^sn8-4-4-4

With mind unified and serene, ^sn8-4-5-1

meditate on the ugly aspects of the body. ^sn8-4-5-2

With mindfulness immersed in the body, ^sn8-4-5-3

be full of disillusionment. ^sn8-4-5-4

Meditate on the signless, ^sn8-4-6-1

give up the underlying tendency to conceit; ^sn8-4-6-2

and when you comprehend conceit, ^sn8-4-6-3

you will live at peace.” ^sn8-4-6-4

### Well-Spoken Words (Linked Discourses 8.5) ^sn8-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn8-5-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn8-5-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn8-5-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn8-5-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn8-5-1-5

“Mendicants, speech that has four factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people. ^sn8-5-2-1

What four? ^sn8-5-2-2

It’s when a mendicant speaks well, not poorly; their speech is principled, not unprincipled; they speak pleasantly, not unpleasantly; and they speak truthfully, not falsely. ^sn8-5-2-3

Speech with these four factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people.” ^sn8-5-2-4

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn8-5-2-5

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn8-5-2-6

“Good people say well-spoken words are foremost; ^sn8-5-3-1

second, speech ought be principled, not unprincipled; ^sn8-5-3-2

third, speak pleasantly, not unpleasantly; ^sn8-5-3-3

and fourth, speak truthfully, not falsely.” ^sn8-5-3-4

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-5-4-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-5-4-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-5-4-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-5-4-4

“Speak only such words ^sn8-5-5-1

as do not hurt yourself ^sn8-5-5-2

nor harm others; ^sn8-5-5-3

such speech is truly well spoken. ^sn8-5-5-4

Speak only pleasing words, ^sn8-5-6-1

words gladly welcomed. ^sn8-5-6-2

Pleasing words are those ^sn8-5-6-3

that bring nothing bad to others. ^sn8-5-6-4

Truth itself is undying speech: ^sn8-5-7-1

this is an ancient teaching. ^sn8-5-7-2

Good people say the teaching and meaning ^sn8-5-7-3

are grounded in truth. ^sn8-5-7-4

The words spoken by the Buddha ^sn8-5-8-1

for finding the sanctuary, extinguishment, ^sn8-5-8-2

for making an end of suffering: ^sn8-5-8-3

this really is the best kind of speech.” ^sn8-5-8-4

### With Sāriputta (Linked Discourses 8.6) ^sn8-6-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn8-6-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Sāriputta was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk. His words were polished, clear, articulate, and expressed the meaning. ^sn8-6-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn8-6-1-3

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, ^sn8-6-1-4

“This Venerable Sāriputta is educating the mendicants. … ^sn8-6-1-5

And those mendicants are paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn8-6-1-6

Why don’t I extoll him in his presence with fitting verses?” ^sn8-6-1-7

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward Sāriputta, and said, ^sn8-6-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Reverend Sāriputta! I feel inspired to speak, Reverend Sāriputta!” ^sn8-6-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said Sāriputta. ^sn8-6-2-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled Sāriputta in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-6-2-4

“Deep in wisdom, intelligent, ^sn8-6-3-1

expert in the path and what is not the path; ^sn8-6-3-2

Sāriputta, so greatly wise, ^sn8-6-3-3

teaches Dhamma to the mendicants. ^sn8-6-3-4

He teaches in brief, ^sn8-6-4-1

or he speaks at length. ^sn8-6-4-2

His call, like a myna bird, ^sn8-6-4-3

overflows with eloquence. ^sn8-6-4-4

While he teaches ^sn8-6-5-1

the mendicants listen to his sweet voice, ^sn8-6-5-2

sounding attractive, ^sn8-6-5-3

clear and graceful. ^sn8-6-5-4

They listen joyfully, ^sn8-6-5-5

their hearts elated.” ^sn8-6-5-6

### The Invitation to Admonish (Linked Discourses 8.7) ^sn8-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery, together with a large Saṅgha of around five hundred monks, all of whom were perfected ones. ^sn8-7-1-1

Now, at that time it was the sabbath—the full moon on the fifteenth day—and the Buddha was sitting in the open surrounded by the Saṅgha of monks for the invitation to admonish. ^sn8-7-1-2

Then the Buddha looked around the Saṅgha of monks, who were silent. He addressed them: ^sn8-7-1-3

“Come now, monks, I invite you all: ^sn8-7-1-4

Is there anything I’ve done by way of body or speech that you would criticize?” ^sn8-7-1-5

When he had spoken, Venerable Sāriputta rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said: ^sn8-7-2-1

“There is nothing, sir, that you’ve done by way of body or speech that we would criticize. ^sn8-7-2-2

For the Blessed One gave rise to the unarisen path, gave birth to the unborn path, and explained the unexplained path. He is the knower of the path, the discoverer of the path, the expert on the path. ^sn8-7-2-3

And now the disciples live following the path; they acquire it later. ^sn8-7-2-4

And sir, I invite the Blessed One. ^sn8-7-2-5

Is there anything I’ve done by way of body or speech that you would criticize?” ^sn8-7-2-6

“There is nothing, Sāriputta, that you’ve done by way of body or speech that I would criticize. ^sn8-7-3-1

Sāriputta, you are astute. You have great wisdom, widespread wisdom, laughing wisdom, swift wisdom, sharp wisdom, penetrating wisdom. ^sn8-7-3-2

A wheel-turning monarch’s oldest son rightly keeps wielding the power set in motion by his father. ^sn8-7-3-3

In the same way, Sāriputta rightly keeps rolling the supreme Wheel of Dhamma that was rolled forth by me.” ^sn8-7-3-4

“Since it seems I have done nothing worthy of the Blessed One’s criticism, ^sn8-7-4-1

is there anything these five hundred monks have done by way of body or speech that you would criticize?” ^sn8-7-4-2

“There is nothing, Sāriputta, that these five hundred monks have done by way of body or speech that I would criticize. ^sn8-7-4-3

For of these five hundred monks, sixty have the three knowledges, sixty have the six direct knowledges, sixty are freed both ways, and the rest are freed by wisdom.” ^sn8-7-4-4

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-7-5-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-7-5-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-7-5-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-7-5-4

“Today, on the fifteenth day sabbath, ^sn8-7-6-1

five hundred monks have gathered together <j>to purify their precepts. ^sn8-7-6-2

These untroubled sages <j>have cut off their fetters and bonds, ^sn8-7-6-3

they will not be reborn again. ^sn8-7-6-4

Just as a wheel-turning monarch ^sn8-7-7-1

surrounded by ministers ^sn8-7-7-2

travels all around this ^sn8-7-7-3

land girt by sea. ^sn8-7-7-4

So disciples with the three knowledges, ^sn8-7-8-1

conquerors of death, ^sn8-7-8-2

revere the winner of the battle, ^sn8-7-8-3

the unsurpassed caravan leader. ^sn8-7-8-4

All are sons of the Blessed One— ^sn8-7-9-1

there is no rubbish here. ^sn8-7-9-2

I bow to the Kinsman of the Sun, ^sn8-7-9-3

destroyer of the dart of craving.” ^sn8-7-9-4

### Over a Thousand (Linked Discourses 8.8) ^sn8-8-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery, together with a large Saṅgha of 1,250 mendicants. ^sn8-8-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about extinguishment. ^sn8-8-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn8-8-1-3

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, ^sn8-8-1-4

“The Buddha is educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about extinguishment. ^sn8-8-1-5

And those mendicants are paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn8-8-1-6

Why don’t I extoll him in his presence with fitting verses?” ^sn8-8-1-7

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-8-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-8-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-8-2-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-8-2-4

“Over a thousand mendicants ^sn8-8-3-1

revere the Holy One ^sn8-8-3-2

as he teaches the immaculate Dhamma, ^sn8-8-3-3

extinguishment, fearing nothing from any quarter. ^sn8-8-3-4

They listen to the immaculate Dhamma ^sn8-8-4-1

taught by the fully awakened Buddha; ^sn8-8-4-2

the Buddha is so brilliant, ^sn8-8-4-3

at the fore of the mendicant Saṅgha, ^sn8-8-4-4

Blessed One, your name is ‘Giant’, ^sn8-8-5-1

seventh of the seers. ^sn8-8-5-2

You are like a great cloud ^sn8-8-5-3

that rains on your disciples. ^sn8-8-5-4

I’ve left my day’s meditation, ^sn8-8-6-1

out of desire to see the teacher. ^sn8-8-6-2

Great hero, your disciple Vaṅgīsa ^sn8-8-6-3

bows at your feet.” ^sn8-8-6-4

“Vaṅgīsa, had you previously composed these verses, or did they spring to mind in the moment?” ^sn8-8-7-1

“They sprang to mind in the moment, sir.” ^sn8-8-7-2

“Well then, Vaṅgīsa, speak some more spontaneously inspired verses.” ^sn8-8-7-3

“Yes, sir,” replied Vaṅgīsa. Then he extolled the Buddha with some more spontaneously inspired verses, not previously composed: ^sn8-8-7-4

“Having overcome Māra’s wrong path, ^sn8-8-8-1

you wander with hard-heartedness dissolved. ^sn8-8-8-2

See him, the liberator from bonds, ^sn8-8-8-3

dissecting the unattached section by section. ^sn8-8-8-4

You have explained in many ways ^sn8-8-9-1

the path to cross the flood. ^sn8-8-9-2

The Seers of Truth stand unfaltering ^sn8-8-9-3

in the freedom from death explained by you. ^sn8-8-9-4

As the bringer of light who has pierced the truth, ^sn8-8-10-1

you’ve seen what lies beyond all planes. ^sn8-8-10-2

When you saw and realized this for yourself, ^sn8-8-10-3

you taught it first to the group of five. ^sn8-8-10-4

When the Dhamma has been so well taught, ^sn8-8-11-1

how could those who know it be negligent? ^sn8-8-11-2

That’s why, being diligent, we should always train ^sn8-8-11-3

respectfully in the Buddha’s teaching.” ^sn8-8-11-4

### With Koṇḍañña (Linked Discourses 8.9) ^sn8-9-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn8-9-1-1

Then Venerable Koṇḍañña Who Understood approached the Buddha after a very long absence. He bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet, caressing them and covering them with kisses, and pronounced his name: ^sn8-9-1-2

“I am Koṇḍañña, Blessed One! I am Koṇḍañña, Holy One!” ^sn8-9-1-3

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, ^sn8-9-1-4

“This Venerable Koṇḍañña Who Understood has approached the Buddha after a very long absence. He bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet, caressing them and covering them with kisses, and pronounced his name: ^sn8-9-1-5

‘I am Koṇḍañña, Blessed One! I am Koṇḍañña, Holy One!’ ^sn8-9-1-6

Why don’t I extoll him in the Buddha’s presence with fitting verses?” ^sn8-9-1-7

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-9-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-9-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-9-2-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled Koṇḍañña in the Buddha’s presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-9-2-4

“The senior monk awakened after the Buddha, ^sn8-9-3-1

Koṇḍañña, is keenly energetic. ^sn8-9-3-2

He regularly gains ^sn8-9-3-3

the blissful meditations of seclusion. ^sn8-9-3-4

Whatever can be attained by a disciple ^sn8-9-4-1

who does the Teacher’s bidding, ^sn8-9-4-2

he has attained it all, ^sn8-9-4-3

through diligently training himself. ^sn8-9-4-4

With great power and the three knowledges, ^sn8-9-5-1

expert in encompassing the minds of others, ^sn8-9-5-2

Koṇḍañña, the heir to the Buddha, ^sn8-9-5-3

bows at the Teacher’s feet.” ^sn8-9-5-4

### With Moggallāna (Linked Discourses 8.10) ^sn8-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying on the slopes of Isigili at the Black Rock, together with a large Saṅgha of five hundred mendicants, all of whom were perfected ones. ^sn8-10-1-1

Thereupon, with his mind, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna checked to see whose mind was liberated and free of attachments. ^sn8-10-1-2

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, ^sn8-10-1-3

“The Buddha is staying on the slopes of Isigili … with five hundred perfected ones. ^sn8-10-1-4

Mahāmoggallāna is checking to see whose mind is liberated and free of attachments. ^sn8-10-1-5

Why don’t I extoll him in the Buddha’s presence with fitting verses?” ^sn8-10-1-6

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-10-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-10-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-10-2-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled Mahāmoggallāna in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-10-2-4

“As the sage, who has gone beyond suffering, ^sn8-10-3-1

sits upon the mountain slope, ^sn8-10-3-2

he is revered by disciples with the three knowledges, ^sn8-10-3-3

conquerors of death. ^sn8-10-3-4

Moggallāna, of great psychic power, ^sn8-10-4-1

encompasses with his mind, ^sn8-10-4-2

scrutinizing their minds, ^sn8-10-4-3

liberated, free of attachments. ^sn8-10-4-4

That is how those endowed <j>with many fine qualities ^sn8-10-5-1

revere Gotama, ^sn8-10-5-2

the sage gone beyond suffering, ^sn8-10-5-3

perfect in every respect.” ^sn8-10-5-4

### At Gaggarā (Linked Discourses 8.11) ^sn8-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond, together with a large Saṅgha of five hundred mendicants, seven hundred male and seven hundred female lay followers, and many thousands of deities. ^sn8-11-1-1

But the Buddha outshone them all in beauty and glory. ^sn8-11-1-2

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa thought, ^sn8-11-1-3

“The Buddha is staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond, together with a large Saṅgha of five hundred mendicants, seven hundred male and seven hundred female lay followers, and many thousands of deities. ^sn8-11-1-4

And he outshines them all in beauty and glory. ^sn8-11-1-5

Why don’t I extoll him in his presence with fitting verses?” ^sn8-11-1-6

Then Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^sn8-11-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^sn8-11-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^sn8-11-2-3

Then Vaṅgīsa extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses: ^sn8-11-2-4

“Like the moon on a cloudless night, ^sn8-11-3-1

like the shining immaculate sun, ^sn8-11-3-2

so too Aṅgīrasa, O great sage, ^sn8-11-3-3

your glory outshines the entire world.” ^sn8-11-3-4

### With Vaṅgīsa (Linked Discourses 8.12) ^sn8-12-0

At one time Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn8-12-1-1

Now at that time Vaṅgīsa had recently attained perfection. While experiencing the bliss of freedom, on that occasion he recited these verses: ^sn8-12-1-2

“We used to wander, drunk on poetry, ^sn8-12-2-1

village to village, town to town. ^sn8-12-2-2

Then we saw the Buddha, ^sn8-12-2-3

and faith arose in us. ^sn8-12-2-4

He taught me Dhamma: ^sn8-12-3-1

the aggregates, sense fields, and elements. ^sn8-12-3-2

When I heard his teaching ^sn8-12-3-3

I went forth to homelessness. ^sn8-12-3-4

It was truly for the benefit of many ^sn8-12-4-1

that the sage achieved awakening— ^sn8-12-4-2

for the monks and for the nuns ^sn8-12-4-3

who see that they’ve reached certainty. ^sn8-12-4-4

It was so welcome for me ^sn8-12-5-1

to be in the presence of the Buddha. ^sn8-12-5-2

I’ve attained the three knowledges, ^sn8-12-5-3

and fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions. ^sn8-12-5-4

I know my past lives, ^sn8-12-6-1

my clairvoyance is purified, ^sn8-12-6-2

I am master of three knowledges, <j>attained in psychic power, ^sn8-12-6-3

expert in encompassing the minds of others.” ^sn8-12-6-4

The Linked Discourses with Vaṅgīsa are complete. ^sn8-12-7-8

## Saṁyutta 9 ^sn9-0

### Seclusion (Linked Discourses 9.1) ^sn9-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn9-1-1-1

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-1-1-2

Now at that time that mendicant, during their day’s meditation, was thinking bad, unskillful thoughts to do with the domestic life. ^sn9-1-1-3

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that mendicant, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached that mendicant wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-1-1-4

“You entered the woods desiring seclusion, ^sn9-1-2-1

yet your mind strays to outward things. ^sn9-1-2-2

As a person, dispel the desire for people; ^sn9-1-2-3

then you’ll be happy, free of greed. ^sn9-1-2-4

Give up discontent; be mindful; ^sn9-1-3-1

let us remind you to be mindful. ^sn9-1-3-2

The dusty abyss is so hard to cross; ^sn9-1-3-3

don’t let sensual dust drag you down. ^sn9-1-3-4

Just as a bird strewn with dirt ^sn9-1-4-1

sheds that clingy dust with a shake; ^sn9-1-4-2

so too, an energetic, mindful mendicant ^sn9-1-4-3

sheds that clingy dust with a shake.” ^sn9-1-4-4

Impelled by that deity, that mendicant was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-1-5-1

### Application (Linked Discourses 9.2) ^sn9-2-0

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-2-1-1

Now at that time that mendicant went for the day’s meditation, but fell asleep. ^sn9-2-1-2

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that mendicant, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached that mendicant wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-2-1-3

“Get up, mendicant! Why lie down? ^sn9-2-2-1

What’s the point in sleeping? ^sn9-2-2-2

How can the afflicted slumber, ^sn9-2-2-3

inflicted by an arrow strike? ^sn9-2-2-4

You should amplify the faith ^sn9-2-3-1

that led you to go forth ^sn9-2-3-2

from the home life to homelessness. ^sn9-2-3-3

Don’t fall under the sway of slumber.” ^sn9-2-3-4

“Sensual pleasures are impermanent and unstable, ^sn9-2-4-1

but dullards still fall for them. ^sn9-2-4-2

Among those who are bound, <j>they’re free and unattached: ^sn9-2-4-3

why bother a renunciate? ^sn9-2-4-4

By removing desire and greed, ^sn9-2-5-1

by going beyond ignorance, ^sn9-2-5-2

that knowledge has been perfectly cleansed: ^sn9-2-5-3

why bother a renunciate? ^sn9-2-5-4

By breaking ignorance with knowledge, ^sn9-2-6-1

by the ending of defilements, ^sn9-2-6-2

they’re sorrowless, unstressed: ^sn9-2-6-3

why bother a renunciate? ^sn9-2-6-4

Energetic, resolute, ^sn9-2-7-1

always staunchly vigorous, ^sn9-2-7-2

aspiring to extinguishment: ^sn9-2-7-3

why bother a renunciate?” ^sn9-2-7-4

### With Kassapagotta (Linked Discourses 9.3) ^sn9-3-0

At one time Venerable Kassapagotta was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-3-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Kassapagotta, having withdrawn for his day’s meditation, tried to advise a nimrod. ^sn9-3-1-2

Then the deity haunting that forest approached Kassapagotta wanting to stir him up, and recited these verses: ^sn9-3-1-3

“A nimrod wandering the rugged hills ^sn9-3-2-1

is unintelligent, unthinking. ^sn9-3-2-2

It’s a waste of time to advise him, mendicant; ^sn9-3-2-3

he strikes me as an idiot. ^sn9-3-2-4

He listens without understanding, ^sn9-3-3-1

looks without seeing. ^sn9-3-3-2

Though the teaching is spoken, ^sn9-3-3-3

the fool doesn’t get it. ^sn9-3-3-4

Even if you lit ten lamps ^sn9-3-4-1

and brought them to him, Kassapa, ^sn9-3-4-2

he wouldn’t see anything, ^sn9-3-4-3

for he has no eyes to see.” ^sn9-3-4-4

Impelled by that deity, Venerable Kassapagotta was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-3-5-1

### Several Mendicants Set Out Wandering (Linked Discourses 9.4) ^sn9-4-0

At one time several mendicants were staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-4-1-1

Then after completing the three months of the rainy season residence, those mendicants set out wandering. ^sn9-4-1-2

Not seeing those mendicants, the deity haunting that forest cried. And on that occasion they spoke this verse: ^sn9-4-1-3

“Dissatisfaction, <j>it seems to me today, ^sn9-4-2-1

seeing so many vacated seats. ^sn9-4-2-2

They were so learned, such brilliant speakers! ^sn9-4-2-3

Where have these disciples of Gotama gone?” ^sn9-4-2-4

When they had spoken, another deity replied with this verse: ^sn9-4-3-1

“They’ve gone to Magadha, they’ve gone to Kosala, ^sn9-4-4-1

and some are in the Vajjian lands. ^sn9-4-4-2

Like deer that wander free of ties, ^sn9-4-4-3

the mendicants live with no abode.” ^sn9-4-4-4

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 9.5) ^sn9-5-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-5-1-1

Now at that time Ānanda was spending too much time persuading lay people. ^sn9-5-1-2

Then the deity haunting that forest had sympathy for Ānanda, wanting what’s best for him. So they approached him wanting to stir him up, and recited these verses: ^sn9-5-1-3

“You’ve left for the jungle, the root of a tree, ^sn9-5-2-1

having laid extinguishment in your heart. ^sn9-5-2-2

Meditate, Gotama, don’t be negligent! ^sn9-5-2-3

What use is this hullabaloo to you?” ^sn9-5-2-4

Impelled by that deity, Venerable Ānanda was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-5-3-1

### With Anuruddha (Linked Discourses 9.6) ^sn9-6-0

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-6-1-1

Then a certain deity of the company of the thirty-three named Penelope had been Anuruddha’s partner in a former life. She went up to Anuruddha, and recited these verses: ^sn9-6-1-2

“Set your heart there, ^sn9-6-2-1

where you used to live: ^sn9-6-2-2

among the gods of the thirty-three, ^sn9-6-2-3

whose every desire is granted! ^sn9-6-2-4

At the fore of a retinue ^sn9-6-2-5

of divine maidens, you’ll shine!” ^sn9-6-2-6

“Divine maidens are in a sorry state, ^sn9-6-3-1

stuck in substantial reality. ^sn9-6-3-2

And those beings too are in a sorry state, ^sn9-6-3-3

who are attached to divine maidens.” ^sn9-6-3-4

“They don’t know pleasure ^sn9-6-4-1

who don’t see the Garden of Delight! ^sn9-6-4-2

It’s the abode of lordly gods, ^sn9-6-4-3

the glorious host of Thirty!” ^sn9-6-4-4

“Fool, don’t you understand ^sn9-6-5-1

the saying of the perfected ones: ^sn9-6-5-2

all conditions are impermanent, ^sn9-6-5-3

their nature is to rise and fall; ^sn9-6-5-4

having arisen, they cease; ^sn9-6-5-5

their settling is such bliss. ^sn9-6-5-6

O Penelope, weaver of the web— ^sn9-6-6-1

there are no more abodes for me in the host of gods. ^sn9-6-6-2

Transmigration through births is finished, ^sn9-6-6-3

now there’ll be no more future lives.” ^sn9-6-6-4

### With Nāgadatta (Linked Discourses 9.7) ^sn9-7-0

At one time Venerable Nāgadatta was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-7-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Nāgadatta had been entering the village too early and returning late in the day. ^sn9-7-1-2

Then the deity haunting that forest had sympathy for Nāgadatta, wanting what’s best for him. So they approached him wanting to stir him up, and recited these verses: ^sn9-7-1-3

“Entering the village too early, ^sn9-7-2-1

and returning too late in the day, ^sn9-7-2-2

Nāgadatta mixes with lay people, ^sn9-7-2-3

sharing their joys and sorrows. ^sn9-7-2-4

I’m afraid for Nāgadatta; he’s reckless, ^sn9-7-3-1

shackled to families. ^sn9-7-3-2

May he not come under the King of Death’s power, ^sn9-7-3-3

under the sway of the terminator!” ^sn9-7-3-4

Impelled by that deity, Venerable Nāgadatta was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-7-4-1

### The Mistress of the House (Linked Discourses 9.8) ^sn9-8-0

At one time one of the monks was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-8-1-1

Now at that time that monk had become too closely involved in the affairs of a certain family. ^sn9-8-1-2

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that monk, wanting what’s best for him. So, wanting to stir him up, they manifested in the appearance of the mistress of that family, approached the monk, and addressed him in verse: ^sn9-8-1-3

“At river banks and guest houses, ^sn9-8-2-1

hotels and highways, ^sn9-8-2-2

people gather to gossip about ^sn9-8-2-3

what’s going on between you and me.” ^sn9-8-2-4

“There are many sounds echoing ^sn9-8-3-1

that a fervent ascetic must endure. ^sn9-8-3-2

They ought not be dismayed by that, ^sn9-8-3-3

for that’s not what defiles you. ^sn9-8-3-4

If you’re startled by every little sound, ^sn9-8-4-1

like a wind-deer in the wood, ^sn9-8-4-2

they’ll call you ‘flighty minded’; ^sn9-8-4-3

and your practice won’t succeed.” ^sn9-8-4-4

### A Vajji (Linked Discourses 9.9) ^sn9-9-0

At one time a certain Vajjian mendicant was staying near Vesālī in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-9-1-1

Now at that time the Vajjis were holding an all-night event in Vesālī. ^sn9-9-1-2

Then that mendicant, complaining at the noise of musical instruments being beaten and played, on that occasion spoke this verse: ^sn9-9-1-3

“We dwell alone in the wilderness, ^sn9-9-2-1

like a log dumped in the forest. ^sn9-9-2-2

On a night like this, ^sn9-9-2-3

who’s worse off than me?” ^sn9-9-2-4

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that mendicant, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached that mendicant wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-9-3-1

“You dwell alone in the wilderness, ^sn9-9-4-1

like a log dumped in the forest. ^sn9-9-4-2

Lots of people are jealous of you, ^sn9-9-4-3

like beings in hell of those going to heaven.” ^sn9-9-4-4

Impelled by that deity, that mendicant was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-9-5-1

### Recitation (Linked Discourses 9.10) ^sn9-10-0

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-10-1-1

Now at that time that mendicant had previously been spending too much time rehearsing. But some time later they adhered to passivity and silence. ^sn9-10-1-2

Not hearing the teaching, the deity haunting that forest approached that mendicant, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-10-1-3

“Mendicant, why don’t you recite <j>passages of the teaching, ^sn9-10-2-1

living together with other mendicants? ^sn9-10-2-2

Hearing the teaching, one garners confidence; ^sn9-10-2-3

and in this very life, one garners praise.” ^sn9-10-2-4

“I used to be zealous for <j>passages of the teaching, ^sn9-10-3-1

until I encountered dispassion. ^sn9-10-3-2

When I encountered dispassion, ^sn9-10-3-3

whatever was seen, heard, and thought, ^sn9-10-3-4

this, good people say, <j>is the laying down by complete understanding.” ^sn9-10-3-5

### Unskillful Thoughts (Linked Discourses 9.11) ^sn9-11-0

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-11-1-1

Now at that time that mendicant, during their day’s meditation, was thinking bad, unskillful thoughts, that is, ^sn9-11-1-2

sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^sn9-11-1-3

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that mendicant, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached that mendicant wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-11-1-4

“Because of irrational application of mind, ^sn9-11-2-1

you’re consumed by your thoughts. ^sn9-11-2-2

When you’ve given up irrationality, ^sn9-11-2-3

make sure your thoughts are rational. ^sn9-11-2-4

Thinking about the Teacher, the teaching, ^sn9-11-3-1

the Saṅgha, and your own ethics, ^sn9-11-3-2

you’ll find gladness, ^sn9-11-3-3

and rapture and bliss as well, no doubt. ^sn9-11-3-4

And when you’re full of joy, ^sn9-11-3-5

you’ll make an end to suffering.” ^sn9-11-3-6

Impelled by that deity, that mendicant was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-11-4-1

### Midday (Linked Discourses 9.12) ^sn9-12-0

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-12-1-1

The deity haunting that forest approached that mendicant and spoke this verse in their presence: ^sn9-12-1-2

“In the still of high noon, ^sn9-12-2-1

when the birds have settled down, ^sn9-12-2-2

the mighty forest itself resounds— ^sn9-12-2-3

that strikes me as so scary!” ^sn9-12-2-4

“In the still of high noon, ^sn9-12-3-1

when the birds have settled down, ^sn9-12-3-2

the mighty forest itself resounds— ^sn9-12-3-3

that strikes me as so delightful!” ^sn9-12-3-4

### Undisciplined Faculties (Linked Discourses 9.13) ^sn9-13-0

At one time several mendicants were staying in the Kosalan lands in a certain forest grove. They were restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties. ^sn9-13-1-1

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for those mendicants, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached those mendicants wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-13-1-2

“The mendicants used to live happily, ^sn9-13-2-1

as disciples of Gotama. ^sn9-13-2-2

Desireless they sought alms; ^sn9-13-2-3

desireless they used their lodgings. ^sn9-13-2-4

Knowing that the world was impermanent ^sn9-13-2-5

they made an end of suffering. ^sn9-13-2-6

But now they’ve made themselves hard to look after, ^sn9-13-3-1

like chiefs in a village. ^sn9-13-3-2

They eat and eat and then lie down, ^sn9-13-3-3

unconscious in the homes of others. ^sn9-13-3-4

Having raised my cupped palms to the Saṅgha, ^sn9-13-4-1

I speak here only about certain people. ^sn9-13-4-2

They’re dregs, with no protector, ^sn9-13-4-3

just like those who have passed away. ^sn9-13-4-4

I’m speaking about ^sn9-13-5-1

those who live negligently. ^sn9-13-5-2

To those who live diligently ^sn9-13-5-3

I pay homage.” ^sn9-13-5-4

Impelled by that deity, those mendicants were struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-13-6-1

### The Thief of Scent (Linked Discourses 9.14) ^sn9-14-0

At one time one of the mendicants was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove. ^sn9-14-1-1

Now at that time, after the meal, on their return from almsround, that mendicant plunged into a lotus pond and sniffed a pink lotus. ^sn9-14-1-2

The deity haunting that forest had sympathy for that mendicant, and wanted what’s best for them. So they approached that mendicant wanting to stir them up, and addressed them in verse: ^sn9-14-1-3

“This water flower has not been given. ^sn9-14-2-1

When you sniff it, ^sn9-14-2-2

this is one factor of theft. ^sn9-14-2-3

Good fellow, you are a thief of scent!” ^sn9-14-2-4

“I do not take, nor do I break; ^sn9-14-3-1

I sniff the water flower from afar. ^sn9-14-3-2

So how do you explain why ^sn9-14-3-3

you call me a thief of scent? ^sn9-14-3-4

Why don’t you accuse someone ^sn9-14-4-1

who does such vandalizing ^sn9-14-4-2

as digging up the roots, ^sn9-14-4-3

or breaking off the flowers?” ^sn9-14-4-4

“I have nothing to say ^sn9-14-5-1

to a person who is a crude vandal, ^sn9-14-5-2

soiled like a used nappy. ^sn9-14-5-3

You’re the one who deserves to be spoken to. ^sn9-14-5-4

To the man who has not a blemish ^sn9-14-6-1

who is always seeking purity, ^sn9-14-6-2

even a hair-tip of evil ^sn9-14-6-3

seems as big as a stormcloud.” ^sn9-14-6-4

“Indeed, O spirit, you understand me, ^sn9-14-7-1

and you empathize with me. ^sn9-14-7-2

Please speak to me again, ^sn9-14-7-3

whenever you see something like this.” ^sn9-14-7-4

“We are no dependent of yours, ^sn9-14-8-1

nor are we your servant. ^sn9-14-8-2

You yourself should know, mendicant, ^sn9-14-8-3

the way that leads to a good place.” ^sn9-14-8-4

Impelled by that deity, that mendicant was struck with a sense of urgency. ^sn9-14-9-1

The Linked Discourses in the Forest are complete. ^sn9-14-11-5

## Saṁyutta 10 ^sn10-0

### With Indaka (Linked Discourses 10.1) ^sn10-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn10-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha on Mount Indra’s Peak, the haunt of the native spirit Indaka. ^sn10-1-1-2

Then the native spirit Indaka went up to the Buddha, and addressed him in verse: ^sn10-1-1-3

“The Buddhas say that form is not the soul. ^sn10-1-2-1

Then how does this one obtain this body? ^sn10-1-2-2

From where do his bones and organs come? ^sn10-1-2-3

How does this one coalesce in the womb?” ^sn10-1-2-4

“First there’s the zygote; ^sn10-1-3-1

then there’s the blastocyst; ^sn10-1-3-2

from there arises the gastrula; ^sn10-1-3-3

which produces the embryonic mass. ^sn10-1-3-4

From that the limb buds appear, ^sn10-1-3-5

the head hair, body hair, and nails. ^sn10-1-3-6

And whatever the mother eats— ^sn10-1-4-1

the food and drink that she consumes— ^sn10-1-4-2

nourishes them there, ^sn10-1-4-3

the person in the mother’s womb.” ^sn10-1-4-4

### Sakka In Name Only (Linked Discourses 10.2) ^sn10-2-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn10-2-1-1

Then a spirit who was Sakka in name only went up to the Buddha, and addressed him in verse: ^sn10-2-1-2

“You’ve given up all ties, ^sn10-2-2-1

and are fully freed. ^sn10-2-2-2

It’s not a good idea for you, ascetic, ^sn10-2-2-3

to be instructing others.” ^sn10-2-2-4

“No matter how you explain why ^sn10-2-3-1

people gather together, Sakka, ^sn10-2-3-2

it’s unworthy for a wise person ^sn10-2-3-3

not to think of the other with sympathy. ^sn10-2-3-4

If you instruct others ^sn10-2-4-1

with a mind clear and confident, ^sn10-2-4-2

your compassion and empathy ^sn10-2-4-3

don’t create attachments.” ^sn10-2-4-4

### With Spiky (Linked Discourses 10.3) ^sn10-3-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Gayā on the cut-stone ledge in the haunt of Spiky the native spirit. ^sn10-3-1-1

Now at that time the native spirits Shaggy and Spiky were passing by not far from the Buddha. ^sn10-3-1-2

So Shaggy said to Spiky, ^sn10-3-1-3

“That’s an ascetic.” ^sn10-3-1-4

“That’s no ascetic, he’s a faker! ^sn10-3-1-5

I’ll soon find out whether he’s an ascetic or a faker.” ^sn10-3-1-6

Then Spiky went up to the Buddha and leaned up against his body, ^sn10-3-2-1

but the Buddha pulled away. ^sn10-3-2-2

Then Spiky said to the Buddha, ^sn10-3-2-3

“Are you afraid, ascetic?” ^sn10-3-2-4

“No, respectable sir, I’m not afraid. ^sn10-3-2-5

But your touch is nasty.” ^sn10-3-2-6

“I will ask you a question, ascetic. ^sn10-3-2-7

If you don’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane, or explode your heart, or grab you by the feet and throw you to the far shore of the Ganges!” ^sn10-3-2-8

“I don’t see anyone in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans who could do that to me. ^sn10-3-2-9

But anyway, ask what you wish.” ^sn10-3-2-10

“Where do greed and hate come from? ^sn10-3-3-1

From where spring discontent, desire, and terror? ^sn10-3-3-2

Where do the mind’s thoughts originate, ^sn10-3-3-3

like a crow let loose by boys.” ^sn10-3-3-4

“Greed and hate come from here; ^sn10-3-4-1

from here spring discontent, desire, and terror; ^sn10-3-4-2

here’s where the mind’s thoughts originate, ^sn10-3-4-3

like a crow let loose by boys. ^sn10-3-4-4

Born of affection, originating in oneself, ^sn10-3-5-1

like prop roots sprouting from a banyan; ^sn10-3-5-2

the many kinds of attachment to sensual pleasures ^sn10-3-5-3

are like camel’s foot creeper <j>creeping through the woods. ^sn10-3-5-4

Those who understand where they come from ^sn10-3-6-1

get rid of them—listen up, spirit! ^sn10-3-6-2

They cross this flood so hard to cross, ^sn10-3-6-3

not crossed before, so as to not be reborn.” ^sn10-3-6-4

### With Maṇibhadda (Linked Discourses 10.4) ^sn10-4-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Magadhans at the Maṇimālika Shrine, the haunt of the native spirit Maṇibhadda. ^sn10-4-1-1

Then the native spirit Maṇibhadda went up to the Buddha, and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn10-4-1-2

“It’s always auspicious for the mindful; ^sn10-4-2-1

the mindful prosper in happiness. ^sn10-4-2-2

Each new day is better for the mindful, ^sn10-4-2-3

and they are freed from enmity.” ^sn10-4-2-4

“It’s always auspicious for the mindful; ^sn10-4-3-1

the mindful prosper in happiness. ^sn10-4-3-2

Each new day is better for the mindful, ^sn10-4-3-3

but they’re not freed from enmity. ^sn10-4-3-4

But someone whose mind delights in harmlessness, ^sn10-4-4-1

all day and all night, ^sn10-4-4-2

with love for all living creatures— ^sn10-4-4-3

they have no enmity for anyone.” ^sn10-4-4-4

### With Sānu (Linked Discourses 10.5) ^sn10-5-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn10-5-1-1

Now at that time a certain lay woman had a son named Sānu who had been seized by a native spirit. ^sn10-5-1-2

And as that lay woman wept, on that occasion she recited these verses: ^sn10-5-1-3

“I have heard this from the perfected ones. ^sn10-5-2-1

The native spirits will not mess with anyone ^sn10-5-2-2

who leads the spiritual life ^sn10-5-2-3

by observing the sabbath ^sn10-5-2-4

complete in all eight factors ^sn10-5-3-1

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^sn10-5-3-2

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^sn10-5-3-3

as well as on the fortnight of special displays. ^sn10-5-3-4

But now today I see ^sn10-5-3-5

native spirits messing with Sānu.” ^sn10-5-3-6

“What you heard from the perfected ones is right. ^sn10-5-4-1

The native spirits will not mess with anyone ^sn10-5-4-2

who leads the spiritual life ^sn10-5-4-3

by observing the sabbath ^sn10-5-4-4

complete in all eight factors ^sn10-5-5-1

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^sn10-5-5-2

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^sn10-5-5-3

as well as on the fortnight of special displays. ^sn10-5-5-4

When Sānu regains consciousness tell him ^sn10-5-6-1

this saying of the native spirits: ^sn10-5-6-2

Don’t do bad deeds ^sn10-5-6-3

either openly or in secret. ^sn10-5-6-4

If you should do a bad deed, ^sn10-5-7-1

or you’re doing one now, ^sn10-5-7-2

you won’t be freed from suffering, ^sn10-5-7-3

though you fly away and flee.” ^sn10-5-7-4

“Mum, they cry for the dead, ^sn10-5-8-1

or for one who’s alive but missing. ^sn10-5-8-2

I’m alive and you can see me, ^sn10-5-8-3

so mum, why do you weep for me?” ^sn10-5-8-4

“Son, they cry for the dead, ^sn10-5-9-1

or for one who’s alive but missing. ^sn10-5-9-2

But someone who has given up sensual pleasures ^sn10-5-9-3

only to come back here again: ^sn10-5-9-4

they cry for them as well, ^sn10-5-9-5

for though still alive they’re really dead. ^sn10-5-9-6

My dear, you’ve been rescued from hot coals, ^sn10-5-10-1

and you want to plunge right back in them! ^sn10-5-10-2

My dear, you’ve been rescued from the abyss, ^sn10-5-10-3

and you want to plunge right back there! ^sn10-5-10-4

Hurry up, good luck to you! ^sn10-5-11-1

To whom should we complain? ^sn10-5-11-2

A valuable saved from a fire, ^sn10-5-11-3

you want to burn again!” ^sn10-5-11-4

### With Piyaṅkara (Linked Discourses 10.6) ^sn10-6-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn10-6-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Anuruddha rose at the crack of dawn and recited passages of the teaching. ^sn10-6-1-2

Then the native spirit Piyaṅkara’s Mother soothed her little child, saying: ^sn10-6-1-3

“Don’t make a sound, Piyaṅkara! ^sn10-6-2-1

A mendicant recites passages of the teaching. ^sn10-6-2-2

When we understand a passage, ^sn10-6-2-3

we can practice for our welfare. ^sn10-6-2-4

Let us restrain ourselves from harming living creatures, ^sn10-6-3-1

and speak no lying words. ^sn10-6-3-2

We should train ourselves well in ethics, ^sn10-6-3-3

and hopefully we’ll be freed from the goblin realm.” ^sn10-6-3-4

### With Punabbasu (Linked Discourses 10.7) ^sn10-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn10-7-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about extinguishment. ^sn10-7-1-2

And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening. ^sn10-7-1-3

Then the native spirit Punabbasu’s Mother soothed her little children, saying: ^sn10-7-1-4

“Hush, little Uttarā! ^sn10-7-2-1

Hush, Punabbasu! ^sn10-7-2-2

For I want to listen to the teaching ^sn10-7-2-3

of the Teacher, the supreme Buddha. ^sn10-7-2-4

Extinguishment, the Blessed One said, ^sn10-7-3-1

is the release from all ties. ^sn10-7-3-2

I have a lasting love ^sn10-7-3-3

for this teaching. ^sn10-7-3-4

In this world, your own child is dear; ^sn10-7-4-1

in this world, your own husband is dear; ^sn10-7-4-2

but even greater than that is my love ^sn10-7-4-3

for this teaching’s quest. ^sn10-7-4-4

For neither son nor husband, ^sn10-7-5-1

dear as they are, can free you from suffering; ^sn10-7-5-2

as listening to the true teaching ^sn10-7-5-3

frees living creatures from suffering. ^sn10-7-5-4

In this world mired in suffering, ^sn10-7-6-1

fettered by old age and death, ^sn10-7-6-2

I want to listen to the teaching ^sn10-7-6-3

through which the Buddha awakened, ^sn10-7-6-4

which frees you from old age and death. ^sn10-7-6-5

So hush, Punabbasu!” ^sn10-7-6-6

“Mom, I’m not speaking, ^sn10-7-7-1

and Uttarā is silent, too. ^sn10-7-7-2

Focus just on the teaching, ^sn10-7-7-3

for it’s nice to listen to the true teaching. ^sn10-7-7-4

And it’s because we haven’t understood the teaching ^sn10-7-7-5

that we live in suffering, Mom. ^sn10-7-7-6

He is a beacon for those who are lost ^sn10-7-8-1

among gods and humans. ^sn10-7-8-2

The Buddha, bearing his final body, ^sn10-7-8-3

the Clear-eyed One teaches Dhamma.” ^sn10-7-8-4

“It’s good that my child’s so astute, ^sn10-7-9-1

this child I bore and suckled! ^sn10-7-9-2

My child loves the pure teaching ^sn10-7-9-3

of the supreme Buddha. ^sn10-7-9-4

Punabbasu, may you be happy! ^sn10-7-10-1

Today, I rise. ^sn10-7-10-2

Hear me too, Uttarā: ^sn10-7-10-3

I have seen the noble truths!” ^sn10-7-10-4

### With Sudatta (Linked Discourses 10.8) ^sn10-8-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha in the Cool Grove. ^sn10-8-1-1

Now at that time the householder Anāthapiṇḍika had arrived at Rājagaha on some business. ^sn10-8-1-2

He heard ^sn10-8-1-3

a rumor that a Buddha had arisen in the world. ^sn10-8-1-4

Right away he wanted to go and see the Buddha, but he thought, ^sn10-8-1-5

“It’s too late to go and see the Buddha today. ^sn10-8-1-6

I’ll go and see him tomorrow.” He went to bed thinking of the Buddha. ^sn10-8-1-7

During the night he got up three times thinking it was morning. ^sn10-8-1-8

Then he approached the Sivaka Gate, ^sn10-8-1-9

and non-human beings opened it for him. ^sn10-8-1-10

But as he was leaving the city, light vanished and darkness appeared to him. He felt fear, terror, and goosebumps, and wanted to turn back. ^sn10-8-1-11

Then the invisible spirit Sivaka called out: ^sn10-8-1-12

“A hundred elephants, a hundred horses, ^sn10-8-2-1

a hundred mule-drawn chariots, ^sn10-8-2-2

a hundred thousand maidens ^sn10-8-2-3

bedecked with jeweled earrings: ^sn10-8-2-4

these are not worth a sixteenth part ^sn10-8-2-5

of a single forward stride! ^sn10-8-2-6

Forward, householder! ^sn10-8-3-1

Forward, householder! ^sn10-8-3-2

Going forward is better for you, ^sn10-8-3-3

not turning back!” ^sn10-8-3-4

Then darkness vanished and light appeared to Anāthapiṇḍika. His fear, terror, and goosebumps settled down. ^sn10-8-4-1

But for a second time, light vanished and darkness appeared to him. … ^sn10-8-4-2

For a second time the invisible spirit Sivaka called out … ^sn10-8-4-3

“… Going forward is better for you, ^sn10-8-6-3

not turning back!” ^sn10-8-6-4

Then darkness vanished and light appeared to Anāthapiṇḍika. His fear, terror, and goosebumps settled down. ^sn10-8-7-1

But for a third time, light vanished and darkness appeared to him. … ^sn10-8-7-2

For a third time the invisible spirit Sivaka called out … ^sn10-8-7-3

“… Going forward is better for you, ^sn10-8-9-3

not turning back!” ^sn10-8-9-4

Then darkness vanished and light appeared to Anāthapiṇḍika. His fear, terror, and goosebumps settled down. ^sn10-8-10-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went to the Cool Grove and approached the Buddha. ^sn10-8-10-2

Now at that time the Buddha had risen at the crack of dawn and was walking mindfully in the open. ^sn10-8-11-1

He saw Anāthapiṇḍika coming off in the distance. ^sn10-8-11-2

So he stepped down from the walking path, sat down on the seat spread out, ^sn10-8-11-3

and said to Anāthapiṇḍika, ^sn10-8-11-4

“Come, Sudatta.” ^sn10-8-11-5

Then Anāthapiṇḍika thought, “The Buddha calls me by name!” Smiling and elated, he bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet and said to him, ^sn10-8-11-6

“Sir, I trust the Buddha slept well?” ^sn10-8-11-7

“A brahmin who is fully quenched ^sn10-8-12-1

always sleeps at ease. ^sn10-8-12-2

Sensual pleasures slip off them, ^sn10-8-12-3

they’re cooled, free of attachments. ^sn10-8-12-4

Since they’ve cut off all clinging, ^sn10-8-13-1

and removed the stress from the heart, ^sn10-8-13-2

the peaceful sleep at ease, ^sn10-8-13-3

having found peace of mind.” ^sn10-8-13-4

### With the Nun Sukkā (1st) (Linked Discourses 10.9) ^sn10-9-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn10-9-1-1

Now, at that time the nun Sukkā was teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly. ^sn10-9-1-2

Then a native spirit was so devoted to Sukkā that he went from street to street and from square to square, and on that occasion recited these verses: ^sn10-9-1-3

“What’s up with these people in Rājagaha? ^sn10-9-2-1

They sleep like they’ve been drinking mead! ^sn10-9-2-2

They don’t attend on Sukkā ^sn10-9-2-3

as she’s teaching the state free of death. ^sn10-9-2-4

But the wise— ^sn10-9-3-1

it’s as if they drink it up, ^sn10-9-3-2

so irresistible, delectable, and nutritious, ^sn10-9-3-3

like travelers enjoying a cool cloud.” ^sn10-9-3-4

### With the Nun Sukkā (2nd) (Linked Discourses 10.10) ^sn10-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn10-10-1-1

Now at that time a certain lay follower gave food to the nun Sukkā. ^sn10-10-1-2

Then a native spirit was so devoted to Sukkā that he went from street to street and from square to square, and on that occasion recited these verses: ^sn10-10-1-3

“O! He has made so much merit! ^sn10-10-2-1

That lay follower is so very wise. ^sn10-10-2-2

He just gave food to Sukkā, ^sn10-10-2-3

who is released from all ties.” ^sn10-10-2-4

### With the Nun Cīrā (Linked Discourses 10.11) ^sn10-11-0

So I have heard. ^sn10-11-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn10-11-1-2

Now at that time a certain lay follower gave a robe to the nun Cīrā. ^sn10-11-1-3

Then a native spirit was so devoted to Cīrā that he went from street to street and from square to square, and on that occasion recited these verses: ^sn10-11-1-4

“O! He has made so much merit! ^sn10-11-2-1

That lay-follower is so very wise. ^sn10-11-2-2

He gave a robe to Cīrā, ^sn10-11-2-3

who is released from all yokes.” ^sn10-11-2-4

### With Āḷavaka (Linked Discourses 10.12) ^sn10-12-0

So I have heard. ^sn10-12-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Āḷavī in the haunt of the native spirit Āḷavaka. ^sn10-12-1-2

Then the native spirit Āḷavaka went up to the Buddha, and said to him: ^sn10-12-1-3

“Get out, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-4

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out. ^sn10-12-1-5

“Get in, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-6

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in. ^sn10-12-1-7

And for a second time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha, ^sn10-12-1-8

“Get out, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-9

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out. ^sn10-12-1-10

“Get in, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-11

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in. ^sn10-12-1-12

And for a third time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha, ^sn10-12-1-13

“Get out, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-14

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out. ^sn10-12-1-15

“Get in, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-16

Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in. ^sn10-12-1-17

And for a fourth time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha, ^sn10-12-1-18

“Get out, ascetic!” ^sn10-12-1-19

“No, respectable sir, I won’t get out. ^sn10-12-1-20

Do what you must.” ^sn10-12-1-21

“I will ask you a question, ascetic. ^sn10-12-1-22

If you don’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane, or explode your heart, or grab you by the feet and throw you to the far shore of the Ganges!” ^sn10-12-1-23

“I don’t see anyone in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans who could do that to me. ^sn10-12-1-24

But anyway, ask what you wish.” ^sn10-12-1-25

“What’s a person’s best wealth? ^sn10-12-2-1

What brings happiness when practiced well? ^sn10-12-2-2

What’s the sweetest taste of all? ^sn10-12-2-3

The one they say has the best life: how do they live?” ^sn10-12-2-4

“Faith here is a person’s best wealth. ^sn10-12-3-1

The teaching brings happiness when practiced well. ^sn10-12-3-2

Truth is the sweetest taste of all. ^sn10-12-3-3

The one they say has the best life lives by wisdom.” ^sn10-12-3-4

“How do you cross the flood? ^sn10-12-4-1

How do you cross the deluge? ^sn10-12-4-2

How do you get over suffering? ^sn10-12-4-3

How do you get purified?” ^sn10-12-4-4

“By faith you cross the flood, ^sn10-12-5-1

and by diligence the deluge. ^sn10-12-5-2

By energy you get past suffering, ^sn10-12-5-3

and you’re purified by wisdom.” ^sn10-12-5-4

“How do you get wisdom? ^sn10-12-6-1

How do you earn wealth? ^sn10-12-6-2

How do you get a good reputation? ^sn10-12-6-3

How do you hold on to friends? ^sn10-12-6-4

How do the departed not grieve ^sn10-12-6-5

when passing from this world to the next?” ^sn10-12-6-6

“One who is diligent and discerning ^sn10-12-7-1

gains wisdom by wanting to learn, ^sn10-12-7-2

having faith in the perfected ones, ^sn10-12-7-3

and the teaching for becoming extinguished. ^sn10-12-7-4

Being responsible, acting appropriately, ^sn10-12-8-1

and working hard you earn wealth. ^sn10-12-8-2

Truthfulness wins you a good reputation. ^sn10-12-8-3

You hold on to friends by giving. ^sn10-12-8-4

That’s how the departed do not grieve ^sn10-12-8-5

when passing from this world to the next. ^sn10-12-8-6

A faithful householder of discernment ^sn10-12-9-1

who possesses these four qualities ^sn10-12-9-2

does not grieve after passing away: ^sn10-12-9-3

truth, principle, steadfastness, and generosity. ^sn10-12-9-4

Go ahead, ask others as well, ^sn10-12-10-1

there are many ascetics and brahmins. ^sn10-12-10-2

See whether anything better is found ^sn10-12-10-3

than truth, principle, generosity, and patience.” ^sn10-12-10-4

“Why now would I question ^sn10-12-11-1

the many ascetics and brahmins? ^sn10-12-11-2

Today I understand ^sn10-12-11-3

what’s good for the next life. ^sn10-12-11-4

It was truly for my benefit ^sn10-12-12-1

that the Buddha came to stay at Āḷavī. ^sn10-12-12-2

Today I understand ^sn10-12-12-3

where a gift is very fruitful. ^sn10-12-12-4

I myself will journey ^sn10-12-13-1

village to village, town to town, ^sn10-12-13-2

paying homage to the Buddha, ^sn10-12-13-3

and the clear rightness of the teaching!” ^sn10-12-13-4

The Linked Discourses with Native Spirits are complete. ^sn10-12-14-5

## Saṁyutta 11 ^sn11-0

### With Suvīra (Linked Discourses 11.1) ^sn11-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn11-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn11-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn11-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn11-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn11-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn11-1-1-6

“Once upon a time, mendicants, the titans marched against the gods. ^sn11-1-2-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the godling Suvīra, ^sn11-1-2-2

‘Dear Suvīra, the titans march against the gods! ^sn11-1-2-3

Go, and march against the titans!’ ^sn11-1-2-4

‘Yes, lord,’ replied Suvīra. But he fell into negligence. ^sn11-1-2-5

For a second time Sakka addressed Suvīra, ^sn11-1-2-6

‘Dear Suvīra, the titans march against the gods! ^sn11-1-2-7

Go, and march against the titans!’ ^sn11-1-2-8

‘Yes, lord,’ replied Suvīra. But for a second time he fell into negligence. ^sn11-1-2-9

For a third time Sakka addressed Suvīra, ^sn11-1-2-10

‘Dear Suvīra, the titans march against the gods! ^sn11-1-2-11

Go, and march against the titans!’ ^sn11-1-2-12

‘Yes, lord,’ replied Suvīra. But for a third time he fell into negligence. ^sn11-1-2-13

Then Sakka addressed the godling Suvīra in verse: ^sn11-1-2-14

‘Suvīra, go to that place ^sn11-1-3-1

where you can achieve happiness ^sn11-1-3-2

without working for it or trying hard— ^sn11-1-3-3

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-1-3-4

‘That a lazy man who does no work, ^sn11-1-4-1

and doesn’t do his duty, ^sn11-1-4-2

can fulfill all his desires: ^sn11-1-4-3

Sakka, render me this boon!’ ^sn11-1-4-4

‘Suvīra, go to that place ^sn11-1-5-1

where a lazy man who does no work ^sn11-1-5-2

prospers in unending happiness— ^sn11-1-5-3

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-1-5-4

‘O Sakka, first among gods, ^sn11-1-6-1

that we might find the happiness ^sn11-1-6-2

that’s sorrowless, unstressed: ^sn11-1-6-3

Sakka, render me this boon!’ ^sn11-1-6-4

‘If there exists anywhere a place ^sn11-1-7-1

where one can live happily without working, ^sn11-1-7-2

that surely would be extinguishment’s path! ^sn11-1-7-3

Go there, Suvīra, ^sn11-1-7-4

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-1-7-5

Since, mendicants, even that Sakka, lord of gods—while living off of the fruit of his good deeds, and ruling as sovereign lord over these gods of the thirty-three—will speak in praise of initiative and energy, ^sn11-1-8-1

you can excel here, for you who have gone forth in such a well explained teaching and training can try hard, strive, and make an effort to attain the unattained, achieve the unachieved, and realize the unrealized.” ^sn11-1-8-2

### With Susīma (Linked Discourses 11.2) ^sn11-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-2-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn11-2-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn11-2-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn11-2-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn11-2-1-5

“Once upon a time, mendicants, the titans marched against the gods. ^sn11-2-2-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the godling Susīma, ^sn11-2-2-2

‘Dear Susīma, the titans march against the gods! ^sn11-2-2-3

Go, and march against the titans!’ ^sn11-2-2-4

‘Yes, lord,’ replied Susīma. But he fell into negligence. ^sn11-2-2-5

For a second time … ^sn11-2-2-6

For a third time … ^sn11-2-2-8

Then Sakka addressed the godling Susīma in verse: ^sn11-2-2-10

‘Susīma, go to that place ^sn11-2-3-1

where you can achieve happiness ^sn11-2-3-2

without working for it or trying hard— ^sn11-2-3-3

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-2-3-4

‘That a lazy man who does no work, ^sn11-2-4-1

and doesn’t do his duty, ^sn11-2-4-2

should fulfill all his desires: ^sn11-2-4-3

Sakka, render me this boon!’ ^sn11-2-4-4

‘Susīma, go to that place ^sn11-2-5-1

where a lazy man who does no work ^sn11-2-5-2

prospers in unending happiness— ^sn11-2-5-3

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-2-5-4

‘O Sakka, first among gods, ^sn11-2-6-1

that we might find the happiness ^sn11-2-6-2

that’s sorrowless, unstressed: ^sn11-2-6-3

Sakka, render me this boon!’ ^sn11-2-6-4

‘If there exists anywhere a place ^sn11-2-7-1

where one can live happily without working, ^sn11-2-7-2

that surely would be extinguishment’s path! ^sn11-2-7-3

Susīma, go to that place ^sn11-2-7-4

and take me with you!’ ^sn11-2-7-5

Since, mendicants, even that Sakka, lord of gods—while living off of the fruit of his good deeds, and ruling as sovereign lord over these gods of the thirty-three—will speak in praise of initiative and energy, ^sn11-2-8-1

you can excel at this, for you who have gone forth in such a well explained teaching and training can try hard, strive, and make an effort to attain the unattained, achieve the unachieved, and realize the unrealized.” ^sn11-2-8-2

### The Banner’s Crest (Linked Discourses 11.3) ^sn11-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-3-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn11-3-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn11-3-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn11-3-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn11-3-1-5

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn11-3-2-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the gods of the thirty-three: ^sn11-3-2-2

‘Good fellows, when the gods are fighting, if you get scared or terrified, just look up at my banner’s crest. ^sn11-3-3-1

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-3-2

If you can’t see my banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of the Progenitor, king of gods. ^sn11-3-4-1

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-4-2

If you can’t see his banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of Varuṇa, king of gods. ^sn11-3-5-1

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-5-2

If you can’t see his banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of Īsāna, king of gods. ^sn11-3-6-1

Then your fear and terror will go away.’ ^sn11-3-6-2

However, when they look up at those banner’s crests their fear and terror might go away or it might not. ^sn11-3-7-1

Why is that? ^sn11-3-8-1

Because Sakka is not free of greed, hate, and delusion. He is fearful, scared, nervous, quick to flee. ^sn11-3-8-2

But, mendicants, I say this: ^sn11-3-9-1

If you’ve gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut and you get scared or terrified, just recollect me: ^sn11-3-9-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn11-3-9-3

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-9-4

If you can’t recollect me, then recollect the teaching: ^sn11-3-10-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn11-3-10-2

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-10-3

If you can’t recollect the teaching, then recollect the Saṅgha: ^sn11-3-11-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn11-3-11-2

Then your fear and terror will go away. ^sn11-3-11-3

Why is that? ^sn11-3-12-1

Because the Realized One is free of greed, hate, and delusion. He is fearless, brave, bold, and stands his ground.” ^sn11-3-12-2

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn11-3-12-3

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn11-3-12-4

“In the wilderness, at a tree’s root, ^sn11-3-13-1

or an empty hut, O mendicants, ^sn11-3-13-2

recollect the Buddha, ^sn11-3-13-3

and no fear will come to you. ^sn11-3-13-4

If you can’t recollect the Buddha— ^sn11-3-14-1

the eldest in the world, the captain of men— ^sn11-3-14-2

then recollect the teaching, ^sn11-3-14-3

emancipating, well taught. ^sn11-3-14-4

If you can’t recollect the teaching— ^sn11-3-15-1

emancipating, well taught— ^sn11-3-15-2

then recollect the Saṅgha, ^sn11-3-15-3

the supreme field of merit. ^sn11-3-15-4

Thus recollecting the Buddha, ^sn11-3-16-1

the teaching, and the Saṅgha, mendicants, ^sn11-3-16-2

fear and terror ^sn11-3-16-3

and goosebumps will be no more.” ^sn11-3-16-4

### With Vepacitti (Linked Discourses 11.4) ^sn11-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-4-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn11-4-1-2

Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, addressed the titans, ^sn11-4-1-3

‘My good fellows, if the titans defeat the gods in this battle, bind Sakka, the lord of gods, by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the citadel of the titans.’ ^sn11-4-1-4

Meanwhile, Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the gods of the thirty-three, ^sn11-4-1-5

‘My good fellows, if the gods defeat the titans in this battle, bind Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods.’ ^sn11-4-1-6

In that battle the gods won and the titans lost. ^sn11-4-1-7

So the gods of the thirty-three bound Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and brought him to Sakka’s presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods. ^sn11-4-1-8

And as Sakka was entering and leaving the hall, Vepacitti abused and insulted him with rude, harsh words. ^sn11-4-1-9

So Mātali the chariot handler addressed Sakka in verse, ^sn11-4-1-10

‘O Maghavā, O Sakka, ^sn11-4-2-1

is it from fear or from weakness ^sn11-4-2-2

that you endure such harsh words ^sn11-4-2-3

in the presence of Vepacitti?’ ^sn11-4-2-4

‘It’s not out of fear or weakness ^sn11-4-3-1

that I’m patient with Vepacitti. ^sn11-4-3-2

For how can a sensible person like me ^sn11-4-3-3

get in a fight with a fool?’ ^sn11-4-3-4

‘Fools would vent even more ^sn11-4-4-1

if there’s no-one to put a stop to them. ^sn11-4-4-2

So a wise one should stop ^sn11-4-4-3

a fool with forceful punishment.’ ^sn11-4-4-4

‘I think that this is the only way ^sn11-4-5-1

to put a stop to a fool, ^sn11-4-5-2

when you know that the other is upset, ^sn11-4-5-3

be mindful and stay calm.’ ^sn11-4-5-4

‘In this mere endurance, Vāsava, ^sn11-4-6-1

I see a flaw. ^sn11-4-6-2

When a fool thinks, ^sn11-4-6-3

“He endures me out of fear,” ^sn11-4-6-4

the simpleton will go after you even harder, ^sn11-4-6-5

like a cow chasing someone who runs away.’ ^sn11-4-6-6

‘Let him think this if he wishes, or not— ^sn11-4-7-1

“He endures me out of fear.” ^sn11-4-7-2

For the highest goal, one’s own, ^sn11-4-7-3

naught better than patience is found. ^sn11-4-7-4

When a strong person ^sn11-4-8-1

endures a weakling, ^sn11-4-8-2

they call that the ultimate patience, ^sn11-4-8-3

for a weakling must always be patient. ^sn11-4-8-4

The strength of folly ^sn11-4-9-1

is really just weakness, they say. ^sn11-4-9-2

But no-one can challenge a person ^sn11-4-9-3

who’s strong, guarded by the teaching. ^sn11-4-9-4

When you get angry at an angry person ^sn11-4-10-1

you just make things worse for yourself. ^sn11-4-10-2

When you don’t get angry at an angry person ^sn11-4-10-3

you win a battle hard to win. ^sn11-4-10-4

When you know that the other is angry, ^sn11-4-11-1

you act for the good of both ^sn11-4-11-2

yourself and the other ^sn11-4-11-3

if you’re mindful and stay calm. ^sn11-4-11-4

People inexpert in the teaching ^sn11-4-12-1

consider one who heals both ^sn11-4-12-2

oneself and the other ^sn11-4-12-3

to be a fool.’ ^sn11-4-12-4

Since, mendicants, even that Sakka, lord of gods—while living off of the fruit of his good deeds, and ruling as sovereign lord over these gods of the thirty-three—will speak in praise of patience and gentleness, ^sn11-4-13-1

you can excel at this, for you who have gone forth in such a well explained teaching and training can develop patience and gentleness.” ^sn11-4-13-2

### Victory by Good Speech (Linked Discourses 11.5) ^sn11-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-5-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn11-5-1-2

Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, said to Sakka, lord of gods, ^sn11-5-1-3

‘Lord of gods, let there be victory by fine words!’ ^sn11-5-1-4

‘Vepacitti, let there be victory by fine words!’ ^sn11-5-1-5

Then the gods and the titans appointed a panel of judges, saying, ^sn11-5-1-6

‘These will understand our good and bad statements.’ ^sn11-5-1-7

Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, said to Sakka, lord of gods, ^sn11-5-1-8

‘Lord of gods, recite a verse!’ ^sn11-5-1-9

When he said this, Sakka said to him, ^sn11-5-1-10

‘Vepacitti, you are the elder gods here. ^sn11-5-1-11

Recite a verse.’ ^sn11-5-1-12

So Vepacitti spoke this verse: ^sn11-5-1-13

‘Fools would vent even more ^sn11-5-2-1

if there’s no-one to put a stop to them. ^sn11-5-2-2

So an intelligent person should stop ^sn11-5-2-3

a fool with forceful punishment.’ ^sn11-5-2-4

The titans applauded Vepacitti’s verse, while the gods kept silent. ^sn11-5-3-1

Then Vepacitti said to Sakka, ^sn11-5-3-2

‘Lord of gods, recite a verse!’ ^sn11-5-3-3

So Sakka spoke this verse: ^sn11-5-3-4

‘I think that this is the only way ^sn11-5-4-1

to put a stop to a fool, ^sn11-5-4-2

when you know that the other is upset, ^sn11-5-4-3

be mindful and stay calm.’ ^sn11-5-4-4

The gods applauded Sakka’s verse, while the titans kept silent. ^sn11-5-5-1

Then Sakka said to Vepacitti, ^sn11-5-5-2

‘Vepacitti, recite a verse!’ ^sn11-5-5-3

So Vepacitti spoke this verse: ^sn11-5-5-4

‘In this mere endurance, Vāsava, ^sn11-5-6-1

I see a flaw. ^sn11-5-6-2

When a fool thinks, ^sn11-5-6-3

“He endures me out of fear,” ^sn11-5-6-4

the simpleton will go after you even harder, ^sn11-5-6-5

like a cow chasing someone who runs away.’ ^sn11-5-6-6

The titans applauded Vepacitti’s verse, while the gods kept silent. ^sn11-5-7-1

Then Vepacitti said to Sakka, ^sn11-5-7-2

‘Lord of gods, recite a verse!’ ^sn11-5-7-3

So Sakka spoke this verse: ^sn11-5-7-4

‘Let him think this if he wishes, or not— ^sn11-5-8-1

“He endures me out of fear.” ^sn11-5-8-2

For the highest goal, one’s own, ^sn11-5-8-3

naught better than patience is found. ^sn11-5-8-4

When a strong person ^sn11-5-9-1

endures a weakling, ^sn11-5-9-2

they call that the ultimate patience, ^sn11-5-9-3

for a weakling must always be patient. ^sn11-5-9-4

The strength of folly ^sn11-5-10-1

is really just weakness, they say. ^sn11-5-10-2

But no-one can challenge a person ^sn11-5-10-3

who’s strong, guarded by the teaching. ^sn11-5-10-4

When you get angry at an angry person ^sn11-5-11-1

you just make things worse for yourself. ^sn11-5-11-2

When you don’t get angry at an angry person ^sn11-5-11-3

you win a battle hard to win. ^sn11-5-11-4

When you know that the other is angry, ^sn11-5-12-1

you act for the good of both ^sn11-5-12-2

yourself and the other ^sn11-5-12-3

if you’re mindful and stay calm. ^sn11-5-12-4

People inexpert in the teaching ^sn11-5-13-1

consider one who heals both ^sn11-5-13-2

oneself and the other ^sn11-5-13-3

to be a fool.’ ^sn11-5-13-4

The gods applauded Sakka’s verses, while the titans kept silent. ^sn11-5-14-1

Then the panel of judges consisting of both gods and titans said this, ^sn11-5-14-2

‘The verses spoken by Vepacitti ^sn11-5-14-3

evoke punishment and violence. That’s how you get fights, arguments, and quarrels. ^sn11-5-14-4

The verses spoken by Sakka ^sn11-5-14-5

don’t evoke punishment and violence. That’s how you stay free of fights, arguments, and quarrels. ^sn11-5-14-6

Sakka, lord of gods, wins victory by fine words!’ ^sn11-5-14-7

And that’s how Sakka came to win victory by fine words.” ^sn11-5-14-8

### Bird Nests (Linked Discourses 11.6) ^sn11-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-6-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn11-6-1-2

In that battle the titans won and the gods lost. ^sn11-6-1-3

Defeated, the gods fled north with the titans in pursuit. ^sn11-6-1-4

Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed his chariot handler Mātali in verse: ^sn11-6-1-5

‘Mātali, don’t ram the bird nests ^sn11-6-2-1

in the Red Silk-Cotton Woods with your draft-pole. ^sn11-6-2-2

I’d rather give up our lives to the titans ^sn11-6-2-3

than deprive these birds of their nests.’ ^sn11-6-2-4

‘Yes, lord,’ replied Mātali. And he turned the chariot back around, with its team of a thousand thoroughbreds. ^sn11-6-3-1

Then the titans thought, ^sn11-6-3-2

‘Now Sakka’s chariot has turned back. ^sn11-6-3-3

The titans will have to fight the gods a second time!’ Terrified, they retreated right away to the citadel of the titans. ^sn11-6-3-4

And that’s how Sakka came to win victory by principle.” ^sn11-6-3-5

### Not Betray (Linked Discourses 11.7) ^sn11-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-7-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, as Sakka, lord of gods, was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn11-7-1-2

‘I should never betray even a sworn enemy.’ ^sn11-7-1-3

And then Vepacitti, lord of titans, knowing Sakka’s train of thought, approached him. ^sn11-7-1-4

Sakka saw Vepacitti coming off in the distance, ^sn11-7-1-5

and said to him, ^sn11-7-1-6

‘Stop, Vepacitti, you’re caught!’ ^sn11-7-1-7

‘Good fellow, don’t give up the idea you just had!’ ^sn11-7-2-1

‘Swear, Vepacitti, that you won’t betray me.’ ^sn11-7-3-1

‘Whatever bad things happen to a liar, ^sn11-7-4-1

or to someone who slanders the noble ones, ^sn11-7-4-2

or to someone who betrays a friend, ^sn11-7-4-3

or to someone who’s ungrateful, ^sn11-7-4-4

the same bad things impact ^sn11-7-4-5

anyone who betrays you, Sujā’s husband.’” ^sn11-7-4-6

### Verocana, Lord of Titans (Linked Discourses 11.8) ^sn11-8-0

Near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove. ^sn11-8-1-1

Now at that time the Buddha had gone into retreat for the day’s meditation. ^sn11-8-1-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, and Verocana, lord of titans, approached the Buddha and stationed themselves one by each door-post. ^sn11-8-1-3

Then Verocana spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn11-8-1-4

“A man should make an effort ^sn11-8-2-1

until his goal is accomplished. ^sn11-8-2-2

A goal is splendid when accomplished— ^sn11-8-2-3

this is the word of Verocana!” ^sn11-8-2-4

“A man should make an effort ^sn11-8-3-1

until his goal is accomplished. ^sn11-8-3-2

For the goal that’s splendid when accomplished, ^sn11-8-3-3

naught better than patience is found.” ^sn11-8-3-4

“All beings are in need, ^sn11-8-4-1

as they deserve in each case. ^sn11-8-4-2

But even so, harnessed work is the highest ^sn11-8-4-3

thing of value for all living creatures. ^sn11-8-4-4

A goal is splendid when accomplished— ^sn11-8-4-5

this is the word of Verocana!” ^sn11-8-4-6

“All beings are in need, ^sn11-8-5-1

as they deserve in each case. ^sn11-8-5-2

But rather than harnessed work being the highest ^sn11-8-5-3

thing of value for all living creatures, ^sn11-8-5-4

for the goal that’s splendid when accomplished, ^sn11-8-5-5

naught better than patience is found.” ^sn11-8-5-6

### Seers in the Wilderness (Linked Discourses 11.9) ^sn11-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-9-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, several seers who were ethical, of good character, settled in leaf huts in a wilderness region. ^sn11-9-1-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, and Vepacitti, lord of titans, went to those seers. ^sn11-9-1-3

Vepacitti put on his lined sandals, strapped on his sword, and, carrying a sunshade, entered the hermitage through the main gate. He walked right past those seers, keeping them at arm’s length. ^sn11-9-1-4

Sakka took off his lined sandals, gave his sword to others, and, putting down his sunshade, entered the hermitage through the same gate. He stood downwind of those seers, revering them with cupped palms. ^sn11-9-1-5

Then those seers addressed Sakka in verse: ^sn11-9-1-6

‘When seers have been long initiated, ^sn11-9-2-1

the odor of their bodies goes with the gale. ^sn11-9-2-2

You’d better leave, O thousand-eyed! ^sn11-9-2-3

The odor of the seers is unclean, king of gods.’ ^sn11-9-2-4

‘When seers have been long initiated, ^sn11-9-3-1

let the odor of their bodies go with the gale. ^sn11-9-3-2

We yearn for this odor, sirs, ^sn11-9-3-3

like a colorful crown of flowers. ^sn11-9-3-4

The gods don’t see it as repulsive.’” ^sn11-9-3-5

### Seers by the Ocean (Linked Discourses 11.10) ^sn11-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn11-10-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, several seers who were ethical, of good character, settled in leaf huts on the ocean shore. ^sn11-10-1-2

Now at that time a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn11-10-1-3

Then the seers thought, ^sn11-10-1-4

‘The gods are principled, the titans are unprincipled. ^sn11-10-1-5

We may be at risk from the titans. ^sn11-10-1-6

Why don’t we approach Sambara, lord of titans, and beg him for a pledge of safety?’ ^sn11-10-1-7

Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, those seers vanished from those leaf huts on the ocean shore and reappeared in Sambara’s presence. ^sn11-10-1-8

Then those seers addressed Sambara in verse: ^sn11-10-1-9

‘The seers have come to Sambara ^sn11-10-2-1

to beg for a pledge of safety. ^sn11-10-2-2

For you can give them what you wish, ^sn11-10-2-3

whether danger or safety.’ ^sn11-10-2-4

‘There is no safety for seers, ^sn11-10-3-1
