### Gotama (Linked Discourses 12.10) ^sn12-10-0

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn12-10-1-1

‘Alas, this world has fallen into trouble. It’s born, grows old, dies, passes away, and is reborn, ^sn12-10-1-2

yet it doesn’t understand how to escape from this suffering, from old age and death. ^sn12-10-1-3

Oh, when will an escape be found from this suffering, from old age and death?’ ^sn12-10-1-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-10-2-1

‘When what exists is there old age and death? What is a requirement for old age and death?’ ^sn12-10-2-2

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-10-2-3

‘When rebirth exists there’s old age and death. Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’ ^sn12-10-2-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-10-3-1

‘When what exists is there rebirth? … ^sn12-10-3-2

continued existence … ^sn12-10-3-3

grasping … ^sn12-10-3-4

craving … ^sn12-10-3-5

feeling … ^sn12-10-3-6

contact … ^sn12-10-3-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-10-3-8

name and form … ^sn12-10-3-9

consciousness … ^sn12-10-3-10

‘When what exists are there choices? What is a requirement for choices?’ ^sn12-10-3-11

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-10-3-12

‘When ignorance exists there are choices. Ignorance is a requirement for choices.’ ^sn12-10-3-13

And so, ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-10-4-1

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-10-4-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-10-4-3

‘Origination, origination.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another. ^sn12-10-4-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-10-5-1

‘When what doesn’t exist is there no old age and death? When what ceases do old age and death cease?’ ^sn12-10-5-2

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-10-5-3

‘When rebirth doesn’t exist there’s no old age and death. When rebirth ceases, old age and death cease.’ ^sn12-10-5-4

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-10-6-1

‘When what doesn’t exist is there no rebirth? … ^sn12-10-6-2

continued existence … ^sn12-10-6-3

grasping … ^sn12-10-6-4

craving … ^sn12-10-6-5

feeling … ^sn12-10-6-6

contact … ^sn12-10-6-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-10-6-8

name and form … ^sn12-10-6-9

consciousness … ^sn12-10-6-10

‘When what doesn’t exist are there no choices? When what ceases do choices cease?’ ^sn12-10-6-11

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-10-6-12

‘When ignorance doesn’t exist there are no choices. When ignorance ceases, choices cease.’ ^sn12-10-6-13

And so, when ignorance ceases, choices cease. ^sn12-10-7-1

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-10-7-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-10-7-3

‘Cessation, cessation.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another.” ^sn12-10-7-4

### Fuel (Linked Discourses 12.11) ^sn12-11-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-11-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. … ^sn12-11-1-2

“Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-11-1-3

What four? ^sn12-11-1-4

Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth. ^sn12-11-1-5

These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-11-1-6

What is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of these four fuels? ^sn12-11-2-1

Craving. ^sn12-11-2-2

And what is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of craving? ^sn12-11-2-3

Feeling. ^sn12-11-2-4

And what is the source of feeling? ^sn12-11-2-5

Contact. ^sn12-11-2-6

And what is the source of contact? ^sn12-11-2-7

The six sense fields. ^sn12-11-2-8

And what is the source of the six sense fields? ^sn12-11-2-9

Name and form. ^sn12-11-2-10

And what is the source of name and form? ^sn12-11-2-11

Consciousness. ^sn12-11-2-12

And what is the source of consciousness? ^sn12-11-2-13

Choices. ^sn12-11-2-14

And what is the source of choices? ^sn12-11-2-15

Ignorance. ^sn12-11-2-16

And so, mendicants, ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-11-3-1

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-11-3-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-11-3-3

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-11-3-4

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-11-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-11-3-6

### Phagguna of the Top-Knot (Linked Discourses 12.12) ^sn12-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-12-1-1

“Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-12-1-2

What four? ^sn12-12-1-3

Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth. ^sn12-12-1-4

These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those that are about to be born.” ^sn12-12-1-5

When he said this, Venerable Phagguna of the Top-Knot said to the Buddha, ^sn12-12-2-1

“But sir, who consumes consciousness as fuel?” ^sn12-12-2-2

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-12-2-3

“I don’t speak of one who consumes. ^sn12-12-2-4

If I were to speak of one who consumes, then it would be cogent to ask ^sn12-12-2-5

who consumes. ^sn12-12-2-6

But I don’t speak like that. ^sn12-12-2-7

Hence it would be cogent to ask: ^sn12-12-2-8

‘For what is consciousness the fuel?’ ^sn12-12-2-9

And a cogent answer to this would be: ^sn12-12-2-10

‘Consciousness as fuel is a requirement for regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. When the being is present, there are the six sense fields. The six sense fields are requirements for contact.’” ^sn12-12-2-11

“But sir, who contacts?” ^sn12-12-3-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-12-3-2

“I don’t speak of one who contacts. ^sn12-12-3-3

If I were to speak of one who contacts, then it would be cogent to ask ^sn12-12-3-4

who contacts. ^sn12-12-3-5

But I don’t speak like that. ^sn12-12-3-6

Hence it would be cogent to ask: ^sn12-12-3-7

‘What is a requirement for contact?’ ^sn12-12-3-8

And a cogent answer to this would be: ^sn12-12-3-9

‘The six sense fields are requirements for contact. Contact is a requirement for feeling.’” ^sn12-12-3-10

“But sir, who feels?” ^sn12-12-4-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-12-4-2

“I don’t speak of one who feels. ^sn12-12-4-3

If I were to speak of one who feels, then it would be cogent to ask ^sn12-12-4-4

who feels. ^sn12-12-4-5

But I don’t speak like that. ^sn12-12-4-6

Hence it would be cogent to ask: ^sn12-12-4-7

‘What is a requirement for feeling?’ ^sn12-12-4-8

And a cogent answer to this would be: ^sn12-12-4-9

‘Contact is a requirement for feeling. Feeling is a requirement for craving.’” ^sn12-12-4-10

“But sir, who craves?” ^sn12-12-5-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-12-5-2

“I don’t speak of one who craves. ^sn12-12-5-3

If I were to speak of one who craves, then it would be cogent to ask ^sn12-12-5-4

who craves. ^sn12-12-5-5

But I don’t speak like that. ^sn12-12-5-6

Hence it would be cogent to ask: ^sn12-12-5-7

‘What is a requirement for craving?’ ^sn12-12-5-8

And a cogent answer to this would be: ^sn12-12-5-9

‘Feeling is a requirement for craving. Craving is a requirement for grasping.’” ^sn12-12-5-10

“But sir, who grasps?” ^sn12-12-6-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-12-6-2

“I don’t speak of one who grasps. ^sn12-12-6-3

If I were to speak of one who grasps, then it would be cogent to ask ^sn12-12-6-4

who grasps. ^sn12-12-6-5

But I don’t speak like that. ^sn12-12-6-6

Hence it would be cogent to ask: ^sn12-12-6-7

‘What is a requirement for grasping?’ ^sn12-12-6-8

And a cogent answer to this would be: ^sn12-12-6-9

‘Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-12-6-10

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence.’ … ^sn12-12-6-11

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-12-6-12

When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with no residue left behind, contact ceases. ^sn12-12-7-1

When contact ceases, feeling ceases. ^sn12-12-7-2

When feeling ceases, craving ceases. ^sn12-12-7-3

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-12-7-4

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-12-7-5

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-12-7-6

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-12-7-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-12-7-8

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Linked Discourses 12.13) ^sn12-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-13-1-1

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-13-1-2

They don’t understand rebirth … ^sn12-13-1-3

continued existence … ^sn12-13-1-4

grasping … ^sn12-13-1-5

craving … ^sn12-13-1-6

feeling … ^sn12-13-1-7

contact … ^sn12-13-1-8

the six sense fields … ^sn12-13-1-9

name and form … ^sn12-13-1-10

consciousness … ^sn12-13-1-11

They don’t understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-13-1-12

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn12-13-1-13

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-13-2-1

They understand rebirth … ^sn12-13-2-2

continued existence … ^sn12-13-2-3

grasping … ^sn12-13-2-4

craving … ^sn12-13-2-5

feeling … ^sn12-13-2-6

contact … ^sn12-13-2-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-13-2-8

name and form … ^sn12-13-2-9

consciousness … ^sn12-13-2-10

They understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-13-2-11

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn12-13-2-12

### Ascetics and Brahmins (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.14) ^sn12-14-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-14-1-1

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand these things, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. What things don’t they understand? ^sn12-14-1-2

They don’t understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-2-1

They don’t understand rebirth … ^sn12-14-2-2

continued existence … ^sn12-14-2-3

grasping … ^sn12-14-2-4

craving … ^sn12-14-2-5

feeling … ^sn12-14-2-6

contact … ^sn12-14-2-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-14-2-8

name and form … ^sn12-14-2-9

consciousness … ^sn12-14-2-10

They don’t understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-2-11

They don’t understand these things, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-2-12

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn12-14-2-13

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand these things, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. What things do they understand? ^sn12-14-3-1

They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-4-1

They understand rebirth … ^sn12-14-4-2

continued existence … ^sn12-14-4-3

grasping … ^sn12-14-4-4

craving … ^sn12-14-4-5

feeling … ^sn12-14-4-6

contact … ^sn12-14-4-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-14-4-8

name and form … ^sn12-14-4-9

consciousness … ^sn12-14-4-10

They understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-4-11

They understand these things, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-14-4-12

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn12-14-4-13

### Kaccānagotta (Linked Discourses 12.15) ^sn12-15-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-15-1-1

Then Venerable Kaccānagotta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn12-15-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘right view’. ^sn12-15-1-3

How is right view defined?” ^sn12-15-1-4

“Kaccāna, this world mostly relies on the duality of existence and non-existence. ^sn12-15-2-1

But for one who truly sees the origin of the world with right understanding, the concept of non-existence regarding the world does not occur. ^sn12-15-2-2

And for one who truly sees the cessation of the world with right understanding, the concept of existence regarding the world does not occur. ^sn12-15-2-3

This world is for the most part shackled by getting involved, grasping, and insisting. ^sn12-15-2-4

But if—for all that getting involved and grasping, mental fixation, and insistence and underlying tendency—they do not get attracted, grasp, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’, ^sn12-15-2-5

they’ll have no doubt or uncertainty that what arises is just suffering arising, and what ceases is just suffering ceasing. Their knowledge of this is independent of others. ^sn12-15-2-6

This is how right view is defined. ^sn12-15-2-7

‘All exists’: this is one extreme. ^sn12-15-3-1

‘All does not exist’: this is the second extreme. ^sn12-15-3-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-15-3-3

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-15-3-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-15-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-15-3-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-15-3-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-15-3-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-15-3-9

### A Dhamma Speaker (Linked Discourses 12.16) ^sn12-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-16-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn12-16-1-2

“Sir, they speak of a ‘Dhamma speaker’. ^sn12-16-1-3

How is a Dhamma speaker defined?” ^sn12-16-1-4

“If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn12-16-2-1

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn12-16-2-2

If they’re freed by not grasping, by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’. ^sn12-16-2-3

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment regarding rebirth … ^sn12-16-3-1

continued existence … ^sn12-16-3-2

grasping … ^sn12-16-3-3

craving … ^sn12-16-3-4

feeling … ^sn12-16-3-5

contact … ^sn12-16-3-6

the six sense fields … ^sn12-16-3-7

name and form … ^sn12-16-3-8

consciousness … ^sn12-16-3-9

choices … ^sn12-16-3-10

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn12-16-3-11

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn12-16-3-12

If they’re freed by not grasping, by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.” ^sn12-16-3-13

### With Kassapa, the Naked Ascetic (Linked Discourses 12.17) ^sn12-17-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-17-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn12-17-1-2

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms. ^sn12-17-1-3

The naked ascetic Kassapa saw the Buddha coming off in the distance. ^sn12-17-1-4

He went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-17-1-5

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he stood to one side and said to the Buddha, ^sn12-17-1-6

“I’d like to ask the worthy Gotama about a certain point, if you’d take the time to answer.” ^sn12-17-1-7

“Kassapa, it’s the wrong time for questions. ^sn12-17-1-8

We’ve entered an inhabited area.” ^sn12-17-1-9

A second time, ^sn12-17-2-1

and a third time, Kassapa spoke to the Buddha and the Buddha replied. ^sn12-17-2-5

When this was said, Kassapa said to the Buddha, ^sn12-17-2-7

“I don’t want to ask much.” ^sn12-17-2-8

“Ask what you wish, Kassapa.” ^sn12-17-2-9

“Well, worthy Gotama, is suffering made by oneself?” ^sn12-17-3-1

“Not so, Kassapa,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-17-3-2

“Then is suffering made by another?” ^sn12-17-3-3

“Not so, Kassapa,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-17-3-4

“Well, is suffering made by both oneself and another?” ^sn12-17-3-5

“Not so, Kassapa,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-17-3-6

“Then does suffering arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-17-3-7

“Not so, Kassapa,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-17-3-8

“Well, is there no such thing as suffering?” ^sn12-17-3-9

“It’s not that there’s no such thing as suffering. ^sn12-17-3-10

Suffering is real.” ^sn12-17-3-11

“Then does the worthy Gotama not know or see suffering?” ^sn12-17-3-12

“It’s not that I don’t know or see suffering. ^sn12-17-3-13

I do know suffering, ^sn12-17-3-14

I do see suffering.” ^sn12-17-3-15

“Worthy Gotama, when asked these questions, you say ‘not so’. ^sn12-17-4-1

Yet you say that there is such a thing as suffering. ^sn12-17-4-5

And you say that you do know suffering, ^sn12-17-4-7

and you do see suffering. ^sn12-17-4-8

Sir, let the Blessed One explain suffering to me! ^sn12-17-4-9

Let the Blessed One teach me about suffering!” ^sn12-17-4-10

“Suppose that he who does the deed and he who experiences the result are one and the same. Then for one who has existed since the beginning, suffering is made by oneself. In speaking like this, one implies this is eternal. ^sn12-17-5-1

Suppose that he who does the deed is one, and he who experiences the result is another. Then for one stricken by feeling, suffering is made by another. In speaking like this, one implies this is annihilated. ^sn12-17-5-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-17-5-3

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-17-5-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-17-5-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-17-5-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-17-5-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-17-5-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-17-5-9

When this was said, Kassapa said to the Buddha, ^sn12-17-6-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^sn12-17-6-2

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn12-17-6-3

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn12-17-6-4

Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?” ^sn12-17-6-5

“Kassapa, if someone formerly ordained in another sect wishes to take the going forth, the ordination in this teaching and training, they must spend four months on probation. When four months have passed, if the mendicants are satisfied, they’ll give the going forth, the ordination into monkhood. ^sn12-17-7-1

However, I have recognized individual differences.” ^sn12-17-7-2

“Sir, if four months probation are required in such a case, I’ll spend four years on probation. When four years have passed, if the mendicants are satisfied, let them give me the going forth, the ordination into monkhood.” ^sn12-17-8-1

And the naked ascetic Kassapa received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^sn12-17-9-1

Not long after his ordination, Venerable Kassapa, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn12-17-9-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn12-17-9-3

And Venerable Kassapa became one of the perfected. ^sn12-17-9-4

### With Timbaruka (Linked Discourses 12.18) ^sn12-18-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-18-1-1

Then the wanderer Timbaruka went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-18-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^sn12-18-1-3

“Well, worthy Gotama, are pleasure and pain made by oneself?” ^sn12-18-2-1

“Not so, Timbaruka,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-18-2-2

“Then are pleasure and pain made by another?” ^sn12-18-2-3

“Not so, Timbaruka,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-18-2-4

“Well, are pleasure and pain made by both oneself and another?” ^sn12-18-2-5

“Not so, Timbaruka,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-18-2-6

“Then do pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-18-2-7

“Not so, Timbaruka,” said the Buddha. ^sn12-18-2-8

“Well, is there no such thing as pleasure and pain?” ^sn12-18-2-9

“It’s not that there’s no such thing as pleasure and pain. ^sn12-18-2-10

Pleasure and pain are real.” ^sn12-18-2-11

“Then does the worthy Gotama not know or see suffering?” ^sn12-18-2-12

“It’s not that I don’t know or see pleasure and pain. ^sn12-18-2-13

I do know pleasure and pain, ^sn12-18-2-14

I do see pleasure and pain.” ^sn12-18-2-15

“Worthy Gotama, when asked these questions, you say ‘not so’. ^sn12-18-3-1

Yet you say that there is such a thing as pleasure and pain. ^sn12-18-3-5

And you say that you do know pleasure and pain, ^sn12-18-3-8

and you do see pleasure and pain. ^sn12-18-3-9

Sir, explain pleasure and pain to me! ^sn12-18-3-10

Teach me about pleasure and pain!” ^sn12-18-3-11

“Suppose that the feeling and the one who feels it are one and the same. Then for one who has existed since the beginning, pleasure and pain is made by oneself. I don’t say this. ^sn12-18-4-1

Suppose that the feeling is one thing and the one who feels it is another. Then for one stricken by feeling, pleasure and pain is made by another. I don’t say this. ^sn12-18-4-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-18-4-3

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-18-4-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-18-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-18-4-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-18-4-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-18-4-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-18-4-9

When he said this, the wanderer Timbaruka said to the Buddha, ^sn12-18-5-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^sn12-18-5-2

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn12-18-5-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn12-18-5-4

### The Astute and the Foolish (Linked Discourses 12.19) ^sn12-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-19-1-1

“Mendicants, for a fool shrouded by ignorance and coupled to craving, this body has been produced. ^sn12-19-1-2

So there is this duality: this body and external name and form. Contact depends on this duality. When contacted through one or other of the six sense fields, the fool experiences pleasure and pain. ^sn12-19-1-3

For an astute person shrouded by ignorance and coupled to craving, this body has been produced. ^sn12-19-2-1

So there is this duality: this body and external name and form. Contact depends on this duality. When contacted through one or other of the six sense fields, the astute person experiences pleasure and pain. ^sn12-19-2-2

What, then, is the difference between the foolish and the astute?” ^sn12-19-3-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn12-19-3-2

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-19-4-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-19-4-2

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-19-4-3

“For a fool shrouded by ignorance and coupled to craving, this body has been produced. But the fool has not given up that ignorance or finished that craving. ^sn12-19-5-1

Why is that? ^sn12-19-5-2

The fool has not completed the spiritual journey for the complete ending of suffering. ^sn12-19-5-3

Therefore, when their body breaks up, the fool is reborn in another body. ^sn12-19-5-4

When they have passed on to another body, they’re not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-19-5-5

They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-19-5-6

For an astute person shrouded by ignorance and coupled to craving, this body has been produced. But the astute person has given up that ignorance and finished that craving. ^sn12-19-6-1

Why is that? ^sn12-19-6-2

The astute person has completed the spiritual journey for the complete ending of suffering. ^sn12-19-6-3

Therefore, when their body breaks up, the astute person is not reborn in another body. ^sn12-19-6-4

Not passing on to another body, they’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-19-6-5

They’re freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-19-6-6

This is the difference here between the foolish and the astute, that is, leading the spiritual life.” ^sn12-19-6-7

### Conditions (Linked Discourses 12.20) ^sn12-20-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-20-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you dependent origination and dependently originated phenomena. ^sn12-20-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-20-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-20-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-20-1-5

“And what is dependent origination? ^sn12-20-2-1

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death. ^sn12-20-2-2

Whether Realized Ones arise or not, this law of nature persists, this regularity of natural principles, this surety of natural principles, specific conditionality. ^sn12-20-2-3

A Realized One awakens to this and comprehends it, ^sn12-20-2-4

then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it. ^sn12-20-2-5

‘Look,’ he says, ^sn12-20-2-6

‘Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’ ^sn12-20-2-7

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth … ^sn12-20-3-1

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence … ^sn12-20-3-2

Craving is a requirement for grasping … ^sn12-20-3-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving … ^sn12-20-3-4

Contact is a requirement for feeling … ^sn12-20-3-5

The six sense fields are requirements for contact … ^sn12-20-3-6

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields … ^sn12-20-3-7

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form … ^sn12-20-3-8

Choices are a requirement for consciousness … ^sn12-20-3-9

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-20-3-10

Whether Realized Ones arise or not, this law of nature persists, this regularity of natural principles, this surety of natural principles, specific conditionality. ^sn12-20-3-11

A Realized One awakens to this and comprehends it, ^sn12-20-3-12

then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it. ^sn12-20-3-13

‘Look,’ he says, ^sn12-20-3-14

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices.’ ^sn12-20-3-15

So the fact that this is real, not unreal, not otherwise; the specific conditionality of it: ^sn12-20-3-16

this is called dependent origination. ^sn12-20-3-17

And what are the dependently originated phenomena? ^sn12-20-4-1

Old age and death are impermanent, conditioned, dependently originated, liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn12-20-4-2

Rebirth … ^sn12-20-4-3

Continued existence … ^sn12-20-4-4

Grasping … ^sn12-20-4-5

Craving … ^sn12-20-4-6

Feeling … ^sn12-20-4-7

Contact … ^sn12-20-4-8

The six sense fields … ^sn12-20-4-9

Name and form … ^sn12-20-4-10

Consciousness … ^sn12-20-4-11

Choices … ^sn12-20-4-12

Ignorance is impermanent, conditioned, dependently originated, liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn12-20-4-13

These are called the dependently originated phenomena. ^sn12-20-4-14

When a noble disciple has clearly seen with right wisdom this dependent origination and these dependently originated phenomena as they are, it will be quite impossible for them to turn back to the first beginning, thinking: ^sn12-20-5-1

‘Did I exist in the past? Did I not exist in the past? What was I in the past? How was I in the past? After being what, what did I become in the past?’ ^sn12-20-5-2

Or to turn forward to the final end, thinking: ^sn12-20-5-3

‘Will I exist in the future? Will I not exist in the future? What will I be in the future? How will I be in the future? After being what, what will I become in the future?’ ^sn12-20-5-4

Or to be undecided about the present, thinking: ^sn12-20-5-5

‘Am I? Am I not? What am I? How am I? This sentient being—where did it come from? And where will it go?’ ^sn12-20-5-6

Why is that? ^sn12-20-5-8

Because that noble disciple has clearly seen with right wisdom this dependent origination and these dependently originated phenomena as they are.” ^sn12-20-5-9

### The Ten Powers (Linked Discourses 12.21) ^sn12-21-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-21-1-1

“Mendicants, a Realized One has ten powers and four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^sn12-21-1-2

Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn12-21-1-3

Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the disappearance of feeling. ^sn12-21-1-4

Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the disappearance of perception. ^sn12-21-1-5

Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the disappearance of choices. ^sn12-21-1-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness. ^sn12-21-1-7

When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-21-1-8

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^sn12-21-1-9

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-21-1-10

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-21-1-11

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-21-1-12

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-21-1-13

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-21-1-14

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-21-1-15

### The Ten Powers (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.22) ^sn12-22-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-22-1-1

“Mendicants, a Realized One has ten powers and four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^sn12-22-1-2

Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn12-22-1-3

Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the ending of feeling. ^sn12-22-1-4

Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the ending of perception. ^sn12-22-1-5

Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the ending of choices. ^sn12-22-1-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness. ^sn12-22-1-7

When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-22-1-8

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^sn12-22-1-9

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-22-1-10

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-22-1-11

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-22-1-12

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-22-1-13

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-22-1-14

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-22-1-15

The teaching well-explained thus by me is clarified, revealed, illuminated, and stripped of patchwork. ^sn12-22-2-1

In this teaching thus well-explained, it is quite enough for a gentleman who has gone forth out of faith to rouse up energy. ^sn12-22-2-2

‘Gladly, let only skin, sinews, and tendons remain! Let the flesh and blood waste away in my body! I will not stop trying until I have achieved what is possible by human strength, energy, and vigor.’ ^sn12-22-2-3

A lazy person lives in suffering, mixed up with bad, unskillful qualities, neglecting their heart’s great goal. ^sn12-22-3-1

But an energetic person lives happily, secluded from bad, unskillful qualities, fulfilling their heart’s great goal. ^sn12-22-3-2

The best isn’t reached by the worst. ^sn12-22-3-3

The best is reached by the best. ^sn12-22-3-4

This spiritual life is the cream, mendicants, and the Teacher is before you. ^sn12-22-3-5

So you should rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized, thinking: ^sn12-22-3-6

‘In this way our going forth will not be barren, but will be fruitful and fertile. ^sn12-22-3-7

And the services of those whose robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick we enjoy shall be very fruitful and beneficial for them.’ ^sn12-22-3-8

That’s how you should train. ^sn12-22-3-9

Considering what is good for yourself, mendicants, is quite enough for you to persist with diligence. ^sn12-22-3-10

Considering what is good for others is quite enough for you to persist with diligence. ^sn12-22-3-11

Considering what is good for both is quite enough for you to persist with diligence.” ^sn12-22-3-12

### Vital Conditions (Linked Discourses 12.23) ^sn12-23-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-23-1-1

“Mendicants, I say that the ending of defilements is for one who knows and sees, not for one who does not know or see. ^sn12-23-1-2

For one who knows and sees what? ^sn12-23-1-3

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn12-23-1-4

Such is feeling … ^sn12-23-1-5

Such is perception … ^sn12-23-1-6

Such are choices … ^sn12-23-1-7

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^sn12-23-1-8

The ending of the defilements is for one who knows and sees this. ^sn12-23-1-9

I say that this knowledge of ending has a vital condition, it doesn’t lack a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-1

And what is it? ^sn12-23-2-2

You should say: ‘Freedom.’ ^sn12-23-2-3

I say that freedom has a vital condition, it doesn’t lack a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-4

And what is it? ^sn12-23-2-5

You should say: ‘Dispassion.’ ^sn12-23-2-6

I say that dispassion has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-7

And what is it? ^sn12-23-2-8

You should say: ‘Disillusionment.’ ^sn12-23-2-9

I say that disillusionment has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-10

And what is it? ^sn12-23-2-11

You should say: ‘Truly knowing and seeing.’ ^sn12-23-2-12

I say that truly knowing and seeing has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-13

And what is it? ^sn12-23-2-14

You should say: ‘Immersion.’ ^sn12-23-2-15

I say that immersion has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-2-16

And what is it? ^sn12-23-3-1

You should say: ‘Bliss.’ ^sn12-23-3-2

I say that bliss has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-3-3

And what is it? ^sn12-23-3-4

You should say: ‘Tranquility.’ ^sn12-23-3-5

I say that tranquility has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-3-6

And what is it? ^sn12-23-3-7

You should say: ‘Rapture.’ ^sn12-23-3-8

I say that rapture has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-3-9

And what is it? ^sn12-23-3-10

You should say: ‘Joy.’ ^sn12-23-3-11

I say that joy has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-3-12

And what is it? ^sn12-23-3-13

You should say: ‘Faith.’ ^sn12-23-3-14

I say that faith has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-3-15

And what is it? ^sn12-23-4-1

You should say: ‘Suffering.’ ^sn12-23-4-2

I say that suffering has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-4-3

And what is it? ^sn12-23-4-4

You should say: ‘Rebirth.’ ^sn12-23-4-5

I say that rebirth has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-4-6

And what is it? ^sn12-23-4-7

You should say: ‘Continued existence.’ ^sn12-23-4-8

I say that continued existence has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-4-9

And what is it? ^sn12-23-4-10

You should say: ‘Grasping.’ ^sn12-23-4-11

I say that grasping has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-4-12

And what is it? ^sn12-23-4-13

You should say: ‘Craving.’ ^sn12-23-4-14

I say that craving has a vital condition. ^sn12-23-4-15

And what is it? ^sn12-23-5-1

You should say: ‘Feeling.’ … ^sn12-23-5-2

You should say: ‘Contact.’ … ^sn12-23-5-3

You should say: ‘The six sense fields.’ … ^sn12-23-5-4

You should say: ‘Name and form.’ … ^sn12-23-5-5

You should say: ‘Consciousness.’ … ^sn12-23-5-6

You should say: ‘Choices.’ … ^sn12-23-5-7

I say that choices have a vital condition, they don’t lack a vital condition. ^sn12-23-5-8

And what is the vital condition for choices? ^sn12-23-5-9

You should say: ‘Ignorance.’ ^sn12-23-5-10

So, mendicants: ignorance is the vital condition for choices. ^sn12-23-6-1

Choices are the vital condition for consciousness. ^sn12-23-6-2

Consciousness is the vital condition for name and form. ^sn12-23-6-3

Name and form are the vital conditions for the six sense fields. ^sn12-23-6-4

The six sense fields are the vital conditions for contact. ^sn12-23-6-5

Contact is the vital condition for feeling. ^sn12-23-6-6

Feeling is the vital condition for craving. ^sn12-23-6-7

Craving is the vital condition for grasping. ^sn12-23-6-8

Grasping is the vital condition for continued existence. ^sn12-23-6-9

Continued existence is the vital condition for rebirth. ^sn12-23-6-10

Rebirth is the vital condition for suffering. ^sn12-23-6-11

Suffering is the vital condition for faith. ^sn12-23-6-12

Faith is the vital condition for joy. ^sn12-23-6-13

Joy is the vital condition for rapture. ^sn12-23-6-14

Rapture is the vital condition for tranquility. ^sn12-23-6-15

Tranquility is the vital condition for bliss. ^sn12-23-6-16

Bliss is the vital condition for immersion. ^sn12-23-6-17

Immersion is the vital condition for truly knowing and seeing. ^sn12-23-6-18

Truly knowing and seeing is the vital condition for disillusionment. ^sn12-23-6-19

Disillusionment is the vital condition for dispassion. ^sn12-23-6-20

Dispassion is the vital condition for freedom. ^sn12-23-6-21

Freedom is the vital condition for the knowledge of ending. ^sn12-23-6-22

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^sn12-23-7-1

In the same way, ignorance is the vital condition for choices. … Freedom is the vital condition for the knowledge of ending.” ^sn12-23-8-1

### Followers of Other Religions (Linked Discourses 12.24) ^sn12-24-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn12-24-1-1

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms. ^sn12-24-1-2

Then it occurred to him, ^sn12-24-1-3

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Rājagaha. ^sn12-24-1-4

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^sn12-24-1-5

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^sn12-24-2-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The wanderers said to him: ^sn12-24-2-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, there are ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. Some of them declare that suffering is made by oneself. ^sn12-24-3-1

Some of them declare that suffering is made by another. ^sn12-24-3-2

Some of them declare that suffering is made by both oneself and another. ^sn12-24-3-3

Some of them declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-24-3-4

What does the ascetic Gotama say about this? How does he explain it? ^sn12-24-3-5

How should we answer so as to repeat what the ascetic Gotama has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn12-24-3-6

“Reverends, the Buddha has said that suffering is dependently originated. ^sn12-24-4-1

Dependent on what? ^sn12-24-4-2

Dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-4-3

In speaking like this, you would repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You would explain in line with his teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism. ^sn12-24-4-4

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-5-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-5-2

In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself and another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-5-3

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-5-4

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. ^sn12-24-6-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. ^sn12-24-6-2

In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself and another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. ^sn12-24-6-3

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.” ^sn12-24-6-4

Venerable Ānanda heard this discussion between Venerable Sāriputta and those wanderers of other religions. ^sn12-24-7-1

Then Ānanda wandered for alms in Rājagaha. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^sn12-24-7-2

and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^sn12-24-7-3

“Good, good, Ānanda! It’s just as Sāriputta has so rightly explained. ^sn12-24-8-1

I have said that suffering is dependently originated. ^sn12-24-8-2

Dependent on what? ^sn12-24-8-3

Dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-8-4

Saying this you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism. ^sn12-24-8-5

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. … ^sn12-24-9-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-24-9-4

In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. … ^sn12-24-10-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. ^sn12-24-10-4

Ānanda, this one time I was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn12-24-11-1

Then I robed up in the morning and, taking my bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms. ^sn12-24-11-2

Then I thought: ^sn12-24-11-3

‘It’s too early to wander for alms in Rājagaha. ^sn12-24-11-4

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?’ ^sn12-24-11-5

Then I went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^sn12-24-12-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, I sat down to one side. …” ^sn12-24-12-2

(The wanderers asked the Buddha the very same questions, and he gave the same answers.) ^sn12-24-13-1

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^sn12-24-17-1

how the whole matter is stated with one phrase. ^sn12-24-17-2

Could there be a detailed explanation of this matter that is both deep and appears deep?” ^sn12-24-17-3

“Well then, Ānanda, clarify this matter yourself.” ^sn12-24-18-1

“Sir, suppose they were to ask me: ^sn12-24-18-2

‘Reverend Ānanda, what is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of old age and death?’ ^sn12-24-18-3

I’d answer like this: ^sn12-24-18-4

‘Reverends, rebirth is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of old age and death.’ ^sn12-24-18-5

That’s how I’d answer such a question. ^sn12-24-18-6

Suppose they were to ask me: ^sn12-24-19-1

‘What is the source of rebirth?’ ^sn12-24-19-2

I’d answer like this: ^sn12-24-19-3

‘Continued existence is the source of rebirth.’ ^sn12-24-19-4

That’s how I’d answer such a question. ^sn12-24-19-5

Suppose they were to ask me: ^sn12-24-20-1

‘What is the source of continued existence?’ ^sn12-24-20-2

I’d answer like this: ^sn12-24-20-3

‘Grasping is the source of continued existence.’ ^sn12-24-20-4

That’s how I’d answer such a question. ^sn12-24-20-5

Suppose they were to ask me: ^sn12-24-21-1

‘What is the source of grasping?’ … ^sn12-24-21-2

craving … ^sn12-24-21-3

feeling … ^sn12-24-21-4

Suppose they were to ask me: ^sn12-24-21-5

‘What is the source of contact?’ ^sn12-24-21-6

I’d answer like this: ^sn12-24-21-7

‘The six sense fields are the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of contact.’ ^sn12-24-21-8

‘When the six fields of contact fade away and cease no residue left behind, contact ceases. ^sn12-24-21-9

When contact ceases, feeling ceases. ^sn12-24-21-10

When feeling ceases, craving ceases. ^sn12-24-21-11

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-24-21-12

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-24-21-13

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-24-21-14

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-24-21-15

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’ ^sn12-24-21-16

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-24-21-17

### With Bhūmija (Linked Discourses 12.25) ^sn12-25-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-25-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Bhūmija came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-25-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him: ^sn12-25-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, there are ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself. ^sn12-25-2-1

Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by another. ^sn12-25-2-2

Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by both oneself and another. ^sn12-25-2-3

Some of them declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-25-2-4

What does the Buddha say about this? How does he explain it? ^sn12-25-2-5

How should we answer so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn12-25-2-6

“Reverend, the Buddha has said that pleasure and pain are dependently originated. ^sn12-25-3-1

Dependent on what? ^sn12-25-3-2

Dependent on contact. ^sn12-25-3-3

In speaking like this, you would repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You would explain in line with his teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism. ^sn12-25-3-4

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. … ^sn12-25-4-1

In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-25-4-4

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. … ^sn12-25-5-1

In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.” ^sn12-25-5-4

Venerable Ānanda heard this discussion between Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Bhūmija. ^sn12-25-6-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^sn12-25-6-2

and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^sn12-25-6-3

“Good, good, Ānanda! It’s just as Sāriputta has so rightly explained. ^sn12-25-7-1

I have said that pleasure and pain are dependently originated. ^sn12-25-7-2

Dependent on what? ^sn12-25-7-3

Dependent on contact. ^sn12-25-7-4

In speaking like this, you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism. ^sn12-25-7-5

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. … ^sn12-25-8-1

In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-25-8-4

Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. … ^sn12-25-9-1

In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. ^sn12-25-9-4

Ānanda, as long as there’s a body, the intention that gives rise to bodily action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. ^sn12-25-10-1

As long as there’s a voice, the intention that gives rise to verbal action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. ^sn12-25-10-2

As long as there’s a mind, the intention that gives rise to mental action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. But this is only when dependent on ignorance. ^sn12-25-10-3

By oneself one instigates the choice that gives rise to bodily, verbal, and mental action, conditioned by which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^sn12-25-11-1

Or else others instigate the choice … ^sn12-25-11-2

One instigates the choice after deliberation … ^sn12-25-11-3

Or else one instigates the choice without deliberation … ^sn12-25-11-4

Ignorance is included in all these things. ^sn12-25-14-1

But when ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, that body, that voice, and that mind no longer exist dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^sn12-25-14-2

That field does not exist, that ground does not exist, that basis does not exist, that foundation does not exist, dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself.” ^sn12-25-14-3

### With Upavāṇa (Linked Discourses 12.26) ^sn12-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-26-1-1

Then Venerable Upavāṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn12-26-1-2

“Sir, there are some ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself. ^sn12-26-2-1

There are some who declare that suffering is made by another. ^sn12-26-2-2

There are some who declare that suffering is made by both oneself and another. ^sn12-26-2-3

There are some who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-26-2-4

What does the Buddha say about this? How does he explain it? How should we answer so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn12-26-2-5

“Upavāṇa, I have said that suffering is dependently originated. ^sn12-26-3-1

Dependent on what? ^sn12-26-3-2

Dependent on contact. ^sn12-26-3-3

In speaking like this, you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism. ^sn12-26-3-4

In the case of those ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. … ^sn12-26-4-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact. ^sn12-26-4-4

In the case of those ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. … ^sn12-26-5-1

In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.” ^sn12-26-5-4

### Dependency (Linked Discourses 12.27) ^sn12-27-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-27-1-1

“Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-27-1-2

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-27-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-27-1-4

And what is old age and death? ^sn12-27-2-1

The old age, decrepitude, broken teeth, grey hair, wrinkly skin, diminished vitality, and failing faculties of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings. ^sn12-27-2-2

This is called old age. ^sn12-27-2-3

The passing away, passing on, disintegration, demise, mortality, death, decease, breaking up of the aggregates, and laying to rest of the corpse of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings. ^sn12-27-2-4

This is called death. ^sn12-27-2-5

Such is old age, and such is death. ^sn12-27-2-6

This is called old age and death. ^sn12-27-2-7

Rebirth is the origin of old age and death. ^sn12-27-2-8

When rebirth ceases, old age and death cease. ^sn12-27-2-9

The practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death is simply this noble eightfold path, ^sn12-27-2-10

that is: right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn12-27-2-11

And what is rebirth? … ^sn12-27-3-1

And what is continued existence? … ^sn12-27-3-2

And what is grasping? … ^sn12-27-3-3

And what is craving? … ^sn12-27-3-4

And what is feeling? … ^sn12-27-3-5

And what is contact? … ^sn12-27-3-6

And what are the six sense fields? … ^sn12-27-3-7

And what are name and form? … ^sn12-27-3-8

And what is consciousness? … ^sn12-27-3-9

And what are choices? ^sn12-27-4-1

There are three kinds of choices. ^sn12-27-4-2

Choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn12-27-4-3

These are called choices. ^sn12-27-4-4

Ignorance is the origin of choices. ^sn12-27-4-5

When ignorance ceases, choices cease. ^sn12-27-4-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path, ^sn12-27-4-7

that is: right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn12-27-4-8

When a noble disciple thus understands dependency, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation, ^sn12-27-5-1

that noble disciple is called: ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.” ^sn12-27-5-2

continued existence … ^sn12-28-5-2

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Linked Discourses 12.29) ^sn12-29-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-29-1-1

“There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t completely understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. They don’t completely understand rebirth … ^sn12-29-1-3

continued existence … ^sn12-29-1-4

grasping … ^sn12-29-1-5

craving … ^sn12-29-1-6

feeling … ^sn12-29-1-7

contact … ^sn12-29-1-8

the six sense fields … ^sn12-29-1-9

name and form … ^sn12-29-1-10

consciousness … ^sn12-29-1-11

They don’t completely understand choices, ^sn12-29-1-12

their origin, ^sn12-29-1-13

their cessation, ^sn12-29-1-14

and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-29-1-15

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn12-29-1-16

There are ascetics and brahmins who completely understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. They completely understand rebirth … ^sn12-29-2-1

continued existence … ^sn12-29-2-2

grasping … ^sn12-29-2-3

craving … ^sn12-29-2-4

feeling … ^sn12-29-2-5

contact … ^sn12-29-2-6

the six sense fields … ^sn12-29-2-7

name and form … ^sn12-29-2-8

consciousness … ^sn12-29-2-9

They completely understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-29-2-10

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn12-29-2-11

### Ascetics and Brahmins (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.30) ^sn12-30-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-30-1-1

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. It’s impossible that they will abide having transcended old age and death. ^sn12-30-1-3

They don’t understand rebirth … ^sn12-30-1-4

continued existence … ^sn12-30-1-5

grasping … ^sn12-30-1-6

craving … ^sn12-30-1-7

feeling … ^sn12-30-1-8

contact … ^sn12-30-1-9

the six sense fields … ^sn12-30-1-10

name and form … ^sn12-30-1-11

consciousness … ^sn12-30-1-12

They don’t understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. It’s impossible that they will abide having transcended choices. ^sn12-30-1-13

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. It’s possible that they will abide having transcended old age and death. ^sn12-30-2-1

They understand rebirth … ^sn12-30-2-2

continued existence … ^sn12-30-2-3

grasping … ^sn12-30-2-4

craving … ^sn12-30-2-5

feeling … ^sn12-30-2-6

contact … ^sn12-30-2-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-30-2-8

name and form … ^sn12-30-2-9

consciousness … ^sn12-30-2-10

They understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-30-2-11

### What Has Come to Be (Linked Discourses 12.31) ^sn12-31-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī. ^sn12-31-1-1

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Sāriputta, ^sn12-31-1-2

“Sāriputta, this was said in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Ajita’: ^sn12-31-1-3

‘There are those who have appraised the teaching, ^sn12-31-2-1

and many kinds of trainees here. ^sn12-31-2-2

Now you, being alert, are asked ^sn12-31-2-3

to tell me their behavior, good fellow.’ ^sn12-31-2-4

How should we see the detailed meaning of this brief statement?” ^sn12-31-3-1

When he said this, Sāriputta kept silent. ^sn12-31-3-2

For a second time … ^sn12-31-4-1

For a third time … ^sn12-31-4-3

Sāriputta kept silent. ^sn12-31-6-2

“Sāriputta, do you see that this has come to be?” ^sn12-31-7-1

“Sir, one truly sees with right wisdom that this has come to be. ^sn12-31-7-2

Seeing this, one is practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding what has come to be. ^sn12-31-7-3

One truly sees with right wisdom that it originated with that as fuel. ^sn12-31-7-4

Seeing this, one is practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the fuel for its origination. ^sn12-31-7-5

One truly sees with right wisdom that when that fuel ceases, what has come to be is liable to cease. ^sn12-31-7-6

Seeing this, one is practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding what is liable to cease. ^sn12-31-7-7

In this way one is a trainee. ^sn12-31-7-8

And how, sir, has one appraised the teaching? ^sn12-31-8-1

Sir, one truly sees with right wisdom that this has come to be. ^sn12-31-8-2

Seeing this, one is freed by not grasping through disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding what has come to be. ^sn12-31-8-3

One truly sees with right wisdom that it originated with that as fuel. ^sn12-31-8-4

Seeing this, one is freed by not grasping through disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the fuel for its origination. ^sn12-31-8-5

One truly sees with right wisdom that when that fuel ceases, what has come to be is liable to cease. ^sn12-31-8-6

Seeing this, one is freed by not grasping through disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding what is liable to cease. ^sn12-31-8-7

In this way one has appraised the teaching. ^sn12-31-8-8

Sir, regarding what was said in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Ajita’: ^sn12-31-8-9

‘There are those who have appraised the teaching, ^sn12-31-9-1

and many kinds of trainees here. ^sn12-31-9-2

Now you, being alert, are asked ^sn12-31-9-3

to tell me their behavior, good fellow.’ ^sn12-31-9-4

This is how I understand the detailed meaning of what was said in brief.” ^sn12-31-10-1

“Good, good, Sāriputta! (The Buddha repeated all of Sāriputta’s explanation, concluding:) ^sn12-31-11-1

This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what was said in brief.” ^sn12-31-14-1

### With Kaḷāra the Aristocrat (Linked Discourses 12.32) ^sn12-32-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-32-1-1

Then the mendicant Kaḷāra the Aristocrat went up to Venerable Sāriputta and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-32-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him, ^sn12-32-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, the mendicant Phagguna of the Top-Knot has resigned the training and returned to a lesser life.” ^sn12-32-1-4

“That venerable mustn’t have gained any solace in this teaching and training.” ^sn12-32-1-5

“Well then, has Venerable Sāriputta found solace in this teaching and training?” ^sn12-32-2-1

“Reverend, I have no uncertainty.” ^sn12-32-3-1

“But what of the future?” ^sn12-32-3-2

“I have no doubt.” ^sn12-32-4-1

Then Kaḷāra the Aristocrat went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn12-32-5-1

“Sir, Venerable Sāriputta has declared enlightenment: ^sn12-32-5-2

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’” ^sn12-32-5-3

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^sn12-32-6-1

“Please, monk, in my name tell Sāriputta that ^sn12-32-6-2

the teacher summons him.” ^sn12-32-6-3

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to Sāriputta and said to him, ^sn12-32-6-4

“Reverend Sāriputta, the teacher summons you.” ^sn12-32-6-5

“Yes, reverend,” replied Sāriputta. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^sn12-32-6-6

“Sāriputta, is it really true that you have declared enlightenment: ^sn12-32-6-7

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place”’?” ^sn12-32-6-8

“Sir, I did not state the matter in these words and phrases.” ^sn12-32-6-9

“Sāriputta, no matter how a gentleman declares enlightenment, what he has declared should be regarded as such.” ^sn12-32-6-10

“Sir, did I not also say that ^sn12-32-6-11

I did not state the matter in these words and phrases?” ^sn12-32-6-12

“Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-7-1

‘But Reverend Sāriputta, how have you known and seen so that you’ve declared enlightenment: ^sn12-32-7-2

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’ ^sn12-32-7-3

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-7-4

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-8-1

I would answer: ^sn12-32-8-4

‘Reverends, when the source of rebirth ended, I knew “it is ended”. ^sn12-32-8-5

Knowing it is ended, ^sn12-32-8-6

I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^sn12-32-8-7

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-8-8

“But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-9-1

‘But what is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of rebirth?’ ^sn12-32-9-2

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-9-3

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-9-4

I would answer: ^sn12-32-9-6

‘Continued existence is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of rebirth.’ ^sn12-32-9-7

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-9-8

“But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-10-1

‘What is the source of continued existence?’ ^sn12-32-10-2

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-10-3

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-10-4

I’d answer: ^sn12-32-10-6

‘Grasping is the source of continued existence.’ ^sn12-32-10-7

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-10-8

“But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-11-1

‘What is the source of grasping?’ … ^sn12-32-11-2

But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-11-3

‘What is the source of craving?’ ^sn12-32-11-4

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-11-5

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-11-6

I’d answer: ^sn12-32-11-8

‘Feeling is the source of craving.’ ^sn12-32-11-9

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-11-10

“But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-12-1

‘But how have you known and seen so that the relishing of feelings is no longer present?’ ^sn12-32-12-2

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-12-3

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-12-4

I’d answer: ^sn12-32-12-5

‘Reverends, there are three feelings. ^sn12-32-12-6

What three? ^sn12-32-12-7

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn12-32-12-8

These three feelings are impermanent, ^sn12-32-12-9

and what’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn12-32-12-10

When I understood this, the relishing of feelings was no longer present.’ ^sn12-32-12-11

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-12-12

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^sn12-32-13-1

The same point may also be briefly explained in this way: ^sn12-32-13-2

‘Suffering includes whatever is felt.’ ^sn12-32-13-3

But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn12-32-14-1

‘But Reverend, how have you been liberated that you declare enlightenment: ^sn12-32-14-2

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”?’ ^sn12-32-14-3

How would you answer?” ^sn12-32-14-4

“Sir, if they were to ask me this, ^sn12-32-14-5

I’d answer: ^sn12-32-14-8

‘Because of an inner release with the ending of all grasping, I live mindfully so that defilements don’t defile me and I don’t look down on myself.’ ^sn12-32-14-9

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn12-32-14-10

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^sn12-32-15-1

The same point may also be briefly explained in this way: ^sn12-32-15-2

‘I have no uncertainty regarding the defilements spoken of by the ascetic. I have no doubt that I’ve given them up.’” ^sn12-32-15-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn12-32-15-4

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^sn12-32-15-5

Then soon after the Buddha left, Venerable Sāriputta said to the mendicants, ^sn12-32-16-1

“Reverends, the Buddha asked me the first question without letting me know beforehand, so I hesitated. ^sn12-32-16-2

But when the Buddha agreed with my answer, I thought: ^sn12-32-16-3

‘If the Buddha were to question me all day on this matter in different words and ways, I could answer all day with different words and ways. ^sn12-32-16-4

If he were to question me all night, ^sn12-32-16-5

all day and night, ^sn12-32-16-6

for two days and nights, ^sn12-32-16-7

for three, ^sn12-32-16-9

four, ^sn12-32-16-11

five, ^sn12-32-16-13

six, ^sn12-32-16-15

or seven days and nights, I could answer in different words and ways for seven days and nights.’” ^sn12-32-16-17

Then Kaḷāra the Aristocrat went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn12-32-17-1

“Sir, Venerable Sāriputta has roared his lion’s roar!” ^sn12-32-17-2

(And he told the Buddha all that Sāriputta had said.) ^sn12-32-17-3

“Mendicant, Sāriputta has clearly penetrated the principle of the teachings, so that he could answer any questions I might ask him in different words and ways up to the seventh day and night.” ^sn12-32-18-1

### Grounds for Knowledge (Linked Discourses 12.33) ^sn12-33-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-33-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach forty-four grounds for knowledge. ^sn12-33-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-33-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-33-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-33-1-5

“And what are the forty-four grounds for knowledge? ^sn12-33-2-1

Knowledge of old age and death, knowledge of the origin of old age and death, knowledge of the cessation of old age and death, and knowledge of the practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death. ^sn12-33-2-2

Knowledge of rebirth … ^sn12-33-2-3

Knowledge of continued existence … ^sn12-33-2-4

Knowledge of grasping … ^sn12-33-2-5

Knowledge of craving … ^sn12-33-2-6

Knowledge of feeling … ^sn12-33-2-7

Knowledge of contact … ^sn12-33-2-8

Knowledge of the six sense fields … ^sn12-33-2-9

Knowledge of name and form … ^sn12-33-2-10

Knowledge of consciousness … ^sn12-33-2-11

Knowledge of choices, knowledge of the origin of choices, knowledge of the cessation of choices, and knowledge of the practice that leads to the cessation of choices. ^sn12-33-2-12

These are called the forty-four grounds for knowledge. ^sn12-33-2-13

And what is old age and death? ^sn12-33-3-1

The old age, decrepitude, broken teeth, grey hair, wrinkly skin, diminished vitality, and failing faculties of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings. ^sn12-33-3-2

This is called old age. ^sn12-33-3-3

The passing away, passing on, disintegration, demise, mortality, death, decease, breaking up of the aggregates, and laying to rest of the corpse of the various sentient beings in the various orders of sentient beings. ^sn12-33-3-4

This is called death. ^sn12-33-3-5

Such is old age, and such is death. ^sn12-33-3-6

This is called old age and death. ^sn12-33-3-7

Rebirth is the origin of old age and death. ^sn12-33-4-1

When rebirth ceases, old age and death cease. ^sn12-33-4-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn12-33-4-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn12-33-4-4

A noble disciple understands old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. This is their knowledge of the teaching. ^sn12-33-5-1

With this teaching that is seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, they apply the method to the past and future. ^sn12-33-5-2

Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the past directly knew old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them directly knew these things in exactly the same way that I do now. ^sn12-33-6-1

Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the future will directly know old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them will directly know these things in exactly the same way that I do now. ^sn12-33-7-1

This is their inferential knowledge. ^sn12-33-7-2

A noble disciple has purified and cleansed these two knowledges— ^sn12-33-8-1

knowledge of the teaching and inferential knowledge. ^sn12-33-8-2

When a noble disciple has done this, they’re one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’. ^sn12-33-8-3

And what is rebirth? … ^sn12-33-9-1

And what is continued existence? … ^sn12-33-9-2

And what is grasping? … ^sn12-33-9-3

And what is craving? … ^sn12-33-9-4

And what is feeling? … ^sn12-33-9-5

And what is contact? … ^sn12-33-9-6

And what are the six sense fields? … ^sn12-33-9-7

And what are name and form? … ^sn12-33-9-8

And what is consciousness? … ^sn12-33-9-9

And what are choices? ^sn12-33-9-10

There are three kinds of choices. ^sn12-33-9-11

Choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn12-33-9-12

These are called choices. ^sn12-33-9-13

Ignorance is the origin of choices. ^sn12-33-10-1

When ignorance ceases, choices cease. ^sn12-33-10-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn12-33-10-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn12-33-10-4

A noble disciple understands choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. This is their knowledge of the teaching. ^sn12-33-11-1

With this teaching that is seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, they apply the method to the past and future. ^sn12-33-11-2

Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the past directly knew choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them directly knew these things in exactly the same way that I do now. ^sn12-33-12-1

Whatever ascetics and brahmins in the future will directly know choices, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation, all of them will directly know these things in exactly the same way that I do now. ^sn12-33-13-1

This is their inferential knowledge. ^sn12-33-13-2

A noble disciple has purified and cleansed these two knowledges— ^sn12-33-14-1

knowledge of the teaching and inferential knowledge. ^sn12-33-14-2

When a noble disciple has done this, they’re one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.” ^sn12-33-14-3

### Grounds for Knowledge (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.34) ^sn12-34-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-34-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach seventy-seven grounds for knowledge. ^sn12-34-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-34-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-34-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-34-1-5

“And what are the seventy-seven grounds for knowledge? ^sn12-34-2-1

The knowledge that rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, ^sn12-34-2-2

and the knowledge that when rebirth doesn’t exist, there is no old age and death. ^sn12-34-2-3

Also regarding the past: the knowledge that rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, and the knowledge that when rebirth doesn’t exist, there is no old age and death. ^sn12-34-2-4

Also regarding the future: the knowledge that rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, and the knowledge that when rebirth doesn’t exist, there is no old age and death. ^sn12-34-2-5

And also their knowledge that even this knowledge of the stability of natural principles is liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn12-34-2-6

The knowledge that continued existence is a requirement for rebirth … ^sn12-34-3-1

The knowledge that ignorance is a requirement for choices, and the knowledge that when ignorance doesn’t exist, there are no choices. ^sn12-34-3-10

Also regarding the past: the knowledge that ignorance is a requirement for choices, and the knowledge that when ignorance doesn’t exist, there are no choices. ^sn12-34-3-11

Also regarding the future: the knowledge that ignorance is a requirement for choices, and the knowledge that when ignorance doesn’t exist, there are no choices. ^sn12-34-3-12

And also their knowledge that even this knowledge of the stability of natural principles is liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn12-34-3-13

These are called the seventy-seven grounds for knowledge.” ^sn12-34-3-14

### Ignorance is a Condition (Linked Discourses 12.35) ^sn12-35-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-35-1-1

“Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-35-1-2

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-35-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.” ^sn12-35-1-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha, ^sn12-35-1-5

“What are old age and death, sir, and who do they belong to?” ^sn12-35-1-6

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. “You might say, ‘What are old age and death, and who do they belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Old age and death are one thing, who they belong to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-35-1-7

Mendicant, if you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-1-8

If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-1-9

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-35-1-10

‘Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’” ^sn12-35-1-11

“What is rebirth, sir, and who does it belong to?” ^sn12-35-2-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. “You might say, ‘What is rebirth, and who does it belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Rebirth is one thing, who it belongs to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-35-2-2

Mendicant, if you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-2-3
