If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-2-4

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-35-2-5

‘Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth.’” ^sn12-35-2-6

“What is continued existence, sir, and who is it for?” ^sn12-35-3-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. “You might say, ‘What is continued existence, and who does it belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Continued existence is one thing, who it belongs to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-35-3-2

Mendicant, if you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-3-3

If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-3-4

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-35-3-5

‘Grasping is a requirement for continued existence.’ … ^sn12-35-3-6

‘Craving is a requirement for grasping.’ … ^sn12-35-3-7

‘Feeling is a requirement for craving.’ … ^sn12-35-3-8

‘Contact is a requirement for feeling.’ … ^sn12-35-3-9

‘The six sense fields are requirements for contact.’ … ^sn12-35-3-10

‘Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields.’ … ^sn12-35-3-11

‘Consciousness is a requirement for name and form.’ … ^sn12-35-3-12

‘Choices are a requirement for consciousness.’” ^sn12-35-3-13

“What are choices, sir, and who do they belong to?” ^sn12-35-4-1

“That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. “You might say, ‘What are choices, and who do they belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Choices are one thing, who they belong to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-35-4-2

Mendicant, if you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-4-3

If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-35-4-4

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-35-4-5

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices.’ ^sn12-35-4-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-35-5-1

‘What are old age and death, and who do they belong to?’ or ‘old age and death are one thing, who they belong to is another’, or ‘the soul and the body are one and the same’, or ‘the soul is one thing, the body another.’ ^sn12-35-5-2

These are all cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-35-5-3

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-35-6-1

‘What is rebirth, and who does it belong to?’ or ‘rebirth is one thing, who it belongs to is another’, or ‘the soul and the body are one and the same’, or ‘the soul is one thing, the body another.’ ^sn12-35-6-2

These are all cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-35-6-3

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-35-7-1

‘What is continued existence …’ ^sn12-35-7-2

‘What is grasping …’ ^sn12-35-7-3

‘What is craving …’ ^sn12-35-7-4

‘What is feeling …’ ^sn12-35-7-5

‘What is contact …’ ^sn12-35-7-6

‘What are the six sense fields …’ ^sn12-35-7-7

‘What are name and form …’ ^sn12-35-7-8

‘What is consciousness …’ ^sn12-35-7-9

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-35-8-1

‘What are choices, and who do they belong to?’ or ‘choices are one thing, who they belong to is another’, or ‘the soul and the body are one and the same’, or ‘the soul is one thing, the body another.’ ^sn12-35-8-2

These are all cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn12-35-8-3

### Ignorance is a Condition (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.36) ^sn12-36-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-36-1-1

“Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-36-1-2

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-36-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-36-1-4

Mendicants, you might say, ‘What are old age and death, and who do they belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Old age and death are one thing, who they belong to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-36-2-1

If you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-36-2-2

If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-36-2-3

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-36-2-4

‘Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’ ^sn12-36-2-5

‘What is rebirth …’ ^sn12-36-3-1

‘What is continued existence …’ ^sn12-36-3-2

‘What is grasping …’ ^sn12-36-3-3

‘What is craving …’ ^sn12-36-3-4

‘What is feeling …’ ^sn12-36-3-5

‘What is contact …’ ^sn12-36-3-6

‘What are the six sense fields …’ ^sn12-36-3-7

‘What are name and form …’ ^sn12-36-3-8

‘What is consciousness …’ ^sn12-36-3-9

You might say, ‘What are choices, and who do they belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Choices are one thing, who they belong to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs. ^sn12-36-3-10

If you have the view that the soul and the body are one and the same, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-36-3-11

If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life. ^sn12-36-3-12

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-36-3-13

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices.’ ^sn12-36-3-14

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-36-4-1

‘What are old age and death, and who do they belong to?’ or ‘old age and death are one thing, who they belong to is another’, or ‘the soul and the body are one and the same’, or ‘the soul is one thing, the body another’. ^sn12-36-4-2

These are all cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-36-4-3

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, then any twists, ducks, and dodges are given up: ^sn12-36-5-1

‘What is rebirth …’ ^sn12-36-5-2

‘What is continued existence …’ ^sn12-36-5-3

‘What is grasping …’ ^sn12-36-5-4

‘What is craving …’ ^sn12-36-5-5

‘What is feeling …’ ^sn12-36-5-6

‘What is contact …’ ^sn12-36-5-7

‘What are the six sense fields …’ ^sn12-36-5-8

‘What are name and form …’ ^sn12-36-5-9

‘What is consciousness …’ ^sn12-36-5-10

‘What are choices, and who do they belong to?’ or ‘choices are one thing, who they belong to is another’, or ‘the soul and the body are one and the same’, or ‘the soul is one thing, the body another’. ^sn12-36-5-11

These are all cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn12-36-5-12

### Not Yours (Linked Discourses 12.37) ^sn12-37-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-37-1-1

“Mendicants, this body doesn’t belong to you or to anyone else. ^sn12-37-1-2

It’s old deeds, and should be seen as produced by choices and intentions, as something to be felt. ^sn12-37-1-3

A learned noble disciple carefully and rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself: ^sn12-37-2-1

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-37-2-2

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^sn12-37-2-3

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-37-2-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-37-2-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-37-2-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-37-2-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-37-2-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-37-2-9

### Intention (Linked Discourses 12.38) ^sn12-38-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-38-1-1

“Mendicants, what you intend or plan, and what you have underlying tendencies for become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-38-1-2

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-38-1-3

When consciousness is established and grows, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-38-1-4

When there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, future rebirth, old age, and death come to be, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-38-1-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-38-1-6

If you don’t intend or plan, but still have underlying tendencies, this becomes a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-38-2-1

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-38-2-2

When consciousness is established and grows, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-38-2-3

When there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, future rebirth, old age, and death come to be, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-38-2-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-38-2-5

If you don’t intend or plan or have underlying tendencies, this doesn’t become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-38-3-1

With no support, consciousness is not established. ^sn12-38-3-2

When consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, there’s no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-38-3-3

When there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, future rebirth, old age, and death cease, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-38-3-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-38-3-5

### Intention (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.39) ^sn12-39-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-39-1-1

“Mendicants, what you intend or plan, and what you have underlying tendencies for become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-39-1-2

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-39-1-3

When consciousness is established, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-39-1-4

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. ^sn12-39-1-5

The six sense fields are requirements for contact. ^sn12-39-1-6

Contact is a requirement for feeling. … ^sn12-39-1-7

craving … ^sn12-39-1-8

grasping … ^sn12-39-1-9

continued existence … ^sn12-39-1-10

rebirth … ^sn12-39-1-11

old age and death, ^sn12-39-1-12

sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress come to be. ^sn12-39-1-13

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-39-1-14

If you don’t intend or plan, but still have underlying tendencies, this becomes a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-39-2-1

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-39-2-2

When consciousness is established, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-39-2-3

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. … ^sn12-39-2-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-39-2-5

If you don’t intend or plan or have underlying tendencies, this doesn’t become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-39-3-1

With no support, consciousness is not established. ^sn12-39-3-2

When consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-39-3-3

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. … ^sn12-39-3-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-39-3-5

### Intention (3rd) (Linked Discourses 12.40) ^sn12-40-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-40-1-1

“Mendicants, what you intend or plan, and what you have underlying tendencies for become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-40-1-2

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-40-1-3

When consciousness is established and grows, there is an inclination. ^sn12-40-1-4

When there is an inclination, there is coming and going. ^sn12-40-1-5

When there is coming and going, there is passing away and reappearing. ^sn12-40-1-6

When there is passing away and reappearing, future rebirth, old age, and death come to be, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-40-1-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-40-1-8

If you don’t intend or plan, but still have underlying tendencies, this becomes a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-40-2-1

When this support exists, consciousness becomes established. ^sn12-40-2-2

When consciousness is established and grows, there is an inclination. ^sn12-40-2-3

When there is an inclination, there is coming and going. ^sn12-40-2-4

When there is coming and going, there is passing away and reappearing. ^sn12-40-2-5

When there is passing away and reappearing, future rebirth, old age, and death come to be, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-40-2-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-40-2-7

If you don’t intend or plan or have underlying tendencies, this doesn’t become a support for the continuation of consciousness. ^sn12-40-3-1

With no support, consciousness is not established. ^sn12-40-3-2

When consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, there’s no inclination. ^sn12-40-3-3

When there’s no inclination, there’s no coming and going. ^sn12-40-3-4

When there’s no coming and going, there’s no passing away and reappearing. ^sn12-40-3-5

When there’s no passing away and reappearing, future rebirth, old age, and death cease, as do sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn12-40-3-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-40-3-7

### Fears and Enmities (Linked Discourses 12.41) ^sn12-41-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-41-1-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the Buddha said to the householder Anāthapiṇḍika: ^sn12-41-1-2

“Householder, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn12-41-2-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^sn12-41-2-2

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^sn12-41-3-1

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. That fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^sn12-41-3-2

Anyone who steals brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. That fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from stealing. ^sn12-41-4-1

Anyone who commits sexual misconduct creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. That danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from sexual misconduct. ^sn12-41-5-1

Anyone who lies brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. That fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from lying. ^sn12-41-6-1

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. That danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^sn12-41-7-1

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^sn12-41-7-2

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^sn12-41-8-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn12-41-8-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn12-41-8-3

They have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^sn12-41-9-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn12-41-9-2

They have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^sn12-41-10-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn12-41-10-2

And a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn12-41-11-1

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^sn12-41-11-2

And what is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom? ^sn12-41-12-1

A noble disciple carefully and rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself: ^sn12-41-12-2

‘When this exists, that comes to be; when this doesn’t exist, that is not. ^sn12-41-12-3

Due to the arising of this, that arises; due to the cessation of this, that ceases. ^sn12-41-12-4

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-41-12-5

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-41-12-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-41-12-7

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-41-12-8

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-41-12-9

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’ ^sn12-41-12-10

This is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom. ^sn12-41-12-11

When a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn12-41-13-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn12-41-13-2

### Fears and Enmities (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.42) ^sn12-42-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-42-1-1

“Mendicants, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn12-42-1-2

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^sn12-42-1-3

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^sn12-42-2-1

Killing living creatures … ^sn12-42-2-2

stealing … ^sn12-42-2-3

sexual misconduct … ^sn12-42-2-4

lying … ^sn12-42-2-5

consuming intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor … ^sn12-42-2-6

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^sn12-42-2-7

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^sn12-42-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn12-42-3-2

the teaching … ^sn12-42-3-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn12-42-3-4

and their ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones. ^sn12-42-3-5

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^sn12-42-3-6

And what is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom? ^sn12-42-4-1

A noble disciple carefully and rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself … ^sn12-42-4-2

This is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom. ^sn12-42-4-3

When a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn12-42-5-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn12-42-5-2

### Suffering (Linked Discourses 12.43) ^sn12-43-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-43-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the origin and disappearance of suffering. ^sn12-43-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-43-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-43-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-43-1-5

“And what, mendicants, is the origin of suffering? ^sn12-43-2-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-43-2-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-43-2-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. … ^sn12-43-2-4

This is the origin of suffering. ^sn12-43-2-5

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-43-3-1

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn12-43-3-2

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn12-43-3-3

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn12-43-3-4

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-43-3-5

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-43-3-6

Feeling is a requirement for craving. … ^sn12-43-3-7

This is the origin of suffering. ^sn12-43-3-8

And what is the disappearance of suffering? ^sn12-43-4-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-43-4-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-43-4-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-43-4-4

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-43-4-5

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-43-4-6

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-43-4-7

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-43-4-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-43-4-9

This is the disappearance of suffering. ^sn12-43-4-10

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-43-5-1

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn12-43-5-2

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn12-43-5-3

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn12-43-5-4

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-43-5-5

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-43-5-6

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-43-5-7

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-43-5-8

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-43-5-9

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-43-5-10

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-43-5-11

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-43-5-12

This is the disappearance of suffering.” ^sn12-43-5-13

### The World (Linked Discourses 12.44) ^sn12-44-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-44-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the origin and disappearance of the world. ^sn12-44-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-44-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-44-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-44-1-5

“And what, mendicants, is the origin of the world? ^sn12-44-2-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-44-2-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-44-2-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-44-2-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-44-2-5

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn12-44-2-6

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn12-44-2-7

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-44-2-8

This is the origin of the world. ^sn12-44-2-9

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-44-3-1

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn12-44-3-2

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn12-44-3-3

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn12-44-3-4

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-44-3-5

Contact is a requirement for feeling. … ^sn12-44-3-6

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-44-3-7

This is the origin of the world. ^sn12-44-3-8

And what is the ending of the world? ^sn12-44-4-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-44-4-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-44-4-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-44-4-4

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-44-4-5

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-44-4-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-44-4-7

This is the ending of the world. ^sn12-44-4-8

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-44-5-1

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn12-44-5-2

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn12-44-5-3

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn12-44-5-4

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-44-5-5

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-44-5-6

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-44-5-7

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-44-5-8

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-44-5-9

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-44-5-10

This is the ending of the world.” ^sn12-44-5-11

### At Ñātika (Linked Discourses 12.45) ^sn12-45-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-45-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^sn12-45-1-2

Then while the Buddha was in private retreat he spoke this exposition of the teaching: ^sn12-45-1-3

“Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-45-2-1

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-45-2-2

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-45-2-3

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-45-2-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-45-2-5

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-45-3-1

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn12-45-3-2

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn12-45-3-3

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn12-45-3-4

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-45-3-5

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-45-3-6

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-45-3-7

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-45-3-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-45-3-9

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-45-4-1

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-45-4-2

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-45-4-3

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-45-4-4

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-45-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-45-4-6

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn12-45-5-1

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn12-45-5-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn12-45-5-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn12-45-5-4

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn12-45-5-5

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-45-5-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-45-5-7

Now at that time a certain monk was standing listening in on the Buddha. ^sn12-45-6-1

The Buddha saw him ^sn12-45-6-2

and said, ^sn12-45-6-3

“Monk, did you hear that exposition of the teaching?” ^sn12-45-6-4

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-45-6-5

“Learn that exposition of the teaching, ^sn12-45-6-6

memorize it, ^sn12-45-6-7

and remember it. ^sn12-45-6-8

That exposition of the teaching is beneficial and relevant to the fundamentals of the spiritual life.” ^sn12-45-6-9

### A Certain Brahmin (Linked Discourses 12.46) ^sn12-46-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-46-1-1

Then a certain brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-46-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^sn12-46-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, are he who does the deed and he who experiences the result one and the same?” ^sn12-46-2-1

“‘He who does the deed and he who experiences the result are one and the same’: this is one extreme, brahmin.” ^sn12-46-2-2

“Then is he who does the deed one and he who experiences the result another?” ^sn12-46-3-1

“‘He who does the deed is one and he who experiences the result is another’: this is the second extreme. ^sn12-46-3-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-46-3-3

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-46-3-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-46-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-46-3-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-46-3-7

When choices cease … ^sn12-46-3-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-46-3-9

When he said this, the brahmin said to the Buddha, ^sn12-46-4-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^sn12-46-4-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn12-46-4-3

### Jānussoṇi (Linked Discourses 12.47) ^sn12-47-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-47-1-1

Then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. Seated to one side he said to the Buddha: ^sn12-47-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, does all exist?” ^sn12-47-2-1

“‘All exists’: this is one extreme, brahmin.” ^sn12-47-2-2

“Then does all not exist?” ^sn12-47-3-1

“‘All does not exist’: this is the second extreme. ^sn12-47-3-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-47-3-3

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-47-3-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-47-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-47-3-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-47-3-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-47-3-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-47-3-9

When he said this, the brahmin Jānussoṇi said to the Buddha, ^sn12-47-4-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^sn12-47-4-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn12-47-4-3

### A Cosmologist (Linked Discourses 12.48) ^sn12-48-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-48-1-1

Then a brahmin cosmologist went up to the Buddha … ^sn12-48-1-2

Seated to one side he said to the Buddha: ^sn12-48-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, does all exist?” ^sn12-48-2-1

“‘All exists’: this is the oldest cosmology, brahmin.” ^sn12-48-2-2

“Then does all not exist?” ^sn12-48-3-1

“‘All does not exist’: this is the second cosmology.” ^sn12-48-3-2

“Well, is all oneness?” ^sn12-48-4-1

“‘All is oneness’: this is the third cosmology.” ^sn12-48-4-2

“Then is all expansiveness?” ^sn12-48-5-1

“‘All is expansiveness’: this is the fourth cosmology. ^sn12-48-5-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn12-48-6-1

‘Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-48-6-2

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-48-6-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-48-6-4

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-48-6-5

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-48-6-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’” ^sn12-48-6-7

When he said this, the brahmin cosmologist said to the Buddha, ^sn12-48-7-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^sn12-48-7-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn12-48-7-3

### A Noble Disciple (Linked Discourses 12.49) ^sn12-49-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-49-1-1

“Mendicants, a learned noble disciple doesn’t think: ^sn12-49-1-2

‘When what exists, what is? Due to the arising of what, what arises? ^sn12-49-1-4

When what exists do name and form come to be? When what exists do the six sense fields … contact … feeling … craving … grasping … continued existence … rebirth … old age and death come to be?’ ^sn12-49-1-5

Rather, a learned noble disciple has only knowledge about this that is independent of others: ^sn12-49-2-1

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-49-2-2

When ignorance exists choices come to be. ^sn12-49-2-3

When choices exist consciousness comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-4

When consciousness exists name and form come to be. ^sn12-49-2-5

When name and form exist the six sense fields come to be. ^sn12-49-2-6

When the six sense fields exist contact comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-7

When contact exists feeling comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-8

When feeling exists craving comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-9

When craving exists grasping comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-10

When grasping exists continued existence comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-11

When continued existence exists rebirth comes to be. ^sn12-49-2-12

When rebirth exists old age and death come to be.’ ^sn12-49-2-13

They understand: ^sn12-49-2-14

‘That is how this world originates.’ ^sn12-49-2-15

A learned noble disciple doesn’t think: ^sn12-49-3-1

‘When what doesn’t exist, what is not? Due to the cessation of what, what ceases? ^sn12-49-3-3

When what doesn’t exist do choices not come to be? When what doesn’t exist do name and form not come to be? When what doesn’t exist do the six sense fields … contact … feeling … craving … grasping … continued existence … rebirth … old age and death not come to be?’ ^sn12-49-3-4

Rather, a learned noble disciple has only knowledge about this that is independent of others: ^sn12-49-4-1

‘When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. ^sn12-49-4-2

When ignorance doesn’t exist choices don’t come to be. ^sn12-49-4-3

When choices don’t exist consciousness doesn’t come to be. ^sn12-49-4-4

When consciousness doesn’t exist name and form don’t come to be. ^sn12-49-4-5

When name and form don’t exist the six sense fields don’t come to be. … ^sn12-49-4-6

continued existence doesn’t come to be … ^sn12-49-4-7

rebirth doesn’t come to be … ^sn12-49-4-8

When rebirth doesn’t exist old age and death don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-49-4-9

They understand: ^sn12-49-4-10

‘That is how this world ceases.’ ^sn12-49-4-11

A noble disciple comes to truly understand the origin and disappearance of the world. Such a noble disciple is one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands knocking at the door to freedom from death’.” ^sn12-49-5-1

### A Noble Disciple (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.50) ^sn12-50-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-50-1-1

“Mendicants, a learned noble disciple doesn’t think: ^sn12-50-1-2

‘When what exists, what is? Due to the arising of what, what arises? ^sn12-50-1-3

When what exists do choices come to be? When what exists does consciousness come to be? When what exists do name and form … the six sense fields … contact … feeling … craving … grasping … continued existence … rebirth … old age and death come to be?’ ^sn12-50-1-4

Rather, a learned noble disciple has only knowledge about this that is independent of others: ^sn12-50-2-1

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-50-2-2

When ignorance exists, choices come to be. ^sn12-50-2-3

When choices exist consciousness comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-4

When consciousness exists name and form come to be. ^sn12-50-2-5

When name and form exist the six sense fields come to be. ^sn12-50-2-6

When the six sense fields exist contact comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-7

When contact exists feeling comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-8

When feeling exists craving comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-9

When craving exists grasping comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-10

When grasping exists continued existence comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-11

When continued existence exists rebirth comes to be. ^sn12-50-2-12

When rebirth exists old age and death come to be.’ ^sn12-50-2-13

They understand: ^sn12-50-2-14

‘That is how this world originates.’ ^sn12-50-2-15

A learned noble disciple doesn’t think: ^sn12-50-3-1

‘When what doesn’t exist, what is not? Due to the cessation of what, what ceases? ^sn12-50-3-2

When what doesn’t exist do choices not come to be? When what doesn’t exist does consciousness not come to be? When what doesn’t exist do name and form … the six sense fields … contact … feeling … craving … grasping … continued existence … rebirth … old age and death not come to be?’ ^sn12-50-3-3

Rather, a learned noble disciple has only knowledge about this that is independent of others: ^sn12-50-4-1

‘When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^sn12-50-4-2

When ignorance doesn’t exists, choices don’t come to be. ^sn12-50-4-3

When choices don’t exist consciousness doesn’t come to be. ^sn12-50-4-4

When consciousness doesn’t exist name and form don’t come to be. ^sn12-50-4-5

When name and form don’t exist the six sense fields don’t come to be. … ^sn12-50-4-6

When rebirth doesn’t exist old age and death don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-50-4-7

They understand: ^sn12-50-4-8

‘That is how this world ceases.’ ^sn12-50-4-9

A noble disciple comes to truly understand the origin and ending of the world. Such a noble disciple is one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.” ^sn12-50-5-1

### An Inquiry (Linked Discourses 12.51) ^sn12-51-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-51-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn12-51-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn12-51-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn12-51-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn12-51-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-51-1-6

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is inquiring, how do you define when they are inquiring for the complete ending of suffering?” ^sn12-51-2-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn12-51-2-2

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-51-2-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-51-2-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-51-2-5

“Mendicants, take a mendicant who makes an inquiry: ^sn12-51-3-1

‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms. ^sn12-51-3-2

What is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of this suffering? ^sn12-51-3-3

When what exists do old age and death come to be? When what does not exist do old age and death not come to be?’ ^sn12-51-3-4

While making an inquiry they understand: ^sn12-51-3-5

‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms. The source of this suffering is rebirth. ^sn12-51-3-6

When rebirth exists, old age and death come to be. When rebirth doesn’t exist, old age and death don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-51-3-7

They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. And they practice in line with that path. ^sn12-51-4-1

This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of old age and death. ^sn12-51-4-2

Then they inquire further: ^sn12-51-5-1

‘But what is the source of this rebirth? When what exists does rebirth come to be? And when what does not exist does rebirth not come to be?’ ^sn12-51-5-2

While making an inquiry they understand: ^sn12-51-5-3

‘Continued existence is the source of rebirth. ^sn12-51-5-4

When continued existence exists, rebirth comes to be. When continued existence does not exist, rebirth doesn’t come to be.’ ^sn12-51-5-5

They understand rebirth, its origin, its cessation, and the fitting practice for its cessation. And they practice in line with that path. ^sn12-51-6-1

This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of rebirth. ^sn12-51-6-2

Then they inquire further: ^sn12-51-7-1

‘But what is the source of this continued existence? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-2

‘But what is the source of this grasping? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-3

‘But what is the source of this craving? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-4

‘But what is the source of this feeling? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-5

‘But what is the source of this contact? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-6

‘But what is the source of these six sense fields? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-7

‘But what is the source of this name and form? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-8

‘But what is the source of this consciousness? …’ … ^sn12-51-7-9

‘But what is the source of these choices? ^sn12-51-7-10

When what exists do choices come to be? When what does not exist do choices not come to be?’ ^sn12-51-7-11

While making an inquiry they understand: ^sn12-51-7-12

‘Ignorance is the source of choices. ^sn12-51-7-13

When ignorance exists, choices come to be. When ignorance does not exist, choices don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-51-7-14

They understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. And they practice in line with that path. ^sn12-51-8-1

This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of choices. ^sn12-51-8-2

If an ignoramus makes a good choice, consciousness passes on by means of goodness. ^sn12-51-9-1

If they make a bad choice, consciousness passes on by means of badness. ^sn12-51-9-2

If they make an imperturbable choice, consciousness passes on by means of imperturbability. ^sn12-51-9-3

When a mendicant has given up ignorance and given rise to knowledge, with the fading away of ignorance and the arising of knowledge they don’t make a good choice, a bad choice, or an imperturbable choice. ^sn12-51-9-4

Not choosing or intending, they don’t grasp at anything in the world. ^sn12-51-9-5

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn12-51-9-6

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn12-51-9-7

If they feel a pleasant feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn12-51-10-1

If they feel a painful feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn12-51-10-2

If they feel a neutral feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn12-51-10-3

If they feel a pleasant feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn12-51-10-4

If they feel a painful feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn12-51-10-5

If they feel a neutral feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn12-51-10-6

Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn12-51-11-1

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here. Only bodily remains will be left.’ ^sn12-51-11-2

Suppose a person were to remove a hot clay pot from a potter’s kiln and place it down on level ground. ^sn12-51-12-1

Its heat would dissipate right there, and the shards would be left behind. ^sn12-51-12-2

In the same way, feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn12-51-12-3

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here. Only bodily remains will be left.’ ^sn12-51-12-4

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn12-51-13-1

Would a mendicant who has ended the defilements still make good choices, bad choices, or imperturbable choices?” ^sn12-51-13-2

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-13-3

“And when there are no choices at all, with the cessation of choices, would consciousness still be found?” ^sn12-51-14-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-14-2

“And when there’s no consciousness at all, would name and form still be found?” ^sn12-51-15-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-15-2

“And when there are no name and form at all, would the six sense fields still be found?” ^sn12-51-16-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-16-2

“And when there are no six sense fields at all, would contact still be found?” ^sn12-51-17-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-17-2

“And when there’s no contact at all, would feeling still be found?” ^sn12-51-18-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-18-2

“And when there’s no feeling at all, would craving still be found?” ^sn12-51-19-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-19-2

“And when there’s no craving at all, would grasping still be found?” ^sn12-51-20-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-20-2

“And when there’s no grasping at all, would continued existence still be found?” ^sn12-51-21-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-21-2

“And when there’s no continued existence at all, would rebirth still be found?” ^sn12-51-22-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-22-2

“And when there’s no rebirth at all, would old age and death still be found?” ^sn12-51-23-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-51-23-2

“Good, good, mendicants! That’s how it is, not otherwise. ^sn12-51-24-1

Trust me on this, mendicants; be convinced. Have no doubts or uncertainties in this matter. ^sn12-51-24-2

Just this is the end of suffering.” ^sn12-51-24-3

### Grasping (Linked Discourses 12.52) ^sn12-52-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-52-1-1

“There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-52-1-2

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-52-1-3

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn12-52-1-4

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn12-52-1-5

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-52-1-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-52-1-7

Suppose a great mass of fire was burning with ten, twenty, thirty, or forty loads of wood. ^sn12-52-2-1

And from time to time someone would toss in dry grass, cow dung, or wood. ^sn12-52-2-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the great mass of fire would burn for a long time. ^sn12-52-2-3

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-52-2-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-52-2-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-52-2-6

There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-52-3-1

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-52-3-2

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-52-3-3

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-52-3-4

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-52-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-52-3-6

Suppose a great mass of fire was burning with ten, twenty, thirty, or forty loads of wood. ^sn12-52-4-1

And no-one would toss in dry grass, cow dung, or wood from time to time. ^sn12-52-4-2

As the original fuel is used up and no more is added, the great mass of fire would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^sn12-52-4-3

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-52-4-4

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-52-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-52-4-6

### Fetters (Linked Discourses 12.53) ^sn12-53-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-53-1-1

“There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-53-1-2

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-53-1-3

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn12-53-1-4

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn12-53-1-5

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-53-1-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-53-1-7

Suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn12-53-2-1

And from time to time someone would pour oil in and adjust the wick. ^sn12-53-2-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the oil lamp would burn for a long time. ^sn12-53-2-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-53-2-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-53-2-5

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn12-53-2-6

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn12-53-2-7

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-53-2-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-53-2-9

There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-53-3-1

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-53-3-2

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-53-3-3

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-53-3-4

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-53-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-53-3-6

Suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn12-53-4-1

And no-one would pour oil in and adjust the wick from time to time. ^sn12-53-4-2

As the original fuel is used up and no more is added, the oil lamp would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^sn12-53-4-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-53-4-4

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-53-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-53-4-6

### Fetters (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.54) ^sn12-54-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-54-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn12-54-1-2

And from time to time someone would pour oil in and adjust the wick. ^sn12-54-1-3

Fed and fuelled by that, the oil lamp would burn for a long time. ^sn12-54-1-4

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-54-1-5

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-54-1-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-54-1-7

Suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn12-54-2-1

And no-one would pour oil in and adjust the wick from time to time. ^sn12-54-2-2

As the original fuel is used up and no more is added, the oil lamp would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^sn12-54-2-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-54-2-4

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-54-2-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-54-2-6

### A Great Tree (Linked Discourses 12.55) ^sn12-55-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-55-1-1

“There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-55-1-2

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-55-1-3

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. … ^sn12-55-1-4

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-55-1-5

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-55-2-1

And its roots going downwards and across all draw the sap upwards. ^sn12-55-2-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time. ^sn12-55-2-3

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-55-2-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-55-2-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-55-2-6

There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-55-3-1

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-55-3-2

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-55-3-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-55-3-4

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-55-4-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. ^sn12-55-4-2

They’d cut the tree down at the roots, dig it up, and pull the roots out, down to the fibers and stems. ^sn12-55-4-3

They’d cut the tree apart, cut up the parts, and chop it into splinters. They’d dry the splinters in the wind and sun, burn them with fire, and reduce them to ashes. Then they’d winnow the ashes in a strong wind, or float them away down a swift stream. ^sn12-55-4-4

In this way the great tree is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-55-4-6

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-55-4-7

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-55-4-8

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-55-4-9

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-55-4-10

### A Great Tree (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.56) ^sn12-56-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-56-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-56-1-2

And its roots going downwards and across all draw the sap upwards. ^sn12-56-1-3

Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time. ^sn12-56-1-4

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-56-1-5

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-56-1-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-56-1-7

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-56-2-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. ^sn12-56-2-2

They’d cut the tree down at the roots, dig them up, and pull them out, down to the fibers and stems. ^sn12-56-2-3

They’d cut the tree apart, cut up the parts, and chop it into splinters. They’d dry the splinters in the wind and sun, burn them with fire, and reduce them to ashes. Then they’d winnow the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^sn12-56-2-4

In this way the great tree is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-56-2-5

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-56-2-6

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-56-2-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-56-2-8

### A Sapling (Linked Discourses 12.57) ^sn12-57-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-57-1-1

“There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-57-1-2

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-57-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-57-1-4

Suppose there was a sapling. ^sn12-57-2-1

And from time to time someone would clear around the roots, supply soil, and water it. ^sn12-57-2-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the sapling would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn12-57-2-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-57-2-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. … ^sn12-57-2-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-57-2-6

There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-57-3-1

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-57-3-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-57-3-3

Suppose there was a sapling. ^sn12-57-4-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. … ^sn12-57-4-2

They’d cut the sapling apart, cut up the parts, and chop it into splinters. They’d dry the splinters in the wind and sun, burn them with fire, and reduce them to ashes. Then they’d winnow the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^sn12-57-4-3

In this way the sapling is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-57-4-4

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-57-4-5

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn12-57-4-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-57-4-7

### Name and Form (Linked Discourses 12.58) ^sn12-58-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-58-1-1

“There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-58-1-2

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. … ^sn12-58-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-58-1-4

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-58-2-1

And its roots going downwards and across all draw the sap upwards. ^sn12-58-2-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time. ^sn12-58-2-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, name and form are conceived. … ^sn12-58-2-4

There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-58-3-1

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. … ^sn12-58-3-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-58-3-3

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-58-4-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. … ^sn12-58-4-2

In this way the great tree is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-58-4-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-58-4-4

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. … ^sn12-58-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-58-4-6

### Consciousness (Linked Discourses 12.59) ^sn12-59-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-59-1-1

“There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, consciousness is conceived. ^sn12-59-1-2

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. … ^sn12-59-1-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-59-1-4

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-59-2-1

And its roots going downwards and across all draw the sap upwards. … ^sn12-59-2-2

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, consciousness is conceived. … ^sn12-59-2-3

There are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, consciousness is not conceived. ^sn12-59-3-1

When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. … ^sn12-59-3-2

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-59-3-3

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-59-4-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. … ^sn12-59-4-2

In this way the great tree is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-59-4-3

In the same way, there are things that tighten the fetters. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, consciousness is not conceived. ^sn12-59-4-4

When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. … ^sn12-59-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-59-4-6

### Causation (Linked Discourses 12.60) ^sn12-60-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kurus, near the Kuru town named Kammāsadamma. ^sn12-60-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^sn12-60-1-2

“It’s incredible, sir! It’s amazing, ^sn12-60-1-3

in that this dependent origination is deep and appears deep, yet to me it seems as plain as can be.” ^sn12-60-1-4

“Not so, Ānanda! Not so, Ānanda! ^sn12-60-2-1

This dependent origination is deep and appears deep. ^sn12-60-2-2

It is because of not understanding and not penetrating this teaching that this population has become tangled like string, knotted like a ball of thread, and matted like rushes and reeds, and it doesn’t escape the places of loss, the bad places, the underworld, transmigration. ^sn12-60-2-3

There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-60-3-1

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-60-3-2

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn12-60-3-3

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn12-60-3-4

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn12-60-3-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-60-3-6

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-60-4-1

And its roots going downwards and across all draw the sap upwards. ^sn12-60-4-2

Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time. ^sn12-60-4-3

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the gratification provided by these things, your craving grows. ^sn12-60-4-4

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn12-60-4-5

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. … ^sn12-60-4-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-60-4-7

There are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-60-5-1

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-60-5-2

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn12-60-5-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-60-5-4

Suppose there was a great tree. ^sn12-60-6-1

Then a person comes along with a spade and basket. ^sn12-60-6-2

They’d cut the tree down at the roots, dig them up, and pull them out, down to the fibers and stems. ^sn12-60-6-3

Then they’d split the tree apart, cut up the parts, and chop it into splinters. They’d dry the splinters in the wind and sun, burn them with fire, and reduce them to ashes. Then they’d winnow the ashes in a strong wind, or float them away down a swift stream. ^sn12-60-6-4

In this way the great tree is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn12-60-6-7

In the same way, there are things that fuel grasping. When you concentrate on the drawbacks of these things, your craving ceases. ^sn12-60-6-8

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn12-60-6-9

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn12-60-6-10

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn12-60-6-11

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn12-60-6-12

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-60-6-13

### Unlearned (Linked Discourses 12.61) ^sn12-61-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-61-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. … ^sn12-61-1-2

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person might become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed from this body made up of the four principal states. ^sn12-61-1-3

Why is that? ^sn12-61-1-4

This body made up of the four principal states is seen to accumulate and disperse, to be taken up and laid to rest. ^sn12-61-1-5

That’s why an unlearned ordinary person might become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed from it. ^sn12-61-1-6

But an unlearned ordinary person is unable to become disillusioned, dispassionate, or freed from that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’. ^sn12-61-2-1

Why is that? ^sn12-61-2-2

Because for a long time they’ve been attached to it, thought of it as their own, and mistaken it: ^sn12-61-2-3

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn12-61-2-4

That’s why an unlearned ordinary person is unable to become disillusioned, dispassionate, or freed from it. ^sn12-61-2-5

But an unlearned ordinary person would be better off taking this body made up of the four principal states to be their self, rather than the mind. ^sn12-61-3-1

Why is that? ^sn12-61-3-2

This body made up of the four principal states is seen to last for a year, or for two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or a hundred years, or even longer. ^sn12-61-3-3

But that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’ arises as one thing and ceases as another all day and all night. ^sn12-61-4-1

It’s like a monkey moving through the forest. It grabs hold of one branch, lets it go, and grabs another; then it lets that go and grabs yet another. ^sn12-61-4-2

In the same way, that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’ arises as one thing and ceases as another all day and all night. ^sn12-61-4-3

In this case, a learned noble disciple carefully and rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself: ^sn12-61-5-1

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-61-5-2

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^sn12-61-5-3

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn12-61-5-4

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn12-61-5-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-61-5-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. ^sn12-61-5-7

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. … ^sn12-61-5-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’ ^sn12-61-5-9

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn12-61-6-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn12-61-6-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn12-61-6-3

### Unlearned (2nd) (Linked Discourses 12.62) ^sn12-62-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-62-1-1

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person might become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed from this body made up of the four principal states. ^sn12-62-1-2

Why is that? ^sn12-62-1-3

This body made up of the four principal states is seen to accumulate and disperse, to be taken up and laid to rest. ^sn12-62-1-4

That’s why an unlearned ordinary person might become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed from it. ^sn12-62-1-5

But an unlearned ordinary person is unable to become disillusioned, dispassionate, or freed from that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’. ^sn12-62-1-6

Why is that? ^sn12-62-1-7

Because for a long time they’ve been attached to it, thought of it as their own, and mistaken it: ^sn12-62-1-8

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn12-62-1-9

That’s why an unlearned ordinary person is unable to become disillusioned, dispassionate, or freed from it. ^sn12-62-1-10

But an unlearned ordinary person would be better off taking this body made up of the four principal states to be their self, rather than the mind. ^sn12-62-2-1

Why is that? ^sn12-62-2-2

This body made up of the four principal states is seen to last for a year, or for two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or a hundred years, or even longer. ^sn12-62-2-3

But that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’ arises as one thing and ceases as another all day and all night. ^sn12-62-2-4

In this case, a learned noble disciple carefully and rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself: ^sn12-62-3-1

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn12-62-3-2

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases.’ ^sn12-62-3-3

Pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn12-62-3-4

With the cessation of that contact to be experienced as pleasant, the corresponding pleasant feeling ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-3-5

Painful feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as painful. ^sn12-62-3-6

With the cessation of that contact to be experienced as painful, the corresponding painful feeling ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-3-7

Neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn12-62-3-8

With the cessation of that contact to be experienced as neutral, the corresponding neutral feeling ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-3-9

When you rub two sticks together, heat is generated and fire is produced. But when you part the sticks and lay them aside, any corresponding heat ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-4-1

In the same way, pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn12-62-4-2

With the cessation of that contact to be experienced as pleasant, the corresponding pleasant feeling ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-4-3

Painful feeling … ^sn12-62-4-4

Neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn12-62-4-5

With the cessation of that contact to be experienced as neutral, the corresponding neutral feeling ceases and stops. ^sn12-62-4-6

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with contact, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn12-62-5-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn12-62-5-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn12-62-5-3

### A Child’s Flesh (Linked Discourses 12.63) ^sn12-63-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-63-1-1

“Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-63-1-2

What four? ^sn12-63-1-3

Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth. ^sn12-63-1-4

These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-63-1-5

And how should you regard edible food? ^sn12-63-2-1

Suppose a couple who were husband and wife set out to cross a desert, taking limited supplies. ^sn12-63-2-2

They had an only child, dear and beloved. ^sn12-63-2-3

As the couple were crossing the desert their limited quantity of supplies would run out, ^sn12-63-2-4

and they’d still have the rest of the desert to cross. ^sn12-63-2-5

Then it would occur to that couple: ^sn12-63-2-6

‘Our limited quantity of supplies has run out, ^sn12-63-2-7

and we still have the rest of the desert to cross. ^sn12-63-2-8

Why don’t we kill our only child, so dear and beloved, and prepare dried and spiced meat? Then we can make it across the desert by eating our child’s flesh. Let not all three perish.’ ^sn12-63-2-9

Then that couple would kill their only child, so dear and beloved, and prepare dried and spiced meat. They’d make it across the desert by eating their child’s flesh. ^sn12-63-2-10

And as they’d eat their child’s flesh, they’d beat their breasts and cry: ^sn12-63-2-11

‘Where are you, our only child? Where are you, our only child?’ ^sn12-63-2-12

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn12-63-3-1

Would they eat that food for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration?” ^sn12-63-3-2

“No, sir.” ^sn12-63-3-3

“Wouldn’t they eat that food just so they could make it across the desert?” ^sn12-63-3-4

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-63-3-5

“I say that this is how you should regard edible food. ^sn12-63-3-6

When edible food is completely understood, desire for the five kinds of sensual stimulation is completely understood. ^sn12-63-3-7

When desire for the five kinds of sensual stimulation is completely understood, a noble disciple is bound by no fetter that might return them again to this world. ^sn12-63-3-8

And how should you regard contact as fuel? ^sn12-63-4-1

Suppose there was a flayed cow. If she stands by a wall, the creatures on the wall bite her. ^sn12-63-4-2

If she stands under a tree, the creatures in the tree bite her. ^sn12-63-4-3

If she stands in some water, the creatures in the water bite her. ^sn12-63-4-4

If she stands in the open, the creatures in the open bite her. ^sn12-63-4-5

Wherever that flayed cow stands, the creatures there would bite her. ^sn12-63-4-6

I say that this is how you should regard contact as fuel. ^sn12-63-4-7

When contact as fuel is completely understood, the three feelings are completely understood. ^sn12-63-4-8

When the three feelings are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say. ^sn12-63-4-9

And how should you regard mental intention as fuel? ^sn12-63-5-1

Suppose there was a pit of glowing coals deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing coals that neither flamed nor smoked. ^sn12-63-5-2

Then a person would come along who wants to live and doesn’t want to die, who wants to be happy and recoils from pain. ^sn12-63-5-3

Two strong men would grab them by the arms and drag them towards the pit of glowing coals. ^sn12-63-5-4

Then that person’s intention, aim, and wish would be to get far away. ^sn12-63-5-5

Why is that? ^sn12-63-5-6

Because that person would think: ^sn12-63-5-7

‘If I fall in that pit of glowing coals, that will result in my death or deadly pain.’ ^sn12-63-5-8

I say that this is how you should regard mental intention as fuel. ^sn12-63-5-9

When mental intention as fuel is completely understood, the three cravings are completely understood. ^sn12-63-5-10

When the three cravings are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say. ^sn12-63-5-11

And how should you regard consciousness as fuel? ^sn12-63-6-1

Suppose they were to arrest a bandit, a criminal and present him to the king, saying: ^sn12-63-6-2

‘Your Majesty, this is a bandit, a criminal. Punish him as you will.’ ^sn12-63-6-3

The king would say: ^sn12-63-6-4

‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the morning with a hundred spears!’ ^sn12-63-6-5

The king’s men did as they were told. ^sn12-63-6-6

Then at midday the king would say: ^sn12-63-6-7

‘Look here, how is that man?’ ^sn12-63-6-8

‘He’s still alive, Your Majesty.’ ^sn12-63-6-9

The king would say: ^sn12-63-6-10

‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the middle of the day with a hundred spears!’ ^sn12-63-6-11

The king’s men did as they were told. ^sn12-63-6-12

Then late in the afternoon the king would say: ^sn12-63-6-13

‘Look here, how is that man?’ ^sn12-63-6-14

‘He’s still alive, Your Majesty.’ ^sn12-63-6-15

The king would say: ^sn12-63-6-16

‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the late afternoon with a hundred spears!’ ^sn12-63-6-17

The king’s men did as they were told. ^sn12-63-6-18

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn12-63-6-19

Would that man experience pain and distress from being struck with three hundred spears a day?” ^sn12-63-6-20

“Sir, that man would experience pain and distress from being struck with one spear, ^sn12-63-6-21

let alone three hundred spears!” ^sn12-63-6-22

“I say that this is how you should regard consciousness as fuel. ^sn12-63-6-23

When consciousness as fuel is completely understood, name and form is completely understood. When name and form are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say.” ^sn12-63-6-24

### If There is Desire (Linked Discourses 12.64) ^sn12-64-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-64-1-1

“Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-64-1-2

What four? ^sn12-64-1-3

Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth. ^sn12-64-1-4

These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born. ^sn12-64-1-5

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for edible food, consciousness becomes established there and grows. ^sn12-64-2-1

Where consciousness is established and grows, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-64-2-2

Where name and form are conceived, there is the growth of choices. ^sn12-64-2-3

Where choices grow, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-2-4

Where there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-2-5

Where there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say this is full of sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-2-6

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for contact as fuel … ^sn12-64-3-1

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for mental intention as fuel … ^sn12-64-3-2

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for consciousness as fuel, consciousness becomes established there and grows. ^sn12-64-3-3

Where consciousness is established and grows, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-64-3-4

Where name and form are conceived, there is the growth of choices. ^sn12-64-3-5

Where choices grow, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-3-6
