Where there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-3-7

Where there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say this is full of sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-3-8

Suppose an artist or painter had some dye, red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. And on a polished plank or a wall or a canvas they’d create the form of a woman or a man, whole in its major and minor limbs. ^sn12-64-4-1

In the same way, if there is desire, relishing, and craving for edible food, consciousness becomes established there and grows. ^sn12-64-4-2

Where consciousness is established and grows, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-64-4-3

Where name and form are conceived, there is the growth of choices. ^sn12-64-4-4

Where choices grow, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-4-5

Where there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-4-6

Where there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say this is full of sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-4-7

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for contact as fuel … ^sn12-64-5-1

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for mental intention as fuel … ^sn12-64-5-2

If there is desire, relishing, and craving for consciousness as fuel, consciousness becomes established there and grows. ^sn12-64-5-3

Where consciousness is established and grows, name and form are conceived. ^sn12-64-5-4

Where name and form are conceived, there is the growth of choices. ^sn12-64-5-5

Where choices grow, there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-5-6

Where there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-5-7

Where there is rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say this is full of sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-5-8

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for edible food, consciousness does not become established there and doesn’t grow. ^sn12-64-6-1

Where consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-64-6-2

Where name and form are not conceived, there is no growth of choices. ^sn12-64-6-3

Where choices don’t grow, there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-6-4

Where there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-6-5

Where there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say there’s no sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-6-6

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for contact as fuel … ^sn12-64-7-1

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for mental intention as fuel … ^sn12-64-7-2

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for consciousness as fuel, consciousness doesn’t become established there and doesn’t grow. ^sn12-64-7-3

Where consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-64-7-4

Where name and form are not conceived, there is no growth of choices. ^sn12-64-7-5

Where choices don’t grow, there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-7-6

Where there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-7-7

Where there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say there’s no sorrow, anguish, and distress. ^sn12-64-7-8

Suppose there was a bungalow or a hall with a peaked roof, with windows on the northern, southern, or eastern side. When the sun rises and a ray of light enters through a window, where would it land?” ^sn12-64-8-1

“On the western wall, sir.” ^sn12-64-8-2

“If there was no western wall, where would it land?” ^sn12-64-8-3

“On the ground, sir.” ^sn12-64-8-4

“If there was no ground, where would it land?” ^sn12-64-8-5

“In water, sir.” ^sn12-64-8-6

“If there was no water, where would it land?” ^sn12-64-8-7

“It wouldn’t land, sir.” ^sn12-64-8-8

“In the same way, if there is no desire, relishing, and craving for edible food, consciousness does not become established there and doesn’t grow. … ^sn12-64-8-9

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for contact as fuel … ^sn12-64-9-1

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for mental intention as fuel … ^sn12-64-9-2

If there is no desire, relishing, and craving for consciousness as fuel, consciousness doesn’t become established there and doesn’t grow. ^sn12-64-9-3

Where consciousness is not established and doesn’t grow, name and form are not conceived. ^sn12-64-9-4

Where name and form are not conceived, there is no growth of choices. ^sn12-64-9-5

Where choices don’t grow, there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^sn12-64-9-6

Where there is no regeneration into a new state of existence in the future, there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future. ^sn12-64-9-7

Where there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say there’s no sorrow, anguish, and distress.” ^sn12-64-9-8

### The City (Linked Discourses 12.65) ^sn12-65-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-65-1-1

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn12-65-1-2

‘Alas, this world has fallen into trouble. It’s born, grows old, dies, passes away, and is reborn, ^sn12-65-1-3

yet it doesn’t understand how to escape from this suffering, from old age and death. ^sn12-65-1-4

Oh, when will an escape be found from this suffering, from old age and death?’ ^sn12-65-1-5

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-1-6

‘When what exists is there old age and death? What is a requirement for old age and death?’ ^sn12-65-1-7

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-1-8

‘When rebirth exists there’s old age and death. Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’ ^sn12-65-1-9

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-2-1

‘When what exists is there rebirth? … ^sn12-65-2-2

continued existence … ^sn12-65-2-3

grasping … ^sn12-65-2-4

craving … ^sn12-65-2-5

feeling … ^sn12-65-2-6

contact … ^sn12-65-2-7

the six sense fields … ^sn12-65-2-8

name and form … ^sn12-65-2-9

What is a requirement for name and form?’ ^sn12-65-2-10

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-2-11

‘When consciousness exists there are name and form. Consciousness is a requirement for name and form.’ ^sn12-65-2-12

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-2-13

‘When what exists is there consciousness? What is a requirement for consciousness?’ ^sn12-65-2-14

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-2-15

‘When name and form exist there’s consciousness. Name and form are requirements for consciousness.’ ^sn12-65-2-16

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-3-1

This consciousness turns back from name and form, and doesn’t go beyond that. ^sn12-65-3-2

This is the extent to which one may be reborn, grow old, die, pass away, or reappear. That is: name and form are requirements for consciousness. ^sn12-65-3-3

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. ^sn12-65-3-4

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. ^sn12-65-3-5

The six sense fields are requirements for contact. … ^sn12-65-3-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-65-3-7

‘Origination, origination.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another. ^sn12-65-3-8

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-4-1

‘When what doesn’t exist is there no old age and death? ^sn12-65-4-2

When what ceases do old age and death cease?’ ^sn12-65-4-3

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-4-4

‘When rebirth doesn’t exist there is no old age and death. ^sn12-65-4-5

When rebirth ceases old age and death cease.’ ^sn12-65-4-6

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-4-7

‘When what doesn’t exist is there no rebirth … ^sn12-65-4-8

continued existence … ^sn12-65-4-9

grasping … ^sn12-65-4-10

craving … ^sn12-65-4-11

feeling … ^sn12-65-4-12

contact … ^sn12-65-4-13

six sense fields … ^sn12-65-4-14

name and form? ^sn12-65-4-15

When what ceases do name and form cease?’ ^sn12-65-4-16

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-4-17

‘When consciousness doesn’t exist there is no name and form. ^sn12-65-4-18

When consciousness ceases name and form cease.’ ^sn12-65-4-19

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-5-1

‘When what doesn’t exist is there no consciousness? ^sn12-65-5-2

When what ceases does consciousness cease?’ ^sn12-65-5-3

Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom: ^sn12-65-5-4

‘When name and form don’t exist, there is no consciousness. ^sn12-65-5-5

When name and form cease, consciousness ceases.’ ^sn12-65-5-6

Then it occurred to me: ^sn12-65-6-1

I have discovered the path to awakening. That is: ^sn12-65-6-2

When name and form cease, consciousness ceases. ^sn12-65-6-3

When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. ^sn12-65-6-4

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. ^sn12-65-6-5

When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. … ^sn12-65-6-6

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn12-65-6-7

‘Cessation, cessation.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another. ^sn12-65-6-8

Suppose a person was walking through a forest. They’d see an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by humans in the past. ^sn12-65-7-1

Following it along, ^sn12-65-7-2

they’d see an ancient city, an ancient capital, inhabited by humans in the past. It was lovely, complete with parks, groves, lotus ponds, and embankments. ^sn12-65-7-3

Then that person would inform a king or their chief minister: ^sn12-65-7-4

‘Please sir, you should know this. While walking through a forest I saw an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by humans in the past. ^sn12-65-7-5

Following it along I saw an ancient city, an ancient capital, inhabited by humans in the past. It was lovely, complete with parks, groves, lotus ponds, and embankments. ^sn12-65-7-6

Sir, you should rebuild that city!’ ^sn12-65-7-7

Then that king or their chief minister would have that city rebuilt. ^sn12-65-7-8

And after some time that city was successful and prosperous, populous, full of people, attained to growth and expansion. ^sn12-65-7-9

In the same way, I saw an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past. ^sn12-65-7-10

And what is that ancient path, the ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past? ^sn12-65-8-1

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn12-65-8-2

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn12-65-8-3

This is that ancient path, the ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past. ^sn12-65-8-4

Following it along, I directly knew old age and death, ^sn12-65-8-5

their origin, ^sn12-65-8-6

their cessation, ^sn12-65-8-7

and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-65-8-8

Following it along, I directly knew rebirth … ^sn12-65-8-10

continued existence … ^sn12-65-8-11

grasping … ^sn12-65-8-12

craving … ^sn12-65-8-13

feeling … ^sn12-65-8-14

contact … ^sn12-65-8-15

the six sense fields … ^sn12-65-8-16

name and form … ^sn12-65-8-17

consciousness … ^sn12-65-8-18

Following it along, I directly knew choices, ^sn12-65-8-20

their origin, ^sn12-65-8-21

their cessation, ^sn12-65-8-22

and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-65-8-23

Having directly known this, I told the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen. ^sn12-65-8-24

And that’s how this spiritual life has become successful and prosperous, extensive, popular, widespread, and well proclaimed wherever there are gods and humans.” ^sn12-65-8-25

### Probing Within (Linked Discourses 12.66) ^sn12-66-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-66-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kurus, near the Kuru town named Kammāsadamma. ^sn12-66-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn12-66-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn12-66-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn12-66-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-66-1-6

“Mendicants, do you probe within?” ^sn12-66-1-7

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^sn12-66-1-8

“Sir, I probe within.” ^sn12-66-1-9

“But mendicant, how do you probe within?” ^sn12-66-1-10

Then that mendicant answered, ^sn12-66-1-11

but the Buddha was not happy with the answer. ^sn12-66-1-12

When he had spoken, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^sn12-66-2-1

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^sn12-66-2-2

Let the Buddha speak of probing within. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn12-66-2-3

“Well then, Ānanda, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn12-66-2-4

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn12-66-2-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn12-66-2-6

“Take a mendicant who probes within: ^sn12-66-3-1

‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms. ^sn12-66-3-2

But what is the source of this suffering? When what exists do old age and death come to be? And when what does not exist do old age and death not come to be?’ ^sn12-66-3-3

While probing they know: ^sn12-66-3-4

‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms. ^sn12-66-3-5

The source of this suffering is attachment. When attachments exist old age and death come to be. And when attachments do not exist old age and death don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-66-3-6

They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. ^sn12-66-3-7

And they practice in line with that path. ^sn12-66-3-8

This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of old age and death. ^sn12-66-3-9

They probe further within: ^sn12-66-4-1

‘But what is the source of this attachment? When what exists does attachment come to be? And when what does not exist does attachment not come to be?’ ^sn12-66-4-2

While probing they know: ^sn12-66-4-3

‘The source of this attachment is craving. When craving exists attachments come to be. And when craving doesn’t exist attachments don’t come to be.’ ^sn12-66-4-4

They understand attachments, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. ^sn12-66-4-5

And they practice in line with that path. ^sn12-66-4-6

This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of attachments. ^sn12-66-4-7

They probe further within: ^sn12-66-5-1

‘But where does that craving arise and where does it settle?’ ^sn12-66-5-2

While probing they know: ^sn12-66-5-3

‘That craving arises and settles on whatever in the world seems nice and pleasant. ^sn12-66-5-4

And what in the world seems nice and pleasant? ^sn12-66-5-5

The eye in the world seems nice and pleasant, and it is there that craving arises and settles. ^sn12-66-5-6

The ear … ^sn12-66-5-7

nose … ^sn12-66-5-8

tongue … ^sn12-66-5-9

body … ^sn12-66-5-10

mind in the world seems nice and pleasant, and it is there that craving arises and settles.’ ^sn12-66-5-11

There were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe. ^sn12-66-6-1

Their craving grew. ^sn12-66-6-2

As their craving grew, their attachments grew. ^sn12-66-6-3

As their attachments grew, their suffering grew. ^sn12-66-6-4

And as their suffering grew, they were not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were not freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-6-5

There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future who will see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe. ^sn12-66-7-1

Their craving will grow. ^sn12-66-7-2

As their craving grows, their attachments will grow. ^sn12-66-7-3

As their attachments grow, their suffering will grow. ^sn12-66-7-4

And as their suffering grows, they will not be freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They will not be freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-7-5

There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe. ^sn12-66-8-1

Their craving grows. ^sn12-66-8-2

As their craving grows, their attachments grow. ^sn12-66-8-3

As their attachments grow, their suffering grows. ^sn12-66-8-4

And as their suffering grows, they are not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are not freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-8-5

Suppose there was a bronze goblet of beverage that had a nice color, aroma, and flavor. ^sn12-66-9-1

But it was mixed with poison. ^sn12-66-9-2

Then along comes a man struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^sn12-66-9-3

They’d say to him: ^sn12-66-9-4

‘Here, my man, this bronze goblet of beverage has a nice color, aroma, and flavor. ^sn12-66-9-5

But it’s mixed with poison. ^sn12-66-9-6

Drink it if you like. ^sn12-66-9-7

If you drink it, the color, aroma, and flavor will be appetizing, but it will result in death or deadly pain.’ ^sn12-66-9-8

He wouldn’t put that beverage down. Hastily, without reflection, he’d drink it, ^sn12-66-9-9

resulting in death or deadly pain. ^sn12-66-9-10

In the same way, there are ascetics and brahmins of the past … ^sn12-66-9-11

future … ^sn12-66-9-12

There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe. Their craving grows. ^sn12-66-9-13

As their craving grows, their attachments grow. ^sn12-66-9-14

As their attachments grow, their suffering grows. ^sn12-66-9-15

And as their suffering grows, they are not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are not freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-9-16

There were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous. ^sn12-66-10-1

They gave up craving. ^sn12-66-10-2

Giving up craving, they gave up attachments. ^sn12-66-10-3

Giving up attachments, they gave up suffering. ^sn12-66-10-4

Giving up suffering, they were freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-10-5

There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future who will see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous. ^sn12-66-11-1

They will give up craving. ^sn12-66-11-2

Giving up craving … ^sn12-66-11-3

they will be freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-11-4

There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous. ^sn12-66-12-1

They give up craving. ^sn12-66-12-2

Giving up craving, they give up attachments. ^sn12-66-12-3

Giving up attachments, they give up suffering. ^sn12-66-12-4

Giving up suffering, they are freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-12-5

Suppose there was a bronze goblet of beverage that had a nice color, aroma, and flavor. ^sn12-66-13-1

But it was mixed with poison. ^sn12-66-13-2

Then along comes a man struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^sn12-66-13-3

They’d say to him: ^sn12-66-13-4

‘Here, my man, this bronze goblet of beverage has a nice color, aroma, and flavor. ^sn12-66-13-5

But it’s mixed with poison. ^sn12-66-13-6

Drink it if you like. ^sn12-66-13-7

If you drink it, its nice color, aroma, and flavor will refresh you. But drinking it will result in death or deadly pain.’ ^sn12-66-13-8

Then that man might think: ^sn12-66-13-9

‘I can allay my thirst with a fermented drink of beer, buttermilk, salt lassi, or salted infusion. But in any case, I shouldn’t drink that beverage, for it would be for my lasting harm and suffering.’ ^sn12-66-13-10

He’d put that beverage down. After appraisal, he wouldn’t drink it, ^sn12-66-13-11

and it wouldn’t result in death or deadly pain. ^sn12-66-13-12

In the same way, there were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous. ^sn12-66-13-13

They gave up craving. ^sn12-66-13-14

Giving up craving, they gave up attachments. ^sn12-66-13-15

Giving up attachments, they gave up suffering. ^sn12-66-13-16

Giving up suffering, they were freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were freed from suffering, I say. ^sn12-66-13-17

There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future … ^sn12-66-14-1

There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous. ^sn12-66-14-2

They give up craving. ^sn12-66-14-3

Giving up craving, they give up attachments. ^sn12-66-14-4

Giving up attachments, they give up suffering. ^sn12-66-14-5

Giving up suffering, they are freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn12-66-14-6

### Sheaves of Reeds (Linked Discourses 12.67) ^sn12-67-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn12-67-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-67-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^sn12-67-1-3

“Well, Reverend Sāriputta, are old age and death made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-67-1-4

“No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, old age and death are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-1-5

Rather, rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.” ^sn12-67-1-6

“Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is rebirth made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-67-2-1

“No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, rebirth is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-2-2

Rather, continued existence is a requirement for rebirth.” ^sn12-67-2-3

“Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is continued existence made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-1

“Is grasping made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-2

“Is craving made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-3

“Is feeling made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-4

“Is contact made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-5

“Are the six sense fields made by oneself? …” … ^sn12-67-3-6

“Well, Reverend Sāriputta, are name and form made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-67-3-7

“No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, name and form are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-3-8

Rather, consciousness is a requirement for name and form.” ^sn12-67-3-9

“Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is consciousness made by oneself? Or by another? Or by both oneself and another? Or does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?” ^sn12-67-4-1

“No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, consciousness is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-4-2

Rather, name and form are requirements for consciousness.” ^sn12-67-4-3

“Just now I understood you to say: ^sn12-67-5-1

‘No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, name and form are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-5-2

Rather, consciousness is a requirement for name and form.’ ^sn12-67-5-3

But I also understood you to say: ^sn12-67-6-1

‘No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, consciousness is not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor does it arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another. ^sn12-67-6-2

Rather, name and form are requirements for consciousness.’ ^sn12-67-6-3

How then should we see the meaning of this statement?” ^sn12-67-7-1

“Well then, reverend, I shall give you a simile. ^sn12-67-7-2

For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said. ^sn12-67-7-3

Suppose there were two sheaves of reeds leaning up against each other. ^sn12-67-7-4

In the same way, name and form are requirements for consciousness. ^sn12-67-7-5

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. ^sn12-67-7-6

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. ^sn12-67-7-7

The six sense fields are requirements for contact. … ^sn12-67-7-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn12-67-7-9

If the first of those sheaves of reeds were to be pulled away, the other would collapse. ^sn12-67-7-10

And if the other were to be pulled away, the first would collapse. ^sn12-67-7-11

In the same way, when name and form cease, consciousness ceases. ^sn12-67-7-12

When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. ^sn12-67-7-13

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. ^sn12-67-7-14

When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. … ^sn12-67-7-15

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn12-67-7-16

“It’s incredible, Reverend Sāriputta, it’s amazing! ^sn12-67-8-1

How well spoken this was by Venerable Sāriputta! ^sn12-67-8-3

And we can express our agreement with Venerable Sāriputta’s statement on these thirty-six grounds. ^sn12-67-8-4

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn12-67-9-1

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn12-67-9-2

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding old age and death, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’. ^sn12-67-9-3

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment regarding rebirth … ^sn12-67-9-4

continued existence … ^sn12-67-9-5

grasping … ^sn12-67-9-6

craving … ^sn12-67-9-7

feeling … ^sn12-67-9-8

contact … ^sn12-67-9-9

the six sense fields … ^sn12-67-9-10

name and form … ^sn12-67-9-11

consciousness … ^sn12-67-9-12

choices … ^sn12-67-9-13

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn12-67-9-14

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn12-67-9-15

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.” ^sn12-67-9-16

### At Kosambī (Linked Discourses 12.68) ^sn12-68-0

At one time the venerables Musīla, Saviṭṭha, Nārada, and Ānanda were staying near Kosambī in Ghosita’s monastery. ^sn12-68-1-1

Then Venerable Saviṭṭha said to Venerable Musīla: ^sn12-68-1-2

“Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-1-3

rebirth is a requirement for old age and death?” ^sn12-68-1-4

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-1-5

rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.” ^sn12-68-1-6

“Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberateation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-2-1

continued existence is a requirement for rebirth … ^sn12-68-2-2

grasping is a requirement for continued existence … ^sn12-68-2-3

craving is a requirement for grasping … ^sn12-68-2-4

feeling is a requirement for craving … ^sn12-68-2-5

contact is a requirement for feeling … ^sn12-68-2-6

the six sense fields are requirements for contact … ^sn12-68-2-7

name and form are requirements for the six sense fields … ^sn12-68-2-8

consciousness is a requirement for name and form … ^sn12-68-2-9

choices are a requirement for consciousness … ^sn12-68-2-10

ignorance is a requirement for choices?” ^sn12-68-2-11

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-2-12

ignorance is a requirement for choices.” ^sn12-68-2-13

“Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-3-1

when rebirth ceases, old age and death cease?” ^sn12-68-3-2

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-3-3

when rebirth ceases, old age and death cease.” ^sn12-68-3-4

“Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-4-1

when continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases … ^sn12-68-4-2

when grasping ceases, continued existence ceases … ^sn12-68-4-3

when craving ceases, grasping ceases … ^sn12-68-4-4

when feeling ceases, craving ceases … ^sn12-68-4-5

when contact ceases, feeling ceases … ^sn12-68-4-6

when the six sense fields cease, contact ceases … ^sn12-68-4-7

when name and form cease, the six sense fields cease … ^sn12-68-4-8

when consciousness ceases name and form cease … ^sn12-68-4-9

when choices cease consciousness ceases … ^sn12-68-4-10

when ignorance ceases, choices cease?” ^sn12-68-4-11

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-4-12

when ignorance ceases, choices cease.” ^sn12-68-4-13

“Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-5-1

the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment?” ^sn12-68-5-2

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-5-3

the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment.” ^sn12-68-5-4

“Then Venerable Musīla is a perfected one, with defilements ended.” ^sn12-68-6-1

When he said this, Musīla kept silent. ^sn12-68-6-2

Then Venerable Nārada said to Venerable Saviṭṭha, ^sn12-68-7-1

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, please let me answer these questions. ^sn12-68-7-2

Ask me ^sn12-68-7-3

and I will answer them for you.” ^sn12-68-7-4

“By all means, Venerable Nārada, try these questions. ^sn12-68-7-5

I’ll ask you ^sn12-68-7-6

and you can answer them for me.” ^sn12-68-7-7

(Saviṭṭha repeats exactly the same series of questions, and Nārada answers just as Musīla did.) ^sn12-68-8-1

“Reverend Nārada, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that ^sn12-68-12-1

the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment?” ^sn12-68-12-2

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, I know and see that ^sn12-68-12-3

the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment.” ^sn12-68-12-4

“Then Venerable Nārada is a perfected one, with defilements ended.” ^sn12-68-13-1

“I have truly seen clearly with right wisdom that the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment. Yet I am not a perfected one. ^sn12-68-13-2

Suppose there was a well on a desert road that had neither rope nor bucket. ^sn12-68-13-3

Then along comes a person struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^sn12-68-13-4

They’d know that there was water, but they couldn’t physically touch it. ^sn12-68-13-5

In the same way, I have truly seen clearly with right wisdom that the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment. Yet I am not a perfected one.” ^sn12-68-13-6

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to Venerable Saviṭṭha, ^sn12-68-14-1

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, what do you have to say to Venerable Nārada when he speaks like this?” ^sn12-68-14-2

“Reverend Ānanda, I have nothing to say to Venerable Nārada when he speaks like this, except what is good and wholesome.” ^sn12-68-14-3

### Surge (Linked Discourses 12.69) ^sn12-69-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-69-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn12-69-1-2

“Mendicants, when the ocean surges it makes the rivers surge. When the rivers surge they make the streams surge. When the streams surge they make the lakes surge. When the lakes surge they make the ponds surge. ^sn12-69-1-4

In the same way, when ignorance surges it makes choices surge. When choices surge they make consciousness surge. When consciousness surges it makes name and form surge. When name and form surge they make the six sense fields surge. When the six sense fields surge they make contact surge. When contact surges it makes feeling surge. When feeling surges it makes craving surge. When craving surges it makes grasping surge. When grasping surges it makes continued existence surge. When continued existence surges it makes rebirth surge. When rebirth surges it makes old age and death surge. ^sn12-69-1-5

When the ocean recedes it makes the rivers recede. When the rivers recede they make the streams recede. When the streams recede they make the lakes recede. When the lakes recede they make the ponds recede. ^sn12-69-2-1

In the same way, when ignorance recedes it makes choices recede. When choices recede they make consciousness recede. When consciousness recedes it makes name and form recede. When name and form recede they make the six sense fields recede. When the six sense fields recede they make contact recede. When contact recedes it makes feeling recede. When feeling recedes it makes craving recede. When craving recedes it makes grasping recede. When grasping recedes it makes continued existence recede. When continued existence recedes it makes rebirth recede. When rebirth recedes it makes old age and death recede.” ^sn12-69-2-2

### The Wanderer Susīma (Linked Discourses 12.70) ^sn12-70-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-70-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn12-70-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha was honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And he received robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^sn12-70-1-3

And the mendicant Saṅgha was also honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And they received robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^sn12-70-1-4

But the wanderers who followed other religions were not honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And they didn’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^sn12-70-1-5

Now at that time the wanderer Susīma was residing at Rājagaha together with a large community of wanderers. ^sn12-70-2-1

Then his community said to Susīma, ^sn12-70-2-2

“Reverend Susīma, please lead the spiritual life with the ascetic Gotama. ^sn12-70-2-3

Memorize that teaching and have us recite it with you. ^sn12-70-2-4

When we’ve memorized it we’ll recite it to the laity. ^sn12-70-2-5

In this way we too will be honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And we’ll receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.” ^sn12-70-2-6

“Yes, reverends,” replied Susīma to his own assembly. Then he went to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn12-70-2-7

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda, ^sn12-70-2-8

“Reverend Ānanda, I wish to lead the spiritual life in this teaching and training.” ^sn12-70-2-9

Then Ānanda took Susīma to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn12-70-3-1

“Sir, this wanderer Susīma says that ^sn12-70-3-2

he wishes to lead the spiritual life in this teaching and training.” ^sn12-70-3-3

“Well then, Ānanda, give Susīma the going forth.” ^sn12-70-3-4

And the wanderer Susīma received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^sn12-70-3-5

Now at that time several mendicants had declared their enlightenment in the Buddha’s presence: ^sn12-70-4-1

“We understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn12-70-4-2

Venerable Susīma heard about this. ^sn12-70-4-3

He went up to those mendicants, and exchanged greetings with them. ^sn12-70-4-6

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to those mendicants, ^sn12-70-4-7

“Is it really true that the venerables have declared enlightenment in the Buddha’s presence?” ^sn12-70-4-8

“Yes, reverend.” ^sn12-70-4-10

“But knowing and seeing thus, do you wield the many kinds of psychic power? That is, multiplying yourselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. Do you control the body as far as the realm of divinity?” ^sn12-70-5-1

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-5-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus do you, with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far?” ^sn12-70-6-1

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-6-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with your mind? ^sn12-70-7-1

Do you understand mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’, ^sn12-70-7-2

and mind without greed as ‘mind without greed’? ^sn12-70-7-3

Do you understand mind with hate as ‘mind with hate’, ^sn12-70-7-4

and mind without hate as ‘mind without hate’? ^sn12-70-7-5

Do you understand mind with delusion as ‘mind with delusion’, ^sn12-70-7-6

and mind without delusion as ‘mind without delusion’? ^sn12-70-7-7

Do you understand constricted mind as ‘constricted mind’, ^sn12-70-7-8

and scattered mind as ‘scattered mind’? ^sn12-70-7-9

Do you understand expansive mind as ‘expansive mind’, ^sn12-70-7-10

and unexpansive mind as ‘unexpansive mind’? ^sn12-70-7-11

Do you understand mind that is not supreme as ‘mind that is not supreme’, ^sn12-70-7-12

and mind that is supreme as ‘mind that is supreme’? ^sn12-70-7-13

Do you understand mind immersed in samādhi as ‘mind immersed in samādhi’, ^sn12-70-7-14

and mind not immersed in samādhi as ‘mind not immersed in samādhi’? ^sn12-70-7-15

Do you understand freed mind as ‘freed mind’, ^sn12-70-7-16

and unfreed mind as ‘unfreed mind’?” ^sn12-70-7-17

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-7-18

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you recollect many kinds of past lives? That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding? Do you remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ Do you recollect your many kinds of past lives, with features and details?” ^sn12-70-8-1

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-8-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place—and understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds? ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, do you see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. And do you understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds?” ^sn12-70-9-1

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-9-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you have direct meditative experience of the peaceful liberations that are formless, transcending form?” ^sn12-70-10-1

“No, reverend.” ^sn12-70-10-2

“Well now, venerables, how could there be such a declaration when these things are not attained?” ^sn12-70-11-1

“Reverend Susīma, we are freed by wisdom.” ^sn12-70-11-2

“I don’t understand the detailed meaning of what you have said in brief. ^sn12-70-12-1

Please teach me this matter so I can understand the detailed meaning.” ^sn12-70-12-2

“Reverend Susīma, whether you understand or not, we are freed by wisdom.” ^sn12-70-12-3

Then Susīma went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^sn12-70-13-1

and informed the Buddha of all he had discussed with those mendicants. ^sn12-70-13-2

“Susīma, first comes knowledge of the stability of natural principles. Afterwards there is knowledge of extinguishment.” ^sn12-70-13-3

“Sir, I don’t understand the detailed meaning of what you have said in brief. ^sn12-70-14-1

Please teach me this matter so I can understand the detailed meaning.” ^sn12-70-14-2

“Susīma, whether you understand or not, first comes knowledge of the stability of natural principles. Afterwards there is knowledge of extinguishment. ^sn12-70-14-3

What do you think, Susīma? ^sn12-70-15-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn12-70-15-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn12-70-15-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn12-70-15-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn12-70-15-7

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-8

“Is feeling permanent or impermanent?” ^sn12-70-15-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-10

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn12-70-15-11

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-12

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn12-70-15-13

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn12-70-15-14

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-15

“Is perception permanent or impermanent?” ^sn12-70-15-16

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn12-70-15-17

“Are choices permanent or impermanent?” ^sn12-70-15-18

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-19

“But if they’re impermanent, are they suffering or happiness?” ^sn12-70-15-20

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-21

“But if they’re impermanent, suffering, and perishable, are they fit to be regarded thus: ^sn12-70-15-22

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn12-70-15-23

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-24

“Is consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn12-70-15-25

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-26

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn12-70-15-27

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-28

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn12-70-15-29

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn12-70-15-30

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-15-31

“So, Susīma, you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn12-70-16-1

You should truly see any kind of feeling at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> feeling—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn12-70-16-2

You should truly see any kind of perception at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> perception—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn12-70-16-3

You should truly see any kind of choices at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> choices—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn12-70-16-4

You should truly see any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn12-70-16-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn12-70-17-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn12-70-17-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn12-70-17-3

Susīma, do you see that rebirth is a requirement for old age and death?” ^sn12-70-18-1

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-18-2

“Do you see that continued existence is a requirement for rebirth?” ^sn12-70-18-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-18-4

“Do you see that grasping is a requirement for continued existence?” ^sn12-70-18-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-18-6

“Do you see that craving is a requirement for grasping?” ^sn12-70-18-7

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-18-8

“Do you see that feeling is a requirement for craving … ^sn12-70-18-9

contact is a requirement for feeling … ^sn12-70-18-10

the six sense fields are requirements for contact … ^sn12-70-18-11

name and form are requirements for the six sense fields … ^sn12-70-18-12

consciousness is a requirement for name and form … ^sn12-70-18-13

choices are a requirement for consciousness … ^sn12-70-18-14

ignorance is a requirement for choices?” ^sn12-70-18-15

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-18-16

“Do you see that when rebirth ceases old age and death cease?” ^sn12-70-19-1

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-19-2

“Do you see that when continued existence ceases rebirth ceases?” ^sn12-70-19-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-19-4

“Do you see that when grasping ceases continued existence ceases … ^sn12-70-19-5

when craving ceases, grasping ceases … ^sn12-70-19-6

when feeling ceases, craving ceases … ^sn12-70-19-7

when contact ceases, feeling ceases … ^sn12-70-19-8

when the six sense fields cease, contact ceases … ^sn12-70-19-9

when name and form cease, the six sense fields cease … ^sn12-70-19-10

when consciousness ceases name and form cease … ^sn12-70-19-11

when choices cease consciousness ceases … ^sn12-70-19-12

when ignorance ceases choices cease?” ^sn12-70-19-13

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-19-14

“But knowing and seeing thus, do you wield the many kinds of psychic power? …” ^sn12-70-20-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-20-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus do you, with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far?” ^sn12-70-21-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-21-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with your mind? …” ^sn12-70-22-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-22-4

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details?” ^sn12-70-23-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-23-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, see sentient beings passing away and being reborn … according to their deeds?” ^sn12-70-24-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-24-2

“Well, knowing and seeing thus, do you have direct meditative experience of the peaceful liberations that are formless, transcending form?” ^sn12-70-25-1

“No, sir.” ^sn12-70-25-2

“Well now, Susīma, how could there be such a declaration when these things are not attained?” ^sn12-70-26-1

Then Venerable Susīma bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet and said, ^sn12-70-27-1

“I made a mistake, sir. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of me to go forth as a thief in such a well-explained teaching and training. ^sn12-70-27-2

Please, sir, accept my mistake for what it is, so I will restrain myself in future.” ^sn12-70-27-3

“Indeed, Susīma, you made a mistake. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of you to go forth as a thief of the teachings in such a well-explained teaching and training. ^sn12-70-28-1

Suppose they were to arrest a bandit, a criminal and present him to the king, saying: ^sn12-70-28-2

‘Your Majesty, this is a bandit, a criminal. Punish him as you will.’ ^sn12-70-28-3

The king would say: ^sn12-70-28-4

‘Go, my men, and tie this man’s arms tightly behind his back with a strong rope. Shave his head and march him from street to street and from square to square to the beating of a harsh drum. Then take him out the south gate and there, to the south of the city, chop off his head.’ ^sn12-70-28-5

The king’s men would do as they were told. ^sn12-70-28-6

What do you think, Susīma? ^sn12-70-28-7

Wouldn’t that man experience pain and distress because of that?” ^sn12-70-28-8

“Yes, sir.” ^sn12-70-28-9

“Although that man would experience pain and distress because of that, ^sn12-70-29-1

going forth as a thief of the teachings in such a well-explained teaching and training has a more painful and bitter result. And it even leads to the underworld. ^sn12-70-29-2

But since you have recognized your mistake for what it is, and have dealt with it properly, I accept it. ^sn12-70-29-3

For it is growth in the training of the Noble One to recognize a mistake for what it is, deal with it properly, and commit to restraint in the future.” ^sn12-70-29-4

### Old Age and Death (Linked Discourses 12.71) ^sn12-71-0

So I have heard. ^sn12-71-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn12-71-1-2

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-71-1-4

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn12-71-1-5

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^sn12-71-2-1

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn12-71-2-2

### A Set of Ten on Rebirth, Etc. (Linked Discourses 12.72–81) ^sn12-72-81-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-72-81-1-1

“… they don’t understand rebirth …” ^sn12-72-81-1-2

“… continued existence …” ^sn12-72-81-2-1

“… grasping …” ^sn12-72-81-3-1

“… craving …” ^sn12-72-81-4-1

“… feeling …” ^sn12-72-81-5-1

“… contact …” ^sn12-72-81-6-1

“… the six sense fields …” ^sn12-72-81-7-1

“… name and form …” ^sn12-72-81-8-1

“… consciousness …” ^sn12-72-81-9-1

“… choices … ^sn12-72-81-10-1

… they understand …” ^sn12-72-81-10-2

### The Teacher (Linked Discourses 12.82) ^sn12-82-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn12-82-1-1

“Mendicants, one who does not truly know or see old age and death should seek the Teacher so as to truly know old age and death. ^sn12-82-1-2

One who does not truly know or see the origin of old age and death should seek the Teacher so as to truly know the origin of old age and death. ^sn12-82-1-3

One who does not truly know or see the cessation of old age and death should seek the Teacher so as to truly know the cessation of old age and death. ^sn12-82-1-4

One who does not truly know or see the practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death should seek the Teacher so as to truly know the practice that leads to the cessation of old age and death.” ^sn12-82-1-5

(One discourse.) ^sn12-82-1-6

(All the abbreviated texts should be told in full.) ^sn12-82-1-8

“… choices …” ^sn12-92-1-1

“… practice meditation …” ^sn12-104-114-1-2
