## Saṁyutta 13 ^sn13-0

### A Fingernail (Linked Discourses 13.1) ^sn13-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn13-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn13-1-1-2

Then the Buddha, picking up a little bit of dirt on his fingernail, addressed the mendicants: ^sn13-1-1-3

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-1-1-4

Which is more: the little bit of dirt on my fingernail, or this great earth?” ^sn13-1-1-5

“Sir, the great earth is far more. ^sn13-1-2-1

The little bit of dirt on your fingernail is tiny. ^sn13-1-2-2

Compared to the great earth, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-1-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple accomplished in view, an individual with comprehension, the suffering that’s over and done with is more, ^sn13-1-2-4

what’s left is tiny. ^sn13-1-2-5

Compared to the mass of suffering in the past that’s over and done with, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part, since there are at most seven more lives. ^sn13-1-2-6

That’s how very beneficial it is to comprehend the teaching ^sn13-1-2-7

and gain the vision of the teaching.” ^sn13-1-2-8

### A Lotus Pond (Linked Discourses 13.2) ^sn13-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-2-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose there was a lotus pond that was fifty leagues long, fifty leagues wide, and fifty leagues deep, full to the brim so a crow could drink from it. ^sn13-2-1-2

Then a person would pick up some water on the tip of a blade of grass. ^sn13-2-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-2-1-4

Which is more: the water on the tip of the blade of grass, or the water in the lotus pond?” ^sn13-2-1-5

“Sir, the water in the lotus pond is certainly more. ^sn13-2-2-1

The water on the tip of a blade of grass is tiny. ^sn13-2-2-2

Compared to the water in the lotus pond, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-2-2-3

“In the same way, for an individual with comprehension, a noble disciple accomplished in view, the suffering that’s over and done with is more, what’s left is tiny. ^sn13-2-3-1

Compared to the mass of suffering in the past that’s over and done with, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part, since there are at most seven more lives. ^sn13-2-3-2

That’s how very beneficial it is to comprehend the teaching and gain the vision of the teaching.” ^sn13-2-3-3

### Where the Waters Join Together (Linked Discourses 13.3) ^sn13-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-3-1-1

“Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamunā, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge. ^sn13-3-1-2

Suppose a person was to draw two or three drops of water from such a place. ^sn13-3-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-3-1-4

Which is more: the two or three drops drawn out or the water in the confluence?” ^sn13-3-1-5

“Sir, the water in the confluence is certainly more. ^sn13-3-2-1

The two or three drops drawn out are tiny. ^sn13-3-2-2

Compared to the water in the confluence, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-3-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-3-2-4

### Where the Waters Join Together (2nd) (Linked Discourses 13.4) ^sn13-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-4-1-1

“Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamunā, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge. ^sn13-4-1-2

Suppose that water dried up and evaporated except for two or three drops. ^sn13-4-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-4-1-4

Which is more: the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, or the two or three drops left?” ^sn13-4-1-5

“Sir, the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated is certainly more. ^sn13-4-2-1

The two or three drops left are tiny. ^sn13-4-2-2

Compared to the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-4-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-4-2-4

### The Earth (Linked Discourses 13.5) ^sn13-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-5-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose a person was to place seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds on the great earth. ^sn13-5-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-5-1-3

Which is more: the seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds, or the great earth?” ^sn13-5-1-4

“Sir, the great earth is certainly more. ^sn13-5-2-1

The seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds are tiny. ^sn13-5-2-2

Compared to the great earth, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-5-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-5-2-4

### The Earth (2nd) (Linked Discourses 13.6) ^sn13-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-6-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose the great earth was worn away and eroded except for seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds. ^sn13-6-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-6-1-3

Which is more: the great earth that has been worn away and eroded, or the seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds that are left?” ^sn13-6-1-4

“Sir, the great earth that has been worn away and eroded is certainly more. ^sn13-6-2-1

The seven clay balls the size of jujube seeds are tiny. ^sn13-6-2-2

Compared to the great earth that has been worn away and eroded, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-6-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-6-2-4

### The Ocean (Linked Discourses 13.7) ^sn13-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-7-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose a man was to draw up two or three drops of water from the ocean. ^sn13-7-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-7-1-3

Which is more: the two or three drops drawn out or the water in the ocean?” ^sn13-7-1-4

“Sir, the water in the ocean is certainly more. ^sn13-7-2-1

The two or three drops drawn out are tiny. ^sn13-7-2-2

Compared to the water in the ocean, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-7-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-7-2-4

### The Ocean (2nd) (Linked Discourses 13.8) ^sn13-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-8-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose the water in the ocean dried up and evaporated except for two or three drops. ^sn13-8-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-8-1-3

Which is more: the water in the ocean that has dried up and evaporated, or the two or three drops left?” ^sn13-8-1-4

“Sir, the water in the ocean that has dried up and evaporated is certainly more. ^sn13-8-2-1

The two or three drops left are tiny. ^sn13-8-2-2

Compared to the water in the ocean that has dried up and evaporated, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-8-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-8-2-4

### A Mountain (Linked Discourses 13.9) ^sn13-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-9-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose a person was to place seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds on the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^sn13-9-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-9-1-3

Which is more: the seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds, or the Himalayas, the king of mountains?” ^sn13-9-1-4

“Sir, the Himalayas, the king of mountains, is certainly more. ^sn13-9-2-1

The seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds are tiny. ^sn13-9-2-2

Compared to the Himalayas, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-9-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …” ^sn13-9-2-4

### A Mountain (2nd) (Linked Discourses 13.10) ^sn13-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-10-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose the Himalayas, the king of mountains, was worn away and eroded except for seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds. ^sn13-10-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-10-1-3

Which is more: the portion of the Himalayas, the king of mountains, that has been worn away and eroded, or the seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds that are left?” ^sn13-10-1-4

“Sir, the portion of the Himalayas, the king of mountains, that has been worn away and eroded is certainly more. ^sn13-10-2-1

The seven pebbles the size of mustard seeds are tiny. ^sn13-10-2-2

Compared to the Himalayas, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-10-2-3

“In the same way, for a noble disciple accomplished in view, an individual with comprehension, the suffering that’s over and done with is more, ^sn13-10-3-1

what’s left is tiny. ^sn13-10-3-2

Compared to the mass of suffering in the past that’s over and done with, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part, since there are at most seven more lives. ^sn13-10-3-3

That’s how very beneficial it is to comprehend the teaching and gain the vision of the teaching.” ^sn13-10-3-4

### A Mountain (3rd) (Linked Discourses 13.11) ^sn13-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn13-11-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose a person was to place on Sineru, the king of mountains, seven pebbles the size of mung beans. ^sn13-11-1-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn13-11-1-3

Which is more: the seven pebbles the size of mung beans, or Sineru, the king of mountains?” ^sn13-11-1-4

“Sir, Sineru, the king of mountains, is certainly more. ^sn13-11-2-1

The seven pebbles the size of mung beans are tiny. ^sn13-11-2-2

Compared to Sineru, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.” ^sn13-11-2-3

“In the same way, compared with the achievements of a noble disciple accomplished in view, the achievements of the ascetics, brahmins, and wanderers of other religions is not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part. ^sn13-11-3-1

So great is the achievement of the individual accomplished in view, so great is their direct knowledge.” ^sn13-11-3-2

The Linked Discourses on comprehension are complete. ^sn13-11-3-4

## Saṁyutta 14 ^sn14-0

### Diversity of Elements (Linked Discourses 14.1) ^sn14-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-1-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the diversity of elements. ^sn14-1-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn14-1-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn14-1-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn14-1-1-5

“And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-1-2-1

The eye element, sight element, and eye consciousness element. The ear element, sound element, and ear consciousness element. The nose element, smell element, and nose consciousness element. The tongue element, taste element, and tongue consciousness element. The body element, touch element, and body consciousness element. The mind element, ideas element, and mind consciousness element. ^sn14-1-2-2

This is called the diversity of elements.” ^sn14-1-2-3

### Diversity of Contacts (Linked Discourses 14.2) ^sn14-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-2-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts. ^sn14-2-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-2-1-3

The eye element, ear element, nose element, tongue element, body element, and mind element. ^sn14-2-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-2-1-5

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of contacts? ^sn14-2-2-1

The eye element gives rise to eye contact. ^sn14-2-2-2

The ear element … ^sn14-2-2-3

nose … ^sn14-2-2-4

tongue … ^sn14-2-2-5

body … ^sn14-2-2-6

The mind element gives rise to mind contact. ^sn14-2-2-7

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts.” ^sn14-2-2-8

### Not Diversity of Contacts (Linked Discourses 14.3) ^sn14-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-3-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts. Diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements. ^sn14-3-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-3-1-3

The eye element, ear element, nose element, tongue element, body element, and mind element. ^sn14-3-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-3-1-6

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of contacts, while diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements? ^sn14-3-2-1

The eye element gives rise to eye contact. Eye contact doesn’t give rise to the eye element. … ^sn14-3-2-2

The mind element gives rise to mind contact. Mind contact doesn’t give rise to the mind element. ^sn14-3-2-3

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, while diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements.” ^sn14-3-2-4

### Diversity of Feelings (Linked Discourses 14.4) ^sn14-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-4-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings. ^sn14-4-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-4-1-3

The eye element, ear element, nose element, tongue element, body element, and mind element. ^sn14-4-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-4-1-5

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings? ^sn14-4-2-1

The eye element gives rise to eye contact. Eye contact gives rise to the feeling born of eye contact. … ^sn14-4-2-2

The mind element gives rise to mind contact. Mind contact gives rise to the feeling born of mind contact. ^sn14-4-2-3

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings.” ^sn14-4-2-4

### Diversity of Feelings (2nd) (Linked Discourses 14.5) ^sn14-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-5-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts. Diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings. Diversity of feelings doesn’t give rise to diversity of contacts. Diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements. ^sn14-5-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-5-1-3

The eye element, ear element, nose element, tongue element, body element, and mind element. ^sn14-5-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-5-1-5

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts give rise to diversity of feelings, while diversity of feelings doesn’t give rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements? ^sn14-5-2-1

The eye element gives rise to eye contact. Eye contact gives rise to feeling born of eye contact. Feeling born of eye contact doesn’t give rise to eye contact. Eye contact doesn’t give rise to the eye element. … ^sn14-5-2-2

The mind element gives rise to mind contact. Mind contact gives rise to feeling born of mind contact. Feeling born of mind contact doesn’t give rise to mind contact. Mind contact doesn’t give rise to the mind element. ^sn14-5-2-3

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings, while diversity of feelings doesn’t give rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements.” ^sn14-5-2-4

### External Diversity of Elements (Linked Discourses 14.6) ^sn14-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-6-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the diversity of elements. ^sn14-6-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-6-1-4

The sight element, the sound element, the smell element, the taste element, the touch element, and the idea element. ^sn14-6-1-5

This is called the diversity of elements.” ^sn14-6-1-6

### Diversity of Perceptions (Linked Discourses 14.7) ^sn14-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-7-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions. Diversity of perceptions gives rise to diversity of thoughts. Diversity of thoughts gives rise to diversity of desires. Diversity of desires gives rise to diversity of passions. Diversity of passions gives rise to diversity of searches. ^sn14-7-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-7-1-3

The sight element, the sound element, the smell element, the taste element, the touch element, and the idea element. ^sn14-7-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-7-1-5

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of perceptions, and diversity of perceptions give rise to diversity of thoughts, and diversity of thoughts give rise to diversity of desires, and diversity of desires give rise to diversity of passions, and diversity of passions give rise to diversity of searches? ^sn14-7-2-1

The sight element gives rise to the perception of sights. The perception of sights gives rise to thoughts about sights. Thoughts about sights give rise to the desire for sights. The desire for sights gives rise to the passion for sights. The passion for sights gives rise to searching for sights. … ^sn14-7-3-1

The idea element gives rise to the perception of ideas. The perception of ideas gives rise to thoughts about ideas. Thoughts about ideas give rise to the desire for ideas. The desire for ideas gives rise to the passion for ideas. The passion for ideas gives rise to searching for ideas. ^sn14-7-3-2

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions, and diversity of perceptions gives rise to diversity of thoughts, and diversity of thoughts gives rise to diversity of desires, and diversity of desires gives rise to diversity of passions, and diversity of passions gives rise to diversity of searches.” ^sn14-7-4-1

### No Diversity of Searches (Linked Discourses 14.8) ^sn14-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-8-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions. Diversity of perceptions gives rise to diversity of thoughts. Diversity of thoughts gives rise to diversity of desires. Diversity of desires gives rise to diversity of passions. Diversity of passions gives rise to diversity of searches. ^sn14-8-1-2

Diversity of searches doesn’t give rise to diversity of passions. Diversity of passions doesn’t give rise to diversity of desires. Diversity of desires doesn’t give rise to diversity of thoughts. Diversity of thoughts doesn’t give rise to diversity of perceptions. Diversity of perceptions doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements. ^sn14-8-1-3

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-8-1-4

The sight element, the sound element, the smell element, the taste element, the touch element, and the idea element. ^sn14-8-1-5

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-8-1-6

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of perceptions … ^sn14-8-2-1

diversity of perceptions doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements? ^sn14-8-2-2

The sight element gives rise to the perception of sights … ^sn14-8-3-1

The idea element gives rise to the perception of ideas … the search for ideas. ^sn14-8-3-2

The search for ideas doesn’t give rise to the passion for ideas. The passion for ideas doesn’t give rise to the desire for ideas. The desire for ideas doesn’t give rise to thoughts about ideas. Thoughts about ideas don’t give rise to perceptions of ideas. Perceptions of ideas don’t give rise to the idea element. ^sn14-8-3-3

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions … ^sn14-8-4-1

diversity of perceptions doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements.” ^sn14-8-4-2

### Diversity of Gains (Linked Discourses 14.9) ^sn14-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-9-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions. Diversity of perceptions gives rise to diversity of thoughts. Diversity of thoughts gives rise to diversity of contacts. Diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings. Diversity of feelings gives rise to diversity of desires. Diversity of desires gives rise to diversity of passions. Diversity of passions gives rise to diversity of searches. Diversity of searches gives rise to diversity of gains. ^sn14-9-1-2

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-9-1-3

The sight element, the sound element, the smell element, the taste element, the touch element, and the idea element. ^sn14-9-1-4

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-9-1-5

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of perceptions … diversity of searches give rise to diversity of gains? ^sn14-9-2-1

The sight element gives rise to the perception of sights. The perception of sights gives rise to thoughts about sights. Thoughts about sights give rise to sight contact. Sight contact gives rise to feeling born of sight contact. Feeling born of sight contact gives rise to the desire for sights. The desire for sights gives rise to the passion for sights. The passion for sights gives rise to searching for sights. Searching for sights gives rise to gaining sights … ^sn14-9-3-1

The idea element gives rise to the perception of ideas. The perception of ideas gives rise to thoughts about ideas. Thoughts about ideas give rise to idea contact. Idea contact gives rise to feeling born of idea contact. Feeling born of idea contact gives rise to the desire for ideas. The desire for ideas gives rise to the passion for ideas. The passion for ideas gives rise to searching for ideas. Searching for ideas gives rise to gaining ideas. ^sn14-9-3-2

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions … diversity of searches gives rise to diversity of gains.” ^sn14-9-4-1

### No Diversity of Gains (Linked Discourses 14.10) ^sn14-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-10-1-1

“Mendicants, diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions. ^sn14-10-1-2

Diversity of perceptions gives rise to diversity of thoughts. … ^sn14-10-1-3

contacts … ^sn14-10-1-4

feelings … ^sn14-10-1-5

desires … ^sn14-10-1-6

passions … ^sn14-10-1-7

Diversity of searches gives rise to diversity of gains. ^sn14-10-1-8

Diversity of gains doesn’t give rise to diversity of searches. ^sn14-10-1-9

Diversity of searches doesn’t give rise to diversity of passions. … ^sn14-10-1-10

desires … ^sn14-10-1-11

feelings … ^sn14-10-1-12

contacts … ^sn14-10-1-13

thoughts … ^sn14-10-1-14

Diversity of perceptions doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements. ^sn14-10-1-15

And what is the diversity of elements? ^sn14-10-1-16

The sight element, the sound element, the smell element, the taste element, the touch element, and the idea element. ^sn14-10-1-17

This is called the diversity of elements. ^sn14-10-1-18

And how does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of perceptions, ^sn14-10-2-1

and diversity of perceptions give rise to diversity of thoughts? ^sn14-10-2-2

contacts … ^sn14-10-2-3

feelings … ^sn14-10-2-4

desires … ^sn14-10-2-5

passions … ^sn14-10-2-6

searches … ^sn14-10-2-7

gains … ^sn14-10-2-8

while diversity of gains doesn’t give rise to diversity of searches … ^sn14-10-2-9

passions … ^sn14-10-2-10

desires … ^sn14-10-2-11

feelings … ^sn14-10-2-12

contacts … ^sn14-10-2-13

thoughts … perceptions … elements? ^sn14-10-2-14

The sight element gives rise to the perception of sights … ^sn14-10-3-1

The idea element gives rise to the perception of ideas … ^sn14-10-3-2

The search for ideas gives rise to gaining ideas. ^sn14-10-3-3

The gaining of ideas doesn’t give rise to the search for ideas. The search for ideas doesn’t give rise to the passion for ideas. The passion for ideas doesn’t give rise to the desire for ideas. The desire for ideas doesn’t give rise to feeling born of idea contact. Feeling born of idea contact doesn’t give rise to idea contact. Idea contact doesn’t give rise to thoughts about ideas. Thoughts about ideas don’t give rise to perceptions of ideas. Perceptions of ideas don’t give rise to the idea element. ^sn14-10-3-4

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of perceptions … ^sn14-10-4-1

diversity of perceptions doesn’t give rise to diversity of elements.” ^sn14-10-4-9

### Seven Elements (Linked Discourses 14.11) ^sn14-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-11-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these seven elements. ^sn14-11-1-2

What seven? ^sn14-11-1-3

The element of luminosity, the element of beauty, the element of the dimension of infinite space, the element of the dimension of infinite consciousness, the element of the dimension of nothingness, the element of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the element of the cessation of perception and feeling. ^sn14-11-1-4

These are the seven elements.” ^sn14-11-1-5

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha, ^sn14-11-2-1

“Sir, due to what does each of these elements appear?” ^sn14-11-2-2

“Mendicant, the element of light appears due to the element of darkness. ^sn14-11-3-1

The element of beauty appears due to the element of ugliness. ^sn14-11-3-2

The element of the dimension of infinite space appears due to the element of form. ^sn14-11-3-3

The element of the dimension of infinite consciousness appears due to the element of the dimension of infinite space. ^sn14-11-3-4

The element of the dimension of nothingness appears due to the element of the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn14-11-3-5

The element of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception appears due to the element of the dimension of nothingness. ^sn14-11-3-6

The element of the cessation of perception and feeling appears due to the element of cessation.” ^sn14-11-3-7

“Sir, how is each of these elements to be attained?” ^sn14-11-4-1

“The elements of light, beauty, the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness, and the dimension of nothingness are attainments with perception. ^sn14-11-5-1

The element of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception is an attainment with residual conditioned phenomena. ^sn14-11-5-2

The element of the cessation of perception and feeling is an attainment of cessation.” ^sn14-11-5-3

### With a Cause (Linked Discourses 14.12) ^sn14-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-12-1-1

“Mendicants, sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts arise for a reason, not without reason. ^sn14-12-1-2

And how do sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts arise for a reason, not without reason? ^sn14-12-2-1

The element of sensuality gives rise to sensual perceptions. Sensual perceptions give rise to sensual purposes. Sensual purposes give rise to sensual desires. Sensual desires give rise to sensual passions. Sensual passions give rise to searches for sensual pleasures. ^sn14-12-2-2

An unlearned ordinary person on a search for sensual pleasures behaves badly in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-2-3

The element of malice gives rise to malicious perceptions. Malicious perceptions give rise to malicious thoughts. … malicious desires … malicious passions … malicious searches … ^sn14-12-3-1

An unlearned ordinary person on a malicious search behaves badly in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-3-2

The element of cruelty gives rise to cruel perceptions. Cruel perceptions give rise to cruel purposes. … cruel desires … cruel passions … cruel searches … ^sn14-12-4-1

An unlearned ordinary person on a cruel search behaves badly in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-4-2

Suppose a person was to drop a burning torch in a thicket of dry grass. If they don’t quickly extinguish it with their hands and feet, the creatures living in the grass and wood would come to ruin. ^sn14-12-5-1

In the same way, a corrupt perception might arise in an ascetic or brahmin. If they don’t quickly give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it, they’ll suffer in the present life, with distress, anguish, and fever. ^sn14-12-5-2

And when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect to be reborn in a bad place. ^sn14-12-5-3

Thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness arise for a reason, not without reason. ^sn14-12-6-1

And how do thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness arise for a reason, not without reason? ^sn14-12-7-1

The element of renunciation gives rise to perceptions of renunciation. ^sn14-12-7-2

Perceptions of renunciation give rise to thoughts of renunciation. ^sn14-12-7-3

The purpose of renunciation give rise to enthusiasm for renunciation. ^sn14-12-7-4

Enthusiasm for renunciation gives rise to passion for renunciation. ^sn14-12-7-5

Fervor for renunciation gives rise to the search for renunciation. ^sn14-12-7-6

A learned noble disciple on a search for renunciation behaves well in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-7-7

The element of good will gives rise to perceptions of good will. ^sn14-12-8-1

Perceptions of good will give rise to thoughts of good will. … ^sn14-12-8-2

enthusiasm for good will … ^sn14-12-8-3

passion for good will … ^sn14-12-8-4

the search for good will. ^sn14-12-8-5

A learned noble disciple on a search for good will behaves well in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-8-6

The element of harmlessness gives rise to perceptions of harmlessness. ^sn14-12-9-1

Perceptions of harmlessness give rise to thoughts of harmlessness. … ^sn14-12-9-2

enthusiasm for harmlessness … ^sn14-12-9-3

passion for harmlessness … ^sn14-12-9-4

the search for harmlessness. ^sn14-12-9-5

A learned noble disciple on a search for harmlessness behaves well in three ways: by body, speech, and mind. ^sn14-12-9-6

Suppose a person was to drop a burning torch in a thicket of dry grass. If they were to quickly extinguish it with their hands and feet, the creatures living in the grass and wood wouldn’t come to ruin. ^sn14-12-10-1

In the same way, a corrupt perception might arise in an ascetic or brahmin. If they quickly give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it, they’ll be happy in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever. ^sn14-12-10-2

And when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect to be reborn in a good place.” ^sn14-12-10-3

### In the Brick Hall (Linked Discourses 14.13) ^sn14-13-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^sn14-13-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn14-13-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn14-13-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn14-13-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn14-13-1-5

“Mendicants, an element gives rise to a perception, a view, and a thought.” ^sn14-13-2-1

When he said this, Venerable Kaccāna said to the Buddha, ^sn14-13-2-2

“Sir, regarding ^sn14-13-2-3

those who are not fully awakened Buddhas, there is a view that they are in fact fully awakened Buddhas. Due to what does this view appear?” ^sn14-13-2-4

“It’s a mighty thing, Kaccāna, the element of ignorance. ^sn14-13-3-1

An inferior element gives rise to inferior perception, inferior view, inferior thought, inferior intention, inferior aim, inferior wish, an inferior person, and inferior speech. ^sn14-13-3-2

One explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals the inferior. ^sn14-13-3-3

I say that their rebirth is inferior. ^sn14-13-3-4

A middling element gives rise to middling perception, middling view, middling thought, middling intention, middling aim, middling wish, a middling person, and middling speech. ^sn14-13-4-1

One explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals the middling. ^sn14-13-4-2

I say that their rebirth is middling. ^sn14-13-4-3

A superior element gives rise to superior perception, superior view, superior thought, superior intention, superior aim, superior wish, a superior person, and superior speech. ^sn14-13-5-1

One explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals the superior. ^sn14-13-5-2

I say that their rebirth is superior.” ^sn14-13-5-3

### Inferior Convictions (Linked Discourses 14.14) ^sn14-14-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-14-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-14-1-2

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-14-1-3

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions. ^sn14-14-1-4

In the past, too, sentient beings came together and converged because of an element. … ^sn14-14-2-1

In the future, too, sentient beings will join together and converge because of an element. … ^sn14-14-3-1

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-14-4-1

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-14-4-2

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions.” ^sn14-14-4-3

### Walking Together (Linked Discourses 14.15) ^sn14-15-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn14-15-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Sāriputta was walking together with several mendicants not far from the Buddha. ^sn14-15-1-2

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was doing likewise, as were ^sn14-15-1-3

Venerable Mahākassapa, ^sn14-15-1-4

Venerable Anuruddha, ^sn14-15-1-5

Venerable Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī, ^sn14-15-1-6

Venerable Upāli, ^sn14-15-1-7

Venerable Ānanda, ^sn14-15-1-8

and Devadatta. ^sn14-15-1-9

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn14-15-2-1

“Mendicants, do you see Sāriputta walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-2

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-3

“All of those mendicants have great wisdom. ^sn14-15-2-4

Do you see Moggallāna walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-6

“All of those mendicants have great psychic power. ^sn14-15-2-7

Do you see Kassapa walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-8

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-9

“All of those mendicants advocate austerities. ^sn14-15-2-10

Do you see Anuruddha walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-11

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-12

“All of those mendicants have clairvoyance. ^sn14-15-2-13

Do you see Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-14

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-15

“All of those mendicants are Dhamma speakers. ^sn14-15-2-16

Do you see Upāli walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-17

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-18

“All of those mendicants are experts in monastic law. ^sn14-15-2-19

Do you see Ānanda walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-20

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-21

“All of those mendicants are very learned. ^sn14-15-2-22

Do you see Devadatta walking together with several mendicants?” ^sn14-15-2-23

“Yes, sir.” ^sn14-15-2-24

“All of those mendicants have corrupt wishes. ^sn14-15-2-25

Sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-15-3-1

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-15-3-2

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions. ^sn14-15-3-3

In the past, ^sn14-15-3-4

in the future, ^sn14-15-4-1

and also in the present, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-15-5-1

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-15-5-2

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions.” ^sn14-15-5-3

### With Verses (Linked Discourses 14.16) ^sn14-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-16-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-1-2

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-16-1-3

In the past … ^sn14-16-1-4

In the future … ^sn14-16-2-1

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-3-1

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-16-3-2

It’s like how dung joins together with dung, ^sn14-16-4-1

urine with urine, ^sn14-16-4-2

spit with spit, ^sn14-16-4-3

pus with pus, ^sn14-16-4-4

and blood with blood. ^sn14-16-4-5

In the same way, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-4-6

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-16-4-7

In the past … ^sn14-16-4-8

In the future … ^sn14-16-4-9

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-4-10

Those who have inferior convictions join together and converge with those who have inferior convictions. ^sn14-16-4-11

Sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-5-1

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions. ^sn14-16-5-2

In the past … ^sn14-16-5-3

In the future … ^sn14-16-6-1

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-6-2

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions. ^sn14-16-6-3

It’s like how milk joins together with milk, ^sn14-16-7-1

oil with oil, ^sn14-16-7-2

ghee with ghee, ^sn14-16-7-3

honey with honey, ^sn14-16-7-4

and molasses with molasses. ^sn14-16-7-5

In the same way, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-7-6

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions. ^sn14-16-7-7

In the past … ^sn14-16-7-8

In the future … ^sn14-16-7-9

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. ^sn14-16-7-10

Those who have good convictions join together and converge with those who have good convictions.” ^sn14-16-7-11

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn14-16-8-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn14-16-8-2

“Socializing creates a snarl; ^sn14-16-9-1

they’re cut off by being aloof. ^sn14-16-9-2

If you’re lost in the middle of a great sea, ^sn14-16-9-3

and you clamber up on a little log, you’ll sink. ^sn14-16-9-4

So too, a person who lives well ^sn14-16-10-1

sinks by relying on a lazy person. ^sn14-16-10-2

Hence you should avoid such ^sn14-16-10-3

a lazy person who lacks energy. ^sn14-16-10-4

Dwell with the noble ones ^sn14-16-11-1

who are secluded and determined ^sn14-16-11-2

and always energetic; ^sn14-16-11-3

the astute who practice absorption.” ^sn14-16-11-4

### Faithless (Linked Discourses 14.17) ^sn14-17-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-17-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-17-1-2

the faithless with the faithless, ^sn14-17-1-3

the unconscientious with the unconscientious, ^sn14-17-1-4

the imprudent with the imprudent, ^sn14-17-1-5

the unlearned with the unlearned, ^sn14-17-1-6

the lazy with the lazy, ^sn14-17-1-7

the unmindful with the unmindful, ^sn14-17-1-8

and the witless with the witless. ^sn14-17-1-9

In the future, too, sentient beings will join together and converge because of an element. … ^sn14-17-3-1

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. … ^sn14-17-4-1

the faithful with the faithful, ^sn14-17-5-2

the conscientious with the conscientious, ^sn14-17-5-3

the prudent with the prudent, ^sn14-17-5-4

the learned with the learned, ^sn14-17-5-5

the energetic with the energetic, ^sn14-17-5-6

the mindful with the mindful, ^sn14-17-5-7

and the wise with the wise. ^sn14-17-5-8

In the past … ^sn14-17-5-9

In the future … ^sn14-17-5-10

At present, too, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element. …” ^sn14-17-5-11

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-25-1-2

those who kill living creatures with those who kill living creatures, ^sn14-25-1-3

those who steal … ^sn14-25-1-4

commit sexual misconduct … ^sn14-25-1-5

lie … ^sn14-25-1-6

consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor … ^sn14-25-1-7

Those who refrain from killing living creatures … ^sn14-25-2-1

who refrain from stealing … ^sn14-25-2-2

who refrain from sexual misconduct … ^sn14-25-2-3

who refrain from lying … ^sn14-25-2-4

those who refrain from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor with those who refrain from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor.” ^sn14-25-2-5

### Seven Ways of Performing Deeds (Linked Discourses 14.26) ^sn14-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-26-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-26-1-2

those who kill living creatures with those who kill living creatures, ^sn14-26-1-3

those who steal … ^sn14-26-1-4

commit sexual misconduct … ^sn14-26-1-5

lie … ^sn14-26-1-6

backbite … ^sn14-26-1-7

speak harshly … ^sn14-26-1-8

talk nonsense … ^sn14-26-1-9

Those who refrain from killing living creatures. … ^sn14-26-2-1

who refrain from stealing … ^sn14-26-2-2

who refrain from sexual misconduct … ^sn14-26-2-3

who refrain from lying … ^sn14-26-2-4

who refrain from backbiting … ^sn14-26-2-5

who refrain from harsh speech … ^sn14-26-2-6

who refrain from talking nonsense with those who refrain from talking nonsense.” ^sn14-26-2-7

### Ten Ways of Performing Deeds (Linked Discourses 14.27) ^sn14-27-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-27-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-27-1-2

those who kill living creatures with those who kill living creatures, ^sn14-27-1-3

those who steal … ^sn14-27-1-4

commit sexual misconduct … ^sn14-27-1-5

lie … ^sn14-27-1-6

backbite … ^sn14-27-1-7

speak harshly … ^sn14-27-1-8

talk nonsense … ^sn14-27-1-9

are covetous … ^sn14-27-1-10

are malicious … ^sn14-27-1-11

have wrong view … ^sn14-27-1-12

Those who refrain from killing living creatures … ^sn14-27-2-1

who refrain from stealing … ^sn14-27-2-2

who refrain from sexual misconduct … ^sn14-27-2-3

who refrain from lying … ^sn14-27-2-4

who refrain from backbiting … ^sn14-27-2-5

who refrain from harsh speech … ^sn14-27-2-6

who refrain from talking nonsense … ^sn14-27-2-7

are not covetous … ^sn14-27-2-8

are not malicious … ^sn14-27-2-9

have right view with those who have right view.” ^sn14-27-2-10

### The Eightfold Path (Linked Discourses 14.28) ^sn14-28-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-28-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings join together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-28-1-2

those of wrong view with those of wrong view … ^sn14-28-1-3

wrong purpose … ^sn14-28-1-4

wrong speech … ^sn14-28-1-5

wrong action … ^sn14-28-1-6

wrong livelihood … ^sn14-28-1-7

wrong effort … ^sn14-28-1-8

wrong mindfulness … ^sn14-28-1-9

wrong immersion … ^sn14-28-1-10

Those who have right view … ^sn14-28-2-1

right purpose … ^sn14-28-2-2

right speech … ^sn14-28-2-3

right action … ^sn14-28-2-4

right livelihood … ^sn14-28-2-5

right effort … ^sn14-28-2-6

right mindfulness … ^sn14-28-2-7

right immersion with those who have right immersion.” ^sn14-28-2-8

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-29-1-1

“Mendicants, sentient beings come together and converge because of an element: ^sn14-29-1-2

those of wrong view with those of wrong view … ^sn14-29-1-3

wrong purpose … ^sn14-29-1-4

wrong speech … ^sn14-29-1-5

right speech … ^sn14-29-2-3

right livelihood … ^sn14-29-2-5

right effort … ^sn14-29-2-6

right mindfulness … ^sn14-29-2-7

right immersion … ^sn14-29-2-8

right knowledge … ^sn14-29-2-9

right freedom with those who have right freedom.” ^sn14-29-2-10

(Tell all in full for the past, future, and present.) ^sn14-29-2-12

### Four Elements (Linked Discourses 14.30) ^sn14-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. … ^sn14-30-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four elements. ^sn14-30-1-2

What four? ^sn14-30-1-3

The elements of earth, water, fire, and air. ^sn14-30-1-4

These are the four elements.” ^sn14-30-1-5

### Before Awakening (Linked Discourses 14.31) ^sn14-31-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-31-1-1

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn14-31-1-2

‘What’s the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to the earth element … ^sn14-31-1-3

the water element … ^sn14-31-1-4

the fire element … ^sn14-31-1-5

and the air element?’ ^sn14-31-1-6

Then it occurred to me: ^sn14-31-2-1

‘The pleasure and happiness that arise from the earth element: this is its gratification. ^sn14-31-2-2

That the earth element is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn14-31-2-3

Removing and giving up desire and greed for the earth element: this is its escape. ^sn14-31-2-4

The pleasure and happiness that arise from the water element … ^sn14-31-2-5

The pleasure and happiness that arise from the fire element … ^sn14-31-2-6

The pleasure and happiness that arise from the air element: this is its gratification. ^sn14-31-2-7

That the air element is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn14-31-2-8

Removing and giving up desire and greed for the air element: this is its escape.’ ^sn14-31-2-9

As long as I didn’t truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn14-31-3-1

But when I did truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn14-31-4-1

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn14-31-4-2

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn14-31-4-3

### In Search of Gratification (Linked Discourses 14.32) ^sn14-32-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-32-1-1

“Mendicants, I went in search of the earth element’s gratification, and I found it. I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of gratification in the earth element. ^sn14-32-1-2

I went in search of the earth element’s drawback, and I found it. I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the drawback in the earth element. ^sn14-32-1-3

I went in search of escape from the earth element, and I found it. I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of escape from the earth element. ^sn14-32-1-4

I went in search of the water element’s gratification … ^sn14-32-2-1

I went in search of the fire element’s gratification … ^sn14-32-2-2

I went in search of the air element’s gratification … ^sn14-32-2-3

As long as I didn’t truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn14-32-3-1

But when I did truly understand the four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn14-32-4-1

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn14-32-4-2

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn14-32-4-3

### If There Was No (Linked Discourses 14.33) ^sn14-33-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-33-1-1

“Mendicants, if there were no gratification in the earth element, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn14-33-1-2

But since there is gratification in the earth element, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn14-33-1-3

If the earth element had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn14-33-1-4

But since the earth element has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn14-33-1-5

If there were no escape from the earth element, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^sn14-33-1-6

But since there is an escape from the earth element, sentient beings do escape from it. ^sn14-33-1-7

If there were no gratification in the water element … ^sn14-33-2-1

If there were no gratification in the fire element … ^sn14-33-2-2

If there were no gratification in the air element … ^sn14-33-2-3

As long as sentient beings don’t truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they haven’t escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they don’t live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries. ^sn14-33-3-1

But when sentient beings truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they’ve escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries.” ^sn14-33-4-1

### Exclusively Painful (Linked Discourses 14.34) ^sn14-34-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-34-1-1

“Mendicants, if the earth element were exclusively painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn14-34-1-2

But because the earth element is pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn14-34-1-3

If the water element … ^sn14-34-2-1

If the fire element … ^sn14-34-2-2

If the air element … ^sn14-34-2-3

If the earth element was perfectly pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn14-34-3-1

But because the earth element is painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn14-34-3-2

If the water element … ^sn14-34-4-1

If the fire element … ^sn14-34-4-2

If the air element was perfectly pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn14-34-4-3

But because the air element is painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it.” ^sn14-34-4-4

### Taking Pleasure (Linked Discourses 14.35) ^sn14-35-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-35-1-1

“Mendicants, if you take pleasure in the earth element, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn14-35-1-2

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn14-35-1-3

If you take pleasure in the water element … ^sn14-35-1-4

If you take pleasure in the fire element … ^sn14-35-1-5

If you take pleasure in the air element, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn14-35-1-6

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn14-35-1-7

If you don’t take pleasure in the earth element, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn14-35-2-1

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say. ^sn14-35-2-2

If you don’t take pleasure in the water element … ^sn14-35-2-3

If you don’t take pleasure in the fire element … ^sn14-35-2-4

If you don’t take pleasure in the air element, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn14-35-2-5

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say.” ^sn14-35-2-6

### Arising (Linked Discourses 14.36) ^sn14-36-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-36-1-1

“Mendicants, the arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the earth element is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn14-36-1-2

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the water element … ^sn14-36-1-3

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the fire element … ^sn14-36-1-4

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the air element is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn14-36-1-5

The cessation, settling, and disappearance of the earth element is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death. ^sn14-36-2-1

The cessation, settling, and ending of the water element … ^sn14-36-2-2

The cessation, settling, and ending of the fire element … ^sn14-36-2-3

The cessation, settling, and disappearance of the air element is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death.” ^sn14-36-2-4

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Linked Discourses 14.37) ^sn14-37-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-37-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four elements. ^sn14-37-1-2

What four? ^sn14-37-1-3

The elements of earth, water, fire, and air. ^sn14-37-1-4

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn14-37-1-5

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn14-37-1-6

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand these four elements’ gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn14-37-2-1

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn14-37-2-2

### Ascetics and Brahmins (2nd) (Linked Discourses 14.38) ^sn14-38-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-38-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four elements. ^sn14-38-1-2

What four? ^sn14-38-1-3

The elements of earth, water, fire, and air. ^sn14-38-1-4

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these four elements’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape … ^sn14-38-1-5

(Tell all in full.) ^sn14-38-1-6

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand these four elements’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape …” ^sn14-38-1-7

### Ascetics and Brahmins (3rd) (Linked Discourses 14.39) ^sn14-39-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn14-39-1-1

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand the earth element, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn14-39-1-2

They don’t understand the water element … ^sn14-39-1-3

fire element … ^sn14-39-1-4

air element … ^sn14-39-1-5

Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn14-39-1-6

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand the earth element, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn14-39-2-1

They do understand the water element … ^sn14-39-2-2

the fire element … ^sn14-39-2-3

the air element … ^sn14-39-2-4

Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn14-39-2-5

The Linked Discourses on the elements are complete. ^sn14-39-3-5

## Saṁyutta 15 ^sn15-0

### Grass and Sticks (Linked Discourses 15.1) ^sn15-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn15-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn15-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn15-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn15-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn15-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn15-1-1-6

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-1-1-7

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-1-1-8

Suppose a person was to strip all the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in the Black Plum Tree Land, gather them together into one pile, and chop them each into four inch pieces. They’d lay them down, saying: ^sn15-1-1-9

‘This is my mother, this is my grandmother.’ The grass, sticks, branches, and leaves of the Black Plum Tree Land would run out before that person’s mothers and grandmothers. ^sn15-1-1-10

Why is that? ^sn15-1-1-11

This transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-1-1-12

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-1-1-13

For such a long time you have undergone suffering, agony, and disaster, swelling the cemeteries. ^sn15-1-1-14

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-1-1-15

### The Earth (Linked Discourses 15.2) ^sn15-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-2-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-2-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-2-1-3

Suppose a person was to make the whole earth into clay balls the size of jujube seeds. They’d lay them down, saying: ^sn15-2-1-4

‘This is my father, this is my grandfather.’ The whole earth would run out before that person’s fathers and grandfathers. ^sn15-2-1-5

Why is that? ^sn15-2-1-6

This transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-2-1-7

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-2-1-8

For such a long time you have undergone suffering, agony, and disaster, swelling the cemeteries. ^sn15-2-1-9

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-2-1-10

### Tears (Linked Discourses 15.3) ^sn15-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-3-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-3-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-3-1-3

What do you think? Which is more: the flow of tears you’ve shed while roaming and transmigrating for such a very long time—weeping and wailing from being coupled with the unloved and separated from the loved—or the water in the four oceans?” ^sn15-3-1-4

“As we understand the Buddha’s teaching, the flow of tears we’ve shed while roaming and transmigrating is more than the water in the four oceans.” ^sn15-3-1-5

“Good, good, mendicants! It’s good that you understand my teaching like this. ^sn15-3-2-1

The flow of tears you’ve shed while roaming and transmigrating is indeed more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-3-2-2

For a long time you’ve undergone the death of a mother … ^sn15-3-2-3

father … ^sn15-3-2-5

brother … ^sn15-3-2-6

sister … ^sn15-3-2-7

son … ^sn15-3-2-8

daughter … ^sn15-3-2-9

loss of relatives … ^sn15-3-2-10

loss of wealth … ^sn15-3-2-11

or loss through illness. From being coupled with the unloved and separated from the loved, the flow of tears you’ve shed while roaming and transmigrating is indeed more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-3-2-12

Why is that? ^sn15-3-2-13

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-3-2-14

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-3-2-15

### Mother’s Milk (Linked Discourses 15.4) ^sn15-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-4-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-4-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-4-1-3

What do you think? Which is more: the mother’s milk you’ve drunk while roaming and transmigrating for such a very long time, or the water in the four oceans?” ^sn15-4-1-4

“As we understand the Buddha’s teaching, the mother’s milk we’ve drunk while roaming and transmigrating is more than the water in the four oceans.” ^sn15-4-1-5

“Good, good, mendicants! It’s good that you understand my teaching like this. ^sn15-4-2-1

The mother’s milk you’ve drunk while roaming and transmigrating for such a very long time is more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-4-2-2

Why is that? ^sn15-4-2-3

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-4-2-4

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-4-2-5

### A Mountain (Linked Discourses 15.5) ^sn15-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-5-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and asked him, ^sn15-5-1-2

“Sir, how long is an eon?” ^sn15-5-1-3

“Mendicant, an eon is long. ^sn15-5-1-4

It’s not easy to calculate how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it lasts.” ^sn15-5-1-5

“But sir, is it possible to give a simile?” ^sn15-5-2-1

“It’s possible,” said the Buddha. ^sn15-5-2-2

“Suppose there was a huge stone mountain, a league long, a league wide, and a league high, with no cracks or holes, one solid mass. ^sn15-5-2-3

And as each century passed someone would stroke it once with a fine cloth from Kāsi. ^sn15-5-2-4

By this means the huge stone mountain would be worn away before the eon comes to an end. ^sn15-5-2-5

That’s how long an eon is. ^sn15-5-2-6

And we’ve transmigrated through many such eons, many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands. ^sn15-5-2-7

Why is that? ^sn15-5-2-8

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-5-2-9

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-5-2-11

### A Mustard Seed (Linked Discourses 15.6) ^sn15-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-6-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn15-6-1-2

“Sir, how long is an eon?” ^sn15-6-1-3

“Mendicant, an eon is long. ^sn15-6-1-4

It’s not easy to calculate how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it lasts.” ^sn15-6-1-5

“But sir, is it possible to give a simile?” ^sn15-6-2-1

“It’s possible,” said the Buddha. ^sn15-6-2-2

“Suppose there was an iron citadel, a league long, a league wide, and a league high, full of mustard seeds pressed into balls. ^sn15-6-2-3

And as each century passed someone would remove a single mustard seed. ^sn15-6-2-4

By this means the huge heap of mustard seeds would be used up before the eon comes to an end. ^sn15-6-2-5

That’s how long an eon is. ^sn15-6-2-6

And we’ve transmigrated through many such eons, many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands. ^sn15-6-2-7

Why is that? ^sn15-6-2-8

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-6-2-9

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-6-2-10

### Disciples (Linked Discourses 15.7) ^sn15-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-7-1-1

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn15-7-1-2

“Sir, how many eons have passed?” ^sn15-7-1-3

“Mendicants, many eons have passed. ^sn15-7-1-4

It’s not easy to calculate ^sn15-7-1-5

how many eons have passed, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of eons.” ^sn15-7-1-6

“But sir, is it possible to give a simile?” ^sn15-7-2-1

“It’s possible,” said the Buddha. ^sn15-7-2-2

“Suppose there were four disciples with a lifespan of a hundred years. ^sn15-7-2-3

And each day they would each recollect a hundred thousand eons. ^sn15-7-2-4

Those four disciples would pass away after a hundred years and there would still be eons that they haven’t recollected. ^sn15-7-2-5

That’s how many eons have passed. ^sn15-7-2-6

It’s not easy to calculate ^sn15-7-2-7

how many eons have passed, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of eons. ^sn15-7-2-8

Why is that? ^sn15-7-2-9

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-7-2-10

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-7-2-11

### The Ganges (Linked Discourses 15.8) ^sn15-8-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn15-8-1-1

Then a certain brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn15-8-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and asked the Buddha, ^sn15-8-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, how many eons have passed?” ^sn15-8-1-4

“Brahmin, many eons have passed. ^sn15-8-1-5

It’s not easy to calculate ^sn15-8-1-6

how many eons have passed, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of eons.” ^sn15-8-1-7

“But worthy Gotama, can you give a simile?” ^sn15-8-2-1

“I can,” said the Buddha. ^sn15-8-2-2

“Consider the Ganges river from where it originates to where it meets the ocean. Between these places it’s not easy to calculate ^sn15-8-2-3

how many grains of sand there are, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of grains of sand. ^sn15-8-2-4

The eons that have passed are more than this. ^sn15-8-2-5

It’s not easy to calculate ^sn15-8-2-6

how many eons have passed, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of eons. ^sn15-8-2-7

Why is that? ^sn15-8-2-8

This transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-8-2-9

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-8-2-10

For such a long time you have undergone suffering, agony, and disaster, swelling the cemeteries. ^sn15-8-2-11

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-8-2-12

When he said this, the brahmin said to the Buddha, ^sn15-8-3-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^sn15-8-3-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn15-8-3-3

### A Stick (Linked Discourses 15.9) ^sn15-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-9-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-9-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-9-1-3

Suppose a stick was tossed up in the air. Sometimes it’d fall on its bottom, sometimes the middle, and sometimes the end. ^sn15-9-1-4

It’s the same for sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. Sometimes they go from this world to the other world, and sometimes they come from the other world to this world. ^sn15-9-1-5

Why is that? ^sn15-9-1-6

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-9-1-7

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-9-1-8

### A Single Individual (Linked Discourses 15.10) ^sn15-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn15-10-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn15-10-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn15-10-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn15-10-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn15-10-1-5

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-10-2-1

One individual roaming and transmigrating for an eon would amass a heap of bones the size of this Mount Vepulla, if they were gathered together and not lost. ^sn15-10-2-2

Why is that? ^sn15-10-2-3

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-10-2-4

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-10-2-5

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn15-10-3-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn15-10-3-2

“If the bones of a single individual ^sn15-10-4-1

for a single eon were gathered up, ^sn15-10-4-2

they’d make a pile the size of a mountain: ^sn15-10-4-3

so said the great seer. ^sn15-10-4-4

And this is declared to be ^sn15-10-5-1

as huge as Mount Vepulla, ^sn15-10-5-2

higher than the Vulture’s Peak ^sn15-10-5-3

in the Magadhan mountain range. ^sn15-10-5-4

But then, with right understanding, ^sn15-10-6-1

one sees the noble truths— ^sn15-10-6-2

suffering, suffering’s origin, ^sn15-10-6-3

suffering’s transcendence, ^sn15-10-6-4

and the noble eightfold path ^sn15-10-6-5

that leads to the stilling of suffering. ^sn15-10-6-6

After roaming on seven times at most, ^sn15-10-7-1

that individual ^sn15-10-7-2

makes an end of suffering, ^sn15-10-7-3

with the ending of all fetters.” ^sn15-10-7-4

### In a Sorry State (Linked Discourses 15.11) ^sn15-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī. ^sn15-11-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-11-1-3

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-11-1-4

When you see someone in a sorry state, in distress, you should conclude: ^sn15-11-1-5

‘In all this long time, we too have undergone the same thing.’ ^sn15-11-1-6

Why is that? This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-11-1-7

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-11-1-8

### In a Good Way (Linked Discourses 15.12) ^sn15-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-12-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-12-1-2

When you see someone in a good way, in a happy state, you should conclude: ^sn15-12-1-3

‘In all this long time, we too have undergone the same thing.’ ^sn15-12-1-4

Why is that? ^sn15-12-1-5

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-12-1-6

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-12-1-8

### Thirty Mendicants (Linked Discourses 15.13) ^sn15-13-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn15-13-1-1

Then thirty mendicants from Pāvā went to the Buddha. All of them lived in the wilderness, ate only almsfood, wore rag robes, and owned just three robes; yet they all still had fetters. They bowed to the Buddha and sat down to one side. ^sn15-13-1-2

Then it occurred to the Buddha, ^sn15-13-1-3

“These thirty mendicants from Pāvā live in the wilderness, eat only almsfood, wear rag robes, and own just three robes; yet they all still have fetters. ^sn15-13-1-4

Why don’t I teach them the Dhamma in such a way that their minds are freed from defilements by not grasping while sitting in this very seat?” ^sn15-13-1-5

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn15-13-1-6

“Mendicants!” ^sn15-13-1-7

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn15-13-1-8

The Buddha said this: ^sn15-13-1-9

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-13-2-1

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-13-2-2

What do you think? Which is more: the flow of blood you’ve shed when your head was chopped off while roaming and transmigrating for such a very long time, or the water in the four oceans?” ^sn15-13-2-3

“As we understand the Buddha’s teaching, the flow of blood we’ve shed when our head was chopped off while roaming and transmigrating is more than the water in the four oceans.” ^sn15-13-2-4

“Good, good, mendicants! It’s good that you understand my teaching like this. ^sn15-13-3-1

The flow of blood you’ve shed when your head was chopped off while roaming and transmigrating is indeed more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-13-3-2

For a long time you’ve been cows, and the flow of blood you’ve shed when your head was chopped off as a cow is more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-13-3-3

For a long time you’ve been buffalo … ^sn15-13-3-4

sheep … ^sn15-13-3-5

goats … ^sn15-13-3-6

deer … ^sn15-13-3-7

chickens … ^sn15-13-3-8

pigs … ^sn15-13-3-9

For a long time you’ve been bandits, arrested for raiding villages, ^sn15-13-3-10

highway robbery, ^sn15-13-3-11

or adultery. And the flow of blood you’ve shed when your head was chopped off as a bandit is more than the water in the four oceans. ^sn15-13-3-12

Why is that? ^sn15-13-3-13

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-13-3-14

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-13-3-15

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn15-13-4-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^sn15-13-4-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the minds of the thirty mendicants from Pāvā were freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn15-13-4-3

### Mother (Linked Discourses 15.14) ^sn15-14-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-14-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-14-1-2

It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your mother. ^sn15-14-1-3

Why is that? ^sn15-14-1-4

This transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-14-1-5

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-14-1-6

### Father (Linked Discourses 15.15) ^sn15-15-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-15-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. … ^sn15-15-1-2

It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your father. … ^sn15-15-1-3

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-15-1-4

### Brother (Linked Discourses 15.16) ^sn15-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-16-1-1

“It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your brother. … ^sn15-16-1-2

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-16-1-3

### Sister (Linked Discourses 15.17) ^sn15-17-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-17-1-1

“It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your sister. … ^sn15-17-1-2

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-17-1-3

### Son (Linked Discourses 15.18) ^sn15-18-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-18-1-1

“It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your son. … ^sn15-18-1-2

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-18-1-3

### Daughter (Linked Discourses 15.19) ^sn15-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn15-19-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-19-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-19-1-3

It’s not easy to find a sentient being who in all this long time has not previously been your daughter. ^sn15-19-1-4

Why is that? ^sn15-19-1-5

This transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-19-1-6

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-19-1-7

For such a long time you have undergone suffering, agony, and disaster, swelling the cemeteries. ^sn15-19-1-8

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-19-1-9

### Mount Vepulla (Linked Discourses 15.20) ^sn15-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn15-20-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn15-20-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn15-20-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn15-20-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn15-20-1-5

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn15-20-2-1

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn15-20-2-2

Once upon a time, mendicants, this Mount Vepulla was known as Pācīnavaṁsa. ^sn15-20-2-3

And at that time people were known as Tivaras. ^sn15-20-2-4

The lifespan of the Tivaras was 40,000 years. ^sn15-20-2-5

It took them four days to climb Mount Vepulla, and four days to descend. ^sn15-20-2-6

At that time Kakusandha, the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha arose in the world. ^sn15-20-2-7

Kakusandha had a fine pair of chief disciples named Vidhura and Sañjīva. ^sn15-20-2-8

See, mendicants! This mountain’s name has vanished, those people have passed away, and that Buddha has become fully quenched. ^sn15-20-2-9

So impermanent are conditions, ^sn15-20-2-10

so unstable are conditions, ^sn15-20-2-11

so unreliable are conditions. ^sn15-20-2-12

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions. ^sn15-20-2-13

Once upon a time this Mount Vepulla was known as Vaṅkaka. ^sn15-20-3-1

And at that time people were known as Rohitassas. ^sn15-20-3-2

The lifespan of the Rohitassas was 30,000 years. ^sn15-20-3-3

It took them three days to climb Mount Vepulla, and three days to descend. ^sn15-20-3-4

At that time Koṇāgamana, the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha arose in the world. ^sn15-20-3-5

Koṇāgamana had a fine pair of chief disciples named Bhiyyosa and Uttara. ^sn15-20-3-6

See, mendicants! This mountain’s name has vanished, those people have passed away, and that Buddha has become fully quenched. ^sn15-20-3-7

So impermanent are conditions … ^sn15-20-3-8

Once upon a time this Mount Vepulla was known as Supassa. ^sn15-20-4-1

And at that time people were known as Suppiyas. ^sn15-20-4-2

The lifespan of the Suppiyas was 20,000 years. ^sn15-20-4-3

It took them two days to climb Mount Vepulla, and two days to descend. ^sn15-20-4-4

At that time Kassapa, the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha arose in the world. ^sn15-20-4-5

Kassapa had a fine pair of chief disciples named Tissa and Bhāradvāja. ^sn15-20-4-6

See, mendicants! This mountain’s name has vanished, those people have passed away, and that Buddha has become fully quenched. ^sn15-20-4-7

So impermanent are conditions … ^sn15-20-4-8

These days this Mount Vepulla is known as Vepulla. ^sn15-20-5-1

And these people are known as Magadhans. ^sn15-20-5-2

The lifespan of the Magadhans is short, brief, and fleeting. ^sn15-20-5-3

A long life is a hundred years or a little more. ^sn15-20-5-4

It takes the Magadhans an hour to climb Mount Vepulla, and an hour to descend. ^sn15-20-5-5

And now I am the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha who has arisen in the world. ^sn15-20-5-6

I have a fine pair of chief disciples named Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^sn15-20-5-7

There will come a time when this mountain’s name will vanish, those people will die, and I will be fully extinguished. ^sn15-20-5-8

So impermanent are conditions, ^sn15-20-5-9

so unstable are conditions, ^sn15-20-5-10

so unreliable are conditions. ^sn15-20-5-11

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn15-20-5-12

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn15-20-6-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn15-20-6-2

“For the Tivaras it was Pācīnavaṁsa, ^sn15-20-7-1

for the Rohitassas, Vaṅkaka, ^sn15-20-7-2

Supassa for the Suppiyas, ^sn15-20-7-3

and Vepulla for the Magadhans. ^sn15-20-7-4

Oh! Conditions are impermanent, ^sn15-20-8-1

their nature is to rise and fall; ^sn15-20-8-2

having arisen, they cease; ^sn15-20-8-3

their settling is such bliss.” ^sn15-20-8-4

The Linked Discourses on the unknown beginning are complete. ^sn15-20-9-5

## Saṁyutta 16 ^sn16-0

### Content (Linked Discourses 16.1) ^sn16-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn16-1-1-1

“Mendicants, Kassapa is content with any kind of robe, and praises such contentment. ^sn16-1-1-2

He doesn’t employ improper solicitation for the sake of a robe. He doesn’t worry if he doesn’t get a robe. And if he does get a robe, he uses it untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawback, and understanding the escape. ^sn16-1-1-3

Kassapa is content with any kind of almsfood … ^sn16-1-2-1

Kassapa is content with any kind of lodging … ^sn16-1-3-1

Kassapa is content with any kind of medicines and supplies for the sick … ^sn16-1-4-1

So you should train like this: ‘We will be content with any kind of robe, and praise such contentment. We won’t employ improper solicitation for the sake of a robe. ^sn16-1-5-1

We won’t worry if we don’t get a robe. And if we do get a robe, we’ll use it untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawback, and understanding the escape.’ ^sn16-1-5-2

(All should be told in full the same way.) ^sn16-1-5-3

‘We will be content with any kind of almsfood …’ ^sn16-1-6-1

‘We will be content with any kind of lodging …’ ^sn16-1-6-2

‘We will be content with any kind of medicines and supplies for the sick …’ ^sn16-1-6-3
