That’s how you should train. ^sn16-1-6-4

I will exhort you with the example of Kassapa or someone like him. You should practice accordingly.” ^sn16-1-6-5

### Imprudent (Linked Discourses 16.2) ^sn16-2-0

So I have heard. ^sn16-2-1-1

At one time Venerable Mahākassapa and Venerable Sāriputta were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn16-2-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Mahākassapa, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn16-2-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Mahākassapa: ^sn16-2-1-4

“Reverend Kassapa, it’s said that without being keen and prudent you can’t achieve awakening, extinguishment, and the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn16-2-1-5

But if you are keen and prudent you can achieve awakening, extinguishment, and the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn16-2-1-6

To what extent is this the case?” ^sn16-2-2-1

“Reverend, take a mendicant who doesn’t foster keenness by thinking: ‘If unarisen unskillful qualities arise in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give up arisen unskillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give rise to unarisen skillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If arisen skillful qualities cease in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ^sn16-2-2-3

That’s how you’re not keen. ^sn16-2-2-4

And how are you not prudent? ^sn16-2-3-1

Take a mendicant who doesn’t foster prudence by thinking: ‘If unarisen unskillful qualities arise in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give up arisen unskillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give rise to unarisen skillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If arisen skillful qualities cease in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ^sn16-2-3-2

That’s how you’re not prudent. ^sn16-2-3-3

That’s how without being keen and prudent you can’t achieve awakening, extinguishment, and the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn16-2-3-4

And how are you keen? ^sn16-2-4-1

Take a mendicant who fosters keenness by thinking: ‘If unarisen unskillful qualities arise in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give up arisen unskillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give rise to unarisen skillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If arisen skillful qualities cease in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ^sn16-2-4-2

That’s how you’re keen. ^sn16-2-4-3

And how are you prudent? ^sn16-2-5-1

Take a mendicant who fosters prudence by thinking: ‘If unarisen unskillful qualities arise in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give up arisen unskillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If I don’t give rise to unarisen skillful qualities, they’ll lead to harm.’ ‘If arisen skillful qualities cease in me, they’ll lead to harm.’ ^sn16-2-5-2

That’s how you’re prudent. ^sn16-2-5-3

That’s how if you’re keen and prudent you can achieve awakening, extinguishment, and the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.” ^sn16-2-5-4

### Like the Moon (Linked Discourses 16.3) ^sn16-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn16-3-1-1

“Mendicants, you should approach families like the moon: ^sn16-3-1-2

withdrawn in body and mind, ever the newcomer, and never rude. ^sn16-3-1-3

Suppose a person were to look down at an old well, a rugged cliff, or an inaccessible riverland. ^sn16-3-1-4

They’d withdraw their body and mind. ^sn16-3-1-5

In the same way, you should approach families like the moon: ^sn16-3-1-6

withdrawn in body and mind, always the newcomer, and never rude. ^sn16-3-1-7

Kassapa approaches families like the moon: ^sn16-3-2-1

withdrawn in body and mind, ever the newcomer, and never rude. ^sn16-3-2-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn16-3-2-3

What kind of mendicant is worthy of approaching families?” ^sn16-3-2-4

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn16-3-2-5

Then the Buddha waved his hand in space. ^sn16-3-3-1

“Mendicants, this hand is not stuck or held or caught in space. ^sn16-3-3-2

In the same way, when approaching families, a mendicant’s mind is not stuck or held or caught, thinking: ^sn16-3-3-3

‘May those who want material things get them, and may those who want merit make merits!’ ^sn16-3-3-4

They’re just as pleased and happy when others get something as they are when they get something. ^sn16-3-3-5

This kind of mendicant is worthy of approaching families. ^sn16-3-3-6

When Kassapa approaches families, his mind is not stuck or held or caught, thinking: ^sn16-3-4-1

‘May those who want material things get them, and may those who want merit make merits!’ ^sn16-3-4-2

He’s just as pleased and happy when others get something as he is when he gets something. ^sn16-3-4-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn16-3-5-1

What kind of mendicant’s teaching is pure, and what kind is impure?” ^sn16-3-5-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn16-3-5-3

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn16-3-5-4

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn16-3-5-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn16-3-5-6

“Whoever teaches Dhamma to others with the thought: ^sn16-3-6-1

‘Oh! May they listen to the teaching from me. When they’ve heard it, may they gain confidence in the teaching and demonstrate their confidence to me.’ ^sn16-3-6-2

Such a mendicant’s teaching is impure. ^sn16-3-6-3

Whoever teaches Dhamma to others with the thought: ^sn16-3-7-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn16-3-7-2

Oh! May they listen to the teaching from me. When they’ve heard it, may they understand the teaching and practice accordingly.’ ^sn16-3-7-3

So they teach others because of the clear rightness of the teaching, out of compassion, kindness, and sympathy. ^sn16-3-7-4

Such a mendicant’s teaching is pure. ^sn16-3-7-5

Kassapa teaches Dhamma to others with the thought: ^sn16-3-8-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn16-3-8-2

Oh! May they listen to the teaching from me. When they’ve heard it, may they understand the teaching and practice accordingly.’ ^sn16-3-8-3

Thus he teaches others because of the clear rightness of the teaching, out of compassion, kindness, and sympathy. ^sn16-3-8-4

I will exhort you with the example of Kassapa or someone like him. You should practice accordingly.” ^sn16-3-8-5

### Visiting Families (Linked Discourses 16.4) ^sn16-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn16-4-1-1

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn16-4-1-2

What kind of mendicant is worthy of visiting families? And what kind of mendicant is not worthy of visiting families?” ^sn16-4-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn16-4-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn16-4-1-5

“Whoever visits families with the thought: ^sn16-4-2-1

‘May they give to me, may they not fail to give. ^sn16-4-2-2

May they give a lot, not a little. ^sn16-4-2-3

May they give me fine things, not coarse. ^sn16-4-2-4

May they give quickly, not slowly. ^sn16-4-2-5

May they give carefully, not carelessly.’ ^sn16-4-2-6

If a mendicant with such a thought approaches a family and they don’t give, the mendicant feels slighted. And they experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-2-7

If they give only a little … ^sn16-4-2-8

if they give coarse things … ^sn16-4-2-9

if they give slowly … ^sn16-4-2-10

if they give carelessly, the mendicant feels slighted. And they experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-2-11

That kind of mendicant is not worthy of visiting families. ^sn16-4-2-12

Whoever visits families with the thought: ^sn16-4-3-1

‘When among other families, how could I possibly think: ^sn16-4-3-2

“May they give to me, may they not fail to give. ^sn16-4-3-3

May they give a lot, not a little. ^sn16-4-3-4

May they give me fine things, not coarse. ^sn16-4-3-5

May they give quickly, not slowly. ^sn16-4-3-6

May they give carefully, not carelessly.”’ ^sn16-4-3-7

If a mendicant with such a thought approaches a family and they don’t give, the mendicant doesn’t feel slighted. And they don’t experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-3-8

If they give only a little … ^sn16-4-3-9

if they give coarse things … ^sn16-4-3-10

if they give slowly … ^sn16-4-3-11

if they give carelessly, the mendicant doesn’t feel slighted. And they don’t experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-3-12

That kind of mendicant is worthy of visiting families. ^sn16-4-3-13

Kassapa visits families with the thought: ^sn16-4-4-1

‘When among other families, how could I possibly think: ^sn16-4-4-2

“May they give to me, may they not fail to give. ^sn16-4-4-3

May they give a lot, not a little. ^sn16-4-4-4

May they give me fine things, not coarse. ^sn16-4-4-5

May they give quickly, not slowly. ^sn16-4-4-6

May they give carefully, not carelessly.”’ ^sn16-4-4-7

With such a thought, if he approaches a family and they don’t give, he doesn’t feel slighted. And he doesn’t experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-4-8

If they give only a little … ^sn16-4-4-9

if they give coarse things … ^sn16-4-4-10

if they give slowly … ^sn16-4-4-11

if they give carelessly, he doesn’t feel slighted. And he doesn’t experience pain and sadness because of that. ^sn16-4-4-12

I will exhort you with the example of Kassapa or someone like him. You should practice accordingly.” ^sn16-4-4-13

### Old Age (Linked Discourses 16.5) ^sn16-5-0

So I have heard. ^sn16-5-1-1

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn16-5-1-2

Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn16-5-1-3

“You’re old now, Kassapa. Those worn-out hempen rag robes must be a burden for you. ^sn16-5-1-4

So Kassapa, you should wear clothes given by householders, accept invitations for the meal, and stay in my presence.” ^sn16-5-1-5

“For a long time, sir, I’ve lived in the wilderness, eaten only almsfood, worn rag robes, and owned just three robes; and I’ve praised these things. I’ve been one of few wishes, content, secluded, aloof, and energetic; and I’ve praised these things.” ^sn16-5-2-1

“But seeing what benefit, Kassapa, have you long practiced these things?” ^sn16-5-3-1

“Sir, seeing two benefits I have long practiced these things. ^sn16-5-4-1

I see happiness for myself in this life. And I have sympathy for future generations, thinking: ^sn16-5-5-1

‘Hopefully those who come after might follow my example.’ ^sn16-5-5-2

For they may think: ‘It seems that the disciples awakened after the Buddha for a long time lived in the wilderness, ate only almsfood, wore rag robes, and owned just three robes; and they praised these things. They were of few wishes, content, secluded, aloof, and energetic; and they praised these things.’ ^sn16-5-5-3

They’ll practice accordingly, which will be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^sn16-5-5-12

Seeing these two benefits I have long practiced these things.” ^sn16-5-6-1

“Good, good, Kassapa! ^sn16-5-7-1

You’re acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^sn16-5-7-2

So Kassapa, wear worn-out hempen rag robes, walk for alms, and stay in the wilderness.” ^sn16-5-7-3

### Advice (Linked Discourses 16.6) ^sn16-6-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn16-6-1-1

Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn16-6-1-2

“Kassapa, advise the mendicants! ^sn16-6-1-3

Give them a Dhamma talk! ^sn16-6-1-4

Either you or I should advise the mendicants ^sn16-6-1-5

and give them a Dhamma talk.” ^sn16-6-1-6

“Sir, the mendicants these days are hard to admonish, having qualities that make them hard to admonish. They’re impatient, and don’t take instruction respectfully. ^sn16-6-2-1

Take the monk named Bhaṇḍa, Ānanda’s protégé. He’s been competing in studies with the monk named Abhiñjika, Anuruddha’s protégé. They say: ^sn16-6-2-2

‘Come on, monk, who can speak more? Who can speak better? Who can speak longer?’” ^sn16-6-2-3

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^sn16-6-3-1

“Please, monk, in my name tell the monk called Bhaṇḍa, Ānanda’s protégé, and the monk called Abhiñjika, Anuruddha’s protégé that ^sn16-6-3-2

the teacher summons them.” ^sn16-6-3-3

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to those monks and said, ^sn16-6-3-4

“Venerables, the teacher summons you.” ^sn16-6-3-5

“Yes, reverend,” those monks replied. They went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^sn16-6-4-1

“Is it really true, monks, that you’ve been competing in studies, saying: ^sn16-6-4-2

‘Come on, monk, who can speak more? Who can speak better? Who can speak longer?’” ^sn16-6-4-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn16-6-4-4

“Have you ever known me to teach the Dhamma like this: ^sn16-6-4-5

‘Please mendicants, compete in studies to ^sn16-6-4-6

see who can recite more and better and longer’?” ^sn16-6-4-7

“No, sir.” ^sn16-6-4-8

“If you’ve never known me to teach the Dhamma like this, what then do you know and see, you futile men, that after going forth in such a well explained teaching and training you’d compete in studies to ^sn16-6-4-9

see who can speak more and better and longer?” ^sn16-6-4-10

Then those monks bowed with their heads at the Buddha’s feet and said, ^sn16-6-5-1

“We made a mistake, sir. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of us in that after going forth in such a well explained teaching and training we competed in studies to ^sn16-6-5-2

see who can speak more and better and longer. ^sn16-6-5-3

Please, sir, accept our mistake for what it is, so we will restrain ourselves in future.” ^sn16-6-5-4

“Indeed, monks, you made a mistake. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of you to act in that way. ^sn16-6-6-1

But since you have recognized your mistake for what it is, and have dealt with it properly, I accept it. ^sn16-6-6-3

For it is growth in the training of the Noble One to recognize a mistake for what it is, deal with it properly, and commit to restraint in the future.” ^sn16-6-6-4

### Advice (2nd) (Linked Discourses 16.7) ^sn16-7-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove. ^sn16-7-1-1

Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^sn16-7-1-2

The Buddha said to him, ^sn16-7-1-3

“Kassapa, advise the mendicants! ^sn16-7-1-4

Give them a Dhamma talk! ^sn16-7-1-5

Either you or I should advise the mendicants ^sn16-7-1-6

and give them a Dhamma talk.” ^sn16-7-1-7

“Sir, the mendicants these days are hard to admonish, having qualities that make them hard to admonish. They’re impatient, and don’t take instruction respectfully. ^sn16-7-2-1

Sir, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^sn16-7-2-2

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^sn16-7-3-1

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^sn16-7-3-2

A faithless individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-1

An individual with no conscience is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-2

An imprudent individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-3

A lazy individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-4

A witless individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-5

An irritable individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-6

An acrimonious individual is in decline. ^sn16-7-4-7

When there are no mendicant advisers there is decline. ^sn16-7-4-8

Sir, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^sn16-7-5-1

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^sn16-7-6-1

In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^sn16-7-6-2

A faithful individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-1

An individual with a conscience doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-2

A prudent individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-3

An energetic individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-4

A wise individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-5

A loving individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-6

A kind individual doesn’t decline. ^sn16-7-7-7

When there are mendicant advisers there is no decline.” ^sn16-7-7-8

“Good, good, Kassapa! ^sn16-7-8-1

Whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect decline, not growth … ^sn16-7-8-2

When there are no mendicant advisers there is decline. ^sn16-7-9-15

Whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities can expect growth, not decline … ^sn16-7-10-1

When there are mendicant advisers there is no decline.” ^sn16-7-12-8

### Advice (3rd) (Linked Discourses 16.8) ^sn16-8-0

Near Rājagaha, in the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn16-8-1-1

Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn16-8-1-2

“Kassapa, advise the mendicants! ^sn16-8-1-3

Give them a Dhamma talk! ^sn16-8-1-4

Either you or I should advise the mendicants ^sn16-8-1-5

and give them a Dhamma talk.” ^sn16-8-1-6

“Sir, the mendicants these days are hard to admonish, having qualities that make them hard to admonish. They’re impatient, and don’t take instruction respectfully.” ^sn16-8-2-1

“Kassapa, that’s because formerly the senior mendicants lived in the wilderness, ate only almsfood, wore rag robes, and owned just three robes; and they praised these things. They were of few wishes, content, secluded, aloof, and energetic; and they praised these things. ^sn16-8-2-2

The senior mendicants invite such a mendicant to a seat, saying: ^sn16-8-3-1

‘Welcome, mendicant! What is this mendicant’s name? This mendicant is good-natured; he really wants to train. Please, mendicant, here, take a seat.’ ^sn16-8-3-2

Then the junior mendicants think: ^sn16-8-4-1

‘It seems that when a mendicant lives in the wilderness … and is energetic, and praises these things, senior mendicants invite them to a seat …’ ^sn16-8-4-2

They practice accordingly. ^sn16-8-4-4

That is for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^sn16-8-4-5

But these days, Kassapa, the senior mendicants don’t live in the wilderness … and aren’t energetic; and they don’t praise these things. ^sn16-8-5-1

When a mendicant is well-known and famous, a recipient of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick, senior mendicants invite them to a seat: ^sn16-8-6-1

‘Welcome, mendicant! What is this mendicant’s name? This mendicant is good-natured; he’s fond of his fellow monks. Please, mendicant, here, take a seat.’ ^sn16-8-6-2

Then the junior mendicants think: ^sn16-8-7-1

‘It seems that when a mendicant is well-known and famous, a recipient of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick, senior mendicants invite them to a seat …’ ^sn16-8-7-2

They practice accordingly. ^sn16-8-7-4

That is for their lasting harm and suffering. ^sn16-8-7-5

And if it could ever be rightly said that ^sn16-8-7-6

spiritual practitioners have been imperiled by the peril of a spiritual practitioner, and vanquished by the vanquishing of a spiritual practitioner, it is these days that this could be rightly said.” ^sn16-8-7-7

### Absorptions and Insights (Linked Discourses 16.9) ^sn16-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn16-9-1-1

“Mendicants, whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn16-9-1-2

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-1-3

Whenever I want, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, I enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn16-9-2-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-2-2

Whenever I want, with the fading away of rapture, I enter and remain in the third absorption, where I meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^sn16-9-3-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-3-2

Whenever I want, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, I enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^sn16-9-4-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-4-2

Whenever I want, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, I enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space. ^sn16-9-5-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-5-2

Whenever I want, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, I enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn16-9-6-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-6-2

Whenever I want, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, I enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn16-9-7-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-7-2

Whenever I want, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, I enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^sn16-9-8-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-8-2

Whenever I want, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, I enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling. ^sn16-9-9-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-9-2

Whenever I want, I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. I control the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^sn16-9-10-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-10-2

Whenever I want, with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far. ^sn16-9-11-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-11-2

Whenever I want, I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^sn16-9-12-1

I understand mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’, ^sn16-9-12-2

and mind without greed as ‘mind without greed’; ^sn16-9-12-3

mind with hate … ^sn16-9-12-4

mind without hate … ^sn16-9-12-5

mind with delusion … ^sn16-9-12-6

mind without delusion … ^sn16-9-12-7

constricted mind … ^sn16-9-12-8

scattered mind … ^sn16-9-12-9

expansive mind … ^sn16-9-12-10

unexpansive mind … ^sn16-9-12-11

mind that is not supreme … ^sn16-9-12-12

mind that is supreme … ^sn16-9-12-13

mind immersed in samādhi … ^sn16-9-12-14

mind not immersed in samādhi … ^sn16-9-12-15

freed mind … ^sn16-9-12-16

unfreed mind … ^sn16-9-12-17

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-12-18

Whenever I want, I recollect my many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. I remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so I recollect my many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^sn16-9-13-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-13-2

Whenever I want, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. I understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. I understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^sn16-9-14-1

And so does Kassapa. ^sn16-9-14-2

I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn16-9-15-1

And so does Kassapa.” ^sn16-9-15-2

### The Nuns’ Quarters (Linked Discourses 16.10) ^sn16-10-0

So I have heard. ^sn16-10-1-1

At one time Venerable Mahākassapa was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn16-10-1-2

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to Mahākassapa and said, ^sn16-10-1-3

“Kassapa, come, sir. Let’s go to one of the nuns’ quarters.” ^sn16-10-1-4

“You go, Reverend Ānanda. You’re the one with many duties and responsibilities.” ^sn16-10-1-5

And a second time … ^sn16-10-1-6

And a third time, Ānanda said, ^sn16-10-1-9

“Come, Honorable Kassapa. Let’s go to one of the nuns’ quarters.” ^sn16-10-1-10

Then Venerable Mahākassapa robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went with Venerable Ānanda as his second monk to one of the nuns’ quarters, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^sn16-10-2-1

And then several nuns went up to Mahākassapa, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^sn16-10-2-2

Mahākassapa educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired those nuns with a Dhamma talk, ^sn16-10-2-3

after which he rose from his seat and left. ^sn16-10-2-4

But the nun Thullatissā was upset and blurted out, ^sn16-10-3-1

“What is the venerable Mahākassapa thinking, that he’d teach Dhamma in front of the venerable Ānanda, the Videhan sage? ^sn16-10-3-2

He’s like a needle peddler who thinks they can sell a needle to a needle maker!” ^sn16-10-3-3

Mahākassapa heard Thullatissā say these words, ^sn16-10-4-1

and he said to Ānanda, ^sn16-10-4-2

“Is that right, Reverend Ānanda? Am I the needle peddler and you the needle maker? ^sn16-10-4-3

Or am I the needle maker and you the needle peddler?” ^sn16-10-4-4

“Forgive her, sir. The lady’s a fool.” ^sn16-10-4-5

“Hold on, Reverend Ānanda! Don’t make the Saṅgha investigate you further! ^sn16-10-4-6

What do you think, Reverend Ānanda? ^sn16-10-5-1

Was it you who the Buddha brought up before the Saṅgha of mendicants, saying: ^sn16-10-5-2

‘Mendicants, whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn16-10-5-3

And so does Ānanda’?” ^sn16-10-5-4

“No, sir.” ^sn16-10-5-5

“I was the one the Buddha brought up before the Saṅgha of mendicants, saying: ^sn16-10-6-1

‘Mendicants, whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn16-10-6-2

And so does Kassapa. …’ ^sn16-10-6-3

(The nine progressive meditations and the five insights should be told in full.) ^sn16-10-6-4

What do you think, Reverend Ānanda? ^sn16-10-7-1

Was it you who the Buddha brought up before the Saṅgha of mendicants, saying: ^sn16-10-7-2

‘I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn16-10-7-3

And so does Ānanda’?” ^sn16-10-7-4

“No, sir.” ^sn16-10-7-5

“I was the one the Buddha brought up before the Saṅgha of mendicants, saying: ^sn16-10-8-1

‘I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn16-10-8-2

And so does Kassapa.’ ^sn16-10-8-3

Reverend, you might as well think to hide a bull elephant that’s three or three and a half meters tall behind a palm leaf as to hide my six insights.” ^sn16-10-9-1

But the nun Thullatissā fell from the spiritual life. ^sn16-10-10-1

### Robes (Linked Discourses 16.11) ^sn16-11-0

At one time Venerable Mahākassapa was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn16-11-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Ānanda was wandering in the Southern Hills together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^sn16-11-1-2

And at that time thirty of Ānanda’s mendicant protégés resigned the training and returned to a lesser life. Most of them were youths. ^sn16-11-2-1

When Venerable Ānanda had wandered in the Southern Hills as long as he pleased, he set out for Rājagaha, to the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. He went up to Venerable Mahākassapa, bowed, and sat down to one side. Mahākassapa said to him: ^sn16-11-2-2

“Reverend Ānanda, for how many reasons did the Buddha lay down a rule against eating in groups of more than three among families?” ^sn16-11-2-3

“Sir, the Buddha laid down that rule for three reasons. ^sn16-11-3-1

For keeping difficult individuals in check and for the comfort of good-hearted mendicants. To prevent those of corrupt wishes from taking sides and dividing the Saṅgha. And out of consideration for families. ^sn16-11-3-2

These are the three reasons why the Buddha laid down that rule.” ^sn16-11-3-3

“So what then are you doing, wandering together with these junior mendicants? They don’t guard their sense doors, they eat too much, and they’re not committed to wakefulness. ^sn16-11-4-1

It’s like you’re wandering about wrecking crops and ruining families! ^sn16-11-4-2

Your following is falling away, Reverend Ānanda, ^sn16-11-4-3

and those just getting started are falling apart. ^sn16-11-4-4

Yet this boy knows no limit!” ^sn16-11-4-5

“Though there are grey hairs on my head, ^sn16-11-5-1

I still can’t escape being called a boy by Venerable Mahākassapa.” ^sn16-11-5-2

“It’s because you wander with these junior mendicants. … ^sn16-11-5-3

Your following is falling away, Reverend Ānanda, ^sn16-11-5-4

and those just getting started are falling apart. ^sn16-11-5-5

Yet this boy knows no limit!” ^sn16-11-5-6

The nun Thullanandā heard a rumor that ^sn16-11-6-1

the venerable Mahākassapa had rebuked the worthy Ānanda the Videhan sage by calling him a boy. ^sn16-11-6-2

She was upset and blurted out, ^sn16-11-7-1

“How can the venerable Mahākassapa, who formerly followed another religion, presume to rebuke the venerable Ānanda the Videhan sage by calling him a boy?” ^sn16-11-7-2

Mahākassapa heard Thullanandā say these words, ^sn16-11-7-3

and he said to Ānanda, ^sn16-11-8-1

“Indeed, Reverend Ānanda, the nun Thullanandā spoke rashly and without reflection. ^sn16-11-8-2

Since I shaved off my hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness, I don’t recall acknowledging any other teacher apart from the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^sn16-11-8-3

Formerly when I was still a layman, I thought: ^sn16-11-8-4

‘Life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open. ^sn16-11-8-5

It’s not easy for someone living at home to lead the spiritual life utterly full and pure, like a polished shell. ^sn16-11-8-6

Why don’t I shave off my hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness?’ ^sn16-11-8-7

After some time I made an outer robe of patches and, in the name of the perfected ones in the world, I shaved off my hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn16-11-8-8

When I had gone forth, I traveled along the road between Rājagaha and Nāḷandā, where I saw the Buddha sitting at the Many Sons Shrine. ^sn16-11-9-1

Seeing him, I thought: ^sn16-11-9-2

‘Were I ever to see a Teacher, it would be this Blessed One! ^sn16-11-9-3

Were I ever to see a Holy One, it would be this Blessed One! ^sn16-11-9-4

Were I ever to see a fully awakened Buddha, it would be this Blessed One!’ ^sn16-11-9-5

Then I bowed with my head at the Buddha’s feet and said: ^sn16-11-9-7

‘Sir, the Buddha is my Teacher, I am his disciple! ^sn16-11-9-8

The Buddha is my Teacher, I am his disciple!’ ^sn16-11-9-9

The Buddha said to me, ^sn16-11-9-10

‘Kassapa, if anyone was to say to such a wholehearted disciple that they know when they don’t know, or that they see when they don’t see, their head would explode. ^sn16-11-9-11

But Kassapa, when I say that I know I really do know; and when I say that I see I really do see. ^sn16-11-9-12

So you should train like this: ^sn16-11-10-1

“I will set up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for seniors, juniors, and those in the middle.” ^sn16-11-10-2

That’s how you should train. ^sn16-11-10-3

And you should train like this: ^sn16-11-11-1

“Whenever I hear a teaching connected with what’s skillful, I will pay attention, apply the mind, concentrate wholeheartedly, and actively listen to that teaching.” ^sn16-11-11-2

That’s how you should train. ^sn16-11-11-3

And you should train like this: ^sn16-11-12-1

“I will never neglect mindfulness of the body that is full of pleasure.” ^sn16-11-12-2

That’s how you should train.’ ^sn16-11-12-3

And when the Buddha had given me this advice he rose from his seat and left. ^sn16-11-13-1

For seven days I ate the nation’s almsfood as a debtor. on the eighth day I became enlightened. ^sn16-11-13-2

And then the Buddha left the road and went to the root of a certain tree. ^sn16-11-14-1

So I spread out my outer robe of patches folded in four and said to him, ^sn16-11-14-2

‘Sir, sit here. That would be for my lasting welfare and happiness.’ ^sn16-11-14-3

The Buddha sat on the seat spread out ^sn16-11-14-4

and said to me, ^sn16-11-14-5

‘Kassapa, this outer robe of patches is soft.’ ^sn16-11-14-6

‘Sir, please accept my outer robe of patches out of sympathy.’ ^sn16-11-14-7

‘In that case, Kassapa, will you wear my worn-out hempen rag robe?’ ^sn16-11-14-8

‘I will wear it, sir.’ ^sn16-11-14-9

And so I presented my outer robe of patches to the Buddha, ^sn16-11-14-10

and the Buddha presented me with his worn-out hempen rag robe. ^sn16-11-14-11

For if anyone should be rightly called ^sn16-11-15-1

the Buddha’s true-born son, born from his mouth, born of the teaching, created by the teaching, heir to the teaching, and receiver of his worn-out hempen rag robes, it’s me. ^sn16-11-15-2

Whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. … ^sn16-11-16-1

(The nine progressive meditations and the five insights should be told in full.) ^sn16-11-16-3

I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn16-11-17-1

Reverend, you might as well think to hide a bull elephant that’s three or three and a half meters tall behind a palm leaf as to hide my six insights.” ^sn16-11-17-2

But the nun Thullanandā fell from the spiritual life. ^sn16-11-18-1

### The Realized One After Death (Linked Discourses 16.12) ^sn16-12-0

At one time Venerable Mahākassapa and Venerable Sāriputta were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn16-12-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Mahākassapa, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn16-12-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Mahākassapa: ^sn16-12-1-3

“Reverend Kassapa, does a realized one still exist after death?” ^sn16-12-1-4

“Reverend, this has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-1-5

“Well then, does a realized one no longer exist after death?” ^sn16-12-1-7

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-1-8

“Well then, does a realized one both still exist and no longer exist after death?” ^sn16-12-1-10

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-1-11

“Well then, does a realized one neither still exist nor no longer exist after death?” ^sn16-12-1-13

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-1-14

“And why has this not been declared by the Buddha?” ^sn16-12-1-16

“Because it’s not beneficial or relevant to the fundamentals of the spiritual life. It doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn16-12-1-17

That’s why it has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-1-18

“So what now has been declared by the Buddha?” ^sn16-12-2-1

“‘This is suffering’ has been declared by the Buddha. ^sn16-12-2-2

‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ^sn16-12-2-3

‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ^sn16-12-2-4

‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’ has been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-2-5

“And why has this been declared by the Buddha?” ^sn16-12-2-6

“Because it’s beneficial and relevant to the fundamentals of the spiritual life. It leads to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn16-12-2-7

That’s why it has been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn16-12-2-8

### The Counterfeit of the True Teaching (Linked Discourses 16.13) ^sn16-13-0

So I have heard. ^sn16-13-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn16-13-1-2

Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn16-13-1-3

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why there used to be fewer training rules but more enlightened mendicants? ^sn16-13-1-4

And what is the cause, what is the reason why these days there are more training rules and fewer enlightened mendicants?” ^sn16-13-1-5

“That’s how it is, Kassapa. When sentient beings are in decline and the true teaching is disappearing there are more training rules and fewer enlightened mendicants. ^sn16-13-1-6

The true teaching doesn’t disappear as long the counterfeit of the true teaching hasn’t appeared in the world. ^sn16-13-1-7

But when the counterfeit of the true teaching appears in the world then the true teaching disappears. ^sn16-13-1-8

It’s like native gold, which doesn’t disappear as long as counterfeit gold hasn’t appeared in the world. ^sn16-13-2-1

But when counterfeit gold appears in the world then native gold disappears. ^sn16-13-2-2

In the same way, the true teaching doesn’t disappear as long the counterfeit of the true teaching hasn’t appeared in the world. ^sn16-13-2-3

But when the counterfeit of the true teaching appears in the world then the true teaching disappears. ^sn16-13-2-4

It’s not the elements of earth, water, fire, or air that make the true teaching disappear. ^sn16-13-3-1

Rather, it’s the futile people who appear right here that make the true teaching disappear. ^sn16-13-3-2

The true teaching doesn’t disappear like a ship that sinks all at once. ^sn16-13-3-3

There are five detrimental things that lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^sn16-13-4-1

What five? ^sn16-13-4-2

It’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen lack respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and immersion. ^sn16-13-4-3

These five detrimental things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^sn16-13-4-4

There are five things that lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^sn16-13-5-1

What five? ^sn16-13-5-2

It’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen maintain respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and immersion. ^sn16-13-5-3

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.” ^sn16-13-5-4

The Linked Discourses with Kassapa are complete. ^sn16-13-6-7

## Saṁyutta 17 ^sn17-0

### Grim (Linked Discourses 17.1) ^sn17-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn17-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn17-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn17-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn17-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn17-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn17-1-1-6

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-1-2-1

So you should train like this: ^sn17-1-2-2

‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’ ^sn17-1-2-3

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-1-2-4

### A Hook (Linked Discourses 17.2) ^sn17-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-2-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-2-1-2

Suppose an angler was to cast a baited hook into a deep lake. ^sn17-2-1-3

Seeing the bait, a fish would swallow it. ^sn17-2-1-4

And so the fish that swallowed the hook would meet with tragedy and disaster, and the angler can do what he wants with it. ^sn17-2-1-5

‘Angler’ is a term for Māra the Wicked. ^sn17-2-2-1

‘Hook’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-2-2-2

Whoever enjoys and likes arisen possessions, honor, and popularity is called a mendicant who has swallowed Māra’s hook. They’ve met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn17-2-2-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity—bitter and harsh, an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-2-2-4

So you should train like this: ^sn17-2-2-5

‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’ ^sn17-2-2-6

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-2-2-7

### A Turtle (Linked Discourses 17.3) ^sn17-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-3-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-3-1-2

Once upon a time in a certain lake there was a large family of turtles that had lived there for a long time. ^sn17-3-1-3

Then one of the turtles said to another, ^sn17-3-1-4

‘My dear turtle, don’t you go to that place.’ ^sn17-3-1-5

But that turtle did go to that place, ^sn17-3-1-6

and a hunter pierced her with a harpoon. ^sn17-3-1-7

Then that turtle went back to the other turtle. ^sn17-3-1-8

When the other turtle saw her coming off in the distance, ^sn17-3-1-9

he said, ^sn17-3-1-10

‘My dear turtle, I hope you didn’t go to that place!’ ^sn17-3-1-11

‘I did.’ ^sn17-3-1-12

‘But my dear turtle, I hope you’re not hurt or injured!’ ^sn17-3-1-13

‘I’m not hurt or injured. But this cord keeps dragging behind me.’ ^sn17-3-1-14

‘Indeed, my dear turtle, you’re hurt and injured! ^sn17-3-1-15

Your father and grandfather met with tragedy and disaster because of such a cord. ^sn17-3-1-16

Go now, you are no longer one of us.’ ^sn17-3-1-17

‘Hunter’ is a term for Māra the Wicked. ^sn17-3-2-1

‘Harpoon’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-3-2-2

‘Cord’ is a term for greed and relishing. ^sn17-3-2-3

Whoever enjoys and likes arisen possessions, honor, and popularity is called a mendicant who has been pierced with a harpoon. They’ve met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn17-3-2-4

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-3-2-6

### A Fleecy Sheep (Linked Discourses 17.4) ^sn17-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-4-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-4-1-2

Suppose a fleecy sheep was to enter a briar patch. ^sn17-4-1-3

She’d get caught, snagged, and trapped at every turn, coming to ruin. ^sn17-4-1-4

In the same way, take a certain mendicant whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. They robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the village or town for alms. ^sn17-4-1-5

They get caught, snagged, and trapped at every turn, coming to ruin. ^sn17-4-1-6

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-4-1-7

### A Dung Beetle (Linked Discourses 17.5) ^sn17-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-5-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-5-1-2

Suppose there was a dung-eating beetle full of dung, stuffed with dung, and before her was a huge pile of dung. ^sn17-5-1-3

She’d look down on other beetles, thinking: ^sn17-5-1-4

‘For I am a dung-eating beetle full of dung, stuffed with dung, and before me is a huge pile of dung.’ ^sn17-5-1-5

In the same way, take a certain mendicant whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. They robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the village or town for alms. ^sn17-5-1-6

There they eat as much as they like, get invited back tomorrow, and have plenty of almsfood. ^sn17-5-1-7

When they get back to the monastery, they boast in the middle of a group of mendicants: ^sn17-5-1-8

‘I ate as much as I liked, got invited back tomorrow, and had plenty of almsfood. I get robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. But these other mendicants have little merit or significance, so they don’t get these things.’ ^sn17-5-1-9

With a mind overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity, they look down on other good-hearted mendicants. ^sn17-5-1-10

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^sn17-5-1-11

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-5-1-12

### A Bolt of Lightning (Linked Discourses 17.6) ^sn17-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-6-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-6-1-2

Let forked lightning fall on whom? Let possessions, honor, and popularity come to a trainee who has not achieved their heart’s desire. ^sn17-6-1-3

‘Forked lightning’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-6-2-1

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-6-2-2

### A Poisoned Arrow (Linked Discourses 17.7) ^sn17-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-7-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-7-1-2

Let whom be pierced by a poisoned arrow? Let possessions, honor, and popularity come to a trainee who has not achieved their heart’s desire. ^sn17-7-1-3

‘Arrow’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-7-2-1

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-7-2-2

### A Jackal (Linked Discourses 17.8) ^sn17-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-8-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-8-1-2

Mendicants, did you hear an old jackal howling at the crack of dawn?” ^sn17-8-1-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn17-8-1-4

“That old jackal has the disease called mange. He’s not happy in his den, or at the root of a tree, or out in the open. ^sn17-8-1-5

Wherever he goes, stands, sits, or lies down he meets with tragedy and disaster. ^sn17-8-1-6

In the same way, take a certain mendicant whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. They’re not happy in an empty hut, at the root of a tree, or out in the open. ^sn17-8-1-7

Wherever they go, stand, sit, or lie down they meet with tragedy and disaster. ^sn17-8-1-8

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-8-1-9

### Gale-force Winds (Linked Discourses 17.9) ^sn17-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-9-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-9-1-2

High in the atmosphere there are gale-force winds blowing. ^sn17-9-1-3

Any bird that flies there is flung about by those gale-force winds. ^sn17-9-1-4

Their feet go one way, their wings another, their head another, and their body another. ^sn17-9-1-5

In the same way, take a certain monk whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters the village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^sn17-9-1-6

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^sn17-9-1-7

Lust infects his mind. ^sn17-9-1-8

He disavows the training and returns to a lesser life. ^sn17-9-1-9

Some take his robe, others his bowl, others his sitting cloth, others his needle case, just like the bird flung about by the gale-force winds. ^sn17-9-1-10

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-9-1-11

### With Verses (Linked Discourses 17.10) ^sn17-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-10-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-10-1-2

Take a case where I see a certain individual whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by honor. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn17-10-1-3

Take another case where I see a certain individual whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by lack of honor. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn17-10-1-4

And take another case where I see a certain individual whose mind is overcome and overwhelmed by both honor and lack of honor. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn17-10-1-5

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-10-1-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn17-10-2-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn17-10-2-2

“Whether they’re honored ^sn17-10-3-1

or not honored, or both, ^sn17-10-3-2

their immersion doesn’t waver ^sn17-10-3-3

as they abide in the immeasurables. ^sn17-10-3-4

They persistently meditate ^sn17-10-4-1

with subtle view and discernment. ^sn17-10-4-2

Rejoicing in the ending of grasping, ^sn17-10-4-3

they’re said to be a true person.” ^sn17-10-4-4

### A Gold Cup (Linked Discourses 17.11) ^sn17-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-11-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-11-1-2

When I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand: ^sn17-11-1-3

‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of a gold cup filled with silver powder.’ ^sn17-11-1-4

But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-11-1-5

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-11-1-6

### A Silver Pot (Linked Discourses 17.12) ^sn17-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-12-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-12-1-2

When I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand: ^sn17-12-1-3

‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of a silver pot filled with gold powder.’ ^sn17-12-1-4

But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-12-1-5

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-12-1-6

### A Gold Ingot, Etc. (Linked Discourses 17.13–20) ^sn17-13-20-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-13-20-1-1

“Mendicants, when I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand: ^sn17-13-20-1-2

‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of a gold ingot.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-3

“‘… for the sake of a hundred gold ingots.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-4

“‘… for the sake of a mountain of gold.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-5

“‘… for the sake of a hundred mountains of gold.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-6

“‘… for the sake of the whole earth full of gold.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-7

“‘… for any kind of material reward.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-8

“‘… for the sake of life.’ …” ^sn17-13-20-1-9

“‘… for the sake of the finest lady in the land.’ ^sn17-13-20-1-10

But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-13-20-1-11

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-13-20-1-12

### A Lady (Linked Discourses 17.21) ^sn17-21-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-21-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-21-1-2

Even if you’re alone with a lady she might not occupy your mind, but possessions, honor and popularity would still occupy your mind. ^sn17-21-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-21-1-4

### The Finest Lady in the Land (Linked Discourses 17.22) ^sn17-22-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-22-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-22-1-2

Even if you’re alone with the finest lady in the land she might not occupy your mind, but possessions, honor and popularity would still occupy your mind. ^sn17-22-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-22-1-4

### An Only Son (Linked Discourses 17.23) ^sn17-23-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-23-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-23-1-2

A faithful laywoman with a dear and beloved only son would rightly appeal to him, ^sn17-23-1-3

‘My darling, please be like the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī.’ ^sn17-23-1-4

These are a standard and a measure for my male lay disciples, that is, the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī. ^sn17-23-1-5

‘But my darling, if you go forth from the lay life to homelessness, ^sn17-23-1-6

please be like Sāriputta and Moggallāna.’ ^sn17-23-1-7

These are a standard and a measure for my monk disciples, that is, Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^sn17-23-1-8

‘And my darling, may you not come into possessions, honor, and popularity while you’re still a trainee and haven’t achieved your heart’s desire.’ ^sn17-23-1-9

If a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire comes into possessions, honor, and popularity it’s an obstacle for them. ^sn17-23-1-10

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-23-1-11

### An Only Daughter (Linked Discourses 17.24) ^sn17-24-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-24-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-24-1-2

A faithful laywoman with a dear and beloved only daughter would rightly appeal to her, ^sn17-24-1-3

‘My darling, please be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother.’ ^sn17-24-1-4

These are a standard and a measure for my female lay disciples, that is, the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother. ^sn17-24-1-5

‘But my darling, if you go forth from the lay life to homelessness, ^sn17-24-1-6

please be like the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā.’ ^sn17-24-1-7

These are a standard and a measure for my nun disciples, that is, the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā. ^sn17-24-1-8

‘And my darling, may you not come into possessions, honor, and popularity while you’re still a trainee and haven’t achieved your heart’s desire.’ ^sn17-24-1-9

If a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire comes into possessions, honor, and popularity it’s an obstacle for them. ^sn17-24-1-10

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-24-1-11

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Linked Discourses 17.25) ^sn17-25-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-25-1-1

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand the gratification, drawback, and escape when it comes to possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-25-1-2

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn17-25-1-3

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand the gratification, drawback, and escape when it comes to possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-25-1-4

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn17-25-1-5

### Ascetics and Brahmins (2nd) (Linked Discourses 17.26) ^sn17-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-26-1-1

“There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand the origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape when it comes to possessions, honor, and popularity … ^sn17-26-1-2

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand …” ^sn17-26-1-3

### Ascetics and Brahmins (3rd) (Linked Discourses 17.27) ^sn17-27-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-27-1-1

“There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand possessions, honor, and popularity, their origin, their cessation, and the path that leads to their cessation … ^sn17-27-1-2

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand …” ^sn17-27-1-3

### Skin (Linked Discourses 17.28) ^sn17-28-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-28-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-28-1-2

They cut through the outer skin, the inner skin, the flesh, sinews, and bones, until they reach the marrow and keep pressing. ^sn17-28-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-28-1-4

### A Rope (Linked Discourses 17.29) ^sn17-29-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-29-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-29-1-2

They cut through the outer skin, the inner skin, the flesh, sinews, and bones, until they reach the marrow and keep pressing. ^sn17-29-1-3

Suppose a strong man was to twist a strong horse-hair rope around your shin and tighten it. ^sn17-29-2-1

It would cut through the outer skin, the inner skin, the flesh, sinews, and bones, until it reached the marrow and kept pressing. ^sn17-29-2-2

In the same way, possessions, honor, and popularity cut through the outer skin, the inner skin, the flesh, sinews, and bones, until they reach the marrow and keep pressing. ^sn17-29-2-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-29-2-4

### A Mendicant With Defilements Ended (Linked Discourses 17.30) ^sn17-30-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-30-1-1

“Mendicants, possessions, honor, and popularity are an obstacle even for a mendicant who is perfected, with defilements ended.” ^sn17-30-1-2

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^sn17-30-1-3

“Sir, what do possessions, honor, and popularity obstruct for a mendicant with defilements ended?” ^sn17-30-1-4

“Ānanda, I don’t say that possessions, honor, and popularity obstruct the unshakable freedom of heart. ^sn17-30-1-5

But I do say that possessions, honor, and popularity obstruct the achievement of blissful meditations in this life for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^sn17-30-1-6

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity—bitter and harsh, an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-30-1-7

So you should train like this: ^sn17-30-1-8

‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’ ^sn17-30-1-9

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-30-1-10

### Schism (Linked Discourses 17.31) ^sn17-31-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-31-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-31-1-2

Devadatta split the Saṅgha because his mind was overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-31-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-31-1-4

### Skillful Root (Linked Discourses 17.32) ^sn17-32-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-32-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-32-1-2

Devadatta cut off his skillful root because his mind was overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-32-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-32-1-4

### Skillful Quality (Linked Discourses 17.33) ^sn17-33-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-33-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-33-1-2

Devadatta cut off his skillful quality because his mind was overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-33-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-33-1-4

### Bright Quality (Linked Discourses 17.34) ^sn17-34-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-34-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim … ^sn17-34-1-2

Devadatta cut off his bright quality because his mind was overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-34-1-3

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-34-1-4

### Shortly After He Left (Linked Discourses 17.35) ^sn17-35-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had left. ^sn17-35-1-1

There the Buddha spoke to the mendicants about Devadatta: ^sn17-35-1-2

“Possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall. ^sn17-35-1-3

It’s like a banana plant … ^sn17-35-2-1

or a bamboo … ^sn17-35-3-1

or a reed, all of which bear fruit to their own ruin and downfall … ^sn17-35-4-1

It’s like a mule, which becomes pregnant to its own ruin and downfall. ^sn17-35-5-1

In the same way, possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall. ^sn17-35-5-2

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-35-5-3

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-35-5-4

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn17-35-6-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn17-35-6-2

“The banana plant is destroyed by its own fruit, ^sn17-35-7-1

as are the bamboo and the reed. ^sn17-35-7-2

Honor destroys a reprobate, ^sn17-35-7-3

as pregnancy destroys a mule.” ^sn17-35-7-4

### Five Hundred Carts (Linked Discourses 17.36) ^sn17-36-0

Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn17-36-1-1

Now at that time Prince Ajātasattu was going with five hundred carts at daybreak and dusk to attend on Devadatta, presenting him with an offering of five hundred servings of food. ^sn17-36-1-2

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn17-36-1-3

“Sir, Prince Ajātasattu is going with five hundred carts at daybreak and dusk to attend on Devadatta, presenting him with an offering of five hundred servings of food.” ^sn17-36-1-4

“Mendicants, don’t envy Devadatta’s possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-36-1-5

As long as Prince Ajātasattu goes with five hundred carts at daybreak and dusk to attend on Devadatta, presenting him with an offering of five hundred servings of food, Devadatta can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities. ^sn17-36-1-6

If they were to lance a boil on a wild dog’s nose, it would get even wilder. ^sn17-36-2-1

In the same way, as long as Prince Ajātasattu goes with five hundred carts at daybreak and dusk to attend on Devadatta, presenting him with an offering of five hundred servings of food, Devadatta can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities. ^sn17-36-2-2

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …” ^sn17-36-2-3

### Mother (Linked Discourses 17.37) ^sn17-37-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-37-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-37-1-2

When I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand: ^sn17-37-1-3

‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of their mother.’ ^sn17-37-1-4

But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-37-1-5

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity—bitter and harsh, an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-37-1-6

So you should train like this: ^sn17-37-1-7

‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’ ^sn17-37-1-8

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-37-1-10

### Father, Etc. (Linked Discourses 17.38–43) ^sn17-38-43-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn17-38-43-1-1

“Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-38-43-1-2

When I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand: ^sn17-38-43-1-3

‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of their father.’ …” ^sn17-38-43-1-4

(Tell in full as in SN 17.37.) ^sn17-38-43-1-5

“‘… brother.’ …” ^sn17-38-43-1-6

“‘… sister.’ …” ^sn17-38-43-1-7

“‘… son.’ …” ^sn17-38-43-1-8

“‘… daughter.’ …” ^sn17-38-43-1-9

“‘… wife.’ ^sn17-38-43-1-10

But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity. ^sn17-38-43-1-11

So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity—bitter and harsh, an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn17-38-43-1-12

So you should train like this: ^sn17-38-43-1-13

‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’ ^sn17-38-43-1-14

That’s how you should train.” ^sn17-38-43-1-15

The Linked Discourses on possessions, honor, and popularity are complete. ^sn17-38-43-2-5

## Saṁyutta 18 ^sn18-0

### The Eye, Etc. (Linked Discourses 18.1) ^sn18-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn18-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn18-1-1-2

Then Venerable Rāhula went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn18-1-1-3

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn18-1-1-4

“What do you think, Rāhula? ^sn18-1-2-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn18-1-2-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn18-1-2-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn18-1-2-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn18-1-2-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn18-1-2-7

“No, sir.” ^sn18-1-2-8

“Is the ear permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-3-1

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-1-3-2

“Is the nose permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-3-3

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-1-3-4

“Is the tongue permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-3-5

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-1-3-6

“Is the body permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-3-7

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-1-3-8

“Is the mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-1-3-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn18-1-3-10

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn18-1-3-11

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn18-1-3-12

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn18-1-3-13

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn18-1-3-14

“No, sir.” ^sn18-1-3-15

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, the ear, the nose, the tongue, the body, and the mind. ^sn18-1-4-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn18-1-4-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn18-1-4-3

(The ten discourses of this series should be told in full the same way.) ^sn18-1-4-4

### Sights, Etc. (Linked Discourses 18.2) ^sn18-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn18-2-1-1

“What do you think, Rāhula? ^sn18-2-1-2

Are sights permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-2-1-3

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-2-1-4

“… sounds … ^sn18-2-1-5

smells … ^sn18-2-1-6

tastes … ^sn18-2-1-7

touches … ^sn18-2-1-8

Are ideas permanent or impermanent?” ^sn18-2-1-9

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-2-1-10

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^sn18-2-1-11

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. …” ^sn18-2-1-12

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn18-3-1-4

nose consciousness … ^sn18-3-1-6

craving for smells … ^sn18-8-1-6

the fire element … ^sn18-9-1-6

the space element … ^sn18-9-1-8

### Tendency (Linked Discourses 18.21) ^sn18-21-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn18-21-1-1

Then Venerable Rāhula went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn18-21-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs?” ^sn18-21-1-3

“Rāhula, one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn18-21-1-4

One truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn18-21-1-5

perception … ^sn18-21-1-6

choices … ^sn18-21-1-7

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn18-21-1-8

That’s how to know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs.” ^sn18-21-1-9

### Rid of Conceit (Linked Discourses 18.22) ^sn18-22-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn18-22-1-1

Then Venerable Rāhula went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn18-22-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed?” ^sn18-22-1-3

“Rāhula, when one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ one is freed by not grasping. ^sn18-22-1-4

When one truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn18-22-2-1

perception … ^sn18-22-2-2

choices … ^sn18-22-2-3

When one truly sees any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ one is freed by not grasping. ^sn18-22-2-4

That’s how to know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed.” ^sn18-22-2-5

The Linked Discourses with Rāhula are complete. ^sn18-22-3-7

## Saṁyutta 19 ^sn19-0

### A Skeleton (Linked Discourses 19.1) ^sn19-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn19-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn19-1-1-2

Now at that time Venerable Mark and Venerable Mahāmoggallāna were staying on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn19-1-1-3

Then Mahāmoggallāna robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to Mark and said to him, ^sn19-1-1-4

“Come, Reverend Mark, let’s enter Rājagaha for alms.” ^sn19-1-1-5

“Yes, reverend,” Mark replied. ^sn19-1-1-6

As Mahāmoggallāna was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain he smiled at a certain spot. ^sn19-1-1-7

So Lakkhaṇa said to Mahāmoggallāna, ^sn19-1-1-8

“What is the cause, Reverend Moggallāna, what is the reason you smiled?” ^sn19-1-1-9

“Reverend Mark, it’s the wrong time for this question. ^sn19-1-1-10

Ask me when we’re in the Buddha’s presence.” ^sn19-1-1-11

Then Mark and Mahāmoggallāna wandered for alms in Rājagaha. After the meal, on their return from almsround, they went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Mark said to Mahāmoggallāna: ^sn19-1-2-1

“Just now, as Mahāmoggallāna was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain he smiled at a certain spot. ^sn19-1-2-2

What is the cause, Reverend Moggallāna, what is the reason you smiled?” ^sn19-1-2-3

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a skeleton flying through the air. ^sn19-1-3-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking, clawing, and stabbing it in the ribs ^sn19-1-3-2

as it screeched in pain. ^sn19-1-3-3

It occurred to me: ^sn19-1-3-4

‘Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^sn19-1-3-5

That there can be such a sentient being, ^sn19-1-3-6

such a spirit, ^sn19-1-3-7

such an incarnation!’” ^sn19-1-3-8

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants: ^sn19-1-4-1

“Mendicants, there are disciples who live full of vision and knowledge, ^sn19-1-4-2

since a disciple knows, sees, and witnesses such a thing. ^sn19-1-4-3

Formerly, I too saw that being, but I did not speak of it. ^sn19-1-4-4

For if I had spoken of it others would not have believed me, ^sn19-1-4-5

which would be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^sn19-1-4-6

That being used to be a cattle butcher right here in Rājagaha. ^sn19-1-4-7

As a result of that deed he burned in hell for many years, many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands of years. Now he experiences the residual result of that deed in such an incarnation.” ^sn19-1-4-8

(Tell all these discourses in full like this.) ^sn19-1-4-9

### A Piece of Meat (Linked Discourses 19.2) ^sn19-2-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a scrap of meat flying through the air. ^sn19-2-1-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking and clawing ^sn19-2-1-2

as it screeched in pain. …” … ^sn19-2-1-3

“That being used to be a cattle butcher right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-2-1-4

### A Piece of Flesh (Linked Discourses 19.3) ^sn19-3-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a piece of flesh flying through the air. ^sn19-3-1-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking and clawing ^sn19-3-1-2

as it screeched in pain. …” … ^sn19-3-1-3

“That being used to be a bird hunter right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-3-1-4

### A Flayed Man (Linked Discourses 19.4) ^sn19-4-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a flayed man flying through the air. ^sn19-4-1-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking and clawing ^sn19-4-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-4-1-3

“That being used to be a sheep butcher right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-4-1-4

### Sword Hairs (Linked Discourses 19.5) ^sn19-5-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man whose body hairs were swords flying through the air. ^sn19-5-1-1

And those swords kept rising up and falling on his body ^sn19-5-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-5-1-3

“That being used to be a pig butcher right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-5-1-4

### Spear Hairs (Linked Discourses 19.6) ^sn19-6-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man whose body hairs were spears flying through the air. ^sn19-6-1-1

And those spears kept rising up and falling on his body ^sn19-6-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-6-1-3

“That being used to be a deer hunter right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-6-1-4

### Arrow Hairs (Linked Discourses 19.7) ^sn19-7-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man whose body hairs were arrows flying through the air. ^sn19-7-1-1

And those arrows kept rising up and falling on his body ^sn19-7-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-7-1-3

“That being used to be a punisher right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-7-1-4

### Needle Hairs (Linked Discourses 19.8) ^sn19-8-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man whose body hairs were needles flying through the air. ^sn19-8-1-1

And those needles kept rising up and falling on his body ^sn19-8-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-8-1-3

“That being used to be an equerry right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-8-1-4

### Needle Hairs (2nd) (Linked Discourses 19.9) ^sn19-9-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man whose body hairs were needles flying through the air. ^sn19-9-1-1

The needles bored into his head and out his mouth, ^sn19-9-1-2

into his mouth and out his chest, ^sn19-9-1-3

into his chest and out his belly, ^sn19-9-1-4

into his belly and out his thighs, ^sn19-9-1-5

into his thighs and out his calves, ^sn19-9-1-6

and into his calves and out his feet. ^sn19-9-1-7

And he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-9-1-8

“That being used to be an informant right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-9-1-9

### Pot Balls (Linked Discourses 19.10) ^sn19-10-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man with testicles big as pots flying through the air. ^sn19-10-1-1

When he was walking he had to lift his testicles on to his shoulder. ^sn19-10-1-2

And when he sat down, he sat right on them. ^sn19-10-1-3

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing him, pecking and clawing ^sn19-10-1-4

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-10-1-5

“That being used to be a corrupt village head right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-10-1-6

### Over His Head (Linked Discourses 19.11) ^sn19-11-0

So I have heard. ^sn19-11-1-1

At one time near Rājagaha in the Bamboo Grove … ^sn19-11-1-2

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man sunk over his head in a sewer. …” … ^sn19-11-1-3

“That being used to be an adulterer right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-11-1-4

### A Dung Eater (Linked Discourses 19.12) ^sn19-12-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man sunk in a sewer, eating dung with both hands. …” … ^sn19-12-1-1

“That being used to be a nasty brahmin right here in Rājagaha. ^sn19-12-1-2

In the time of the Buddha Kassapa’s dispensation he invited the Saṅgha of mendicants for a meal. He filled a trough with dung and said: ^sn19-12-1-3

‘My good men, eat as much as you like, and take what’s left.’ …” ^sn19-12-1-4

### A Flayed Woman (Linked Discourses 19.13) ^sn19-13-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a flayed woman flying through the air. ^sn19-13-1-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing her, pecking and clawing ^sn19-13-1-2

as she screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-13-1-3

“That woman used to be an adulteress right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-13-1-4

### A Fishwife (Linked Discourses 19.14) ^sn19-14-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a woman stinking and dingy as a catfish flying through the air. ^sn19-14-1-1

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing her, pecking and clawing ^sn19-14-1-2

as she screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-14-1-3

“That woman used to be a sorceress right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-14-1-4

### A Sweltering Woman (Linked Discourses 19.15) ^sn19-15-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a scorched woman, sooty and sweaty, flying through the air, ^sn19-15-1-1

as she screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-15-1-2
