“That woman used to be the king of Kaliṅga’s chief queen. ^sn19-15-1-3

She was of jealous nature, and poured a brazier of hot coals over her co-wife. …” … ^sn19-15-1-4

### A Headless Trunk (Linked Discourses 19.16) ^sn19-16-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a headless trunk flying through the air. ^sn19-16-1-1

Its eyes and mouth were on its chest. ^sn19-16-1-2

Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking and clawing ^sn19-16-1-3

as it screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-16-1-4

“That being used to be an executioner named Taker right here in Rājagaha. …” ^sn19-16-1-5

### A Bad Monk (Linked Discourses 19.17) ^sn19-17-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a monk flying through the air. ^sn19-17-1-1

His outer robe, bowl, belt, and body were burning, blazing, and glowing ^sn19-17-1-2

as he screamed in pain. …” … ^sn19-17-1-3

“That monk used to be a bad monk in the time of Buddha Kassapa’s dispensation. …” ^sn19-17-1-4

### A Bad Nun (Linked Discourses 19.18) ^sn19-18-0

“I saw a nun flying through the air. ^sn19-18-1-1

Her outer robe was burning …” … ^sn19-18-1-2

“She used to be a bad nun …” ^sn19-18-1-3

### A Bad Trainee Nun (Linked Discourses 19.19) ^sn19-19-0

“I saw a trainee nun flying through the air. ^sn19-19-1-1

Her outer robe was burning …” … ^sn19-19-1-2

“She used to be a bad trainee nun …” ^sn19-19-1-3

### A Bad Novice Monk (Linked Discourses 19.20) ^sn19-20-0

“I saw a novice monk flying through the air. ^sn19-20-1-1

His outer robe was burning …” … ^sn19-20-1-2

“He used to be a bad novice monk …” ^sn19-20-1-3

### A Bad Novice Nun (Linked Discourses 19.21) ^sn19-21-0

“Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a novice nun flying through the air. ^sn19-21-1-1

Her outer robe, bowl, belt, and body were burning, blazing, and glowing ^sn19-21-1-2

as she screamed in pain. ^sn19-21-1-3

It occurred to me: ^sn19-21-1-4

‘Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^sn19-21-1-5

That there can be such a sentient being, ^sn19-21-1-6

such a spirit, ^sn19-21-1-7

such an incarnation!’” ^sn19-21-1-8

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants: ^sn19-21-2-1

“Mendicants, there are disciples who live full of vision and knowledge, ^sn19-21-2-2

since a disciple knows, sees, and witnesses such a thing. ^sn19-21-2-3

Formerly, I too saw that novice nun, but I did not speak of it. ^sn19-21-2-4

For if I had spoken of it others would not have believed me, ^sn19-21-2-6

which would be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^sn19-21-2-7

That female novice used to be a bad novice nun in the time of the Buddha Kassapa’s dispensation. ^sn19-21-2-8

As a result of that deed she burned in hell for many years, many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands of years. Now she experiences the residual result of that deed in such an incarnation.” ^sn19-21-2-9

The Linked Discourses with Mark are complete. ^sn19-21-4-5

## Saṁyutta 20 ^sn20-0

### A Roof Peak (Linked Discourses 20.1) ^sn20-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn20-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn20-1-1-2

There the Buddha … said: ^sn20-1-1-3

“Mendicants, the rafters of a bungalow all lean to the peak and meet at the peak, and when the peak is demolished they’re all demolished too. ^sn20-1-1-4

In the same way all unskillful qualities are rooted in ignorance and meet in ignorance, and when ignorance is demolished they’re all demolished too. ^sn20-1-1-5

So you should train like this: ^sn20-1-1-6

‘We will stay diligent.’ ^sn20-1-1-7

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-1-1-8

### A Fingernail (Linked Discourses 20.2) ^sn20-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-2-1-1

Then the Buddha, picking up a little bit of dirt on his fingernail, addressed the mendicants: ^sn20-2-1-2

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn20-2-1-3

Which is more: the little bit of dirt on my fingernail, or this great earth?” ^sn20-2-1-4

“Sir, the great earth is far more. ^sn20-2-1-5

The little bit of dirt on your fingernail is tiny. ^sn20-2-1-6

Compared to the great earth, it doesn’t count, there’s no comparison, it’s not worth a fraction.” ^sn20-2-1-7

“In the same way the sentient beings reborn as humans are few, ^sn20-2-1-8

while those not reborn as humans are many. ^sn20-2-1-9

So you should train like this: ^sn20-2-1-10

‘We will stay diligent.’ ^sn20-2-1-11

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-2-1-12

### Families (Linked Discourses 20.3) ^sn20-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-3-1-1

“Mendicants, those families with many women and few men are easy prey for bandits and  grain-jar thieves. ^sn20-3-1-2

In the same way any mendicant who has not developed and cultivated the heart’s release by love is easy prey for non-humans. ^sn20-3-1-3

Those families with few women and many men are hard prey for bandits and grain-jar thieves. ^sn20-3-1-4

In the same way a mendicant who has developed and cultivated the heart’s release by love is hard prey for non-humans. ^sn20-3-1-5

So you should train like this: ^sn20-3-1-6

‘We will develop the heart’s release by love. We’ll cultivate it, make it our vehicle and our basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^sn20-3-1-7

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-3-1-8

### Cauldrons (Linked Discourses 20.4) ^sn20-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-4-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose one person was to give a gift of a hundred cauldrons of rice in the morning, at midday, and in the evening. And someone else was to develop a heart of love, even just as long as it takes to pull a cow’s udder. The latter would be more fruitful. ^sn20-4-1-2

So you should train like this: ^sn20-4-1-3

‘We will develop the heart’s release by love. We’ll cultivate it, make it our vehicle and our basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^sn20-4-1-4

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-4-1-5

### A Spear (Linked Discourses 20.5) ^sn20-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-5-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose there was a sharp-pointed spear. ^sn20-5-1-2

And a man came along and thought, ^sn20-5-1-3

‘With my hand or fist I’ll fold this sharp spear over, crumple it, and bend it back!’ ^sn20-5-1-4

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn20-5-1-5

Is that man capable of doing so?” ^sn20-5-1-6

“No, sir. ^sn20-5-1-7

Why not? ^sn20-5-1-8

Because it’s not easy to fold that sharp spear over, crumple it, and bend it back with the hand or fist. ^sn20-5-1-9

That man will eventually get weary and frustrated.” ^sn20-5-1-10

“In the same way, suppose a mendicant has developed the heart’s release by love, has cultivated it, made it a vehicle and a basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. Should any non-human think to overthrow their mind, ^sn20-5-2-1

they’ll eventually get weary and frustrated. ^sn20-5-2-2

So you should train like this: ^sn20-5-2-3

‘We will develop the heart’s release by love. We’ll cultivate it, make it our vehicle and our basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^sn20-5-2-4

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-5-2-5

### The Archers (Linked Discourses 20.6) ^sn20-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-6-1-1

“Mendicants, suppose there were four well-trained expert archers with strong bows standing in the four quarters. ^sn20-6-1-2

And a man came along and thought, ^sn20-6-1-3

‘When these four well-trained expert archers shoot arrows in four quarters, I’ll catch them before they reach the ground, and then I’ll bring them back.’ ^sn20-6-1-4

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn20-6-1-5

Are they qualified to be called ‘a speedster endowed with super-speed’?” ^sn20-6-1-6

“If he could catch an arrow shot by just one well-trained expert archer before it reaches the ground and bring it back, ^sn20-6-2-1

he’d be qualified to be called ‘a speedster endowed with super-speed’. How much more so arrows shot by four archers!” ^sn20-6-2-2

“As fast as that man is, the sun and moon are faster. ^sn20-6-3-1

As fast as that man is, as fast as the sun and moon are, and as fast as the deities that run before the sun and moon are, the waning of the life forces is faster. ^sn20-6-3-2

So you should train like this: ^sn20-6-3-3

‘We will stay diligent.’ ^sn20-6-3-4

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-6-3-5

### The Drum Called Plugger (Linked Discourses 20.7) ^sn20-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-7-1-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, the Dasārahas had a <em>mridangam</em> drum called the Plugger. ^sn20-7-1-2

When the Plugger cracked, the Dasārahas patched it with another plug. ^sn20-7-1-3

Eventually, there came a time when the original wooden shell for the drumskin disappeared ^sn20-7-1-4

and all that was left of the <em>mridangam</em> Plugger was a mass of plugs. ^sn20-7-1-5

In the same way, in a future time there will be mendicants who won’t want to listen when discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited. They won’t actively listen or try to understand, nor will they think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing. ^sn20-7-1-6

But when discourses composed by poets—poetry, with fancy words and phrases, composed by outsiders or spoken by disciples—are being recited they will want to listen. They’ll actively listen and try to understand, and they’ll think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing. ^sn20-7-2-1

And that is how the discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—will disappear. ^sn20-7-2-2

So you should train like this: ^sn20-7-2-3

‘When discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited we will want to listen. We will actively listen and trying to understand, and we will think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing.’ ^sn20-7-2-4

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-7-2-5

### Wood Blocks (Linked Discourses 20.8) ^sn20-8-0

So I have heard. ^sn20-8-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn20-8-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn20-8-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn20-8-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn20-8-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn20-8-1-6

“Mendicants, these days the Licchavis live using wood blocks as pillows, and they exercise diligently and keenly. ^sn20-8-2-1

King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, finds no vulnerability, he’s got no opportunity. ^sn20-8-2-2

But in the future the Licchavis will become delicate, with soft and tender hands and feet. ^sn20-8-2-3

They’ll sleep on soft beds with down pillows until the sun comes up. ^sn20-8-2-4

King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, will find a vulnerability and get an opportunity. ^sn20-8-2-5

These days the mendicants live using wood blocks as pillows, and they meditate diligently and keenly. ^sn20-8-3-1

Māra the Wicked finds no vulnerability, he’s got no opportunity. ^sn20-8-3-2

But in the future the mendicants will become delicate, with soft and tender hands and feet. ^sn20-8-3-3

They’ll sleep on soft beds with down pillows until the sun comes up. ^sn20-8-3-4

Māra the Wicked will find a vulnerability and get an opportunity. ^sn20-8-3-5

So you should train like this: ^sn20-8-3-6

‘We will live using wood blocks as pillows, and we will meditate diligently and keenly.’ ^sn20-8-3-7

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-8-3-8

### A Bull Elephant (Linked Discourses 20.9) ^sn20-9-0

So I have heard. ^sn20-9-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn20-9-1-2

Now at that time a certain junior mendicant went to visit families too often. ^sn20-9-1-3

The mendicants said to him, ^sn20-9-1-4

“Venerable, don’t go to visit families too often.” ^sn20-9-1-5

But that mendicant, when spoken to by the mendicants, said this, ^sn20-9-1-6

“But these senior mendicants think they can go to visit families, so why can’t I?” ^sn20-9-1-7

And then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said: ^sn20-9-2-1

“Once upon a time, mendicants, there was a great lake in the jungle, ^sn20-9-3-1

with bull elephants living nearby. ^sn20-9-3-2

They’d plunge into the lake and pull up lotus bulbs with their trunks. They’d wash them thoroughly until they were free of mud before chewing and swallowing them. ^sn20-9-3-3

That was good for their appearance and strength, and wouldn’t result in death or deadly pain. ^sn20-9-3-4

The young cubs, following the example of the great bull elephants, plunged into the lake and pulled up lotus bulbs with their trunks. But they didn’t wash them thoroughly, and while they were still muddy they chewed and swallowed them. ^sn20-9-3-5

That was not good for their appearance and strength, and resulted in death or deadly pain. ^sn20-9-3-6

In the same way, there are senior mendicants who robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms. ^sn20-9-4-1

There they speak on the teachings, ^sn20-9-4-2

and lay people demonstrate their confidence in them. ^sn20-9-4-3

And when they get things, they use them untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. ^sn20-9-4-4

That’s good for their appearance and strength, and doesn’t result in death or deadly pain. ^sn20-9-4-5

Junior mendicants, following the example of the senior mendicants, robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms. ^sn20-9-4-6

There they speak on the teachings, ^sn20-9-4-7

and lay people demonstrate their confidence in them. ^sn20-9-4-8

But when they get things, they use them tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, not understanding the escape. ^sn20-9-4-9

That’s not good for their appearance and strength, and results in death or deadly pain. ^sn20-9-4-10

So you should train like this: ^sn20-9-4-11

‘When we get things, we will use them untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape.’ ^sn20-9-4-12

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-9-4-13

### A Cat (Linked Discourses 20.10) ^sn20-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-10-1-1

Now at that time a certain mendicant socialized with families too often. ^sn20-10-1-2

The mendicants said to him, ^sn20-10-1-3

“Venerable, don’t socialize with families too often.” ^sn20-10-1-4

But that mendicant, when spoken to by the mendicants, did not stop. ^sn20-10-1-5

And then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said: ^sn20-10-1-6

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a cat was standing by an alley or a drain or a dustbin hunting a little mouse, thinking, ^sn20-10-2-1

‘When that little mouse comes out to feed, I’ll catch it right there and eat it!’ ^sn20-10-2-2

And then that little mouse came out to feed. ^sn20-10-2-3

The cat caught it and hastily swallowed it without chewing. ^sn20-10-2-4

But that little mouse ate its intestines and mesentery, ^sn20-10-2-5

resulting in death and deadly pain. ^sn20-10-2-6

In the same way, take a certain monk who robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters the village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^sn20-10-3-1

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^sn20-10-3-2

Lust infects his mind, ^sn20-10-3-3

resulting in death or deadly pain. ^sn20-10-3-4

For it is death in the training of the Noble One to disavow the training and return to a lesser life. ^sn20-10-3-5

And it is deadly pain to commit one of the corrupt offenses ^sn20-10-3-6

for which rehabilitation is possible. ^sn20-10-3-7

So you should train like this: ^sn20-10-3-8

‘We will enter the village or town for alms guarding body, speech, and mind, establishing mindfulness, and restraining the sense faculties.’ ^sn20-10-3-9

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-10-3-10

### A Jackal (Linked Discourses 20.11) ^sn20-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-11-1-1

“Mendicants, did you hear an old jackal howling at the crack of dawn?” ^sn20-11-1-2

“Yes, sir.” ^sn20-11-1-3

“That old jackal has the disease called mange. ^sn20-11-1-4

Yet it still goes where it wants, ^sn20-11-1-5

stands where it wants, ^sn20-11-1-6

sits where it wants, ^sn20-11-1-7

and lies down where it wants. ^sn20-11-1-8

And the cool breeze still blows on it. ^sn20-11-1-9

A certain person here who claims to follow the Sakyan would be lucky to experience even such an incarnation. ^sn20-11-1-10

So you should train like this: ^sn20-11-1-11

‘We will stay diligent.’ ^sn20-11-1-12

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-11-1-13

### A Jackal (2nd) (Linked Discourses 20.12) ^sn20-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn20-12-1-1

“Mendicants, did you hear an old jackal howling at the crack of dawn?” ^sn20-12-1-2

“Yes, sir.” ^sn20-12-1-3

“There might be some gratitude and thankfulness in that old jackal, but there is none in a certain person here who claims to follow the Sakyan. ^sn20-12-1-4

So you should train like this: ^sn20-12-1-5

‘We will be grateful and thankful. ^sn20-12-1-6

We won’t forget even a small thing done for us.’ ^sn20-12-1-7

That’s how you should train.” ^sn20-12-1-8

The Linked Discourses with similes are complete. ^sn20-12-2-5

## Saṁyutta 21 ^sn21-0

### With Kolita (Linked Discourses 21.1) ^sn21-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn21-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn21-1-1-2

There Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-1-1-3

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn21-1-1-4

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn21-1-1-5

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this: ^sn21-1-2-1

“Just now, reverends, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind: ^sn21-1-2-2

‘They speak of this thing called “noble silence”. ^sn21-1-2-3

What then is this noble silence?’ ^sn21-1-2-4

It occurred to me: ^sn21-1-2-5

‘As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn21-1-2-6

This is called noble silence.’ ^sn21-1-2-7

And so, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I was entering and remaining in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn21-1-2-8

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by placing the mind beset me. ^sn21-1-2-9

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn21-1-3-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! Don’t neglect noble silence, brahmin! Settle your mind in noble silence; unify your mind and bring it to immersion in noble silence.’ ^sn21-1-3-2

And so, after some time, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I entered and remained in the second absorption … ^sn21-1-3-3

So if anyone should be rightly called ^sn21-1-3-4

a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn21-1-3-5

### With Upatissa (Linked Discourses 21.2) ^sn21-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-2-1-1

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-2-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn21-2-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn21-2-1-4

Sāriputta said this: ^sn21-2-1-5

“Just now, reverends, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind: ^sn21-2-2-1

‘Is there anything in the world whose decay and perishing would give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me?’ ^sn21-2-2-2

It occurred to me: ^sn21-2-2-3

‘There is nothing in the world whose decay and perishing would give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me.’” ^sn21-2-2-4

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to him, ^sn21-2-3-1

“Even if the Teacher were to decay and perish? Wouldn’t that give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in you?” ^sn21-2-3-2

“Even if the Teacher were to decay and perish, that wouldn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me. Still, I would think: ^sn21-2-3-3

‘Oh lord, the illustrious Teacher, so mighty and powerful, has vanished! ^sn21-2-3-4

If the Buddha was to remain for a long time, that would be for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.’” ^sn21-2-3-5

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn21-2-3-6

So even if the Teacher were to decay and perish, it wouldn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in him.” ^sn21-2-3-7

### A Mound of Salt (Linked Discourses 21.3) ^sn21-3-0

So I have heard. ^sn21-3-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn21-3-1-2

At that time Venerables Sāriputta and Moggallāna were staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn21-3-1-3

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Moggallāna, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn21-3-1-4

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Sāriputta sat down to one side, and said to Mahāmoggallāna: ^sn21-3-1-5

“Reverend Moggallāna, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn21-3-2-1

Have you spent the day in a peaceful meditation?” ^sn21-3-2-2

“Reverend, I’ve spent the day in a coarse meditation. ^sn21-3-2-3

But I have had some Dhamma talk.” ^sn21-3-2-4

“Who did you have a Dhamma talk with?” ^sn21-3-2-5

“With the Buddha.” ^sn21-3-2-6

“But Reverend, the Buddha is far away. He’s staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn21-3-2-7

Did you go to him with your psychic power, ^sn21-3-2-8

or did he come to you?” ^sn21-3-2-9

“No reverend, I didn’t go to him with my psychic power, ^sn21-3-2-10

nor did he come to me. ^sn21-3-2-11

Rather, the Buddha cleared his clairvoyance and clairaudience towards me, ^sn21-3-2-12

and I cleared my clairvoyance and clairaudience towards him.” ^sn21-3-2-13

“But what manner of Dhamma talk did you have together?” ^sn21-3-2-14

“Well, reverend, I said to the Buddha, ^sn21-3-3-1

‘Sir, they speak of one who is energetic. ^sn21-3-3-2

How is an energetic person defined?’ ^sn21-3-3-3

When I said this, the Buddha said, ^sn21-3-3-4

‘Moggallāna, it’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up: ^sn21-3-3-5

“Gladly, let only skin, sinews, and bones remain! Let the flesh and blood waste away in my body! I will not stop trying until I have achieved what is possible by human strength, energy, and vigor.” ^sn21-3-3-6

That’s how an individual is energetic.’ ^sn21-3-3-7

That’s the Dhamma talk I had together with the Buddha.” ^sn21-3-3-8

“Reverend, next to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna I’m like a few pieces of gravel next to the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^sn21-3-4-1

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna is so mighty and powerful he could, if he wished, live on for the proper lifespan.” ^sn21-3-4-3

“Reverend, next to Venerable Sāriputta I’m like a few grains of salt next to a mound of salt. ^sn21-3-5-1

Venerable Sāriputta has been commended, complimented, and praised by the Buddha: ^sn21-3-5-3

‘Sāriputta is full of wisdom, ^sn21-3-6-1

ethics, and peace. ^sn21-3-6-2

Even a mendicant who has crossed over ^sn21-3-6-3

might at best equal him.’” ^sn21-3-6-4

And so these two spiritual giants agreed with each other’s fine words. ^sn21-3-7-1

### A Junior Mendicant (Linked Discourses 21.4) ^sn21-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-4-1-1

Now at that time a certain junior monk, after his meal, on his return from almsround, entered his dwelling, where he adhered to passivity and silence. And he didn’t help the mendicants out when it was time to sew robes. ^sn21-4-1-2

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn21-4-1-3

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^sn21-4-2-1

“Please, monk, in my name tell that monk that the Teacher summons him.” ^sn21-4-2-2

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to that monk and said to him, ^sn21-4-2-3

“Reverend, the teacher summons you.” ^sn21-4-2-4

“Yes, reverend,” that monk replied. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn21-4-2-5

“Is it really true, monk, that after your meal, on your return from almsround, you entered your dwelling, where you adhered to passivity and silence, and you didn’t help the mendicants out when it was time to sew robes?” ^sn21-4-2-6

“Sir, I am doing my own work.” ^sn21-4-2-7

Then the Buddha, knowing that monk’s train of thought, addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-4-3-1

“Mendicants, don’t complain about this monk. ^sn21-4-3-2

This monk gets the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when he wants, without trouble or difficulty. He has realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life, and lives having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^sn21-4-3-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-4-4-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-4-4-2

“Not by being slack, ^sn21-4-5-1

or with little strength ^sn21-4-5-2

may this be realized—extinguishment, ^sn21-4-5-3

the release from all suffering. ^sn21-4-5-4

This young monk, ^sn21-4-6-1

this best of men, ^sn21-4-6-2

bears his final body, ^sn21-4-6-3

having vanquished Māra with his legions.” ^sn21-4-6-4

### With Sujāta (Linked Discourses 21.5) ^sn21-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-5-1-1

Then Venerable Sujāta went to see the Buddha. ^sn21-5-1-2

The Buddha saw him coming off in the distance, ^sn21-5-1-3

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-5-1-4

“This gentleman shines in both ways. ^sn21-5-1-5

He’s attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. And he has realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lives having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^sn21-5-1-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-5-1-7

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-5-1-8

“This mendicant truly shines. ^sn21-5-2-1

His heart is upright, ^sn21-5-2-2

he’s unfettered, detached, ^sn21-5-2-3

quenched by not grasping. ^sn21-5-2-4

He bears his final body, ^sn21-5-2-5

having vanquished Māra with his legions.” ^sn21-5-2-6

### With Bhaddiya the Dwarf (Linked Discourses 21.6) ^sn21-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-6-1-1

Then Venerable Bhaddiya the Dwarf went to see the Buddha. ^sn21-6-1-2

The Buddha saw him coming off in the distance, ^sn21-6-1-3

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-6-1-4

“Mendicants, do you see this monk coming—ugly, unsightly, deformed, and despised by the mendicants?” ^sn21-6-1-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn21-6-1-6

“That mendicant is very mighty and powerful. It’s not easy to find an attainment that he has not already attained. ^sn21-6-1-7

And he has realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lives having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^sn21-6-1-8

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-6-1-9

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-6-1-10

“Geese, herons, and peacocks, ^sn21-6-2-1

elephants and spotted deer— ^sn21-6-2-2

though their bodies are not equal, ^sn21-6-2-3

they all fear the lion. ^sn21-6-2-4

So it is for humans— ^sn21-6-3-1

if a little person is wise, ^sn21-6-3-2

they’re the truly great one, ^sn21-6-3-3

not the fool with a good body.” ^sn21-6-3-4

### With Visākha, Pañcāli’s Son (Linked Discourses 21.7) ^sn21-7-0

So I have heard. ^sn21-7-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn21-7-1-2

Now at that time Venerable Visākha, Pañcāli’s son, was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk. His words were polished, clear, articulate, expressing the meaning, comprehensive, and independent. ^sn21-7-1-3

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall. He sat down on the seat spread out, ^sn21-7-2-1

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-7-2-2

“Mendicants, who was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk?” ^sn21-7-2-3

“Sir, it was Venerable Visākha, Pañcāli’s son.” ^sn21-7-2-4

Then the Buddha said to Visākha: ^sn21-7-3-1

“Good, good, Visākha! ^sn21-7-3-2

It’s good that you educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk, with words that are polished, clear, articulate, expressing the meaning, comprehensive, and independent.” ^sn21-7-3-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-7-4-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-7-4-2

“Though an astute person is mixed up with fools, ^sn21-7-5-1

they don’t know unless he speaks. ^sn21-7-5-2

But when he speaks they know, ^sn21-7-5-3

he’s teaching the state free of death. ^sn21-7-5-4

He should speak and illustrate the teaching, ^sn21-7-6-1

holding up the banner of the seers. ^sn21-7-6-2

Words well spoken are the seers’ banner, ^sn21-7-6-3

for the teaching is the banner of the seers.” ^sn21-7-6-4

### With Nanda (Linked Discourses 21.8) ^sn21-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-8-1-1

Then Venerable Nanda—the Buddha’s cousin on his mother’s side—dressed in nicely pressed and ironed robes, applied eyeshadow, and took a glazed bowl. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn21-8-1-2

“Nanda, as a gentleman who has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, it’s not appropriate for you to dress in nicely pressed and ironed robes, apply eyeshadow, and carry a glazed bowl. ^sn21-8-1-3

It’s appropriate for you to stay in the wilderness, eat only almsfood, wear rag robes, and live without concern for sensual pleasures.” ^sn21-8-1-4

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-8-1-5

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-8-1-6

“When will I see Nanda ^sn21-8-2-1

in the wilderness, wearing rag robes, ^sn21-8-2-2

feeding on scraps offered by strangers, ^sn21-8-2-3

unconcerned for sensual pleasures?” ^sn21-8-2-4

Then some time later Venerable Nanda stayed in the wilderness, ate only almsfood, wore rag robes, and lived without concern for sensual pleasures. ^sn21-8-3-1

### With Tissa (Linked Discourses 21.9) ^sn21-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-9-1-1

Then Venerable Tissa—the Buddha’s cousin on his father’s side—went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. He was miserable and sad, with tears flowing. Then the Buddha said to him: ^sn21-9-1-2

“Tissa, why are you sitting there so miserable and sad, with tears flowing?” ^sn21-9-1-3

“Sir, it’s because the mendicants beset me on all sides with sneering and jeering.” ^sn21-9-1-4

“That’s because you admonish others, but don’t accept admonition yourself. ^sn21-9-1-5

As a gentleman who has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, it’s not appropriate for you to admonish others without accepting admonition yourself. ^sn21-9-1-6

It’s appropriate for you to ^sn21-9-1-7

admonish others and accept admonition yourself.” ^sn21-9-1-8

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-9-2-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-9-2-2

“Why are you angry? Don’t be angry! ^sn21-9-3-1

It’s better to not be angry, Tissa. ^sn21-9-3-2

For this spiritual life is lived ^sn21-9-3-3

in order to remove anger, conceit, and denigration.” ^sn21-9-3-4

### Senior In Name Only (Linked Discourses 21.10) ^sn21-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn21-10-1-1

Now at that time there was a certain mendicant who was Senior in name only. He lived alone and praised living alone. ^sn21-10-1-2

He entered the village for alms alone, returned alone, sat in private alone, and focussed on walking mindfully alone. ^sn21-10-1-3

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn21-10-1-4

“Sir, there’s a certain mendicant who is Senior in name only. He lives alone and praises living alone.” ^sn21-10-1-5

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^sn21-10-2-1

“Please, monk, in my name tell the mendicant Senior that ^sn21-10-2-2

the teacher summons him.” ^sn21-10-2-3

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to Venerable Senior and said to him, ^sn21-10-2-4

“Reverend Senior, the teacher summons you.” ^sn21-10-2-5

“Yes, reverend,” that monk replied. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn21-10-2-6

“Is it really true, Senior, that you live alone and praise living alone?” ^sn21-10-2-7

“Yes, sir.” ^sn21-10-2-8

“But in what way do you live alone and praise living alone?” ^sn21-10-2-9

“Well, sir, I enter the village for alms alone, return alone, sit in private alone, and focus on walking mindfully alone. ^sn21-10-2-10

That’s how I live alone and praise living alone.” ^sn21-10-2-11

“That is a kind of living alone, I don’t deny it. ^sn21-10-3-1

But as to how living alone is fulfilled in detail, ^sn21-10-3-2

listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn21-10-3-3

“Yes, sir,” he replied. ^sn21-10-3-4

“And how, Senior, is living alone fulfilled in detail? ^sn21-10-3-5

It’s when what’s in the past is given up, what’s in the future is relinquished, and desire and greed for present incarnations is eliminated. ^sn21-10-3-6

That’s how living alone is fulfilled in detail.” ^sn21-10-3-7

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-10-4-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-10-4-2

“The vanquisher of all, knower of all, so very intelligent, ^sn21-10-5-1

is unsullied in the midst of all things. ^sn21-10-5-2

He’s given up all, freed in the ending of craving: ^sn21-10-5-3

I declare that man to be one who lives alone.” ^sn21-10-5-4

### With Mahākappina (Linked Discourses 21.11) ^sn21-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-11-1-1

Then Venerable Mahākappina went to see the Buddha. ^sn21-11-1-2

The Buddha saw him coming off in the distance, ^sn21-11-1-3

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-11-1-4

“Mendicants, do you see that monk coming—white, thin, with a pointy nose?” ^sn21-11-1-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn21-11-1-6

“That mendicant is very mighty and powerful. ^sn21-11-1-7

It’s not easy to find an attainment that he has not already attained. ^sn21-11-1-8

And he has realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lives having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^sn21-11-1-9

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-11-2-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-11-2-2

“The aristocrat is best among people ^sn21-11-3-1

who take clan as the standard. ^sn21-11-3-2

But one accomplished in knowledge and conduct ^sn21-11-3-3

is best among gods and humans. ^sn21-11-3-4

The sun blazes by day, ^sn21-11-4-1

the moon radiates at night, ^sn21-11-4-2

the aristocrat shines in armor, ^sn21-11-4-3

and the brahmin shines in absorption. ^sn21-11-4-4

But all day and all night, ^sn21-11-4-5

the Buddha blazes with glory.” ^sn21-11-4-6

### Companions (Linked Discourses 21.12) ^sn21-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn21-12-1-1

Then two mendicants who were companions, protégés of Venerable Mahākappina, went to see the Buddha. ^sn21-12-1-2

The Buddha saw them coming off in the distance, ^sn21-12-1-3

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn21-12-1-4

“Mendicants, do you see those monks coming who are companions, protégés of Venerable Mahākappina?” ^sn21-12-1-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn21-12-1-6

“Those mendicants are very mighty and powerful. ^sn21-12-1-7

It’s not easy to find an attainment that they have not already attained. ^sn21-12-1-8

And they’ve realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. They live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^sn21-12-1-9

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn21-12-2-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn21-12-2-2

“These companion mendicants ^sn21-12-3-1

have been together for a long time. ^sn21-12-3-2

The true teaching has brought them together, ^sn21-12-3-3

the teaching proclaimed by the Buddha. ^sn21-12-3-4

They’ve been well trained by Kappina ^sn21-12-4-1

in the teaching proclaimed by the Noble One. ^sn21-12-4-2

They bear their final body, ^sn21-12-4-3

having vanquished Māra with his legions.” ^sn21-12-4-4

The Linked Discourses on monks are complete. ^sn21-12-5-7

The Book of Causality is finished. ^sn21-12-6-6

## Saṁyutta 22 ^sn22-0

### Nakula’s Father (Linked Discourses 22.1) ^sn22-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^sn22-1-1-2

Then the householder Nakula’s father went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^sn22-1-1-3

“Sir, I’m an old man, elderly and senior. I’m advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life. My body is ailing and I’m constantly unwell. ^sn22-1-2-1

I hardly ever get to see the esteemed mendicants. ^sn22-1-2-2

May the Buddha please advise me ^sn22-1-2-3

and instruct me. ^sn22-1-2-4

It will be for my lasting welfare and happiness.” ^sn22-1-2-5

“That’s so true, householder! That’s so true, householder! ^sn22-1-3-1

For this body is ailing, swaddled in its shell. ^sn22-1-3-2

If anyone dragging around this body claimed to be healthy even for an hour, what is that but foolishness? ^sn22-1-3-3

So you should train like this: ^sn22-1-3-4

‘Though my body is ailing, my mind will be healthy.’ ^sn22-1-3-5

That’s how you should train.” ^sn22-1-3-6

And then the householder Nakula’s father approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right. Then he went up to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, and sat down to one side. Sāriputta said to him: ^sn22-1-4-1

“Householder, your faculties are so very clear, ^sn22-1-4-2

and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn22-1-4-3

Did you get to hear a Dhamma talk in the Buddha’s presence today?” ^sn22-1-4-4

“What else, sir, could it be? ^sn22-1-5-1

Just now the Buddha anointed me with the deathless ambrosia of a Dhamma talk.” ^sn22-1-5-2

“But with what kind of ambrosial Dhamma talk did the Buddha anoint you?” ^sn22-1-5-3

So Nakula’s father told Sāriputta all that had happened, and said, ^sn22-1-5-4

“That’s the ambrosial Dhamma talk with which I was anointed by the Buddha.” ^sn22-1-6-8

“But didn’t you feel the need to ask the Buddha the further question: ^sn22-1-7-1

‘Sir, how do you define someone ailing in body and ailing in mind, and someone ailing in body and healthy in mind’?” ^sn22-1-7-2

“Sir, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta. ^sn22-1-7-3

May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this.” ^sn22-1-7-4

“Well then, householder, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn22-1-8-1

“Yes, sir,” replied Nakula’s father. ^sn22-1-8-2

Sāriputta said this: ^sn22-1-8-3

“And how is one ailing in body and ailing in mind? ^sn22-1-9-1

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the qualities of a noble one. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the qualities of a true person. ^sn22-1-9-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-1-9-3

They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am form, form is mine!’ ^sn22-1-9-4

But that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-9-5

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-9-6

They regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling. ^sn22-1-10-1

They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am feeling, feeling is mine!’ ^sn22-1-10-2

But that feeling of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-10-3

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-10-4

They regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception. ^sn22-1-11-1

They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am perception, perception is mine!’ ^sn22-1-11-2

But that perception of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-11-3

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-11-4

They regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices. ^sn22-1-12-1

They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am choices, choices are mine!’ ^sn22-1-12-2

But those choices of theirs decay and perish, ^sn22-1-12-3

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-12-4

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-1-13-1

They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am consciousness, consciousness is mine!’ ^sn22-1-13-2

But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-13-3

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-13-4

That’s how one is ailing in body and ailing in mind. ^sn22-1-13-5

And how is one ailing in body and healthy in mind? ^sn22-1-14-1

It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-1-14-2

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-1-14-3

They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am form, form is mine!’ ^sn22-1-14-4

So when that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-14-5

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-14-6

They don’t regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling. ^sn22-1-15-1

They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am feeling, feeling is mine!’ ^sn22-1-15-2

So when that feeling of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-15-3

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-15-4

They don’t regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception. ^sn22-1-16-1

They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am perception, perception is mine!’ ^sn22-1-16-2

So when that perception of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-16-3

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-16-4

They don’t regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices. ^sn22-1-17-1

They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am choices, choices are mine!’ ^sn22-1-17-2

So when those choices of theirs decay and perish, ^sn22-1-17-3

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-17-4

They don’t regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-1-18-1

They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am consciousness, consciousness is mine!’ ^sn22-1-18-2

So when that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-1-18-3

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-1-18-4

That’s how an individual is ailing in body and healthy in mind.” ^sn22-1-18-5

That’s what Venerable Sāriputta said. ^sn22-1-19-1

Satisfied, Nakula’s father approved what Sāriputta said. ^sn22-1-19-2

### At Devadaha (Linked Discourses 22.2) ^sn22-2-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-2-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, where they have a town named Devadaha. ^sn22-2-1-2

Then several mendicants who were heading for the west went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn22-2-1-3

“Sir, we wish to go to a western land to take up residence there.” ^sn22-2-1-4

“But mendicants, have you taken leave of Sāriputta?” ^sn22-2-2-1

“No, sir, we haven’t.” ^sn22-2-2-2

“You should take leave of Sāriputta. ^sn22-2-2-3

He’s astute, and supports his spiritual companions, the mendicants.” ^sn22-2-2-4

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn22-2-2-5

Now at that time Venerable Sāriputta was sitting not far from the Buddha in a clump of golden shower trees. ^sn22-2-3-1

And then those mendicants approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. They rose from their seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on their right. Then they went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn22-2-3-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him, ^sn22-2-3-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, we wish to go to a western land to take up residence there. ^sn22-2-3-4

We have taken leave of the Teacher.” ^sn22-2-3-5

“Reverends, there are those who question a mendicant who has gone abroad— ^sn22-2-4-1

astute aristocrats, brahmins, householders, and ascetics— ^sn22-2-4-2

for astute people are inquisitive: ^sn22-2-4-3

‘But what does the venerables’ Teacher teach? What does he explain?’ I trust the venerables have properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered the teachings, and penetrated them with wisdom. That way, when answering you will repeat what the Buddha has said and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You will explain in line with the teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.” ^sn22-2-4-4

“Reverend, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta. ^sn22-2-5-1

May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this.” ^sn22-2-5-2

“Well then, reverends, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn22-2-5-3

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^sn22-2-5-4

Sāriputta said this: ^sn22-2-5-5

“Reverends, there are those who question a mendicant who has gone abroad— ^sn22-2-6-1

astute aristocrats, brahmins, householders, and ascetics— ^sn22-2-6-2

for astute people are inquisitive: ^sn22-2-6-3

‘But what does the venerables’ Teacher teach? What does he explain?’ ^sn22-2-6-4

When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer: ^sn22-2-6-5

‘Reverend, our Teacher explained the removal of desire and lust.’ ^sn22-2-6-6

When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further: ^sn22-2-7-1

‘But regarding what does the venerables’ teacher explain the removal of desire and lust?’ ^sn22-2-7-4

When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer: ^sn22-2-7-5

‘Our teacher explains the removal of desire and lust for form, ^sn22-2-7-6

feeling, ^sn22-2-7-7

perception, ^sn22-2-7-8

choices, ^sn22-2-7-9

and consciousness.’ ^sn22-2-7-10

When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further: ^sn22-2-8-1

‘But what drawback has he seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form, ^sn22-2-8-4

feeling, ^sn22-2-8-5

perception, ^sn22-2-8-6

choices, ^sn22-2-8-7

and consciousness?’ ^sn22-2-8-8

When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer: ^sn22-2-8-9

‘If you’re not free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes it gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-2-8-10

If you’re not free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for feeling … ^sn22-2-8-11

perception … ^sn22-2-8-12

choices … ^sn22-2-8-13

consciousness, when that consciousness decays and perishes it gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-2-8-15

This is the drawback our Teacher has seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form, ^sn22-2-8-16

feeling, ^sn22-2-8-17

perception, ^sn22-2-8-18

choices, ^sn22-2-8-19

and consciousness.’ ^sn22-2-8-20

When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further: ^sn22-2-9-1

‘But what benefit has he seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form, ^sn22-2-9-4

feeling, ^sn22-2-9-5

perception, ^sn22-2-9-6

choices, ^sn22-2-9-7

and consciousness?’ ^sn22-2-9-8

When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer: ^sn22-2-9-9

‘If you are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-2-9-10

If you are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for feeling … ^sn22-2-9-11

perception … ^sn22-2-9-12

choices … ^sn22-2-9-13

consciousness, when that consciousness decays and perishes it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-2-9-14

This is the benefit our Teacher has seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form, feeling, ^sn22-2-9-15

perception, ^sn22-2-9-16

choices, ^sn22-2-9-17

and consciousness.’ ^sn22-2-9-18

If those who acquired and kept unskillful qualities were to live happily in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever; and if, when their body breaks up, after death, they could expect to go to a good place, the Buddha would not praise giving up unskillful qualities. ^sn22-2-10-1

But since those who acquire and keep unskillful qualities live unhappily in the present life, full of distress, anguish, and fever; and since, when their body breaks up, after death, they can expect to go to a bad place, the Buddha praises giving up unskillful qualities. ^sn22-2-10-2

If those who embraced and kept skillful qualities were to live unhappily in the present life, full of distress, anguish, and fever; and if, when their body breaks up, after death, they could expect to go to a bad place, the Buddha would not praise embracing skillful qualities. ^sn22-2-11-1

But since those who embrace and keep skillful qualities live happily in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever; and since, when their body breaks up, after death, they can expect to go to a good place, the Buddha praises embracing skillful qualities.” ^sn22-2-11-2

This is what Venerable Sāriputta said. ^sn22-2-12-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what Sāriputta said. ^sn22-2-12-2

### With Hāliddikāni (Linked Discourses 22.3) ^sn22-3-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-3-1-1

At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain. ^sn22-3-1-2

Then the householder Hāliddikāni went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn22-3-1-3

“Sir, this was said by the Buddha in the Chapter of the Eights, in ‘The Questions of Māgandiya’: ^sn22-3-1-4

‘After leaving their bastion to migrate without abode, ^sn22-3-2-1

a sage doesn’t get close to anyone in town. ^sn22-3-2-2

Rid of sensual pleasures, not expecting, ^sn22-3-2-3

they wouldn’t get in arguments with people.’ ^sn22-3-2-4

How should we see the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement?” ^sn22-3-3-1

“Householder, the form element is a shelter for consciousness. ^sn22-3-4-1

One whose consciousness is shackled by greed for the form element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion. ^sn22-3-4-2

The feeling element is a shelter for consciousness. ^sn22-3-4-3

One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the feeling element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion. ^sn22-3-4-4

The perception element is a shelter for consciousness. ^sn22-3-4-5

One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the perception element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion. ^sn22-3-4-6

The choices element is a shelter for consciousness. ^sn22-3-4-7

One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the choices element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion. ^sn22-3-4-8

That’s how one is a migrant going from  bastion to bastion. ^sn22-3-4-9

And how is one a migrant with no bastion? ^sn22-3-5-1

The Realized One has given up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the form element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-3-5-2

That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant with no bastion. ^sn22-3-5-3

The Realized One has given up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the feeling element … ^sn22-3-5-4

the perception element … ^sn22-3-5-5

the choices element … ^sn22-3-5-6

the consciousness element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-3-5-7

That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant with no bastion. ^sn22-3-5-8

That’s how one is a migrant with no shelter. ^sn22-3-5-9

And how is one a migrant going from abode to abode? ^sn22-3-6-1

Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sights, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode. ^sn22-3-6-2

Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sounds … ^sn22-3-6-3

smells … ^sn22-3-6-4

tastes … ^sn22-3-6-5

touches … ^sn22-3-6-6

thoughts, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode. ^sn22-3-6-7

That’s how one is a migrant going from abode to abode. ^sn22-3-6-8

And how is one a migrant without abode? ^sn22-3-7-1

The Realized One has given up chasing the abodes that are the features of  sights. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-3-7-2

That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant without abode. ^sn22-3-7-3

The Realized One has given up chasing the abodes that are the features of sounds … ^sn22-3-7-4

smells … ^sn22-3-7-5

tastes … ^sn22-3-7-6

touches … ^sn22-3-7-7

ideas. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-3-7-8

That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant without abode. ^sn22-3-7-9

That’s how one is a migrant without abode. ^sn22-3-7-10

And how does one get close to people in town? ^sn22-3-8-1

It’s when someone mixes closely with laypeople, sharing their joys and sorrows—happy when they’re happy and sad when they’re sad—and getting themselves involved in their business. ^sn22-3-8-2

That’s how one gets close to people in town. ^sn22-3-8-3

And how does one not get close to people in town? ^sn22-3-9-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t mix closely with laypeople, not sharing their joys and sorrows—not happy when they’re happy or sad when they’re sad—and not getting themselves involved in their business. ^sn22-3-9-2

That’s how one doesn’t get close to people in town. ^sn22-3-9-3

And how is one not rid of sensual pleasures? ^sn22-3-10-1

It’s when someone isn’t rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^sn22-3-10-2

That’s how one is not rid of sensual pleasures. ^sn22-3-10-3

And how is one rid of sensual pleasures? ^sn22-3-11-1

It’s when someone is rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^sn22-3-11-2

That’s how one is rid of sensual pleasures. ^sn22-3-11-3

And how is one expecting? ^sn22-3-12-1

It’s when someone thinks: ^sn22-3-12-2

‘In the future, may I be of such form, such feeling, such perception, such choices, and such consciousness!’ ^sn22-3-12-3

That’s how one is expecting. ^sn22-3-12-4

And how is one not expecting? ^sn22-3-13-1

It’s when someone doesn’t think: ^sn22-3-13-2

‘In the future, may I be of such form, such feeling, such perception, such choices, and such consciousness!’ ^sn22-3-13-3

That’s how one is not expecting. ^sn22-3-13-4

And how does one argue with people? ^sn22-3-14-1

It’s when someone takes part in this sort of discussion: ^sn22-3-14-2

‘You don’t understand this teaching and training. I understand this teaching and training. What, you understand this teaching and training? You’re practicing wrong. I’m practicing right. You said last what you should have said first. You said first what you should have said last. I stay on topic, you don’t. What you’ve thought so much about has been disproved. Your doctrine is refuted. Go on, save your doctrine! You’re trapped; get yourself out of this—if you can!’ ^sn22-3-14-3

That’s how one argues with people. ^sn22-3-14-4

And how does one not argue with people? ^sn22-3-15-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t take part in this sort of discussion: ^sn22-3-15-2

‘You don’t understand this teaching and training … get yourself out of this—if you can!’ ^sn22-3-15-3

That’s how one doesn’t argue with people. ^sn22-3-15-4

So, householder, that’s how to understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief in the Chapter of the Eights, in ‘The Questions of Māgandiya’: ^sn22-3-16-1

‘After leaving their bastion to migrate without abode, ^sn22-3-17-1

a sage doesn’t get close to anyone in town. ^sn22-3-17-2

Rid of sensual pleasures, not expecting, ^sn22-3-17-3

they wouldn’t get in arguments with people.’” ^sn22-3-17-4

### Hāliddikāni (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.4) ^sn22-4-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-4-1-1

At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain. ^sn22-4-1-2

Then the householder Hāliddikāni went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna … and asked him, ^sn22-4-1-3

“Sir, this was said by the Buddha in ‘The Questions of Sakka’: ^sn22-4-1-4

‘Those ascetics and brahmins who are freed due to the ending of craving have reached the ultimate goal, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate end, and are best among gods and humans.’ ^sn22-4-1-5

How should we see the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement?” ^sn22-4-2-1

“Householder, consider any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the form element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. With the ending, fading away, cessation, giving away, and letting go of that, the mind is said to be ‘well freed’. ^sn22-4-3-1

Consider any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the feeling element … ^sn22-4-4-1

the perception element … ^sn22-4-4-2

the choices element … ^sn22-4-4-3

the consciousness element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. With the ending, fading away, cessation, giving away, and letting go of that, the mind is said to be ‘well freed’. ^sn22-4-4-4

So, householder, that’s how to understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief in ‘The Questions of Sakka’: ^sn22-4-5-1

‘Those ascetics and brahmins who are freed due to the ending of craving have reached the ultimate goal, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate end, and are best among gods and humans.’” ^sn22-4-5-2

### Development of Immersion (Linked Discourses 22.5) ^sn22-5-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-5-1-1

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-5-1-2

“Mendicants, develop immersion. ^sn22-5-1-4

A mendicant who has immersion truly understands. ^sn22-5-1-5

What do they truly understand? ^sn22-5-1-6

The origin and ending of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-5-1-7

And what is the origin of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness? ^sn22-5-2-1

It’s when a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging. ^sn22-5-2-2

What do they approve, welcome, and keep clinging to? ^sn22-5-3-1

They approve, welcome, and keep clinging to form. ^sn22-5-3-2

This gives rise to relishing. ^sn22-5-3-3

Relishing forms is grasping. ^sn22-5-3-4

Their grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn22-5-3-5

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn22-5-3-6

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn22-5-3-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn22-5-3-8

They approve, welcome, and keep clinging to feeling … ^sn22-5-4-1

perception … ^sn22-5-4-2

choices … ^sn22-5-4-3

consciousness. ^sn22-5-4-4

This gives rise to relishing. ^sn22-5-4-5

Relishing consciousness is grasping. ^sn22-5-4-6

Their grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn22-5-4-7

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn22-5-4-8

Rebirth is a condition that gives rise to old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-5-4-9

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn22-5-4-10

This is the origin of form, ^sn22-5-5-1

feeling, ^sn22-5-5-2

perception, ^sn22-5-5-3

choices, ^sn22-5-5-4

and consciousness. ^sn22-5-5-5

And what is the ending of form, feeling, ^sn22-5-6-1

perception, ^sn22-5-6-2

choices, ^sn22-5-6-3

and consciousness? ^sn22-5-6-4

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, or keep clinging. ^sn22-5-7-1

What don’t they approve, welcome, or keep clinging to? ^sn22-5-8-1

They don’t approve, welcome, or keep clinging to form. ^sn22-5-8-2

As a result, relishing of form ceases. ^sn22-5-8-3

When that relishing ceases, grasping ceases. ^sn22-5-8-4

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. … ^sn22-5-8-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn22-5-8-6

They don’t approve, welcome, or keep clinging to feeling … ^sn22-5-9-1

perception … ^sn22-5-10-1

choices … ^sn22-5-10-2

consciousness. ^sn22-5-11-1

As a result, relishing of consciousness ceases. ^sn22-5-11-2

When that relishing ceases, grasping ceases. … ^sn22-5-11-3

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn22-5-11-4

This is the ending of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.” ^sn22-5-12-1

### Retreat (Linked Discourses 22.6) ^sn22-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-6-1-1

“Mendicants, meditate in retreat. ^sn22-6-1-2

A mendicant in retreat truly understands. ^sn22-6-1-3

What do they truly understand? ^sn22-6-1-4

The origin and ending of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. …” ^sn22-6-1-5

(Tell in full as in the previous discourse.) ^sn22-6-1-6

### Anxiety Because of Grasping (Linked Discourses 22.7) ^sn22-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-7-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you how grasping leads to anxiety, and how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety. ^sn22-7-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn22-7-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn22-7-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn22-7-1-5

“And how does grasping lead to anxiety? ^sn22-7-2-1

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-7-2-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-7-2-3

But that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-7-2-4

and consciousness latches on to the perishing of form. ^sn22-7-2-5

Anxieties occupy their mind, born of latching on to the perishing of form, and originating in accordance with natural principles. ^sn22-7-2-6

So they become frightened, worried, concerned, and anxious because of grasping. ^sn22-7-2-7

They regard feeling as self … ^sn22-7-3-1

They regard perception as self … ^sn22-7-4-1

They regard choices as self … ^sn22-7-4-2

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-7-5-1

But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-7-5-2

and consciousness latches on to the perishing of consciousness. ^sn22-7-5-3

Anxieties occupy their mind, born of latching on to the perishing of consciousness, and originating in accordance with natural principles. ^sn22-7-5-4

So they become frightened, worried, concerned, and anxious because of grasping. ^sn22-7-5-5

That’s how grasping leads to anxiety. ^sn22-7-5-6

And how does not grasping lead to freedom from anxiety? ^sn22-7-6-1

It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-7-6-2

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-7-6-3

When that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-7-6-4

consciousness doesn’t latch on to the perishing of form. ^sn22-7-6-5

Anxieties—born of latching on to the perishing of form and originating in accordance with natural principles—don’t occupy their mind. ^sn22-7-6-6

So they don’t become frightened, worried, concerned, or anxious because of grasping. ^sn22-7-6-7

They don’t regard feeling as self … ^sn22-7-7-1

They don’t regard perception as self … ^sn22-7-8-1

They don’t regard choices as self … ^sn22-7-8-2

They don’t regard consciousness as self … ^sn22-7-9-1

When that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-7-9-2

consciousness doesn’t latch on to the perishing of consciousness. ^sn22-7-9-3

Anxieties—born of latching on to the perishing of consciousness and originating in accordance with natural principles—don’t occupy their mind. ^sn22-7-9-4

So they don’t become frightened, worried, concerned, or anxious because of grasping. ^sn22-7-9-5

That’s how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety.” ^sn22-7-9-6

### Anxiety Because of Grasping (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.8) ^sn22-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-8-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you how grasping leads to anxiety, and how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety. ^sn22-8-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. ^sn22-8-1-3

And how does grasping lead to anxiety? ^sn22-8-1-4

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person regards form like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn22-8-1-5

But that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-8-1-6

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-8-1-7

They regard feeling … ^sn22-8-1-8

perception … ^sn22-8-1-9

choices … ^sn22-8-1-10

consciousness like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn22-8-1-11

But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-8-1-12

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-8-1-13

That’s how grasping leads to anxiety. ^sn22-8-1-14

And how does not grasping lead to freedom from anxiety? ^sn22-8-2-1

It’s when a learned noble disciple regards form like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-8-2-2

When that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-8-2-3

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-8-2-4

They regard feeling … ^sn22-8-2-5

perception … ^sn22-8-2-6

choices … ^sn22-8-2-7

consciousness like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-8-2-8

When that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-8-2-9

it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-8-2-10

That’s how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety.” ^sn22-8-2-11

### Impermanence in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 22.9) ^sn22-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-9-1-1

“Mendicants, form of the past and future is impermanent, ^sn22-9-1-2

let alone the present. ^sn22-9-1-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past form, ^sn22-9-1-4

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future form, ^sn22-9-1-5

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form. ^sn22-9-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-9-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-9-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-9-1-9

Consciousness of the past and future is impermanent, ^sn22-9-1-14

let alone the present. ^sn22-9-1-15

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past consciousness, ^sn22-9-1-16

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future consciousness, ^sn22-9-1-17

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present consciousness.” ^sn22-9-1-18

### Suffering in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 22.10) ^sn22-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-10-1-1

“Mendicants, form of the past and future is suffering, ^sn22-10-1-2

let alone the present. ^sn22-10-1-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past form, ^sn22-10-1-4

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future form, ^sn22-10-1-5

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form. ^sn22-10-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-10-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-10-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-10-1-9

Consciousness of the past and future is suffering, ^sn22-10-1-10

let alone the present. ^sn22-10-1-11

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past consciousness, ^sn22-10-1-12

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future consciousness, ^sn22-10-1-13

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present consciousness.” ^sn22-10-1-14

### Not-Self in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 22.11) ^sn22-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-11-1-1

“Mendicants, form of the past and future is not-self, ^sn22-11-1-2

let alone the present. ^sn22-11-1-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past form, ^sn22-11-1-4

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future form, ^sn22-11-1-5

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form. ^sn22-11-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-11-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-11-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-11-1-9

Consciousness of the past and future is not-self, ^sn22-11-1-10

let alone the present. ^sn22-11-1-11

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with past consciousness, ^sn22-11-1-12

doesn’t look forward to enjoying future consciousness, ^sn22-11-1-13

and they practice for the disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present consciousness.” ^sn22-11-1-14

### Impermanence (Linked Discourses 22.12) ^sn22-12-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-12-1-1

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-12-1-2

“Mendicants, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are impermanent. ^sn22-12-1-4

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-12-1-5

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-12-1-6

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-12-1-7

### Suffering (Linked Discourses 22.13) ^sn22-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-13-1-1

“Mendicants, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are suffering. ^sn22-13-1-2

Seeing this … ^sn22-13-1-3

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-13-1-4

### Not-Self (Linked Discourses 22.14) ^sn22-14-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-14-1-1

“Mendicants, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are not-self. ^sn22-14-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-14-1-3

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-14-1-4

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-14-1-5

### That Which is Impermanent (Linked Discourses 22.15) ^sn22-15-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-15-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-15-1-2

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-15-1-3

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-15-1-4

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-15-1-5

Feeling is impermanent … ^sn22-15-1-6

Perception is impermanent … ^sn22-15-1-10

Choices are impermanent … ^sn22-15-1-11

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-15-1-12

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-15-1-13

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-15-1-14

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-15-1-15

Seeing this … ^sn22-15-1-16

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-15-1-17

### That Which is Suffering (Linked Discourses 22.16) ^sn22-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-16-1-1

“Mendicants, form is suffering. ^sn22-16-1-2

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-16-1-3

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-16-1-4

Feeling is suffering … ^sn22-16-1-5

Perception is suffering … ^sn22-16-1-6

Choices are suffering … ^sn22-16-1-7

Consciousness is suffering. ^sn22-16-1-8

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-16-1-9

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-16-1-10

Seeing this … ^sn22-16-1-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-16-1-12

### That Which is Not-Self (Linked Discourses 22.17) ^sn22-17-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-17-1-1

“Mendicants, form is not-self. ^sn22-17-1-2

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-17-1-3

Feeling is not-self … ^sn22-17-1-4

Perception is not-self … ^sn22-17-1-5

Choices are not-self … ^sn22-17-1-6

Consciousness is not-self. ^sn22-17-1-7

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-17-1-8

Seeing this … ^sn22-17-1-9

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-17-1-10

### Impermanence With Its Cause (Linked Discourses 22.18) ^sn22-18-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-18-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-18-1-2

The cause and reason that gives rise to form is also impermanent. ^sn22-18-1-3

Since form is produced by what is impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn22-18-1-4

Feeling is impermanent … ^sn22-18-1-5

Perception is impermanent … ^sn22-18-1-8

Choices are impermanent … ^sn22-18-1-9

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-18-1-12

The cause and reason that gives rise to consciousness is also impermanent. ^sn22-18-1-13

Since consciousness is produced by what is impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn22-18-1-14

Seeing this … ^sn22-18-1-15

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-18-1-16

### Suffering With Its Cause (Linked Discourses 22.19) ^sn22-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-19-1-1

“Mendicants, form is suffering. ^sn22-19-1-2

The cause and reason that gives rise to form is also suffering. ^sn22-19-1-3

Since form is produced by what is suffering, how could it be happiness? ^sn22-19-1-4

Feeling is suffering … ^sn22-19-1-5

Perception is suffering … ^sn22-19-1-6

Choices are suffering … ^sn22-19-1-7

Consciousness is suffering. ^sn22-19-1-8

The cause and reason that gives rise to consciousness is also suffering. ^sn22-19-1-9

Since consciousness is produced by what is suffering, how could it be happiness? ^sn22-19-1-10

Seeing this … ^sn22-19-1-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-19-1-12

### Not-Self With Its Cause (Linked Discourses 22.20) ^sn22-20-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-20-1-1

“Mendicants, form is not-self. ^sn22-20-1-2

The cause and reason that gives rise to form is also not-self. ^sn22-20-1-3

Since form is produced by what is not-self, how could it be self? ^sn22-20-1-4

Feeling is not-self … ^sn22-20-1-5

Perception is not-self … ^sn22-20-1-6

Choices are not-self … ^sn22-20-1-7

Consciousness is not-self. ^sn22-20-1-8

The cause and reason that gives rise to consciousness is also not-self. ^sn22-20-1-9

Since consciousness is produced by what is not-self, how could it be self? ^sn22-20-1-10

Seeing this … ^sn22-20-1-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-20-1-12

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 22.21) ^sn22-21-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-21-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^sn22-21-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘cessation’. ^sn22-21-1-3

The cessation of what things does this refer to?” ^sn22-21-1-4

“Ānanda, form is impermanent, conditioned, dependently originated, liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn22-21-1-5

Its cessation is what ‘cessation’ refers to. ^sn22-21-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-21-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-21-1-9

Choices … ^sn22-21-1-10

Consciousness is impermanent, conditioned, dependently originated, liable to end, vanish, fade away, and cease. ^sn22-21-1-12

Its cessation is what ‘cessation’ refers to. ^sn22-21-1-13

When they speak of ‘cessation’, its the cessation of these things that this refers to.” ^sn22-21-1-14

### The Burden of Responsibility (Linked Discourses 22.22) ^sn22-22-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-22-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the burden, the bearer of the burden, the taking up of the burden, and the putting down of the burden. ^sn22-22-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-22-1-3

And what is the burden? ^sn22-22-1-4

The five grasping aggregates, it should be said. ^sn22-22-1-5

What five? ^sn22-22-1-6

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-22-1-7

This is called the burden. ^sn22-22-1-8

And who is the bearer of the burden? ^sn22-22-2-1

The individual, it should be said; ^sn22-22-2-2

the venerable of such and such name and clan. ^sn22-22-2-3

This is called the bearer of the burden. ^sn22-22-2-4

And what is the taking up of the burden? ^sn22-22-3-1

It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure there wherever it alights. That is, ^sn22-22-3-2

craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^sn22-22-3-3

This is called the taking up of the burden. ^sn22-22-3-4

And what is the putting down of the burden? ^sn22-22-4-1

It’s the fading away and cessation of that very same craving no residue left behind; giving it away, letting it go, releasing it, and not clinging to it. ^sn22-22-4-2

This is called the putting down of the burden.” ^sn22-22-4-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-22-5-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn22-22-5-2

“The five aggregates are indeed burdens, ^sn22-22-6-1

and the individual is the bearer of the burden. ^sn22-22-6-2

Taking up the burden is suffering in the world, ^sn22-22-6-3

and putting the burden down is happiness. ^sn22-22-6-4

When the heavy burden is put down ^sn22-22-7-1

without taking up another, ^sn22-22-7-2

one who has plucked out craving, root and all, ^sn22-22-7-3

is hungerless, quenched.” ^sn22-22-7-4

### Complete Understanding (Linked Discourses 22.23) ^sn22-23-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-23-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that should be completely understood, and complete understanding. ^sn22-23-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-23-1-3

And what things should be completely understood? ^sn22-23-1-4

Form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-23-1-5

These are called the things that should be completely understood. ^sn22-23-1-6

And what is complete understanding? ^sn22-23-1-7

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn22-23-1-8

This is called complete understanding.” ^sn22-23-1-9

### Directly Knowing (Linked Discourses 22.24) ^sn22-24-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-24-1-1

“Mendicants, without directly knowing and completely understanding form, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn22-24-1-2
