Without directly knowing and completely understanding feeling … ^sn22-24-1-3

perception … ^sn22-24-1-4

choices … ^sn22-24-1-5

consciousness, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn22-24-1-6

By directly knowing and completely understanding form, having dispassion for it and giving it up, you can end suffering. ^sn22-24-1-7

By directly knowing and completely understanding feeling … ^sn22-24-1-8

perception … ^sn22-24-1-9

choices … ^sn22-24-1-10

consciousness, having dispassion for it and giving it up, you can end suffering.” ^sn22-24-1-11

### Desire and Greed (Linked Discourses 22.25) ^sn22-25-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-25-1-1

“Mendicants, give up desire and greed for form. ^sn22-25-1-2

Thus that form will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-25-1-3

Give up desire and greed for feeling … ^sn22-25-1-4

perception … ^sn22-25-1-6

choices … ^sn22-25-1-8

consciousness. ^sn22-25-1-10

Thus that consciousness will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn22-25-1-11

### Gratification (Linked Discourses 22.26) ^sn22-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-26-1-1

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn22-26-1-2

‘What’s the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form … ^sn22-26-1-3

feeling … ^sn22-26-1-4

perception … ^sn22-26-1-5

choices … ^sn22-26-1-6

and consciousness?’ ^sn22-26-1-7

Then it occurred to me: ^sn22-26-1-8

‘The pleasure and happiness that arise from form: this is its gratification. ^sn22-26-1-9

That form is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-26-1-10

Removing and giving up desire and greed for form: this is its escape. ^sn22-26-1-11

The pleasure and happiness that arise from feeling … ^sn22-26-1-12

perception … ^sn22-26-1-15

choices … ^sn22-26-1-16

consciousness: this is its gratification. ^sn22-26-1-19

That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-26-1-20

Removing and giving up desire and greed for consciousness: this is its escape.’ ^sn22-26-1-21

As long as I didn’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn22-26-2-1

But when I did truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn22-26-2-2

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn22-26-2-4

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn22-26-2-5

### Gratification (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.27) ^sn22-27-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-27-1-1

“Mendicants, I went in search of form’s gratification, ^sn22-27-1-2

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-3

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of form’s gratification. ^sn22-27-1-4

I went in search of form’s drawback, ^sn22-27-1-5

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-6

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of form’s drawback. ^sn22-27-1-7

I went in search of form’s escape, ^sn22-27-1-8

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-9

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of form’s escape. ^sn22-27-1-10

I went in search of the gratification of feeling … ^sn22-27-1-11

perception … ^sn22-27-1-12

choices … ^sn22-27-1-13

and consciousness, ^sn22-27-1-14

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-15

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of consciousness’s gratification. ^sn22-27-1-16

I went in search of consciousness’s drawback, ^sn22-27-1-17

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-18

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of consciousness’s drawback. ^sn22-27-1-19

I went in search of consciousness’s escape, ^sn22-27-1-20

and I found it. ^sn22-27-1-21

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of consciousness’s escape. ^sn22-27-1-22

As long as I didn’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening … ^sn22-27-1-23

But when I did truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening … ^sn22-27-1-24

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn22-27-1-25

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn22-27-1-26

### Gratification (3rd) (Linked Discourses 22.28) ^sn22-28-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-28-1-1

“Mendicants, if there were no gratification in form, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn22-28-1-2

But since there is gratification in form, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn22-28-1-3

If form had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-28-1-4

But since form has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-28-1-5

If there were no escape from form, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^sn22-28-1-6

But since there is an escape from form, sentient beings do escape from it. ^sn22-28-1-7

If there were no gratification in feeling … ^sn22-28-1-8

perception … ^sn22-28-1-9

choices … ^sn22-28-1-10

consciousness, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn22-28-1-12

But since there is gratification in consciousness, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn22-28-1-13

If consciousness had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-28-1-14

But since consciousness has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-28-1-15

If there were no escape from consciousness, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^sn22-28-1-16

But since there is an escape from consciousness, sentient beings do escape from it. ^sn22-28-1-17

As long as sentient beings don’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they haven’t escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they don’t live detached, liberated, with a mind free of limits. ^sn22-28-2-1

But when sentient beings truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they’ve escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they live detached, liberated, with a mind free of limits.” ^sn22-28-2-3

### Taking Pleasure (Linked Discourses 22.29) ^sn22-29-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-29-1-1

“Mendicants, if you take pleasure in form, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn22-29-1-2

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn22-29-1-3

If you take pleasure in feeling … ^sn22-29-1-4

perception … ^sn22-29-1-5

choices … ^sn22-29-1-6

consciousness, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn22-29-1-7

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn22-29-1-8

If you don’t take pleasure in form, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn22-29-1-9

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say. ^sn22-29-1-10

If you don’t take pleasure in feeling … ^sn22-29-1-11

perception … ^sn22-29-1-12

choices … ^sn22-29-1-13

consciousness, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn22-29-1-14

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-29-1-15

### Arising (Linked Discourses 22.30) ^sn22-30-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-30-1-1

“Mendicants, the arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of form is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn22-30-1-2

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of feeling … ^sn22-30-1-3

perception … ^sn22-30-1-4

choices … ^sn22-30-1-5

consciousness is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn22-30-1-6

The cessation, settling, and disappearance of form is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death. ^sn22-30-1-7

The cessation, settling, and ending of feeling … ^sn22-30-1-8

perception … ^sn22-30-1-9

choices … ^sn22-30-1-10

consciousness is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death.” ^sn22-30-1-11

### The Root of Gloom (Linked Discourses 22.31) ^sn22-31-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-31-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you gloom and the root of gloom. ^sn22-31-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-31-1-3

And what is gloom? ^sn22-31-1-4

Form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are gloom. ^sn22-31-1-5

This is called gloom. ^sn22-31-1-6

And what is the root of gloom? ^sn22-31-1-7

It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure there wherever it alights. That is, ^sn22-31-1-8

craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^sn22-31-1-9

This is called the root of gloom.” ^sn22-31-1-10

### Brittle (Linked Discourses 22.32) ^sn22-32-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-32-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you what is brittle and what is not brittle. ^sn22-32-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-32-1-3

And what is brittle? What is not brittle? ^sn22-32-1-4

Form is brittle, ^sn22-32-1-5

but its cessation, settling, and ending is not brittle. ^sn22-32-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-32-1-7

perception … ^sn22-32-1-9

choices … ^sn22-32-1-10

consciousness is brittle, ^sn22-32-1-12

but its cessation, settling, and ending is not brittle.” ^sn22-32-1-13

### It’s Not Yours (Linked Discourses 22.33) ^sn22-33-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-33-1-1

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn22-33-1-2

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-33-1-3

And what isn’t yours? ^sn22-33-1-4

Form isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-33-1-5

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-33-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-33-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-33-1-9

Choices … ^sn22-33-1-10

Consciousness isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-33-1-12

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-33-1-13

Suppose a person was to carry off the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove, or burn them, or do what they want with them. ^sn22-33-2-1

Would you think: ^sn22-33-2-2

‘This person is carrying us off, burning us, or doing what they want with us’?” ^sn22-33-2-3

“No, sir. ^sn22-33-2-4

Why is that? ^sn22-33-2-5

Because to us that’s neither self nor belonging to self.” ^sn22-33-2-6

“In the same way, mendicants, form isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-33-2-7

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-33-2-8

Feeling … ^sn22-33-2-9

Perception … ^sn22-33-2-11

Choices … ^sn22-33-2-12

Consciousness isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-33-2-13

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn22-33-2-14

### It’s Not Yours (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.34) ^sn22-34-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-34-1-1

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn22-34-1-2

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-34-1-3

And what isn’t yours? ^sn22-34-1-4

Form isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-34-1-5

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-34-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-34-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-34-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-34-1-9

Consciousness isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn22-34-1-10

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn22-34-1-11

Give up what’s not yours. ^sn22-34-1-12

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn22-34-1-13

### A Mendicant (Linked Discourses 22.35) ^sn22-35-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-35-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn22-35-1-2

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn22-35-1-3

“Mendicant, you’re reckoned by what you have an underlying tendency for. ^sn22-35-1-5

You’re not reckoned by what you have no underlying tendency for.” ^sn22-35-1-6

“Understood, Blessed One! Understood, Holy One!” ^sn22-35-1-7

“But how do you see the detailed meaning of my brief statement?” ^sn22-35-2-1

“If you have an underlying tendency for form, you’re reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-2-2

If you have an underlying tendency for feeling … ^sn22-35-2-3

perception … ^sn22-35-2-4

choices … ^sn22-35-2-5

consciousness, you’re reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-2-6

If you have no underlying tendency for form, you’re not reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-2-7

If you have no underlying tendency for feeling … ^sn22-35-2-8

perception … ^sn22-35-2-9

choices … ^sn22-35-2-10

consciousness, you’re not reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-2-11

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^sn22-35-2-12

“Good, good, mendicant! ^sn22-35-3-1

It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this. ^sn22-35-3-2

If you have an underlying tendency for form, you’re reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-3-3

If you have an underlying tendency for feeling … ^sn22-35-3-4

perception … ^sn22-35-3-5

choices … ^sn22-35-3-6

consciousness, you’re reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-3-7

If you have no underlying tendency for form, you’re not reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-3-8

If you have no underlying tendency for feeling … ^sn22-35-3-9

perception … ^sn22-35-3-10

choices … ^sn22-35-3-11

consciousness, you’re not reckoned by that. ^sn22-35-3-12

This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” ^sn22-35-3-13

And then that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn22-35-4-1

Then that mendicant, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn22-35-5-1

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn22-35-5-2

And that mendicant became one of the perfected. ^sn22-35-5-3

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 22.37) ^sn22-37-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-37-1-1

And then Venerable Ānanda … ^sn22-37-1-2

sitting to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn22-37-1-3

“Ānanda, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn22-37-2-1

‘Reverend Ānanda, what are the things for which arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident?’ ^sn22-37-2-2

How would you answer?” ^sn22-37-2-3

“Sir, suppose they were to ask me: ^sn22-37-2-4

‘What are the things for which arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident?’ ^sn22-37-2-5

I’d answer like this: ^sn22-37-2-6

‘Reverend, the arising of form is evident, its vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-37-2-7

The arising of feeling … ^sn22-37-2-8

perception … ^sn22-37-2-9

choices … ^sn22-37-2-10

consciousness is evident, its vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-37-2-11

These are the things for which arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident.’ ^sn22-37-2-12

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn22-37-2-13

“Good, good, Ānanda. ^sn22-37-3-1

The arising of form is evident, its vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-37-3-2

The arising of feeling … ^sn22-37-3-3

perception … ^sn22-37-3-4

choices … ^sn22-37-3-5

consciousness is evident, its vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-37-3-6

These are the things for which arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-37-3-7

That’s how you should answer such a question.” ^sn22-37-3-8

### With Ānanda (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.38) ^sn22-38-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-38-1-1

Sitting to one side, the Buddha said to Ānanda: ^sn22-38-1-2

“Ānanda, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn22-38-2-1

‘Reverend Ānanda, what are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident? ^sn22-38-2-2

What are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident? ^sn22-38-2-3

What are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting are evident?’ ^sn22-38-2-4

How would you answer?” ^sn22-38-2-5

“Sir, suppose they were to ask me: ^sn22-38-2-6

‘Reverend Ānanda, what are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident? ^sn22-38-2-7

What are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident? ^sn22-38-2-8

What are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting are evident?’ ^sn22-38-2-9

I’d answer like this: ^sn22-38-2-10

‘Whatever form has passed, ceased, and perished, ^sn22-38-2-11

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-2-12

Whatever feeling … ^sn22-38-2-13

perception … ^sn22-38-2-15

choices … ^sn22-38-2-16

consciousness has passed, ceased, and perished, ^sn22-38-2-18

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-2-19

These the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-2-20

Whatever form is not yet born, and has not yet appeared, ^sn22-38-3-1

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident. ^sn22-38-3-2

Whatever feeling … ^sn22-38-3-3

perception … ^sn22-38-3-5

choices … ^sn22-38-3-6

consciousness is not yet born, and has not yet appeared, ^sn22-38-3-8

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident. ^sn22-38-3-9

These are the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident. ^sn22-38-3-10

Whatever form has been born, and has appeared, ^sn22-38-4-1

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting is evident. ^sn22-38-4-2

Whatever feeling … ^sn22-38-4-3

perception … ^sn22-38-4-4

choices … ^sn22-38-4-5

consciousness has been born, and has appeared, its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting are evident. ^sn22-38-4-7

These are the things for which arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident.’ ^sn22-38-4-8

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn22-38-4-9

“Good, good, Ānanda. ^sn22-38-5-1

Whatever form has passed, ceased, and perished, ^sn22-38-5-2

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-5-3

Whatever feeling … ^sn22-38-5-4

perception … ^sn22-38-5-5

choices … ^sn22-38-5-6

consciousness has passed, ceased, and perished, ^sn22-38-5-7

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-5-8

These the things for which arising, vanishing, and change while persisting were evident. ^sn22-38-5-9

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident. ^sn22-38-6-2

perception … ^sn22-38-6-4

choices … ^sn22-38-6-5

consciousness is not yet born, and has not yet appeared, ^sn22-38-6-6

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting will be evident. ^sn22-38-6-7

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting are evident. ^sn22-38-7-2

perception … ^sn22-38-7-4

choices … ^sn22-38-7-5

consciousness has been born, and has appeared, ^sn22-38-7-6

its arising, vanishing, and change while persisting are evident. ^sn22-38-7-7

### In Line With the Teaching (Linked Discourses 22.39) ^sn22-39-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-39-1-1

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is practicing in line with the teaching, this is what’s in line with the teaching. ^sn22-39-1-2

They should live full of disillusionment for form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-39-1-3

Living in this way, they completely understand form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-39-1-4

Completely understanding form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness, they’re freed from these things. ^sn22-39-1-5

They’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-39-1-6

### In Line With the Teaching (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.40) ^sn22-40-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-40-1-1

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is practicing in line with the teaching, this is what’s in line with the teaching. They should meditate observing impermanence in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. … ^sn22-40-1-2

They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-40-1-3

### In Line With the Teaching (3rd) (Linked Discourses 22.41) ^sn22-41-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-41-1-1

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is practicing in line with the teaching, this is what’s in line with the teaching. They should meditate observing suffering in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. … ^sn22-41-1-2

They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-41-1-3

### In Line With the Teaching (4th) (Linked Discourses 22.42) ^sn22-42-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-42-1-1

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is practicing in line with the teaching, this is what’s in line with the teaching. They should meditate observing not-self in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. … ^sn22-42-1-2

They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-42-1-3

### Be Your Own Island (Linked Discourses 22.43) ^sn22-43-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-43-1-1

“Mendicants, be your own island, your own refuge, with no other refuge. Let the teaching be your island and your refuge, with no other refuge. ^sn22-43-1-2

When you live like this, you should examine rationally: ‘From what are sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress born and produced?’ ^sn22-43-1-3

And, mendicants, from what are sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress born and produced? ^sn22-43-2-1

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-43-2-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-43-2-3

But that form of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-43-2-4

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-43-2-5

They regard feeling as self … ^sn22-43-2-6

They regard perception as self … ^sn22-43-2-9

They regard choices as self … ^sn22-43-2-10

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-43-2-11

But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes, ^sn22-43-2-12

which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-43-2-13

Sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are given up when you understand the impermanence of form—its perishing, fading away, and cessation—and you truly see with right understanding that all form, whether past or present, is impermanent, suffering, and perishable. ^sn22-43-3-1

When these things are given up there’s no anxiety. Without anxiety you live happily. A mendicant who lives happily is said to be quenched in that respect. ^sn22-43-3-2

Sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are given up when you understand the impermanence of feeling … ^sn22-43-3-3

perception … ^sn22-43-3-5

choices … ^sn22-43-3-6

consciousness—its perishing, fading away, and cessation—and you truly see with right understanding that all consciousness, whether past or present, is impermanent, suffering, and perishable. ^sn22-43-3-8

When these things are given up there’s no anxiety. Without anxiety you live happily. A mendicant who lives happily is said to be quenched in that respect.” ^sn22-43-3-9

### Practice (Linked Discourses 22.44) ^sn22-44-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-44-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the practice that leads to the origin of substantial reality and the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-44-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-44-1-3

And what is the practice that leads to the origin of substantial reality? ^sn22-44-1-4

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-44-1-5

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-44-1-6

They regard feeling as self … ^sn22-44-1-7

They regard perception as self … ^sn22-44-1-8

They regard choices as self … ^sn22-44-1-9

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-44-1-10

This is called the practice that leads to the origin of substantial reality. ^sn22-44-1-11

And that’s why it’s called a way of regarding things that leads to the origin of suffering. ^sn22-44-1-12

And what is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality? ^sn22-44-2-1

It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-44-2-2

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-44-2-3

They don’t regard feeling as self … ^sn22-44-2-4

They don’t regard perception as self … ^sn22-44-2-5

They don’t regard choices as self … ^sn22-44-2-6

They don’t regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-44-2-7

This is called the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-44-2-8

And that’s why it’s called a way of regarding things that leads to the cessation of suffering.” ^sn22-44-2-9

### Impermanence (Linked Discourses 22.45) ^sn22-45-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-45-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-45-1-2

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-45-1-3

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-45-1-4

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-45-1-5

Seeing truly with right understanding like this, the mind becomes dispassionate and freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-45-1-6

Feeling is impermanent … ^sn22-45-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-45-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-45-1-9

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-45-1-10

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-45-1-11

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-45-1-12

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-45-1-13

Seeing truly with right understanding like this, the mind becomes dispassionate and freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-45-1-14

If a mendicant’s mind is dispassionate towards the form element, the feeling element, ^sn22-45-1-15

the perception element, ^sn22-45-1-16

the choices element, ^sn22-45-1-17

and the consciousness element, it’s freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-45-1-18

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn22-45-1-19

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-45-1-20

### Impermanence (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.46) ^sn22-46-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-46-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-46-1-2

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-46-1-3

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-46-1-4

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-46-1-5

Feeling is impermanent … ^sn22-46-1-6

Perception is impermanent … ^sn22-46-1-7

Choices are impermanent … ^sn22-46-1-8

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-46-1-9

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-46-1-10

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-46-1-11

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-46-1-12

Seeing truly with right understanding like this, they have no theories about the first beginning. ^sn22-46-2-1

Not having theories about the first beginning, they have no theories about the final end. ^sn22-46-2-2

Not having theories about the final end, they don’t obstinately stick to them. ^sn22-46-2-3

Not misapprehending, the mind becomes dispassionate towards form, ^sn22-46-2-4

feeling, ^sn22-46-2-5

perception, ^sn22-46-2-6

choices, ^sn22-46-2-7

and consciousness; it’s freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-46-2-8

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn22-46-2-9

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-46-2-10

### Ways of Regarding (Linked Discourses 22.47) ^sn22-47-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-47-1-1

“Mendicants, whatever ascetics and brahmins regard various kinds of things as self, all regard the five grasping aggregates, or one of them. ^sn22-47-1-2

What five? ^sn22-47-1-3

It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-47-1-4

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-47-1-5

They regard feeling … ^sn22-47-1-6

perception … ^sn22-47-1-7

choices … ^sn22-47-1-8

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-47-1-9

So they’re not rid of this way of regarding things, and the conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-47-2-1

As long as they’re not rid of the conceit ‘I am’, the five faculties are conceived— ^sn22-47-2-2

the eye, ear, nose, tongue, and body. ^sn22-47-2-3

The mind, ideas, and the element of ignorance are all present. ^sn22-47-2-4

Struck by feelings born of contact with ignorance, an unlearned ordinary person thinks ‘I am’, ^sn22-47-2-5

‘I am this’, ^sn22-47-2-6

‘I will be’, ^sn22-47-2-7

‘I will not be’, ^sn22-47-2-8

‘I will have form’, ^sn22-47-2-9

‘I will be formless’, ^sn22-47-2-10

‘I will be percipient’, ^sn22-47-2-11

‘I will not be percipient’, ^sn22-47-2-12

‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient’. ^sn22-47-2-13

The five faculties stay right where they are. ^sn22-47-3-1

But a learned noble disciple gives up ignorance about them and gives rise to knowledge. ^sn22-47-3-2

With the fading away of ignorance and the arising of knowledge, they don’t think ‘I am’, ^sn22-47-3-3

‘I am this’, ^sn22-47-3-4

‘I will be’, ^sn22-47-3-5

‘I will not be’, ^sn22-47-3-6

‘I will have form’, ^sn22-47-3-7

‘I will be formless’, ^sn22-47-3-8

‘I will be percipient’, ^sn22-47-3-9

‘I will be non-percipient’, ^sn22-47-3-10

‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient’.” ^sn22-47-3-11

### Aggregates (Linked Discourses 22.48) ^sn22-48-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-48-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the five aggregates and the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-48-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-48-1-3

And what are the five aggregates? ^sn22-48-1-4

Any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: this is called the aggregate of form. ^sn22-48-1-5

Any kind of feeling at all … ^sn22-48-1-6

Any kind of perception at all … ^sn22-48-1-7

Any kind of choices at all … ^sn22-48-1-8

Any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: this is called the aggregate of consciousness. ^sn22-48-1-9

These are called the five aggregates. ^sn22-48-1-10

And what are the five grasping aggregates? ^sn22-48-2-1

Any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or fine; inferior or superior; far or near, which is accompanied by defilements and fuels grasping: this is called the grasping aggregate of form. ^sn22-48-2-2

Any kind of feeling at all … ^sn22-48-2-3

Any kind of perception at all … ^sn22-48-2-4

Any kind of choices at all … ^sn22-48-2-5

Any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or fine; inferior or superior; far or near, which is accompanied by defilements and fuels grasping: this is called the grasping aggregate of consciousness. ^sn22-48-2-6

These are called the five grasping aggregates.” ^sn22-48-2-7

### With Soṇa (Linked Discourses 22.49) ^sn22-49-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-49-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn22-49-1-2

Then the householder Soṇa went up to the Buddha … ^sn22-49-1-3

The Buddha said to him: ^sn22-49-1-4

“Soṇa, there are ascetics and brahmins who—based on form, which is impermanent, suffering, and perishable—regard themselves thus: ‘I’m better’, ^sn22-49-2-1

or ‘I’m equal’, ^sn22-49-2-2

or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn22-49-2-3

What is that but a failure to see truly? ^sn22-49-2-4

Based on feeling … ^sn22-49-2-5

perception … ^sn22-49-2-9

choices … ^sn22-49-2-10

consciousness, which is impermanent, suffering, and perishable, they regard themselves thus: ‘I’m better’, ^sn22-49-2-14

or ‘I’m equal’, ^sn22-49-2-15

or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn22-49-2-16

What is that but a failure to see truly? ^sn22-49-2-17

There are ascetics and brahmins who—based on form, which is impermanent, suffering, and perishable—don’t regard themselves thus: ‘I’m better’, ^sn22-49-3-1

or ‘I’m equal’, ^sn22-49-3-2

or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn22-49-3-3

What is that but seeing truly? ^sn22-49-3-4

Based on feeling … ^sn22-49-3-5

perception … ^sn22-49-3-6

choices … ^sn22-49-3-7

consciousness, which is impermanent, suffering, and perishable, they don’t regard themselves thus: ‘I’m better’, ^sn22-49-3-8

or ‘I’m equal’, ^sn22-49-3-9

or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn22-49-3-10

What is that but seeing truly? ^sn22-49-3-11

What do you think, Soṇa? ^sn22-49-4-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-49-4-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-49-4-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-49-4-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-49-4-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-8

“Is feeling … ^sn22-49-4-9

perception … ^sn22-49-4-11

choices … ^sn22-49-4-12

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-49-4-13

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-14

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-49-4-15

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-16

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-49-4-17

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-49-4-18

“No, sir.” ^sn22-49-4-19

“So, Soṇa, you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-49-5-1

You should truly see any kind of feeling … ^sn22-49-6-1

perception … ^sn22-49-6-2

choices … ^sn22-49-6-3

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-49-6-4

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-49-7-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-49-7-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-49-7-3

### With Soṇa (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.50) ^sn22-50-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-50-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn22-50-1-2

Then the householder Soṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn22-50-1-3

“Soṇa, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t understand form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-50-2-1

They don’t understand feeling … ^sn22-50-2-2

perception … ^sn22-50-2-3

choices … ^sn22-50-2-4

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-50-2-5

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn22-50-2-6

There are ascetics and brahmins who do understand form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-50-3-1

They do understand feeling … ^sn22-50-3-2

perception … ^sn22-50-3-3

choices … ^sn22-50-3-4

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-50-3-5

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn22-50-3-6

### The End of Relishing (Linked Discourses 22.51) ^sn22-51-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-51-1-1

“Mendicants, form really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn22-51-1-2

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn22-51-1-3

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn22-51-1-4

When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed. ^sn22-51-1-5

Feeling … ^sn22-51-1-6

Perception … ^sn22-51-1-10

Choices … ^sn22-51-1-11

Consciousness really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn22-51-1-15

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn22-51-1-16

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn22-51-1-17

When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed.” ^sn22-51-1-18

### The End of Relishing (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.52) ^sn22-52-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-52-1-1

“Mendicants, rationally apply the mind to form. Truly see the impermanence of form. ^sn22-52-1-2

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with form. ^sn22-52-1-3

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn22-52-1-4

When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed. ^sn22-52-1-5

Rationally apply the mind to feeling … ^sn22-52-1-6

perception … ^sn22-52-1-10

choices … ^sn22-52-1-11

consciousness. Truly see the impermanence of consciousness. ^sn22-52-1-15

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with consciousness. ^sn22-52-1-16

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn22-52-1-17

When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed.” ^sn22-52-1-18

### Involvement (Linked Discourses 22.53) ^sn22-53-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-53-1-1

“Mendicants, if you get involved, you’re not free. If you’re not involved, you’re free. ^sn22-53-1-2

As long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with form, supported by form, established on form. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-53-1-3

Or consciousness would get involved with feeling … ^sn22-53-1-4

Or consciousness would get involved with perception … ^sn22-53-1-5

Or as long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with choices, supported by choices, established on choices. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-53-1-6

Mendicants, suppose you say: ^sn22-53-2-1

‘Apart from form, feeling, perception, and choices, I will describe the coming and going of consciousness, its passing away and reappearing, its growth, increase, and maturity.’ That is not possible. ^sn22-53-2-2

If a mendicant has given up greed for the form element, ^sn22-53-3-1

the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-53-3-2

If a mendicant has given up greed for the feeling element … ^sn22-53-3-3

perception element … ^sn22-53-3-4

choices element … ^sn22-53-3-5

consciousness element, ^sn22-53-3-6

the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-53-3-7

Since that consciousness does not become established and does not grow, not making choices, it is freed. ^sn22-53-3-8

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn22-53-3-9

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-53-3-10

### Seeds of Plants (Linked Discourses 22.54) ^sn22-54-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-54-1-1

“Mendicants, there are five kinds of plants. ^sn22-54-1-2

What five? ^sn22-54-1-3

Plants propagated from roots, trunks, cuttings, or joints; and those from regular seeds are the fifth. ^sn22-54-1-4

Suppose these five kinds of plants were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. But there’s no earth or water. ^sn22-54-1-5

Then would these five kinds of plants reach growth, increase, and maturity?” ^sn22-54-1-6

“No, sir.” ^sn22-54-1-7

“Suppose these five kinds of plants were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. And there is earth and water. ^sn22-54-1-8

Then would these five kinds of plants reach growth, increase, and maturity?” ^sn22-54-1-9

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-54-1-10

“The four grounds for consciousness should be seen as like the earth element. ^sn22-54-1-11

Relishing and greed should be seen as like the water element. ^sn22-54-1-12

Consciousness with its fuel should be seen as like the five kinds of plants. ^sn22-54-1-13

As long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with form, supported by form, established on form. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-54-2-1

Or consciousness would get involved with feeling … ^sn22-54-2-2

Or consciousness would get involved with perception … ^sn22-54-2-3

Or as long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with choices, supported by choices, established on choices. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-54-2-4

Mendicants, suppose you say: ^sn22-54-3-1

‘Apart from form, feeling, perception, and choices, I will describe the coming and going of consciousness, its passing away and reappearing, its growth, increase, and maturity.’ That is not possible. ^sn22-54-3-2

If a mendicant has given up greed for the form element, ^sn22-54-4-1

the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-54-4-2

If a mendicant has given up greed for the feeling element … ^sn22-54-4-3

perception element … ^sn22-54-4-4

choices element … ^sn22-54-4-5

consciousness element, ^sn22-54-4-6

the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-54-4-7

Since that consciousness does not become established and does not grow, not making choices, it is freed. ^sn22-54-4-8

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn22-54-4-9

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended … there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-54-4-10

### An Inspired Saying (Linked Discourses 22.55) ^sn22-55-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-55-1-1

There the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment: ^sn22-55-1-2

“‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^sn22-55-1-3

A mendicant who makes such a resolution can cut off the lower fetters.” ^sn22-55-1-4

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha, ^sn22-55-1-5

“But sir, how ^sn22-55-1-6

can a mendicant who makes such a resolution cut off the lower fetters?” ^sn22-55-1-7

“Mendicant, take an unlearned ordinary person who has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in their teaching. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in their teaching. ^sn22-55-2-1

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-55-2-2

They regard feeling … ^sn22-55-2-3

perception … ^sn22-55-2-4

choices … ^sn22-55-2-5

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-55-2-6

They don’t truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-55-3-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-55-3-2

perception … ^sn22-55-3-3

choices … ^sn22-55-3-4

consciousness—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-55-3-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-55-4-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-55-4-2

perception … ^sn22-55-4-3

choices … ^sn22-55-4-4

consciousness—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-55-4-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-55-5-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-55-5-2

perception … ^sn22-55-5-3

choices … ^sn22-55-5-4

consciousness—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-55-5-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-55-6-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-55-6-2

perception … ^sn22-55-6-3

choices … ^sn22-55-6-4

consciousness—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-55-6-5

They don’t truly understand that form will vanish. ^sn22-55-6-6

They don’t truly understand that feeling … ^sn22-55-6-7

perception … ^sn22-55-6-8

choices … ^sn22-55-6-9

consciousness will vanish. ^sn22-55-6-10

But a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-55-7-1

They don’t regard form as self … ^sn22-55-7-2

They don’t regard feeling … ^sn22-55-7-3

perception … ^sn22-55-7-4

choices … ^sn22-55-7-5

consciousness as self. ^sn22-55-7-6

They truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-55-8-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-55-8-2

perception … ^sn22-55-8-3

choices … ^sn22-55-8-4

consciousness—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-55-8-5

They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-55-8-6

They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-55-8-7

They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-55-8-8

They truly understand that form will vanish. ^sn22-55-8-9

They truly understand that feeling … ^sn22-55-8-10

perception … ^sn22-55-8-11

choices … ^sn22-55-8-12

consciousness will vanish. ^sn22-55-8-13

It’s because of the vanishing of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that a mendicant who makes such a resolution— ^sn22-55-9-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine’— ^sn22-55-9-2

can cut off the lower fetters.” ^sn22-55-9-3

“Sir, you say that a mendicant who makes such a resolution can cut off the lower fetters. ^sn22-55-9-4

But how are they to know and see in order to end the defilements without delay?” ^sn22-55-10-1

“Mendicant, an unlearned ordinary person worries about things that aren’t a worry. ^sn22-55-10-2

For an unlearned ordinary person worries: ^sn22-55-10-3

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^sn22-55-10-4

A learned noble disciple doesn’t worry about things that aren’t a worry. ^sn22-55-11-1

For a learned noble disciple doesn’t worry: ^sn22-55-11-2

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^sn22-55-11-3

As long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with form, supported by form, established on form. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-55-11-4

Or consciousness would get involved with feeling … ^sn22-55-11-5

Or consciousness would get involved with perception … ^sn22-55-11-6

Or consciousness would get involved with choices, supported by choices, grounded on choices. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature. ^sn22-55-11-7

Suppose, mendicant, you were to say: ^sn22-55-12-1

‘Apart from form, feeling, perception, and choices, I will describe the coming and going of consciousness, its passing away and reappearing, its growth, increase, and maturity.’ That is not possible. ^sn22-55-12-2

If a mendicant has given up greed for the form element, the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-55-13-1

If a mendicant has given up greed for the feeling element … ^sn22-55-13-2

perception element … ^sn22-55-13-3

choices element … ^sn22-55-13-4

consciousness element, the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness. ^sn22-55-13-5

Since that consciousness does not become established and does not grow, not making choices, it is freed. ^sn22-55-13-6

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn22-55-13-7

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended … there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-55-13-8

The ending of the defilements is for one who knows and sees this.” ^sn22-55-13-9

### Rounds of the Grasping Aggregates (Linked Discourses 22.56) ^sn22-56-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-56-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-56-1-2

What five? ^sn22-56-1-3

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-56-1-4

As long as I didn’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates in four rounds, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn22-56-1-5

But when I did truly understand these five grasping aggregates in four rounds, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn22-56-1-6

And how are there four rounds? ^sn22-56-2-1

I directly knew form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-56-2-2

I directly knew feeling … ^sn22-56-2-3

perception … ^sn22-56-2-4

choices … ^sn22-56-2-5

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-56-2-6

And what is form? ^sn22-56-3-1

The four principal states, and form derived from the four principal states. ^sn22-56-3-2

This is called form. ^sn22-56-3-3

Form originates from fuel. ^sn22-56-3-4

When fuel ceases, form ceases. ^sn22-56-3-5

The practice that leads to the cessation of form is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-56-3-6

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-56-3-7

Whatever ascetics and brahmins have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form: they are practicing well. ^sn22-56-4-1

Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training. ^sn22-56-4-2

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed. ^sn22-56-5-1

Those who are well freed are consummate ones. ^sn22-56-5-2

For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found. ^sn22-56-5-3

And what is feeling? ^sn22-56-6-1

There are these six classes of feeling: ^sn22-56-6-2

feeling born of contact through the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn22-56-6-3

This is called feeling. ^sn22-56-6-4

Feeling originates from contact. ^sn22-56-6-5

When contact ceases, feeling ceases. ^sn22-56-6-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path … ^sn22-56-6-7

And what is perception? ^sn22-56-9-1

There are these six classes of perception: ^sn22-56-9-2

perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^sn22-56-9-3

This is called perception. ^sn22-56-9-4

Perception originates from contact. ^sn22-56-9-5

When contact ceases, perception ceases. ^sn22-56-9-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path … ^sn22-56-9-7

And what are choices? ^sn22-56-10-1

There are these six classes of intention: ^sn22-56-10-2

intention regarding sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^sn22-56-10-3

These are called choices. ^sn22-56-10-4

Choices originate from contact. ^sn22-56-10-5

When contact ceases, choices cease. ^sn22-56-10-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path … ^sn22-56-10-7

And what is consciousness? ^sn22-56-13-1

There are these six classes of consciousness: ^sn22-56-13-2

eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind consciousness. ^sn22-56-13-3

This is called consciousness. ^sn22-56-13-4

Consciousness originates from name and form. ^sn22-56-13-5

When name and form cease, consciousness ceases. ^sn22-56-13-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of consciousness is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-56-13-7

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-56-13-8

Whatever ascetics and brahmins have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness: they are practicing well. ^sn22-56-14-1

Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training. ^sn22-56-14-2

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed. ^sn22-56-15-1

Those who are well freed are consummate ones. ^sn22-56-15-2

For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.” ^sn22-56-15-3

### Seven Cases (Linked Discourses 22.57) ^sn22-57-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-57-1-1

“Mendicants, in this teaching and training a mendicant who is skilled in seven cases and who examines in three ways is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person. ^sn22-57-1-2

And how is a mendicant skilled in seven cases? ^sn22-57-1-3

It’s when a mendicant understands form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-57-1-4

They understand form’s gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn22-57-1-5

They understand feeling … ^sn22-57-1-6

perception … ^sn22-57-1-7

choices … ^sn22-57-1-8

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-57-1-9

They understand consciousness’s gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn22-57-1-10

And what is form? ^sn22-57-2-1

The four principal states, and form derived from the four principal states. ^sn22-57-2-2

This is called form. ^sn22-57-2-3

Form originates from fuel. ^sn22-57-2-4

When fuel ceases, form ceases. ^sn22-57-2-5

The practice that leads to the cessation of form is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-57-2-6

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-57-2-7

The pleasure and happiness that arise from form: this is its gratification. ^sn22-57-3-1

That form is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-57-3-3

Removing and giving up desire and greed for form: this is its escape. ^sn22-57-3-5

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation; ^sn22-57-4-1

its gratification, drawback, and escape—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form: they are practicing well. ^sn22-57-4-2

Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training. ^sn22-57-4-3

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation; ^sn22-57-5-1

its gratification, drawback, and escape—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed. ^sn22-57-5-2

Those who are well freed are consummate ones. ^sn22-57-5-3

For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found. ^sn22-57-5-4

And what is feeling? ^sn22-57-6-1

There are these six classes of feeling: ^sn22-57-6-2

feeling born of eye contact … ^sn22-57-6-3

feeling born of mind contact. ^sn22-57-6-4

This is called feeling. ^sn22-57-6-5

Feeling originates from contact. ^sn22-57-6-6

When contact ceases, feeling ceases. ^sn22-57-6-7

The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-57-6-8

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-57-6-9

The pleasure and happiness that arise from feeling: this is its gratification. ^sn22-57-7-1

That feeling is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-57-7-3

Removing and giving up desire and greed for feeling: this is its escape. … ^sn22-57-7-5

And what is perception? ^sn22-57-10-1

There are these six classes of perception: ^sn22-57-10-2

perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^sn22-57-10-3

This is called perception. ^sn22-57-10-4

Perception originates from contact. ^sn22-57-10-5

When contact ceases, perception ceases. ^sn22-57-10-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path … ^sn22-57-10-7

And what are choices? ^sn22-57-11-1

There are these six classes of intention: ^sn22-57-11-2

intention regarding sights … ^sn22-57-11-3

intention regarding ideas. ^sn22-57-11-4

These are called choices. ^sn22-57-11-5

Choices originate from contact. ^sn22-57-11-6

When contact ceases, choices cease. ^sn22-57-11-7

The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path … ^sn22-57-11-8

And what is consciousness? ^sn22-57-14-1

There are these six classes of consciousness: ^sn22-57-14-2

eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind consciousness. ^sn22-57-14-3

This is called consciousness. ^sn22-57-14-4

Consciousness originates from name and form. ^sn22-57-14-5

When name and form cease, consciousness ceases. ^sn22-57-14-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of consciousness is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-57-14-7

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-57-14-8

The pleasure and happiness that arise from consciousness: this is its gratification. ^sn22-57-15-1

That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-57-15-3

Removing and giving up desire and greed for consciousness: this is its escape. ^sn22-57-15-5

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation; ^sn22-57-16-1

its gratification, drawback, and escape—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness: they are practicing well. ^sn22-57-16-2

Those who practice well have a footing  in this teaching and training. ^sn22-57-16-3

Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation; ^sn22-57-17-1

its gratification, drawback, and escape—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed. ^sn22-57-17-2

Those who are well freed are consummate ones. ^sn22-57-17-3

For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found. ^sn22-57-17-4

That’s how a mendicant is skilled in seven cases. ^sn22-57-17-5

And how does a mendicant examine in three ways? ^sn22-57-18-1

It’s when a mendicant examines by way of the elements, sense fields, and dependent origination. ^sn22-57-18-2

That’s how a mendicant examines in three ways. ^sn22-57-18-3

In this teaching and training, a mendicant who is skilled in seven cases and who examines in three ways is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person.” ^sn22-57-18-4

### The Fully Awakened Buddha (Linked Discourses 22.58) ^sn22-58-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-58-1-1

“Mendicants, a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha is freed by not grasping, due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form. They’re called a fully awakened Buddha. ^sn22-58-1-2

A mendicant freed by wisdom is also freed by not grasping, due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form. They’re called a mendicant freed by wisdom. ^sn22-58-1-3

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha is freed by not grasping, due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding feeling … ^sn22-58-2-1

perception … ^sn22-58-3-1

choices … ^sn22-58-3-2

consciousness. They’re called a fully awakened Buddha. ^sn22-58-3-3

A mendicant freed by wisdom is also freed by not grasping, due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness. They’re called a mendicant freed by wisdom. ^sn22-58-3-4

What, then, is the difference between a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, and a mendicant freed by wisdom?” ^sn22-58-4-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn22-58-4-2

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn22-58-4-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn22-58-4-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn22-58-4-5

“The Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha gave rise to the unarisen path, gave birth to the unborn path, and explained the unexplained path. He is the knower of the path, the discoverer of the path, the expert on the path. ^sn22-58-5-1

And now the disciples live following the path; they acquire it later. ^sn22-58-5-2

This is the difference between a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, and a mendicant freed by wisdom.” ^sn22-58-5-3

### The Characteristic of Not-Self (Linked Discourses 22.59) ^sn22-59-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn22-59-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the group of five mendicants: ^sn22-59-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^sn22-59-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn22-59-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn22-59-1-5

“Mendicants, form is not-self. ^sn22-59-2-1

For if form were self, it wouldn’t lead to affliction. And you could compel form: ^sn22-59-2-2

‘May my form be like this! May it not be like that!’ ^sn22-59-2-3

But because form is not-self, it leads to affliction. And you can’t compel form: ^sn22-59-2-4

‘May my form be like this! May it not be like that!’ ^sn22-59-2-5

Feeling is not-self … ^sn22-59-3-1

Perception is not-self … ^sn22-59-4-1

Choices are not-self … ^sn22-59-4-2

Consciousness is not-self. ^sn22-59-5-1

For if consciousness were self, it wouldn’t lead to affliction. And you could compel consciousness: ^sn22-59-5-2

‘May my consciousness be like this! May it not be like that!’ ^sn22-59-5-3

But because consciousness is not-self, it leads to affliction. And you can’t compel consciousness: ^sn22-59-5-4

‘May my consciousness be like this! May it not be like that!’ ^sn22-59-5-5

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-59-6-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-59-6-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-59-6-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-59-6-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-59-6-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-8

“Is feeling permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-59-6-9

“Is perception permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-59-6-10

“Are choices permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-59-6-11

“Is consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-59-6-12

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-13

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-59-6-14

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-15

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-59-6-16

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-59-6-17

“No, sir.” ^sn22-59-6-18

“So you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-59-7-1

Any kind of feeling at all … ^sn22-59-8-1

Any kind of perception at all … ^sn22-59-9-1

Any kind of choices at all … ^sn22-59-9-2

You should truly see any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-59-10-1

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-59-11-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-59-11-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-59-11-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-59-12-1

Satisfied, the group of five mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^sn22-59-12-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the minds of the group of five mendicants were freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-59-12-3

### With Mahāli (Linked Discourses 22.60) ^sn22-60-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-60-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn22-60-1-2

Then Mahāli the Licchavi went up to the Buddha … and said to him: ^sn22-60-1-3

“Sir, Pūraṇa Kassapa says this: ^sn22-60-2-1

‘There is no cause or reason for the corruption of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-2-2

Sentient beings are corrupted without cause or reason. ^sn22-60-2-3

There’s no cause or reason for the purification of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-2-4

Sentient beings are purified without cause or reason.’ ^sn22-60-2-5

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn22-60-2-6

“Mahāli, there is a cause and reason for the corruption of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-3-1

Sentient beings are corrupted with cause and reason. ^sn22-60-3-2

There is a cause and reason for the purification of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-3-3

Sentient beings are purified with cause and reason.” ^sn22-60-3-4

“But sir, what is the cause and reason for the corruption of sentient beings? ^sn22-60-4-1

How are sentient beings corrupted with cause and reason?” ^sn22-60-4-2

“Mahāli, if form were exclusively painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn22-60-5-1

But because form is pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn22-60-5-2

Since they are aroused by it, they’re caught up in it, and so they become corrupted. ^sn22-60-5-3

This is a cause and reason for the corruption of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-5-4

This is how sentient beings are corrupted with cause and reason. ^sn22-60-5-5

If feeling … ^sn22-60-6-1

perception … ^sn22-60-7-1

choices … ^sn22-60-7-2

consciousness were exclusively painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn22-60-8-1

But because consciousness is pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn22-60-8-2

Since they are aroused by it, they’re caught up in it, and so they become corrupted. ^sn22-60-8-3

This is a cause and reason for the corruption of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-8-4

This is how sentient beings are corrupted with cause and reason.” ^sn22-60-8-5

“But sir, what is the cause and reason for the purification of sentient beings? ^sn22-60-9-1

How are sentient beings purified with cause and reason?” ^sn22-60-9-2

“Mahāli, if form was perfectly pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-60-9-3

But because form is painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-60-9-4

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they are purified. ^sn22-60-9-5

This is a cause and reason for the purification of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-9-6

This is how sentient beings are purified with cause and reason. ^sn22-60-9-7

If feeling … ^sn22-60-10-1

perception … ^sn22-60-10-2

choices … ^sn22-60-10-3

consciousness was perfectly pleasurable—soaked and steeped in pleasure and not steeped in pain—sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-60-10-4

But because consciousness is painful—soaked and steeped in pain and not steeped in pleasure—sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn22-60-10-5

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they are purified. ^sn22-60-10-6

This is a cause and reason for the purification of sentient beings. ^sn22-60-10-7

This is how sentient beings are purified with cause and reason.” ^sn22-60-10-8

### Burning (Linked Discourses 22.61) ^sn22-61-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-61-1-1

“Mendicants, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are burning. ^sn22-61-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-61-1-3

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-61-1-4

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-61-1-5

### The Scope of Definition (Linked Discourses 22.62) ^sn22-62-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-62-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these three scopes of definition, labeling, and description. They’re uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now, nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^sn22-62-1-2

What three? ^sn22-62-1-3

When form has passed, ceased, and perished, its designation, label, and description is ‘was’. ^sn22-62-1-4

It’s not ‘is’ or ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-1-5

When feeling … ^sn22-62-2-1

perception … ^sn22-62-3-1

choices … ^sn22-62-3-2

consciousness has passed, ceased, and perished, its designation, label, and description is ‘was’. ^sn22-62-4-1

It’s not ‘is’ or ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-4-2

When form is not yet born, and has not yet appeared, its designation, label, and description is ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-5-1

It’s not ‘is’ or ‘was’. ^sn22-62-5-2

When feeling … ^sn22-62-6-1

perception … ^sn22-62-7-1

choices … ^sn22-62-7-2

consciousness is not yet born, and has not yet appeared, its designation, label, and description is ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-8-1

It’s not ‘is’ or ‘was’. ^sn22-62-8-2

When form has been born, and has appeared, its designation, label, and description is ‘is’. ^sn22-62-9-1

It’s not ‘was’ or ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-9-2

When feeling … ^sn22-62-10-1

perception … ^sn22-62-11-1

choices … ^sn22-62-11-2

consciousness has been born, and has appeared, its designation, label, and description is ‘is’. ^sn22-62-12-1

It’s not ‘was’ or ‘will be’. ^sn22-62-12-2

These are the three scopes of definition, labeling, and description. They’re uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now, nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^sn22-62-13-1

Even those those who were loud and bold advocates of the doctrines of no-cause, inaction, and nihilism, didn’t imagine that these three scopes of definition should be criticized or rejected. ^sn22-62-13-2

Why is that? ^sn22-62-13-3

For fear of being blamed, attacked, provoked, and faulted.” ^sn22-62-13-4

### When You Grasp (Linked Discourses 22.63) ^sn22-63-0

So I have heard. ^sn22-63-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn22-63-1-2

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn22-63-1-3

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn22-63-1-4

“When you grasp, mendicant, you’re bound by Māra. ^sn22-63-1-5

Not grasping, you’re free from the Wicked One.” ^sn22-63-1-6

“Understood, Blessed One! Understood, Holy One!” ^sn22-63-1-7

“But how do you see the detailed meaning of my brief statement?” ^sn22-63-2-1

“Sir, when you grasp form you’re bound by Māra. ^sn22-63-2-2

Not grasping, you’re free from the Wicked One. ^sn22-63-2-3

When you grasp feeling … ^sn22-63-2-4

perception … ^sn22-63-2-6

choices … ^sn22-63-2-7

consciousness, you’re bound by Māra. ^sn22-63-2-8

Not grasping, you’re free from the Wicked One. ^sn22-63-2-9

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^sn22-63-2-10

“Good, good, mendicant! ^sn22-63-3-1

It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this. ^sn22-63-3-2

When you grasp form you’re bound by Māra. ^sn22-63-3-3

Not grasping, you’re free from the Wicked One. ^sn22-63-3-4

When you grasp feeling … ^sn22-63-3-5

perception … ^sn22-63-3-6

choices … ^sn22-63-3-7

consciousness, you’re bound by Māra. ^sn22-63-3-8

Not grasping, you’re free from the Wicked One. ^sn22-63-3-9

This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” ^sn22-63-3-10

And then that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn22-63-4-1

Then that mendicant, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn22-63-4-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn22-63-4-3

And that mendicant became one of the perfected. ^sn22-63-4-4

### Impermanence (Linked Discourses 22.66) ^sn22-66-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-66-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn22-66-1-2

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn22-66-1-3

“Mendicant, give up desire for whatever’s impermanent.” ^sn22-66-1-4

“Understood, Blessed One! Understood, Holy One!” ^sn22-66-1-5

“But how do you see the detailed meaning of my brief statement?” ^sn22-66-2-1

“Sir, form is impermanent; I should give up desire for it. ^sn22-66-2-2

Feeling … ^sn22-66-2-3

Perception … ^sn22-66-2-4

Choices … ^sn22-66-2-5

Consciousness is impermanent; I should give up desire for it. ^sn22-66-2-6

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^sn22-66-2-7

### With Rādha (Linked Discourses 22.71) ^sn22-71-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-71-1-1

Then Venerable Rādha went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn22-71-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs?” ^sn22-71-1-3

“Rādha, one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-71-1-4

One truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn22-71-1-5

perception … ^sn22-71-1-6

choices … ^sn22-71-1-7

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-71-1-8

That’s how to know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs.” … ^sn22-71-1-9

And Venerable Rādha became one of the perfected. ^sn22-71-1-10

### With Surādha (Linked Discourses 22.72) ^sn22-72-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-72-1-1

Then Venerable Surādha said to the Buddha: ^sn22-72-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed?” ^sn22-72-1-3

“Surādha, one is freed by not grasping having truly seen any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-72-1-4

One is freed by not grasping having truly seen any kind of feeling … ^sn22-72-1-5

perception … ^sn22-72-1-6

choices … ^sn22-72-1-7

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-72-1-8

That’s how to know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed.” … ^sn22-72-1-12

And Venerable Surādha became one of the perfected. ^sn22-72-1-13

### Gratification (Linked Discourses 22.73) ^sn22-73-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-73-1-1

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t truly understand the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-73-1-2

feeling, ^sn22-73-1-3

perception, ^sn22-73-1-4

choices, ^sn22-73-1-5

and consciousness. ^sn22-73-1-6
