A learned noble disciple does truly understand the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-73-1-7

feeling, ^sn22-73-1-8

perception, ^sn22-73-1-9

choices, ^sn22-73-1-10

and consciousness.” ^sn22-73-1-11

### Origin (Linked Discourses 22.74) ^sn22-74-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-74-1-1

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t truly understand the origin, the disappearance, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-74-1-2

feeling, ^sn22-74-1-3

perception, ^sn22-74-1-4

choices, ^sn22-74-1-5

and consciousness. ^sn22-74-1-6

A learned noble disciple does truly understand the origin, the disappearance, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-74-1-7

feeling, ^sn22-74-1-8

perception, ^sn22-74-1-9

choices, ^sn22-74-1-10

and consciousness.” ^sn22-74-1-11

### Origin (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.75) ^sn22-75-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-75-1-1

“Mendicants, a learned noble disciple truly understands the origin, the disappearance, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-75-1-2

feeling, ^sn22-75-1-3

perception, ^sn22-75-1-4

choices, ^sn22-75-1-5

and consciousness.” ^sn22-75-1-6

### The Perfected Ones (Linked Discourses 22.76) ^sn22-76-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-76-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-76-1-2

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-76-1-3

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-76-1-4

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-76-1-5

Feeling … ^sn22-76-1-6

Perception … ^sn22-76-1-7

Choices … ^sn22-76-1-8

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-76-1-9

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-76-1-10

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-76-1-11

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-76-1-12

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-76-2-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-76-2-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-76-2-3

As far as there are abodes of sentient beings, even up until the pinnacle of existence, the perfected ones are the foremost and the best.” ^sn22-76-2-4

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-76-3-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn22-76-3-2

“Oh! How happy are the perfected ones! ^sn22-76-4-1

Craving is not found in them, ^sn22-76-4-2

the conceit ‘I am’ is cut off, ^sn22-76-4-3

and the net of delusion is shattered. ^sn22-76-4-4

They’ve attained imperturbability, ^sn22-76-5-1

their minds are unclouded, ^sn22-76-5-2

nothing in the world clings to them, ^sn22-76-5-3

manifesting divinity, undefiled. ^sn22-76-5-4

Completely understanding the five aggregates, ^sn22-76-6-1

their domain is the seven good qualities. ^sn22-76-6-2

Those true persons are praiseworthy, ^sn22-76-6-3

the Buddha’s true-born sons. ^sn22-76-6-4

Endowed with the seven gems, ^sn22-76-7-1

and trained in the three trainings, ^sn22-76-7-2

the great heroes live on, ^sn22-76-7-3

with fear and dread given up. ^sn22-76-7-4

Endowed with ten factors, ^sn22-76-8-1

those giants are immersed in samādhi. ^sn22-76-8-2

They are the best in the world, ^sn22-76-8-3

craving is not found in them. ^sn22-76-8-4

The adept’s knowledge has arisen: ^sn22-76-9-1

‘This bag of bones is my last.’ ^sn22-76-9-2

They are independent of others ^sn22-76-9-3

in the core of the spiritual path. ^sn22-76-9-4

They do not waver due to the discriminations, ^sn22-76-10-1

they’re freed from future lives. ^sn22-76-10-2

They’ve reached the level of the tamed, ^sn22-76-10-3

in the world, they’re the winners. ^sn22-76-10-4

Above, below, all round, ^sn22-76-11-1

relishing is not found in them. ^sn22-76-11-2

They roar their lion’s roar: ^sn22-76-11-3

‘The awakened are supreme in the world!’” ^sn22-76-11-4

### The Perfected Ones (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.77) ^sn22-77-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-77-1-1

“Mendicants, form is impermanent. ^sn22-77-1-2

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-77-1-3

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn22-77-1-4

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-77-1-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-77-2-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-77-2-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-77-2-3

As far as there are abodes of sentient beings, even up until the pinnacle of existence, the perfected ones are the foremost and the best.” ^sn22-77-2-4

### The Lion (Linked Discourses 22.78) ^sn22-78-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-78-1-1

“Mendicants, towards evening the lion, king of beasts, emerges from his den, yawns, surveys the four quarters, and roars his lion’s roar three times. Then he sets out on the hunt. ^sn22-78-1-2

And whatever animals hear the roar of the lion, king of beasts, are typically filled with fear, awe, and terror. They return to their lairs, be they in a hole, the water, or a wood; and the birds take to the air. ^sn22-78-1-3

Even the royal elephants, bound with strong harness in the villages, towns, and capital cities, break apart their bonds, and urinate and defecate in terror as they flee here and there. ^sn22-78-1-4

That’s how powerful is the lion, king of beasts, over animals, how illustrious and mighty. ^sn22-78-1-5

In the same way, when a Realized One arises in the world—perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed—he teaches the Dhamma: ^sn22-78-2-1

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the disappearance of form. ^sn22-78-2-2

Such is feeling … ^sn22-78-2-3

Such is perception … ^sn22-78-2-4

Such are choices … ^sn22-78-2-5

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.’ ^sn22-78-2-6

Now, there are gods who are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy, living for ages in their divine palaces. When they hear this teaching by the Realized One, they’re typically filled with fear, awe, and terror. ^sn22-78-2-7

‘Oh no! It turns out we’re impermanent, though we thought we were permanent! ^sn22-78-2-8

It turns out we don’t last, though we thought we were everlasting! ^sn22-78-2-9

It turns out we’re short-lived, though we thought we were eternal! ^sn22-78-2-10

It turns out that we’re impermanent, not lasting, short-lived, and included within substantial reality.’ ^sn22-78-2-11

That’s how powerful is the Realized One in the world with its gods, how illustrious and mighty.” ^sn22-78-2-12

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-78-2-13

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn22-78-2-14

“The Buddha, the unrivaled teacher ^sn22-78-3-1

in all the world with its gods, ^sn22-78-3-2

rolls forth the Wheel of Dhamma ^sn22-78-3-3

from his own insight: ^sn22-78-3-4

substantial reality, its cessation, ^sn22-78-4-1

the origin of substantial reality, ^sn22-78-4-2

and the noble eightfold path ^sn22-78-4-3

that leads to the stilling of suffering. ^sn22-78-4-4

And then the long-lived gods, ^sn22-78-5-1

so beautiful and glorious, ^sn22-78-5-2

are afraid and full of terror, ^sn22-78-5-3

like the other beasts when they hear a lion. ^sn22-78-5-4

‘We haven’t passed beyond substantial reality! ^sn22-78-6-1

It turns out we’re impermanent!’ ^sn22-78-6-2

So they say when they hear the word ^sn22-78-6-3

of the perfected one, free and unaffected.” ^sn22-78-6-4

### Bitable (Linked Discourses 22.79) ^sn22-79-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-79-1-1

“Mendicants, whatever ascetics and brahmins recollect many kinds of past lives, all recollect the five grasping aggregates, or one of them. ^sn22-79-1-2

What five? ^sn22-79-1-3

‘I had such form in the past.’ ^sn22-79-1-4

Recollecting thus, it’s only form that they recollect. ^sn22-79-1-5

‘I had such feeling … ^sn22-79-1-6

perception … ^sn22-79-1-8

choices … ^sn22-79-1-9

consciousness in the past.’ ^sn22-79-1-10

Recollecting thus, it’s only consciousness that they recollect. ^sn22-79-1-11

And why do you call it form? ^sn22-79-2-1

It’s deformed; that’s why it’s called ‘form’. ^sn22-79-2-2

Deformed by what? ^sn22-79-2-3

Deformed by cold, heat, hunger, and thirst, and deformed by the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. ^sn22-79-2-4

It’s deformed; that’s why it’s called ‘form’. ^sn22-79-2-5

And why do you call it feeling? ^sn22-79-3-1

It feels; that’s why it’s called ‘feeling’. ^sn22-79-3-2

And what does it feel? ^sn22-79-3-3

It feels pleasure, pain, and neutral. ^sn22-79-3-4

It feels; that’s why it’s called ‘feeling’. ^sn22-79-3-5

And why do you call it perception? ^sn22-79-4-1

It perceives; that’s why it’s called ‘perception’. ^sn22-79-4-2

And what does it perceive? ^sn22-79-4-3

It perceives blue, yellow, red, and white. ^sn22-79-4-4

It perceives; that’s why it’s called ‘perception’. ^sn22-79-4-5

And why do you call them choices? ^sn22-79-5-1

Choices produce conditioned phenomena; that’s why they’re called ‘choices’. ^sn22-79-5-2

And what are the conditioned phenomena that they produce? ^sn22-79-5-3

Form is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into form. Feeling is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into feeling. Perception is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into perception. Choices are conditioned phenomena; choices are what make them into choices. Consciousness is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into consciousness. ^sn22-79-5-4

Choices produce conditioned phenomena; that’s why they’re called ‘choices’. ^sn22-79-5-5

And why do you call it consciousness? ^sn22-79-6-1

It cognizes; that’s why it’s called ‘consciousness’. ^sn22-79-6-2

And what does it cognize? ^sn22-79-6-3

It cognizes sour, bitter, pungent, sweet, hot, mild, salty, and bland. ^sn22-79-6-4

It cognizes; that’s why it’s called ‘consciousness’. ^sn22-79-6-5

A learned noble disciple reflects on this: ^sn22-79-7-1

‘Currently I’m bitten by form. ^sn22-79-7-2

In the past I was also bitten by form just like now. ^sn22-79-7-3

If I were to look forward to enjoying form in the future, I’d be bitten by form in the future just as I am today.’ ^sn22-79-7-4

Reflecting like this they are not concerned with past form, ^sn22-79-7-5

they don’t look forward to enjoying future form, ^sn22-79-7-6

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form. ^sn22-79-7-7

‘Currently I’m bitten by feeling … ^sn22-79-8-1

perception … ^sn22-79-9-1

choices … ^sn22-79-9-2

consciousness. ^sn22-79-10-1

In the past I was also bitten by consciousness just like now. ^sn22-79-10-2

If I were to look forward to enjoying consciousness in the future, I’d be bitten by consciousness in the future just as I am today.’ ^sn22-79-10-3

Reflecting like this they are not concerned with past consciousness, ^sn22-79-10-5

they don’t look forward to enjoying future consciousness, ^sn22-79-10-6

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present consciousness. ^sn22-79-10-7

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-79-11-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-79-11-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-79-11-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-79-11-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-79-11-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-8

“Is feeling … ^sn22-79-11-9

perception … ^sn22-79-11-10

choices … ^sn22-79-11-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-79-11-12

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-13

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-79-11-14

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-15

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-79-11-16

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-79-11-17

“No, sir.” ^sn22-79-11-18

“So you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-79-11-19

You should truly see any kind of feeling … ^sn22-79-11-20

perception … ^sn22-79-11-21

choices … ^sn22-79-11-22

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-79-11-23

This is called a noble disciple who gets rid of and doesn’t accumulate; ^sn22-79-12-1

who gives up and doesn’t grasp; ^sn22-79-12-2

who unmeshes and doesn’t enmesh; ^sn22-79-12-3

who dissipates and doesn’t get clouded. ^sn22-79-12-4

And what things do they get rid of and not accumulate? ^sn22-79-12-5

They get rid of form and don’t accumulate it. ^sn22-79-12-6

They get rid of feeling … ^sn22-79-12-7

perception … ^sn22-79-12-8

choices … ^sn22-79-12-9

consciousness and don’t accumulate it. ^sn22-79-12-10

And what things do they give up and not grasp? ^sn22-79-12-11

They give up form and don’t grasp it. ^sn22-79-12-12

They give up feeling … ^sn22-79-12-13

perception … ^sn22-79-12-14

choices … ^sn22-79-12-15

consciousness and don’t grasp it. ^sn22-79-12-16

And what things do they unmesh and not enmesh? ^sn22-79-12-17

They unmesh form and don’t enmesh it. ^sn22-79-12-18

They unmesh feeling … ^sn22-79-12-19

perception … ^sn22-79-12-20

choices … ^sn22-79-12-21

consciousness and don’t enmesh it. ^sn22-79-12-22

And what things do they dissipate and not get clouded by? ^sn22-79-12-23

They dissipate form and don’t get clouded by it. ^sn22-79-12-24

They dissipate feeling … ^sn22-79-12-25

perception … ^sn22-79-12-26

choices … ^sn22-79-12-27

consciousness and don’t get clouded by it. ^sn22-79-12-28

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-79-13-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-79-13-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-79-13-3

This is called a mendicant who neither gets rid of nor accumulates, but remains after getting rid. They neither give up nor grasp, but remain after giving up. They neither unmesh nor enmesh, but remain after unmeshing. They neither dissipate nor get clouded, but remain after dissipating. ^sn22-79-14-1

And what things do they neither get rid of nor accumulate, but remain after getting rid of them? ^sn22-79-14-2

They neither get rid of nor accumulate form, but remain after getting rid of it. ^sn22-79-14-3

They neither get rid of nor accumulate feeling … ^sn22-79-14-4

perception … ^sn22-79-14-5

choices … ^sn22-79-14-6

consciousness, but remain after getting rid of it. ^sn22-79-14-7

And what things do they neither give up nor grasp, but remain after giving them up? ^sn22-79-14-8

They neither give up nor grasp form, but remain after giving it up. ^sn22-79-14-9

They neither give up nor grasp feeling … ^sn22-79-14-10

perception … ^sn22-79-14-11

choices … ^sn22-79-14-12

consciousness, but remain after giving it up. ^sn22-79-14-13

And what things do they neither discard nor amass, but remain after discarding them? ^sn22-79-14-14

They neither unmesh nor enmesh form, but remain after unmeshing it. ^sn22-79-14-15

They neither unmesh nor enmesh feeling … ^sn22-79-14-16

perception … ^sn22-79-14-17

choices … ^sn22-79-14-18

consciousness, but remain after unmeshing it. ^sn22-79-14-19

And what things do they neither dissipate nor get clouded by, but remain after dissipating them? ^sn22-79-14-20

They neither dissipate nor get clouded by form, but remain after dissipating it. ^sn22-79-14-21

They neither dissipate nor get clouded by feeling … ^sn22-79-14-22

perception … ^sn22-79-14-23

choices … ^sn22-79-14-24

consciousness, but remain after dissipating it. ^sn22-79-14-25

When a mendicant’s mind is freed like this, the gods together with Indra, the Divinity, and the Progenitor worship them from afar: ^sn22-79-14-26

‘Homage to you, O thoroughbred! ^sn22-79-15-1

Homage to you, supreme among men! ^sn22-79-15-2

We don’t understand ^sn22-79-15-3

the basis of your absorption.’” ^sn22-79-15-4

### Beggars (Linked Discourses 22.80) ^sn22-80-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^sn22-80-1-1

Then the Buddha, having dismissed the mendicant Saṅgha for some reason, robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Kapilavatthu for alms. ^sn22-80-1-2

He wandered for alms in Kapilavatthu. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Great Wood for the day’s meditation, ^sn22-80-1-3

plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a young wood-apple tree to meditate. ^sn22-80-1-4

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn22-80-2-1

“I have purged the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn22-80-2-2

But there are mendicants here who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training. ^sn22-80-2-3

Not seeing me they may change and fall apart. ^sn22-80-2-4

If a young calf doesn’t see its mother it may change and fall apart. … ^sn22-80-2-5

Or if young seedlings don’t get water they may change and fall apart. ^sn22-80-2-7

In the same way, there are mendicants here who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training. ^sn22-80-2-8

Not seeing me they may change and fall apart. ^sn22-80-2-9

Why don’t I support the mendicant Saṅgha now as I did in the past?” ^sn22-80-2-10

Then the divinity Sahampati knew the Buddha’s train of thought. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of the Buddha. ^sn22-80-3-1

He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said: ^sn22-80-3-2

“That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One! ^sn22-80-3-3

The Buddha has purged the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn22-80-3-4

But there are mendicants who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training. … ^sn22-80-3-5

May the Buddha be happy with the mendicant Saṅgha! ^sn22-80-3-11

May the Buddha welcome the mendicant Saṅgha! ^sn22-80-3-12

May the Buddha support the mendicant Saṅgha now as he did in the past!” ^sn22-80-3-13

The Buddha consented with silence. ^sn22-80-4-1

Then the divinity Sahampati, knowing that the Buddha had consented, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^sn22-80-4-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the Banyan Tree Monastery, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^sn22-80-5-1

Then he used his psychic power to will that the mendicants would come to him timidly, alone or in pairs. ^sn22-80-5-2

Those mendicants approached the Buddha timidly, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^sn22-80-5-3

“This is the last of lifestyles, mendicants, namely relying on alms. ^sn22-80-6-1

The world curses you: ‘You beggar, walking bowl in hand!’ ^sn22-80-6-2

Yet earnest gentlemen take it up for a good reason. ^sn22-80-6-3

Not to escape a summons by a king or a summons for a bandit, or because you were in debt or in fear, or in order to make a living. ^sn22-80-6-4

But because they’re swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re swamped by suffering, mired in suffering. ^sn22-80-6-5

And they think, ‘Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.’ ^sn22-80-6-6

That’s how this gentleman has gone forth. ^sn22-80-7-1

Yet they covet sensual pleasures; they’re infatuated, full of ill will and malicious intent. They are unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties. ^sn22-80-7-2

Suppose there was a firebrand for lighting a funeral pyre, burning at both ends, and smeared with dung in the middle. It couldn’t be used as timber either in the village or the wilderness. ^sn22-80-7-3

I say that individual is just like this. They’ve missed out on the pleasures of the lay life, and haven’t fulfilled the goal of the ascetic life. ^sn22-80-7-4

There are these three unskillful thoughts. ^sn22-80-8-1

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^sn22-80-8-2

And where do these three unskillful thoughts cease without remainder? ^sn22-80-8-3

In those who meditate with their mind firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation; or who develop signless immersion. ^sn22-80-8-4

This is quite enough motivation to develop signless immersion. ^sn22-80-8-5

When signless immersion is developed and cultivated it is very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn22-80-8-6

There are these two views. ^sn22-80-9-1

Views favoring existence and views favoring nonexistence. ^sn22-80-9-2

A learned noble disciple reflects on this: ^sn22-80-9-3

‘Is there anything in the world that I could grasp without fault?’ ^sn22-80-9-4

They understand: ^sn22-80-9-5

‘There’s nothing in the world that I could grasp without fault. ^sn22-80-9-6

For in grasping I would grasp only at form, feeling, ^sn22-80-9-7

perception, ^sn22-80-9-8

choices, or consciousness. ^sn22-80-9-9

That grasping of mine would be a condition for continued existence. ^sn22-80-9-10

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn22-80-9-11

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn22-80-9-12

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.’ ^sn22-80-9-13

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-80-10-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-80-10-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-80-10-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-80-10-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-80-10-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-80-10-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-80-10-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-80-10-8

“Is feeling … ^sn22-80-10-9

perception … ^sn22-80-10-10

choices … ^sn22-80-10-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-80-10-12

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-80-10-13

Seeing this … ^sn22-80-10-14

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-80-10-15

### At Pārileyyaka (Linked Discourses 22.81) ^sn22-81-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn22-81-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Kosambī for alms. ^sn22-81-1-2

After the meal, on his return from almsround, he set his lodgings in order himself. Taking his bowl and robe, without informing his attendants or taking leave of the mendicant Saṅgha, he set out to go wandering alone, with no companion. ^sn22-81-1-3

Then, not long after the Buddha had left, one of the mendicants went to Venerable Ānanda and told him what had happened. ^sn22-81-2-1

Ānanda said, “Reverend, when the Buddha leaves like this it means he wants to stay alone. ^sn22-81-2-3

At this time no-one should follow him.” ^sn22-81-2-4

Then the Buddha, traveling stage by stage, arrived at Pārileyyaka, ^sn22-81-3-1

where he stayed at the root of a sacred sal tree. ^sn22-81-3-2

Then several mendicants went up to Venerable Ānanda and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn22-81-3-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him, ^sn22-81-3-4

“Reverend, it’s been a long time since we’ve heard a Dhamma talk from the Buddha. ^sn22-81-3-5

We wish to hear a Dhamma talk from the Buddha.” ^sn22-81-3-6

Then Venerable Ānanda together with those mendicants went to Pārileyyaka to see the Buddha. They bowed and sat down to one side, ^sn22-81-4-1

and the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired them with a Dhamma talk. ^sn22-81-4-2

Now at that time one of the monks had the thought, ^sn22-81-4-3

“How do you know and see in order to end the defilements without delay?” ^sn22-81-4-4

Then the Buddha, knowing that monk’s train of thought, addressed the mendicants: ^sn22-81-4-5

“Mendicants, I’ve taught the Dhamma analytically. ^sn22-81-4-6

I’ve analytically taught the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, ^sn22-81-4-7

the four right efforts, ^sn22-81-4-8

the four bases of psychic power, ^sn22-81-4-9

the five faculties, ^sn22-81-4-10

the five powers, ^sn22-81-4-11

the seven awakening factors, ^sn22-81-4-12

and the noble eightfold path. ^sn22-81-4-13

That’s how I’ve taught the Dhamma analytically. ^sn22-81-4-14

Though I’ve taught the Dhamma analytically, still a certain mendicant present here has this thought: ^sn22-81-4-15

‘How do you know and see in order to end the defilements without delay?’ ^sn22-81-4-16

And how, mendicants, do you know and see in order to end the defilements without delay? ^sn22-81-5-1

Take an unlearned ordinary person who has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-81-5-2

They regard form as self. ^sn22-81-5-3

But that regarding is just a conditioned phenomenon. ^sn22-81-5-4

And what’s the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of that conditioned phenomenon? ^sn22-81-5-5

When an unlearned ordinary person is struck by feelings born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. ^sn22-81-5-6

That conditioned phenomenon is born from that. ^sn22-81-5-7

So that conditioned phenomenon is impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-5-8

And that craving, ^sn22-81-5-9

that feeling, that contact, ^sn22-81-5-10

and that ignorance are also impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-5-11

That’s how you should know and see in order to end the defilements without delay. ^sn22-81-5-12

Perhaps they don’t regard form as self, ^sn22-81-6-1

but they still regard self as possessing form. ^sn22-81-6-2

But that regarding is just a conditioned phenomenon. … ^sn22-81-6-3

Perhaps they don’t regard form as self, or self as possessing form, ^sn22-81-7-1

but they still regard form in self. ^sn22-81-7-2

But that regarding is just a conditioned phenomenon. … ^sn22-81-7-3

Perhaps they don’t regard form as self, or self as possessing form, or form in self, ^sn22-81-8-1

but they still regard self in form. ^sn22-81-8-2

But that regarding is just a conditioned phenomenon. … ^sn22-81-8-3

Perhaps they don’t regard form as self, or self as possessing form, or form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-81-9-1

But they regard feeling as self … ^sn22-81-9-2

perception as self … ^sn22-81-9-3

choices as self … ^sn22-81-9-4

consciousness as self … ^sn22-81-9-5

But that regarding is just a conditioned phenomenon. ^sn22-81-9-6

And what’s the source of that conditioned phenomenon? ^sn22-81-9-7

When an unlearned ordinary person is struck by feelings born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. ^sn22-81-9-8

That conditioned phenomenon is born from that. ^sn22-81-9-9

So that conditioned phenomenon is impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-9-10

And that craving, ^sn22-81-9-11

that feeling, ^sn22-81-9-12

that contact, ^sn22-81-9-13

and that ignorance are also impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-9-14

That’s how you should know and see in order to end the defilements without delay. ^sn22-81-9-15

Perhaps they don’t regard form ^sn22-81-10-1

or feeling ^sn22-81-10-2

or perception ^sn22-81-10-3

or choices ^sn22-81-10-4

or consciousness as self. ^sn22-81-10-5

Still, they have such a view: ^sn22-81-10-6

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable.’ ^sn22-81-10-7

But that eternalist view is just a conditioned phenomenon. ^sn22-81-10-8

And what’s the source of that conditioned phenomenon? … ^sn22-81-10-9

That’s how you should know and see in order to end the defilements without delay. ^sn22-81-10-10

Perhaps they don’t regard form ^sn22-81-11-1

or feeling ^sn22-81-11-2

or perception ^sn22-81-11-3

or choices ^sn22-81-11-4

or consciousness as self. ^sn22-81-11-5

Nor do they have such a view: ^sn22-81-11-6

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable.’ ^sn22-81-11-7

Still, they have such a view: ^sn22-81-11-8

‘I might not be, and it might not be mine. I will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^sn22-81-11-9

But that annihilationist view is just a conditioned phenomenon. ^sn22-81-11-10

And what’s the source of that conditioned phenomenon? … ^sn22-81-11-11

That’s how you should know and see in order to end the defilements without delay. ^sn22-81-11-15

Perhaps they don’t regard form or feeling ^sn22-81-12-1

or perception ^sn22-81-12-2

or choices ^sn22-81-12-3

or consciousness as self. ^sn22-81-12-4

Nor do they have such a view: ^sn22-81-12-5

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable.’ ^sn22-81-12-6

Nor do they have such a view: ^sn22-81-12-7

‘I might not be, and it might not be mine. I will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^sn22-81-12-8

Still, they have doubts and uncertainties. They’re undecided about the true teaching. ^sn22-81-12-9

That doubt and uncertainty, the indecision about the true teaching, is just a conditioned phenomenon. ^sn22-81-12-10

And what’s the source of that conditioned phenomenon? ^sn22-81-12-11

When an unlearned ordinary person is struck by feelings born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. ^sn22-81-12-12

That conditioned phenomenon is born from that. ^sn22-81-12-13

So that conditioned phenomenon is impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-12-14

And that craving, ^sn22-81-12-15

that feeling, ^sn22-81-12-16

that contact, ^sn22-81-12-17

and that ignorance are also impermanent, conditioned, and dependently originated. ^sn22-81-12-18

That’s how you should know and see in order to end the defilements without delay.” ^sn22-81-12-19

### A Full Moon Night (Linked Discourses 22.82) ^sn22-82-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery, together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^sn22-82-1-1

Now, at that time it was the sabbath—the full moon on the fifteenth day—and the Buddha was sitting in the open surrounded by the Saṅgha of monks. ^sn22-82-1-2

Then one of the mendicants rose from their seat, arranged their robe over one shoulder, raised their cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said: ^sn22-82-2-1

“Sir, I’d like to ask the Buddha about a certain point, if you’d take the time to answer.” ^sn22-82-2-2

“Well then, mendicant, take your own seat and ask what you wish.” ^sn22-82-2-3

“Yes, sir,” replied that mendicant. He took his seat and said to the Buddha: ^sn22-82-2-4

“Sir, are these the five grasping aggregates, that is: ^sn22-82-2-5

form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness?” ^sn22-82-2-6

“Yes, they are,” replied the Buddha. ^sn22-82-3-1

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he asked another question: ^sn22-82-3-3

“But sir, what is the root of these five grasping aggregates?” ^sn22-82-4-1

“These five grasping aggregates are rooted in desire.” … ^sn22-82-4-2

“But sir, is that grasping the exact same thing as the five grasping aggregates? Or is grasping one thing and the five grasping aggregates another?” ^sn22-82-4-3

“Neither. Rather, the desire and greed for them is the grasping there.” ^sn22-82-4-4

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant asked another question: ^sn22-82-4-5

“But sir, can there be different kinds of desire and greed for the five grasping aggregates?” ^sn22-82-5-1

“There can,” said the Buddha. ^sn22-82-5-2

“It’s when someone thinks: ^sn22-82-5-3

‘In the future, may I be of such form, such feeling, such perception, such choices, or such consciousness!’ ^sn22-82-5-4

That’s how there can be different kinds of desire and greed for the five grasping aggregates.” ^sn22-82-5-5

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant asked another question: ^sn22-82-5-6

“Sir, what is the scope of the term ‘aggregates’ as applied to the aggregates?” ^sn22-82-6-1

“Any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: this is called the aggregate of form. ^sn22-82-6-2

Any kind of feeling at all … ^sn22-82-6-3

Any kind of perception at all … ^sn22-82-6-4

Any kind of choices at all … ^sn22-82-6-5

Any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: this is called the aggregate of consciousness. ^sn22-82-6-6

That’s the scope of the term ‘aggregates’ as applied to the aggregates.” ^sn22-82-6-7

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant asked another question: ^sn22-82-6-8

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the aggregate of form is found? ^sn22-82-7-1

What is the cause, what is the reason why the aggregate of feeling … ^sn22-82-7-2

perception … ^sn22-82-7-3

choices … ^sn22-82-7-4

consciousness is found?” ^sn22-82-7-5

“The four principal states are the reason why the aggregate of form is found. ^sn22-82-7-6

Contact is the reason why the aggregates of feeling, ^sn22-82-7-7

perception, ^sn22-82-7-8

and choices are found. ^sn22-82-7-9

Name and form are the reasons why the aggregate of consciousness is found.” ^sn22-82-7-10

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant asked another question: ^sn22-82-7-11

“Sir, how does substantialist view come about?” ^sn22-82-8-1

“It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-82-8-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-82-8-3

They regard feeling … ^sn22-82-8-4

perception … ^sn22-82-8-5

choices … ^sn22-82-8-6

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-82-8-7

That’s how substantialist view comes about.” ^sn22-82-8-8

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant … asked another question: ^sn22-82-8-9

“But sir, how does substantialist view not come about?” ^sn22-82-9-1

“It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-82-9-2

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-82-9-3

They don’t regard feeling … ^sn22-82-9-4

perception … ^sn22-82-9-5

choices … ^sn22-82-9-6

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-82-9-7

That’s how substantialist view does not come about.” ^sn22-82-9-8

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant … asked another question: ^sn22-82-9-9

“Sir, what’s the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form, ^sn22-82-10-1

feeling, ^sn22-82-10-2

perception, ^sn22-82-10-3

choices, ^sn22-82-10-4

and consciousness?” ^sn22-82-10-5

“The pleasure and happiness that arise from form: this is its gratification. ^sn22-82-10-6

That form is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-82-10-7

Removing and giving up desire and greed for form: this is its escape. ^sn22-82-10-8

The pleasure and happiness that arise from feeling … ^sn22-82-10-9

perception … ^sn22-82-10-10

choices … ^sn22-82-10-11

consciousness: this is its gratification. ^sn22-82-10-12

That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn22-82-10-13

Removing and giving up desire and greed for consciousness: this is its escape.” ^sn22-82-10-14

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he asked another question: ^sn22-82-10-15

“Sir, how does one know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs?” ^sn22-82-11-1

“One truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-82-11-2

They truly see any kind of feeling … ^sn22-82-11-3

perception … ^sn22-82-11-4

choices … ^sn22-82-11-5

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near, <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-82-11-6

That’s how to know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs.” ^sn22-82-11-7

Now at that time one of the mendicants had the thought: ^sn22-82-12-1

“So it seems, worthy sir, that form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are not-self. ^sn22-82-12-2

Then how will the deeds done by not-self affect the self?” ^sn22-82-12-3

Then the Buddha, knowing that monk’s train of thought, addressed the mendicants: ^sn22-82-12-4

“It’s possible that some futile person here—unknowing and ignorant, their mind dominated by craving—thinks they can overstep the teacher’s instructions. They think: ^sn22-82-13-1

‘So it seems, worthy sir, that form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are not-self. ^sn22-82-13-2

Then how will the deeds done by not-self affect the self?’ ^sn22-82-13-3

Now, mendicants, you have been educated by me in questioning with regards to all these things in all such cases. ^sn22-82-13-4

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-82-14-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-82-14-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-82-14-3

“Is feeling … ^sn22-82-14-4

perception … ^sn22-82-14-5

choices … ^sn22-82-14-6

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-82-14-7

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-82-14-8

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-82-14-9

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-82-14-10

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-82-14-11

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-82-14-12

“No, sir.” ^sn22-82-14-13

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-82-14-14

Seeing this … ^sn22-82-14-15

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-82-14-16

“Two on the aggregates; exactly the same; <j>and can there be; ^sn22-82-15-1

on the term; and on the cause; ^sn22-82-15-2

two questions on substantial reality; ^sn22-82-15-3

gratification; and that with consciousness: ^sn22-82-15-4

these are the ten questions ^sn22-82-15-5

the mendicant came to ask.” ^sn22-82-15-6

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 22.83) ^sn22-83-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-83-1-1

There Ānanda addressed the mendicants: ^sn22-83-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn22-83-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn22-83-1-4

Ānanda said this: ^sn22-83-1-5

“Reverends, the venerable named Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī was very helpful to me when I was just ordained. ^sn22-83-2-1

He gave me this advice: ^sn22-83-2-2

‘Reverend Ānanda, the thought “I am” occurs because of grasping, not without grasping. ^sn22-83-2-3

Grasping what? ^sn22-83-2-4

The thought “I am” occurs because of grasping form, ^sn22-83-2-5

feeling, ^sn22-83-2-6

perception, ^sn22-83-2-7

choices, ^sn22-83-2-8

and consciousness, not without grasping. ^sn22-83-2-9

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. They’d look because of grasping, not without grasping. ^sn22-83-3-1

In the same way, the thought “I am” occurs because of grasping form, ^sn22-83-3-2

feeling, ^sn22-83-3-3

perception, ^sn22-83-3-4

choices, ^sn22-83-3-5

and consciousness, not without grasping. ^sn22-83-3-6

What do you think, Reverend Ānanda? ^sn22-83-4-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?’ ^sn22-83-4-2

‘Impermanent, reverend.’ ^sn22-83-4-3

‘Is feeling … ^sn22-83-4-4

perception … ^sn22-83-4-5

choices … ^sn22-83-4-6

consciousness permanent or impermanent?’ ^sn22-83-4-7

‘Impermanent, reverend.’ … ^sn22-83-4-8

‘So you should truly see … ^sn22-83-4-9

Seeing this … ^sn22-83-4-10

They understand: “… there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^sn22-83-4-11

Reverends, the venerable named Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī was very helpful to me when I was just ordained. ^sn22-83-4-12

He gave me this advice. ^sn22-83-4-13

And when I heard this teaching from Venerable Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī, I comprehended the teaching.” ^sn22-83-4-14

### With Tissa (Linked Discourses 22.84) ^sn22-84-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-84-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Tissa—the Buddha’s cousin on his father’s side—informed several mendicants: ^sn22-84-1-2

“Reverends, my body feels like it’s drugged. I’m disorientated, the teachings don’t spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness fill my mind. I lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, and have doubts about the teachings.” ^sn22-84-1-3

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn22-84-2-1

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^sn22-84-3-1

“Please, monk, in my name tell the mendicant Tissa that the Teacher summons him.” ^sn22-84-3-2

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to Tissa and said to him, ^sn22-84-3-3

“Reverend Tissa, the teacher summons you.” ^sn22-84-3-4

“Yes, reverend,” Tissa replied. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn22-84-3-5

“Is it really true, Tissa, that you informed several mendicants that ^sn22-84-3-6

your body feels like it’s drugged … and you have doubts about the teachings?” ^sn22-84-3-7

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-84-3-8

“What do you think, Tissa? ^sn22-84-3-9

If you’re not rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes, will it give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn22-84-3-10

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-84-3-11

“Good, good, Tissa! ^sn22-84-4-1

That’s how it is, Tissa, ^sn22-84-4-2

when you’re not rid of greed for form. ^sn22-84-4-3

If you’re not rid of greed for feeling … ^sn22-84-4-4

perception … ^sn22-84-4-5

choices … ^sn22-84-4-6

consciousness, when that consciousness decays and perishes, will it give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn22-84-5-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-84-5-4

“Good, good, Tissa! ^sn22-84-6-1

That’s how it is, Tissa, ^sn22-84-6-2

when you’re not rid of greed for consciousness. ^sn22-84-6-3

What do you think, Tissa? ^sn22-84-6-4

If you are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes, will it give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn22-84-6-5

“No, sir.” ^sn22-84-6-6

“Good, good, Tissa! ^sn22-84-7-1

That’s how it is, Tissa, ^sn22-84-7-2

when you are rid of greed for form … ^sn22-84-7-3

feeling … ^sn22-84-7-4

perception … ^sn22-84-7-5

choices … ^sn22-84-7-6

consciousness. ^sn22-84-8-3

What do you think, Tissa? ^sn22-84-8-4

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-84-8-5

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-84-8-6

“Is feeling … ^sn22-84-8-7

perception … ^sn22-84-8-8

choices … ^sn22-84-8-9

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-84-8-10

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-84-8-11

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-84-8-12

Seeing this … ^sn22-84-8-13

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-84-8-14

Suppose, Tissa, there were two individuals. ^sn22-84-9-1

One was not skilled in the path, the other was. ^sn22-84-9-2

The one not skilled in the path would question the one skilled in the path, ^sn22-84-9-3

who would reply: ^sn22-84-9-4

‘Come, my man, this is the path. ^sn22-84-9-5

Go down it an hour, ^sn22-84-9-6

and you’ll see a fork in the road. Ignore the left, and take the right-hand path. ^sn22-84-9-7

Go an hour more, ^sn22-84-9-8

and you’ll see a dense forest grove. ^sn22-84-9-9

Go an hour more, ^sn22-84-9-10

and you’ll see an expanse of low-lying marshes. ^sn22-84-9-11

Go an hour more, ^sn22-84-9-12

and you’ll see a large, steep cliff. ^sn22-84-9-13

Go an hour more, ^sn22-84-9-14

and you’ll see level, cleared parkland.’ ^sn22-84-9-15

I’ve made up this simile to make a point. ^sn22-84-10-1

And this is what it means. ^sn22-84-10-2

‘A person who is not skilled in the path’ is a term for an ordinary unlearned person. ^sn22-84-10-3

‘A person who is skilled in the path’ is a term for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^sn22-84-10-4

‘A fork in the road’ is a term for doubt. ^sn22-84-10-5

‘The left-hand path’ is a term for the wrong eightfold path, that is, ^sn22-84-10-6

wrong view … wrong immersion. ^sn22-84-10-7

‘The right-hand path’ is a term for the noble eightfold path, that is, ^sn22-84-10-8

right view … right immersion. ^sn22-84-10-9

‘A dense forest grove’ is a term for ignorance. ^sn22-84-10-10

‘An expanse of low-lying marshes’ is a term for sensual pleasures. ^sn22-84-10-11

‘A large, steep cliff’ is a term for anger and distress. ^sn22-84-10-12

‘Level, cleared parkland’ is a term for extinguishment. ^sn22-84-10-13

Rejoice, Tissa, rejoice! ^sn22-84-10-14

I am here with advice, with support, with instruction.” ^sn22-84-10-15

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-84-11-1

Satisfied, Venerable Tissa approved what the Buddha said. ^sn22-84-11-2

### With Yamaka (Linked Discourses 22.85) ^sn22-85-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn22-85-1-1

Now at that time a mendicant called Yamaka had the following harmful misconception: ^sn22-85-1-2

“As I understand the Buddha’s teaching, a mendicant who has ended the defilements is annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and doesn’t exist after death.” ^sn22-85-1-3

Several mendicants heard this rumour. ^sn22-85-2-1

They went to Yamaka and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn22-85-2-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him, ^sn22-85-2-4

“Is it really true, Reverend Yamaka, that you have such a harmful misconception: ^sn22-85-3-1

‘As I understand the Buddha’s teaching, a mendicant who has ended the defilements is annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and doesn’t exist after death’?” ^sn22-85-3-2

“Yes, reverends, that’s how I understand the Buddha’s teaching.” ^sn22-85-3-3

“Don’t say that, Yamaka! Don’t misrepresent the Buddha, for misrepresentation of the Buddha is not good. And the Buddha would not say that.” ^sn22-85-4-1

But even though admonished by those mendicants, Yamaka obstinately stuck to that misconception and insisted on stating it. ^sn22-85-4-3

When those mendicants were unable to dissuade Yamaka from that misconception, they rose from their seats and went to see Venerable Sāriputta. They told him what had happened, and said, ^sn22-85-5-1

“May Venerable Sāriputta please go to the mendicant Yamaka out of sympathy.” ^sn22-85-5-4

Sāriputta consented with silence. ^sn22-85-5-5

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Yamaka and exchanged greetings with him. Seated to one side he said to Yamaka: ^sn22-85-5-6

“Is it really true, Reverend Yamaka, that you have such a harmful misconception: ^sn22-85-6-1

‘As I understand the Buddha’s teaching, a mendicant who has ended the defilements is annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and doesn’t exist after death’?” ^sn22-85-6-2

“Yes, reverend, that’s how I understand the Buddha’s teaching.” ^sn22-85-6-3

“What do you think, Yamaka? ^sn22-85-7-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-85-7-2

“Impermanent, reverend.” ^sn22-85-7-3

“Is feeling …” … ^sn22-85-7-4

“… perception …” … ^sn22-85-7-5

“… choices …” … ^sn22-85-7-6

“… consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-85-7-7

“Impermanent, reverend.” ^sn22-85-7-8

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-85-7-9

Seeing this … ^sn22-85-7-10

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-85-7-11

What do you think, Reverend Yamaka? ^sn22-85-8-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to form?” ^sn22-85-8-2

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-8-3

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to feeling?” … ^sn22-85-8-4

“… perception?” … ^sn22-85-8-6

“… choices?” … ^sn22-85-8-7

“ … consciousness?” ^sn22-85-8-8

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-8-9

“What do you think, Reverend Yamaka? ^sn22-85-9-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to form?” ^sn22-85-9-2

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-9-3

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies apart from form?” ^sn22-85-9-4

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-9-5

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to feeling?” … ^sn22-85-9-6

“… apart from feeling?” … ^sn22-85-9-7

“… in relation to perception?” … ^sn22-85-9-8

“… apart from perception?” … ^sn22-85-9-9

“… in relation to choices?” … ^sn22-85-9-10

“… apart from choices?” … ^sn22-85-9-11

“… in relation to consciousness?” … ^sn22-85-9-12

“… apart from consciousness?” ^sn22-85-9-14

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-9-15

“What do you think, Yamaka? ^sn22-85-10-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to one who has form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn22-85-10-2

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-10-3

“What do you think, Yamaka? ^sn22-85-11-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to that one who is without form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn22-85-11-2

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-85-11-3

“In that case, Reverend Yamaka, since a realized one is not found by you as a genuine fact in this very life, is it cogent for you to declare: ^sn22-85-11-4

‘As I understand the Buddha’s teaching, a mendicant who has ended the defilements is annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and doesn’t exist after death.’?” ^sn22-85-11-5

“Reverend Sāriputta, in my ignorance, I used to have that misconception. ^sn22-85-12-1

But now that I’ve heard the teaching from Venerable Sāriputta, I’ve given up that misconception, and I’ve comprehended the teaching.” ^sn22-85-12-2

“Reverend Yamaka, suppose they were to ask you: ^sn22-85-13-1

‘When their body breaks up, after death, what happens to a perfected one, who has ended the defilements?’ ^sn22-85-13-2

How would you answer?” ^sn22-85-13-3

“Reverend, if they were to ask this, ^sn22-85-13-4

I’d answer like this: ^sn22-85-13-6

‘Reverend, form is impermanent. ^sn22-85-13-7

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-85-13-8

What’s suffering has ceased and disappeared. ^sn22-85-13-9

Feeling … ^sn22-85-13-10

perception … ^sn22-85-13-11

choices … ^sn22-85-13-12

consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-85-13-13

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-85-13-14

What’s suffering has ceased and disappeared.’ ^sn22-85-13-15

That’s how I’d answer such a question.” ^sn22-85-13-16

“Good, good, Reverend Yamaka! ^sn22-85-14-1

Well then, I shall give you a simile to make the meaning even clearer. ^sn22-85-14-2

Suppose there was a householder or householder’s son who was rich, with a lot of money and great wealth, ^sn22-85-14-3

and a bodyguard for protection. ^sn22-85-14-4

Then along comes a person who wants to harm, injure, and threaten him, and take his life. ^sn22-85-14-5

They’d think: ^sn22-85-14-6

‘This householder or householder’s son is rich, with a lot of money and great wealth, ^sn22-85-14-7

and a bodyguard for protection. ^sn22-85-14-8

It won’t be easy to take his life by force. ^sn22-85-14-9

Why don’t I get close to him, then take his life?’ ^sn22-85-14-10

So he goes up to that householder or householder’s son and says: ^sn22-85-14-11

‘Sir, I would serve you.’ ^sn22-85-14-12

They would serve that householder or householder’s son. ^sn22-85-14-13

They’d get up before him and go to bed after him, and be obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely. ^sn22-85-14-14

The householder or householder’s son would consider them as a friend ^sn22-85-14-15

and companion, ^sn22-85-14-16

and come to trust them. ^sn22-85-14-17

But when that person realizes that ^sn22-85-14-18

they’ve gained the trust of the householder or householder’s son, then, when they know he’s alone, they’d take his life with a sharp knife. ^sn22-85-14-19

What do you think, Yamaka? ^sn22-85-15-1

When that person went to the householder or householder’s son and offered to ^sn22-85-15-2

serve him, weren’t they a killer then, ^sn22-85-15-3

though he didn’t know that ^sn22-85-15-4

this was his killer? ^sn22-85-15-5

And when they got up before him and went to bed after him, being obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely, weren’t they a killer then, ^sn22-85-15-6

though he didn’t know that ^sn22-85-15-7

this was his killer? ^sn22-85-15-8

And when, knowing he was alone, they took his life with a sharp knife, weren’t they a killer then, ^sn22-85-15-9

though he didn’t know that ^sn22-85-15-10

this was his killer?” ^sn22-85-15-11

“Yes, reverend.” ^sn22-85-15-12

“In the same way, an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-85-15-13

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-85-15-14

They regard feeling … ^sn22-85-15-15

perception … ^sn22-85-15-16

choices … ^sn22-85-15-17

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-85-15-18

They don’t truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-85-16-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-16-2

perception … ^sn22-85-16-3

choices … ^sn22-85-16-4

consciousness—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-85-16-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-85-17-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-17-2

perception … ^sn22-85-17-3

choices … ^sn22-85-17-4

consciousness—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-85-17-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-85-18-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-18-2

perception … ^sn22-85-18-3

choices … ^sn22-85-18-4

consciousness—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-85-18-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-85-19-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-19-2

perception … ^sn22-85-19-3

choices … ^sn22-85-19-4

consciousness—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-85-19-5

They don’t truly understand form—which is a killer—as a killer. ^sn22-85-20-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-20-2

perception … ^sn22-85-20-3

choices … ^sn22-85-20-4

consciousness—which is a killer—as a killer. ^sn22-85-20-5

They’re attracted to form, grasp it, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’. ^sn22-85-21-1

They’re attracted to feeling … ^sn22-85-21-2

perception … ^sn22-85-21-3

choices … ^sn22-85-21-4

consciousness, grasp it, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’. ^sn22-85-21-5

And when you’ve gotten involved with and grasped these five grasping aggregates, they lead to your lasting harm and suffering. ^sn22-85-21-6

A learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-85-22-1

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-85-22-2

They don’t regard feeling … ^sn22-85-22-3

perception … ^sn22-85-22-4

choices … ^sn22-85-22-5

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, ^sn22-85-22-6

consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-85-22-7

They truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-85-23-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-23-2

perception … ^sn22-85-23-3

choices … ^sn22-85-23-4

consciousness—which is impermanent—as impermanent. ^sn22-85-23-5

They truly understand form—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-85-24-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-24-2

perception … ^sn22-85-24-3

choices … ^sn22-85-24-4

consciousness—which is suffering—as suffering. ^sn22-85-24-5

They truly understand form—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-85-25-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-25-2

perception … ^sn22-85-25-3

choices … ^sn22-85-25-4

consciousness—which is not-self—as not-self. ^sn22-85-25-5

They truly understand form—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-85-26-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-26-2

perception … ^sn22-85-26-3

choices … ^sn22-85-26-4

consciousness—which is conditioned—as conditioned. ^sn22-85-26-5

They truly understand form—which is a killer—as a killer. ^sn22-85-27-1

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-85-27-2

perception … ^sn22-85-27-3

choices … ^sn22-85-27-4

consciousness—which is a killer—as a killer. ^sn22-85-27-5

Not being attracted to form, they don’t grasp it, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’. ^sn22-85-28-1

Not being attracted to feeling … ^sn22-85-28-2

perception … ^sn22-85-28-3

choices … ^sn22-85-28-4

consciousness, they don't grasp it, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’. ^sn22-85-28-5

And when you’re not attracted to and don’t grasp these five grasping aggregates, they lead to your lasting welfare and happiness.” ^sn22-85-28-6

“Reverend Sāriputta, this is how it is when you have such venerables as spiritual companions to advise and instruct you out of kindness and sympathy. ^sn22-85-28-7

And after hearing this teaching by Venerable Sāriputta, my mind is freed from the defilements by not grasping.” ^sn22-85-28-8

### With Anurādha (Linked Discourses 22.86) ^sn22-86-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn22-86-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Anurādha was staying not far from the Buddha in a wilderness hut. ^sn22-86-1-2

Then several wanderers of other religions went up to Venerable Anurādha and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn22-86-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him: ^sn22-86-1-4

“Reverend Anurādha, when a Realized One is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them in these four ways: ^sn22-86-1-5

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn22-86-1-6

When they said this, Venerable Anurādha said to those wanderers: ^sn22-86-2-1

“Reverends, when a Realized One is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them other than these four ways: ^sn22-86-2-2

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn22-86-2-3

When he said this, the wanderers said to him: ^sn22-86-2-4

“This mendicant must be junior, recently gone forth, or else a foolish, incompetent senior mendicant.” ^sn22-86-2-5

Then, after rebuking Venerable Anurādha by calling him “junior” and “foolish”, the wanderers rose from their seats and left. ^sn22-86-2-6

Soon after they had left, Anurādha thought: ^sn22-86-3-1

“If those wanderers were to inquire further, ^sn22-86-3-2

how should I answer them so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should I explain in line with his teaching, so that there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn22-86-3-3

Then Venerable Anurādha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him all that had happened. ^sn22-86-4-1

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn22-86-7-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-86-7-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-86-7-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-86-7-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-86-7-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-86-7-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-86-7-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-7-8

“Is feeling …” … ^sn22-86-7-9

“… perception …” … ^sn22-86-7-10

“… choices …” … ^sn22-86-7-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-86-7-12

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn22-86-7-13

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-86-7-14

Seeing this … ^sn22-86-7-15

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-86-7-16

What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn22-86-8-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to form?” ^sn22-86-8-2

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-8-3

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to feeling?” … ^sn22-86-8-4

“… perception?” … ^sn22-86-8-5

“… choices?” … ^sn22-86-8-6

“… consciousness?” ^sn22-86-8-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-8-8

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn22-86-9-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to form?” ^sn22-86-9-2

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-9-3

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies apart from form?” ^sn22-86-9-4

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-9-5

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to feeling?” … ^sn22-86-9-6

“… apart from feeling?” … ^sn22-86-9-7

“… in relation to perception?” … ^sn22-86-9-8

“… apart from perception?” … ^sn22-86-9-9

“… in relation to choices?” … ^sn22-86-9-10

“… apart from choices?” … ^sn22-86-9-11

“… in relation to consciousness?” … ^sn22-86-9-12

“… apart from consciousness?” ^sn22-86-9-13

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-9-14

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn22-86-10-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to one who has form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn22-86-10-2

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-10-3

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn22-86-11-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to that one who is without form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn22-86-11-2

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-11-3

“In that case, Anurādha, since a realized one is not found by you as a genuine fact in this very life, is it cogent for you to declare: ^sn22-86-12-1

‘Reverends, when a Realized One is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them other than these four ways: ^sn22-86-12-2

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists’?” ^sn22-86-12-3

“No, sir.” ^sn22-86-12-4

“Good, good, Anurādha! ^sn22-86-13-1

In the past, as today, what I describe is suffering and the cessation of suffering.” ^sn22-86-13-2

### With Vakkali (Linked Discourses 22.87) ^sn22-87-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn22-87-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Vakkali was staying in a potter’s shed, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn22-87-1-2

Then he addressed his carers, ^sn22-87-1-3

“Please, reverends, go to the Buddha, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^sn22-87-1-4

‘Sir, the mendicant Vakkali is sick, suffering, and gravely ill. He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn22-87-1-5

And then say: ^sn22-87-1-6

‘Sir, please go to the mendicant Vakkali out of sympathy.’” ^sn22-87-1-7

“Yes, reverend,” those monks replied. They did as he asked. ^sn22-87-1-8

The Buddha consented with silence. ^sn22-87-1-12

Then the Buddha robed up and, taking his bowl and robes, went to Venerable Vakkali. ^sn22-87-2-1

Venerable Vakkali saw the Buddha coming off in the distance and stirred in his cot. ^sn22-87-2-2

But the Buddha said to him, ^sn22-87-2-3

“It’s all right, Vakkali, don’t get up. ^sn22-87-2-4

There are some seats spread out, I will sit there.” ^sn22-87-2-5

He sat on the seat spread out and said to Vakkali, ^sn22-87-2-6

“I hope you’re keeping well, Vakkali; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn22-87-2-7

“Sir, I’m not all right, I’m not getting by. My pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is apparent, not its fading.” ^sn22-87-2-8

“I hope you don’t have any remorse or regret?” ^sn22-87-2-9

“Indeed, sir, I have no little remorse and regret.” ^sn22-87-2-10

“I hope you have no reason to blame yourself when it comes to ethical conduct?” ^sn22-87-2-11

“No sir, I have no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct.” ^sn22-87-2-12

“In that case, Vakkali, why do you have remorse and regret?” ^sn22-87-2-13

“For a long time I’ve wanted to go and see the Buddha, but I was physically too weak.” ^sn22-87-2-14

“Enough, Vakkali! Why would you want to see this rotting body? ^sn22-87-3-1

One who sees the teaching sees me. ^sn22-87-3-2

One who sees me sees the teaching. ^sn22-87-3-3

Seeing the teaching, you see me. ^sn22-87-3-4

Seeing me, you see the teaching. ^sn22-87-3-5

What do you think, Vakkali? ^sn22-87-4-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-87-4-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-87-4-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-87-4-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-87-4-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn22-87-4-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-87-4-7

“No, sir.” ^sn22-87-4-8

“Is feeling … ^sn22-87-4-9

perception … ^sn22-87-4-10

choices … ^sn22-87-4-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-87-4-12

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn22-87-4-13

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-87-4-16

Seeing this … ^sn22-87-4-17

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-87-4-18

And then, after giving Venerable Vakkali this advice, the Buddha rose from his seat and went to the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn22-87-5-1

Then Venerable Vakkali addressed his carers, ^sn22-87-5-2

“Come on, reverends, lift my cot and take me to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili. ^sn22-87-5-3

For how on earth could one such as I imagine dying in an inhabited area!” ^sn22-87-5-4

“Yes, reverend,” replied those mendicants, and did as he asked. ^sn22-87-5-5

Meanwhile, the Buddha spent the rest of that night and day on Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn22-87-5-6

Then, late at night, two glorious deities, lighting up the entire Vulture’s Peak, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. ^sn22-87-5-7

One deity said to him, ^sn22-87-5-8

“Sir, the mendicant Vakkali is intent on liberation!” ^sn22-87-5-9

And another deity said to him, ^sn22-87-5-10

“He’ll definitely be well freed!” ^sn22-87-5-11

This is what those deities said. ^sn22-87-5-12

Then they bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on their right side, before vanishing right there. ^sn22-87-5-13

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn22-87-6-1

“Come, mendicants, go to the mendicant Vakkali and tell him: ^sn22-87-6-2

‘Vakkali, hear the word of the Buddha and two deities. ^sn22-87-7-1

Late last night, two glorious deities, lighting up the entire Vulture’s Peak, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. ^sn22-87-7-2

One deity said to him, ^sn22-87-7-3

“Sir, the mendicant Vakkali is intent on liberation!” ^sn22-87-7-4

The other deity said to him, ^sn22-87-7-5

“He’ll definitely be well freed!” ^sn22-87-7-6

And the Buddha said, ^sn22-87-7-7

“Do not fear, Vakkali, ^sn22-87-7-8

do not fear! ^sn22-87-7-9

Your death will not be a bad one; your passing will not be a bad one.”’” ^sn22-87-7-10

“Yes, sir,” those monks replied. They went to Vakkali and said to him: ^sn22-87-7-11

“Vakkali, hear the word of the Buddha and two deities.” ^sn22-87-7-12

Then Vakkali addressed his carers, ^sn22-87-8-1

“Please, reverends, help me off my cot. ^sn22-87-8-2

For how on earth could one such as I presume to listen to the Buddha’s instructions sitting on a high seat?” ^sn22-87-8-3

“Yes, reverend,” replied those mendicants, and helped him off his cot. ^sn22-87-8-4

They repeated what the Buddha had said. ^sn22-87-8-5

Vakkali said, “Well then, reverends, in my name bow with your head at the Buddha’s feet. Say to him: ^sn22-87-8-14

‘Sir, the mendicant Vakkali is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn22-87-8-15

He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn22-87-8-16

And then say: ^sn22-87-8-17

‘Form is impermanent. ^sn22-87-8-18

I have no doubt of that. ^sn22-87-8-19

I’m certain that what is impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-87-8-20

And I’m certain that I have no desire or greed or fondness for what is impermanent, suffering, and perishable. ^sn22-87-8-21

Feeling is impermanent … ^sn22-87-8-22

Perception is impermanent … ^sn22-87-8-26

Choices are impermanent … ^sn22-87-8-27

Consciousness is impermanent. ^sn22-87-8-31

I have no doubt of that. ^sn22-87-8-32

I’m certain that what is impermanent is suffering. ^sn22-87-8-33

And I’m certain that I have no desire or greed or fondness for what is impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’” ^sn22-87-8-34

“Yes, reverend,” those monks replied, and left. ^sn22-87-8-35

And then, not long after those mendicants had left, Venerable Vakkali committed suicide. ^sn22-87-8-36

Then those mendicants went up to the Buddha and told him Vakkali’s message. ^sn22-87-9-1

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn22-87-10-1

“Come, mendicants, let’s go to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili, ^sn22-87-10-2

where Vakkali, the gentleman, committed suicide.” ^sn22-87-10-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn22-87-10-4

Then the Buddha together with several mendicants went to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili. ^sn22-87-10-5

The Buddha saw Vakkali off in the distance on his cot, lying with aggregates cast off. ^sn22-87-10-6

Now at that time a cloud of black smoke was moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between. ^sn22-87-11-1

The Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn22-87-11-2

“Mendicants, do you see that cloud of black smoke moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between?” ^sn22-87-11-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-87-11-4

“That’s Māra the Wicked searching for Vakkali’s consciousness, wondering: ^sn22-87-11-5

‘Where is Vakkali’s consciousness established?’ ^sn22-87-11-6

But since his consciousness is not established, Vakkali is quenched.” ^sn22-87-11-7

### With Assaji (Linked Discourses 22.88) ^sn22-88-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn22-88-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Assaji was staying in a monastery built by a Kassapa, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn22-88-1-2

Then he addressed his carers: ^sn22-88-1-3

“Please, reverends, go to the Buddha, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^sn22-88-1-4

‘Sir, the mendicant Assaji is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn22-88-1-5

He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn22-88-1-6

And then say: ^sn22-88-1-7

‘Sir, please go to the mendicant Assaji out of sympathy.’” ^sn22-88-1-8

“Yes, reverend,” those monks replied. They did as he asked. ^sn22-88-1-9

The Buddha consented with silence. ^sn22-88-1-12

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to Venerable Assaji. ^sn22-88-2-1

Venerable Assaji saw the Buddha coming off in the distance, ^sn22-88-2-2

and stirred in his cot. ^sn22-88-2-3

But the Buddha said to him, ^sn22-88-2-4

“It’s all right, Assaji, don’t get up. ^sn22-88-2-5

There are some seats spread out, I will sit there.” ^sn22-88-2-6

He sat on the seat spread out and said, ^sn22-88-2-7

“I hope you’re keeping well, Assaji; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn22-88-2-8

“Sir, I’m not all right, I’m not getting by. My pain is terrible and growing, not fading, its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn22-88-3-1

“I hope you don’t have any remorse or regret?” ^sn22-88-3-2

“Indeed, sir, I have no little remorse and regret.” ^sn22-88-3-3

“I hope you have no reason to blame yourself when it comes to ethical conduct?” ^sn22-88-3-4

“No sir, I have no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct.” ^sn22-88-3-5

“In that case, Assaji, why do you have remorse and regret?” ^sn22-88-3-6

“Sir, before my time of illness I meditated having completely stilled the physical process. But now I can’t get immersion. ^sn22-88-3-7

Since I can’t get immersion, I think: ^sn22-88-3-8

‘May I not decline!’” ^sn22-88-3-9

“There are, Assaji, ascetics and brahmins for whom samādhi is the essence, equating immersion with the ascetic life. They think: ^sn22-88-3-10

‘May we not decline!’ ^sn22-88-3-11

What do you think, Assaji? ^sn22-88-4-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-88-4-2

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn22-88-4-3

“Is consciousness permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-88-4-4

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-88-4-5

Seeing this … ^sn22-88-4-6

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn22-88-4-7

If they feel a pleasant feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t relish it. ^sn22-88-4-8

If they feel a painful feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t relish it. ^sn22-88-4-9

If they feel a neutral feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t relish it. ^sn22-88-4-10

If they feel a pleasant feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn22-88-4-11

If they feel a painful feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn22-88-4-12

If they feel a neutral feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn22-88-4-13

Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn22-88-4-14

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life is over, everything that’s felt, being no longer relished, will become cool right here.’ ^sn22-88-4-15

Suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn22-88-5-1

As the oil and the wick are used up, it would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^sn22-88-5-2

In the same way, feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn22-88-5-3

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life is over, everything that’s felt, being no longer relished, will become cool right here.’” ^sn22-88-5-4

### With Khemaka (Linked Discourses 22.89) ^sn22-89-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn22-89-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Khemaka was staying in the Jujube Tree Monastery, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn22-89-1-2

In the late afternoon those senior mendicants came out of retreat and addressed Venerable Dāsaka, ^sn22-89-1-3

“Please, Reverend Dāsaka, go to the mendicant Khemaka and say to him: ^sn22-89-1-4

‘Reverend Khemaka, the seniors ^sn22-89-1-5

hope you’re keeping well; they hope you’re all right. They hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.’” ^sn22-89-1-6

“Yes, reverends,” replied Dāsaka. He went to Khemaka and said to him: ^sn22-89-1-7

“Reverend Khemaka, the seniors ^sn22-89-1-8

hope you’re keeping well; they hope you’re all right. They hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn22-89-1-9

“Reverend, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. My pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn22-89-1-10

Then Dāsaka went to those senior mendicants and told them what had happened. They said, ^sn22-89-2-1

“Please, Reverend Dāsaka, go to the mendicant Khemaka and say to him: ^sn22-89-2-4

‘Reverend Khemaka, the seniors say that ^sn22-89-2-5

these five grasping aggregates have been taught by the Buddha, that is: ^sn22-89-2-6

the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-89-2-7

Do you regard anything among these five grasping aggregates as self or as belonging to self?’” ^sn22-89-2-8

“Yes, reverends,” replied Dāsaka. He relayed the message to Khemaka, who replied: ^sn22-89-3-1

“These five grasping aggregates have been taught by the Buddha, that is: ^sn22-89-3-6

the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-89-3-7

I do not regard anything among these five grasping aggregates as self or as belonging to self.” ^sn22-89-3-8

Then Dāsaka went to those senior mendicants and told them what had happened. They said: ^sn22-89-4-1

“Please, Reverend Dāsaka, go to the mendicant Khemaka and say to him: ^sn22-89-4-6

‘Reverend Khemaka, the seniors say that ^sn22-89-4-7

these five grasping aggregates have been taught by the Buddha, that is: ^sn22-89-4-8

the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-89-4-9

If, as it seems, Venerable Khemaka does not regard anything among these five grasping aggregates as self or as belonging to self, ^sn22-89-4-10

then he is a perfected one, with defilements ended.’” ^sn22-89-4-11

“Yes, reverends,” replied Dāsaka. He relayed the message to Khemaka, who replied: ^sn22-89-5-1

“These five grasping aggregates have been taught by the Buddha, that is: ^sn22-89-5-6

the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-89-5-7

I do not regard anything among these five grasping aggregates as self or as belonging to self, yet I am not a perfected one, with defilements ended. ^sn22-89-5-8

For when it comes to the five grasping aggregates I’m not rid of the conceit ‘I am’. But I don’t regard anything as ‘I am this’.” ^sn22-89-5-9

Then Dāsaka went to those senior mendicants and told them what had happened. They said: ^sn22-89-6-1

“Please, Reverend Dāsaka, go to the mendicant Khemaka and say to him: ^sn22-89-7-1

‘Reverend Khemaka, the seniors ask, ^sn22-89-7-2

when you say “I am”, what is it that you’re talking about? ^sn22-89-7-3

Is it form or apart from form? ^sn22-89-7-4

Is it feeling … ^sn22-89-7-5

perception … ^sn22-89-7-6

choices … ^sn22-89-7-7

consciousness, or apart from consciousness? ^sn22-89-7-8
