When you say “I am”, what is it that you’re talking about?” ^sn22-89-7-9

“Yes, reverends,” replied Dāsaka. He relayed the message to Khemaka, who replied: ^sn22-89-8-1

“Enough, Reverend Dāsaka! What’s the point in running back and forth? ^sn22-89-8-10

Bring my staff, ^sn22-89-8-11

I’ll go to see the senior mendicants myself.” ^sn22-89-8-12

Then Venerable Khemaka, leaning on a staff, went to those senior mendicants and exchanged greetings with them. ^sn22-89-9-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. They said to him: ^sn22-89-9-2

“Reverend Khemaka, when you say ‘I am’, what is it that you’re talking about? ^sn22-89-9-3

Is it form or apart from form? ^sn22-89-9-4

Is it feeling … ^sn22-89-9-5

perception … ^sn22-89-9-6

choices … ^sn22-89-9-7

consciousness, or apart from consciousness? ^sn22-89-9-8

When you say ‘I am’, what is it that you’re talking about?” ^sn22-89-9-9

“Reverends, I don’t say ‘I am’ with reference to form, or apart from form. ^sn22-89-9-10

I don’t say ‘I am’ with reference to feeling … ^sn22-89-9-11

perception … ^sn22-89-9-12

choices … ^sn22-89-9-13

consciousness, or apart from consciousness. ^sn22-89-9-14

For when it comes to the five grasping aggregates I’m not rid of the conceit ‘I am’. But I don’t regard anything as ‘I am this’. ^sn22-89-9-15

It’s like the scent of a blue water lily, or a pink or white lotus. ^sn22-89-10-1

Would it be right to say that the scent belongs to the petals or the stalk or the pistil?” ^sn22-89-10-2

“No, reverend.” ^sn22-89-10-3

“Then, reverends, how should it be said?” ^sn22-89-10-4

“It would be right to say that the scent belongs to the flower.” ^sn22-89-10-5

“In the same way, reverends, I don’t say ‘I am’ with reference to form, or apart from form. ^sn22-89-10-6

I don’t say ‘I am’ with reference to feeling … ^sn22-89-10-7

perception … ^sn22-89-10-8

choices … ^sn22-89-10-9

consciousness, or apart from consciousness. ^sn22-89-10-10

For when it comes to the five grasping aggregates I’m not rid of the conceit ‘I am’. But I don’t regard anything as ‘I am this’. ^sn22-89-10-11

Although a noble disciple has given up the five lower fetters, they still have a lingering residue of the conceit ‘I am’, the desire ‘I am’, and the underlying tendency ‘I am’ which has not been eradicated. ^sn22-89-11-1

After some time they meditate observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-89-11-2

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn22-89-11-3

Such is feeling … ^sn22-89-11-4

Such is perception … ^sn22-89-11-5

Such are choices … ^sn22-89-11-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.’ ^sn22-89-11-7

As they do so, that lingering residue is eradicated. ^sn22-89-11-8

Suppose there was a cloth that was dirty and soiled, so the owners give it to a launderer. ^sn22-89-12-1

The launderer kneads it thoroughly with salt, lye, and cow dung, and rinses it in clear water. ^sn22-89-12-2

Although that cloth is clean and bright, it still has a lingering scent of salt, lye, or cow dung that had not been eradicated. ^sn22-89-12-3

The launderer returns it to its owners, who store it in a fragrant casket. ^sn22-89-12-4

And that lingering scent would be eradicated. ^sn22-89-12-5

In the same way, although a noble disciple has given up the five lower fetters, they still have a lingering residue of the conceit ‘I am’, the desire ‘I am’, and the underlying tendency ‘I am’ which has not been eradicated. ^sn22-89-12-6

After some time they meditate observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-89-12-7

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the disappearance of form. ^sn22-89-12-8

Such is feeling … ^sn22-89-12-9

Such is perception … ^sn22-89-12-10

Such are choices … ^sn22-89-12-11

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.’ ^sn22-89-12-12

As they do so, that lingering residue is eradicated.” ^sn22-89-12-13

When he said this, the senior mendicants said to Venerable Khemaka, ^sn22-89-13-1

“We didn’t want to trouble Venerable Khemaka with our questions. But you’re capable of explaining, teaching, asserting, establishing, clarifying, analyzing, and revealing the Buddha’s instructions in detail. ^sn22-89-13-2

And that’s just what you’ve done.” ^sn22-89-13-3

That’s what Venerable Khemaka said. ^sn22-89-14-1

Satisfied, the senior mendicants approved what Venerable Khemaka said. ^sn22-89-14-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the minds of sixty senior mendicants and of Venerable Khemaka were freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-89-14-3

### With Channa (Linked Discourses 22.90) ^sn22-90-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn22-90-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Channa came out of retreat. Taking a latchkey, he went from dwelling to dwelling, going up to the senior mendicants and saying, ^sn22-90-1-2

“May the venerable senior mendicants advise me and instruct me! May they give me a Dhamma talk so that I can see the teaching!” ^sn22-90-1-3

When he said this, the senior mendicants said to Venerable Channa: ^sn22-90-2-1

“Reverend Channa, form, ^sn22-90-2-2

feeling, ^sn22-90-2-3

perception, ^sn22-90-2-4

choices, ^sn22-90-2-5

and consciousness are impermanent. ^sn22-90-2-6

Form, ^sn22-90-2-7

feeling, ^sn22-90-2-8

perception, ^sn22-90-2-9

choices, ^sn22-90-2-10

and consciousness are not-self. ^sn22-90-2-11

All conditions are impermanent. ^sn22-90-2-12

All things are not-self.” ^sn22-90-2-13

Then Venerable Channa thought, ^sn22-90-3-1

“I too think in this way. … ^sn22-90-3-2

And yet my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided about the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^sn22-90-3-12

Because of anxiety, grasping arises ^sn22-90-3-13

and keeps turning back to the thought: ^sn22-90-3-14

‘So who then is my self?’ ^sn22-90-3-15

But that doesn’t happen for someone who sees the teaching. ^sn22-90-3-16

Who can teach me the Dhamma so that I can see the teaching?” ^sn22-90-3-17

Then Venerable Channa thought, ^sn22-90-4-1

“The Venerable Ānanda is staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. He’s praised by the Buddha and esteemed by his sensible spiritual companions. He’s quite capable of teaching me the Dhamma so that I can see the teaching. ^sn22-90-4-2

Since I have so much trust in Venerable Ānanda, ^sn22-90-4-3

why don’t I go to see him?” ^sn22-90-4-4

Then Channa set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, set out for Kosambī. He went to see Ānanda in Ghosita’s Monastery, exchanged greetings with him, and told him what had happened. Then he said, ^sn22-90-4-5

“May Venerable Ānanda advise me and instruct me! May he give me a Dhamma talk so that I can see the teaching!” ^sn22-90-7-5

“I’m already delighted with Venerable Channa. Hopefully you’ve opened yourself up and severed your hard-heartedness. ^sn22-90-8-1

Listen well, Channa. ^sn22-90-8-2

You are capable of understanding the teaching.” ^sn22-90-8-3

Then right away Channa was filled with lofty rapture and joy, ^sn22-90-8-4

“It seems I’m capable of understanding the teaching!” ^sn22-90-8-5

“Reverend Channa, I heard and learned in the presence of the Buddha his advice to the mendicant Kaccānagotta: ^sn22-90-9-1

‘Kaccāna, this world mostly relies on the duality of existence and non-existence. ^sn22-90-9-2

But for one who truly sees the origin of the world with right understanding, the concept of non-existence regarding the world does not occur. ^sn22-90-9-3

And for one who truly sees the cessation of the world with right understanding, the concept of existence regarding the world does not occur. ^sn22-90-9-4

This world is for the most part shackled by getting involved, grasping, and insisting. ^sn22-90-9-5

But if—for all that getting involved and grasping, mental fixation, and insistence and underlying tendency—they do not get attracted, grasp, and fixate on the thought, ‘my self’, ^sn22-90-9-6

they’ll have no doubt or uncertainty that what arises is just suffering arising, and what ceases is just suffering ceasing. ^sn22-90-9-7

Their knowledge of this is independent of others. ^sn22-90-9-8

This is how right view is defined. ^sn22-90-9-9

“All exists”: this is one extreme. ^sn22-90-9-10

“All does not exist”: this is the second extreme. ^sn22-90-9-11

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way: ^sn22-90-9-12

“Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^sn22-90-9-13

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. … ^sn22-90-9-14

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn22-90-9-15

When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease. … ^sn22-90-9-16

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”’” ^sn22-90-9-17

“Reverend Ānanda, this is how it is when you have such venerables as spiritual companions to advise and instruct you out of kindness and sympathy. ^sn22-90-10-1

And now that I’ve heard this teaching from Venerable Ānanda, I’ve comprehended the teaching.” ^sn22-90-10-2

### Rāhula (Linked Discourses 22.91) ^sn22-91-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-91-1-1

Then Venerable Rāhula went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn22-91-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs?” ^sn22-91-1-3

“Rāhula, one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-91-2-1

One truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn22-91-2-2

perception … ^sn22-91-2-3

choices … ^sn22-91-2-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-91-2-5

That’s how to know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs.” ^sn22-91-2-7

### Rāhula (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.92) ^sn22-92-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-92-1-1

Seated to one side, Rāhula said to the Buddha: ^sn22-92-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed?” ^sn22-92-1-3

“Rāhula, when one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ one is freed by not grasping. ^sn22-92-2-1

One truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn22-92-2-2

perception … ^sn22-92-2-3

choices … ^sn22-92-2-4

When one truly sees any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ one is freed by not grasping. ^sn22-92-2-5

That’s how to know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed.” ^sn22-92-2-6

### A River (Linked Discourses 22.93) ^sn22-93-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-93-1-1

“Suppose, mendicants, there was a mountain river that flowed swiftly, going far, carrying all before it. If wild sugarcane, kusa grass, reeds, vetiver, or trees grew on either bank, they’d overhang the river. And if a person who was being swept along by the current grabbed the wild sugarcane, kusa grass, reeds, vetiver, or trees, it’d fall apart, and they’d come to ruin because of that. ^sn22-93-1-2

In the same way, an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-93-1-3

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-93-1-4

But their form falls apart, ^sn22-93-1-5

and they come to ruin because of that. ^sn22-93-1-6

They regard feeling … ^sn22-93-1-7

perception … ^sn22-93-1-8

choices … ^sn22-93-1-9

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-93-1-10

But their consciousness falls apart, ^sn22-93-1-11

and they come to ruin because of that. ^sn22-93-1-12

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-93-1-13

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-93-1-14

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn22-93-1-15

“Is feeling … ^sn22-93-1-17

perception … ^sn22-93-1-18

choices … ^sn22-93-1-19

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-93-1-20

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-93-1-21

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-93-1-22

Seeing this … ^sn22-93-1-23

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-93-1-24

### Flowers (Linked Discourses 22.94) ^sn22-94-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-94-1-1

“Mendicants, I do not dispute with the world; it is the world that disputes with me. ^sn22-94-1-2

When your speech is in line with the teaching you do not dispute with anyone in the world. ^sn22-94-1-3

What the astute deem as not existing, I too say does not exist. ^sn22-94-1-4

What the astute deem as existing, I too say exists. ^sn22-94-1-5

And what do the astute deem as not existing, which I too say does not exist? ^sn22-94-2-1

Form that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable. ^sn22-94-2-2

Feeling … ^sn22-94-2-3

Perception … ^sn22-94-2-4

Choices … ^sn22-94-2-5

Consciousness that is permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable. ^sn22-94-2-6

This is what the astute deem as not existing, which I too say does not exist. ^sn22-94-2-7

And what do the astute deem as existing, which I too say exists? ^sn22-94-3-1

Form that is impermanent, suffering, and perishable. ^sn22-94-3-2

Feeling … Perception … Choices … ^sn22-94-3-3

Consciousness that is impermanent, suffering, and perishable. ^sn22-94-3-4

This is what the astute deem as existing, which I too say exists. ^sn22-94-3-5

There is a worldly phenomenon in the world that the Realized One understands and comprehends. ^sn22-94-4-1

Then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it. ^sn22-94-4-2

And what is that worldly phenomenon in the world? ^sn22-94-5-1

Form is a worldly phenomenon in the world that the Realized One understands and comprehends. ^sn22-94-5-2

Then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it. ^sn22-94-5-3

This being so, what can I do about a foolish ordinary person, blind and sightless, who does not know or see? ^sn22-94-6-1

Feeling … ^sn22-94-6-2

Perception … ^sn22-94-6-3

Choices … ^sn22-94-6-4

Consciousness is a worldly phenomenon in the world that the Realized One understands and comprehends. ^sn22-94-6-5

Then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it. ^sn22-94-6-6

This being so, what can I do about a foolish ordinary person, blind and sightless, who does not know or see? ^sn22-94-7-1

Suppose there was a blue water lily, or a pink or white lotus. Though it sprouted and grew in the water, it would rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to it. ^sn22-94-8-1

In the same way, though I was born and grew up in the world, I live having mastered the world, unsullied by the world.” ^sn22-94-8-2

### A Lump of Foam (Linked Discourses 22.95) ^sn22-95-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Ayodhya on the bank of the Ganges river. ^sn22-95-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn22-95-1-2

“Mendicants, suppose this Ganges river was carrying along a big lump of foam. And a person with clear eyes would see it and contemplate it, examining it rationally. And it would appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a lump of foam? ^sn22-95-2-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees and contemplates any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or fine; inferior or superior; near or far—examining it rationally. And it appears to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in form? ^sn22-95-2-2

Suppose it was autumn, when the heavens rain heavily, and a bubble on the water forms and pops right away. And a person with clear eyes would see it and contemplate it, examining it rationally. And it would appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a water bubble? ^sn22-95-3-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees and contemplates any kind of feeling at all … examining it rationally. And it appears to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in feeling? ^sn22-95-3-2

Suppose that in the last month of summer, at noon, a shimmering mirage appears. And a person with clear eyes would see it and contemplate it, examining it rationally. And it would appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a mirage? ^sn22-95-4-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees and contemplates any kind of perception at all … examining it carefully. And it appears to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in perception? ^sn22-95-4-2

Suppose there was a person in need of heartwood. Wandering in search of heartwood, they’d take a sharp axe and enter a forest. There they’d see a big banana trunk, straight and young and grown unskewed. They’d cut it down at the base, cut off the top, and unroll the coiled sheaths. But they wouldn’t even find sapwood, much less heartwood. And a person with clear eyes would see it and contemplate it, examining it rationally. And it would appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a banana trunk? ^sn22-95-5-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees and contemplates any kind of choices at all … examining them rationally. And they appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in choices? ^sn22-95-5-2

Suppose an illusionist or their apprentice was to demonstrate an illusion at the crossroads. And a person with clear eyes would see it and contemplate it, examining it rationally. And it would appear to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in an illusion? ^sn22-95-6-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees and contemplates any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or fine; inferior or superior; near or far—examining it rationally. And it appears to them as completely vacuous, hollow, and insubstantial. For what substance could there be in consciousness? ^sn22-95-6-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-95-7-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-95-7-2

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-95-7-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn22-95-8-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn22-95-8-2

“Form is like a lump of foam; ^sn22-95-9-1

feeling is like a bubble; ^sn22-95-9-2

perception seems like a mirage; ^sn22-95-9-3

choices like a banana plant; ^sn22-95-9-4

and consciousness like an illusion: ^sn22-95-9-5

so taught the kinsman of the Sun. ^sn22-95-9-6

However you contemplate them, ^sn22-95-10-1

examining them rationally, ^sn22-95-10-2

they’re vacuous and hollow ^sn22-95-10-3

when you look at them closely. ^sn22-95-10-4

Concerning this body, ^sn22-95-11-1

he of vast wisdom has taught ^sn22-95-11-2

that when three things are given up, ^sn22-95-11-3

you’ll see this form discarded. ^sn22-95-11-4

When vitality, warmth, and consciousness ^sn22-95-12-1

leave this body, ^sn22-95-12-2

it lies dumped there, ^sn22-95-12-3

food for others, insentient. ^sn22-95-12-4

Such is this continuity, ^sn22-95-13-1

this illusion, this lament of fools. ^sn22-95-13-2

It’s said to be a killer, ^sn22-95-13-3

for no core is found here. ^sn22-95-13-4

An energetic mendicant ^sn22-95-14-1

should examine the aggregates like this, ^sn22-95-14-2

with situational awareness and mindfulness ^sn22-95-14-3

whether by day or by night. ^sn22-95-14-4

They should give up all fetters, ^sn22-95-15-1

and make a refuge for themselves. ^sn22-95-15-2

They should live as though their head was on fire, ^sn22-95-15-3

aspiring to the state that does not pass.” ^sn22-95-15-4

### A Lump of Cow Dung (Linked Discourses 22.96) ^sn22-96-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-96-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-96-1-2

“Sir, is there any form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever? ^sn22-96-1-3

Is there any feeling … ^sn22-96-1-4

perception … ^sn22-96-1-5

choices … ^sn22-96-1-6

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever?” ^sn22-96-1-7

“Mendicant, there is no form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-96-1-8

There’s no feeling … ^sn22-96-1-9

perception … ^sn22-96-1-10

choices … ^sn22-96-1-11

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever.” ^sn22-96-1-12

Then the Buddha, picking up a lump of cow dung, said to the mendicants: ^sn22-96-2-1

“There’s not even this much of any incarnation that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-96-2-2

If there were, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering would not be found. ^sn22-96-2-3

But since there isn’t, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering is found. ^sn22-96-2-4

Once upon a time I was an anointed aristocratic king. ^sn22-96-3-1

I had 84,000 cities, with the capital Kusāvatī the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-2

I had 84,000 palaces, with the Palace of Principle the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-3

I had 84,000 chambers, with the Chamber of the Grand Tower the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-4

I had 84,000 couches made of ivory or heartwood or gold or silver, spread with woolen covers—shag-piled or embroidered with flowers—and spread with a fine deer hide, with a canopy above and red pillows at both ends. ^sn22-96-3-5

I had 84,000 bull elephants with gold adornments and banners, covered with snow gold netting, with the royal bull elephant named Sabbath the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-6

I had 84,000 horses with gold adornments and banners, covered with snow gold netting, with the royal steed named Thundercloud the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-7

I had 84,000 chariots with gold adornments and banners, covered with snow gold netting, with the chariot named Triumph the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-8

I had 84,000 jewels, with the jewel-treasure the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-9

I had 84,000 women, with Queen Subhaddā the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-10

I had 84,000 aristocrat vassals, with the commander-treasure the foremost. ^sn22-96-3-11

I had 84,000 milk cows with muslin halter and bronze pails. ^sn22-96-3-12

I had 8,400,000,000 delicate cloths of linen, cotton, silk, and wool. ^sn22-96-3-13

I had 84,000 servings of food, which were presented to me as offerings at daybreak and dusk. ^sn22-96-3-14

Of those 84,000 cities, I only stayed in one, the capital Kusāvatī. ^sn22-96-4-1

Of those 84,000 palaces, I only dwelt in one, the Palace of Principle. ^sn22-96-4-2

Of those 84,000 chambers, I only dwelt in one, the  Chamber of the Grand Tower. ^sn22-96-4-3

Of those 84,000 couches, I only used one, made of ivory or heartwood or gold or silver. ^sn22-96-4-4

Of those 84,000 bull elephants, I only rode one, the royal bull elephant named Sabbath. ^sn22-96-4-5

Of those 84,000 horses, I only rode one, the royal horse named Thundercloud. ^sn22-96-4-6

Of those 84,000 chariots, I only rode one, the chariot named Triumph. ^sn22-96-4-7

Of those 84,000 women, I was only served by one, an aristocrat maiden or a coquette. ^sn22-96-4-8

Of those 8,400,000,000 cloths, I only wore one pair, made of delicate linen, cotton, silk, and wool. ^sn22-96-4-9

Of those 84,000 servings of food, I only had one, eating at most a cup of rice with suitable sauce. ^sn22-96-4-10

And so all those conditioned phenomena have passed, ceased, and perished. ^sn22-96-4-11

So impermanent are conditions, ^sn22-96-4-12

so unstable are conditions, ^sn22-96-4-13

so unreliable are conditions. ^sn22-96-4-14

This is quite enough for you to become disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding all conditions.” ^sn22-96-4-15

### A Fingernail (Linked Discourses 22.97) ^sn22-97-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-97-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-97-1-2

“Sir, is there any form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever? ^sn22-97-1-3

Is there any feeling … ^sn22-97-1-4

perception … ^sn22-97-1-5

choices … ^sn22-97-1-6

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever?” ^sn22-97-1-7

“Mendicant, there is no form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-97-1-8

There’s no feeling … ^sn22-97-1-9

perception … ^sn22-97-1-10

choices … ^sn22-97-1-11

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever.” ^sn22-97-1-12

Then the Buddha, picking up a little bit of dirt on his fingernail, addressed that mendicant: ^sn22-97-2-1

“There’s not even this much of any form that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-97-2-2

If there were, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering would not be found. ^sn22-97-2-3

But since there isn’t, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering is found. ^sn22-97-2-4

There’s not even this much of any feeling … ^sn22-97-3-1

perception … ^sn22-97-4-1

choices … ^sn22-97-4-2

consciousness that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-97-5-1

If there were, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering would not be found. ^sn22-97-5-2

But since there isn’t, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering is found. ^sn22-97-5-3

What do you think, mendicant? ^sn22-97-6-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-97-6-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-97-6-3

“Is feeling … ^sn22-97-6-4

perception … ^sn22-97-6-5

choices … ^sn22-97-6-6

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-97-6-7

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn22-97-6-8

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-97-6-9

Seeing this … ^sn22-97-6-10

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-97-6-11

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 22.98) ^sn22-98-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-98-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-98-1-2

“Sir, is there any form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever? ^sn22-98-1-3

Is there any feeling … ^sn22-98-1-4

perception … ^sn22-98-1-5

choices … ^sn22-98-1-6

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever?” ^sn22-98-1-7

“Mendicant, there is no form at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever. ^sn22-98-1-8

There’s no feeling … ^sn22-98-1-9

perception … ^sn22-98-1-10

choices … ^sn22-98-1-11

consciousness at all that’s permanent, everlasting, eternal, imperishable, and will last forever and ever.” ^sn22-98-1-12

### A Leash (Linked Discourses 22.99) ^sn22-99-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-99-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn22-99-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn22-99-1-3

There comes a time when the ocean dries up and evaporates and is no more. ^sn22-99-1-4

But in any case, there is no making an end of suffering for sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving, I say. ^sn22-99-1-5

There comes a time when Sineru the king of mountains is burned up and destroyed, and is no more. ^sn22-99-1-6

But in any case, there is no making an end of suffering for sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving, I say . ^sn22-99-1-7

There comes a time when the great earth is burned up and destroyed, and is no more. ^sn22-99-1-8

But in any case, there is no making an end of suffering for sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving, I say . ^sn22-99-1-9

Suppose a hound on a leash was tethered to a strong post or pillar. It would just keep running and circling around that post or pillar. ^sn22-99-2-1

In the same way, take an unlearned ordinary person who has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in their teaching. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in their teaching. ^sn22-99-2-2

They regard form … ^sn22-99-2-3

feeling … ^sn22-99-2-4

perception … ^sn22-99-2-5

choices … ^sn22-99-2-6

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-99-2-7

They just keep running and circling around form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-99-2-8

Doing so, they’re not freed from form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. They’re not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-99-2-9

They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^sn22-99-2-10

A learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-99-3-1

They don’t regard form … ^sn22-99-3-2

feeling … ^sn22-99-3-3

perception … ^sn22-99-3-4

choices … ^sn22-99-3-5

or consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn22-99-3-6

They don’t keep running and circling around form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-99-3-7

By not doing so, they’re freed from form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. They’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn22-99-3-8

They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-99-3-9

### A Leash (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.100) ^sn22-100-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-100-1-1

“Mendicants, this transmigration has no known beginning. ^sn22-100-1-2

No first point is found of sentient beings roaming and transmigrating, shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving. ^sn22-100-1-3

Suppose a hound on a leash was tethered to a strong post or pillar. Whether walking, standing, sitting, or lying down, it stays right beside that post or pillar. ^sn22-100-1-4

In the same way, an unlearned ordinary person regards form like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn22-100-1-5

They regard feeling … ^sn22-100-1-6

perception … ^sn22-100-1-7

choices … ^sn22-100-1-8

consciousness like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn22-100-1-9

When walking, they walk right beside the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-100-1-10

When standing … ^sn22-100-1-11

sitting … ^sn22-100-1-12

lying down, they lie down right beside the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-100-1-13

So you should regularly check your own mind: ^sn22-100-1-14

‘For a long time this mind has been corrupted by greed, hate, and delusion.’ ^sn22-100-1-15

Sentient beings are corrupted because the mind is corrupted. ^sn22-100-1-16

Sentient beings are purified because the mind is purified. ^sn22-100-1-17

Mendicants, have you seen the painting named ‘Conduct’?” ^sn22-100-2-1

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-100-2-2

“That painting was devised by the mind, but the mind is even more diverse than that. ^sn22-100-2-3

So you should regularly check your own mind: ^sn22-100-2-4

‘For a long time this mind has been corrupted by greed, hate, and delusion.’ ^sn22-100-2-5

Sentient beings are corrupted because the mind is corrupted. ^sn22-100-2-6

Sentient beings are purified because the mind is purified. ^sn22-100-2-7

I don’t see any other order of beings as elaborate as the animal realm. ^sn22-100-3-1

The creatures in the animal realm were devised by the mind, but the mind is even more diverse than that. ^sn22-100-3-2

So you should regularly check your own mind: ^sn22-100-3-3

‘For a long time this mind has been corrupted by greed, hate, and delusion.’ ^sn22-100-3-4

Sentient beings are corrupted because the mind is corrupted. ^sn22-100-3-5

Sentient beings are purified because the mind is purified. ^sn22-100-3-6

Suppose an artist or painter had some dye, red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. And on a polished plank or a wall or a canvas they’d create the form of a woman or a man, whole in its major and minor limbs. ^sn22-100-4-1

In the same way, when an unlearned ordinary person generates a future life, all they generate is form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-100-4-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-100-4-3

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-100-4-4

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-100-4-5

“Is feeling … ^sn22-100-4-6

perception … ^sn22-100-4-7

choices … ^sn22-100-4-8

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” … ^sn22-100-4-9

“So you should truly see … ^sn22-100-4-10

Seeing this … ^sn22-100-4-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-100-4-12

### The Adze (Linked Discourses 22.101) ^sn22-101-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-101-1-1

“Mendicants, I say that the ending of defilements is for one who knows and sees, not for one who does not know or see. ^sn22-101-1-2

For one who knows and sees what? ^sn22-101-1-3

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn22-101-1-4

Such is feeling … ^sn22-101-1-5

Such is perception … ^sn22-101-1-6

Such are choices … ^sn22-101-1-7

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^sn22-101-1-8

The ending of the defilements is for one who knows and sees this. ^sn22-101-1-9

When a mendicant is not committed to development, they might wish: ^sn22-101-2-1

‘If only my mind were freed from the defilements by not grasping!’ Even so, their mind is not freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-101-2-2

Why is that? ^sn22-101-2-3

You should say: ‘It’s because they’re undeveloped.’ ^sn22-101-2-4

Undeveloped in what? ^sn22-101-2-5

Undeveloped in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven awakening factors, and the noble eightfold path. ^sn22-101-2-6

Suppose there was a chicken with eight or ten or twelve eggs. ^sn22-101-3-1

But she had not properly sat on them to keep them warm and incubated. ^sn22-101-3-2

That chicken might wish: ^sn22-101-3-3

‘If only my chicks could break out of the eggshell with their claws and beak and hatch safely!’ ^sn22-101-3-4

But they can’t break out and hatch safely. ^sn22-101-3-5

Why is that? ^sn22-101-3-6

Because that chicken with eight or ten or twelve eggs ^sn22-101-3-7

has not properly sat on them to keep them warm and incubated. ^sn22-101-3-8

In the same way, when a mendicant is not committed to development, they might wish: ^sn22-101-3-9

‘If only my mind was freed from the defilements by not grasping!’ Even so, their mind is not freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-101-3-10

Why is that? ^sn22-101-3-11

You should say: ‘It’s because they’re undeveloped.’ ^sn22-101-3-12

Undeveloped in what? ^sn22-101-3-13

Undeveloped in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven awakening factors, and the noble eightfold path. ^sn22-101-3-14

When a mendicant is committed to development, they might not wish: ^sn22-101-4-1

‘If only my mind were freed from the defilements by not grasping!’ Even so, their mind is freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-101-4-2

Why is that? ^sn22-101-4-3

You should say: ‘It’s because they are developed.’ ^sn22-101-4-4

Developed in what? ^sn22-101-4-5

Developed in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven awakening factors, and the noble eightfold path. ^sn22-101-4-6

Suppose there was a chicken with eight or ten or twelve eggs. ^sn22-101-5-1

And she properly sat on them to keep them warm and incubated. ^sn22-101-5-2

That chicken might not wish: ^sn22-101-5-3

‘If only my chicks could break out of the eggshell with their claws and beak and hatch safely!’ ^sn22-101-5-4

But still they can break out and hatch safely. ^sn22-101-5-5

Why is that? ^sn22-101-5-6

Because that chicken with eight or ten or twelve eggs ^sn22-101-5-7

properly sat on them to keep them warm and incubated. ^sn22-101-5-8

In the same way, when a mendicant is committed to development, they might not wish: ^sn22-101-5-9

‘If only my mind were freed from the defilements by not grasping!’ Even so, their mind is freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn22-101-5-10

Why is that? ^sn22-101-5-11

You should say: ‘It’s because they are developed.’ ^sn22-101-5-12

Developed in what? ^sn22-101-5-13

Developed in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven awakening factors, and the noble eightfold path. ^sn22-101-5-14

Suppose a mason or their apprentice sees the marks of his fingers and thumb on the handle of his adze. ^sn22-101-6-1

They don’t know ^sn22-101-6-2

how much of the handle was worn away today, how much yesterday, and how much previously. ^sn22-101-6-3

They just know what has been worn away. ^sn22-101-6-4

In the same way, when a mendicant is committed to development, they don’t know ^sn22-101-6-5

how much of the defilements were worn away today, how much yesterday, and how much previously. They just know what has been worn away. ^sn22-101-6-6

Suppose there was a sea-faring ship bound together with ropes. For six months they deteriorated in the water. Then in the cold season it was hauled up on dry land, where the ropes were weathered by wind and sun. When the monsoon clouds soaked it with rain, the ropes would readily collapse and rot away. ^sn22-101-6-7

In the same way, when a mendicant is committed to development their fetters readily collapse and rot away.” ^sn22-101-6-8

### The Perception of Impermanence (Linked Discourses 22.102) ^sn22-102-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-102-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated it eliminates all desire for sensual pleasures, for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, and for rebirth in a future life. It eliminates all ignorance and eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-1-2

In the autumn, a farmer ploughing with a large plough shears through all the root networks. ^sn22-102-2-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-2-2

A reed-cutter, having cut the reeds, grabs them at the top and shakes them down, shakes them about, and flails them about. ^sn22-102-3-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-3-2

When the stalk of a bunch of mangoes is cut, all the mangoes attached to the stalk will follow along. ^sn22-102-4-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-4-2

The rafters of a bungalow all lean to the peak, slope to the peak, and meet at the peak, so the peak is said to be the topmost of them all. ^sn22-102-5-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-5-2

Of all kinds of fragrant root, spikenard is said to be the best. ^sn22-102-6-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-6-2

Of all kinds of fragrant heartwood, red sandalwood is said to be the best. ^sn22-102-7-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-7-2

Of all kinds of fragrant flower, jasmine is said to be the best. ^sn22-102-8-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-8-2

All lesser kings are vassals of a wheel-turning monarch, so the wheel-turning monarch is said to be the foremost of them all. ^sn22-102-9-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-9-2

The radiance of all the stars is not worth a sixteenth part of the moon’s radiance, so the moon’s radiance is said to be the best of them all. ^sn22-102-10-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-10-2

In the autumn, the heavens are sunny and cloudless. And as the sun is rising to the firmament, having dispelled all the darkness of space, it shines and glows and radiates. ^sn22-102-11-1

In the same way, when the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated it eliminates all desire for sensual pleasures, for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, and for rebirth in a future life. It eliminates all ignorance and eradicates all conceit ‘I am’. ^sn22-102-11-2

And how is the perception of impermanence developed and cultivated so that … it eradicates all conceit ‘I am’? ^sn22-102-12-1

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn22-102-12-2

Such is feeling … ^sn22-102-12-3

Such is perception … ^sn22-102-12-4

Such are choices … ^sn22-102-12-5

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^sn22-102-12-6

That’s how the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated so that it eliminates all desire for sensual pleasures, for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, and for rebirth in a future life. That’s how it eliminates all ignorance and eradicates all conceit ‘I am’.” ^sn22-102-12-7

### Ends (Linked Discourses 22.103) ^sn22-103-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-103-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four ends. ^sn22-103-1-2

What four? ^sn22-103-1-3

The end that is substantial reality, the end that is the origin of substantial reality, the end that is the cessation of substantial reality, and the end that is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-103-1-4

And what is the end that is substantial reality? ^sn22-103-1-5

It should be said: the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-103-1-6

What five? ^sn22-103-1-7

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-103-1-8

This is called the end that is substantial reality. ^sn22-103-1-9

And what is the end that is the origin of substantial reality? ^sn22-103-2-1

It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure there wherever it alights. That is, ^sn22-103-2-2

craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^sn22-103-2-3

This is called the end that is the origin of substantial reality. ^sn22-103-2-4

And what is the end that is the cessation of substantial reality? ^sn22-103-3-1

It’s the fading away and cessation of that very same craving no residue left behind; giving it away, letting it go, releasing it, and not clinging to it. ^sn22-103-3-2

This is called the end that is the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-103-3-3

And what is the end that is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality? ^sn22-103-4-1

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-103-4-2

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-103-4-3

This is called the end that is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-103-4-4

These are the four ends.” ^sn22-103-4-5

### Suffering (Linked Discourses 22.104) ^sn22-104-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-104-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering. ^sn22-104-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-104-1-3

And what is suffering? ^sn22-104-1-4

It should be said: the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-104-1-5

What five? ^sn22-104-1-6

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-104-1-7

This is called suffering. ^sn22-104-1-8

And what is the origin of suffering? ^sn22-104-1-9

It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure there wherever it alights. That is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^sn22-104-1-10

This is called the origin of suffering. ^sn22-104-1-11

And what is the cessation of suffering? ^sn22-104-1-12

It’s the fading away and cessation of that very same craving with no residue left behind; giving it away, letting it go, releasing it, and not clinging to it. ^sn22-104-1-13

This is called the cessation of suffering. ^sn22-104-1-14

And what is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering? ^sn22-104-1-15

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-104-1-16

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-104-1-17

This is called the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering.” ^sn22-104-1-18

### Substantial Reality (Linked Discourses 22.105) ^sn22-105-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-105-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you substantial reality, the origin of substantial reality, the cessation of substantial reality, and the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-105-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-105-1-3

And what is substantial reality? ^sn22-105-1-4

It should be said: the five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-105-1-5

What five? ^sn22-105-1-6

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-105-1-7

This is called substantial reality. ^sn22-105-1-8

And what is the origin of substantial reality? ^sn22-105-1-9

It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure there wherever it alights. That is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving to continue existence, and craving to end existence. ^sn22-105-1-10

This is called the origin of substantial reality. ^sn22-105-1-11

And what is the cessation of substantial reality? ^sn22-105-1-12

It’s the fading away and cessation of that very same craving no residue left behind; giving it away, letting it go, releasing it, and not clinging to it. ^sn22-105-1-13

This is called the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn22-105-1-14

And what is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality? ^sn22-105-1-15

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn22-105-1-16

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn22-105-1-17

This is called the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality.” ^sn22-105-1-18

### Should Be Completely Understood (Linked Discourses 22.106) ^sn22-106-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-106-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that should be completely understood, complete understanding, and the individual who has completely understood. ^sn22-106-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-106-1-3

And what things should be completely understood? ^sn22-106-1-4

Form, ^sn22-106-1-5

feeling, ^sn22-106-1-6

perception, ^sn22-106-1-7

choices, ^sn22-106-1-8

and consciousness. ^sn22-106-1-9

These are called the things that should be completely understood. ^sn22-106-1-10

And what is complete understanding? ^sn22-106-1-11

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn22-106-1-12

This is called complete understanding. ^sn22-106-1-13

And who is the individual who has completely understood? ^sn22-106-1-14

It should be said: a perfected one, ^sn22-106-1-15

the venerable of such and such name and clan. ^sn22-106-1-16

This is called the individual who has completely understood.” ^sn22-106-1-17

### Ascetics (1st) (Linked Discourses 22.107) ^sn22-107-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-107-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-107-1-2

What five? ^sn22-107-1-3

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-107-1-4

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape … ^sn22-107-1-5

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand …” ^sn22-107-1-6

### Ascetics (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.108) ^sn22-108-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-108-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-108-1-2

What five? ^sn22-108-1-3

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-108-1-4

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape … Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin … ^sn22-108-1-5

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand … Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn22-108-1-6

### A Stream-Enterer (Linked Discourses 22.109) ^sn22-109-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-109-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-109-1-2

What five? ^sn22-109-1-3

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-109-1-4

A noble disciple comes to truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn22-109-1-5

Such a noble disciple is called a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn22-109-1-6

### A Perfected One (Linked Discourses 22.110) ^sn22-110-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-110-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn22-110-1-2

What five? ^sn22-110-1-3

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-110-1-4

A mendicant comes to be freed by not grasping after truly understanding these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn22-110-1-5

Such a mendicant is called a perfected one, with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment.” ^sn22-110-1-6

### Giving Up Desire (Linked Discourses 22.111) ^sn22-111-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-111-1-1

“Mendicants, you should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form. ^sn22-111-1-2

Thus that form will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-111-1-3

You should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn22-111-1-4

perception … ^sn22-111-1-5

choices … ^sn22-111-1-6

consciousness. ^sn22-111-1-7

Thus that consciousness will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn22-111-1-8

### Giving Up Desire (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.112) ^sn22-112-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-112-1-1

“Mendicants, you should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. ^sn22-112-1-2

Thus that form will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn22-112-1-3

You should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn22-112-1-4

perception … ^sn22-112-1-5

choices … ^sn22-112-1-6

consciousness; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. ^sn22-112-1-8

Thus that consciousness will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn22-112-1-9

### Ignorance (Linked Discourses 22.113) ^sn22-113-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-113-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha ^sn22-113-1-2

and said to him: ^sn22-113-1-3

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘ignorance’. ^sn22-113-1-4

What is ignorance? ^sn22-113-1-5

And how is an ignoramus defined?” ^sn22-113-1-6

“Mendicant, it’s when an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t understand form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-113-1-7

They don’t understand feeling … ^sn22-113-1-8

perception … ^sn22-113-1-9

choices … ^sn22-113-1-10

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-113-1-11

This is called ignorance. ^sn22-113-1-12

And this is how an ignoramus is defined.” ^sn22-113-1-13

### Knowledge (Linked Discourses 22.114) ^sn22-114-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-114-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-114-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘knowledge’. ^sn22-114-1-3

What is knowledge? ^sn22-114-1-4

And how is a knowing one defined?” ^sn22-114-1-5

“Mendicant, it’s when a learned noble disciple understands form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-114-1-6

They understand feeling … ^sn22-114-1-7

perception … ^sn22-114-1-8

choices … ^sn22-114-1-9

consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^sn22-114-1-10

This is called knowledge. ^sn22-114-1-11

And this is how a knowing one is defined.” ^sn22-114-1-12

### A Dhamma speaker (Linked Discourses 22.115) ^sn22-115-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-115-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-115-1-2

“Sir, they speak of a ‘Dhamma speaker’. ^sn22-115-1-3

How is a Dhamma speaker defined?” ^sn22-115-1-4

“Mendicant, if a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn22-115-1-5

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn22-115-1-6

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’. ^sn22-115-1-7

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment with feeling … ^sn22-115-1-8

perception … ^sn22-115-1-9

choices … ^sn22-115-1-10

consciousness, for its fading away and cessation, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn22-115-1-11

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn22-115-1-12

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.” ^sn22-115-1-13

### A Dhamma speaker (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.116) ^sn22-116-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-116-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-116-1-2

“Sir, they speak of a ‘Dhamma speaker’. ^sn22-116-1-3

How is a Dhamma speaker defined? ^sn22-116-1-4

How is a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching defined? And how is a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life defined?” ^sn22-116-1-5

“Mendicant, if a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn22-116-1-6

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn22-116-1-7

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’. ^sn22-116-1-8

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment with feeling … ^sn22-116-1-9

perception … ^sn22-116-1-10

choices … ^sn22-116-1-11

consciousness, for its fading away and cessation, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn22-116-1-12

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn22-116-1-13

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.” ^sn22-116-1-14

### Shackles (Linked Discourses 22.117) ^sn22-117-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-117-1-1

“Mendicants, take an unlearned ordinary person who has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-117-1-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-117-1-3

They’re called an unlearned ordinary person who is bound to form, inside and out. They see neither the near shore nor the far shore. They’re born in bonds and die in bonds, and in bonds they go from this world to the next. ^sn22-117-1-4

They regard feeling … ^sn22-117-1-5

perception … ^sn22-117-1-7

choices … ^sn22-117-1-8

consciousness as self. ^sn22-117-1-9

They’re called an unlearned ordinary person who is bound to consciousness, inside and out. They see neither the near shore nor the far shore. They’re born in bonds and die in bonds, and in bonds they go from this world to the next. ^sn22-117-1-10

A learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn22-117-2-1

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn22-117-2-2

They’re called a learned noble disciple who is not bound to form, inside or out. They see the near shore and the far shore. They’re free from suffering, I say. ^sn22-117-2-3

They don’t regard feeling … ^sn22-117-2-4

perception … ^sn22-117-2-5

choices … ^sn22-117-2-6

consciousness as self. ^sn22-117-2-7

They’re called a learned noble disciple who is not bound to consciousness, inside or out. They see the near shore and the far shore. They’re free from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-117-2-8

### Questioning (Linked Discourses 22.118) ^sn22-118-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-118-1-1

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-118-1-2

Do you regard form like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-118-1-3

“No, sir.” ^sn22-118-1-4

“Good, mendicants! ^sn22-118-1-5

Form should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-118-1-6

Do you regard feeling … ^sn22-118-1-7

perception … ^sn22-118-1-8

choices … ^sn22-118-1-9

consciousness like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn22-118-1-10

“No, sir.” ^sn22-118-1-11

“Good, mendicants! ^sn22-118-1-12

Consciousness should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-118-1-13

Seeing this … ^sn22-118-1-14

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-118-1-15

### Questioning (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.119) ^sn22-119-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-119-1-1

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-119-1-2

Do you regard form like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’?” ^sn22-119-1-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-119-1-4

“Good, mendicants! ^sn22-119-1-5

Form should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-119-1-6

Do you regard feeling … ^sn22-119-1-7

perception … ^sn22-119-1-8

choices … ^sn22-119-1-9

consciousness like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’?” ^sn22-119-1-10

“Yes, sir.” ^sn22-119-1-11

“Good, mendicants! ^sn22-119-1-12

Consciousness should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-119-1-13

Seeing this … ^sn22-119-1-14

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-119-1-15

### Things That Tighten Fetters (Linked Discourses 22.120) ^sn22-120-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-120-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that tighten the fetters, and the fetter. ^sn22-120-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-120-1-3

What are the things that tighten the fetters? And what is the fetter? ^sn22-120-1-4

Form is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn22-120-1-5

The desire and greed for it is the fetter. ^sn22-120-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-120-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-120-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-120-1-9

Consciousness is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn22-120-1-10

The desire and greed for it is the fetter. ^sn22-120-1-11

These are called the things that tighten the fetters, and this is the fetter.” ^sn22-120-1-12

### Things That Fuel Grasping (Linked Discourses 22.121) ^sn22-121-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-121-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that fuel grasping, and the grasping. ^sn22-121-1-2

Listen … ^sn22-121-1-3

What are the things that fuel grasping? And what is the grasping? ^sn22-121-1-4

Form is something that fuels grasping. ^sn22-121-1-5

The desire and greed for it is the grasping. ^sn22-121-1-6

Feeling … ^sn22-121-1-7

Perception … ^sn22-121-1-8

Choices … ^sn22-121-1-9

Consciousness is something that fuels grasping. ^sn22-121-1-10

The desire and greed for it is the grasping. ^sn22-121-1-11

These are called the things that fuel grasping, and this is the grasping.” ^sn22-121-1-12

### An Ethical Mendicant (Linked Discourses 22.122) ^sn22-122-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn22-122-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and said: ^sn22-122-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, what things should an ethical mendicant rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-122-1-3

“Reverend Koṭṭhita, an ethical mendicant should rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^sn22-122-1-4

What five? ^sn22-122-1-5

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-122-1-6

An ethical mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^sn22-122-1-7

It’s possible that an ethical mendicant who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of stream-entry.” ^sn22-122-1-8

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant stream-enterer rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-122-2-1

“A mendicant stream-enterer should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-122-2-2

It’s possible that a mendicant stream-enterer who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of once-return.” ^sn22-122-2-3

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant once-returner rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-122-3-1

“A mendicant once-returner should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-122-3-2

It’s possible that a mendicant once-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of non-return.” ^sn22-122-3-3

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant non-returner rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-122-4-1

“A mendicant non-returner should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-122-4-2

It’s possible that a mendicant non-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize perfection.” ^sn22-122-4-3

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a perfected one rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-122-5-1

“Reverend Koṭṭhita, a perfected one should also rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^sn22-122-5-2

A perfected one has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement. ^sn22-122-5-3

Still, these things, when developed and cultivated, lead to blissful meditation in this life, and also to mindfulness and situational awareness.” ^sn22-122-5-4

### Learned (Linked Discourses 22.123) ^sn22-123-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn22-123-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, sat down to one side, and said: ^sn22-123-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a learned mendicant rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-123-2-1

“A learned mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-123-2-2

What five? ^sn22-123-2-3

That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-123-2-4

A learned mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-123-2-5

It’s possible that a learned mendicant who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of stream-entry.” ^sn22-123-2-6

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant stream-enterer rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-123-3-1

“A mendicant stream-enterer should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self. ^sn22-123-3-2

It’s possible that a mendicant stream-enterer who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of once-return.” … ^sn22-123-3-3

“It’s possible that a mendicant once-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of non-return.” … ^sn22-123-3-4

“It’s possible that a mendicant non-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of perfection.” ^sn22-123-3-5

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a perfected one rationally apply the mind to?” ^sn22-123-4-1

“Reverend Koṭṭhita, a perfected one should rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^sn22-123-4-2

A perfected one has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement. ^sn22-123-4-3

Still, these things, when developed and cultivated, lead to blissful meditation in this life, and also to mindfulness and situational awareness.” ^sn22-123-4-4

### With Kappa (Linked Discourses 22.124) ^sn22-124-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-124-1-1

Then Venerable Kappa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn22-124-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs?” ^sn22-124-1-3

“Kappa, one truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-124-2-1

One truly sees any kind of feeling … ^sn22-124-2-2

perception … ^sn22-124-2-3

choices … ^sn22-124-2-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-124-2-5

That’s how to know and see so that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs.” ^sn22-124-2-6

### With Kappa (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.125) ^sn22-125-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-125-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Kappa said to the Buddha: ^sn22-125-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed?” ^sn22-125-1-3

“Kappa, one is freed by not grasping having truly seen any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-125-2-1

One is freed by not grasping having truly seen any kind of feeling … ^sn22-125-2-2

perception … ^sn22-125-2-3

choices … ^sn22-125-2-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn22-125-2-5

That’s how to know and see so that the heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and going beyond discrimination, it’s peaceful and well freed.” ^sn22-125-2-6

### Liable to Originate (Linked Discourses 22.126) ^sn22-126-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-126-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn22-126-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘ignorance’. ^sn22-126-1-3

What is ignorance? ^sn22-126-1-4

And how is an ignoramus defined?” ^sn22-126-1-5

“Mendicant, it’s when an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t truly understand form, which is liable to originate, as form which is liable to originate. ^sn22-126-2-1

They don’t truly understand form, which is liable to vanish, as form which is liable to vanish. ^sn22-126-2-2

They don’t truly understand form, which is liable to originate and vanish, as form which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-126-2-3

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-126-2-4

perception … ^sn22-126-2-7

choices … ^sn22-126-2-8

consciousness, which is liable to originate, as consciousness which is liable to originate. ^sn22-126-2-11

They don’t truly understand consciousness, which is liable to vanish, as consciousness which is liable to vanish. ^sn22-126-2-12

They don’t truly understand consciousness, which is liable to originate and vanish, as consciousness which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-126-2-13

This is called ignorance. ^sn22-126-2-14

And this is how an ignoramus is defined.” ^sn22-126-2-15

When he said this, the mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn22-126-3-1

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘knowledge’. ^sn22-126-3-2

What is knowledge? ^sn22-126-3-3

And how is a knowing one defined?” ^sn22-126-3-4

“Mendicant, it’s when a learned noble disciple truly understands form, which is liable to originate, as form which is liable to originate. ^sn22-126-4-1

They truly understand form, which is liable to vanish, as form which is liable to vanish. ^sn22-126-4-2

They truly understand form, which is liable to originate and vanish, as form which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-126-4-3

They truly understand feeling … ^sn22-126-4-4

perception … ^sn22-126-4-7

choices … ^sn22-126-4-8

consciousness, which is liable to originate, as consciousness which is liable to originate. ^sn22-126-4-11

They truly understand consciousness, which is liable to vanish, as consciousness which is liable to vanish. ^sn22-126-4-12

They truly understand consciousness, which is liable to originate and vanish, as consciousness which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-126-4-13

This is called knowledge. ^sn22-126-4-14

And this is how a knowing one is defined.” ^sn22-126-4-15

### Liable to Originate (2nd) (Linked Discourses 22.127) ^sn22-127-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. … ^sn22-127-1-1

Mahākoṭṭhita said to Sāriputta: ^sn22-127-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, they speak of this thing called ‘ignorance’. ^sn22-127-1-3

What is ignorance? ^sn22-127-1-4

And how is an ignoramus defined?” ^sn22-127-1-5

“Reverend, it’s when an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t truly understand form, which is liable to originate … liable to vanish … liable to originate and vanish, as form which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-127-2-1

They don’t truly understand feeling … ^sn22-127-2-2

perception … ^sn22-127-2-3

choices … ^sn22-127-2-4

consciousness, which is liable to originate … liable to vanish … liable to originate and vanish, as consciousness which is liable to originate and vanish. ^sn22-127-2-5

This is called ignorance. ^sn22-127-2-6

And this is how an ignoramus is defined.” ^sn22-127-2-7

And how is an ignoramus defined?” ^sn22-129-1-5

perception, ^sn22-129-2-3

choices, ^sn22-129-2-4

and consciousness. ^sn22-129-2-5

### Burning Chaff (Linked Discourses 22.136) ^sn22-136-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-136-1-1

“Mendicants, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are burning chaff. ^sn22-136-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-136-1-3

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn22-136-1-4

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn22-136-1-5

### Impermanence (Linked Discourses 22.137) ^sn22-137-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-137-1-1

“Mendicants, you should give up desire for what is impermanent. ^sn22-137-1-2

And what is impermanent? ^sn22-137-1-3

Form is impermanent; you should give up desire for it. ^sn22-137-1-4

Feeling … ^sn22-137-1-5

Perception … ^sn22-137-1-6

Choices … ^sn22-137-1-7

Consciousness is impermanent; you should give up desire for it. ^sn22-137-1-8

You should give up desire for what is impermanent.” ^sn22-137-1-9

### Full of Disillusionment (Linked Discourses 22.146) ^sn22-146-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-146-1-1

“Mendicants, when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith, this is what’s in line with the teaching. ^sn22-146-1-2

They should live full of disillusionment for form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-146-1-3

Living in this way, they completely understand form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn22-146-1-4

Completely understanding form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness, they’re freed from these things. They’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re freed from suffering, I say.” ^sn22-146-1-5

perception, ^sn22-147-1-5

### In Oneself (Linked Discourses 22.150) ^sn22-150-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-150-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what, do pleasure and pain arise in oneself?” ^sn22-150-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn22-150-1-3

“When form exists, because of grasping form, pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^sn22-150-1-4

When feeling … ^sn22-150-1-5

perception … ^sn22-150-1-6

choices … ^sn22-150-1-7

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness, pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^sn22-150-1-8

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-150-1-9

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-150-1-10

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-11

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn22-150-1-12

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-13

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would pleasure and pain arise in oneself?” ^sn22-150-1-14

“No, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-15

“Is feeling … ^sn22-150-1-16

perception … ^sn22-150-1-17

choices … ^sn22-150-1-18

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-150-1-19

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-20

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-22

“No, sir.” ^sn22-150-1-24

### This is Mine (Linked Discourses 22.151) ^sn22-151-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-151-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, ^sn22-151-1-2

perception … ^sn22-151-1-14

### This is My Self (Linked Discourses 22.152) ^sn22-152-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-152-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn22-152-1-2

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable’?” ^sn22-152-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn22-152-1-4

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn22-152-1-5

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable.’ ^sn22-152-1-6

When feeling … ^sn22-152-1-7

perception … ^sn22-152-1-8

choices … ^sn22-152-1-9

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn22-152-1-10

‘The self and the cosmos are one and the same. After death I will be that, permanent, everlasting, eternal, and imperishable.’ ^sn22-152-1-11

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn22-152-2-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-152-2-2

### With Ānanda (Linked Discourses 22.159) ^sn22-159-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn22-159-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn22-159-1-3

“What do you think, Ānanda? ^sn22-159-2-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-159-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn22-159-2-3

“No, sir.” ^sn22-159-2-8

perception … ^sn22-159-2-10

choices … ^sn22-159-2-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn22-159-2-12
