## Saṁyutta 23 ^sn23-0

### About Māra (Linked Discourses 23.1) ^sn23-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-1-1-1

Then Venerable Rādha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn23-1-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘Māra’. ^sn23-1-2-1

How is Māra defined?” ^sn23-1-2-2

“When there is form, Rādha, there would be Māra, or the murderer, or the murdered. ^sn23-1-2-3

So you should see form as Māra, the murderer, the murdered, the diseased, the boil, the dart, the gloom, the gloominess. ^sn23-1-2-4

Those who see it like this see rightly. ^sn23-1-2-5

When there is feeling … ^sn23-1-2-6

perception … ^sn23-1-2-7

choices … ^sn23-1-2-8

consciousness, there may be Māra, or the murderer, or the murdered. ^sn23-1-2-9

So you should see consciousness as Māra, the murderer, the murdered, the diseased, the boil, the dart, the gloom, the gloominess. ^sn23-1-2-10

Those who see it like this see rightly.” ^sn23-1-2-11

“But sir, what’s the purpose of seeing rightly?” ^sn23-1-3-1

“Disillusionment is the purpose of seeing rightly.” ^sn23-1-3-2

“But what’s the purpose of disillusionment?” ^sn23-1-3-3

“Dispassion is the purpose of disillusionment.” ^sn23-1-3-4

“But what’s the purpose of dispassion?” ^sn23-1-3-5

“Freedom is the purpose of dispassion.” ^sn23-1-3-6

“But what’s the purpose of freedom?” ^sn23-1-3-7

“Extinguishment is the purpose of freedom.” ^sn23-1-3-8

“But sir, what is the purpose of extinguishment?” ^sn23-1-3-9

“Your question goes too far, Rādha. You weren’t able to grasp the limit of questioning. ^sn23-1-3-10

For extinguishment is the objective, destination, and culmination of the spiritual life.” ^sn23-1-3-11

### Sentient Beings (Linked Discourses 23.2) ^sn23-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-2-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Rādha said to the Buddha: ^sn23-2-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called a ‘sentient being’. ^sn23-2-1-3

How is a sentient being defined?” ^sn23-2-1-4

“Rādha, when you cling, strongly cling, to desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form, then a being is spoken of. ^sn23-2-1-5

When you cling, strongly cling, to desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn23-2-1-6

perception … ^sn23-2-1-7

choices … ^sn23-2-1-8

consciousness, then a being is spoken of. ^sn23-2-1-9

Suppose some boys or girls were playing with sandcastles. ^sn23-2-2-1

As long as they’re not rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for those sandcastles, they cling to them, dally with them, treasure them, and treat them as their own. ^sn23-2-2-2

But when they are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for those sandcastles, they scatter, smash, and destroy them with their hands and feet, making them unplayable. ^sn23-2-2-3

In the same way, you should scatter, smash, and destroy form, making it unplayable. And you should practice for the ending of craving. ^sn23-2-2-4

You should scatter, smash, and destroy feeling … ^sn23-2-2-5

perception … ^sn23-2-2-6

choices … ^sn23-2-2-7

consciousness, making it unplayable. And you should practice for the ending of craving. ^sn23-2-2-8

For the ending of craving is extinguishment.” ^sn23-2-2-9

### The Leash to Existence (Linked Discourses 23.3) ^sn23-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-3-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Rādha said to the Buddha: ^sn23-3-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘the cessation of the leash to existence’. ^sn23-3-1-3

What is the leash to existence? And what is the cessation of the leash to existence?” ^sn23-3-1-4

“Rādha, any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies— ^sn23-3-1-5

this is called the leash to existence. ^sn23-3-1-6

Their cessation is the cessation of the leash to existence. ^sn23-3-1-7

Any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn23-3-1-8

perception … ^sn23-3-1-9

choices … ^sn23-3-1-10

consciousness; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies— ^sn23-3-1-11

this is called the leash to existence. ^sn23-3-1-12

Their cessation is the cessation of the leash to existence.” ^sn23-3-1-13

### Should Be Completely Understood (Linked Discourses 23.4) ^sn23-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-4-1-1

Then Venerable Rādha went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn23-4-1-2

“Rādha, I will teach you the things that should be completely understood, complete understanding, and the individual who has completely understood. ^sn23-4-2-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn23-4-2-2

“Yes, sir,” Rādha replied. ^sn23-4-2-3

The Buddha said this: ^sn23-4-2-4

“And what things should be completely understood? ^sn23-4-2-5

Form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-4-2-6

These are called the things that should be completely understood. ^sn23-4-2-7

And what is complete understanding? ^sn23-4-2-8

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn23-4-2-9

This is called complete understanding. ^sn23-4-2-10

And what is the individual who has completely understood? ^sn23-4-2-11

It should be said: a perfected one, ^sn23-4-2-12

the venerable of such and such name and clan. ^sn23-4-2-13

This is called the individual who has completely understood.” ^sn23-4-2-14

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Linked Discourses 23.5) ^sn23-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-5-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-5-1-2

“Rādha, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn23-5-1-3

What five? ^sn23-5-1-4

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-5-1-5

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn23-5-1-6

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^sn23-5-1-7

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn23-5-1-8

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn23-5-1-9

### Ascetics and Brahmins (2nd) (Linked Discourses 23.6) ^sn23-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-6-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-6-1-2

“Rādha, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn23-6-1-3

What five? ^sn23-6-1-4

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-6-1-5

There are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape … Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin … ^sn23-6-1-6

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand … Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^sn23-6-1-7

### A Stream-Enterer (Linked Discourses 23.7) ^sn23-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-7-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-7-1-2

“Rādha, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn23-7-1-3

What five? ^sn23-7-1-4

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-7-1-5

When a noble disciple truly understands these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape, ^sn23-7-1-6

they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn23-7-1-7

### A Perfected One (Linked Discourses 23.8) ^sn23-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-8-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-8-1-2

“Rādha, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn23-8-1-3

What five? ^sn23-8-1-4

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-8-1-5

A mendicant comes to be freed by not grasping after truly understanding these five grasping aggregates’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn23-8-1-6

Such a mendicant is called a perfected one, with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment.” ^sn23-8-1-7

### Desire and Greed (Linked Discourses 23.9) ^sn23-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-9-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-9-1-2

“Rādha, you should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form. ^sn23-9-1-3

Thus that form will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn23-9-1-4

You should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn23-9-1-5

perception … ^sn23-9-1-7

choices … ^sn23-9-1-8

consciousness. ^sn23-9-1-10

Thus that consciousness will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn23-9-1-11

### Desire and Greed (2nd) (Linked Discourses 23.10) ^sn23-10-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-10-1-1

When Venerable Rādha was seated to one side, the Buddha said to him: ^sn23-10-1-2

“Rādha, you should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for form; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. ^sn23-10-1-3

Thus that form will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn23-10-1-4

You should give up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for feeling … ^sn23-10-1-5

perception … ^sn23-10-1-7

choices … ^sn23-10-1-8

consciousness; and any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. ^sn23-10-1-10

Thus that consciousness will be given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.” ^sn23-10-1-11

### About Māra (Linked Discourses 23.11) ^sn23-11-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-11-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Rādha said to the Buddha: ^sn23-11-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘Māra’. ^sn23-11-1-3

How is Māra defined?” ^sn23-11-1-4

“Rādha, form is Māra, feeling is Māra, perception is Māra, choices are Māra, consciousness is Māra. ^sn23-11-1-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn23-11-1-6

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn23-11-1-7

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn23-11-1-8

### Subject to Māra (Linked Discourses 23.12) ^sn23-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-12-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Rādha said to the Buddha: ^sn23-12-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘Māra nature’. ^sn23-12-1-3

What is of Māra nature?” ^sn23-12-1-4

“Rādha, form is of Māra nature. Feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are of Māra nature. ^sn23-12-1-5

Seeing this … ^sn23-12-1-6

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn23-12-1-7

### Impermanence (Linked Discourses 23.13) ^sn23-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn23-13-1-1

Seated to one side, Venerable Rādha said to the Buddha: ^sn23-13-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘impermanence’. ^sn23-13-1-3

What has impermanent nature?” ^sn23-13-1-4

“Rādha, form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are impermanent. ^sn23-13-1-5

Seeing this … ^sn23-13-1-6

## Saṁyutta 24 ^sn24-0

### Winds (Linked Discourses 24.1) ^sn24-1-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove. ^sn24-1-1-1

The Buddha said this: ^sn24-1-1-2

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-1-1-3

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar.’?” ^sn24-1-1-4

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn24-1-2-1

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn24-1-2-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn24-1-2-3

The Buddha said this: ^sn24-1-2-4

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn24-1-3-1

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar.’ ^sn24-1-3-2

When feeling … ^sn24-1-3-3

perception … ^sn24-1-3-4

choices … ^sn24-1-3-5

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-1-3-6

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar.’ ^sn24-1-3-7

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-1-3-8

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-1-3-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-1-3-10

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-1-3-11

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-1-3-12

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would the view arise: ^sn24-1-3-13

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar’?” ^sn24-1-3-14

“No, sir.” ^sn24-1-3-15

“Is feeling … ^sn24-1-4-1

perception … ^sn24-1-4-2

choices … ^sn24-1-4-3

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-1-4-4

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-5

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-1-4-6

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-7

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would the view arise: ^sn24-1-4-8

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar’?” ^sn24-1-4-9

“No, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-10

“That which is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-1-4-11

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-12

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-1-4-13

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-14

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would the view arise: ^sn24-1-4-15

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar’?” ^sn24-1-4-16

“No, sir.” ^sn24-1-4-17

“When a noble disciple has given up doubt regarding these six grounds, and has given up doubt in suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation, ^sn24-1-5-1

they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn24-1-5-2

### This is Mine (Linked Discourses 24.2) ^sn24-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-2-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-2-1-2

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn24-2-1-3

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn24-2-1-6

perception … ^sn24-2-1-8

choices … ^sn24-2-1-9

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-2-1-10

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’ ^sn24-2-1-11

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-2-2-3

perception … ^sn24-2-2-5

choices … ^sn24-2-2-6

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-2-2-7

they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn24-2-3-2

### There’s No Meaning in Giving (Linked Discourses 24.5) ^sn24-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-5-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-5-1-2

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight. ^sn24-5-1-3

This person is made up of the four principal states. When they die, the earth in their body progresses and proceeds to the substance of earth. The water in their body progresses and proceeds to the substance of water. The fire in their body progresses and proceeds to the substance of fire. The air in their body progresses and proceeds to the substance of air. ^sn24-5-1-4

The faculties are transferred to space. ^sn24-5-1-5

Four men with a bier carry away the corpse. ^sn24-5-1-6

Their footprints show the way to the cremation ground. ^sn24-5-1-7

The bones become bleached. ^sn24-5-1-8

Offerings dedicated to the gods end in ashes. ^sn24-5-1-9

Giving is a doctrine of morons. ^sn24-5-1-10

When anyone affirms a positive teaching it’s just baseless, false nonsense. ^sn24-5-1-11

Both the foolish and the astute are annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and don’t exist after death’?” ^sn24-5-1-12

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn24-5-1-13

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn24-5-2-1

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. … ^sn24-5-2-2

Both the foolish and the astute are annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and don’t exist after death.’ ^sn24-5-2-3

When feeling … ^sn24-5-2-4

perception … ^sn24-5-2-5

choices … ^sn24-5-2-6

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-5-2-7

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. … ^sn24-5-2-8

Both the foolish and the astute are annihilated and destroyed when their body breaks up, and don’t exist after death.’ ^sn24-5-2-9

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-5-3-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-5-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-5-3-3

“Is feeling … ^sn24-5-3-8

perception … ^sn24-5-3-9

choices … ^sn24-5-3-10

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-5-3-11

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-5-3-12

“That which is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-5-3-17

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-5-3-18

“No, sir.” ^sn24-5-3-23

“When a noble disciple has given up doubt regarding these six grounds, and has given up doubt in suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation, ^sn24-5-4-1

they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn24-5-4-2

### Acting (Linked Discourses 24.6) ^sn24-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-6-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-6-1-2

‘The one who acts does nothing wrong when they punish, mutilate, torture, aggrieve, oppress, intimidate, or when they encourage others to do the same. Nothing bad is done when they kill, steal, break into houses, plunder wealth, steal from isolated buildings, commit highway robbery, commit adultery, and lie. ^sn24-6-1-3

If you were to reduce all the living creatures of this earth to one heap and mass of flesh with a razor-edged chakram, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil. ^sn24-6-1-4

If you were to go along the south bank of the Ganges killing, mutilating, and torturing, and encouraging others to do the same, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil. ^sn24-6-1-5

If you were to go along the north bank of the Ganges giving and sacrificing and encouraging others to do the same, no merit comes of that, and no outcome of merit. ^sn24-6-1-6

In giving, self-control, restraint, and truthfulness there is no merit or outcome of merit’?” ^sn24-6-1-7

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn24-6-1-8

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn24-6-2-1

‘The one who acts does nothing wrong … ^sn24-6-2-2

there is no merit or outcome of merit.’ ^sn24-6-2-3

When feeling … ^sn24-6-2-4

perception … ^sn24-6-2-5

choices … ^sn24-6-2-6

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-6-2-7

‘The one who acts does nothing wrong … ^sn24-6-2-8

there is no merit or outcome of merit.’ ^sn24-6-2-9

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-6-3-1

### Cause (Linked Discourses 24.7) ^sn24-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-7-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-7-1-2

‘There is no cause or reason for the corruption of sentient beings. ^sn24-7-1-3

Sentient beings are corrupted without cause or reason. ^sn24-7-1-4

There’s no cause or reason for the purification of sentient beings. ^sn24-7-1-5

Sentient beings are purified without cause or reason. ^sn24-7-1-6

There is no power, no energy, no human strength or vigor. ^sn24-7-1-7

All sentient beings, all living creatures, all beings, all souls lack control, power, and energy. Molded by destiny, circumstance, and nature, they experience pleasure and pain in the six classes of rebirth’?” ^sn24-7-1-8

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn24-7-1-9

### The Extensive View (Linked Discourses 24.8) ^sn24-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-8-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-8-1-2

‘There are these seven substances that are not made, not derived, not created, without a creator, barren, steady as a mountain peak, standing firm like a pillar. ^sn24-8-1-3

They don’t move or deteriorate or obstruct each other. They’re unable to cause pleasure, pain, or both pleasure and pain to each other. ^sn24-8-1-4

What seven? ^sn24-8-1-5

The substances of earth, water, fire, air; pleasure, pain, and the soul is the seventh. ^sn24-8-1-6

These seven substances are not made, not derived, not created, without a creator, barren, steady as a mountain peak, standing firm like a pillar. ^sn24-8-1-7

They don’t move or deteriorate or obstruct each other. They’re unable to cause pleasure, pain, or both pleasure and pain to each other. ^sn24-8-1-8

If you chop off someone’s head with a sharp sword, you don’t take anyone’s life. ^sn24-8-1-9

The sword simply passes through the gap between the seven substances. ^sn24-8-1-10

There are 1.4 million main wombs, and 6,000, and 600; 500 deeds, and five, and three; deeds and half-deeds; 62 paths of practice, 62 sub-eons, six classes of rebirth, and eight stages in a person’s life; 4,900 Ājīvaka ascetics, 4,900 wanderers, and 4,900 dragon abodes; 2,000 lordships, 3,000 hells, and 36 realms of dusky sky; seven percipient embryos, seven non-percipient embryos, seven knotless embryos, seven gods, seven mental heavens, seven goblins, seven streams, seven castoff incarnations and 700 castoff incarnations, seven downfalls and 700 downfalls, seven dreams and 700 dreams, and 8.4 million great eons—through all of which the foolish and the astute transmigrate before making an end of suffering. ^sn24-8-1-11

And here there is no such thing as this: “By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice I shall force unripened deeds to bear their fruit, or eliminate old deeds by experiencing their results little by little”—for that cannot be. ^sn24-8-1-12

Pleasure and pain are allotted. Transmigration lasts only for a limited period, so there’s no increase or decrease, no getting better or worse. ^sn24-8-1-13

It’s like how, when you toss a ball of string, it rolls away unraveling. ^sn24-8-1-14

In the same way, after transmigrating the foolish and the astute roll away unraveling as far as pleasure and pain extend’?” ^sn24-8-1-15

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn24-8-1-16

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn24-8-2-1

‘There are these seven substances that are not made … ^sn24-8-2-2

the foolish and the astute will make an end of suffering.’ ^sn24-8-2-3

When feeling … ^sn24-8-2-4

perception … ^sn24-8-2-5

choices … ^sn24-8-2-6

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-8-2-7

‘There are these seven substances that are not made … ^sn24-8-2-8

the foolish and the astute will make an end of suffering.’ ^sn24-8-2-9

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-8-3-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-8-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-8-3-3

“That which is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-8-3-8

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn24-8-3-9

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would such a view arise?” ^sn24-8-3-10

“No, sir.” ^sn24-8-3-13

“When a noble disciple has given up doubt regarding these six grounds, and has given up doubt in suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation, ^sn24-8-4-1

they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn24-8-4-2

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-18-1-2

“No, sir.” ^sn24-18-3-6

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-18-3-8

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-18-3-10

“No, sir.” ^sn24-18-3-13

choices … ^sn24-19-2-5

perception … ^sn24-19-3-10

choices … ^sn24-71-2-5

‘Winds don’t blow; rivers don’t flow; pregnant women don’t give birth; the moon and stars neither rise nor set, but stand firm like a pillar.’ ^sn24-71-2-7

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-71-2-8

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-71-2-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-10

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-71-2-11

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-12

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn24-71-2-13

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn24-71-2-14

“No, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-15

“Is feeling … ^sn24-71-2-16

perception … ^sn24-71-2-17

choices … ^sn24-71-2-18

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-71-2-19

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-20

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-71-2-21

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-22

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn24-71-2-23

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn24-71-2-24

“No, sir.” ^sn24-71-2-25

“So you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn24-71-3-1

You should truly see any kind of feeling … ^sn24-71-3-2

perception … ^sn24-71-3-3

choices … ^sn24-71-3-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn24-71-3-5

Seeing this … ^sn24-71-4-1

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn24-71-4-2

### This is Mine, Etc. (Linked Discourses 24.72–95) ^sn24-72-95-0

(Tell in full as in the 24 discourses of the second chapter.) ^sn24-72-95-1-1

### The Self is Neither Happy Nor Suffering (Linked Discourses 24.96) ^sn24-96-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn24-96-1-1

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise: ^sn24-96-1-2

‘The self is neither happy nor suffering, and is healthy after death’?” ^sn24-96-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn24-96-1-4

“When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises: ^sn24-96-2-1

‘The self is neither happy nor suffering, and healthy after death.’ ^sn24-96-2-2

When feeling … ^sn24-96-2-3

perception … ^sn24-96-2-4

choices … ^sn24-96-2-5

consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises: ^sn24-96-2-6

‘The self is neither happy nor suffering, and is healthy after death.’ ^sn24-96-2-7

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn24-96-3-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-96-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-3

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-96-3-4

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-5

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn24-96-3-6

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn24-96-3-7

“No, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-8

“Is feeling … ^sn24-96-3-9

perception … ^sn24-96-3-10

choices … ^sn24-96-3-11

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn24-96-3-12

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-13

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn24-96-3-14

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-15

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn24-96-3-16

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn24-96-3-17

“No, sir.” ^sn24-96-3-18

“So you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn24-96-4-1

You should truly see any kind of feeling … ^sn24-96-4-2

perception … ^sn24-96-4-3

choices … ^sn24-96-4-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn24-96-4-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn24-96-5-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn24-96-5-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn24-96-5-3

The Linked Discourses on views are complete. ^sn24-96-6-5

## Saṁyutta 25 ^sn25-0

### The Eye (Linked Discourses 25.1) ^sn25-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn25-1-1-1

“Mendicants, the eye is impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn25-1-1-2

The ear, ^sn25-1-1-3

nose, ^sn25-1-1-4

tongue, ^sn25-1-1-5

body, ^sn25-1-1-6

and mind are impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn25-1-1-7

Someone who has faith and confidence in these teachings is called a follower by faith. They’ve stepped into the sure path, they’ve stepped into the level of the true person, and they’ve passed beyond the level of the untrue person. ^sn25-1-1-8

They can’t do any deed which would make them be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^sn25-1-1-9

They can’t die without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. ^sn25-1-1-10

Someone who accepts these teachings after deliberating them with a degree of wisdom is called a follower of teachings. They’ve stepped into the sure path, they’ve stepped into the level of the true person, and they’ve passed beyond the level of the untrue person. ^sn25-1-2-1

They can’t do any deed which would make them be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^sn25-1-2-2

They can’t die without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. ^sn25-1-2-3

Someone who understands and sees these principles is called a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn25-1-2-4

### Sights (Linked Discourses 25.2) ^sn25-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn25-2-1-1

“Mendicants, sights are impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn25-2-1-2

Sounds, ^sn25-2-1-3

smells, ^sn25-2-1-4

tastes, ^sn25-2-1-5

touches, ^sn25-2-1-6

and ideas are impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn25-2-1-7

Someone who has faith and confidence in these teachings is called a follower by faith. They’ve stepped into the sure path, they’ve stepped into the level of the true person, and they’ve passed beyond the level of the untrue person. ^sn25-2-1-8

They can’t do any deed which would make them be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^sn25-2-1-9

They can’t die without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. ^sn25-2-1-10

Someone who accepts these teachings after deliberating them with a degree of wisdom is called a follower of teachings. They’ve stepped into the sure path, they’ve stepped into the level of the true person, and they’ve passed beyond the level of the untrue person. ^sn25-2-2-1

They can’t do any deed which would make them be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^sn25-2-2-2

They can’t die without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. ^sn25-2-2-3

Someone who understands and sees these principles is called ^sn25-2-2-4

a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn25-2-2-5

## Saṁyutta 26 ^sn26-0

### The Eye (Linked Discourses 26.1) ^sn26-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn26-1-1-1

“Mendicants, the arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the eye is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn26-1-1-2

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the ear, ^sn26-1-1-3

nose, ^sn26-1-1-4

tongue, ^sn26-1-1-5

body, ^sn26-1-1-6

and mind is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn26-1-1-7

The cessation, settling, and disappearance of the eye is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death. ^sn26-1-1-8

The cessation, settling, and ending of the ear, ^sn26-1-1-9

nose, ^sn26-1-1-10

tongue, ^sn26-1-1-11

body, ^sn26-1-1-12

and mind is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death.” ^sn26-1-1-13

smells, ^sn26-2-1-4

tastes, ^sn26-2-1-5

smells, ^sn26-2-1-10

## Saṁyutta 27 ^sn27-0

### The Eye (Linked Discourses 27.1) ^sn27-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn27-1-1-1

“Mendicants, desire and greed for the eye, ^sn27-1-1-2

ear, ^sn27-1-1-3

nose, ^sn27-1-1-4

tongue, ^sn27-1-1-5

body, ^sn27-1-1-6

or mind is a corruption of the mind. ^sn27-1-1-7

When a mendicant has given up mental corruption in these six cases, their mind inclines to renunciation. ^sn27-1-1-8

A mind imbued with renunciation is said to be fit for work regarding anything that can be realized by insight.” ^sn27-1-1-9

### Sights (Linked Discourses 27.2) ^sn27-2-0

or ideas is a corruption of the mind. ^sn27-2-1-7

At Sāvatthī. ^sn27-10-1-1

“Mendicants, desire and greed for form, feeling, perception, choices, or consciousness is a corruption of the mind. ^sn27-10-1-2

When a mendicant has given up mental corruption in these five cases, their mind inclines to renunciation. ^sn27-10-1-3

A mind imbued with renunciation is said to be fit for work regarding anything that can be realized by insight.” ^sn27-10-1-4

The Linked Discourses on corruptions are complete. ^sn27-10-2-5

## Saṁyutta 28 ^sn28-0

### Born of Seclusion (Linked Discourses 28.1) ^sn28-1-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn28-1-1-1

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn28-1-1-2

He wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation, ^sn28-1-1-3

plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a tree to meditate. ^sn28-1-1-4

Then in the late afternoon, Sāriputta came out of retreat and went to Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn28-1-2-1

Venerable Ānanda saw him coming off in the distance, ^sn28-1-2-2

and said to him: ^sn28-1-2-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn28-1-2-4

What meditation were you practicing today?” ^sn28-1-2-5

“Reverend, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I entered and remained in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn28-1-3-1

But it didn’t occur to me: ^sn28-1-3-2

‘I am entering the first absorption’ or ‘I have entered the first absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the first absorption’.” ^sn28-1-3-3

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn28-1-3-4

That’s why it didn’t occur to you: ^sn28-1-3-5

‘I am entering the first absorption’ or ‘I have entered the first absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the first absorption’.” ^sn28-1-3-6

### Without Placing the Mind (Linked Discourses 28.2) ^sn28-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-2-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta and said to him: ^sn28-2-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn28-2-1-3

What meditation were you practicing today?” ^sn28-2-1-4

“Reverend, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I entered and remained in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn28-2-2-1

But it didn’t occur to me: ^sn28-2-2-2

‘I am entering the second absorption’ or ‘I have entered the second absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the second absorption’.” ^sn28-2-2-3

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn28-2-2-4

That’s why it didn’t occur to you: ^sn28-2-2-5

‘I am entering the second absorption’ or ‘I have entered the second absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the second absorption’.” ^sn28-2-2-6

### Rapture (Linked Discourses 28.3) ^sn28-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-3-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta and said to him: ^sn28-3-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn28-3-1-3

What meditation were you practicing today?” ^sn28-3-1-4

“Reverend, with the fading away of rapture, I entered and remained in the third absorption, where I meditated with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^sn28-3-2-1

But it didn’t occur to me: ^sn28-3-2-2

‘I am entering the third absorption’ or ‘I have entered the third absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the third absorption’.” ^sn28-3-2-3

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn28-3-2-4

That’s why it didn’t occur to you: ^sn28-3-2-5

‘I am entering the third absorption’ or ‘I have entered the third absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the third absorption’.” ^sn28-3-2-6

### Equanimity (Linked Discourses 28.4) ^sn28-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-4-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta and said to him: ^sn28-4-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn28-4-1-3

What meditation were you practicing today?” ^sn28-4-1-4

“Reverend, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, I entered and remained in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^sn28-4-2-1

But it didn’t occur to me: ^sn28-4-2-2

‘I am entering the fourth absorption’ or ‘I have entered the fourth absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the fourth absorption’.” ^sn28-4-2-3

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn28-4-2-4

That’s why it didn’t occur to you: ^sn28-4-2-5

‘I am entering the fourth absorption’ or ‘I have entered the fourth absorption’ or ‘I am emerging from the fourth absorption’.” ^sn28-4-2-6

### The Dimension of Infinite Space (Linked Discourses 28.5) ^sn28-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-5-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta … ^sn28-5-1-2

“Reverend, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite space. …” … ^sn28-5-1-3

### The Dimension of Infinite Consciousness (Linked Discourses 28.6) ^sn28-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-6-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta … ^sn28-6-1-2

“Reverend, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite consciousness. …” … ^sn28-6-1-3

### The Dimension of Nothingness (Linked Discourses 28.7) ^sn28-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-7-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta … ^sn28-7-1-2

“Reverend, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, I entered and remained in the dimension of nothingness. …” … ^sn28-7-1-3

### The Dimension of Neither Perception Nor Non-Perception (Linked Discourses 28.8) ^sn28-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-8-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta … ^sn28-8-1-2

“Reverend, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, I entered and remained in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. …” … ^sn28-8-1-3

### The Attainment of Cessation (Linked Discourses 28.9) ^sn28-9-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn28-9-1-1

Venerable Ānanda saw Venerable Sāriputta … ^sn28-9-1-2

“Reverend, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, I entered and remained in the cessation of perception and feeling. ^sn28-9-1-3

But it didn’t occur to me: ^sn28-9-1-4

‘I am entering the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I have entered the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I am emerging from the cessation of perception and feeling’.” ^sn28-9-1-5

“That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn28-9-1-6

That’s why it didn’t occur to you: ^sn28-9-1-7

‘I am entering the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I have entered the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I am emerging from the cessation of perception and feeling’.” ^sn28-9-1-8

### With Sucimukhī (Linked Discourses 28.10) ^sn28-10-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn28-10-1-1

Then he robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms. ^sn28-10-1-2

After wandering indiscriminately for almsfood in Rājagaha, he ate his almsfood by a wall. ^sn28-10-1-3

Then the wanderer Sucimukhī went up to Venerable Sāriputta and said to him: ^sn28-10-1-4

“Ascetic, do you eat facing downwards?” ^sn28-10-2-1

“No, sister.” ^sn28-10-2-2

“Well then, do you eat facing upwards?” ^sn28-10-2-3

“No, sister.” ^sn28-10-2-4

“Well then, do you eat facing the cardinal directions?” ^sn28-10-2-5

“No, sister.” ^sn28-10-2-6

“Well then, do you eat facing the intermediate directions?” ^sn28-10-2-7

“No, sister.” ^sn28-10-2-8

“When asked if you eat facing all these directions, you answer ‘no, sister’. ^sn28-10-3-1

How then do you eat, ascetic?” ^sn28-10-4-1

“Sister, those ascetics and brahmins who earn a living by geomancy—a low lore, a wrong livelihood—are said to eat facing downwards. ^sn28-10-4-2

Those ascetics and brahmins who earn a living by astrology—a low lore, a wrong livelihood—are said to eat facing upwards. ^sn28-10-4-3

Those ascetics and brahmins who earn a living by running errands and messages—a wrong livelihood—are said to eat facing the cardinal directions. ^sn28-10-4-4

Those ascetics and brahmins who earn a living by limb-reading—a low lore, a wrong livelihood—are said to eat facing the intermediate directions. ^sn28-10-4-5

I don’t earn a living by any of these means. ^sn28-10-5-1

I seek alms in a principled manner, ^sn28-10-5-2

and I eat it in a principled manner.” ^sn28-10-5-3

Then Sucimukhī the wanderer went around Rājagaha from street to street and from square to square, and announced: ^sn28-10-6-1

“The ascetics who follow the Sakyan eat food in a principled manner! ^sn28-10-6-2

The ascetics who follow the Sakyan eat food blamelessly! ^sn28-10-6-3

Give almsfood to the ascetics who follow the Sakyan!” ^sn28-10-6-4

The Linked Discourses on Sāriputta are complete. ^sn28-10-7-7

## Saṁyutta 29 ^sn29-0

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 29.1) ^sn29-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-1-1-1

“Mendicants, dragons reproduce in these four ways. ^sn29-1-1-2

What four? ^sn29-1-1-3

Dragons are born from eggs, from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn29-1-1-4

These are the four ways that dragons reproduce.” ^sn29-1-1-5

### Better (Linked Discourses 29.2) ^sn29-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-2-1-1

“Mendicants, dragons reproduce in these four ways. ^sn29-2-1-2

What four? ^sn29-2-1-3

Dragons are born from an egg, from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn29-2-1-4

Of these, dragons born from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously are better than those born from an egg. ^sn29-2-1-5

Dragons born from moisture or spontaneously are better than those born from an egg or from a womb. ^sn29-2-1-6

Dragons born spontaneously are better than those born from an egg, from a womb, or from moisture. ^sn29-2-1-7

These are the four ways that dragons reproduce.” ^sn29-2-1-8

### Sabbath (Linked Discourses 29.3) ^sn29-3-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn29-3-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn29-3-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why some egg-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies?” ^sn29-3-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when some egg-born dragons think: ^sn29-3-2-1

‘In the past we did both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn29-3-2-2

When the body broke up, after death, we were reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons. ^sn29-3-2-3

If today we do good things by body, speech, and mind, when the body breaks up, after death, we may be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn29-3-2-4

Come, let us do good things by way of body, speech, and mind.’ ^sn29-3-2-5

This is the cause, this is the reason why some egg-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies.” ^sn29-3-2-6

### Sabbath (2nd) (Linked Discourses 29.4) ^sn29-4-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-4-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn29-4-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why some womb-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies?” ^sn29-4-1-3

(Tell all in full.) ^sn29-4-1-4

### Sabbath (3rd) (Linked Discourses 29.5) ^sn29-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-5-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-5-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why some moisture-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies?” ^sn29-5-1-3

(Tell all in full.) ^sn29-5-1-4

### Sabbath (4th) (Linked Discourses 29.6) ^sn29-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-6-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-6-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why some spontaneously-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies?” ^sn29-6-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when some spontaneously-born dragons think: ^sn29-6-2-1

‘In the past we did both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn29-6-2-2

When the body broke up, after death, we were reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons. ^sn29-6-2-3

If today we do good things by body, speech, and mind, when the body breaks up, after death, we may be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn29-6-2-4

Come, let us do good things by way of body, speech, and mind.’ ^sn29-6-2-5

This is the cause, this is the reason why some spontaneously-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies.” ^sn29-6-2-6

### They’ve Heard (Linked Discourses 29.7) ^sn29-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-7-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-7-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons?” ^sn29-7-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn29-7-2-1

And they’ve heard: ^sn29-7-2-2

‘The egg-born dragons are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn29-7-2-3

They think: ^sn29-7-2-4

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons!’ ^sn29-7-2-5

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons. ^sn29-7-2-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons.” ^sn29-7-2-7

### They’ve Heard (2nd) (Linked Discourses 29.8) ^sn29-8-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-8-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-8-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the womb-born dragons?” ^sn29-8-1-3

(Tell all in full.) ^sn29-8-1-4

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-9-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-9-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the moisture-born dragons?” ^sn29-9-1-3

(Tell all in full.) ^sn29-9-1-4

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-10-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-10-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons?” ^sn29-10-1-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons. ^sn29-10-2-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons.” ^sn29-10-2-7

### Ten Discourses On How Giving Helps to Become Egg-Born (Linked Discourses 29.11–20) ^sn29-11-20-0

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-11-20-1-1

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons?” ^sn29-11-20-1-2

And they’ve heard: ^sn29-11-20-2-2

‘The egg-born dragons are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn29-11-20-2-3

They think: ^sn29-11-20-2-4

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons!’ ^sn29-11-20-2-5

drink … ^sn29-11-20-2-9

clothing … ^sn29-11-20-2-10

a vehicle … ^sn29-11-20-2-11

a garland … ^sn29-11-20-2-12

fragrance … ^sn29-11-20-2-13

makeup … ^sn29-11-20-2-14

a bed … ^sn29-11-20-2-15

a house … ^sn29-11-20-2-16

a lamp. ^sn29-11-20-2-17

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons. ^sn29-11-20-2-18

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born dragons.” ^sn29-11-20-2-19

### Thirty Discourses On How Giving Helps to Become Womb-Born, Etc. (Linked Discourses 29.21–50) ^sn29-21-50-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn29-21-50-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn29-21-50-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the womb-born dragons … ^sn29-21-50-1-3

spontaneously-born dragons?” ^sn29-21-50-1-5

They think: ^sn29-21-50-2-4

drink … ^sn29-21-50-2-7

a lamp. ^sn29-21-50-2-8

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons. ^sn29-21-50-2-9

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born dragons.” ^sn29-21-50-2-10

(Each set of ten discourses of this series should be told in full the same way.) ^sn29-21-50-3-1

The Linked Discourses on dragons are complete. ^sn29-21-50-4-7

## Saṁyutta 30 ^sn30-0

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 30.1) ^sn30-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-1-1-1

“Mendicants, phoenixes reproduce in these four ways. ^sn30-1-1-2

What four? ^sn30-1-1-3

Phoenixes are born from eggs, from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn30-1-1-4

These are the four ways that phoenixes reproduce.” ^sn30-1-1-5

### They Carry Off (Linked Discourses 30.2) ^sn30-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-2-1-1

“Mendicants, phoenixes reproduce in these four ways. ^sn30-2-1-2

What four? ^sn30-2-1-3

Phoenixes are born from eggs, from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn30-2-1-4

These are the four ways that phoenixes reproduce. ^sn30-2-1-5

Of these, phoenixes born from an egg can only carry off dragons born from an egg, not those born from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn30-2-1-6

Phoenixes born from a womb can carry off dragons born from an egg or from a womb, but not those born from moisture or spontaneously. ^sn30-2-1-7

Phoenixes born from moisture can carry off dragons born from an egg, from a womb, or from moisture, but not those born spontaneously. ^sn30-2-1-8

Phoenixes born spontaneously can carry off dragons born from an egg, from a womb, from moisture, or spontaneously. ^sn30-2-1-9

These are the four ways that phoenixes reproduce.” ^sn30-2-1-10

### Both Kinds of Deeds (Linked Discourses 30.3) ^sn30-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-3-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn30-3-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes?” ^sn30-3-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn30-3-1-4

And they’ve heard: ^sn30-3-1-5

‘The egg-born phoenixes are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn30-3-1-6

They think: ^sn30-3-1-7

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes!’ ^sn30-3-1-8

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes. ^sn30-3-1-9

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes.” ^sn30-3-1-10

### Both Kinds of Deeds (2nd–4th) (Linked Discourses 30.4–6) ^sn30-4-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-4-6-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn30-4-6-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the womb-born phoenixes …” ^sn30-4-6-1-3

“… moisture-born phoenixes …” ^sn30-4-6-1-4

“… or spontaneously-born phoenixes?” ^sn30-4-6-1-5

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn30-4-6-1-6

And they’ve heard: ^sn30-4-6-1-7

‘The spontaneously-born phoenixes are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn30-4-6-1-8

They think: ^sn30-4-6-1-9

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born phoenixes!’ ^sn30-4-6-1-10

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born phoenixes. ^sn30-4-6-1-11

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the spontaneously-born phoenixes.” ^sn30-4-6-1-12

### Ten Discourses On How Giving Helps to Become Egg-Born (Linked Discourses 30.7–16) ^sn30-7-16-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-7-16-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn30-7-16-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes?” ^sn30-7-16-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does both kinds of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn30-7-16-1-4

And they’ve heard: ^sn30-7-16-1-5

‘The egg-born phoenixes are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn30-7-16-1-6

They think: ^sn30-7-16-1-7

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes!’ ^sn30-7-16-1-8

They give food …” ^sn30-7-16-1-9

“… drink …” ^sn30-7-16-1-10

“… clothing …” ^sn30-7-16-1-11

“… a vehicle …” ^sn30-7-16-1-12

“… a garland …” ^sn30-7-16-1-13

“… fragrance …” ^sn30-7-16-1-14

“… makeup …” ^sn30-7-16-1-15

“… a bed …” ^sn30-7-16-1-16

“… a house …” ^sn30-7-16-1-17

“… a lamp. ^sn30-7-16-1-18

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes. ^sn30-7-16-1-19

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the egg-born phoenixes.” ^sn30-7-16-1-20

### How Giving Helps to Become Womb-Born, Etc. (Linked Discourses 30.17–46) ^sn30-17-46-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn30-17-46-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn30-17-46-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the womb-born phoenixes … ^sn30-17-46-1-3

moisture-born phoenixes … ^sn30-17-46-1-4

or spontaneously-born phoenixes?” ^sn30-17-46-1-5

(Tell all in full.) ^sn30-17-46-1-17

The Linked Discourses on phoenixes are complete. ^sn30-17-46-2-5

## Saṁyutta 31 ^sn31-0

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 31.1) ^sn31-1-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. … ^sn31-1-1-1

The Buddha said this: ^sn31-1-1-2

“Mendicants, I will teach you about the gods of the centaur realm. ^sn31-1-1-3

Listen … ^sn31-1-1-4

And what are the gods of the centaur realm? ^sn31-1-1-5

There are gods who live in fragrant roots, ^sn31-1-1-6

fragrant heartwood, ^sn31-1-1-7

fragrant softwood, ^sn31-1-1-8

fragrant bark, ^sn31-1-1-9

fragrant shoots, ^sn31-1-1-10

fragrant leaves, ^sn31-1-1-11

fragrant flowers, ^sn31-1-1-12

fragrant fruit, ^sn31-1-1-13

fragrant sap, ^sn31-1-1-14

and fragrant scents. ^sn31-1-1-15

These are called the gods of the centaur realm.” ^sn31-1-1-16

### Good Conduct (Linked Discourses 31.2) ^sn31-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn31-2-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn31-2-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods of the centaur realm?” ^sn31-2-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn31-2-1-4

And they’ve heard: ^sn31-2-1-5

‘The gods of the centaur realm are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn31-2-1-6

They think: ^sn31-2-1-7

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods of the centaur realm!’ ^sn31-2-1-8

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the centaur realm. ^sn31-2-1-9

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods of the centaur realm.” ^sn31-2-1-10

### A Giver of Fragrant Roots (Linked Discourses 31.3) ^sn31-3-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn31-3-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn31-3-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods who live in fragrant roots?” ^sn31-3-1-3

“Mendicant, it’s when someone does good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn31-3-1-4

And they’ve heard: ^sn31-3-1-5

‘The gods who live in fragrant roots are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^sn31-3-1-6

They think: ^sn31-3-1-7

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods who live in fragrant roots!’ ^sn31-3-1-8

They give gifts of fragrant roots. ^sn31-3-1-9

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods who live in fragrant roots. ^sn31-3-1-10

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods who live in fragrant roots.” ^sn31-3-1-11

fragrant scents. ^sn31-4-12-1-33

## Saṁyutta 32 ^sn32-0

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 32.1) ^sn32-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-1-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you about the gods of the thundercloud host. ^sn32-1-1-2

Listen … ^sn32-1-1-3

And what are the gods of the thundercloud host? ^sn32-1-1-4

There are gods of the cool thunderclouds, ^sn32-1-1-5

warm thunderclouds, ^sn32-1-1-6

stormy thunderclouds, ^sn32-1-1-7

windy thunderclouds, ^sn32-1-1-8

and rainy thunderclouds. ^sn32-1-1-9

These are called gods of the thundercloud host.” ^sn32-1-1-10

They think: ^sn32-2-1-7

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods of the thundercloud host!’ ^sn32-2-1-8

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the thundercloud host. ^sn32-2-1-9

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods of the thundercloud host.” ^sn32-2-1-10

a lamp. ^sn32-3-12-1-10

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of cool thunderclouds. ^sn32-3-12-1-11

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods of cool thunderclouds.” ^sn32-3-12-1-12

windy thunderclouds … ^sn32-13-52-1-5

rainy thunderclouds?” ^sn32-13-52-1-6

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of rainy thunderclouds. ^sn32-13-52-1-14

### Gods of the Cool Thunderclouds (Linked Discourses 32.53) ^sn32-53-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-53-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn32-53-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes it becomes cool?” ^sn32-53-1-3

“Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the cool thunderclouds. ^sn32-53-1-4

Sometimes they think: ^sn32-53-1-5

‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, it becomes cool. ^sn32-53-1-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes it becomes cool.” ^sn32-53-1-7

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-54-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn32-54-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes it becomes warm?” ^sn32-54-1-3

“Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the warm thunderclouds. ^sn32-54-1-4

Sometimes they think: ^sn32-54-1-5

‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, it becomes warm. ^sn32-54-1-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes it becomes warm.” ^sn32-54-1-7

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-55-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn32-55-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes it becomes stormy?” ^sn32-55-1-3

“Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the stormy thunderclouds. ^sn32-55-1-4

Sometimes they think: ^sn32-55-1-5

‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, it becomes stormy. ^sn32-55-1-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes it becomes stormy.” ^sn32-55-1-7

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-56-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn32-56-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes it becomes windy?” ^sn32-56-1-3

“Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the windy thunderclouds. ^sn32-56-1-4

Sometimes they think: ^sn32-56-1-5

‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, it becomes windy. ^sn32-56-1-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes it becomes windy.” ^sn32-56-1-7

### Gods of the Rainy Thunderclouds (Linked Discourses 32.57) ^sn32-57-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn32-57-1-1

Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha: ^sn32-57-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes the heavens rain?” ^sn32-57-1-3

“Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the rainy thunderclouds. ^sn32-57-1-4

Sometimes they think: ^sn32-57-1-5

‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, the heavens rain down. ^sn32-57-1-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes the heavens rain.” ^sn32-57-1-7

The Linked Discourses on gods of the clouds are complete. ^sn32-57-2-5

## Saṁyutta 33 ^sn33-0

### Not Knowing Form (Linked Discourses 33.1) ^sn33-1-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn33-1-1-1

Then the wanderer Vacchagotta went up to the Buddha and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn33-1-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^sn33-1-1-3

“What is the cause, worthy Gotama, what is the reason why these various misconceptions arise in the world? That is: ^sn33-1-1-4

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn33-1-1-5

“Vaccha, it is because of not knowing form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation ^sn33-1-1-6

that these various misconceptions arise in the world. ^sn33-1-1-7

This is the cause, this is the reason.” ^sn33-1-1-10

### Not Knowing Feeling (Linked Discourses 33.2) ^sn33-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn33-2-1-1

Then the wanderer Vacchagotta said to the Buddha: ^sn33-2-1-2

“What is the cause, worthy Gotama, what is the reason why these various misconceptions arise in the world? That is: ^sn33-2-1-3

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal … ^sn33-2-1-4

after death, a realized one neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn33-2-1-5

“Vaccha, it is because of not knowing feeling, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation ^sn33-2-1-6

that these various misconceptions arise in the world. ^sn33-2-1-7

This is the cause, this is the reason.” ^sn33-2-1-10

This is the cause, this is the reason.” ^sn33-55-1-6

The Linked Discourses with Vacchagotta are complete. ^sn33-55-2-7

## Saṁyutta 34 ^sn34-0

### Entering Immersion (Linked Discourses 34.1) ^sn34-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn34-1-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four meditators. ^sn34-1-1-2

What four? ^sn34-1-1-3

One meditator is skilled in immersion but not in entering it. ^sn34-1-1-4

One meditator is not skilled in immersion but is skilled in entering it. ^sn34-1-1-5

One meditator is skilled neither in immersion nor in entering it. ^sn34-1-1-6

One meditator is skilled both in immersion and in entering it. ^sn34-1-1-7

Of these, the meditator skilled in immersion and in entering it is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the four. ^sn34-1-1-8

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^sn34-1-1-9

In the same way, the meditator skilled in immersion and entering it is the foremost, best, leading, highest, and finest of the four.” ^sn34-1-1-10

### Remaining in Immersion (Linked Discourses 34.2) ^sn34-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn34-2-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four meditators. ^sn34-2-1-2

What four? ^sn34-2-1-3

One meditator is skilled in immersion but not in remaining in it. ^sn34-2-1-4

One meditator is skilled in remaining in immersion but is not skilled in immersion. ^sn34-2-1-5

One meditator is skilled neither in immersion nor in remaining in it. ^sn34-2-1-6

One meditator is skilled both in immersion and in remaining in it. ^sn34-2-1-7

Of these, the meditator skilled in immersion and in remaining in it is the foremost, best, leading, highest, and finest of the four. ^sn34-2-1-8

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^sn34-2-1-9

In the same way, the meditator skilled in immersion and remaining in it is the foremost, best, leading, highest, and finest of the four.” ^sn34-2-1-10

What four? ^sn34-3-1-3
