### Persistence and What’s Conducive (Linked Discourses 34.55) ^sn34-55-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn34-55-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four meditators. ^sn34-55-1-2

What four? ^sn34-55-1-3

One meditator is skilled in practicing persistently for immersion but not in doing what’s conducive to it. ^sn34-55-1-4

One meditator is skilled in doing what’s conducive to immersion but not in practicing persistently for it. ^sn34-55-1-5

One meditator is skilled neither in practicing persistently for immersion nor in doing what’s conducive to it. ^sn34-55-1-6

One meditator is skilled both in practicing persistently for immersion and in doing what’s conducive to it. ^sn34-55-1-7

Of these, the meditator skilled both in practicing persistently for immersion and in doing what’s conducive to it is the foremost, best, leading, highest, and finest of the four. ^sn34-55-1-8

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^sn34-55-1-9

In the same way, the meditator skilled both in practicing persistently for immersion and in doing what’s conducive to it is the foremost, best, leading, highest, and finest of the four.” ^sn34-55-1-10

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn34-55-1-11

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^sn34-55-1-12

(All should be told in full as per the fifty-fifth discourse.) ^sn34-55-1-14

The Linked Discourses on Absorption are complete. ^sn34-55-2-5

The Book of the Aggregates is finished. ^sn34-55-3-7

## Saṁyutta 35 ^sn35-0

### The Interior as Impermanent (Linked Discourses 35.1) ^sn35-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn35-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn35-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^sn35-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^sn35-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^sn35-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn35-1-1-6

“Mendicants, the eye is impermanent. ^sn35-1-2-1

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn35-1-2-2

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-1-2-3

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-1-2-4

The ear is impermanent. … ^sn35-1-2-5

The nose is impermanent. … ^sn35-1-2-7

The tongue is impermanent. … ^sn35-1-2-9

The body is impermanent. … ^sn35-1-2-13

The mind is impermanent. ^sn35-1-2-15

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn35-1-2-16

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-1-2-17

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-1-2-18

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-1-2-19

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-1-2-20

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-1-2-21

### The Interior as Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.2) ^sn35-2-0

“Mendicants, the eye is suffering. ^sn35-2-1-1

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-2-1-2

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-2-1-3

The ear, ^sn35-2-1-4

nose, ^sn35-2-1-5

tongue, ^sn35-2-1-6

body, ^sn35-2-1-7

and mind are suffering. ^sn35-2-1-8

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-2-1-9

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-2-1-10

Seeing this … ^sn35-2-1-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-2-1-12

### The Interior as Not-Self (Linked Discourses 35.3) ^sn35-3-0

“Mendicants, the eye is not-self. ^sn35-3-1-1

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-3-1-2

The ear, ^sn35-3-1-3

nose, ^sn35-3-1-4

tongue, ^sn35-3-1-5

body, ^sn35-3-1-6

and mind are not-self. ^sn35-3-1-7

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-3-1-8

Seeing this … ^sn35-3-1-9

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-3-1-10

### The Exterior as Impermanent (Linked Discourses 35.4) ^sn35-4-0

“Mendicants, sights are impermanent. ^sn35-4-1-1

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn35-4-1-2

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-4-1-3

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-4-1-4

Sounds, ^sn35-4-1-5

smells, ^sn35-4-1-6

tastes, ^sn35-4-1-7

touches, ^sn35-4-1-8

and ideas are impermanent. ^sn35-4-1-9

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn35-4-1-10

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-4-1-11

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-4-1-12

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^sn35-4-1-13

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-4-1-14

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-4-1-15

### The Exterior as Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.5) ^sn35-5-0

“Mendicants, sights are suffering. ^sn35-5-1-1

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-5-1-2

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ …” ^sn35-5-1-3

### The Exterior as Not-Self (Linked Discourses 35.6) ^sn35-6-0

“Mendicants, sights are not-self. ^sn35-6-1-1

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ …” ^sn35-6-1-2

### The Interior as Impermanent in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.7) ^sn35-7-0

“Mendicants, the eye of the past and future is impermanent, ^sn35-7-1-1

let alone the present. ^sn35-7-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with the eye of the past, ^sn35-7-1-3

they don’t look forward to enjoying the eye in the future, ^sn35-7-1-4

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye in the present. ^sn35-7-1-5

The ear … ^sn35-7-1-6

nose … ^sn35-7-1-7

tongue … ^sn35-7-1-8

body … ^sn35-7-1-13

mind of the past and future is impermanent, ^sn35-7-1-14

let alone the present. ^sn35-7-1-15

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple doesn’t worry about the mind of the past, ^sn35-7-1-16

they don’t look forward to enjoying the mind in the future, ^sn35-7-1-17

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the mind in the present.” ^sn35-7-1-18

### The Interior as Suffering in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.8) ^sn35-8-0

“Mendicants, the eye of the past and future is suffering, ^sn35-8-1-1

let alone the present. ^sn35-8-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with the eye of the past, ^sn35-8-1-3

they don’t look forward to enjoying the eye in the future, ^sn35-8-1-4

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye in the present. …” ^sn35-8-1-5

### The Interior as Not-Self in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.9) ^sn35-9-0

“Mendicants, the eye of the past and future is not-self, ^sn35-9-1-1

let alone the present. ^sn35-9-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple is not concerned with the eye of the past, ^sn35-9-1-3

they don’t look forward to enjoying the eye in the future, ^sn35-9-1-4

and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye in the present. …” ^sn35-9-1-5

### The Exterior as Impermanent in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.10) ^sn35-10-0

“Mendicants, sights of the past and future are impermanent, ^sn35-10-1-1

let alone the present. …” ^sn35-10-1-2

### The Exterior as Suffering in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.11) ^sn35-11-0

“Mendicants, sights of the past and future are suffering, ^sn35-11-1-1

let alone the present. …” ^sn35-11-1-2

### The Exterior as Not-Self in the Three Times (Linked Discourses 35.12) ^sn35-12-0

“Mendicants, sights of the past and future are not-self, ^sn35-12-1-1

let alone the present. …” ^sn35-12-1-2

### Before My Awakening (Interior) (Linked Discourses 35.13) ^sn35-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-13-1-1

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn35-13-1-2

‘What’s the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to the eye … ^sn35-13-1-3

ear … ^sn35-13-1-4

nose … ^sn35-13-1-5

tongue … ^sn35-13-1-6

body … ^sn35-13-1-7

and mind?’ ^sn35-13-1-8

Then it occurred to me: ^sn35-13-1-9

‘The pleasure and happiness that arise from the eye: this is its gratification. ^sn35-13-1-10

That the eye is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn35-13-1-11

Removing and giving up desire and greed for the eye: this is its escape. ^sn35-13-1-12

The pleasure and happiness that arise from the ear … ^sn35-13-1-13

nose … ^sn35-13-1-14

tongue … ^sn35-13-1-15

body … ^sn35-13-1-18

mind: this is its gratification. ^sn35-13-1-19

That the mind is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^sn35-13-1-20

Removing and giving up desire and greed for the mind: this is its escape.’ ^sn35-13-1-21

As long as I didn’t truly understand these six interior sense fields’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn35-13-2-1

But when I did truly understand these six interior sense fields’ gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^sn35-13-2-2

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn35-13-2-3

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn35-13-2-4

### Before My Awakening (Exterior) (Linked Discourses 35.14) ^sn35-14-0

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn35-14-1-1

‘What’s the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to sights … ^sn35-14-1-2

sounds … ^sn35-14-1-3

smells … ^sn35-14-1-4

tastes … ^sn35-14-1-5

touches … ^sn35-14-1-6

and ideas?’ …” ^sn35-14-1-7

### In Search of Gratification (Interior) (Linked Discourses 35.15) ^sn35-15-0

“Mendicants, I went in search of the eye’s gratification, ^sn35-15-1-1

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-2

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the eye’s gratification. ^sn35-15-1-3

I went in search of the eye’s drawback, ^sn35-15-1-4

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-5

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the eye’s drawback. ^sn35-15-1-6

I went in search of escape from the eye, ^sn35-15-1-7

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-8

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of escape from the eye. ^sn35-15-1-9

I went in search of the ear’s … ^sn35-15-1-10

nose’s … ^sn35-15-1-11

tongue’s … ^sn35-15-1-12

body’s … ^sn35-15-1-20

mind’s gratification, ^sn35-15-1-21

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-22

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the mind’s gratification. ^sn35-15-1-23

I went in search of the mind’s drawback, ^sn35-15-1-24

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-25

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the mind’s drawback. ^sn35-15-1-26

I went in search of escape from the mind, ^sn35-15-1-27

and I found it. ^sn35-15-1-28

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of escape from the mind. ^sn35-15-1-29

As long as I didn’t truly understand these six interior sense fields’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening … ^sn35-15-2-1

But when I did truly understand … ^sn35-15-2-2

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^sn35-15-2-3

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^sn35-15-2-4

### In Search of Gratification (Exterior) (Linked Discourses 35.16) ^sn35-16-0

“Mendicants, I went in search of the gratification of sights, ^sn35-16-1-1

and I found it. …” ^sn35-16-1-2

### If There Were No Gratification (Interior) (Linked Discourses 35.17) ^sn35-17-0

“Mendicants, if there were no gratification in the eye, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn35-17-1-1

But since there is gratification in the eye, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn35-17-1-2

If the eye had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn35-17-1-3

But since the eye has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn35-17-1-4

If there were no escape from the eye, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^sn35-17-1-5

But since there is an escape from the eye, sentient beings do escape from it. ^sn35-17-1-6

If there were no gratification in the ear … ^sn35-17-1-7

nose … ^sn35-17-1-8

tongue … ^sn35-17-1-9

body … ^sn35-17-1-15

mind, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^sn35-17-1-16

But since there is gratification in the mind, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^sn35-17-1-17

If the mind had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^sn35-17-1-18

But since the mind has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^sn35-17-1-19

If there were no escape from the mind, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^sn35-17-1-20

But since there is an escape from the mind, sentient beings do escape from it. ^sn35-17-1-21

As long as sentient beings don’t truly understand these six interior sense fields’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they haven’t escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they don’t live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries. ^sn35-17-2-1

But when sentient beings truly understand these six interior sense fields’ gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they’ve escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries.” ^sn35-17-2-2

### If There Were No Gratification (Exterior) (Linked Discourses 35.18) ^sn35-18-0

“Mendicants, if there were no gratification in sights, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by them. …” ^sn35-18-1-1

### Taking Pleasure (Interior) (Linked Discourses 35.19) ^sn35-19-0

“Mendicants, if you take pleasure in the eye, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn35-19-1-1

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn35-19-1-2

If you take pleasure in the ear … ^sn35-19-1-3

nose … ^sn35-19-1-4

tongue … ^sn35-19-1-5

body … ^sn35-19-1-7

mind, you take pleasure in suffering. ^sn35-19-1-8

If you take pleasure in suffering, you’re not free from suffering, I say. ^sn35-19-1-9

If you don’t take pleasure in the eye, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn35-19-2-1

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say. ^sn35-19-2-2

If you don’t take pleasure in the ear … ^sn35-19-2-3

nose … ^sn35-19-2-4

tongue … ^sn35-19-2-5

body … ^sn35-19-2-7

mind, you don’t take pleasure in suffering. ^sn35-19-2-8

If you don’t take pleasure in suffering, you’re free from suffering, I say.” ^sn35-19-2-9

### The Arising of Suffering (Interior) (Linked Discourses 35.21) ^sn35-21-0

“Mendicants, the arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the eye is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn35-21-1-1

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of the ear … ^sn35-21-1-2

nose … ^sn35-21-1-3

tongue … ^sn35-21-1-4

body … ^sn35-21-1-5

and mind is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn35-21-1-6

The cessation, settling, and disappearance of the eye is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death. ^sn35-21-2-1

The cessation, settling, and ending of the ear, ^sn35-21-2-2

nose, ^sn35-21-2-3

tongue, ^sn35-21-2-4

body, ^sn35-21-2-5

and mind is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death.” ^sn35-21-2-6

### The Arising of Suffering (Exterior) (Linked Discourses 35.22) ^sn35-22-0

“Mendicants, the arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of sights is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn35-22-1-1

The arising, continuation, and manifestation and regeneration of sounds, ^sn35-22-1-2

smells, ^sn35-22-1-3

tastes, ^sn35-22-1-4

touches, ^sn35-22-1-5

and ideas is the arising of suffering, the continuation of diseases, and the manifestation of old age and death. ^sn35-22-1-6

sounds, ^sn35-22-2-2

smells, ^sn35-22-2-3

tastes, ^sn35-22-2-4

touches, ^sn35-22-2-5

and ideas is the cessation of suffering, the settling of diseases, and the disappearance of old age and death.” ^sn35-22-2-6

### All (Linked Discourses 35.23) ^sn35-23-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-23-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the all. ^sn35-23-1-2

Listen … ^sn35-23-1-3

And what is the all? ^sn35-23-1-4

It’s just the eye and sights, the ear and sounds, the nose and smells, the tongue and tastes, the body and touches, and the mind and ideas. ^sn35-23-1-5

This is called the all. ^sn35-23-1-6

Mendicants, suppose someone was to say: ^sn35-23-1-7

‘I’ll deny this all and describe another all.’ They’d have no grounds for that claim, ^sn35-23-1-8

they’d be stumped by questions, and, in addition, they’d get frustrated. ^sn35-23-1-9

Why is that? ^sn35-23-1-10

Because they’re out of their element.” ^sn35-23-1-11

### Giving Up (Linked Discourses 35.24) ^sn35-24-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the principle for giving up the all. ^sn35-24-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-24-1-2

And what is the principle for giving up the all? ^sn35-24-1-3

The eye should be given up. Sights should be given up. Eye consciousness should be given up. Eye contact should be given up. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact should also be given up. ^sn35-24-1-4

The ear … nose … ^sn35-24-1-5

tongue … ^sn35-24-1-6

body … ^sn35-24-1-7

mind should be given up. Ideas should be given up. Mind consciousness should be given up. Mind contact should be given up. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact should be given up. ^sn35-24-1-8

This is the principle for giving up the all.” ^sn35-24-1-9

### Giving Up By Direct Knowledge and Complete Understanding (Linked Discourses 35.25) ^sn35-25-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the principle for giving up the all by direct knowledge and complete understanding. ^sn35-25-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-25-1-2

And what is the principle for giving up the all by direct knowledge and complete understanding? ^sn35-25-1-3

The eye should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Sights should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Eye consciousness should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Eye contact should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. ^sn35-25-1-4

The ear … nose … ^sn35-25-1-5

tongue … ^sn35-25-1-6

body … ^sn35-25-1-7

mind should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Ideas should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Mind consciousness should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. Mind contact should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact should be given up by direct knowledge and complete understanding. ^sn35-25-1-8

This is the principle for giving up the all by direct knowledge and complete understanding.” ^sn35-25-1-9

the body … ^sn35-26-1-13

the mind, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn35-26-1-14

mind consciousness … ^sn35-26-1-16

mind contact … ^sn35-26-1-17

painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn35-26-1-18

mind contact … ^sn35-26-2-17

painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact, having dispassion for it and giving it up, you can end suffering. ^sn35-26-2-18

The eye, sights, eye consciousness, and things knowable by eye consciousness. ^sn35-27-2-3

body … ^sn35-27-2-5

The mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and things knowable by mind consciousness. ^sn35-27-2-6

### Burning (Linked Discourses 35.28) ^sn35-28-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Gayā on Gayā Head together with a thousand mendicants. ^sn35-28-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-28-1-2

“Mendicants, all is burning. ^sn35-28-1-3

And what is the all that is burning? ^sn35-28-1-4

The eye is burning. Sights are burning. Eye consciousness is burning. Eye contact is burning. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also burning. ^sn35-28-1-5

Burning with what? ^sn35-28-1-6

Burning with the fires of greed, hate, and delusion. Burning with rebirth, old age, and death, with sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^sn35-28-1-7

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-28-1-8

The mind is burning. Ideas are burning. Mind consciousness is burning. Mind contact is burning. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also burning. ^sn35-28-1-11

Burning with what? ^sn35-28-1-12

Burning with the fires of greed, hate, and delusion. Burning with rebirth, old age, and death, with sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress, I say. ^sn35-28-1-13

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-28-1-14

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-28-1-15

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-28-1-16

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-28-1-17

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-28-2-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^sn35-28-2-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the minds of the thousand mendicants were freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn35-28-2-3

### Overcome (Linked Discourses 35.29) ^sn35-29-0

So I have heard. ^sn35-29-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn35-29-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-29-1-3

“Mendicants, all is overcome. ^sn35-29-1-4

And what is the all that is overcome? ^sn35-29-1-5

The eye is overcome. Sights are overcome. Eye consciousness is overcome. Eye contact is overcome. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also overcome. ^sn35-29-1-6

Overcome by what? ^sn35-29-1-7

Overcome by the fires of greed, hate, and delusion. Overcome by rebirth, old age, and death, by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress, I say. ^sn35-29-1-8

The ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-29-1-9

body … ^sn35-29-1-12

mind is overcome. Ideas are overcome. Mind consciousness is overcome. Mind contact is overcome. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises conditioned by mind contact is also overcome. ^sn35-29-1-13

Overcome by what? ^sn35-29-1-14

Overcome by greed, hate, and delusion. Overcome by rebirth, old age, and death, by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress, I say. ^sn35-29-1-15

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-29-1-16

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-29-1-17

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-29-1-18

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-29-1-19

### The Practice Fit for Uprooting (Linked Discourses 35.30) ^sn35-30-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the practice fit for uprooting all conceiving. ^sn35-30-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. … ^sn35-30-1-2

And what is the practice fit for uprooting all conceiving? ^sn35-30-1-3

It’s when a mendicant does not conceive anything to be the eye, does not conceive it in the eye, does not conceive it as the eye, and does not conceive that ‘the eye is mine.’ ^sn35-30-1-4

They don’t conceive anything to be sights, they don’t conceive it in sights, they don’t conceive it as sights, and they don’t conceive that ‘sights are mine.’ ^sn35-30-1-5

They don’t conceive eye consciousness … ^sn35-30-1-6

eye contact … ^sn35-30-1-7

They don’t conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. They don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-30-1-8

They don’t conceive anything to be the ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-30-1-9

body … ^sn35-30-1-13

mind … ^sn35-30-1-14

They don’t conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. They don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-30-1-18

They don’t conceive anything to be all, they don’t conceive it in all, they don’t conceive it as all, and they don’t conceive that ‘all is mine.’ ^sn35-30-1-19

Not conceiving, they don’t grasp at anything in the world. ^sn35-30-1-20

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn35-30-1-21

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-30-1-22

This is the practice fit for uprooting all conceiving.” ^sn35-30-1-23

### The Practice Conducive to Uprooting (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.31) ^sn35-31-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the practice that’s conducive to uprooting all conceiving. ^sn35-31-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-31-1-2

And what is the practice that’s conducive to uprooting all conceiving? ^sn35-31-1-3

It’s when a mendicant does not conceive anything to be the eye, does not conceive it in the eye, does not conceive it as the eye, and does not conceive that ‘the eye is mine.’ ^sn35-31-1-4

They don’t conceive anything to be sights … ^sn35-31-1-5

eye consciousness … eye contact. And they don’t conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. They don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-31-1-6

For whatever you conceive it to be, whatever you conceive it in, whatever you conceive it as, and whatever you conceive to be ‘mine’: that becomes something else. ^sn35-31-1-7

The world is attached to being, taking pleasure only in being, yet it becomes something else. ^sn35-31-1-8

They don’t conceive anything to be the ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-31-1-9

body … ^sn35-31-1-14

mind … ^sn35-31-1-15

They don’t conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. They don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-31-1-18

For whatever you conceive it to be, whatever you conceive it in, whatever you conceive it as, and whatever you conceive to be ‘mine’: that becomes something else. ^sn35-31-1-19

The world is attached to being, taking pleasure only in being, yet it becomes something else. ^sn35-31-1-20

As far as the aggregates, elements, and sense fields extend, they don’t conceive anything to be that, they don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-31-1-21

Not conceiving, they don’t grasp at anything in the world. ^sn35-31-1-22

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn35-31-1-23

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-31-1-24

This is the practice that’s conducive to uprooting all conceiving.” ^sn35-31-1-25

### The Practice Conducive to Uprooting (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.32) ^sn35-32-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the practice that’s conducive to uprooting all conceiving. ^sn35-32-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-32-1-2

And what is the practice that’s conducive to uprooting all conceiving? ^sn35-32-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-32-2-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-32-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-32-3-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-32-4-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-32-5-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-32-6-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-32-6-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-32-7-1

“Are sights … ^sn35-32-8-1

eye consciousness … ^sn35-32-8-2

eye contact … ^sn35-32-8-3

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-32-11-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-32-13-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-32-25-1

### Ten on Liable to Be Reborn, Etc. (Linked Discourses 35.33–42) ^sn35-33-42-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-33-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to be reborn. ^sn35-33-1-3

And what is the all that is liable to be reborn? ^sn35-33-1-4

The eye, ^sn35-33-1-5

sights, ^sn35-33-1-6

eye consciousness, ^sn35-33-1-7

and eye contact are liable to be reborn. ^sn35-33-1-8

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also liable to be reborn. ^sn35-33-1-9

The ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-33-1-10

body … ^sn35-33-1-15

The mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are liable to be reborn. ^sn35-33-1-16

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also liable to be reborn. ^sn35-33-1-17

Seeing this a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned … ^sn35-33-1-18

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-33-1-19

“Mendicants, all is liable to grow old. …” ^sn35-34-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to fall sick. …” ^sn35-35-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to die. …” ^sn35-36-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to sorrow. …” ^sn35-37-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to corruption. …” ^sn35-38-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to end. …” ^sn35-39-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to fall. …” ^sn35-40-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to originate. …” ^sn35-41-1-1

“Mendicants, all is liable to cease. …” ^sn35-42-1-1

### Nine on Impermanence, Etc. (Linked Discourses 35.43–51) ^sn35-43-51-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-43-1-1

“Mendicants, all is impermanent. ^sn35-43-1-3

And what is the all that is impermanent? ^sn35-43-1-4

The eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are impermanent. ^sn35-43-1-5

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-43-1-6

The ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-43-1-7

body … ^sn35-43-1-9

The mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are impermanent. ^sn35-43-1-10

The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-43-1-11

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned … ^sn35-43-1-12

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-43-1-17

“Mendicants, all is suffering. …” ^sn35-44-1-1

“Mendicants, all is not-self. …” ^sn35-45-1-1

“Mendicants, all is to be directly known. …” ^sn35-46-1-1

“Mendicants, all is to be completely understood. …” ^sn35-47-1-1

“Mendicants, all is to be given up. …” ^sn35-48-1-1

“Mendicants, all is to be realized. …” ^sn35-49-1-1

“Mendicants, all is to be directly known and completely understood. …” ^sn35-50-1-1

“Mendicants, all is harried. …” ^sn35-51-1-1

### Contaminated (Linked Discourses 35.52) ^sn35-52-0

“Mendicants, all is contaminated. ^sn35-52-1-1

And what is the all that is contaminated? ^sn35-52-1-2

The eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are contaminated. ^sn35-52-1-3

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises conditioned by eye contact is also contaminated. ^sn35-52-1-4

The ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-52-1-5

body … ^sn35-52-1-7

The mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are contaminated. ^sn35-52-1-8

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises conditioned by mind contact is also contaminated. ^sn35-52-1-9

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned … ^sn35-52-1-10

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-52-1-15

### Giving Up Ignorance (Linked Discourses 35.53) ^sn35-53-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-53-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-53-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge?” ^sn35-53-1-3

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-53-2-1

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-53-2-5

Knowing and seeing the ear … ^sn35-53-2-6

nose … ^sn35-53-2-7

tongue … ^sn35-53-2-8

body … ^sn35-53-2-9

Knowing and seeing the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-53-2-10

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-53-2-14

That’s how to know and see so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge.” ^sn35-53-2-15

### Giving Up Fetters (Linked Discourses 35.54) ^sn35-54-0

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the fetters are given up?” ^sn35-54-1-1

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye as impermanent, the fetters are given up …” ^sn35-54-1-2

### Uprooting the Fetters (Linked Discourses 35.55) ^sn35-55-0

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the fetters are uprooted?” ^sn35-55-1-1

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye as not-self, the fetters are uprooted …” ^sn35-55-1-2

### Giving Up Defilements (Linked Discourses 35.56) ^sn35-56-0

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the defilements are given up?” … ^sn35-56-1-1

### Uprooting Defilements (Linked Discourses 35.57) ^sn35-57-0

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the defilements are uprooted?” … ^sn35-57-1-1

“Sir, how does one know and see so that the underlying tendencies are given up?” … ^sn35-58-1-1

### Uprooting Tendencies (Linked Discourses 35.59) ^sn35-59-0

### The Complete Understanding of All Grasping (Linked Discourses 35.60) ^sn35-60-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the principle for the complete understanding of all grasping. ^sn35-60-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-60-1-2

And what is the principle for the complete understanding of all grasping? ^sn35-60-1-3

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-60-1-4

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-60-1-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, eye contact, and feeling. ^sn35-60-1-6

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they are liberated, they understand: ‘I have completely understood grasping.’ ^sn35-60-1-7

Ear consciousness arises dependent on the ear and sounds. … ^sn35-60-1-8

Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. … ^sn35-60-1-9

Tongue consciousness arises dependent on the tongue and tastes. … ^sn35-60-1-10

Body consciousness arises dependent on the body and touches. … ^sn35-60-1-11

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-60-1-12

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-60-1-13

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, mind contact, and feeling. ^sn35-60-1-14

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they are liberated, they understand: ‘I have completely understood grasping.’ ^sn35-60-1-15

This is the principle for the complete understanding of all grasping.” ^sn35-60-1-16

### The Depletion of All Fuel for Grasping (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.61) ^sn35-61-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping. ^sn35-61-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-61-1-2

And what is the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping? ^sn35-61-1-3

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-61-1-4

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-61-1-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, eye contact, and feeling. ^sn35-61-1-6

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they are liberated, they understand: ‘I have completely depleted fuel for grasping.’ ^sn35-61-1-7

Ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-61-1-8

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-61-1-9

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-61-1-10

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, mind contact, and feeling. ^sn35-61-1-11

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they are liberated, they understand: ‘I have completely depleted fuel for grasping.’ ^sn35-61-1-12

This is the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping.” ^sn35-61-1-13

### The Depletion of All Fuel for Grasping (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.62) ^sn35-62-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping. ^sn35-62-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-62-1-2

And what is the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping? ^sn35-62-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-62-2-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-62-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-62-3-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-62-4-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-62-5-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-62-6-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-62-6-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-62-7-1

“Sights … ^sn35-62-8-1

eye consciousness … ^sn35-62-8-2

eye contact … ^sn35-62-10-1

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-62-12-1

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn35-62-13-1

“Ear … ^sn35-62-14-1

nose … ^sn35-62-14-2

tongue … ^sn35-62-14-3

body … ^sn35-62-14-4

mind … ^sn35-62-14-5

ideas … ^sn35-62-14-6

mind consciousness … ^sn35-62-14-7

mind contact … ^sn35-62-14-8

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-62-14-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-62-15-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-62-16-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-62-17-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-62-18-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-62-18-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-62-19-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. ^sn35-62-20-1

And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-62-20-2

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-62-20-3

They grow disillusioned with the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact. ^sn35-62-20-4

And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-62-20-5

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-62-20-6

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-62-20-7

This is the principle for depleting all fuel for grasping.” ^sn35-62-20-8

### With Migajāla (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.63) ^sn35-63-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-63-1-1

Then Venerable Migajāla went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-63-1-2

and said to him: ^sn35-63-1-3

“Sir, they speak of one who lives alone. ^sn35-63-1-4

How is one who lives alone defined? And how is living with a partner defined?” ^sn35-63-1-5

“Migajāla, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-63-2-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-63-2-2

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-63-2-3

When there’s relishing there’s lust. ^sn35-63-2-4

When there’s lust there is yoking. ^sn35-63-2-5

A mendicant who is fettered by relishing is said to live with a partner. ^sn35-63-2-6

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-63-2-7

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-63-2-8

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-63-2-9

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-63-2-10

When there’s relishing there’s lust. ^sn35-63-2-11

When there’s lust there is yoking. ^sn35-63-2-12

A mendicant who is fettered by relishing is said to live with a partner. ^sn35-63-2-13

A mendicant who lives like this is said to live with a partner, even if they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest that are quiet and still, far from the madding crowd, remote from human settlements, and fit for retreat. ^sn35-63-2-14

Why is that? ^sn35-63-2-16

For craving is their partner, and they haven’t given it up. ^sn35-63-2-17

That’s why they’re said to live with a partner. ^sn35-63-2-18

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-63-3-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-63-3-2

relishing ceases. ^sn35-63-3-3

When there’s no relishing there’s no lust. ^sn35-63-3-4

When there’s no lust there’s no yoking. ^sn35-63-3-5

A mendicant who is not fettered by relishing is said to live alone. ^sn35-63-3-6

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-63-3-7

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-63-3-8

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-63-3-9

relishing ceases. ^sn35-63-3-10

When there’s no relishing there’s no lust. ^sn35-63-3-11

When there’s no lust there’s no yoking. ^sn35-63-3-12

A mendicant who is not fettered by relishing is said to live alone. ^sn35-63-3-13

A mendicant who lives like this is said to live alone, even if they live within a village crowded by monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; by rulers and their chief ministers, and monastics of other religions and their disciples. ^sn35-63-3-14

Why is that? ^sn35-63-3-16

For craving is their partner, and they have given it up. ^sn35-63-3-17

That’s why they’re said to live alone.” ^sn35-63-3-18

### With Migajāla (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.64) ^sn35-64-0

Then Venerable Migajāla went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-64-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-64-1-2

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-64-1-3

“Migajāla, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-64-2-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-64-2-2

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-64-2-3

Relishing is the origin of suffering, I say. ^sn35-64-2-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-64-2-5

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-64-2-6

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-64-2-7

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-64-2-8

Relishing is the origin of suffering, I say. ^sn35-64-2-9

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-64-3-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, relishing ceases. ^sn35-64-3-2

When relishing ceases, suffering ceases, I say. ^sn35-64-3-3

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-64-3-4

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-64-3-5

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, relishing ceases. ^sn35-64-3-6

When relishing ceases, suffering ceases, I say.” ^sn35-64-3-7

And then Venerable Migajāla approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn35-64-4-1

Then Migajāla, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn35-64-4-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn35-64-4-3

And Migajāla became one of the perfected. ^sn35-64-4-4

### Samiddhi’s Question About Māra (Linked Discourses 35.65) ^sn35-65-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn35-65-1-1

Then Venerable Samiddhi went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-65-1-2

and said to him: ^sn35-65-1-3

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘Māra’. ^sn35-65-1-4

How do we define Māra or what is known as Māra?” ^sn35-65-1-5

“Samiddhi, where there is the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and things knowable by eye consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-1

Where there is the ear, sounds, ear consciousness, and things knowable by ear consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-2

Where there is the nose, smells, nose consciousness, and things knowable by nose consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-3

Where there is the tongue, tastes, tongue consciousness, and things knowable by tongue consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-4

Where there is the body, touches, body consciousness, and things knowable by body consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-5

Where there is the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and things knowable by mind consciousness, there is Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-2-6

Where there is no eye, no sights, no eye consciousness, and no things knowable by eye consciousness, there is no Māra or what is known as Māra. ^sn35-65-3-1

Where there is no ear … ^sn35-65-3-2

no nose … ^sn35-65-3-3

no tongue … ^sn35-65-3-4

no body … ^sn35-65-3-5

Where there is no mind, no ideas, no mind consciousness, and no things knowable by mind consciousness, there is no Māra or what is known as Māra.” ^sn35-65-3-6

### Samiddhi’s Question About a Sentient Being (Linked Discourses 35.66) ^sn35-66-0

“Sir, they speak of this thing called a ‘sentient being’. ^sn35-66-1-1

How do we define a sentient being or what is known as a sentient being?” … ^sn35-66-1-2

### Samiddhi’s Question About Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.67) ^sn35-67-0

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘suffering’. ^sn35-67-1-1

How do we define suffering or what is known as suffering?” … ^sn35-67-1-2

### Samiddhi’s Question About the World (Linked Discourses 35.68) ^sn35-68-0

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘the world’. ^sn35-68-1-1

How do we define the world or what is known as the world?” ^sn35-68-1-2

“Samiddhi, where there is the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and things knowable by eye consciousness, there is the world or what is known as the world. ^sn35-68-1-3

Where there is the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-68-1-4

Where there is the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and things knowable by mind consciousness, there is the world or what is known as the world. ^sn35-68-1-5

Where there is no eye, no sights, no eye consciousness, and no things knowable by eye consciousness, there is no world or what is known as the world. ^sn35-68-2-1

Where there is no ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-68-2-2

Where there is no mind, no ideas, no mind consciousness, and no things knowable by mind consciousness, there is no world or what is known as the world.” ^sn35-68-2-3

### Upasena and the Viper (Linked Discourses 35.69) ^sn35-69-0

At one time the venerables Sāriputta and Upasena were staying near Rājagaha in the Cool Grove, under the Snake’s Hood Grotto. ^sn35-69-1-1

Now at that time a viper fell on Upasena’s body. ^sn35-69-1-2

He addressed the mendicants, ^sn35-69-1-3

“Come, reverends, lift this body onto a cot and carry it outside ^sn35-69-1-4

before it’s scattered right here ^sn35-69-1-5

like a handful of hay.” ^sn35-69-1-6

When he said this, Sāriputta said to him, ^sn35-69-2-1

“But we don’t see any impairment in your body or deterioration of your faculties. ^sn35-69-2-2

Yet you say: ^sn35-69-2-3

‘Come, reverends, lift this body onto a cot and carry it outside ^sn35-69-2-4

before it’s scattered right here ^sn35-69-2-5

like a handful of hay.’” ^sn35-69-2-6

“Reverend Sāriputta, there may be an impairment in body or deterioration of faculties for someone who thinks: ^sn35-69-2-7

‘I am the eye’ or ‘the eye is mine.’ ^sn35-69-2-8

Or ‘I am the ear … nose … tongue … body …’ ^sn35-69-2-9

Or ‘I am the mind’ or ‘the mind is mine.’ ^sn35-69-2-10

But I don’t think like that. ^sn35-69-2-12

So why would there be an impairment in my body or deterioration of my faculties?” ^sn35-69-2-16

“That must be because Venerable Upasena has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit. ^sn35-69-3-1

That’s why it doesn’t occur to you: ^sn35-69-3-2

‘I am the eye’ or ‘the eye is mine.’ Or ‘I am the ear … ^sn35-69-3-3

nose … tongue … body …’ ^sn35-69-3-4

Or ‘I am the mind’ or ‘the mind is mine.’” ^sn35-69-3-5

Then those mendicants lifted Upasena’s body onto a cot and carried it outside. ^sn35-69-3-6

And his body was scattered right there ^sn35-69-3-7

like a handful of hay. ^sn35-69-3-8

### Upavāṇa on What is Apparent in the Present Life (Linked Discourses 35.70) ^sn35-70-0

Then Venerable Upavāṇa went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-70-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-70-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^sn35-70-1-3

In what way is the teaching apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^sn35-70-1-4

“Upavāṇa, take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. They experience both the sight and the desire for the sight. ^sn35-70-2-1

There is desire for sights in them, and they understand that. ^sn35-70-2-2

Since this is so, ^sn35-70-2-3

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn35-70-2-5

Next, take a mendicant who hears … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-70-3-1

Next, take a mendicant who knows an idea with their mind. They experience both the idea and the desire for the idea. ^sn35-70-4-1

There is desire for ideas in them, and they understand that. ^sn35-70-4-2

Since this is so, ^sn35-70-4-3

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn35-70-4-5

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. They experience the sight but no desire for the sight. ^sn35-70-5-1

There is no desire for sights in them, and they understand that. ^sn35-70-5-2

Since this is so, ^sn35-70-5-3

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn35-70-5-5

Next, take a mendicant who hears … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-70-6-1

Next, take a mendicant who knows an idea with their mind. They experience the idea but no desire for the idea. ^sn35-70-7-3

There is no desire for ideas in them, and they understand that. ^sn35-70-7-4

Since this is so, this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^sn35-70-7-5

### Six Fields of Contact (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.71) ^sn35-71-0

“Mendicants, anyone who doesn’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape ^sn35-71-1-1

has not completed the spiritual journey and is far from this teaching and training.” ^sn35-71-1-2

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^sn35-71-2-1

“Here, sir, I’m discouraged. ^sn35-71-2-2

For I don’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape.” ^sn35-71-2-3

“What do you think, mendicant? ^sn35-71-3-1

Do you regard the eye like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-71-3-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-71-4-1

“Good, mendicant! And regarding the eye, you will truly see clearly with right wisdom that: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-71-5-1

Just this is the end of suffering. ^sn35-71-5-2

Do you regard the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-71-5-3

Do you regard the mind like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-71-7-3

“No, sir.” ^sn35-71-8-1

“Good, mendicant! And regarding the mind, you will truly see clearly with right wisdom that: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-71-9-1

Just this is the end of suffering.” ^sn35-71-9-2

### Six Fields of Contact (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.72) ^sn35-72-0

“Mendicants, anyone who doesn’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape ^sn35-72-1-1

has not completed the spiritual journey and is far from this teaching and training.” ^sn35-72-1-2

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^sn35-72-2-1

“Here, sir, I’m discouraged, really discouraged. ^sn35-72-2-2

For I don’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape.” ^sn35-72-2-3

“What do you think, mendicant? ^sn35-72-3-1

Do you regard the eye like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’?” ^sn35-72-3-2

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-72-4-1

“Good, mendicant! And regarding the eye, you will truly see clearly with right wisdom that: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-72-5-1

In this way you will give up the first field of contact, so that there are no more future lives. ^sn35-72-5-2

Do you regard the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-72-6-1

Do you regard the mind like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’?” ^sn35-72-9-1

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-72-10-1

“Good, mendicant! And regarding the mind, you will truly see clearly with right wisdom that: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-72-11-1

In this way you will give up the sixth field of contact, so that there are no more future lives.” ^sn35-72-11-2

### Six Fields of Contact (3rd) (Linked Discourses 35.73) ^sn35-73-0

“Mendicants, anyone who doesn’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape ^sn35-73-1-1

has not completed the spiritual journey and is far from this teaching and training.” ^sn35-73-1-2

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^sn35-73-2-1

“Here, sir, I’m discouraged, really discouraged. ^sn35-73-2-2

For I don’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape.” ^sn35-73-2-3

“What do you think, mendicant? ^sn35-73-3-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-73-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-73-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-73-5-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-73-6-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-73-7-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-73-7-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-73-8-1

“Is the ear … ^sn35-73-9-1

nose … ^sn35-73-9-2

tongue … ^sn35-73-9-3

body … ^sn35-73-9-4

mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-73-9-5

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-73-10-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-73-11-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-73-12-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-73-13-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-73-13-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-73-14-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-73-15-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-73-15-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-73-15-3

### Sick (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.74) ^sn35-74-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-74-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, and said to him, ^sn35-74-1-2

“Sir, in such and such a monastery there’s a mendicant who is junior and not well-known. He’s sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn35-74-1-3

Please go to him out of sympathy.” ^sn35-74-1-4

When the Buddha heard that the mendicant was junior and ill, understanding that he was not well-known, he went to him. ^sn35-74-2-1

That mendicant saw the Buddha coming off in the distance ^sn35-74-2-2

and stirred in his cot. ^sn35-74-2-3

The Buddha said to that monk, ^sn35-74-2-4

“It’s all right, mendicant, don’t get up. ^sn35-74-2-5

There are some seats spread out, I will sit there.” ^sn35-74-2-6

He sat on the seat spread out ^sn35-74-2-7

and said to the mendicant, ^sn35-74-2-8

“I hope you’re keeping well, mendicant; I hope you’re all right. I hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn35-74-2-9

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn35-74-3-1

“I hope you don’t have any remorse or regret?” ^sn35-74-4-1

“Indeed, sir, I have no little remorse and regret.” ^sn35-74-5-1

“I hope you have no reason to blame yourself when it comes to ethical conduct?” ^sn35-74-6-1

“No sir, I have no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct.” ^sn35-74-7-1

“In that case, mendicant, why do you have remorse and regret?” ^sn35-74-8-1

“Because I understand that the Buddha has not taught the Dhamma merely for the sake of ethical purity.” ^sn35-74-9-1

“If that is so, what then do you understand to be the purpose of teaching the Dhamma?” ^sn35-74-10-1

“I understand that the Buddha has taught the Dhamma for the purpose of the fading away of greed.” ^sn35-74-11-1

“Good, good, mendicant! ^sn35-74-12-1

It’s good that you understand that I’ve taught the Dhamma for the purpose of the fading away of greed. ^sn35-74-12-2

For that is indeed the purpose. ^sn35-74-12-3

What do you think, mendicant? ^sn35-74-12-4

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-74-12-5

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn35-74-13-1

“Is the ear … ^sn35-74-14-2

nose … ^sn35-74-14-3

tongue … ^sn35-74-14-4

body … ^sn35-74-14-5

mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-74-14-6

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-74-15-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-74-16-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-74-17-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-74-18-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-74-18-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-74-19-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-74-20-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-74-20-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended … there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-74-20-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-74-21-1

Satisfied, that mendicant approved what the Buddha said. ^sn35-74-21-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in that mendicant: ^sn35-74-21-3

“Everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease.” ^sn35-74-21-4

### Sick (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.75) ^sn35-75-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-75-1-1

“Sir, in such and such a monastery there’s a mendicant who is junior and not well-known. He’s sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn35-75-1-2

Please go to him out of sympathy.” ^sn35-75-1-3

When the Buddha heard that the mendicant was junior and ill, understanding that he was not well-known, he went to him. ^sn35-75-2-1

That mendicant saw the Buddha coming off in the distance ^sn35-75-2-2

and stirred in his cot. ^sn35-75-2-3

Then the Buddha said to that monk, ^sn35-75-2-4

“It’s all right, mendicant, don’t get up. ^sn35-75-2-5

There are some seats spread out, I will sit there.” ^sn35-75-2-6

He sat on the seat spread out and said to the mendicant, ^sn35-75-2-7

“I hope you’re keeping well, mendicant; I hope you’re all right. I hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn35-75-2-8

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. … ^sn35-75-3-1

I have no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct.” ^sn35-75-3-2

“In that case, mendicant, why do you have remorse and regret?” ^sn35-75-4-1

“Because I understand that the Buddha has not taught the Dhamma merely for the sake of ethical purity.” ^sn35-75-5-1

“If that is so, what then do you understand to be the goal of teaching the Dhamma?” ^sn35-75-6-1

“I understand that the Buddha has taught the Dhamma for the goal of complete extinguishment without grasping fuel.” ^sn35-75-7-1

“Good, good, mendicant! ^sn35-75-8-1

It’s good that you understand that I’ve taught the Dhamma for the goal of complete extinguishment with no fuel for grasping. ^sn35-75-8-2

For that is indeed the goal. ^sn35-75-8-3

What do you think, mendicant? ^sn35-75-9-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-75-9-2

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn35-75-10-1

“Is the ear … ^sn35-75-11-2

nose … ^sn35-75-11-3

tongue … ^sn35-75-11-4

body … ^sn35-75-11-5

mind … ^sn35-75-11-6

mind consciousness … ^sn35-75-11-7

mind contact … ^sn35-75-11-8

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-75-11-9

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-75-12-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-75-13-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-75-14-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-75-15-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-75-15-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-75-16-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye … ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-75-17-1

mind … ^sn35-75-17-2

mind consciousness … ^sn35-75-17-3

mind contact … ^sn35-75-17-4

They grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-75-17-5

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-75-17-6

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-75-17-7

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-75-18-1

Satisfied, that mendicant approved what the Buddha said. ^sn35-75-18-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the mind of that mendicant was freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn35-75-18-3

### With Rādha on Impermanence (Linked Discourses 35.76) ^sn35-76-0

The Venerable Rādha went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-76-1-1

and said to him, ^sn35-76-1-2

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-76-1-3

“Rādha, you should give up desire for what is impermanent. ^sn35-76-1-4

And what is impermanent? ^sn35-76-1-5

The eye, sights, eye consciousness, ^sn35-76-1-6

and eye contact are impermanent. ^sn35-76-1-7

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also impermanent. You should give up desire for it. ^sn35-76-1-8

The ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-76-1-9

body … ^sn35-76-1-10

The mind, ^sn35-76-1-11

ideas, ^sn35-76-1-12

mind consciousness, ^sn35-76-1-13

and mind contact are impermanent. ^sn35-76-1-14

And the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also impermanent. You should give up desire for it. ^sn35-76-1-15

You should give up desire for what is impermanent.” ^sn35-76-1-16

### With Rādha on Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.77) ^sn35-77-0

“Rādha, you should give up desire for what is suffering. …” ^sn35-77-1-1

### With Rādha on Not-Self (Linked Discourses 35.78) ^sn35-78-0

“Rādha, you should give up desire for what is not-self. …” ^sn35-78-1-1

### Giving Up Ignorance (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.79) ^sn35-79-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-79-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-79-1-2

“Sir, is there one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge?” ^sn35-79-1-3

“There is, mendicant.” ^sn35-79-2-1

“But what is that one thing?” ^sn35-79-3-1

“Ignorance is one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge.” ^sn35-79-4-1

“But how does a mendicant know and see so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge?” ^sn35-79-5-1

“When a mendicant knows and sees the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-79-6-1

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. … ^sn35-79-6-2

Knowing and seeing the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-79-6-3

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as impermanent, ignorance is given up and knowledge arises. ^sn35-79-6-4

That’s how a mendicant knows and sees so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge.” ^sn35-79-6-5

### Giving Up Ignorance (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.80) ^sn35-80-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-80-1-1

“Sir, is there one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge?” ^sn35-80-1-2

“There is, mendicant.” ^sn35-80-2-1

“But what is that one thing?” ^sn35-80-3-1

“Ignorance is one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge.” ^sn35-80-4-1

“But how does a mendicant know and see so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge?” ^sn35-80-5-1

“Take a mendicant who has heard: ^sn35-80-6-1

‘Nothing is worth insisting on.’ ^sn35-80-6-2

When a mendicant has heard that ^sn35-80-6-3

nothing is worth insisting on, ^sn35-80-6-4

they directly know all things. Directly knowing all things, they completely understand all things. Completely understanding all things, they see all signs as other. They see the eye, sights, ^sn35-80-6-5

eye consciousness, ^sn35-80-6-6

and eye contact as other. ^sn35-80-6-7

And they also see the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact as other. … ^sn35-80-6-8

They see the mind, ideas, ^sn35-80-6-9

mind consciousness, ^sn35-80-6-10

and mind contact as other. ^sn35-80-6-11

And they also see the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as other. ^sn35-80-6-12

That’s how a mendicant knows and sees so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge.” ^sn35-80-6-13

### Several Mendicants (Linked Discourses 35.81) ^sn35-81-0

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-81-1-1

“Sir, sometimes wanderers of other religions ask us: ^sn35-81-1-2

‘Reverends, what’s the goal of leading the spiritual life under the ascetic Gotama?’ ^sn35-81-1-3

We answer them like this: ^sn35-81-1-4

‘The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand suffering.’ ^sn35-81-1-5

Answering this way, we trust that we repeat what the Buddha has said, and don’t misrepresent him with an untruth. We trust our explanation is in line with the teaching, and that there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal or criticism.” ^sn35-81-1-6

“Indeed, in answering this way you repeat what I’ve said, and don’t misrepresent me with an untruth. Your explanation is in line with the teaching, and there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal or criticism. ^sn35-81-2-1

For the goal of leading the spiritual life under me is to completely understand suffering. ^sn35-81-2-2

If wanderers of other religions were to ask you: ^sn35-81-2-3

‘Reverends, what is that suffering?’ ^sn35-81-2-4

You should answer them: ^sn35-81-2-5

‘Reverends, the eye is suffering. The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-81-2-6

Sights … Eye consciousness … Eye contact … ^sn35-81-2-7

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also suffering. ^sn35-81-2-8

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-81-2-9

Ear … Nose … Tongue … Body … Mind … ^sn35-81-2-10

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also suffering. ^sn35-81-2-11

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-81-2-12

This is that suffering. The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this.’ ^sn35-81-2-13

When questioned by wanderers of other religions, that’s how you should answer them.” ^sn35-81-2-14

### A Question On the World (Linked Discourses 35.82) ^sn35-82-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and said to him: ^sn35-82-1-1

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘the world’. ^sn35-82-2-1

How is the world defined?” ^sn35-82-2-2

“It wears away, mendicant, that’s why it’s called ‘the world’. ^sn35-82-2-3

And what is wearing away? ^sn35-82-2-4

The eye is wearing away. ^sn35-82-2-5

Sights … eye consciousness … eye contact is wearing away. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also wearing away. ^sn35-82-2-6

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-82-2-7

The mind … ideas … mind consciousness … mind contact is wearing away. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also wearing away. ^sn35-82-2-8

It wears away, mendicant, that’s why it’s called ‘the world’.” ^sn35-82-2-9

### Phagguna’s Question (Linked Discourses 35.83) ^sn35-83-0

And then Venerable Phagguna went up to the Buddha … and said to him: ^sn35-83-1-1

“Sir, suppose someone were to describe the Buddhas of the past who have become fully quenched, cut off proliferation, cut off the course, finished off the cycle, and passed beyond all suffering. Does the eye exist by which they could be described? ^sn35-83-2-1

Does the ear … nose … tongue … body exist …? ^sn35-83-2-2

Does the mind exist by which they could be described?” ^sn35-83-2-3

“Phagguna, suppose someone were to describe the Buddhas of the past who have become fully quenched, cut off proliferation, cut off the course, finished off the cycle, and passed beyond all suffering. The eye does not exist by which they could be described. ^sn35-83-3-1

The ear … nose … tongue … body does not exist … ^sn35-83-3-2

The mind does not exist by which they could be described.” ^sn35-83-3-3

### Liable to Wear Out (Linked Discourses 35.84) ^sn35-84-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-84-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-84-1-2

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘the world’. ^sn35-84-2-1

How is the world defined?” ^sn35-84-2-2

“Ānanda, that which is liable to wear out is called the world in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-84-2-3

And what is liable to wear out? ^sn35-84-2-4

The eye is liable to wear out. Sights … eye consciousness … eye contact is liable to wear out. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also liable to wear out. ^sn35-84-2-5

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-84-2-6

The mind … ideas … mind consciousness … mind contact is liable to wear out. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also liable to wear out. ^sn35-84-2-7

That which is liable to wear out is called the world in the training of the Noble One.” ^sn35-84-2-8

### The World is Empty (Linked Discourses 35.85) ^sn35-85-0

And then Venerable Ānanda … said to the Buddha: ^sn35-85-1-1

“Sir, they say that ‘the world is empty’. ^sn35-85-1-2

What does the saying ‘the world is empty’ refer to?” ^sn35-85-1-3

“Ānanda, they say that ‘the world is empty’ because it’s empty of self or what belongs to self. ^sn35-85-1-4

And what is empty of self or what belongs to self? ^sn35-85-1-5

The eye, ^sn35-85-1-6

sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are empty of self or what belongs to self. … ^sn35-85-1-7

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also empty of self or what belongs to self. ^sn35-85-1-8

They say that ‘the world is empty’ because it’s empty of self or what belongs to self.” ^sn35-85-1-9

### A Teaching In Brief (Linked Discourses 35.86) ^sn35-86-0

Seated to one side, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^sn35-86-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-86-1-2

“What do you think, Ānanda? ^sn35-86-2-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-86-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-86-3-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-86-4-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-86-5-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-86-6-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-86-6-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-86-7-1

“Are sights … ^sn35-86-8-1

eye consciousness … eye contact … ^sn35-86-10-1

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-86-10-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-86-11-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-86-12-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-86-13-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-86-14-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-86-14-2

“No, sir.” … ^sn35-86-15-1

“Is the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … ^sn35-86-16-1

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-86-18-3

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-86-19-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-86-20-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-86-21-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-86-22-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-86-22-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-86-23-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-86-24-1

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-86-24-2

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-86-24-3

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-86-24-4

### With Channa (Linked Discourses 35.87) ^sn35-87-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn35-87-1-1

Now at that time the venerables Sāriputta, Mahācunda, and Channa were staying on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn35-87-1-2

Now at that time Venerable Channa was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn35-87-1-3

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Mahācunda and said to him, ^sn35-87-1-4

“Come, Reverend Cunda, let’s go to see Venerable Channa and ask about his illness.” ^sn35-87-1-5

“Yes, reverend,” replied Mahācunda. ^sn35-87-1-6

And then Sāriputta and Mahācunda went to see Channa and sat down on the seats spread out. Sāriputta said to Channa: ^sn35-87-2-1

“I hope you’re keeping well, Reverend Channa; I hope you’re all right. I hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn35-87-2-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading. ^sn35-87-3-1

The winds piercing my head are so severe, it feels like a strong man drilling into my head with a sharp point. ^sn35-87-3-2
