The pain in my head is so severe, it feels like a strong man tightening a strong leather strap around my head. ^sn35-87-3-4

The winds slicing my belly are so severe, like a deft butcher or their apprentice were slicing open a cows’s belly with a sharp meat cleaver. ^sn35-87-3-6

The burning in my body is so severe, it feels like two strong men grabbing a weaker man by the arms to burn and scorch him on a pit of glowing coals. ^sn35-87-3-8

I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading. ^sn35-87-3-9

Reverend Sāriputta, I shall commit suicide. I don’t wish to live.” ^sn35-87-3-10

“Please don’t commit suicide! ^sn35-87-4-1

Venerable Channa, keep going! We want you to keep going. ^sn35-87-4-2

If you don’t have any suitable food, we’ll find it for you. ^sn35-87-4-3

If you don’t have suitable medicine, we’ll find it for you. ^sn35-87-4-4

If you don’t have a capable carer, we’ll find one for you. ^sn35-87-4-5

Please don’t commit suicide! ^sn35-87-4-6

Venerable Channa, keep going! We want you to keep going.” ^sn35-87-4-7

“Reverend Sāriputta, it’s not that I don’t have suitable food; ^sn35-87-5-1

I do have suitable food. ^sn35-87-5-2

It’s not that I don’t have suitable medicine; ^sn35-87-5-3

I do have suitable medicine. ^sn35-87-5-4

It’s not that I don’t have a capable carer; ^sn35-87-5-5

I do have a capable carer. ^sn35-87-5-6

Moreover, for a long time now I have served the Teacher with love, not without love. ^sn35-87-5-7

For it is proper for a disciple to serve the Teacher with love, not without love. ^sn35-87-5-8

You should remember this: ‘The mendicant Channa will commit suicide blamelessly.’” ^sn35-87-5-9

“I’d like to ask Venerable Channa about a certain point, if you’d take the time to answer.” ^sn35-87-6-1

“Ask, Reverend Sāriputta. When I’ve heard it I’ll know.” ^sn35-87-6-2

“Reverend Channa, do you regard the eye, eye consciousness, and things knowable by eye consciousness in this way: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’? ^sn35-87-7-1

Do you regard the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-87-7-2

mind, mind consciousness, and things knowable by mind consciousness in this way: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-87-7-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, I regard the eye, eye consciousness, and things knowable by eye consciousness in this way: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn35-87-8-1

I regard the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-87-8-2

mind, mind consciousness, and things knowable by mind consciousness in this way: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’.” ^sn35-87-8-3

“Reverend Channa, what have you seen, what have you directly known in these things that you regard them in this way: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’?” ^sn35-87-9-1

“Reverend Sāriputta, after seeing cessation, after directly knowing cessation in these things I regard them in this way: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’.” ^sn35-87-10-1

When he said this, Venerable Mahācunda said to Venerable Channa, ^sn35-87-11-1

“So, Reverend Channa, you should regularly apply your mind well to this instruction of the Buddha: ^sn35-87-11-2

‘For the dependent there is agitation. For the independent there’s no agitation. ^sn35-87-11-3

When there’s no agitation there is tranquility. ^sn35-87-11-4

When there’s tranquility there’s no inclination. ^sn35-87-11-5

When there’s no inclination, there’s no coming and going. ^sn35-87-11-6

When there’s no coming and going, there’s no passing away and reappearing. ^sn35-87-11-7

When there’s no passing away and reappearing, there’s no this life or the next or between the two. ^sn35-87-11-8

Just this is the end of suffering.’” ^sn35-87-11-9

And when the venerables Sāriputta and Mahācunda had given Venerable Channa this advice they rose from their seat and left. ^sn35-87-12-1

Not long after those venerables had left, Venerable Channa committed suicide. ^sn35-87-12-2

Then Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn35-87-13-1

“Sir, Venerable Channa has committed suicide. ^sn35-87-13-2

Where has he been reborn in his next life?” ^sn35-87-13-3

“Sāriputta, didn’t the mendicant Channa declare his blamelessness to you personally?” ^sn35-87-13-4

“Sir, there is a Vajjian village named Pubbajira. ^sn35-87-13-5

There Channa had families who were friendly, intimate, and hospitable.” ^sn35-87-13-6

“The mendicant Channa did indeed have such families, Sāriputta. ^sn35-87-13-7

But this is not enough for me to call someone ‘blameworthy’. ^sn35-87-13-8

When someone lays down this body and takes up another body, I call them ‘blameworthy’. ^sn35-87-13-9

But the mendicant Channa did no such thing. ^sn35-87-13-10

You should remember this: ‘The mendicant Channa committed suicide blamelessly.’” ^sn35-87-13-11

### With Puṇṇa (Linked Discourses 35.88) ^sn35-88-0

And then Venerable Puṇṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-88-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-88-1-2

“Puṇṇa, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-88-2-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-88-2-2

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-88-2-3

Relishing is the origin of suffering, I say. ^sn35-88-2-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-88-2-5

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-88-2-6

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-88-2-7

this gives rise to relishing. ^sn35-88-2-8

Relishing is the origin of suffering, I say. ^sn35-88-2-9

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-88-3-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, relishing ceases. ^sn35-88-3-2

When relishing ceases, suffering ceases, I say. … ^sn35-88-3-3

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-88-3-4

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, relishing ceases. ^sn35-88-3-5

When relishing ceases, suffering ceases, I say. ^sn35-88-3-6

Puṇṇa, now that I’ve given you this brief advice, what country will you live in?” ^sn35-88-4-1

“Sir, there’s a country named Sunāparanta; I will live there.” ^sn35-88-4-2

“The people of Sunāparanta are wild and rough, Puṇṇa. ^sn35-88-5-1

If they abuse and insult you, what will you think of them?” ^sn35-88-5-3

“If they abuse and insult me, I will think: ^sn35-88-6-1

‘These people of Sunāparanta are gracious, truly gracious, since they don’t hit me with their fists.’ ^sn35-88-6-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-6-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-6-4

“But if they do hit you with their fists, what will you think of them then?” ^sn35-88-7-1

“If they hit me with their fists, I’ll think: ^sn35-88-8-1

‘These people of Sunāparanta are gracious, truly gracious, since they don’t throw stones at me.’ ^sn35-88-8-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-8-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-8-4

“But if they do throw stones at you, what will you think of them then?” ^sn35-88-9-1

“If they throw stones at me, I’ll think: ^sn35-88-10-1

‘These people of Sunāparanta are gracious, truly gracious, since they don’t beat me with a club.’ ^sn35-88-10-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-10-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-10-4

“But if they do beat you with a club, what will you think of them then?” ^sn35-88-11-1

“If they beat me with a club, I’ll think: ^sn35-88-12-1

‘These people of Sunāparanta are gracious, truly gracious, since they don’t stab me with a knife.’ ^sn35-88-12-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-12-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-12-4

“But if they do stab you with a knife, what will you think of them then?” ^sn35-88-13-1

“If they stab me with a knife, I’ll think: ^sn35-88-14-1

‘These people of Sunāparanta are gracious, truly gracious, since they don’t take my life with a sharp knife.’ ^sn35-88-14-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-14-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-14-4

“But if they do take your life with a sharp knife, what will you think of them then?” ^sn35-88-15-1

“If they take my life with a sharp knife, I’ll think: ^sn35-88-16-1

‘There are disciples of the Buddha who looked for a suicide weapon because they were horrified, repelled, and disgusted with the body and with life. And I have found this without looking!’ ^sn35-88-16-2

That’s what I’ll think, Blessed One. ^sn35-88-16-3

That’s what I’ll think, Holy One.” ^sn35-88-16-4

“Good, good Puṇṇa! ^sn35-88-17-1

Having such self-control and peacefulness, you will be quite capable of living in Sunāparanta. ^sn35-88-17-2

Now, Puṇṇa, go at your convenience.” ^sn35-88-17-3

And then Puṇṇa welcomed and agreed with the Buddha’s words. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right. Then he set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, set out for Sunāparanta. ^sn35-88-18-1

Traveling stage by stage, he arrived at Sunāparanta, ^sn35-88-18-2

and stayed there. ^sn35-88-18-3

Within that rainy season he confirmed around five hundred male and five hundred female lay followers. And within that same rainy season he realized the three knowledges. ^sn35-88-18-4

And within that same rainy season he became completely extinguished. ^sn35-88-18-7

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-88-19-1

“Sir, the gentleman named Puṇṇa, who was advised in brief by the Buddha, has passed away. ^sn35-88-19-2

Where has he been reborn in his next life?” ^sn35-88-19-3

“Mendicants, Puṇṇa was astute. He practiced in line with the teaching, and did not trouble me about the teachings. ^sn35-88-20-1

Puṇṇa has become completely quenched.” ^sn35-88-20-2

### With Bāhiya (Linked Discourses 35.89) ^sn35-89-0

Then Venerable Bāhiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-89-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-89-1-2

“What do you think, Bāhiya? ^sn35-89-2-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-89-2-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-89-3-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-89-4-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-89-5-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-89-6-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-89-6-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-89-7-1

“Are sights … ^sn35-89-8-1

eye consciousness … ^sn35-89-9-2

eye contact … ^sn35-89-9-3

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-89-9-4

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-89-10-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-89-11-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-89-12-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-89-13-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-89-13-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-89-14-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-89-15-1

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-89-15-2

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-89-15-3

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-89-15-4

And then Venerable Bāhiya approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn35-89-16-1

Then Bāhiya, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn35-89-16-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn35-89-16-3

And Venerable Bāhiya became one of the perfected. ^sn35-89-16-4

### Turbulence (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.90) ^sn35-90-0

“Mendicants, turbulence is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-90-1-1

That’s why the Realized One lives unperturbed, with dart drawn out. ^sn35-90-1-2

Now, a mendicant might wish: ‘May I live unperturbed, with dart drawn out.’ ^sn35-90-1-3

So let them not conceive anything to be the eye, let them not conceive it in the eye, let them not conceive it as the eye, let them not conceive that ‘the eye is mine.’ ^sn35-90-1-4

Let them not conceive sights … ^sn35-90-1-5

eye consciousness … ^sn35-90-1-6

eye contact … ^sn35-90-1-7

Let them not conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. Let them not conceive it in that, let them not conceive it as that, and let them not conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-90-1-8

Let them not conceive anything to be the ear … ^sn35-90-2-1

nose … ^sn35-90-2-2

tongue … ^sn35-90-2-3

body … ^sn35-90-3-1

mind … ^sn35-90-3-2

ideas … ^sn35-90-3-3

mind consciousness … ^sn35-90-3-4

mind contact … ^sn35-90-3-5

Let them not conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. Let them not conceive it in that, let them not conceive it as that, and let them not conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-90-3-6

Let them not conceive anything to be all, let them not conceive it in all, let them not conceive it as all, let them not conceive that ‘all is mine’. ^sn35-90-3-7

Not conceiving, they don’t grasp at anything in the world. ^sn35-90-4-1

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn35-90-4-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-90-4-3

### Turbulence (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.91) ^sn35-91-0

“Mendicants, turbulence is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-91-1-1

That’s why the Realized One lives unperturbed, with dart drawn out. ^sn35-91-1-2

Now, a mendicant might wish: ‘May I live unperturbed, with dart drawn out.’ ^sn35-91-1-3

So let them not conceive anything to be the eye, let them not conceive it in the eye, let them not conceive it as the eye, let them not conceive that ‘the eye is mine.’ ^sn35-91-1-4

Let them not conceive sights … ^sn35-91-1-5

eye consciousness … ^sn35-91-1-6

eye contact … ^sn35-91-1-7

Let them not conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. Let them not conceive it in that, let them not conceive it as that, and let them not conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-91-1-8

For whatever you conceive it to be, whatever you conceive it in, whatever you conceive it as, and whatever you conceive to be ‘mine’: that becomes something else. ^sn35-91-1-9

The world is attached to being, taking pleasure only in being, yet it becomes something else. ^sn35-91-1-10

Let them not conceive anything to be the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-91-2-1

Let them not conceive anything to be the mind … ^sn35-91-3-1

mind consciousness … ^sn35-91-3-2

mind contact … ^sn35-91-3-3

Let them not conceive anything to be the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. Let them not conceive it in that, let them not conceive it as that, and let them not conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-91-3-4

For whatever you conceive it to be, whatever you conceive it in, whatever you conceive it as, and whatever you conceive to be ‘mine’: that becomes something else. ^sn35-91-3-5

The world is attached to being, taking pleasure only in being, yet it becomes something else. ^sn35-91-3-6

As far as the aggregates, elements, and sense fields extend, they don’t conceive anything to be that, they don’t conceive it in that, they don’t conceive it as that, and they don’t conceive that ‘that is mine.’ ^sn35-91-4-1

Not conceiving, they don’t grasp at anything in the world. ^sn35-91-4-2

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn35-91-4-3

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-91-4-4

### A Duality (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.92) ^sn35-92-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a duality. ^sn35-92-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-92-1-2

And what is a duality? ^sn35-92-1-3

It’s just the eye and sights, the ear and sounds, the nose and smells, the tongue and tastes, the body and touches, and the mind and ideas. ^sn35-92-1-4

This is called a duality. ^sn35-92-1-5

Mendicants, suppose someone was to say: ^sn35-92-2-1

‘I’ll deny this duality and describe another duality.’ They’d have no grounds for that claim, ^sn35-92-2-2

they’d be stumped by questions, and, in addition, they’d get frustrated. ^sn35-92-2-3

Why is that? ^sn35-92-2-5

Because they’re out of their element.” ^sn35-92-2-6

### A Duality (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.93) ^sn35-93-0

“Mendicants, consciousness originates dependent on a duality. ^sn35-93-1-1

And what is that duality? ^sn35-93-1-2

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. ^sn35-93-1-3

The eye is impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-4

Sights are impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-5

So this duality is tottering and toppling; it’s impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-6

Eye consciousness is impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-7

And the causes and reasons that give rise to eye consciousness are also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-8

But since eye consciousness has arisen dependent on conditions that are impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-93-1-9

The meeting, coming together, and joining together of these three things is called eye contact. ^sn35-93-1-10

Eye contact is also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-11

And the causes and reasons that give rise to eye contact are also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-12

But since eye contact has arisen dependent on conditions that are impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-93-1-13

Contacted one feels; contacted one intends; contacted one perceives. ^sn35-93-1-14

So these things too are tottering and toppling; they’re impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-1-15

Ear consciousness … Nose consciousness … Tongue consciousness … Body consciousness … ^sn35-93-2-1

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. ^sn35-93-3-1

The mind is impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-2

Ideas are impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-3

So this duality is tottering and toppling; it’s impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-4

Mind consciousness is impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-5

And the causes and reasons that give rise to mind consciousness are also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-6

But since mind consciousness has arisen dependent on conditions that are impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-93-3-7

The meeting, coming together, and joining together of these three things is called mind contact. ^sn35-93-3-8

Mind contact is also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-9

And the causes and reasons that give rise to mind contact are also impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-10

But since mind contact has arisen dependent on conditions that are impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-93-3-11

Contacted one feels; contacted one intends; contacted one perceives. ^sn35-93-3-12

So these things too are tottering and toppling; they’re impermanent, decaying, and perishing. ^sn35-93-3-13

This is how consciousness originates dependent on a duality.” ^sn35-93-3-14

### Untamed, Unguarded (Linked Discourses 35.94) ^sn35-94-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-94-1-1

“Mendicants, these six fields of contact bring suffering when they’re untamed, unguarded, unprotected, and unrestrained. ^sn35-94-1-2

What six? ^sn35-94-1-3

The field of eye contact brings suffering when it’s untamed, unguarded, unprotected, and unrestrained. ^sn35-94-1-4

The field of ear contact … nose contact … tongue contact … body contact … ^sn35-94-1-5

The field of mind contact brings suffering when it’s untamed, unguarded, unprotected, and unrestrained. ^sn35-94-1-6

These six fields of contact bring suffering when they’re untamed, unguarded, unprotected, and unrestrained. ^sn35-94-1-7

These six fields of contact bring happiness when they’re well tamed, well guarded, well protected, and well restrained. ^sn35-94-2-1

What six? ^sn35-94-2-2

The field of eye contact brings happiness when it’s well tamed, well guarded, well protected, and well restrained. ^sn35-94-2-3

The field of ear contact … nose contact … tongue contact … body contact … ^sn35-94-2-4

The field of mind contact brings happiness when it’s well tamed, well guarded, well protected, and well restrained. ^sn35-94-2-5

These six fields of contact bring happiness when they’re well tamed, well guarded, well protected, and well restrained.” ^sn35-94-2-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-94-2-7

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn35-94-2-8

“Mendicants, it’s just the six fields of contact ^sn35-94-3-1

that lead the unrestrained to suffering. ^sn35-94-3-2

Those who understand how to restrain them ^sn35-94-3-3

live with faith as partner, not festering. ^sn35-94-3-4

When you’ve seen pleasant sights ^sn35-94-4-1

and unpleasant ones, too, ^sn35-94-4-2

get rid of all manner of desire for the pleasant, ^sn35-94-4-3

without hating what you don’t like. ^sn35-94-4-4

When you’ve heard sounds both liked and disliked, ^sn35-94-5-1

don’t fall under the thrall of sounds you like, ^sn35-94-5-2

get rid of hate for the unliked, ^sn35-94-5-3

and don’t hurt your mind <j>by thinking of what you don’t like. ^sn35-94-5-4

When you’ve smelled a pleasant, fragrant scent, ^sn35-94-6-1

and one that’s foul and unpleasant, ^sn35-94-6-2

get rid of aversion for the unpleasant, ^sn35-94-6-3

while not yielding to desire for the pleasant. ^sn35-94-6-4

When you’ve enjoyed a sweet, delicious taste, ^sn35-94-7-1

and sometimes those that are bitter, ^sn35-94-7-2

don’t be attached to enjoying sweet tastes, ^sn35-94-7-3

and don’t despise the bitter. ^sn35-94-7-4

Don’t be intoxicated by a pleasant touch, ^sn35-94-8-1

and don’t tremble at a painful touch. ^sn35-94-8-2

Look with equanimity at the duality <j>of pleasant and painful contacts, ^sn35-94-8-3

without favoring or opposing anything. ^sn35-94-8-4

People generally let their perceptions proliferate; ^sn35-94-9-1

perceiving and proliferating, they are attracted. ^sn35-94-9-2

Casting aside all mental constructions of the domestic life, ^sn35-94-9-3

wander intent on renunciation. ^sn35-94-9-4

When the mind is well developed like this <j>regarding the six, ^sn35-94-10-1

it doesn’t waver at contacts at all. ^sn35-94-10-2

Mendicants, those who have mastered greed and hate ^sn35-94-10-3

go beyond birth and death.” ^sn35-94-10-4

### Māluṅkyaputta (Linked Discourses 35.95) ^sn35-95-0

Then Venerable Māluṅkyaputta went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-95-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-95-1-2

“Well now, Māluṅkyaputta, what are we to say to the young monks, ^sn35-95-2-1

when even an old man like you, elderly and senior, advanced in years, having reached the final stage of life, asks for brief advice?” ^sn35-95-2-2

“Sir, even though I’m an old man, elderly and senior, ^sn35-95-3-1

may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief! May the Holy one please teach me in brief! Hopefully I can understand the meaning of what the Buddha says. Hopefully I can be an heir of the Buddha’s teaching!” ^sn35-95-3-2

“What do you think, Māluṅkyaputta? ^sn35-95-4-1

Do you have any desire or greed or fondness for sights known by the eye that are unseen, unseen before, and which you neither see nor think might be seen?” ^sn35-95-4-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-95-4-3

“Do you have any desire or greed or affection for sounds known by the ear … ^sn35-95-5-1

smells known by the nose … ^sn35-95-6-1

tastes known by the tongue … ^sn35-95-7-1

touches known by the body … ^sn35-95-8-1

ideas known by the mind that are unknown, unknown before, and which you neither know nor think might be known?” ^sn35-95-9-1

“No, sir.” ^sn35-95-9-2

“In that case, when it comes to things that ought be seen, heard, thought, and known: in the seen will be merely the seen; in the heard will be merely the heard; in the thought will be merely the thought; in the known will be merely the known. ^sn35-95-10-1

When this is the case, ^sn35-95-10-2

you won’t be ‘by that’. ^sn35-95-10-3

When you’re not ‘by that’, ^sn35-95-10-4

you won’t be ‘in that’. ^sn35-95-10-5

When you’re not ‘in that’, ^sn35-95-10-6

you won’t be in this life or the next or in between the two. ^sn35-95-10-7

Just this is the end of suffering.” ^sn35-95-10-8

“This is how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement: ^sn35-95-11-1

‘When you see a sight, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-12-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-12-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-12-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-12-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-13-1

arising from sights. ^sn35-95-13-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-13-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-13-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-13-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-13-6

When you hear a sound, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-14-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-14-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-14-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-14-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-15-1

arising from sounds. ^sn35-95-15-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-15-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-15-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-15-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-15-6

When you smell an odor, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-16-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-16-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-16-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-16-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-17-1

arising from smells. ^sn35-95-17-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-17-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-17-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-17-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-17-6

When you enjoy a taste, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-18-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-18-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-18-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-18-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-19-1

arising from tastes. ^sn35-95-19-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-19-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-19-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-19-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-19-6

When you sense a touch, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-20-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-20-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-20-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-20-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-21-1

arising from touches. ^sn35-95-21-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-21-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-21-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-21-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-21-6

When you know an idea, mindfulness is lost ^sn35-95-22-1

as you focus on a pleasant feature. ^sn35-95-22-2

Experiencing it with a mind full of desire, ^sn35-95-22-3

you keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-22-4

Many feelings grow ^sn35-95-23-1

arising from ideas. ^sn35-95-23-2

The mind is damaged ^sn35-95-23-3

by covetousness and cruelty. ^sn35-95-23-4

Heaping up suffering like this, ^sn35-95-23-5

you’re said to be far from extinguishment. ^sn35-95-23-6

There’s no desire for sights ^sn35-95-24-1

when you see a sight with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-24-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-24-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-24-4

Even as you see a sight ^sn35-95-25-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-25-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-25-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-25-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-25-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment. ^sn35-95-25-6

There’s no desire for sounds ^sn35-95-26-1

when you hear a sound with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-26-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-26-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-26-4

Even as you hear a sound ^sn35-95-27-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-27-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-27-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-27-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-27-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment. ^sn35-95-27-6

There’s no desire for odors ^sn35-95-28-1

when you smell an odor with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-28-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-28-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-28-4

Even as you smell an odor ^sn35-95-29-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-29-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-29-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-29-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-29-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment. ^sn35-95-29-6

There’s no desire for tastes ^sn35-95-30-1

when you enjoy a taste with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-30-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-30-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-30-4

Even as you savor a taste ^sn35-95-31-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-31-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-31-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-31-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-31-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment. ^sn35-95-31-6

There’s no desire for touches ^sn35-95-32-1

when you sense a touch with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-32-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-32-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-32-4

Even as you sense a touch ^sn35-95-33-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-33-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-33-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-33-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-33-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment. ^sn35-95-33-6

There’s no desire for ideas ^sn35-95-34-1

when you know an idea with mindfulness. ^sn35-95-34-2

Experiencing it with a mind free of desire, ^sn35-95-34-3

you don’t keep clinging to it. ^sn35-95-34-4

Even as you know an idea ^sn35-95-35-1

and get familiar with how it feels, ^sn35-95-35-2

you wear away, you don’t heap up: ^sn35-95-35-3

that’s how to live mindfully. ^sn35-95-35-4

Eroding suffering like this, ^sn35-95-35-5

you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.’ ^sn35-95-35-6

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^sn35-95-36-1

“Good, good, Māluṅkyaputta! ^sn35-95-36-2

It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this.” And he repeated the verses in full. ^sn35-95-36-3

“This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” ^sn35-95-41-1

And then Venerable Māluṅkyaputta approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn35-95-42-1

Then Māluṅkyaputta, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn35-95-42-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn35-95-42-3

And Venerable Māluṅkyaputta became one of the perfected. ^sn35-95-42-4

### Liable to Decline (Linked Discourses 35.96) ^sn35-96-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you who is liable to decline, who is not liable to decline, and the six fields of mastery. ^sn35-96-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-96-1-2

And how is someone liable to decline? ^sn35-96-1-3

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eye, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-1-4

Suppose that mendicant tolerates them and doesn’t give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. They should understand: ^sn35-96-1-5

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^sn35-96-1-6

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^sn35-96-1-7

Furthermore, when a mendicant hears a sound … smells an odor … tastes a flavor … encounters a touch … ^sn35-96-2-1

knows an idea with the mind, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-2-2

If that mendicant tolerates them and doesn’t give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them, they should understand: ^sn35-96-2-3

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^sn35-96-2-4

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^sn35-96-2-5

That’s how someone is liable to decline. ^sn35-96-2-6

And how is someone not liable to decline? ^sn35-96-3-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eye, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-3-2

Suppose that mendicant doesn’t tolerate them but gives them up, gets rid of them, eliminates them, and obliterates them. They should understand: ^sn35-96-3-3

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^sn35-96-3-4

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’ ^sn35-96-3-5

Furthermore, when a mendicant hears a sound … smells an odor … tastes a flavor … encounters a touch … ^sn35-96-4-1

knows an idea with the mind, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-4-2

Suppose that mendicant doesn’t tolerate them but gives them up, gets rid of them, eliminates them, and obliterates them. They should understand: ^sn35-96-4-3

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^sn35-96-4-4

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’ ^sn35-96-4-5

That’s how someone is not liable to decline. ^sn35-96-4-6

And what are the six fields of mastery? ^sn35-96-5-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eye, bad, unskillful phenomena don’t arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-5-2

They should understand: ^sn35-96-5-3

‘This sense field has been mastered. ^sn35-96-5-4

For this is what the Buddha calls a field of mastery.’ … ^sn35-96-5-5

Furthermore, when a mendicant knows an idea with the mind, bad, unskillful phenomena don’t arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-96-5-6

They should understand: ^sn35-96-5-7

‘This sense field has been mastered. ^sn35-96-5-8

For this is what the Buddha calls a field of mastery.’ ^sn35-96-5-9

These are the six fields of mastery.” ^sn35-96-5-10

### One Who Lives Negligently (Linked Discourses 35.97) ^sn35-97-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you who lives negligently and who lives diligently. ^sn35-97-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-97-1-2

And how does someone live negligently? ^sn35-97-1-3

When you live with the eye faculty unrestrained, your mind becomes polluted when it comes to sights known by the eye. ^sn35-97-1-4

When the mind is polluted, there’s no joy. ^sn35-97-1-5

When there’s no joy, there’s no rapture. ^sn35-97-1-6

When there’s no rapture, there’s no tranquility. ^sn35-97-1-7

When there’s no tranquility, there’s suffering. ^sn35-97-1-8

When one is suffering, the mind does not become immersed in samādhi. ^sn35-97-1-9

When the mind is not immersed in samādhi, principles do not become clear. ^sn35-97-1-10

Because principles have not become clear, you’re reckoned to live negligently. ^sn35-97-1-11

When you live with the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-97-1-12

mind faculty unrestrained, your mind becomes polluted when it comes to ideas known by the mind. ^sn35-97-1-15

When the mind is polluted, there’s no joy. ^sn35-97-1-16

When there’s no joy, there’s no rapture. ^sn35-97-1-17

When there’s no rapture, there’s no tranquility. ^sn35-97-1-18

When there’s no tranquility, there’s suffering. ^sn35-97-1-19

When one is suffering, the mind does not become immersed in samādhi. ^sn35-97-1-20

When the mind is not immersed in samādhi, principles do not become clear. ^sn35-97-1-21

Because principles have not become clear, you’re reckoned to live negligently. ^sn35-97-1-22

That’s how someone lives negligently. ^sn35-97-1-23

And how does someone live diligently? ^sn35-97-2-1

When you live with the eye faculty restrained, your mind doesn’t become polluted when it comes to sights known by the eye. ^sn35-97-2-2

When the mind isn’t polluted, joy springs up. ^sn35-97-2-3

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^sn35-97-2-4

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^sn35-97-2-5

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^sn35-97-2-6

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn35-97-2-7

When the mind is immersed in samādhi, principles become clear. ^sn35-97-2-8

Because principles have become clear, you’re reckoned to live diligently. ^sn35-97-2-9

When you live with the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-97-2-10

mind faculty restrained, your mind doesn’t become polluted when it comes to ideas known by the mind. ^sn35-97-2-12

When the mind isn’t polluted, joy springs up. ^sn35-97-2-13

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^sn35-97-2-14

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^sn35-97-2-15

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^sn35-97-2-16

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn35-97-2-17

When the mind is immersed in samādhi, principles become clear. ^sn35-97-2-18

Because principles have become clear, you’re reckoned to live diligently. ^sn35-97-2-19

That’s how someone lives diligently.” ^sn35-97-2-20

### Restraint (Linked Discourses 35.98) ^sn35-98-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you who is restrained and who is unrestrained. ^sn35-98-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-98-1-2

And how is someone unrestrained? ^sn35-98-1-3

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-98-1-4

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, they should understand: ^sn35-98-1-5

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^sn35-98-1-6

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^sn35-98-1-7

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-98-1-8

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-98-1-9

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, they should understand: ^sn35-98-1-10

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^sn35-98-1-11

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^sn35-98-1-12

This is how someone is unrestrained. ^sn35-98-1-13

And how is someone restrained? ^sn35-98-2-1

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-98-2-2

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, they should understand: ^sn35-98-2-3

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^sn35-98-2-4

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’ ^sn35-98-2-5

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-98-2-6

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-98-2-7

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, they should understand: ^sn35-98-2-8

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^sn35-98-2-9

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’ ^sn35-98-2-10

This is how someone is restrained.” ^sn35-98-2-11

### Immersion (Linked Discourses 35.99) ^sn35-99-0

“Mendicants, develop immersion. ^sn35-99-1-1

A mendicant who has immersion truly understands. ^sn35-99-1-2

What do they truly understand? ^sn35-99-1-3

They truly understand that the eye is impermanent. ^sn35-99-1-4

They truly understand that sights … ^sn35-99-1-5

eye consciousness … ^sn35-99-1-6

eye contact … ^sn35-99-1-7

the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is impermanent. … ^sn35-99-1-8

They truly understand that the mind is impermanent. ^sn35-99-1-9

They truly understand that ideas … ^sn35-99-1-10

mind consciousness … ^sn35-99-1-11

mind contact … ^sn35-99-1-12

the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is impermanent. ^sn35-99-1-13

Mendicants, develop immersion. ^sn35-99-1-14

A mendicant who has immersion truly understands.” ^sn35-99-1-15

### Retreat (Linked Discourses 35.100) ^sn35-100-0

“Mendicants, meditate in retreat. ^sn35-100-1-1

A mendicant in retreat truly understands. ^sn35-100-1-2

What do they truly understand? ^sn35-100-1-3

They truly understand that the eye is impermanent. ^sn35-100-1-4

They truly understand that sights … ^sn35-100-1-5

eye consciousness … ^sn35-100-1-6

eye contact … ^sn35-100-1-7

the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is impermanent. ^sn35-100-1-8

Mendicants, meditate in retreat. ^sn35-100-1-9

A mendicant in retreat truly understands.” ^sn35-100-1-10

### It’s Not Yours (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.101) ^sn35-101-0

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn35-101-1-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-101-1-2

And what isn’t yours? ^sn35-101-1-3

The eye isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-1-4

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-101-1-6

Sights … ^sn35-101-1-7

Eye consciousness … ^sn35-101-1-10

Eye contact … ^sn35-101-1-13

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-1-16

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-101-1-18

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-101-2-1

The mind isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-3-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-101-3-3

Ideas … ^sn35-101-3-4

Mind consciousness … ^sn35-101-3-7

Mind contact … ^sn35-101-3-10

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-3-13

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-101-3-15

Suppose a person was to carry off the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove, or burn them, or do what they want with them. ^sn35-101-4-1

Would you think: ^sn35-101-4-2

‘This person is carrying us off, burning us, or doing what they want with us’?” ^sn35-101-4-3

“No, sir. ^sn35-101-5-1

Why is that? ^sn35-101-6-1

Because  to us that’s neither self nor belonging to self.” ^sn35-101-7-1

“In the same way, the eye isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-8-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. … ^sn35-101-8-3

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-101-8-7

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn35-101-8-9

### It’s Not Yours (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.102) ^sn35-102-0

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn35-102-1-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-102-1-2

And what isn’t yours? ^sn35-102-1-3

The eye isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-102-1-4

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-102-1-6

Sights … ^sn35-102-1-7

Eye consciousness … ^sn35-102-1-10

Eye contact … ^sn35-102-1-13

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-102-1-16

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-102-1-18

Give up what’s not yours. ^sn35-102-1-19

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn35-102-1-20

### About Uddaka (Linked Discourses 35.103) ^sn35-103-0

“Mendicants, Uddaka son of Rāma used to say: ^sn35-103-1-1

‘Hereupon, ever the knowledge master! ^sn35-103-1-2

Hereupon, ever the conqueror of all! ^sn35-103-1-3

Hereupon, ever the undug ^sn35-103-1-4

boil’s root was dug out by me!’ ^sn35-103-1-5

Even though Uddaka son of Rāma was no knowledge master, he said ‘I’m a knowledge master.’ Though he was no conqueror of all, he said ‘I’m conqueror of all.’ And though the boil’s root was not dug out, he said ‘I’ve dug out the boil’s root.’ ^sn35-103-1-6

A mendicant might rightly say here: ^sn35-103-1-7

‘Hereupon, ever the knowledge master! ^sn35-103-1-8

Hereupon, ever the conqueror of all! ^sn35-103-1-9

Hereupon, ever the undug ^sn35-103-1-10

boil’s root is dug out!’ ^sn35-103-1-11

And how is someone a knowledge master? ^sn35-103-2-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn35-103-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is a knowledge master. ^sn35-103-2-3

And how is a mendicant a conqueror of all? ^sn35-103-3-1

It’s when a mendicant comes to be freed by not grasping after truly understanding the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^sn35-103-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is a conqueror of all. ^sn35-103-3-3

And how has a mendicant dug out the undug boil’s root? ^sn35-103-4-1

‘Boil’ is a term for this body made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^sn35-103-4-2

‘Boil’s root’ is a term for craving. ^sn35-103-4-3

It’s when a mendicant has given up craving, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn35-103-4-4

That’s how a mendicant has dug out the undug boil’s root. ^sn35-103-4-5

Uddaka son of Rāma used to say: ^sn35-103-5-1

‘Hereupon, ever the knowledge master! ^sn35-103-5-2

Hereupon, ever the conqueror of all! ^sn35-103-5-3

Hereupon, ever the undug ^sn35-103-5-4

boil’s root is dug out!’ ^sn35-103-5-5

Even though Uddaka son of Rāma was no knowledge master, he said ‘I’m a knowledge master.’ Though he was no conqueror of all, he said ‘I’m conqueror of all.’ And though the boil’s root was not dug out, he said ‘I’ve dug out the boil’s root.’ ^sn35-103-5-6

A mendicant might rightly say here: ^sn35-103-5-7

‘Hereupon, ever the knowledge master! ^sn35-103-5-8

Hereupon, ever the conqueror of all! ^sn35-103-5-9

Hereupon, ever the undug ^sn35-103-5-10

boil’s root is dug out!’” ^sn35-103-5-11

### Sanctuary from the Yoke (Linked Discourses 35.104) ^sn35-104-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn35-104-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you an exposition of the teaching, an explanation of one who has found sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn35-104-1-2

Listen … ^sn35-104-1-3

And what is an exposition of the teaching, an explanation of one who has found sanctuary from the yoke? ^sn35-104-1-4

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-104-1-5

The Realized One has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn35-104-1-6

He teaches yoking oneself to meditation for giving them up. That’s why the Realized One is called one who has found sanctuary from the yoke. … ^sn35-104-1-7

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-104-1-8

The Realized One has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn35-104-1-9

He teaches yoking oneself to meditation for giving them up. That’s why the Realized One is called one who has found sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn35-104-1-10

This is an exposition of the teaching, an explanation of one who has found sanctuary from the yoke.” ^sn35-104-1-11

### Because of Grasping (Linked Discourses 35.105) ^sn35-105-0

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what, do pleasure and pain arise in oneself?” ^sn35-105-1-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn35-105-2-1

“Mendicants, when there’s an eye, because of grasping the eye, pleasure and pain arise in oneself. … ^sn35-105-3-1

When there’s a mind, because of grasping the mind, pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^sn35-105-3-2

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-105-3-3

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-105-3-4

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-105-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-105-5-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-105-6-1

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would pleasure and pain arise in oneself?” ^sn35-105-7-1

“No, sir.” … ^sn35-105-8-1

“Is the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-105-9-1

mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-105-15-1

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-105-16-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-105-17-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-105-18-1

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would pleasure and pain arise in oneself?” ^sn35-105-19-1

“No, sir.” ^sn35-105-20-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-105-21-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-105-21-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-105-21-3

### The Origin of Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.106) ^sn35-106-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the origin and disappearance of suffering. ^sn35-106-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-106-1-2

And what, mendicants, is the origin of suffering? ^sn35-106-1-3

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-106-1-4

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-106-1-5

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-106-1-6

This is the origin of suffering … ^sn35-106-1-7

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. ^sn35-106-1-11

The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-106-1-12

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-106-1-13

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-106-1-14

This is the origin of suffering. ^sn35-106-1-15

And what is the disappearance of suffering? ^sn35-106-2-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-106-2-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-106-2-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-106-2-4

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-106-2-5

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn35-106-2-6

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn35-106-2-7

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn35-106-2-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn35-106-2-9

This is the disappearance of suffering. … ^sn35-106-2-10

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-106-2-12

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-106-2-13

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-106-2-14

When that craving fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-106-2-15

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn35-106-2-16

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn35-106-2-17

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn35-106-2-18

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn35-106-2-19

This is the disappearance of suffering.” ^sn35-106-2-20

### The Origin of the World (Linked Discourses 35.107) ^sn35-107-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the origin and disappearance of the world. ^sn35-107-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-107-1-2

And what, mendicants, is the origin of the world? ^sn35-107-1-3

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-107-1-4

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-107-1-5

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-107-1-6

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn35-107-1-7

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn35-107-1-8

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn35-107-1-9

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn35-107-1-10

This is the origin of the world. … ^sn35-107-1-11

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-107-1-13

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-107-1-14

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-107-1-15

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn35-107-1-16

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn35-107-1-17

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn35-107-1-18

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn35-107-1-19

This is the origin of the world. ^sn35-107-1-20

And what is the ending of the world? ^sn35-107-2-1

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-107-2-2

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-107-2-3

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-107-2-4

When that craving fades away and ceases no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-107-2-5

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn35-107-2-6

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn35-107-2-7

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn35-107-2-8

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn35-107-2-9

This is the ending of the world. … ^sn35-107-2-10

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-107-2-12

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-107-2-13

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-107-2-14

When that craving fades away and ceases no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-107-2-15

When grasping ceases … ^sn35-107-2-16

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn35-107-2-17

This is the ending of the world.” ^sn35-107-2-18

### I’m Better (Linked Discourses 35.108) ^sn35-108-0

“Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, do people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’?” ^sn35-108-1-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn35-108-2-1

“When there’s an eye, because of grasping the eye and insisting on the eye, people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. … ^sn35-108-3-1

When there’s a mind, because of grasping the mind and insisting on the mind, people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn35-108-3-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-108-3-4

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-108-3-5

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-108-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-108-5-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-108-6-1

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’?” ^sn35-108-7-1

“No, sir.” … ^sn35-108-8-1

“Is the mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-108-10-1

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-108-11-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-108-12-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-108-13-1

“But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would people think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’?” ^sn35-108-14-1

“No, sir.” ^sn35-108-15-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-108-16-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-108-16-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-108-16-3

### Things That Tighten Fetters (Linked Discourses 35.109) ^sn35-109-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that tighten the fetters, and the fetter. ^sn35-109-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-109-1-2

What are the things that tighten the fetters? And what is the fetter? ^sn35-109-1-3

The eye is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn35-109-1-4

The desire and greed for it is the fetter. ^sn35-109-1-5

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-109-1-6

mind is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn35-109-1-7

The desire and greed for it is the fetter. ^sn35-109-1-8

These are called the things that tighten the fetters, and this is the fetter.” ^sn35-109-1-9

### Things That Fuel Grasping (Linked Discourses 35.110) ^sn35-110-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that fuel grasping, and the grasping. ^sn35-110-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-110-1-2

What are the things that fuel grasping? And what is the grasping? ^sn35-110-1-3

The eye is something that fuels grasping. ^sn35-110-1-4

The desire and greed for it is the grasping. ^sn35-110-1-5

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-110-1-6

mind is something that fuels grasping. ^sn35-110-1-7

The desire and greed for it is the grasping. ^sn35-110-1-8

These are called the things that fuel grasping, and this is the grasping.” ^sn35-110-1-9

### Complete Understanding of the Interior (Linked Discourses 35.111) ^sn35-111-0

“Mendicants, without directly knowing and completely understanding the eye, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn35-111-1-1

Without directly knowing and completely understanding the ear … ^sn35-111-1-2

nose … ^sn35-111-1-3

tongue … ^sn35-111-1-4

body … ^sn35-111-1-5

mind, without dispassion for it and giving it up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn35-111-1-6

By directly knowing and completely understanding the eye, having dispassion for it and giving it up, you can end suffering. ^sn35-111-1-7

By directly knowing and completely understanding the ear … nose … tongue … ^sn35-111-1-8

body … ^sn35-111-1-9

mind, having dispassion for it and giving it up, you can end suffering.” ^sn35-111-1-10

### Complete Understanding of the Exterior (Linked Discourses 35.112) ^sn35-112-0

“Mendicants, without directly knowing and completely understanding sights … ^sn35-112-1-1

sounds … ^sn35-112-1-2

smells … ^sn35-112-1-3

tastes … ^sn35-112-1-4

touches … ^sn35-112-1-5

ideas, without dispassion for them and giving them up, you can’t end suffering. ^sn35-112-1-6

By directly knowing and completely understanding sights … ^sn35-112-1-7

sounds … ^sn35-112-1-8

smells … ^sn35-112-1-9

tastes … ^sn35-112-1-10

touches … ^sn35-112-1-11

ideas, having dispassion for them and giving them up, you can end suffering.” ^sn35-112-1-12

### Listening In (Linked Discourses 35.113) ^sn35-113-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^sn35-113-1-1

Then while the Buddha was in private retreat he spoke this exposition of the teaching: ^sn35-113-1-2

“Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-113-1-3

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-113-1-4

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-113-1-5

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn35-113-1-6

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn35-113-1-7

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn35-113-1-8

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn35-113-1-9

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn35-113-1-10

Ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-113-1-11

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-113-1-12

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-113-1-13

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-113-1-14

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^sn35-113-1-15

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^sn35-113-1-16

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^sn35-113-1-17

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn35-113-1-18

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn35-113-1-19

Eye consciousness arises dependent on the eye and sights. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-113-2-1

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-113-2-2

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-113-2-3

When that craving fades away and ceases no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-113-2-4

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^sn35-113-2-5

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^sn35-113-2-6

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn35-113-2-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn35-113-2-8

Ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-113-2-9

Mind consciousness arises dependent on the mind and ideas. The meeting of the three is contact. ^sn35-113-2-10

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^sn35-113-2-11

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^sn35-113-2-12

When that craving fades away and ceases no residue left behind, grasping ceases. ^sn35-113-2-13

When grasping ceases … ^sn35-113-2-14

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.” ^sn35-113-2-15

Now at that time a certain monk was standing listening in on the Buddha. ^sn35-113-3-1

The Buddha saw him ^sn35-113-3-2

and said, ^sn35-113-3-3

“Monk, did you hear that exposition of the teaching?” ^sn35-113-3-4

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-113-3-5

“Learn that exposition of the teaching, ^sn35-113-3-6

memorize it, ^sn35-113-3-7

and remember it. ^sn35-113-3-8

That exposition of the teaching is beneficial and relevant to the fundamentals of the spiritual life.” ^sn35-113-3-9

### Māra’s Snare (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.114) ^sn35-114-0

“Mendicants, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-114-1-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-114-1-2

they’re called a mendicant trapped in Māra’s lair, fallen under Māra’s sway, and caught in Māra’s snare. ^sn35-114-1-3

They’re bound by Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-114-1-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-114-2-1

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-114-3-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-114-3-2

they’re called a mendicant trapped in Māra’s lair, fallen under Māra’s sway, and caught in Māra’s snare. ^sn35-114-3-3

They’re bound by Māra, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-114-3-4

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-114-4-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-114-4-2

they’re called a mendicant not trapped in Māra’s lair, not fallen under Māra’s sway, and released from Māra’s snare. ^sn35-114-4-3

They’re free from Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants. ^sn35-114-4-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-114-5-1

There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-114-6-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-114-6-2

they’re called a mendicant not trapped in Māra’s lair, not fallen under Māra’s sway, and released from Māra’s snare. ^sn35-114-6-3

They’re free from Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants.” ^sn35-114-6-4

### Māra’s Snare (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.115) ^sn35-115-0

“Mendicants, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-115-1-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-115-1-2

they’re called a mendicant who is bound in sights known by the eye. They’re trapped in Māra’s lair, fallen under Māra’s sway, and caught in Māra’s snare. ^sn35-115-1-3

They’re bound by Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-115-1-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-115-2-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-115-2-2

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-115-2-3

they’re called a mendicant who is bound in ideas known by the mind. They’re trapped in Māra’s lair, fallen under Māra’s sway, and caught in Māra’s snare. ^sn35-115-2-4

They’re bound by Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-115-2-5

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-115-3-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-115-3-2

they’re called a mendicant who is freed from sights known by the eye. They’re not trapped in Māra’s lair, not fallen under Māra’s sway, and released from Māra’s snare. ^sn35-115-3-3

They’re free from Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants. ^sn35-115-3-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-115-4-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-115-4-2

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-115-4-3

they’re called a mendicant who is freed from ideas known by the mind. They’re not trapped in Māra’s lair, not fallen under Māra’s sway, and released from Māra’s snare. ^sn35-115-4-4

They’re free from Māra’s bonds, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants.” ^sn35-115-4-5

### Traveling to the End of the World (Linked Discourses 35.116) ^sn35-116-0

“Mendicants, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling. ^sn35-116-1-1

But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world.” ^sn35-116-1-2

When he had spoken, the Blessed One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^sn35-116-1-3

Soon after the Buddha left, those mendicants considered, ^sn35-116-1-4

“The Buddha gave this brief summary recital, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail. … ^sn35-116-1-5

Who can explain in detail the meaning of this brief summary recital given by the Buddha?” ^sn35-116-1-8

Then those mendicants thought, ^sn35-116-2-1

“This Venerable Ānanda is praised by the Buddha and esteemed by his sensible spiritual companions. ^sn35-116-2-2

He is capable of explaining in detail the meaning of this brief summary recital given by the Buddha. ^sn35-116-2-3

Let’s go to him, and ask him about this matter.” ^sn35-116-2-4

Then those mendicants went to Ānanda and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-116-3-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. They told him what had happened, and said, ^sn35-116-3-2

“May Venerable Ānanda please explain this.” ^sn35-116-4-13

“Reverends, suppose there was a person in need of heartwood. And while wandering in search of heartwood he’d come across a large tree standing with heartwood. But he’d pass over the roots and trunk, imagining that the heartwood should be sought in the branches and leaves. ^sn35-116-5-1

Such is the consequence for the venerables. Though you were face to face with the Buddha, you bypassed him, imagining that you should ask me about this matter. ^sn35-116-5-2

For he is the Buddha, the one who knows and sees. He is vision, he is knowledge, he is the manifestation of principle, he is the manifestation of divinity. He is the teacher, the proclaimer, the elucidator of meaning, the bestower of freedom from death, the lord of truth, the Realized One. ^sn35-116-5-3

That was the time to approach the Buddha and ask about this matter. ^sn35-116-5-5

You should have remembered it in line with the Buddha’s answer.” ^sn35-116-5-6

“Certainly he is the Buddha, the one who knows and sees. He is vision, he is knowledge, he is the manifestation of principle, he is the manifestation of divinity. He is the teacher, the proclaimer, the elucidator of meaning, the bestower of freedom from death, the lord of truth, the Realized One. ^sn35-116-6-1

That was the time to approach the Buddha and ask about this matter. ^sn35-116-6-3

We should have remembered it in line with the Buddha’s answer. ^sn35-116-6-4

Still, Venerable Ānanda is praised by the Buddha and esteemed by his sensible spiritual companions. ^sn35-116-6-5

You are capable of explaining in detail the meaning of this brief summary recital given by the Buddha. ^sn35-116-6-6

Please explain this, if it’s no trouble.” ^sn35-116-6-7

“Then listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn35-116-7-1

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^sn35-116-7-2

Ānanda said this: ^sn35-116-7-3

“Reverends, the Buddha gave this brief summary recital, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail: ^sn35-116-8-1

‘Mendicants, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling. ^sn35-116-8-2

But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world.’ ^sn35-116-8-3

This is how I understand the detailed meaning of this summary recital. ^sn35-116-8-4

Whatever in the world through which you perceive the world and conceive the world ^sn35-116-8-5

is called the world in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-116-8-6

And through what in the world do you perceive the world and conceive the world? ^sn35-116-8-7

Through the eye in the world you perceive the world and conceive the world. ^sn35-116-8-8

Through the ear … ^sn35-116-8-9

nose … ^sn35-116-8-10

tongue … ^sn35-116-8-11

body … ^sn35-116-8-12

mind in the world you perceive the world and conceive the world. ^sn35-116-8-13

Whatever in the world through which you perceive the world and conceive the world ^sn35-116-8-14

is called the world in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-116-8-15

When the Buddha gave this brief summary recital, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail: ^sn35-116-8-16

‘Mendicants, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling. ^sn35-116-8-17

But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world.’ ^sn35-116-8-18

That is how I understand the detailed meaning of this summary. ^sn35-116-8-19

If you wish, you may go to the Buddha and ask him about this. ^sn35-116-8-20

You should remember it in line with the Buddha’s answer.” ^sn35-116-8-21

“Yes, reverend,” replied those mendicants. Then they rose from their seats and went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn35-116-9-1

Then they said, “And Ānanda explained the meaning to us in this manner, with these words and phrases.” ^sn35-116-10-14

“Mendicants, Ānanda is astute, he has great wisdom. ^sn35-116-11-1

If you came to me and asked this question, I would answer it in exactly the same way as Ānanda. ^sn35-116-11-2

That is what it means, and that’s how you should remember it.” ^sn35-116-11-3

### The Kinds of Sensual Stimulation (Linked Discourses 35.117) ^sn35-117-0

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^sn35-117-1-1

‘My mind might often stray towards the five kinds of sensual stimulation that I formerly experienced—which have passed, ceased, and perished—or to those in the present, or in the future a little.’ ^sn35-117-1-2

Then it occurred to me: ^sn35-117-1-3

‘In my own way I should practice diligence, mindfulness, and protecting the mind regarding the five kinds of sensual stimulation that I formerly experienced—which have passed, ceased, and perished.’ ^sn35-117-1-4

So, mendicants, your minds might also often stray towards the five kinds of sensual stimulation that you formerly experienced—which have passed, ceased, and perished—or to those in the present, or in the future a little. ^sn35-117-1-5

So in your own way you should practice diligence, mindfulness, and protecting the mind regarding the five kinds of sensual stimulation that you formerly experienced—which have passed, ceased, and perished. ^sn35-117-1-6

So you should understand that dimension where the eye ceases and perception of sights fades away. ^sn35-117-1-7

You should understand that dimension where the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-117-1-8

mind ceases and perception of ideas fades away.” ^sn35-117-1-9

When he had spoken, the Blessed One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^sn35-117-1-10

Soon after the Buddha left, those mendicants considered, ^sn35-117-2-1

“The Buddha gave this brief summary, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail. … ^sn35-117-2-2

Who can explain in detail the meaning of this brief summary given by the Buddha?” ^sn35-117-2-6

Then those mendicants thought, ^sn35-117-3-1

“This Venerable Ānanda is praised by the Buddha and esteemed by his sensible spiritual companions. ^sn35-117-3-2

He is capable of explaining in detail the meaning of this brief summary given by the Buddha. ^sn35-117-3-3

Let’s go to him, and ask him about this matter.” ^sn35-117-3-4

Then those mendicants went to Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-117-4-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. They told him what had happened, and said, ^sn35-117-4-2

“May Venerable Ānanda please explain this.” ^sn35-117-5-15

“Reverends, suppose there was a person in need of heartwood. …” ^sn35-117-6-1

“Please explain this, if it’s no trouble.” ^sn35-117-6-2

“Then listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn35-117-7-1

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^sn35-117-7-2

Ānanda said this: ^sn35-117-7-3

“Reverends, the Buddha gave this brief summary, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail: ^sn35-117-8-1

‘So you should understand that dimension where the eye ceases and perception of sights fades away. You should understand that dimension where the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-117-8-2

mind ceases and perception of ideas fades away.’ ^sn35-117-8-3

And this is how I understand the detailed meaning of this summary. ^sn35-117-8-4

The Buddha was referring to the cessation of the six sense fields when he said: ^sn35-117-8-5

‘So you should understand that dimension where the eye ceases and perception of sights fades away. You should understand that dimension where the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-117-8-6

mind ceases and perception of ideas fades away.’ ^sn35-117-8-7

The Buddha gave this brief summary, then entered his dwelling without explaining the meaning in detail. ^sn35-117-8-8

And this is how I understand the detailed meaning of this summary. ^sn35-117-8-11

If you wish, you may go to the Buddha and ask him about this. ^sn35-117-8-12

You should remember it in line with the Buddha’s answer.” ^sn35-117-8-14

“Yes, reverend,” replied those mendicants. Then they rose from their seats and went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn35-117-9-1

Then they said, “And Ānanda explained the meaning to us in this manner, with these words and phrases.” ^sn35-117-10-14

“Mendicants, Ānanda is astute, he has great wisdom. ^sn35-117-11-1

If you came to me and asked this question, I would answer it in exactly the same way as Ānanda. ^sn35-117-11-2

That is what it means, and that’s how you should remember it.” ^sn35-117-11-3

### The Question of Sakka (Linked Discourses 35.118) ^sn35-118-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn35-118-1-1

And then Sakka, lord of gods, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-118-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-118-1-3

What is the cause, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” ^sn35-118-1-4

“Lord of gods, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-118-2-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-118-2-2

their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-118-2-3

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-118-2-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-118-3-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-118-3-2

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-118-3-3

their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-118-3-4

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-118-3-5

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life. ^sn35-118-3-6

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-118-4-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-118-4-2

their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-118-4-3

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-118-4-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-118-5-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-118-5-2

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-118-5-3

their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-118-5-4

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-118-5-5

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.” ^sn35-118-5-6

### The Question of Pañcasikha (Linked Discourses 35.119) ^sn35-119-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn35-119-1-1

And then the centaur Pañcasikha went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-119-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-119-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” ^sn35-119-1-4

“Pañcasikha, there are sights known by the eye … ^sn35-119-1-5

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-119-1-6

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-119-1-7
