their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-119-1-8

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-119-1-9

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life. ^sn35-119-1-10

There are sights known by the eye … ^sn35-119-2-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-119-2-2

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-119-2-3

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-119-2-4

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.” ^sn35-119-2-5

### Sāriputta and the Protégé (Linked Discourses 35.120) ^sn35-120-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn35-120-1-1

Then a certain mendicant went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-120-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn35-120-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, a mendicant protégé of mine has resigned the training and returned to a lesser life.” ^sn35-120-1-4

“That’s how it is, reverend, when someone doesn’t guard the sense doors, eats too much, and is not committed to wakefulness. ^sn35-120-2-1

It is quite impossible for such a mendicant to maintain the full and pure spiritual life for the rest of their life. ^sn35-120-2-2

But it is quite possible for a mendicant to maintain the full and pure spiritual life for the rest of their life if they guard the sense doors, eat in moderation, and are committed to wakefulness. ^sn35-120-2-3

And how does someone guard the sense doors? ^sn35-120-3-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-120-3-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-120-3-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^sn35-120-3-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^sn35-120-3-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^sn35-120-3-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^sn35-120-3-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-120-3-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-120-3-9

That’s how someone guards the sense doors. ^sn35-120-3-10

And how does someone eat in moderation? ^sn35-120-4-1

It’s when a mendicant reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^sn35-120-4-2

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^sn35-120-4-3

That’s how someone eats in moderation. ^sn35-120-4-4

And how is someone committed to wakefulness? ^sn35-120-5-1

It’s when a mendicant practices walking and sitting meditation by day, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^sn35-120-5-2

In the first watch of the night, they continue to practice walking and sitting meditation. ^sn35-120-5-3

In the middle watch, they lie down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^sn35-120-5-4

In the last watch, they get up and continue to practice walking and sitting meditation, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^sn35-120-5-5

That’s how someone is committed to wakefulness. ^sn35-120-5-6

So you should train like this: ^sn35-120-5-7

‘We will guard the sense doors, eat in moderation, and be committed to wakefulness.’ ^sn35-120-5-8

That’s how you should train.” ^sn35-120-5-9

### Advice to Rāhula (Linked Discourses 35.121) ^sn35-121-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn35-121-1-1

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^sn35-121-1-2

“The qualities that ripen in freedom have ripened in Rāhula. ^sn35-121-1-3

Why don’t I lead him further to the ending of defilements?” ^sn35-121-1-4

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. ^sn35-121-1-5

After the meal, on his return from almsround, he addressed Venerable Rāhula, ^sn35-121-1-6

“Rāhula, get your sitting cloth. ^sn35-121-1-7

Let’s go to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation.” ^sn35-121-1-8

“Yes, sir,” replied Rāhula. Taking his sitting cloth he followed behind the Buddha. ^sn35-121-1-9

Now at that time many thousands of deities followed the Buddha, thinking, ^sn35-121-2-1

“Today the Buddha will lead Rāhula further to the ending of defilements!” ^sn35-121-2-2

Then the Buddha plunged deep into the Dark Forest and sat at the root of a tree on the seat spread out. ^sn35-121-2-3

Rāhula bowed to the Buddha and sat down to one side. ^sn35-121-2-4

The Buddha said to him: ^sn35-121-2-5

“What do you think, Rāhula? ^sn35-121-3-1

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-121-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-121-5-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-121-6-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-121-7-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-121-7-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-121-8-1

“Are sights … ^sn35-121-9-1

eye consciousness … ^sn35-121-11-1

eye contact permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-13-1

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn35-121-14-1

“Anything included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that arises dependent on eye contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-15-1

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-121-16-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-121-17-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-121-18-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-121-19-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-121-19-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-121-20-1

“Is the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-121-21-1

mind permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-33-1

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-121-34-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-121-35-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-121-36-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-121-37-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-121-37-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-121-38-1

“Are ideas … ^sn35-121-39-1

mind consciousness … ^sn35-121-41-1

mind contact permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-43-1

“Impermanent, sir.” … ^sn35-121-44-1

“Anything included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-121-45-1

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-121-46-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-121-47-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-121-48-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-121-49-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-121-49-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-121-50-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they become disillusioned with anything included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-121-51-1

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-121-51-2

They grow disillusioned with the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact. And they grow disillusioned with anything included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-121-52-1

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-121-52-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-121-52-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-121-53-1

Satisfied, Venerable Rāhula approved what the Buddha said. ^sn35-121-53-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, Rāhula’s mind was freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn35-121-53-3

And the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in those thousands of deities: ^sn35-121-53-4

“Everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease.” ^sn35-121-53-5

### Things That Tighten Fetters (Linked Discourses 35.122) ^sn35-122-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that tighten the fetters, and the fetter. ^sn35-122-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-122-1-2

What are the things that tighten the fetters? And what is the fetter? ^sn35-122-1-3

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-122-1-4

These are called the things that tighten the fetters. ^sn35-122-1-5

The desire and greed for them is the fetter. ^sn35-122-1-6

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-122-1-7

thoughts known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-122-1-8

These are called the things that tighten the fetters. ^sn35-122-1-9

The desire and greed for them is the fetter.” ^sn35-122-1-10

### Things That Fuel Grasping (Linked Discourses 35.123) ^sn35-123-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the things that fuel grasping, and the grasping. ^sn35-123-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-123-1-2

What are the things that fuel grasping? And what is the grasping? ^sn35-123-1-3

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-123-1-4

These are called the things that fuel grasping. ^sn35-123-1-5

The desire and greed for them is the grasping. ^sn35-123-1-6

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-123-1-7

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-123-1-8

These are called the things that fuel grasping. ^sn35-123-1-9

The desire and greed for them is the grasping.” ^sn35-123-1-10

### At Vesālī (Linked Discourses 35.124) ^sn35-124-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn35-124-1-1

Then the householder Ugga of Vesālī went up to the Buddha, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-124-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-124-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” ^sn35-124-1-4

“Householder, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-124-2-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-124-2-2

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-124-2-3

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-124-2-4

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-124-2-5

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-124-2-6

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-124-2-7

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life. ^sn35-124-2-8

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-124-3-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-124-3-2

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-124-3-3

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-124-3-4

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-124-3-5

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-124-3-6

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-124-3-7

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.” ^sn35-124-3-8

Then the householder Uggata of Elephant Village went up to the Buddha, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-125-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-125-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” … ^sn35-125-1-4

Then the householder Upāli went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-126-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-126-1-3

### With Bhāradvāja (Linked Discourses 35.127) ^sn35-127-0

At one time Venerable Bhāradvāja the Alms-Gatherer was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn35-127-1-1

Then King Udena went up to Bhāradvāja the Alms-Gatherer and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-127-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-127-1-3

“Worthy Bhāradvāja, there are these young monks who are youthful, with pristine black hair, blessed with youth, in the prime of life; and they’ve never played around with sensual pleasures. What is the cause, what is the reason why they practice the full and pure spiritual life as long as they live, maintaining it for a long time?” ^sn35-127-1-4

“Great king, this has been stated by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha: ^sn35-127-1-5

‘Please, monks, think of women your mother’s age as your mother. Think of women your sister’s age as your sister. And think of women your daughter’s age as your daughter.’ ^sn35-127-1-6

This is a cause, great king, this is a reason why these young monks practice the full and pure spiritual life as long as they live, maintaining it for a long time.” ^sn35-127-1-7

“But worthy Bhāradvāja, the mind is wanton. ^sn35-127-2-1

Sometimes thoughts of desire come up even for women your mother’s age, your sister’s age, or your daughter’s age. ^sn35-127-2-2

Is there another cause, another reason why these young monks live the full and pure spiritual life for their entire life?” ^sn35-127-2-3

“Great king, this has been stated by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha: ^sn35-127-3-1

‘Please, monks, examine your own body up from the soles of the feet and down from the tips of the hairs, wrapped in skin and full of many kinds of filth. ^sn35-127-3-2

In this body there is head hair, body hair, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, undigested food, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, snot, synovial fluid, urine.’ ^sn35-127-3-3

This is also a cause, great king, this is a reason why these young monks live the full and pure spiritual life for their entire life, maintaining it for a long time.” ^sn35-127-3-4

“This is easy to do for those mendicants who have developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^sn35-127-3-5

But it’s hard to do for those mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^sn35-127-3-6

Sometimes I plan to focus on something as ugly, but only its beauty comes to mind. ^sn35-127-3-7

Is there another cause, another reason why these young monks live the full and pure spiritual life for their entire life?” ^sn35-127-3-8

“Great king, this has been stated by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha: ^sn35-127-4-1

‘Please, monks, live with sense doors guarded. ^sn35-127-4-2

When you see a sight with your eyes, don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-127-4-3

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protect the faculty of sight, and achieve its restraint. ^sn35-127-4-4

When you hear a sound with your ears … ^sn35-127-4-5

When you smell an odor with your nose … ^sn35-127-4-6

When you taste a flavor with your tongue … ^sn35-127-4-7

When you feel a touch with your body … ^sn35-127-4-8

When you know an idea with your mind, don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-127-4-9

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protect the faculty of mind, and achieve its restraint.’ ^sn35-127-4-10

This is also a cause, great king, this is a reason why these young monks practice the full and pure spiritual life as long as they live, maintaining it for a long time.” ^sn35-127-4-11

“It’s incredible, worthy Bhāradvāja, it’s amazing! ^sn35-127-5-1

How well this was said by the Buddha! ^sn35-127-5-2

This is the real cause, this is the reason why these young monks practice the full and pure spiritual life as long as they live, maintaining it for a long time. ^sn35-127-5-3

For sometimes I too enter the harem with unprotected body, speech, mind, mindfulness, and sense faculties. At those times powerful thoughts of desire get the better of me. ^sn35-127-5-4

But sometimes I enter the harem with protected body, speech, mind, mindfulness, and sense faculties. At those times such thoughts of desire don’t get the better of me. ^sn35-127-5-5

Excellent, worthy Bhāradvāja! Excellent! ^sn35-127-5-6

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the worthy Bhāradvāja has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn35-127-5-7

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn35-127-5-8

From this day forth, may the worthy Bhāradvāja remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn35-127-5-9

### With Soṇa (Linked Discourses 35.128) ^sn35-128-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn35-128-1-1

Then the householder Soṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn35-128-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-128-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” … ^sn35-128-1-4

(Tell in full as in SN 35.118.) ^sn35-128-1-5

### With Ghosita (Linked Discourses 35.129) ^sn35-129-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn35-129-1-1

Then the householder Ghosita went up to Venerable Ānanda, and said to him: ^sn35-129-1-2

“Honorable Ānanda, they speak of ‘the diversity of elements’. ^sn35-129-1-3

In what way did the Buddha speak of the diversity of elements?” ^sn35-129-1-4

“Householder, the eye element is found, as are agreeable sights, and eye consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-5

Pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn35-129-1-6

The eye element is found, as are disagreeable sights, and eye consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-7

Painful feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as painful. ^sn35-129-1-8

The eye element is found, as are sights that are a basis for equanimity, and eye consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-9

Neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn35-129-1-10

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-129-1-11

mind element is found, as are agreeable ideas, and mind consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-17

Pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn35-129-1-18

The mind element is found, as are disagreeable ideas, and mind consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-19

Painful feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as painful. ^sn35-129-1-20

The mind element is found, as are ideas that are a basis for equanimity, and mind consciousness. ^sn35-129-1-21

Neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn35-129-1-22

This is how the Buddha spoke of the diversity of elements.” ^sn35-129-1-23

### With Hāliddikāni (Linked Discourses 35.130) ^sn35-130-0

At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain. ^sn35-130-1-1

Then the householder Hāliddikāni went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna … and asked him, ^sn35-130-1-2

“Sir, this was said by the Buddha: ^sn35-130-1-3

‘Diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings.’ ^sn35-130-1-4

How does diversity of elements give rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings?” ^sn35-130-1-5

“Householder, it’s when a mendicant sees a sight with the eye and understands it to be agreeable. ^sn35-130-1-6

There is eye consciousness; and pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn35-130-1-7

Then they see a sight and understand it to be disagreeable. ^sn35-130-1-8

There is eye consciousness; and painful feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as painful. ^sn35-130-1-9

Then they see a sight and understand it to be a basis for equanimity. ^sn35-130-1-10

There is eye consciousness; and neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn35-130-1-11

Furthermore, a mendicant hears a sound with the ear … ^sn35-130-2-1

smells an odor with the nose … ^sn35-130-2-2

tastes a flavor with the tongue … ^sn35-130-2-3

encounters a touch with the body … ^sn35-130-2-4

knows an idea with the mind and understands it to be agreeable. ^sn35-130-2-5

There is mind consciousness; and pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant. ^sn35-130-2-6

Then they know an idea and understand it to be disagreeable. ^sn35-130-2-7

There is mind consciousness; and painful feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as painful. ^sn35-130-2-8

Then they know an idea and understand it to be a basis for equanimity. ^sn35-130-2-9

Neutral feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as neutral. ^sn35-130-2-10

That’s how diversity of elements gives rise to diversity of contacts, and diversity of contacts gives rise to diversity of feelings.” ^sn35-130-2-11

### Nakula’s Father (Linked Discourses 35.131) ^sn35-131-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^sn35-131-1-1

Then the householder Nakula’s father went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-131-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life? ^sn35-131-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” ^sn35-131-1-4

“Householder, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-131-1-5

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-131-1-6

their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-131-1-7

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-131-1-8

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-131-1-9

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-131-1-10

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-131-1-11

their consciousness has that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-131-1-12

A mendicant with fuel for grasping does not become extinguished. ^sn35-131-1-13

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life. ^sn35-131-1-14

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-131-2-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-131-2-2

their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-131-2-3

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-131-2-4

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-131-2-5

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-131-2-6

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, ^sn35-131-2-7

their consciousness doesn’t have that as support and fuel for grasping. ^sn35-131-2-8

A mendicant free of grasping becomes extinguished. ^sn35-131-2-9

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.” ^sn35-131-2-10

### With Lohicca (Linked Discourses 35.132) ^sn35-132-0

At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis in a wilderness hut near Makkarakaṭa. ^sn35-132-1-1

Then several students, pupils of the brahmin Lohicca, approached Mahākaccāna’s wilderness hut while collecting firewood. They walked and wandered all around the hut, making a colossal racket and all kinds of jeers: ^sn35-132-1-2

“These shavelings, fake ascetics, primitives, black spawn from the feet of our kinsman, the Lord! They’re honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed by so-called inheritors of Vedic culture.” ^sn35-132-1-3

Then Mahākaccāna left his dwelling and said to those young students, ^sn35-132-1-4

“Young students, stop being so noisy. ^sn35-132-1-5

I will speak to you on the teaching.” ^sn35-132-1-6

When this was said, the young students fell silent. ^sn35-132-1-7

Then Mahākaccāna recited these verses for them: ^sn35-132-1-8

“For the brahmins of old, ethics were the highest; ^sn35-132-2-1

they remembered the ancient legends. ^sn35-132-2-2

Their sense doors were guarded, well protected, ^sn35-132-2-3

and they had mastered anger. ^sn35-132-2-4

Those brahmins who remembered <j>the ancient legends ^sn35-132-3-1

enjoyed virtue and absorption. ^sn35-132-3-2

But these have lost their way. <j>Saying, ‘We must pray’, ^sn35-132-4-1

they live out of balance, judging by clan. ^sn35-132-4-2

Mastered by anger, they take up many arms, ^sn35-132-4-3

indifferent to those with craving and without. ^sn35-132-4-4

All is vain for someone <j>who doesn’t guard the sense doors, ^sn35-132-5-1

like the wealth a person finds in a dream. ^sn35-132-5-2

Fasting, sleeping on bare ground, ^sn35-132-5-3

bathing at daybreak, the three Vedas, ^sn35-132-5-4

rough hides, dreadlocks, and mud, ^sn35-132-6-1

hymns, precepts and observances, <j>and fervent austerities, ^sn35-132-6-2

fawning, bent staffs, ^sn35-132-6-3

and sipping water. ^sn35-132-6-4

These emblems of the brahmins ^sn35-132-6-5

are only used to generate profits. ^sn35-132-6-6

A mind that’s serene, ^sn35-132-7-1

clear and undisturbed, ^sn35-132-7-2

kind to all creatures: ^sn35-132-7-3

that’s the path to attainment of divinity!” ^sn35-132-7-4

Then those young students, offended and upset, went to the brahmin Lohicca and said to him, ^sn35-132-8-1

“Please, master, you should know this. The ascetic Mahākaccāna condemns and rejects outright the hymns of the brahmins!” ^sn35-132-8-2

When they said this, Lohicca was offended and upset. ^sn35-132-8-3

Then he thought, ^sn35-132-8-4

“But it wouldn’t be appropriate for me to abuse or insult the ascetic Mahākaccāna solely because of what I’ve heard from these young students. ^sn35-132-8-5

Why don’t I go and ask him about it?” ^sn35-132-8-6

Then the brahmin Lohicca together with those young students went to Venerable Mahākaccāna and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-132-9-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him, ^sn35-132-9-2

“Master Kaccāna, did several young pupils of mine come by here collecting firewood?” ^sn35-132-9-3

“They did, brahmin.” ^sn35-132-9-4

“But did you have some discussion with them?” ^sn35-132-9-5

“I did.” ^sn35-132-9-6

“But what kind of discussion did you have with them?” ^sn35-132-9-7

“This is the discussion I had with these young students.” And he repeated the verses in full. ^sn35-132-9-8

“Worthy Kaccāna spoke of someone who doesn’t guard the sense doors. ^sn35-132-12-1

How do you define someone who doesn’t guard the sense doors?” ^sn35-132-12-2

“Brahmin, take someone who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-132-12-3

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-132-12-4

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^sn35-132-12-5

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^sn35-132-12-6

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^sn35-132-12-7

When they feel a touch with their body … ^sn35-132-12-8

When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-132-12-9

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-132-12-10

That’s how someone doesn’t guard the sense doors.” ^sn35-132-12-11

“It’s incredible, worthy Kaccāna, it’s amazing! ^sn35-132-12-12

How accurately you’ve explained someone whose sense doors are unguarded! ^sn35-132-12-13

You also spoke of someone who does guard the sense doors. ^sn35-132-13-1

How do you define someone who does guard the sense doors?” ^sn35-132-13-2

“Brahmin, take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-132-13-3

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-132-13-4

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^sn35-132-13-5

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^sn35-132-13-6

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^sn35-132-13-7

When they feel a touch with their body … ^sn35-132-13-8

When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-132-13-9

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-132-13-10

That’s how someone guards the sense doors.” ^sn35-132-13-11

“It’s incredible, worthy Kaccāna, it’s amazing! ^sn35-132-14-1

How accurately you’ve explained someone whose sense doors are guarded! ^sn35-132-14-2

Excellent, worthy Kaccāna! Excellent! ^sn35-132-14-3

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the worthy Kaccāna has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn35-132-14-4

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn35-132-14-5

From this day forth, may the worthy Kaccāna remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life. ^sn35-132-14-6

Please come to my family just as you go to the families of the lay followers in Makkarakaṭa. ^sn35-132-14-7

The brahmin boys and girls there will bow to you, rise in your presence, and give you a seat and water. That will be for their lasting welfare and happiness.” ^sn35-132-14-8

### Verahaccāni (Linked Discourses 35.133) ^sn35-133-0

At one time Venerable Udāyī was staying near Kāmaṇḍā in the brahmin Todeyya’s mango grove. ^sn35-133-1-1

Then a boy who was a pupil of the brahmin lady of the Verahaccāni clan went up to Udāyī and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-133-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. ^sn35-133-1-3

Udāyī educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired that young student with a Dhamma talk. ^sn35-133-1-4

Then that young student went to the brahmin lady of the Verahaccāni clan and said to her, ^sn35-133-1-5

“Please, madam, you should know this. ^sn35-133-1-6

The ascetic Udāyī proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure.” ^sn35-133-1-7

“Then, young student, invite him in my name for tomorrow’s meal.” ^sn35-133-2-1

“Yes, madam,” he replied. He went to Udāyī and said, ^sn35-133-2-2

“Sir, would Mister Udāyī please accept an offering of tomorrow’s meal from my tutor’s wife, the brahmin lady of the Verahaccāni clan?” ^sn35-133-2-3

Udāyī consented with silence. ^sn35-133-2-4

Then when the night had passed, Udāyī robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the brahmin lady’s home, and sat down on the seat spread out. ^sn35-133-2-5

Then the brahmin lady served and satisfied Udāyī with her own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^sn35-133-2-6

When Udāyī had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, she put on a pair of slippers, sat on a high seat, covered her head, and said to him, ^sn35-133-2-7

“Ascetic, preach the Dhamma.” ^sn35-133-2-8

“There will be an occasion for that, sister,” he replied, then rose from his seat and left. ^sn35-133-2-9

For a second time that young student went to Venerable Udāyī … ^sn35-133-3-1

And for a second time that young student went to the brahmin lady of the Verahaccāni clan … ^sn35-133-3-4

She said to him, “You keep praising the ascetic Udāyī like this. ^sn35-133-4-1

But when I asked him to preach the Dhamma he just said that there would be an occasion for that, and then he got up and left.” ^sn35-133-4-2

“Madam, that’s because you put on a pair of slippers, sat on a high seat, and covered your head before inviting him to teach. ^sn35-133-4-3

For the good fellows respect the teaching.” ^sn35-133-4-5

“Then, young student, invite him in my name for tomorrow’s meal.” ^sn35-133-4-6

“Yes, madam,” he replied. … ^sn35-133-4-7

Then the brahmin lady served and satisfied Udāyī with her own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^sn35-133-5-2

When Udāyī had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, she took off her slippers, sat on a low seat, uncovered her head, and said to him, ^sn35-133-5-3

“Sir, when what exists do the perfected ones declare that there is pleasure and pain? When what doesn’t exist do the perfected ones not declare that there is pleasure and pain?” ^sn35-133-5-4

“Sister, when there’s an eye, the perfected ones declare that there is pleasure and pain. When there’s no eye, the perfected ones don’t declare that there is pleasure and pain. ^sn35-133-6-1

When there’s an ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-133-6-2

mind, the perfected ones declare that there is pleasure and pain. When there’s no mind, the perfected ones don’t declare that there is pleasure and pain.” ^sn35-133-6-3

When he said this, the brahmin lady said to Udāyī, ^sn35-133-7-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^sn35-133-7-2

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, Master Udāyī has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn35-133-7-3

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn35-133-7-4

From this day forth, may Master Udāyī remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn35-133-7-5

### At Devadaha (Linked Discourses 35.134) ^sn35-134-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near the Sakyan town named Devadaha. ^sn35-134-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-134-1-2

“When it comes to the six fields of contact, mendicants, I don’t say that all mendicants have work to do with diligence, nor do I say that none of them have work to do with diligence. ^sn35-134-1-3

I say that, when it comes to the six fields of contact, mendicants don’t have work to do with diligence if they are perfected, with defilements ended, having completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and become rightly freed through enlightenment. ^sn35-134-1-4

Why is that? ^sn35-134-1-5

They’ve done their work with diligence, and are incapable of negligence. ^sn35-134-1-6

I say that, when it comes to the six fields of contact, mendicants do have work to do with diligence if they are trainees, who haven’t achieved their heart’s desire, but live aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn35-134-1-7

Why is that? ^sn35-134-1-8

There are sights known by the eye that are pleasant and also those that are unpleasant. ^sn35-134-1-9

Though experiencing them again and again they don’t occupy the mind. ^sn35-134-1-10

Their energy is roused up and unflagging, their mindfulness is established and lucid, their body is tranquil and undisturbed, and their mind is immersed in samādhi and unified. ^sn35-134-1-11

Seeing this fruit of diligence, I say that those mendicants have work to do with diligence when it comes to the six fields of contact. … ^sn35-134-1-12

There are ideas known by the mind that are pleasant and also those that are unpleasant. ^sn35-134-1-13

Though experiencing them again and again they don’t occupy the mind. ^sn35-134-1-14

Their energy is roused up and unflagging, their mindfulness is established and lucid, their body is tranquil and undisturbed, and their mind is immersed in samādhi and unified. ^sn35-134-1-15

Seeing this fruit of diligence, I say that those mendicants have work to do with diligence when it comes to the six fields of contact.” ^sn35-134-1-16

### Opportunity (Linked Discourses 35.135) ^sn35-135-0

“You’re fortunate, mendicants, so very fortunate, ^sn35-135-1-1

to have the opportunity to lead the spiritual life. ^sn35-135-1-2

I’ve seen the hell named ‘Related to the Six Fields of Contact’. ^sn35-135-1-3

There, whatever sight you see with your eye is unlikable, not likable; undesirable, not desirable; unpleasant, not pleasant. ^sn35-135-1-4

Whatever sound you hear … ^sn35-135-1-5

Whatever odor you smell … ^sn35-135-1-6

Whatever flavor you taste … ^sn35-135-1-7

Whatever touch you feel … ^sn35-135-1-8

Whatever idea you know with your mind is unlikable, not likable; undesirable, not desirable; unpleasant, not pleasant. ^sn35-135-1-9

You’re fortunate, mendicants, so very fortunate, ^sn35-135-1-10

to have the opportunity to lead the spiritual life. ^sn35-135-1-11

I’ve seen the heaven called ‘Related to the Six Fields of Contact’. ^sn35-135-1-12

There, whatever sight you see with your eye is likable, not unlikable; desirable, not undesirable; pleasant, not unpleasant. ^sn35-135-1-13

Whatever sound … odor … flavor … touch … ^sn35-135-1-14

Whatever idea you know with your mind is likable, not unlikable; desirable, not undesirable; pleasant, not unpleasant. ^sn35-135-1-15

You’re fortunate, mendicants, so very fortunate, ^sn35-135-1-16

to have the opportunity to lead the spiritual life.” ^sn35-135-1-17

### Liking Sights (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.136) ^sn35-136-0

“Mendicants, gods and humans like sights, they love them and enjoy them. ^sn35-136-1-1

But when sights perish, fade away, and cease, gods and humans live in suffering. ^sn35-136-1-2

Gods and humans like sounds … ^sn35-136-1-3

smells … ^sn35-136-1-5

tastes … ^sn35-136-1-6

touches … ^sn35-136-1-7

ideas, they love them and enjoy them. ^sn35-136-1-8

But when ideas perish, fade away, and cease, gods and humans live in suffering. ^sn35-136-1-9

The Realized One has truly understood the origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape of sights, so he doesn’t like, love, or enjoy them. ^sn35-136-1-10

When sights perish, fade away, and cease, the Realized One lives happily. ^sn35-136-1-11

The Realized One has truly understood the origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape of sounds … ^sn35-136-1-12

smells … ^sn35-136-1-13

tastes … ^sn35-136-1-14

touches … ^sn35-136-1-15

ideas, so he doesn’t like, love, or enjoy them. ^sn35-136-1-16

When ideas perish, fade away, and cease, the Realized One lives happily.” ^sn35-136-1-17

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-136-1-18

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn35-136-1-19

“Sights, sounds, tastes, smells, ^sn35-136-2-1

touches and ideas, the lot of them— ^sn35-136-2-2

they’re likable, desirable, and pleasurable ^sn35-136-2-3

as long as you can say that they exist. ^sn35-136-2-4

For all the world with its gods, ^sn35-136-3-1

this is what they deem happiness. ^sn35-136-3-2

And where they cease ^sn35-136-3-3

is deemed as suffering for them. ^sn35-136-3-4

The noble ones have seen that happiness ^sn35-136-4-1

is the cessation of substantial reality. ^sn35-136-4-2

This insight by those who see ^sn35-136-4-3

contradicts the whole world. ^sn35-136-4-4

What others say is happiness ^sn35-136-5-1

the noble ones say is suffering. ^sn35-136-5-2

What others say is suffering ^sn35-136-5-3

the noble ones know as happiness. ^sn35-136-5-4

See, this teaching is hard to understand, ^sn35-136-6-1

it confuses the ignorant. ^sn35-136-6-2

There is darkness for the shrouded; ^sn35-136-6-3

blackness for those who don’t see. ^sn35-136-6-4

But the good are open; ^sn35-136-7-1

like light for those who see. ^sn35-136-7-2

Though close, they do not understand, ^sn35-136-7-3

the seekers inexpert in the teaching. ^sn35-136-7-4

They’re mired in desire for continued existence, ^sn35-136-8-1

flowing along the stream of lives, ^sn35-136-8-2

mired in Māra’s dominion: ^sn35-136-8-3

this teaching isn’t easy for them to understand. ^sn35-136-8-4

Who, apart from the noble ones, ^sn35-136-9-1

is qualified to understand this state? ^sn35-136-9-2

Having rightly understood this state, ^sn35-136-9-3

the undefiled are fully extinguished.” ^sn35-136-9-4

### Liking Sights (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.137) ^sn35-137-0

“Mendicants, gods and humans like sights, they love them and enjoy them. ^sn35-137-1-1

But when sights perish, fade away, and cease, gods and humans live in suffering. … ^sn35-137-1-2

The Realized One has truly understood the origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape of sights, so he doesn’t like, love, or enjoy them. ^sn35-137-1-9

When sights perish, fade away, and cease, the Realized One lives happily. …” ^sn35-137-1-10

### Not Yours (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.138) ^sn35-138-0

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn35-138-1-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-138-1-2

And what isn’t yours? ^sn35-138-1-3

The eye isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-138-1-4

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-138-1-5

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-138-1-6

mind isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-138-1-8

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-138-1-9

Suppose a person was to carry off the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove, or burn them, or do what they want with them. Would you think: ^sn35-138-1-10

‘This person is carrying us off, burning us, or doing what they want with us’?” ^sn35-138-1-11

“No, sir. ^sn35-138-1-12

Why is that? ^sn35-138-1-13

Because  to us that’s neither self nor belonging to self.” ^sn35-138-1-14

“In the same way, the eye isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-138-1-15

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-138-1-16

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-138-1-17

mind isn’t yours: give it up. ^sn35-138-1-19

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn35-138-1-20

### Not Yours (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.139) ^sn35-139-0

“Mendicants, give up what’s not yours. ^sn35-139-1-1

Giving it up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-139-1-2

And what isn’t yours? ^sn35-139-1-3

Sights aren’t yours: give them up. ^sn35-139-1-4

Giving them up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-139-1-5

Sounds, ^sn35-139-1-6

smells, ^sn35-139-1-7

tastes, ^sn35-139-1-8

touches, ^sn35-139-1-9

and ideas aren’t yours: give them up. ^sn35-139-1-10

Giving them up will be for your welfare and happiness. ^sn35-139-1-11

Suppose a person was to carry off the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove … ^sn35-139-1-12

In the same way, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas aren’t yours: give them up. ^sn35-139-1-13

Giving them up will be for your welfare and happiness.” ^sn35-139-1-14

### Interior and Cause Are Impermanent (Linked Discourses 35.140) ^sn35-140-0

“Mendicants, the eye is impermanent. ^sn35-140-1-1

The cause and reason that gives rise to the eye is also impermanent. ^sn35-140-1-2

Since the eye is produced by what is impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-140-1-3

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-140-1-4

mind is impermanent. ^sn35-140-1-7

The cause and reason that gives rise to the mind is also impermanent. ^sn35-140-1-8

Since the mind is produced by what is impermanent, how could it be permanent? ^sn35-140-1-9

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-140-1-10

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-140-1-11

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-140-1-12

### Interior and Cause Are Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.141) ^sn35-141-0

“Mendicants, the eye is suffering. ^sn35-141-1-1

The cause and reason that gives rise to the eye is also suffering. ^sn35-141-1-2

Since the eye is produced by what is suffering, how could it be happiness? ^sn35-141-1-3

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-141-1-4

mind is suffering. ^sn35-141-1-7

The cause and reason that gives rise to the mind is also suffering. ^sn35-141-1-8

Since the mind is produced by what is suffering, how could it be happiness? ^sn35-141-1-9

Seeing this … ^sn35-141-1-10

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-141-1-11

mind is not-self. ^sn35-142-1-7

Seeing this … ^sn35-142-1-10

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-142-1-11

Smells … ^sn35-143-1-5

Tastes … ^sn35-143-1-6

Touches … ^sn35-143-1-7

Seeing this … ^sn35-143-1-11

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-143-1-12

### The Cessation of Deeds (Linked Discourses 35.146) ^sn35-146-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you old deeds, new deeds, the cessation of deeds, and the practice that leads to the cessation of deeds. ^sn35-146-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. … ^sn35-146-1-2

And what is old deeds? ^sn35-146-1-3

The eye is old deeds. It should be seen as produced by choices and intentions, as something to be felt. ^sn35-146-1-4

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-146-1-5

mind is old deeds. It should be seen as produced by choices and intentions, as something to be felt. ^sn35-146-1-6

This is called old deeds. ^sn35-146-1-7

And what is new deeds? ^sn35-146-1-8

The deeds you currently perform by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn35-146-1-9

These are called new deeds. ^sn35-146-1-10

And what is the cessation of deeds? ^sn35-146-1-11

When you experience freedom due to the cessation of deeds by body, speech, and mind. ^sn35-146-1-12

This is called the cessation of deeds. ^sn35-146-1-13

And what’s the practice that leads to the cessation of deeds? ^sn35-146-1-14

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn35-146-1-15

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn35-146-1-16

This is called the practice that leads to the cessation of deeds. ^sn35-146-1-17

So, mendicants, I’ve taught you old deeds, new deeds, the cessation of deeds, and the practice that leads to the cessation of deeds. ^sn35-146-1-18

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^sn35-146-1-19

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^sn35-146-1-20

### The Impermanent as Conducive to Extinguishment (Linked Discourses 35.147) ^sn35-147-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a practice that’s conducive to extinguishment. ^sn35-147-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-147-1-2

And what is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment? ^sn35-147-1-3

It’s when a mendicant sees that the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are impermanent. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-147-1-4

They see that the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-147-1-5

mind, ideas, mind-consciousness, and mind contact are impermanent. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-147-1-6

This is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment.” ^sn35-147-1-7

### The Suffering as Conducive to Extinguishment (Linked Discourses 35.148) ^sn35-148-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a practice that’s conducive to extinguishment. ^sn35-148-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-148-1-2

And what is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment? ^sn35-148-1-3

It’s when a mendicant sees that the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are suffering. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also suffering. ^sn35-148-1-4

They see that the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-148-1-5

mind, ideas, mind-consciousness, and mind contact are suffering. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also suffering. ^sn35-148-1-6

This is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment.” ^sn35-148-1-7

### Not-Self as Conducive to Extinguishment (Linked Discourses 35.149) ^sn35-149-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a practice that’s conducive to extinguishment. ^sn35-149-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-149-1-2

And what is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment? ^sn35-149-1-3

It’s when a mendicant sees that the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are not-self. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also not-self. ^sn35-149-1-4

They see that the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind, ideas, mind-consciousness, and mind contact are not-self. And they see that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also not-self. ^sn35-149-1-5

This is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment.” ^sn35-149-1-6

### A Practice Conducive to Extinguishment (Linked Discourses 35.150) ^sn35-150-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a practice that’s conducive to extinguishment. ^sn35-150-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-150-1-2

And what is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment? ^sn35-150-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-150-1-4

Is the eye permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-150-1-5

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-150-2-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-150-3-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-150-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-150-5-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-150-5-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-150-6-1

“Are sights … ^sn35-150-7-1

eye consciousness … ^sn35-150-9-1

eye contact … ^sn35-150-9-2

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact: is that permanent or impermanent?” ^sn35-150-9-3

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn35-150-10-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn35-150-11-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn35-150-12-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn35-150-13-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn35-150-13-2

“No, sir.” ^sn35-150-14-1

“Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they grow disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-150-15-1

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-150-15-2

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. … ^sn35-150-15-3

They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-150-15-4

This is that practice that’s conducive to extinguishment.” ^sn35-150-15-5

### A Resident Pupil (Linked Discourses 35.151) ^sn35-151-0

“Mendicants, this spiritual life is lived without a resident pupil and without a teaching master. ^sn35-151-1-1

A mendicant who lives with a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in suffering and discomfort. ^sn35-151-1-2

A mendicant who lives without a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in happiness and comfort. ^sn35-151-1-3

And how does a mendicant who lives with a resident pupil and a teaching master live in suffering and discomfort? ^sn35-151-1-4

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eye, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-151-1-5

Those qualities reside within. Since they have bad unskillful qualities residing within, ^sn35-151-1-6

they’re said to have a resident pupil. ^sn35-151-1-7

Those qualities master them. Since they’re mastered by bad unskillful qualities, ^sn35-151-1-8

they’re said to have a teaching master. ^sn35-151-1-9

Furthermore, when a mendicant hears … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-151-2-1

knows an idea with the mind, bad, unskillful phenomena arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-151-3-1

Those qualities reside within. Since they have bad unskillful qualities residing within, ^sn35-151-3-2

they’re said to have a resident pupil. ^sn35-151-3-3

Those qualities master them. Since they’re mastered by bad unskillful qualities, ^sn35-151-3-4

they’re said to have a teaching master. ^sn35-151-3-5

That’s how a mendicant who lives with a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in suffering and discomfort. ^sn35-151-3-6

And how does a mendicant who lives without a resident pupil and a teaching master live in happiness and comfort? ^sn35-151-4-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eye, bad, unskillful phenomena don’t arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-151-4-2

Those qualities don’t reside within. Since they don’t have bad unskillful qualities residing within, ^sn35-151-4-3

they’re said to not have a resident pupil. ^sn35-151-4-4

Those qualities don’t master them. Since they’re not mastered by bad unskillful qualities, ^sn35-151-4-5

they’re said to not have a teaching master. ^sn35-151-4-6

Furthermore, when a mendicant hears … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-151-5-1

knows an idea with the mind, bad, unskillful phenomena don’t arise: memories and thoughts that tighten fetters. ^sn35-151-6-1

Those qualities don’t reside within. Since they don’t have bad unskillful qualities residing within, ^sn35-151-6-2

they’re said to not have a resident pupil. ^sn35-151-6-3

Those qualities don’t master them. Since they’re not mastered by bad unskillful qualities, ^sn35-151-6-4

they’re said to not have a teaching master. ^sn35-151-6-5

That’s how a mendicant who lives without a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in happiness and comfort. ^sn35-151-6-6

This spiritual life is lived without a resident pupil and without a teaching master. ^sn35-151-6-7

A mendicant who lives with a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in suffering and discomfort. ^sn35-151-6-8

A mendicant who lives without a resident pupil and a teaching master lives in happiness and comfort.” ^sn35-151-6-9

### What’s the Goal of the Spiritual Life? (Linked Discourses 35.152) ^sn35-152-0

“Mendicants, if wanderers who follow another religion were to ask you: ^sn35-152-1-1

‘Reverends, what’s the goal of leading the spiritual life under the ascetic Gotama?’ ^sn35-152-1-2

You should answer them: ^sn35-152-1-3

‘The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand suffering.’ ^sn35-152-1-4

If wanderers of other religions were to ask you: ^sn35-152-1-5

‘Reverends, what is that suffering?’ ^sn35-152-1-6

You should answer them: ^sn35-152-1-7

‘The eye is suffering. ^sn35-152-2-1

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-152-2-2

Sights … ^sn35-152-2-3

Eye consciousness … ^sn35-152-2-5

Eye contact … ^sn35-152-2-7

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also suffering. ^sn35-152-2-9

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-152-2-10

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-152-2-11

mind … ^sn35-152-2-12

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also suffering. ^sn35-152-2-14

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this. ^sn35-152-2-15

This is that suffering. ^sn35-152-2-16

The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is to completely understand this.’ ^sn35-152-2-17

When questioned by wanderers of other religions, that’s how you should answer them.” ^sn35-152-2-18

### Is There a Method? (Linked Discourses 35.153) ^sn35-153-0

“Mendicants, is there a method—apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation—that a mendicant can rely on to declare their enlightenment? That is: ^sn35-153-1-1

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’” ^sn35-153-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn35-153-1-3

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn35-153-1-4

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^sn35-153-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^sn35-153-1-6

“There is a method—apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation—that a mendicant can rely on to declare their enlightenment. That is: ^sn35-153-1-7

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^sn35-153-1-8

And what is that method? ^sn35-153-2-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with the eye. When they have greed, hate, and delusion in them, they understand ‘I have greed, hate, and delusion in me.’ ^sn35-153-2-2

When they don’t have greed, hate, and delusion in them, they understand ‘I don’t have greed, hate, and delusion in me.’ ^sn35-153-2-3

Since this is so, are these things understood by faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation?” ^sn35-153-2-4

“No, sir.” ^sn35-153-2-5

“Aren’t they understood by seeing them with wisdom?” ^sn35-153-2-6

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-153-2-7

“This is a method—apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation—that a mendicant can rely on to declare their enlightenment. That is: ^sn35-153-2-8

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^sn35-153-2-9

Furthermore, a mendicant hears a sound … smells an odor … tastes a flavor … encounters a touch … ^sn35-153-3-1

knows an idea with the mind. When they have greed, hate, and delusion in them, they understand ‘I have greed, hate, and delusion in me.’ ^sn35-153-4-1

When they don’t have greed, hate, and delusion in them, they understand ‘I don’t have greed, hate, and delusion in me.’ ^sn35-153-4-2

Since this is so, are these things understood by faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation?” ^sn35-153-4-3

“No, sir.” ^sn35-153-4-4

“Aren’t they understood by seeing them with wisdom?” ^sn35-153-4-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-153-4-6

“This too is a method—apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation—that a mendicant can rely on to declare their enlightenment. That is: ^sn35-153-4-7

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’” ^sn35-153-4-8

### Endowed With Faculties (Linked Discourses 35.154) ^sn35-154-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-154-1-1

“Sir, they speak of someone who is ‘accomplished in the faculties’. ^sn35-154-1-2

How is someone accomplished in the faculties defined?” ^sn35-154-1-3

“Mendicant, if someone meditates observing rise and fall in the eye faculty, they grow disillusioned with the eye faculty. ^sn35-154-2-1

If they meditate observing rise and fall in the ear faculty … nose faculty … tongue faculty … body faculty … ^sn35-154-2-2

mind faculty, they grow disillusioned with the mind faculty. ^sn35-154-2-3

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. … When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-154-2-4

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-154-2-5

This is how someone who is accomplished in the faculties is defined.” ^sn35-154-2-6

### A Dhamma Speaker (Linked Discourses 35.155) ^sn35-155-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-155-1-1

“Sir, they speak of a ‘Dhamma speaker’. ^sn35-155-1-2

How is a Dhamma speaker defined?” ^sn35-155-1-3

“If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn35-155-2-1

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn35-155-2-2

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the eye, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’. ^sn35-155-2-3

If a mendicant teaches Dhamma for disillusionment with the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-155-2-4

mind, for its fading away and cessation, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who speaks on Dhamma’. ^sn35-155-2-5

If they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the mind, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who practices in line with the teaching’. ^sn35-155-2-6

If they’re freed by not grasping by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the mind, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.” ^sn35-155-2-7

### The Interior and the End of Relishing (Linked Discourses 35.156) ^sn35-156-0

“Mendicants, the eye really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn35-156-1-1

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn35-156-1-2

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-156-1-3

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed. ^sn35-156-1-5

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-156-1-6

mind really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn35-156-1-11

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn35-156-1-12

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-156-1-13

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.” ^sn35-156-1-15

### The Exterior and the End of Relishing (Linked Discourses 35.157) ^sn35-157-0

“Mendicants, sights really are impermanent. A mendicant sees that they are impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn35-157-1-1

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn35-157-1-2

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-157-1-3

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed. ^sn35-157-1-5

Sounds … ^sn35-157-1-6

Smells … ^sn35-157-1-7

Tastes … ^sn35-157-1-8

Touches … ^sn35-157-1-9

Ideas really are impermanent. A mendicant sees that they are impermanent: that’s their right view. ^sn35-157-1-10

Seeing rightly, they grow disillusioned. ^sn35-157-1-11

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-157-1-12

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.” ^sn35-157-1-14

### Focus, the Interior, and the End of Relishing (Linked Discourses 35.158) ^sn35-158-0

“Mendicants, rationally apply the mind to the eye. Truly see the impermanence of the eye. ^sn35-158-1-1

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with the eye. ^sn35-158-1-2

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-158-1-3

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed. ^sn35-158-1-5

Rationally apply the mind to the ear … ^sn35-158-1-6

nose … ^sn35-158-1-7

tongue … ^sn35-158-1-8

body … ^sn35-158-1-13

mind. Truly see the impermanence of the mind. ^sn35-158-1-14

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with the mind. ^sn35-158-1-15

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-158-1-16

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.” ^sn35-158-1-18

### Focus, the Exterior, and the End of Relishing (Linked Discourses 35.159) ^sn35-159-0

“Mendicants, rationally apply the mind to sights. Truly see the impermanence of sights. ^sn35-159-1-1

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with sights. ^sn35-159-1-2

When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-159-1-3

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed. ^sn35-159-1-5

Rationally apply the mind to sounds … ^sn35-159-1-6

smells … ^sn35-159-1-7

tastes … ^sn35-159-1-8

touches … ^sn35-159-1-9

ideas. Truly see the impermanence of ideas. ^sn35-159-1-10

When a mendicant does this, they grow disillusioned with ideas. ^sn35-159-1-11

When relishing ends, greed ends. ^sn35-159-1-12

When greed ends, relishing ends. ^sn35-159-1-13

When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.” ^sn35-159-1-14

### On Immersion at Jīvaka’s Mango Grove (Linked Discourses 35.160) ^sn35-160-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha in Jīvaka’s Mango Grove. ^sn35-160-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-160-1-2

“Mendicants, develop immersion. ^sn35-160-1-4

For a mendicant with immersion, things become truly clear. ^sn35-160-1-5

And what becomes truly clear? ^sn35-160-1-6

It becomes truly clear that the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are impermanent. And it also becomes truly clear that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is impermanent. ^sn35-160-1-7

It becomes truly clear that the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-160-1-8

mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are impermanent. ^sn35-160-1-9

And it also becomes truly clear that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is impermanent. ^sn35-160-1-10

Mendicants, develop immersion. ^sn35-160-1-11

For a mendicant with immersion, things become truly clear.” ^sn35-160-1-12

### On Retreat at Jīvaka’s Mango Grove (Linked Discourses 35.161) ^sn35-161-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha in Jīvaka’s Mango Grove. ^sn35-161-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-161-1-2

“Mendicants, meditate in retreat. ^sn35-161-1-3

For a mendicant who meditates in retreat, things become truly clear. ^sn35-161-1-4

And what becomes truly clear? ^sn35-161-1-5

It becomes truly clear that the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are impermanent. And it also becomes truly clear that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is impermanent. … ^sn35-161-1-6

It becomes truly clear that the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are impermanent. ^sn35-161-1-7

And it also becomes truly clear that the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is impermanent. ^sn35-161-1-10

Mendicants, meditate in retreat. ^sn35-161-1-11

For a mendicant who meditates in retreat, things become truly clear.” ^sn35-161-1-12

### With Koṭṭhita on Impermanence (Linked Discourses 35.162) ^sn35-162-0

Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn35-162-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-162-1-2

“Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is impermanent. ^sn35-162-2-1

And what is impermanent? ^sn35-162-2-2

The eye, ^sn35-162-2-3

sights, ^sn35-162-2-4

eye consciousness, ^sn35-162-2-5

and eye contact are impermanent: you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-162-2-6

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also impermanent: you should give up desire for it. ^sn35-162-2-7

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-162-2-8

The mind, ^sn35-162-2-13

ideas, ^sn35-162-2-14

mind consciousness, ^sn35-162-2-15

and mind contact are impermanent: you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-162-2-16

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also impermanent: you should give up desire for it. ^sn35-162-2-17

Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is impermanent.” ^sn35-162-2-18

### With Koṭṭhita on Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.163) ^sn35-163-0

Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita … said to the Buddha: ^sn35-163-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^sn35-163-1-2

“Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is suffering. ^sn35-163-1-3

And what is suffering? ^sn35-163-1-4

The eye, ^sn35-163-1-5

sights, ^sn35-163-1-6

eye consciousness, ^sn35-163-1-7

and eye contact are suffering: you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-163-1-8

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also suffering; you should give up desire for it. ^sn35-163-1-9

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-163-1-10

The mind, ^sn35-163-1-11

ideas, ^sn35-163-1-12

mind consciousness, ^sn35-163-1-13

and mind contact are suffering: you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-163-1-14

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also suffering: you should give up desire for it. ^sn35-163-1-15

Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is suffering.” ^sn35-163-1-16

### With Koṭṭhita on Not-Self (Linked Discourses 35.164) ^sn35-164-0

“Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is not-self. ^sn35-164-1-3

And what is not-self? ^sn35-164-1-4

The eye, ^sn35-164-1-5

sights, ^sn35-164-1-6

eye consciousness, ^sn35-164-1-7

and eye contact are not-self: you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-164-1-8

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also not-self: You should give up desire for it. ^sn35-164-1-9

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-164-1-10

The mind, ^sn35-164-1-11

ideas, ^sn35-164-1-12

mind consciousness, ^sn35-164-1-13

and mind contact … ^sn35-164-1-14

The pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also not-self: you should give up desire for it. ^sn35-164-1-15

Koṭṭhita, you should give up desire for what is not-self.” ^sn35-164-1-16

### Giving Up Wrong View (Linked Discourses 35.165) ^sn35-165-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-165-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-165-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that wrong view is given up?” ^sn35-165-1-3

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact as impermanent, wrong view is given up. … ^sn35-165-2-1

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as impermanent, wrong view is given up. ^sn35-165-2-5

This is how to know and see so that wrong view is given up.” ^sn35-165-2-6

### Giving Up Substantialist View (Linked Discourses 35.166) ^sn35-166-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-166-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-166-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that substantialist view is given up?” ^sn35-166-1-3

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact as suffering, substantialist view is given up. … ^sn35-166-1-4

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as suffering, substantialist view is given up. ^sn35-166-1-8

This is how to know and see so that substantialist view is given up.” ^sn35-166-1-9

### Giving Up Theory of Self (Linked Discourses 35.167) ^sn35-167-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … ^sn35-167-1-1

and said to him: ^sn35-167-1-2

“Sir, how does one know and see so that theory of self is given up?” ^sn35-167-1-3

“Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact as not-self, theory of self is given up. … ^sn35-167-1-4

And also knowing and seeing the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises conditioned by mind contact as not-self, theory of self is given up.” ^sn35-167-1-14

The eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are impermanent: ^sn35-168-1-3

you should give up desire for them. ^sn35-168-1-5

You should give up desire for what is impermanent.” ^sn35-168-1-6

### The Interior Was Impermanent in the Past (Linked Discourses 35.186) ^sn35-186-0

“Mendicants, in the past the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind were impermanent. ^sn35-186-1-1

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. ^sn35-186-1-2

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-186-1-3

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-186-1-4

### The Interior Will Be Impermanent in the Future (Linked Discourses 35.187) ^sn35-187-0

### The Interior and What’s Impermanent in the Past (Linked Discourses 35.204) ^sn35-204-0

“Mendicants, in the past the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind were impermanent. ^sn35-204-1-1

What’s impermanent is suffering. ^sn35-204-1-2

What’s suffering is not-self. ^sn35-204-1-3

And what’s not-self should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ …” ^sn35-204-1-4

### The Ocean (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.228) ^sn35-228-0

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person speaks of the ocean. ^sn35-228-1-1

But that’s not the ocean in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-228-1-2

That’s just a large body of water, a large sea of water. ^sn35-228-1-3

For a person, the eye is an ocean, ^sn35-228-1-4

and its currents are made of sights. ^sn35-228-1-5

Someone who can prevail over those currents is said to have crossed over the ocean of the eye, with its waves and whirlpools, its saltwater crocodiles and monsters. ^sn35-228-2-1

Crossed over, gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground. ^sn35-228-2-2

For a person, the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-228-2-3

mind is an ocean, ^sn35-228-2-7

and its currents are made of ideas. ^sn35-228-2-8

Someone who can prevail over those currents is said to have crossed over the ocean of the mind, with its waves and whirlpools, its saltwater crocodiles and monsters. ^sn35-228-2-9

Crossed over, gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground.” ^sn35-228-2-10

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn35-228-2-11

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn35-228-2-12

“A knowledge master who’s crossed <j>the ocean so hard to cross, ^sn35-228-3-1

with its saltwater crocodiles and monsters, <j>its waves, whirlpools, and dangers; ^sn35-228-3-2

they’ve completed the spiritual journey, <j>and gone to the end of the world, ^sn35-228-3-3

they’re called ‘one who has gone beyond’.” ^sn35-228-3-4

### The Ocean (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.229) ^sn35-229-0

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person speaks of the ocean. ^sn35-229-1-1

But that’s not the ocean in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-229-1-2

That’s just a large body of water, a large sea of water. ^sn35-229-1-3

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-229-1-4

This is called the ocean in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-229-1-5

And it’s here that this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—is for the most part sunk. It’s become tangled like string, knotted like a ball of thread, and matted like rushes and reeds, and it doesn’t escape the places of loss, the bad places, the underworld, transmigration. ^sn35-229-1-6

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-229-2-1

This is called the ocean in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-229-2-2

And it’s here that this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—is for the most part sunk. It’s become tangled like string, knotted like a ball of thread, and matted like rushes and reeds, and it doesn’t escape the places of loss, the bad places, the underworld, transmigration. ^sn35-229-2-3

Those in whom greed, hate, and ignorance ^sn35-229-3-1

have faded away; ^sn35-229-3-2

have crossed the ocean so hard to cross, ^sn35-229-3-3

with its saltwater crocodiles and monsters, <j>its waves and dangers. ^sn35-229-3-4

They’ve slipped their chains, given up death, and have no attachments. ^sn35-229-4-1

They’ve given up suffering, <j>so there are no more future lives. ^sn35-229-4-2

They’ve disappeared, and cannot be measured; ^sn35-229-4-3

and they’ve confounded the King of Death, I say.” ^sn35-229-4-4

### The Simile of the Angler (Linked Discourses 35.230) ^sn35-230-0
