“Mendicants, suppose an angler was to cast a baited hook into a deep lake. ^sn35-230-1-1

Seeing the bait, a fish would swallow it. ^sn35-230-1-2

And so the fish that swallowed the hook would meet with tragedy and disaster, and the angler can do what he wants with it. ^sn35-230-1-3

In the same way, there are these six hooks in the world that mean tragedy and slaughter for living creatures. ^sn35-230-2-1

What six? ^sn35-230-2-2

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-230-2-3

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-230-2-4

they’re called a mendicant who has swallowed Māra’s hook. They’ve met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-230-2-5

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-230-2-6

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-230-3-1

If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them, ^sn35-230-3-2

they’re called a mendicant who has swallowed Māra’s hook. They’ve met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants. ^sn35-230-3-3

There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-230-4-1

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, they’re called a mendicant who hasn’t swallowed Māra’s hook. They’ve broken the hook, destroyed it. They haven’t met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants. ^sn35-230-4-2

There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-230-5-1

ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn35-230-5-2

If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them, they’re called a mendicant who hasn’t swallowed Māra’s hook. They’ve broken the hook, destroyed it. They haven’t met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One cannot do with them what he wants.” ^sn35-230-5-3

### The Simile of the Latex-Producing Tree (Linked Discourses 35.231) ^sn35-231-0

“Mendicants, take any monk or nun who, when it comes to sights known by the eye, still has greed, hate, and delusion, and has not given them up. If even trivial sights come into their range of vision they overcome their mind, let alone those that are compelling. ^sn35-231-1-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-1-2

Because they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. ^sn35-231-1-3

When it comes to sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-231-2-1

ideas known by the mind, they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. If even trivial thoughts come into the range of the mind they overcome their mind, let alone those that are compelling. ^sn35-231-3-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-3-2

Because they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. ^sn35-231-3-3

Suppose there was a latex-producing tree—such as a peepal, a banyan, a wavy leaf fig, or a cluster fig—that’s a tender young sapling. ^sn35-231-4-1

If a man were to chop it here and there with a sharp axe, would latex come out?” ^sn35-231-4-2

“Yes, sir. ^sn35-231-4-3

Why is that? ^sn35-231-4-4

Because it still has latex.” ^sn35-231-4-5

“In the same way, take any monk or nun who, when it comes to sights known by the eye, still has greed, hate, and delusion, and has not given them up. If even trivial sights come into their range of vision they overcome their mind, let alone those that are compelling. ^sn35-231-5-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-5-2

Because they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. ^sn35-231-5-3

When it comes to sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-231-6-1

ideas known by the mind, they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. If even trivial thoughts come into the range of the mind they overcome their mind, let alone those that are compelling. ^sn35-231-7-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-7-2

Because they still have greed, hate, and delusion, and have not given them up. ^sn35-231-7-3

Take any monk or nun who, when it comes to sights known by the eye, has no greed, hate, and delusion left, and has given them up. If even compelling sights come into their range of vision they don’t overcome their mind, let alone those that are trivial. ^sn35-231-8-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-8-2

Because they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up. ^sn35-231-8-3

When it comes to sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ideas known by the mind, they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up. If even compelling ideas come into the range of the mind they don’t overcome their mind, let alone those that are trivial. ^sn35-231-9-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-9-2

Because they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up. ^sn35-231-9-3

Suppose there was a latex-producing tree—such as a peepal, a banyan, a wavy leaf fig, or a cluster fig—that’s dried up, withered, and decrepit. ^sn35-231-9-4

If a man were to chop it here and there with a sharp axe, would latex come out?” ^sn35-231-9-5

“No, sir. ^sn35-231-9-6

Why is that? ^sn35-231-9-7

Because it has no latex left.” ^sn35-231-9-8

“In the same way, take any monk or nun who, when it comes to sights known by the eye, has no greed, hate, and delusion left, and has given them up. If even compelling sights come into their range of vision they don’t overcome their mind, let alone those that are trivial. ^sn35-231-10-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-10-2

Because they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up. ^sn35-231-10-3

When it comes to sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-231-11-1

ideas known by the mind, they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up. If even compelling thoughts come into the range of the mind they don’t overcome their mind, let alone those that are trivial. ^sn35-231-12-1

Why is that? ^sn35-231-12-2

Because they have no greed, hate, and delusion left, and have given them up.” ^sn35-231-12-3

### With Koṭṭhita (Linked Discourses 35.232) ^sn35-232-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn35-232-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-232-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^sn35-232-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, which is it? Is the eye the fetter of sights, or are sights the fetter of the eye? ^sn35-232-2-1

Is the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-232-2-2

mind the fetter of ideas, or are ideas the fetter of the mind?” ^sn35-232-2-3

“Reverend Koṭṭhita, the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn35-232-3-1

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-232-3-2

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-232-3-3

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn35-232-3-5

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-232-3-6

Suppose there was a black ox and a white ox yoked by a single harness or yoke. ^sn35-232-4-1

Would it be right to say that ^sn35-232-4-2

the black ox is the yoke of the white ox, or the white ox is the yoke of the black ox?” ^sn35-232-4-3

“No, reverend. ^sn35-232-4-4

The black ox is not the yoke of the white ox, nor is the white ox the yoke of the black ox. ^sn35-232-4-5

The yoke there is the single harness or yoke that they’re yoked by.” ^sn35-232-4-6

“In the same way, the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn35-232-5-1

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-232-5-2

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-232-5-3

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn35-232-5-4

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-232-5-5

If the eye were the fetter of sights, or if sights were the fetter of the eye, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering would not be found. ^sn35-232-6-1

However, since this is not the case, but ^sn35-232-6-2

the fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering is found. ^sn35-232-6-3

If the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-232-7-1

mind were the fetter of ideas, or if ideas were the fetter of the mind, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering would not be found. ^sn35-232-8-1

However, since this is not the case, but ^sn35-232-8-2

the fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them, this living of the spiritual life for the complete ending of suffering is found. ^sn35-232-8-3

This too is a way to understand how this is so. ^sn35-232-9-1

The Buddha has an eye ^sn35-232-10-1

with which he sees a sight. ^sn35-232-10-2

But he has no desire and greed, ^sn35-232-10-3

for his mind is well freed. ^sn35-232-10-4

The Buddha has an ear … ^sn35-232-10-5

nose … ^sn35-232-10-9

tongue … ^sn35-232-10-13

The Buddha has a body ^sn35-232-10-17

with which he senses touch. ^sn35-232-10-18

But he has no desire and greed, ^sn35-232-10-19

for his mind is well freed. ^sn35-232-10-20

The Buddha has a mind ^sn35-232-10-21

with which he knows ideas. ^sn35-232-10-22

But he has no desire and greed, ^sn35-232-10-23

for his mind is well freed. ^sn35-232-10-24

This too is a way to understand how the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn35-232-11-1

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-232-11-2

The ear … ^sn35-232-11-3

nose … ^sn35-232-11-4

tongue … ^sn35-232-11-5

body … ^sn35-232-11-7

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn35-232-11-8

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them.” ^sn35-232-11-9

### With Kāmabhū (Linked Discourses 35.233) ^sn35-233-0

At one time the venerables Ānanda and Kāmabhū were staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn35-233-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Kāmabhū came out of retreat, went to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-233-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda: ^sn35-233-1-3

“Reverend Ānanda, which is it? Is the eye the fetter of sights, or are sights the fetter of the eye? ^sn35-233-2-1

Is the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-233-2-2

mind the fetter of ideas, or are ideas the fetter of the mind?” ^sn35-233-2-3

“Reverend Kāmabhū, the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn35-233-3-1

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-233-3-2

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-233-3-3

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn35-233-3-4

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn35-233-3-5

Suppose there was a black ox and a white ox yoked by a single harness or yoke. ^sn35-233-4-1

Would it be right to say that ^sn35-233-4-2

the black ox is the yoke of the white ox, or the white ox is the yoke of the black ox?” ^sn35-233-4-3

“No, reverend. ^sn35-233-4-4

The black ox is not the yoke of the white ox, nor is the white ox the yoke of the black ox. ^sn35-233-4-5

The yoke there is the single harness or yoke that they’re yoked by.” ^sn35-233-4-6

“In the same way, the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn35-233-4-7

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-233-4-8

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn35-233-4-9

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them.” ^sn35-233-4-10

### With Udāyī (Linked Discourses 35.234) ^sn35-234-0

At one time the venerables Ānanda and Udāyī were staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn35-234-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Udāyī came out of retreat, went to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn35-234-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda: ^sn35-234-1-3

“Reverend Ānanda, the Buddha has explained, opened, and illuminated in many ways how this body is not-self. ^sn35-234-2-1

Is it possible to explain consciousness in the same way? To teach, assert, establish, clarify, analyze, and reveal how consciousness is not-self?” ^sn35-234-2-2

“It is possible, Reverend Udāyī. ^sn35-234-3-2

Does eye consciousness arise dependent on the eye and sights?” ^sn35-234-4-1

“Yes, reverend.” ^sn35-234-4-2

“If the cause and reason that gives rise to eye consciousness were to totally and utterly cease without remainder, would eye consciousness still be found?” ^sn35-234-4-3

“No, reverend.” ^sn35-234-4-4

“In this way, too, it can be understood how consciousness is not-self. ^sn35-234-4-5

Does ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-234-5-1

mind consciousness arise dependent on the mind and ideas?” ^sn35-234-6-1

“Yes, reverend.” ^sn35-234-6-2

“If the cause and reason that gives rise to mind consciousness were to totally and utterly cease without remainder, would mind consciousness still be found?” ^sn35-234-6-3

“No, reverend.” ^sn35-234-6-4

“In this way, too, it can be understood how consciousness is not-self. ^sn35-234-6-5

Suppose there was a person in need of heartwood. Wandering in search of heartwood, they’d take a sharp axe and enter a forest. There they’d see a big banana trunk, straight and young and grown unskewed. They’d cut it down at the base, cut off the root, cut off the top, and unroll the coiled sheaths. But they wouldn’t even find sapwood, much less heartwood. ^sn35-234-7-1

In the same way, a mendicant sees these six fields of contact as neither self nor belonging to self. ^sn35-234-7-2

So seeing, they don’t grasp anything in the world. ^sn35-234-7-3

Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished. ^sn35-234-7-4

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn35-234-7-5

### The Exposition on Burning (Linked Discourses 35.235) ^sn35-235-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an exposition of the teaching on burning. ^sn35-235-1-1

Listen … ^sn35-235-1-2

And what is the exposition of the teaching on burning? ^sn35-235-1-3

You’d be better off mutilating your eye faculty with a red-hot iron nail, burning, blazing and glowing, than getting caught up in the features by way of the details in sights known by the eye. ^sn35-235-1-4

For if you die at a time when your consciousness is still tied to gratification in the features or details, it’s possible you’ll go to one of two destinations: ^sn35-235-1-5

hell or the animal realm. ^sn35-235-1-6

I speak having seen this drawback. ^sn35-235-1-7

You’d be better off mutilating your ear faculty with a sharp iron spike … ^sn35-235-2-1

You’d be better off mutilating your nose faculty with a sharp nail cutter … ^sn35-235-3-1

You’d be better off mutilating your tongue faculty with a sharp razor … ^sn35-235-4-1

You’d be better off mutilating your body faculty with a sharp spear, burning, blazing and glowing, than getting caught up in the features by way of the details in touches known by the body. ^sn35-235-5-1

For if you die at a time when your consciousness is still tied to gratification in the features or details, it’s possible you’ll go to one of two destinations: ^sn35-235-5-2

hell or the animal realm. ^sn35-235-5-4

I speak having seen this drawback. ^sn35-235-5-5

You’d be better off sleeping. ^sn35-235-6-1

For I say that sleep is useless, fruitless, and folly for the living. But while you’re asleep you won’t fall under the sway of such thoughts that would make you create a schism in the Saṅgha. ^sn35-235-6-2

I speak having seen this drawback. ^sn35-235-6-3

A learned noble disciple reflects on this: ^sn35-235-7-1

‘Forget mutilating the eye faculty with a red-hot iron nail, burning, blazing and glowing! ^sn35-235-7-2

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-7-3

the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-235-7-4

Forget mutilating the ear faculty with a sharp iron spike, burning, blazing and glowing! ^sn35-235-8-1

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-8-2

the ear, sounds, ear consciousness, and ear contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on ear contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-235-8-3

Forget mutilating the nose faculty with a sharp nail cutter, burning, blazing and glowing! ^sn35-235-9-1

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-9-2

the nose, smells, nose consciousness, and nose contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on nose contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-235-9-3

Forget mutilating the tongue faculty with a sharp razor, burning, blazing and glowing! ^sn35-235-10-1

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-10-2

the tongue, tastes, tongue consciousness, and tongue contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on tongue contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-235-10-3

Forget mutilating the body faculty with a sharp spear, burning, blazing and glowing! ^sn35-235-11-1

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-11-2

the body, touches, body consciousness, and body contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on body contact is also impermanent. ^sn35-235-11-3

Forget sleeping! ^sn35-235-12-1

I’d better focus on the fact that ^sn35-235-12-2

the mind, ideas, mind consciousness, and mind contact are impermanent. And the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also impermanent.’ ^sn35-235-12-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, sights, eye consciousness, and eye contact. And they become disillusioned with the painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact. ^sn35-235-13-1

They grow disillusioned with the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind … painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact. ^sn35-235-13-2

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn35-235-13-3

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn35-235-13-4

This is the exposition of the teaching on burning.” ^sn35-235-13-5

### The Simile of Hands and Feet (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.236) ^sn35-236-0

“Mendicants, when there are hands, picking up and putting down are found. ^sn35-236-1-1

When there are feet, coming and going are found. ^sn35-236-1-2

When there are joints, contracting and extending are found. ^sn35-236-1-3

When there’s a belly, hunger and thirst are found. ^sn35-236-1-4

In the same way, when there’s an eye, pleasure and pain arise internally conditioned by eye contact. ^sn35-236-1-5

When there’s an ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-236-1-6

mind, pleasure and pain arise internally conditioned by mind contact. ^sn35-236-1-7

When there are no hands, picking up and putting down aren’t found. ^sn35-236-2-1

When there are no feet, coming and going aren’t found. ^sn35-236-2-2

When there are no joints, contracting and extending aren’t found. ^sn35-236-2-3

When there’s no belly, hunger and thirst aren’t found. ^sn35-236-2-4

In the same way, when there’s no eye, pleasure and pain don’t arise internally conditioned by eye contact. ^sn35-236-2-5

When there’s no ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-236-2-6

mind, pleasure and pain don’t arise internally conditioned by mind contact.” ^sn35-236-2-7

### The Simile of Hands and Feet (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.237) ^sn35-237-0

“Mendicants, when there are hands, there’s picking up and putting down. ^sn35-237-1-1

When there are feet, there’s coming and going. ^sn35-237-1-2

When there are joints, there’s contracting and extending. ^sn35-237-1-3

When there’s a belly, there’s hunger and thirst. ^sn35-237-1-4

In the same way, when there’s an eye, pleasure and pain arise internally conditioned by eye contact. ^sn35-237-1-5

When there’s an ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-237-1-6

mind, pleasure and pain arise internally conditioned by mind contact. ^sn35-237-1-7

When there are no hands, there’s no picking up and putting down. ^sn35-237-2-1

When there are no feet, there’s no coming and going. ^sn35-237-2-2

When there are no joints, there’s no contracting and extending. ^sn35-237-2-3

When there’s no belly, there’s no hunger and thirst. ^sn35-237-2-4

In the same way, when there’s no eye, pleasure and pain don’t arise internally conditioned by eye contact. ^sn35-237-2-5

When there’s no ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-237-2-6

mind, pleasure and pain don’t arise internally conditioned by mind contact.” ^sn35-237-2-7

### The Simile of the Vipers (Linked Discourses 35.238) ^sn35-238-0

“Mendicants, suppose there were four venomous vipers of awesome might. ^sn35-238-1-1

Then a person would come along who wants to live and doesn’t want to die, who wants to be happy and recoils from pain. ^sn35-238-1-2

They’d say to him, ^sn35-238-1-3

‘My man, here are four venomous vipers of awesome might. They must be periodically picked up, washed, fed, and put to sleep. ^sn35-238-1-4

But when one or other of these four venomous vipers gets angry with you, you’ll meet with death or deadly pain. ^sn35-238-1-5

So then, my man, do what has to be done.’ ^sn35-238-1-6

Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers, would flee this way or that. ^sn35-238-2-1

They’d say to him, ^sn35-238-2-2

‘My man, there are five deadly enemies chasing you, thinking: “When we catch sight of him, we’ll murder him right there!” ^sn35-238-2-3

So then, my man, do what has to be done.’ ^sn35-238-2-4

Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies, would flee this way or that. ^sn35-238-3-1

They’d say to him, ^sn35-238-3-2

‘My man, there’s a sixth hidden killer chasing you with a drawn sword, thinking: “When I catch sight of him, I’ll chop off his head right there!” ^sn35-238-3-3

So then, my man, do what has to be done.’ ^sn35-238-3-4

Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies and the hidden killer, would flee this way or that. ^sn35-238-4-1

He’d see an empty village. ^sn35-238-4-2

But whatever house he enters is vacant, deserted, and empty. ^sn35-238-4-3

And whatever vessel he touches is vacant, hollow, and empty. ^sn35-238-4-4

They’d say to him, ^sn35-238-4-5

‘My man, there are bandits who raid villages, and they’re striking now. ^sn35-238-4-6

So then, my man, do what has to be done.’ ^sn35-238-4-7

Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies and the hidden killer and the bandits, would flee this way or that. ^sn35-238-5-1

He’d see a large deluge, whose near shore is dubious and perilous, while the far shore is a sanctuary free of peril. ^sn35-238-5-2

But there’s no ferryboat or bridge for crossing over. ^sn35-238-5-3

Then that man would think, ^sn35-238-5-4

‘Why don’t I gather grass, sticks, branches, and leaves and make a raft? Riding on the raft, and paddling with my hands and feet, I can safely reach the far shore.’ ^sn35-238-5-6

And so that man did exactly that. Having crossed over and gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground. ^sn35-238-6-1

I’ve made up this simile to make a point. ^sn35-238-7-1

And this is the point. ^sn35-238-7-2

‘Four venomous vipers of awesome might’ is a term for the four principal states: ^sn35-238-7-3

the elements of earth, water, fire, and air. ^sn35-238-7-4

‘Five deadly enemies’ is a term for the five grasping aggregates, that is: ^sn35-238-8-1

form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn35-238-8-2

‘The sixth hidden killer with a drawn sword’ is a term for relishing and greed. ^sn35-238-9-1

‘Empty village’ is a term for the six interior sense fields. ^sn35-238-10-1

If an astute, competent, clever person investigates this in relation to the eye, it appears vacant, hollow, and empty. ^sn35-238-10-2

If an astute, competent, clever person investigates this in relation to the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-238-10-3

mind, it appears vacant, hollow, and empty. ^sn35-238-10-4

‘Bandits who raid villages’ is a term for the six exterior sense fields. ^sn35-238-11-1

The eye is struck by both agreeable and disagreeable sights. ^sn35-238-11-2

The ear … ^sn35-238-11-3

nose … ^sn35-238-11-4

tongue … ^sn35-238-11-5

body … ^sn35-238-11-6

mind is struck by both agreeable and disagreeable ideas. ^sn35-238-11-7

‘Large deluge’ is a term for the four floods: ^sn35-238-12-1

the floods of sensual pleasures, desire to be reborn, views, and ignorance. ^sn35-238-12-2

‘The near shore that’s dubious and perilous’ is a term for substantial reality. ^sn35-238-13-1

‘The far shore, a sanctuary free of peril’ is a term for extinguishment. ^sn35-238-14-1

‘The raft’ is a term for the noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn35-238-15-1

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn35-238-15-2

‘Paddling with hands and feet’ is a term for rousing energy. ^sn35-238-16-1

‘Crossed over, gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground’ is a term for a perfected one.” ^sn35-238-17-1

### The Simile of the Chariot (Linked Discourses 35.239) ^sn35-239-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has three qualities they’re full of joy and happiness in this very life, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements. ^sn35-239-1-1

What three? ^sn35-239-1-2

They guard the sense doors, eat in moderation, and are committed to wakefulness. ^sn35-239-1-3

And how does a mendicant guard the sense doors? ^sn35-239-2-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-239-2-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-239-2-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^sn35-239-2-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^sn35-239-2-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^sn35-239-2-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^sn35-239-2-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-239-2-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-239-2-9

Suppose a chariot stood harnessed to thoroughbreds at a level crossroads, with a goad ready. A deft horse trainer, a master charioteer, might mount the chariot, taking the reins in his right hand and goad in the left. He’d drive out and back wherever he wishes, whenever he wishes. ^sn35-239-2-10

In the same way, a mendicant trains to protect, control, tame, and pacify these six senses. ^sn35-239-2-11

That’s how a mendicant guards the sense doors. ^sn35-239-2-12

And how does a mendicant eat in moderation? ^sn35-239-3-1

It’s when a mendicant reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^sn35-239-3-2

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^sn35-239-3-3

It’s like a person who puts ointment on a wound only so that it can heal; or who oils an axle only so that it can carry a load. ^sn35-239-3-4

In the same way, a mendicant reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^sn35-239-3-5

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^sn35-239-3-6

That’s how a mendicant eats in moderation. ^sn35-239-3-7

And how is a mendicant committed to wakefulness? ^sn35-239-4-1

It’s when a mendicant practices walking and sitting meditation by day, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^sn35-239-4-2

In the first watch of the night, they continue to practice walking and sitting meditation. ^sn35-239-4-3

In the middle watch, they lie down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^sn35-239-4-4

In the last watch, they get up and continue to practice walking and sitting meditation, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^sn35-239-4-5

This is how a mendicant is committed to wakefulness. ^sn35-239-4-6

When a mendicant possesses these three qualities they’re full of joy and happiness in this very life, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements.” ^sn35-239-4-7

### The Simile of the Tortoise (Linked Discourses 35.240) ^sn35-240-0

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a tortoise was grazing along the bank of a river in the afternoon. ^sn35-240-1-1

At the same time, a jackal was also hunting along the river bank. ^sn35-240-1-2

The tortoise saw the jackal off in the distance hunting, ^sn35-240-1-3

so it drew its limbs and neck inside its shell, and kept passive and silent. ^sn35-240-1-4

But the jackal also saw the tortoise off in the distance grazing. ^sn35-240-1-5

So it went up to the tortoise and waited nearby, thinking, ^sn35-240-1-6

‘When that tortoise sticks one or other of its limbs or neck out from its shell, I’ll grab it right there, rip it out, and eat it!’ ^sn35-240-1-7

But when that tortoise didn’t stick one or other of its limbs or neck out from its shell, the jackal left disappointed, since it couldn’t find a vulnerability. ^sn35-240-1-8

In the same way, Māra the Wicked is always waiting nearby, thinking: ^sn35-240-2-1

‘Hopefully I can find a vulnerability in the eye, ^sn35-240-2-2

ear, nose, tongue, body, or ^sn35-240-2-3

mind.’ ^sn35-240-2-4

That’s why you should live with sense doors guarded. ^sn35-240-2-5

When you see a sight with your eyes, don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-240-2-6

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-240-2-7

When you hear a sound with your ears … ^sn35-240-2-8

When you smell an odor with your nose … ^sn35-240-2-9

When you taste a flavor with your tongue … ^sn35-240-2-10

When you feel a touch with your body … ^sn35-240-2-11

When you know an idea with your mind, don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^sn35-240-2-12

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^sn35-240-2-13

When you live with your sense doors guarded, Māra will leave you disappointed, since he can’t find a vulnerability, ^sn35-240-2-14

just like the jackal left the tortoise. ^sn35-240-2-15

A mendicant should collect their thoughts ^sn35-240-3-1

as a tortoise draws its limbs into its shell. ^sn35-240-3-2

Independent, not disturbing others, ^sn35-240-3-3

quenched: they wouldn’t blame anyone.” ^sn35-240-3-4

### The Simile of the Tree Trunk (1st) (Linked Discourses 35.241) ^sn35-241-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī on the bank of the Ganges river. ^sn35-241-1-1

Seeing a large tree trunk being carried along by the current, ^sn35-241-1-2

he addressed the mendicants, ^sn35-241-1-3

“Mendicants, do you see that large tree trunk being carried along by the current of the Ganges river?” ^sn35-241-1-4

“Yes, sir.” ^sn35-241-1-5

“Mendicants, assume that that tree trunk doesn’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And assume that it doesn’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and that it doesn’t rot away. ^sn35-241-1-6

In that case, that tree trunk will slant, slope, and incline towards the ocean. ^sn35-241-1-7

Why is that? ^sn35-241-1-8

Because the current of the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines towards the ocean. ^sn35-241-1-9

In the same way, assume that you don’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And assume that you don’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and that you don’t rot away. ^sn35-241-2-1

In that case, you will slant, slope, and incline towards extinguishment. ^sn35-241-2-3

Why is that? ^sn35-241-2-4

Because right view slants, slopes, and inclines towards extinguishment.” ^sn35-241-2-5

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha: ^sn35-241-2-6

“But sir, what’s the near shore and what’s the far shore? What’s sinking in the middle? What’s getting stranded on high ground? What’s getting taken by humans or non-humans? What’s getting caught up in a whirlpool? And what’s rotting away?” ^sn35-241-2-7

“‘The near shore’, mendicant, is a term for the six interior sense fields. ^sn35-241-3-1

‘The far shore’ is a term for the six exterior sense fields. ^sn35-241-3-2

‘Sinking in the middle’ is a term for greed and relishing. ^sn35-241-3-3

‘Stranded on high ground’ is a term for the conceit ‘I am’. ^sn35-241-3-4

And what’s getting taken by humans? ^sn35-241-4-1

It’s when someone mixes closely with laypeople, sharing their joys and sorrows—happy when they’re happy and sad when they’re sad—and getting themselves involved in their business. ^sn35-241-4-2

That’s called getting taken by humans. ^sn35-241-4-3

And what’s getting taken by non-humans? ^sn35-241-5-1

It’s when someone leads the spiritual life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^sn35-241-5-2

That’s called getting taken by non-humans. ^sn35-241-5-3

‘Caught up in a whirlpool’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^sn35-241-5-4

And what’s rotting away? ^sn35-241-6-1

It’s when some person is unethical, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved. ^sn35-241-6-2

This is called ‘rotting away’.” ^sn35-241-6-3

Now at that time Nanda the cowherd was sitting not far from the Buddha. ^sn35-241-7-1

Then he said to the Buddha: ^sn35-241-7-2

“I won’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And I won’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and I won’t rot away. ^sn35-241-7-3

Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?” ^sn35-241-7-4

“Well then, Nanda, return the cows to their owners.” ^sn35-241-7-5

“Sir, the cows will go back by themselves, since they love their calves.” ^sn35-241-7-6

“Still, Nanda, you should return them to their owners.” ^sn35-241-7-7

Then Nanda, after returning the cows to their owners, went up to the Buddha and said to him, ^sn35-241-7-8

“Sir, I have returned the cows to their owners. ^sn35-241-7-9

May I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?” ^sn35-241-7-10

And the cowherd Nanda received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^sn35-241-7-11

Not long after his ordination, ^sn35-241-7-12

Venerable Nanda became one of the perfected. ^sn35-241-7-13

### The Simile of the Tree Trunk (2nd) (Linked Discourses 35.242) ^sn35-242-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kimibilā on the bank of the Ganges river. ^sn35-242-1-1

Seeing a large tree trunk being carried along by the current, ^sn35-242-1-2

he addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-242-1-3

“Mendicants, do you see that large tree trunk being carried along by the current of the Ganges river?” ^sn35-242-1-4

“Yes, sir,” they replied. … ^sn35-242-1-5

When this was said, Venerable Kimbila said to the Buddha: ^sn35-242-1-6

“But sir, what’s the near shore and what’s the far shore? What’s sinking in the middle? What’s getting stranded on high ground? What’s getting taken by humans or non-humans? What’s getting caught up in a whirlpool? And what’s rotting away?” … ^sn35-242-1-7

“And what, Kimbila, is rotting away? ^sn35-242-1-8

It’s when a mendicant has committed the kind of corrupt offense for which no rehabilitation is possible. ^sn35-242-1-9

This is called ‘rotting away’.” ^sn35-242-1-10

### The Explanation on the Festering (Linked Discourses 35.243) ^sn35-243-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^sn35-243-1-1

Now at that time a new town hall had recently been constructed for the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu. It had not yet been occupied by an ascetic or brahmin or any human at all. ^sn35-243-1-2

Then the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn35-243-1-3

“Sir, a new town hall has recently been constructed for the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu. It has not yet been occupied by an ascetic or brahmin or any human at all. ^sn35-243-1-4

May the Buddha be the first to use it, ^sn35-243-1-5

and only then will the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu use it. ^sn35-243-1-6

That would be for the lasting welfare and happiness of the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu.” ^sn35-243-1-7

The Buddha consented with silence. ^sn35-243-1-8

Knowing that the Buddha had consented, the Sakyans rose from their seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on their right. Then they went to the new town hall, where they spread carpets all over, prepared seats, set up a water jar, and placed an oil lamp. Then they went back to the Buddha and told him of their preparations, saying, ^sn35-243-2-1

“Please, sir, come at your convenience.” ^sn35-243-2-3

Then the Buddha robed up and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the new town hall together with the Saṅgha of mendicants. Having washed his feet he entered the town hall and sat against the central column facing east. ^sn35-243-2-4

The Saṅgha of mendicants also washed their feet, entered the town hall, and sat against the west wall facing east, with the Buddha right in front of them. ^sn35-243-2-5

The Sakyans of Kapilavatthu also washed their feet, entered the town hall, and sat against the east wall facing west, with the Buddha right in front of them. ^sn35-243-2-6

The Buddha spent much of the night educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the Sakyans with a Dhamma talk. Then he sent them off, saying, ^sn35-243-2-7

“The night is getting late, Gotamas. ^sn35-243-2-8

Please go at your convenience.” ^sn35-243-2-9

“Yes, sir,” replied the Sakyans. They rose from their seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on their right, before leaving. ^sn35-243-2-10

And then, soon after the Sakyans had left, the Buddha addressed Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, ^sn35-243-3-1

“Moggallāna, the Saṅgha of mendicants is rid of dullness and drowsiness. ^sn35-243-3-2

Give them some Dhamma talk as you feel inspired. ^sn35-243-3-3

My back is sore, ^sn35-243-3-4

I’ll stretch it.” ^sn35-243-3-5

“Yes, sir,” Mahāmoggallāna replied. ^sn35-243-3-6

And then the Buddha spread out his outer robe folded in four and laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^sn35-243-3-7

There Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the mendicants: ^sn35-243-3-8

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn35-243-3-9

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn35-243-3-10

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this: ^sn35-243-3-11

“I will teach you the explanation of the festering and the not festering. ^sn35-243-3-12

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn35-243-3-13

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^sn35-243-3-14

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this: ^sn35-243-3-15

“And how is someone festering? ^sn35-243-4-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with the eye. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-243-4-2

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-243-4-3

They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch … ^sn35-243-4-4

know an idea with the mind. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-243-4-5

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-243-4-6

This is called a mendicant who is festering when it comes to sights known by the eye, ^sn35-243-4-7

sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-243-4-8

ideas known by the mind. ^sn35-243-4-9

When a mendicant lives like this, if Māra comes at them through the eye he finds a vulnerability and gets an opportunity. ^sn35-243-4-10

If Māra comes at them through the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-243-4-11

mind he finds a vulnerability and gets an opportunity. ^sn35-243-4-12

Suppose there was a house made of reeds or straw that was dried up, withered, and decrepit. ^sn35-243-5-1

If a person came to it with a burning grass torch from the east, ^sn35-243-5-2

west, ^sn35-243-5-3

north, ^sn35-243-5-4

south, ^sn35-243-5-5

below, ^sn35-243-5-6

above, ^sn35-243-5-7

or from anywhere, the fire would find a vulnerability, it would get an opportunity. ^sn35-243-5-8

In the same way, when a mendicant lives like this, if Māra comes at them through the eye he finds a vulnerability and gets an opportunity. ^sn35-243-5-9

If Māra comes at them through the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-243-5-10

mind he finds a vulnerability and gets an opportunity. ^sn35-243-5-11

When a mendicant lives like this, they’re mastered by sights, ^sn35-243-5-12

sounds, ^sn35-243-5-13

smells, ^sn35-243-5-14

tastes, ^sn35-243-5-15

touches, ^sn35-243-5-16

and ideas, they don’t master these things. ^sn35-243-5-17

This is called a mendicant who has been mastered by sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. They’re mastered, not a master. Bad, unskillful qualities have mastered them, which are corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, and resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death. ^sn35-243-5-18

That’s how someone is festering. ^sn35-243-5-19

And how is someone not festering? ^sn35-243-6-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with the eye. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-243-6-2

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-243-6-3

They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch … ^sn35-243-6-4

know an idea with the mind. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-243-6-5

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-243-6-6

This is called a mendicant who is not festering when it comes to sights known by the eye, ^sn35-243-6-7

sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ideas known by the mind. ^sn35-243-6-8

When a mendicant lives like this, if Māra comes at them through the eye he doesn’t find a vulnerability or get an opportunity. ^sn35-243-6-9

If Māra comes at them through the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-243-6-10

mind he doesn’t find a vulnerability or get an opportunity. ^sn35-243-6-11

Suppose there was a bungalow or hall made of thick clay with its plaster still wet. ^sn35-243-7-1

If a person came to it with a burning grass torch from the east, ^sn35-243-7-2

west, ^sn35-243-7-3

north, ^sn35-243-7-4

south, ^sn35-243-7-5

below, ^sn35-243-7-6

above, ^sn35-243-7-7

or from anywhere, the fire wouldn’t find a vulnerability, it would get no opportunity. ^sn35-243-7-8

In the same way, when a mendicant lives like this, if Māra comes at them through the eye he doesn’t find a vulnerability or get an opportunity. ^sn35-243-7-9

If Māra comes at them through the ear … nose … tongue … body … mind he doesn’t find a vulnerability or get an opportunity. ^sn35-243-7-10

When a mendicant lives like this, they master sights, ^sn35-243-7-11

sounds, ^sn35-243-7-12

smells, ^sn35-243-7-13

tastes, ^sn35-243-7-14

touches, ^sn35-243-7-15

and ideas, they’re not mastered by these things. ^sn35-243-7-16

This is called a mendicant who has mastered sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. They’re a master, not mastered. Bad, unskillful qualities have been mastered by them, which are corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, and resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death. ^sn35-243-7-17

That’s how someone is not festering.” ^sn35-243-7-18

Then the Buddha got up and said to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna: ^sn35-243-8-1

“Good, good, Moggallāna! ^sn35-243-8-2

It’s good that you’ve taught this explanation of the festering and the not festering.” ^sn35-243-8-3

This is what Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said, ^sn35-243-9-1

and the teacher approved. ^sn35-243-9-2

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what Mahāmoggallāna said. ^sn35-243-9-3

### Entailing Suffering (Linked Discourses 35.244) ^sn35-244-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant truly understands the origin and disappearance of all things that entail suffering, ^sn35-244-1-1

then they’ve seen sensual pleasures in such a way that they have no underlying tendency for desire, affection, infatuation, and passion for sensual pleasures. ^sn35-244-1-2

And they’ve awakened to a way of conduct and a way of living such that, when they live in that way, bad, unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure don’t overwhelm them. ^sn35-244-1-3

And how does a mendicant truly understand the origin and disappearance of all things that entail suffering? ^sn35-244-2-1

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^sn35-244-2-2

Such is feeling … ^sn35-244-2-3

perception … ^sn35-244-2-4

choices … ^sn35-244-2-5

consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^sn35-244-2-6

That’s how a mendicant truly understands the origin and disappearance of all things that entail suffering. ^sn35-244-2-7

And how has a mendicant seen sensual pleasures in such a way that they have no underlying tendency for desire, affection, infatuation, and passion for sensual pleasures? ^sn35-244-3-1

Suppose there was a pit of glowing coals deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing coals that neither flamed nor smoked. Then a person would come along who wants to live and doesn’t want to die, who wants to be happy and recoils from pain. Two strong men would grab each arm and drag them towards the pit of glowing coals. They’d writhe and struggle to and fro. ^sn35-244-3-2

Why is that? ^sn35-244-3-3

For that person knows, ‘If I fall in that pit of glowing coals, that will result in my death or deadly pain.’ ^sn35-244-3-4

In the same way, when a mendicant has seen sensual pleasures as like a pit of glowing coals, they have no underlying tendency for desire, affection, infatuation, and passion for sensual pleasures. ^sn35-244-3-5

And how has a mendicant awakened to a way of conduct and a way of living such that, when they live in that way, bad, unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure don’t overwhelm them? ^sn35-244-4-1

Suppose a person was to enter a thicket full of thorns. They’d have thorns before and behind, to the left and right, below and above. So they’d go forward mindfully and come back mindfully, thinking, ‘May I not get any thorns!’ ^sn35-244-4-2

In the same way, whatever in the world seems nice and pleasant is called a thorn in the training of the Noble One. ^sn35-244-4-3

Understanding that this is a thorn, they should understand restraint and lack of restraint. ^sn35-244-4-4

And how is someone unrestrained? ^sn35-244-5-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with the eye. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-244-5-2

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-244-5-3

They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch … ^sn35-244-5-4

know an idea with the mind. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and a limited heart. ^sn35-244-5-5

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-244-5-6

This is how someone is unrestrained. ^sn35-244-5-7

And how is someone restrained? ^sn35-244-6-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with the eye. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-244-6-2

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-244-6-3

They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch … ^sn35-244-6-4

know an idea with the mind. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-244-6-5

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-244-6-6

This is how someone is restrained. ^sn35-244-6-7

Perhaps while that mendicant is conducting themselves and living in this way, bad, unskillful memories and thoughts that tighten fetters arise every so often due to loss of mindfulness. Slowly mindfulness arises, but then they quickly give up, get rid of, eliminate, and obliterate those thoughts. ^sn35-244-7-1

Suppose there was an iron cauldron that had been heated all day, and a person let two or three drops of water fall onto it. Slowly the drops fall, but then they quickly dry up and evaporate. ^sn35-244-8-1

In the same way, perhaps while that mendicant is conducting themselves and living in this way, bad, unskillful memories and thoughts that tighten fetters arise every so often due to loss of mindfulness. Slowly mindfulness arises, but then they quickly give up, get rid of, eliminate, and obliterate those thoughts. ^sn35-244-8-2

This is how a mendicant has awakened to a way of conduct and a way of living such that, when they live in that way, bad, unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure don’t overwhelm them. ^sn35-244-8-3

While that mendicant conducts themselves in this way and lives in this way, it may be that rulers or their chief ministers, friends or colleagues, relatives or family would invite them to accept wealth, saying, ^sn35-244-8-4

‘Please, my man, why let these ocher robes torment you? ^sn35-244-8-5

Why follow the practice of shaving your head and carrying an alms bowl? ^sn35-244-8-6

Come, return to a lesser life, enjoy wealth, and make merit!’ ^sn35-244-8-7

But it is quite impossible for a mendicant who conducts themselves in this way and lives in this way to resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^sn35-244-8-8

Suppose that, although the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east, a large crowd were to come along with a spade and basket, saying: ‘We’ll make this Ganges river slant, slope, and incline to the west!’ ^sn35-244-9-1

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn35-244-9-2

Would they still succeed?” ^sn35-244-9-3

“No, sir. ^sn35-244-9-4

Why is that? ^sn35-244-9-5

The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn35-244-9-6

It’s not easy to make it slant, slope, and incline to the west. ^sn35-244-9-7

That large crowd will eventually get weary and frustrated.” ^sn35-244-9-8

“In the same way, while that mendicant conducts themselves in this way and lives in this way, it may be that rulers or their chief ministers, friends or colleagues, relatives or family should invite them to accept wealth, saying, ^sn35-244-9-9

‘Please, my man, why let these ocher robes torment you? ^sn35-244-9-10

Why follow the practice of shaving your head and carrying an alms bowl? ^sn35-244-9-11

Come, return to a lesser life, enjoy wealth, and make merit!’ ^sn35-244-9-12

But it is quite impossible for a mendicant who conducts themselves in this way and lives in this way to resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^sn35-244-9-13

Why is that? ^sn35-244-9-14

Because for a long time that mendicant’s mind has slanted, sloped, and inclined to seclusion. So it’s impossible for them to return to a lesser life.” ^sn35-244-9-15

### The Simile of the Parrot Tree (Linked Discourses 35.245) ^sn35-245-0

Then one mendicant went up to another mendicant and asked, ^sn35-245-1-1

“Reverend, at what point is a mendicant’s vision well purified?” ^sn35-245-1-2

“When a mendicant truly understands the origin and disappearance of the six fields of contact, at that point their vision is well purified.” ^sn35-245-1-3

Not content with that answer, that mendicant went up to a series of other mendicants and received the following answers: ^sn35-245-2-1

“When a mendicant truly understands the origin and disappearance of the five grasping aggregates, at that point their vision is well purified.” ^sn35-245-2-3

“When a mendicant truly understands the origin and disappearance of the four principal states, at that point their vision is well purified.” ^sn35-245-3-3

“When a mendicant truly understands that everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease, at that point their vision is well purified.” ^sn35-245-4-3

Not content with any of those answers, that mendicant went up to the Buddha and told him what had happened. Then he asked, ^sn35-245-5-1

“Sir, at what point is a mendicant’s vision well purified?” ^sn35-245-5-12

“Mendicant, suppose a person had never seen a parrot tree before. ^sn35-245-6-1

They’d go up to someone who had seen a parrot tree and ask them, ^sn35-245-6-2

‘My man, what’s a parrot tree like?’ ^sn35-245-6-3

They’d say, ^sn35-245-6-4

‘A parrot tree is blackish, like a charred stump.’ ^sn35-245-6-5

Now, at that time a parrot tree may well have been just as that person saw it. ^sn35-245-6-6

Not content with that answer, that person would go up to a series of other people and receive the following answers: ^sn35-245-7-1

‘A parrot tree is reddish, like a scrap of meat.’ ^sn35-245-7-4

‘A parrot tree has flaking bark and burst pods, like a lebbeck tree.’ ^sn35-245-7-9

‘A parrot tree has luxuriant, shady foliage, like a banyan.’ ^sn35-245-7-14

Now, at each of those times a parrot tree may well have been just as those people saw them. ^sn35-245-7-15

In the same way, those true persons each answered according to what they were focused on when their vision was well purified. ^sn35-245-7-16

Suppose there was a king’s frontier citadel with fortified embankments, ramparts, and arches, and six gates. ^sn35-245-8-1

And it has a gatekeeper who is astute, competent, and clever. He keeps strangers out and lets known people in. ^sn35-245-8-2

A swift pair of messengers would arrive from the east and say to the gatekeeper, ^sn35-245-8-3

‘Worthy man, where is the lord of the city?’ ^sn35-245-8-4

They’d say, ^sn35-245-8-5

‘There he is, sirs, seated at the central square.’ ^sn35-245-8-6

Then that swift pair of messengers would deliver a message of truth to the lord of the city and depart the way they came. ^sn35-245-8-7

A swift pair of messengers would come from the west … ^sn35-245-8-8

north … ^sn35-245-8-9

south … ^sn35-245-8-10

deliver a message of truth to the lord of the city and depart the way they came. ^sn35-245-8-14

I’ve made up this simile to make a point. ^sn35-245-9-1

And this is the point. ^sn35-245-9-2

‘City’ is a term for this body made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^sn35-245-9-3

‘Six gates’ is a term for the six interior sense fields. ^sn35-245-9-4

‘Gatekeeper’ is a term for mindfulness. ^sn35-245-9-5

‘A swift pair of messengers’ is a term for serenity and discernment. ^sn35-245-9-6

‘The lord of the city’ is a term for consciousness. ^sn35-245-9-7

‘The central square’ is a term for the four principal states: ^sn35-245-9-8

the elements of earth, water, fire, and air. ^sn35-245-9-9

‘A message of truth’ is a term for extinguishment. ^sn35-245-9-10

‘The way they came’ is a term for the noble eightfold path, that is, ^sn35-245-9-11

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.” ^sn35-245-9-12

### The Simile of the Harp (Linked Discourses 35.246) ^sn35-246-0

“Mendicants, any monk or nun who has desire or greed or hate or delusion or aversion come up for sights known by the eye should shield their mind from them: ^sn35-246-1-1

‘This path is dangerous and perilous, thorny and tangled; it’s a wrong turn, a bad path, in poor condition. ^sn35-246-1-2

This path is frequented by untrue persons, not by true persons. ^sn35-246-1-3

It’s not worthy of you.’ ^sn35-246-1-4

The mind should be shielded from this when it comes to sights known by the eye. ^sn35-246-1-5

Any monk or nun who has desire or greed or hate or delusion or repulsion come up for sounds … smells … tastes … touches … ^sn35-246-1-6

ideas known by the mind should shield their mind against them: ^sn35-246-1-7

‘This path is dangerous and perilous, thorny and tangled; it’s a wrong turn, a bad path, a sorry state. ^sn35-246-1-8

This path is frequented by untrue persons, not by true persons. ^sn35-246-1-9

It’s not worthy of you.’ ^sn35-246-1-10

The mind should be shielded from this when it comes to ideas known by the mind. ^sn35-246-1-11

Suppose the crops have ripened, ^sn35-246-2-1

but the caretaker is negligent. If an ox fond of crops invades the crops they’d indulge themselves as much as they like. ^sn35-246-2-2

In the same way, when an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t exercise restraint when it comes to the six fields of contact, they indulge themselves in the five kinds of sensual stimulation as much as they like. ^sn35-246-2-3

Suppose the crops have ripened, and the caretaker is diligent. If an ox fond of crops invades the crops ^sn35-246-3-1

the caretaker would grab them firmly by the muzzle. ^sn35-246-3-2

Then they’d grab them above the hump and hold them fast there. ^sn35-246-3-3

Then they’d give them a good thrashing ^sn35-246-3-4

before driving them away. ^sn35-246-3-5

For a second time, ^sn35-246-3-6

and even a third time, the same thing might happen. ^sn35-246-3-7

As a result, no matter how often they might stand or sit in a village or wilderness, that ox fond of crops would never invade that crop again, ^sn35-246-3-12

remembering the beating they got earlier. ^sn35-246-3-13

In the same way, when a mendicant’s mind is straightened up, well straightened up when it comes to the six fields of contact, becomes stilled internally; it settles, unifies, and becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn35-246-3-14

Suppose a king or their chief minister had never heard the sound of an arched harp. ^sn35-246-4-1

When he first hears the sound, ^sn35-246-4-2

he’d say, ^sn35-246-4-3

‘Oh my, what is making this sound, so arousing, sensuous, intoxicating, infatuating, and captivating?’ ^sn35-246-4-4

They’d say to him, ^sn35-246-4-5

‘That, sir, is an arched harp.’ ^sn35-246-4-6

He’d say, ^sn35-246-4-7

‘Go, mister, fetch me that arched harp.’ ^sn35-246-4-8

So they’d fetch it ^sn35-246-4-9

and say, ^sn35-246-4-10

‘This, sir, is that arched harp.’ ^sn35-246-4-11

He’d say, ^sn35-246-4-12

‘I’ve had enough of that arched harp! Just fetch me the sound.’ ^sn35-246-4-13

They’d say, ^sn35-246-4-14

‘Sir, this arched harp is made of many components assembled together, ^sn35-246-4-15

which make a sound when they’re played. That is, ^sn35-246-4-16

it depends on the soundbox, the leather resonator, the neck, the tuning cords, the strings, the plectrum, and a person to play it properly. That’s how an arched harp is made of many components assembled together, ^sn35-246-4-17

which make a sound when they’re played.’ ^sn35-246-4-18

But he’d split that harp into ten pieces or a hundred pieces, then splinter it up. ^sn35-246-4-19

He’d burn the splinters with fire, and reduce them to ashes. ^sn35-246-4-20

Then he’d whisk away the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^sn35-246-4-21

Then he’d say, ^sn35-246-4-22

‘It seems that there’s nothing to this thing called an arched harp or whatever’s called an arched harp! But people waste their time with it, negligent and heedless!’ ^sn35-246-4-23

In the same way, a mendicant searches for form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness anywhere they might be reborn. ^sn35-246-4-24

As they search in this way, ^sn35-246-4-25

their ideas of ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’ are no more.” ^sn35-246-4-29

### The Simile of Six Animals (Linked Discourses 35.247) ^sn35-247-0

“Mendicants, suppose a person with wounded and festering limbs was to enter a thicket of reeds. ^sn35-247-1-1

The kusa thorns would pierce their feet, and the reed leaves would scratch their limbs. ^sn35-247-1-2

And that would cause that person to experience even more pain and distress. ^sn35-247-1-3

In the same way, some mendicant goes to a village or a wilderness and gets scolded, ^sn35-247-1-4

‘This venerable, acting like this, behaving like this, is a filthy village thorn.’ ^sn35-247-1-5

Understanding that they’re a thorn, they should understand restraint and lack of restraint. ^sn35-247-1-6

And how is someone unrestrained? ^sn35-247-2-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-247-2-2

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-247-2-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^sn35-247-2-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^sn35-247-2-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^sn35-247-2-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^sn35-247-2-7

When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted. ^sn35-247-2-8

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-247-2-9

Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope. ^sn35-247-3-1

They’d catch a snake, ^sn35-247-3-2

a dolphin, ^sn35-247-3-3

a bird, ^sn35-247-3-4

a dog, ^sn35-247-3-5

a jackal, ^sn35-247-3-6

and a monkey, ^sn35-247-3-7

tie each up with a strong rope, then tie a knot in the middle and let them loose. ^sn35-247-3-8

Then those six animals with different domains and ranges would each pull towards their own domain and range. ^sn35-247-3-9

The snake would pull one way, thinking ‘I’m going into a termite mound!’ The dolphin would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the water!’ The bird would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m flying into the atmosphere!’ The dog would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the village!’ The jackal would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the charnel ground!’ The monkey would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the jungle!’ ^sn35-247-3-10

When those six animals became exhausted and worn out, the strongest of them would get their way, and they’d all have to submit to their control. ^sn35-247-3-11

In the same way, when a mendicant has not developed or cultivated mindfulness of the body, their eye pulls towards pleasant sights, but is put off by unpleasant sights. Their ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-247-3-12

mind pulls towards pleasant ideas, but is put off by unpleasant ideas. ^sn35-247-3-13

This is how someone is unrestrained. ^sn35-247-3-14

And how is someone restrained? ^sn35-247-4-1

Take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-247-4-2

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-247-4-3

They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch … ^sn35-247-4-4

know an idea with their mind. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart. ^sn35-247-4-5

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^sn35-247-4-6

Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope. ^sn35-247-5-1

They’d catch a snake, ^sn35-247-5-2

a dolphin, ^sn35-247-5-3

a bird, ^sn35-247-5-4

a dog, ^sn35-247-5-5

a jackal, ^sn35-247-5-6

and a monkey, ^sn35-247-5-7

tie each up with a strong rope, then tether them to a strong post or pillar. ^sn35-247-5-8

Then those six animals with different domains and ranges would each pull towards their own domain and range. ^sn35-247-5-9

The snake would pull one way, thinking ‘I’m going into a termite mound!’ The dolphin would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the water!’ The bird would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m flying into the atmosphere!’ The dog would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the village!’ The jackal would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the charnel ground!’ The monkey would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the jungle!’ ^sn35-247-5-10

When those six animals became exhausted and worn out, they’d stand or sit or lie down right by that post or pillar. ^sn35-247-5-11

In the same way, when a mendicant has developed and cultivated mindfulness of the body, their eye doesn’t pull towards pleasant sights, and isn’t put off by unpleasant sights. Their ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-247-5-12

mind doesn’t pull towards pleasant ideas, and isn’t put off by unpleasant ideas. ^sn35-247-5-13

This is how someone is restrained. ^sn35-247-5-14

‘A strong post or pillar’ is a term for mindfulness of the body. ^sn35-247-6-1

So you should train like this: ^sn35-247-6-2

‘We will develop mindfulness of the body. We’ll cultivate it, make it our vehicle and our basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^sn35-247-6-3

That’s how you should train.” ^sn35-247-6-4

### The Sheaf of Barley (Linked Discourses 35.248) ^sn35-248-0

“Mendicants, suppose a sheaf of barley was placed at a crossroads. ^sn35-248-1-1

Then six people would come along carrying flails, ^sn35-248-1-2

and start threshing the sheaf of barley. ^sn35-248-1-3

So that sheaf of barley would be thoroughly threshed by those six flails. ^sn35-248-1-4

Then a seventh individual would come along carrying a flail, ^sn35-248-1-5

and they’d give the sheaf of barley a seventh threshing. ^sn35-248-1-6

So that sheaf of barley would be even more thoroughly threshed by that seventh flail. ^sn35-248-1-7

In the same way, an unlearned ordinary person is struck in the eye by both pleasant and unpleasant sights. ^sn35-248-1-8

They’re struck in the ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn35-248-1-9

mind by both pleasant and unpleasant ideas. ^sn35-248-1-10

And if that unlearned ordinary person intends to be reborn in a new state of existence in the future, that futile person is even more thoroughly struck, like that sheaf of barley threshed by the seventh person. ^sn35-248-1-11

Once upon a time, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^sn35-248-2-1

Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, addressed the titans, ^sn35-248-2-2

‘My good fellows, if the titans defeat the gods in this battle, bind Sakka, the lord of gods, by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the citadel of the titans.’ ^sn35-248-2-3

Meanwhile, Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the gods of the thirty-three, ^sn35-248-2-4

‘My good fellows, if the gods defeat the titans in this battle, bind Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods.’ ^sn35-248-2-5

In that battle the gods won and the titans lost. ^sn35-248-2-6

So the gods of the thirty-three bound Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and brought him to Sakka’s presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods. ^sn35-248-2-7

And there Vepacitti remained bound by his limbs and neck. ^sn35-248-2-8

That is, until he thought, ^sn35-248-2-9

‘It’s the gods who are principled, while the titans are unprincipled. Now I belong right here in the castle of the gods.’ ^sn35-248-2-10

Then he found himself freed from the bonds on his limbs and neck. He entertained himself, supplied and provided with the five kinds of heavenly sensual stimulation. ^sn35-248-2-11

But when he thought, ^sn35-248-2-12

‘It’s the titans who are principled, while the gods are unprincipled. Now I will go over there to the citadel of the titans,’ ^sn35-248-2-13

he found himself bound by his limbs and neck, and the five kinds of heavenly sensual stimulation fell away. ^sn35-248-2-14

That’s how subtly Vepacitti was bound. ^sn35-248-2-15

But the bonds of Māra are even more subtle than that. ^sn35-248-2-16

When you conceive, you’re bound by Māra. Not conceiving, you’re free from the Wicked One. ^sn35-248-2-17

These are what is conceived: ‘I am’, ‘I am this’, ‘I will be’, ‘I will not be’, ‘I will have form’, ‘I will be formless’, ‘I will be percipient’, ‘I will be non-percipient’, ‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’ ^sn35-248-3-1

What is conceived is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-248-3-2

So mendicants, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will live with a heart that does not conceive.’ ^sn35-248-3-3

These are what is perturbed: ‘I am’, ‘I am this’, ‘I will be’, ‘I will not be’, ‘I will have form’, ‘I will be formless’, ‘I will be percipient’, ‘I will be non-percipient’, ‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’ ^sn35-248-4-1

What is perturbed is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-248-4-2

So mendicants, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will live with a heart free of perturbation.’ ^sn35-248-4-3

These are what is trembled: ‘I am’, ‘I am this’, ‘I will be’, ‘I will not be’, ‘I will have form’, ‘I will be formless’, ‘I will be percipient’, ‘I will be non-percipient’, ‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’ ^sn35-248-5-1

What is trembled is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-248-5-2

So mendicants, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will live with a heart free of trembling.’ ^sn35-248-5-3

These are what is proliferated: ‘I am’, ‘I am this’, ‘I will be’, ‘I will not be’, ‘I will have form’, ‘I will be formless’, ‘I will be percipient’, ‘I will be non-percipient’, ‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’ ^sn35-248-6-1

What is proliferated is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-248-6-2

So mendicants, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will live with a heart free of proliferation.’ ^sn35-248-6-3

These are states of conceit: ‘I am’, ‘I am this’, ‘I will be’, ‘I will not be’, ‘I will have form’, ‘I will be formless’, ‘I will be percipient’, ‘I will be non-percipient’, ‘I will be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’ ^sn35-248-7-1

The state of conceit is a disease, a boil, a dart. ^sn35-248-7-2

So mendicants, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will live with a heart that has struck down conceit.’” ^sn35-248-7-3

The Linked Discourses on the six sense fields are complete. ^sn35-248-9-5

## Saṁyutta 36 ^sn36-0

### Immersion (Linked Discourses 36.1) ^sn36-1-0

“Mendicants, there are these three feelings. ^sn36-1-1-1

What three? ^sn36-1-1-2

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn36-1-1-3

These are the three feelings. ^sn36-1-1-4

Stilled, aware, ^sn36-1-2-1

a mindful disciple of the Buddha ^sn36-1-2-2

understands feelings, ^sn36-1-2-3

the cause of feelings, ^sn36-1-2-4

where they cease, ^sn36-1-3-1

and the path that leads to their ending. ^sn36-1-3-2

With the ending of feelings, a mendicant ^sn36-1-3-3

is hungerless, quenched.” ^sn36-1-3-4

### Pleasure (Linked Discourses 36.2) ^sn36-2-0

“Mendicants, there are these three feelings. ^sn36-2-1-1

What three? ^sn36-2-1-2

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn36-2-1-3

These are the three feelings. ^sn36-2-1-4

Whether it’s pleasant or painful, ^sn36-2-2-1

as well as what’s neutral, ^sn36-2-2-2

whatever there is that is felt ^sn36-2-2-3

internally and externally: ^sn36-2-2-4

having known, ‘this is suffering, ^sn36-2-3-1

deceptive, breaking apart’, ^sn36-2-3-2

seeing feelings vanish with every contact, ^sn36-2-3-3

that’s how they become free of desire for them.” ^sn36-2-3-4

### Giving Up (Linked Discourses 36.3) ^sn36-3-0

“Mendicants, there are these three feelings. ^sn36-3-1-1

What three? ^sn36-3-1-2

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn36-3-1-3

The underlying tendency to greed should be given up when it comes to pleasant feeling. The underlying tendency to aversion should be given up when it comes to painful feeling. The underlying tendency to ignorance should be given up when it comes to neutral feeling. ^sn36-3-1-4

When a mendicant has given up these underlying tendencies, they’re called a mendicant without underlying tendencies, who sees rightly, has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering. ^sn36-3-1-5

When you feel pleasure ^sn36-3-2-1

without understanding feeling, ^sn36-3-2-2

the underlying tendency to greed is there, ^sn36-3-2-3

if you don’t see the escape. ^sn36-3-2-4

When you feel pain ^sn36-3-3-1

without understanding feeling, ^sn36-3-3-2

the underlying tendency to aversion is there, ^sn36-3-3-3

if you don’t see the escape. ^sn36-3-3-4

As for that peaceful, neutral feeling: ^sn36-3-4-1

he of vast wisdom has taught ^sn36-3-4-2

that if you relish it, ^sn36-3-4-3

you’re still not released from suffering. ^sn36-3-4-4

But when a mendicant is keen, ^sn36-3-5-1

not neglecting situational awareness, ^sn36-3-5-2

that astute person ^sn36-3-5-3

understands all feelings. ^sn36-3-5-4

Completely understanding feelings, ^sn36-3-6-1

they’re without defilements in this very life. ^sn36-3-6-2

That knowledge master is firm in principle; ^sn36-3-6-3

when their body breaks up, they can’t be reckoned.” ^sn36-3-6-4

### The Abyss (Linked Discourses 36.4) ^sn36-4-0

“Mendicants, when an unlearned ordinary person says that ^sn36-4-1-1

there’s an abyss under the ocean, ^sn36-4-1-2

they’re speaking of something that doesn’t exist. ^sn36-4-1-3

‘Abyss’ is a term for painful physical feelings. ^sn36-4-1-5

When an unlearned ordinary person experiences painful physical feelings they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^sn36-4-1-6

They’re called an unlearned ordinary person who hasn’t stood up in the abyss and has found no footing. ^sn36-4-1-7

When a learned noble disciple experiences painful physical feelings they don’t sorrow or wail or lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^sn36-4-1-8

They’re called a learned noble disciple who has stood up in the abyss and found a footing. ^sn36-4-1-9

If you can’t abide ^sn36-4-2-1

those painful physical feelings ^sn36-4-2-2

that arise and sap your vitality; ^sn36-4-2-3

if you tremble at their touch, ^sn36-4-2-4

weeping and wailing, ^sn36-4-3-1

a weakling lacking strength— ^sn36-4-3-2

you won’t stand up in the abyss ^sn36-4-3-3

and find a footing. ^sn36-4-3-4

If you can endure ^sn36-4-4-1

those painful physical feelings ^sn36-4-4-2

that arise and sap your vitality; ^sn36-4-4-3

if you don’t tremble at their touch— ^sn36-4-4-4

you stand up in the abyss ^sn36-4-4-5

and find a footing.” ^sn36-4-4-6

### Should Be Seen (Linked Discourses 36.5) ^sn36-5-0

“Mendicants, there are these three feelings. ^sn36-5-1-1

What three? ^sn36-5-1-2

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn36-5-1-3

Pleasant feeling should be seen as suffering. Painful feeling should be seen as a dart. Neutral feeling should be seen as impermanent. ^sn36-5-1-4

When a mendicant has seen these three feelings in this way, ^sn36-5-1-5

they’re called a mendicant who sees rightly, has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering. ^sn36-5-1-6

A mendicant who sees pleasure as pain, ^sn36-5-2-1

and suffering as a dart, ^sn36-5-2-2

and that peaceful, neutral feeling ^sn36-5-2-3

as impermanent ^sn36-5-2-4

sees rightly; ^sn36-5-3-1

they completely understand feelings. ^sn36-5-3-2

Completely understanding feelings, ^sn36-5-3-3

they’re without defilements in this very life. ^sn36-5-3-4

That knowledge master is firm in principle; ^sn36-5-3-5

when their body breaks up, they can’t be reckoned.” ^sn36-5-3-6

### An Arrow (Linked Discourses 36.6) ^sn36-6-0

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person feels pleasant, painful, and neutral feelings. ^sn36-6-1-1

A learned noble disciple also feels pleasant, painful, and neutral feelings. ^sn36-6-1-2

What, then, is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an ordinary unlearned person?” ^sn36-6-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn36-6-1-4

“When an unlearned ordinary person experiences painful physical feelings they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^sn36-6-1-5

They experience two feelings: ^sn36-6-1-6

physical and mental. ^sn36-6-1-7

It’s like a person who is struck with an arrow, ^sn36-6-1-8
