form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn38-15-1-4

The Buddha said that these five grasping aggregates are substantial reality.” ^sn38-15-1-5

“But, reverend, is there a path and a practice for completely understanding this substantial reality?” ^sn38-15-1-6

“There is.” … ^sn38-15-1-7

### A Question About What’s Hard to Do (Linked Discourses 38.16) ^sn38-16-0

“Reverend Sāriputta, in this teaching and training, what is hard to do?” ^sn38-16-1-1

“Going forth, reverend, is hard to do in this teaching and training.” ^sn38-16-1-2

“But what’s hard to do for someone who has gone forth?” ^sn38-16-1-3

“When you’ve gone forth it’s hard to be satisfied.” ^sn38-16-1-4

“But what’s hard to do for someone who is satisfied?” ^sn38-16-1-5

“When you’re satisfied, it’s hard to practice in line with the teaching.” ^sn38-16-1-6

“But if a mendicant practices in line with the teaching, will it take them long to become a perfected one?” ^sn38-16-1-7

“Not long, reverend.” ^sn38-16-1-8

The Linked Discourses with Jambukhādaka are complete. ^sn38-16-1-10

## Saṁyutta 39 ^sn39-0

### With Sāmaṇḍaka on Extinguishment (Linked Discourses 39.1–15) ^sn39-1-15-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying in the land of the Vajjis near Ukkacelā on the bank of the Ganges river. ^sn39-1-15-1-1

Then the wanderer Sāmaṇḍaka went up to Venerable Sāriputta and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn39-1-15-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^sn39-1-15-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, they speak of this thing called ‘extinguishment’. ^sn39-1-15-2-1

What is extinguishment?” ^sn39-1-15-2-2

“Reverend, the ending of greed, hate, and delusion ^sn39-1-15-2-3

is called extinguishment.” ^sn39-1-15-2-4

“But, reverend, is there a path and a practice for realizing this extinguishment?” ^sn39-1-15-2-5

“There is, reverend.” ^sn39-1-15-2-6

“Well, what is it?” ^sn39-1-15-3-1

“It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn39-1-15-3-2

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn39-1-15-3-3

This is the path, the practice, for realizing this extinguishment.” ^sn39-1-15-3-4

“Reverend, this is a fine path, a fine practice, for realizing this extinguishment. ^sn39-1-15-3-5

Just this much is enough for diligence.” ^sn39-1-15-3-6

(Tell in full  as the Linked Discourses with Jambukhādaka.) ^sn39-1-15-4-1

### Hard to Do (Linked Discourses 39.16) ^sn39-16-0

“Reverend Sāriputta, in this teaching and training, what is hard to do?” ^sn39-16-1-1

“Going forth, reverend, is hard to do in this teaching and training.” ^sn39-16-1-2

“But what’s hard to do for someone who has gone forth?” ^sn39-16-1-3

“When you’ve gone forth it’s hard to be satisfied.” ^sn39-16-1-4

“But what’s hard to do for someone who is satisfied?” ^sn39-16-1-5

“When you’re satisfied, it’s hard to practice in line with the teaching.” ^sn39-16-1-6

“But if a mendicant practices  in line with the teaching, will it take them long to become a perfected one?” ^sn39-16-1-7

“Not long, reverend.” ^sn39-16-1-8

The Linked Discourses with Sāmaṇḍaka are complete. ^sn39-16-1-11

## Saṁyutta 40 ^sn40-0

### A Question About the First Absorption (Linked Discourses 40.1) ^sn40-1-0

At one time Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn40-1-1-1

There Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the mendicants: ^sn40-1-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn40-1-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn40-1-1-4

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this: ^sn40-1-1-5

“Just now, reverends, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind: ^sn40-1-2-1

‘They speak of this thing called the “first absorption”. ^sn40-1-2-2

What is the first absorption?’ ^sn40-1-2-3

It occurred to me: ^sn40-1-2-4

‘It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn40-1-2-5

This is called the first absorption.’ ^sn40-1-2-6

And so … I was entering and remaining in the first absorption. ^sn40-1-2-7

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by sensual pleasures beset me. ^sn40-1-2-8

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-1-3-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-1-3-2

Don’t neglect the first absorption, brahmin! Settle your mind in the first absorption; unify your mind and immerse it in the first absorption.’ ^sn40-1-3-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the first absorption. ^sn40-1-3-4

So if anyone should be rightly called ^sn40-1-3-5

a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-1-3-6

### A Question About the Second Absorption (Linked Discourses 40.2) ^sn40-2-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘second absorption’. ^sn40-2-1-1

What is the second absorption? ^sn40-2-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-2-1-3

‘As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn40-2-1-4

This is called the second absorption.’ ^sn40-2-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the second absorption. ^sn40-2-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by placing the mind beset me. ^sn40-2-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-2-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-2-2-2

Don’t neglect the second absorption, brahmin! Settle your mind in the second absorption; unify your mind and immerse it in the second absorption.’ ^sn40-2-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the second absorption. ^sn40-2-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called ^sn40-2-2-5

a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-2-2-6

### A Question About the Third Absorption (Linked Discourses 40.3) ^sn40-3-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘third absorption’. ^sn40-3-1-1

What is the third absorption? ^sn40-3-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-3-1-3

‘With the fading away of rapture, a mendicant enters and remains in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.” ^sn40-3-1-4

This is called the third absorption.’ ^sn40-3-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the third absorption. ^sn40-3-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by rapture beset me. ^sn40-3-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-3-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-3-2-2

Don’t neglect the third absorption, brahmin! Settle your mind in the third absorption; unify your mind and immerse it in the third absorption.’ ^sn40-3-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the third absorption. ^sn40-3-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-3-2-5

### A Question About the Fourth Absorption (Linked Discourses 40.4) ^sn40-4-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘fourth absorption’. ^sn40-4-1-1

What is the fourth absorption? ^sn40-4-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-4-1-3

‘It’s when, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, a mendicant enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^sn40-4-1-4

This is called the fourth absorption.’ ^sn40-4-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the fourth absorption. ^sn40-4-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by pleasure beset me. ^sn40-4-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-4-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-4-2-2

Don’t neglect the fourth absorption, brahmin! Settle your mind in the fourth absorption; unify your mind and immerse it in the fourth absorption.’ ^sn40-4-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the fourth absorption. ^sn40-4-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-4-2-5

### A Question About the Dimension of Infinite Space (Linked Discourses 40.5) ^sn40-5-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘dimension of infinite space’. ^sn40-5-1-1

What is the dimension of infinite space? ^sn40-5-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-5-1-3

‘It’s when a mendicant—going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity—aware that “space is infinite”, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^sn40-5-1-4

This is called the dimension of infinite space.’ ^sn40-5-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the dimension of infinite space. ^sn40-5-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by forms beset me. ^sn40-5-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-5-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-5-2-2

Don’t neglect the dimension of infinite space, brahmin! Settle your mind in the dimension of infinite space; unify your mind and immerse it in the dimension of infinite space.’ ^sn40-5-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite space. ^sn40-5-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-5-2-5

### A Question About the Dimension of Infinite Consciousness (Linked Discourses 40.6) ^sn40-6-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘dimension of infinite consciousness’. ^sn40-6-1-1

What is the dimension of infinite consciousness? ^sn40-6-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-6-1-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn40-6-1-4

This is called the dimension of infinite consciousness.’ ^sn40-6-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn40-6-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite space beset me. ^sn40-6-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-6-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-6-2-2

Don’t neglect the dimension of infinite consciousness, brahmin! Settle your mind in the dimension of infinite consciousness; unify your mind and immerse it in the dimension of infinite consciousness.’ ^sn40-6-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn40-6-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-6-2-5

### A Question About the Dimension of Nothingness (Linked Discourses 40.7) ^sn40-7-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘dimension of nothingness’. ^sn40-7-1-1

What is the dimension of nothingness? ^sn40-7-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-7-1-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn40-7-1-4

This is called the dimension of nothingness.’ ^sn40-7-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn40-7-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite consciousness beset me. ^sn40-7-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-7-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-7-2-2

Don’t neglect the dimension of nothingness, brahmin! Settle your mind in the dimension of nothingness; unify your mind and immerse it in the dimension of nothingness.’ ^sn40-7-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn40-7-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-7-2-5

### A Question About the Dimension of Neither Perception Nor Non-Perception (Linked Discourses 40.8) ^sn40-8-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘dimension of neither perception nor non-perception’. ^sn40-8-1-1

What is the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception? ^sn40-8-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-8-1-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^sn40-8-1-4

This is called the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’ ^sn40-8-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^sn40-8-1-6

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of nothingness beset me. ^sn40-8-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-8-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-8-2-2

Don’t neglect the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, brahmin! Settle your mind in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception; unify your mind and immerse it in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’ ^sn40-8-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^sn40-8-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-8-2-5

### A Question About the Signless (Linked Discourses 40.9) ^sn40-9-0

“They speak of this thing called the ‘signless immersion of the heart’. ^sn40-9-1-1

What is the signless immersion of the heart? ^sn40-9-1-2

It occurred to me: ^sn40-9-1-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart. ^sn40-9-1-4

This is called the signless immersion of the heart.’ ^sn40-9-1-5

And so … I was entering and remaining in the signless immersion of the heart. ^sn40-9-1-6

While I was in that meditation, my consciousness followed after signs. ^sn40-9-1-7

Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said, ^sn40-9-2-1

‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! ^sn40-9-2-2

Don’t neglect the signless immersion of the heart, brahmin! Settle your mind in the signless immersion of the heart; unify your mind and immerse it in the signless immersion of the heart.’ ^sn40-9-2-3

And so, after some time … I entered and remained in the signless immersion of the heart. ^sn40-9-2-4

So if anyone should be rightly called ^sn40-9-2-5

a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.” ^sn40-9-2-6

### With Sakka (Linked Discourses 40.10) ^sn40-10-0

And then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared among the gods of the thirty-three. ^sn40-10-1-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with five hundred deities came up to Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and stood to one side. Mahāmoggallāna said to him: ^sn40-10-1-2

“Lord of gods, it’s good to go for refuge to the Buddha. ^sn40-10-2-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-2-2

It’s good to go for refuge to the teaching. ^sn40-10-2-3

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-2-4

It’s good to go for refuge to the Saṅgha. ^sn40-10-2-5

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn40-10-2-6

“My good Moggallāna, it’s good to go for refuge to the Buddha … ^sn40-10-3-1

the teaching … ^sn40-10-3-3

the Saṅgha. ^sn40-10-3-5

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn40-10-3-6

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with six hundred deities … ^sn40-10-4-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with seven hundred deities … ^sn40-10-4-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eight hundred deities … ^sn40-10-4-3

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eighty thousand deities … ^sn40-10-4-4

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with five hundred deities came up to Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and stood to one side. Mahāmoggallāna said to him: ^sn40-10-7-1

“Lord of gods, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn40-10-8-1

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn40-10-8-2

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-8-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^sn40-10-9-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn40-10-9-2

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-9-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^sn40-10-10-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn40-10-10-2

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-10-3

It’s good to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn40-10-11-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn40-10-11-2

“My good Moggallāna, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn40-10-12-1

the teaching … ^sn40-10-13-1

the Saṅgha … ^sn40-10-14-1

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … ^sn40-10-15-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn40-10-15-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with six hundred deities … ^sn40-10-16-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with seven hundred deities … ^sn40-10-16-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eight hundred deities … ^sn40-10-16-3

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eighty thousand deities … ^sn40-10-16-4

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with five hundred deities came up to Mahāmoggallāna, ^sn40-10-25-1

bowed, and stood to one side. Mahāmoggallāna said to him: ^sn40-10-25-2

“Lord of gods, it’s good to go for refuge to the Buddha. ^sn40-10-26-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-26-2

They surpass other gods in ten respects: ^sn40-10-26-3

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, sovereignty, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^sn40-10-26-4

It’s good to go for refuge to the teaching … ^sn40-10-27-1

It’s good to go for refuge to the Saṅgha. ^sn40-10-28-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-28-2

They surpass other gods in ten respects: ^sn40-10-28-3

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, sovereignty, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.” ^sn40-10-28-4

“My good Moggallāna, it’s good to go for refuge to the Buddha …” ^sn40-10-29-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with six hundred deities … ^sn40-10-32-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with seven hundred deities … ^sn40-10-32-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eight hundred deities … ^sn40-10-32-3

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eighty thousand deities … ^sn40-10-32-4

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with five hundred deities came up to Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and stood to one side. Mahāmoggallāna said to him: ^sn40-10-37-1

“Lord of gods, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn40-10-38-1

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha … teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn40-10-38-2

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn40-10-38-3

They surpass other gods in ten respects: ^sn40-10-38-4

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, sovereignty, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^sn40-10-38-5

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching … ^sn40-10-39-1

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha … ^sn40-10-40-1

It’s good to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones …” ^sn40-10-41-1

“My good Moggallāna, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha …” ^sn40-10-42-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with six hundred deities … ^sn40-10-46-1

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with seven hundred deities … ^sn40-10-46-2

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eight hundred deities … ^sn40-10-46-3

Then Sakka, lord of gods, with eighty thousand deities … ^sn40-10-46-4

### With Candana, Etc. (Linked Discourses 40.11) ^sn40-11-0

Then the godling Candana … ^sn40-11-1-1

the godling Suyāma … ^sn40-11-2-1

the godling Santusita … ^sn40-11-3-1

the godling Sunimmita … ^sn40-11-4-1

the godling Vasavatti … ^sn40-11-5-1

(Tell in full  as in the Discourse With Sakka.) ^sn40-11-6-1

The Linked Discourses on Moggallāna are complete. ^sn40-11-7-7

## Saṁyutta 41 ^sn41-0

### The Fetter (Linked Discourses 41.1) ^sn41-1-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-1-1-1

Now at that time, after the meal, on return from almsround, several senior mendicants sat together in the pavilion and this discussion came up among them: ^sn41-1-1-2

“Reverends, the ‘fetter’ and the ‘things that tighten fetters’: do these things differ in both meaning and phrasing? Or do they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing?” ^sn41-1-1-3

Some senior mendicants answered like this: ^sn41-1-1-4

“Reverends, the ‘fetter’ and the ‘things that tighten fetters’: these things differ in both meaning and phrasing.” ^sn41-1-1-5

But some senior mendicants answered like this: ^sn41-1-1-6

“Reverends, the ‘fetter’ and the ‘things that tighten fetters’ mean the same thing; they differ only in the phrasing.” ^sn41-1-1-7

Now at that time the householder Citta had arrived at Migapathaka on some business. ^sn41-1-2-1

He heard about what those senior mendicants were discussing. ^sn41-1-2-2

So he went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to them, ^sn41-1-3-1

“Sirs, I heard that you were discussing whether the ‘fetter’ and the ‘things that tighten fetters’ differ in both meaning and phrasing, or whether they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing.” ^sn41-1-3-2

“That’s right, householder.” ^sn41-1-3-7

“Sirs, the ‘fetter’ and the ‘things that tighten fetters’: these things differ in both meaning and phrasing. ^sn41-1-4-1

Well then, sirs, I shall give you a simile. ^sn41-1-4-2

For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said. ^sn41-1-4-3

Suppose there was a black ox and a white ox yoked by a single harness or yoke. ^sn41-1-4-4

Would it be right to say that ^sn41-1-4-5

the black ox is the yoke of the white ox, or the white ox is the yoke of the black ox?” ^sn41-1-4-6

“No, householder. ^sn41-1-4-7

The black ox is not the yoke of the white ox, nor is the white ox the yoke of the black ox. ^sn41-1-4-8

The yoke there is the single harness or yoke that they’re yoked by.” ^sn41-1-4-9

“In the same way, the eye is not the fetter of sights, nor are sights the fetter of the eye. ^sn41-1-4-10

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them. ^sn41-1-4-11

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn41-1-4-12

mind is not the fetter of ideas, nor are ideas the fetter of the mind. ^sn41-1-4-17

The fetter there is the desire and greed that arises from the pair of them.” ^sn41-1-4-18

“You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate, ^sn41-1-4-19

to traverse the Buddha’s deep teachings with the eye of wisdom.” ^sn41-1-4-20

### Isidatta (1st) (Linked Discourses 41.2) ^sn41-2-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-2-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to them, ^sn41-2-1-2

“Sirs, would the senior monks please accept tomorrow’s meal from me?” ^sn41-2-1-3

They consented with silence. ^sn41-2-1-4

Knowing that the senior mendicants had consented, Citta rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled them, keeping them on his right, before leaving. ^sn41-2-1-5

Then when the night had passed, the senior mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, went to Citta’s home, and sat down on the seats spread out. ^sn41-2-1-6

Then Citta went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and asked the senior venerable, ^sn41-2-2-1

“Honorable Senior, they speak of ‘the diversity of elements’. ^sn41-2-2-2

In what way did the Buddha speak of the diversity of elements?” ^sn41-2-2-3

When he said this, the senior venerable kept silent. ^sn41-2-2-4

For a second time … ^sn41-2-2-5

And for a third time, Citta asked him, ^sn41-2-2-9

“Honorable Senior, they speak of ‘the diversity of elements’. ^sn41-2-2-10

In what way did the Buddha speak of the diversity of elements?” ^sn41-2-2-11

And a second time and a third time the senior venerable kept silent. ^sn41-2-2-12

Now at that time Venerable Isidatta was the most junior mendicant in that Saṅgha. ^sn41-2-3-1

He said to the senior venerable, ^sn41-2-3-2

“Honorable Senior, may I answer Citta’s question?” ^sn41-2-3-3

“Answer it, Reverend Isidatta.” ^sn41-2-3-4

“Householder, is this your question: ^sn41-2-3-5

‘They speak of “the diversity of elements”. ^sn41-2-3-6

In what way did the Buddha speak of the diversity of elements?’” ^sn41-2-3-7

“Yes, sir.” ^sn41-2-3-8

“This is the diversity of elements spoken of by the Buddha. ^sn41-2-3-9

The eye element, the sights element, the eye consciousness element … ^sn41-2-3-10

The mind element, the idea element, the mind consciousness element. ^sn41-2-3-11

This is how the Buddha spoke of the diversity of elements.” ^sn41-2-3-12

Then Citta, having approved and agreed with what Isidatta said, served and satisfied the senior mendicants with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^sn41-2-4-1

When the senior mendicants had eaten and washed their hands and bowls, they rose from their seats and left. ^sn41-2-4-2

Then the senior venerable said to Venerable Isidatta, ^sn41-2-4-3

“Isidatta, it’s good that you felt inspired to answer that question, because I didn’t. ^sn41-2-4-4

So when a similar question comes up, you should also answer it as you feel inspired.” ^sn41-2-4-5

### With Isidatta (2nd) (Linked Discourses 41.3) ^sn41-3-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-3-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to them, ^sn41-3-1-2

“Sirs, would the senior monks please accept tomorrow’s meal from me?” ^sn41-3-1-3

They consented with silence. ^sn41-3-1-4

Then, knowing that the senior mendicants had consented, Citta rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled them, keeping them on his right, before leaving. ^sn41-3-1-5

Then when the night had passed, the senior mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, went to Citta’s home, and sat down on the seats spread out. ^sn41-3-1-6

So he went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the senior venerable: ^sn41-3-2-1

“Honorable Senior, there are many different views that arise in the world. For example: ^sn41-3-2-2

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists. ^sn41-3-2-3

And also the sixty-two misconceptions spoken of in “The Divine Net”. ^sn41-3-2-4

When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?” ^sn41-3-2-5

When he said this, the senior venerable kept silent. ^sn41-3-3-1

For a second time … ^sn41-3-3-2

And for a third time, Citta said to him: ^sn41-3-3-3

“Honorable Senior, there are many different views that arise in the world. … ^sn41-3-3-4

When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?” ^sn41-3-3-7

And a second time and a third time the senior venerable kept silent. ^sn41-3-3-8

Now at that time Venerable Isidatta was the most junior mendicant in that Saṅgha. ^sn41-3-4-1

He said to the senior venerable, ^sn41-3-4-2

“Honorable Senior, may I answer Citta’s question?” ^sn41-3-4-3

“Answer it, Reverend Isidatta.” ^sn41-3-4-4

“Householder, is this your question: ^sn41-3-4-5

‘There are many different views that arise in the world … ^sn41-3-4-6

When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?’” ^sn41-3-4-8

“Yes, sir.” ^sn41-3-4-9

“Householder, there are many different views that arise in the world. For example: ^sn41-3-4-10

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists. ^sn41-3-4-11

And also the sixty-two misconceptions spoken of in “The Divine Net”. ^sn41-3-4-12

These views come to be when substantialist view exists. When substantialist view does not exist they do not come to be.” ^sn41-3-4-13

“But sir, how does substantialist view come about?” ^sn41-3-5-1

“It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn41-3-5-2

They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn41-3-5-3

They regard feeling … ^sn41-3-5-4

perception … ^sn41-3-5-5

choices … ^sn41-3-5-6

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn41-3-5-7

That’s how substantialist view comes about.” ^sn41-3-5-8

“But sir, how does substantialist view not come about?” ^sn41-3-6-1

“It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons. ^sn41-3-6-2

They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^sn41-3-6-3

They don’t regard feeling … ^sn41-3-6-4

perception … ^sn41-3-6-5

choices … ^sn41-3-6-6

consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^sn41-3-6-7

That’s how substantialist view does not come about.” ^sn41-3-6-8

“Sir, where has Master Isidatta come from?” ^sn41-3-7-1

“I come from Avanti, householder.” ^sn41-3-7-2

“Sir, there’s a friend of mine named Isidatta who I’ve never met. He’s gone forth from a good family in Avanti. ^sn41-3-7-3

Have you met him?” ^sn41-3-7-4

“Yes, householder.” ^sn41-3-7-5

“Sir, where is that venerable now staying?” ^sn41-3-7-6

When he said this, Isidatta kept silent. ^sn41-3-7-7

“Sir, are you that Isidatta?” ^sn41-3-7-8

“Yes, householder.” ^sn41-3-7-9

“Sir, I hope Master Isidatta is happy here in Macchikāsaṇḍa, ^sn41-3-7-10

for the Wild Mango Grove is lovely. ^sn41-3-7-11

I’ll make sure that Master Isidatta is provided with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.” ^sn41-3-7-12

“That’s nice of you to say, householder.” ^sn41-3-7-13

Then Citta, having approved and agreed with what Isidatta said, served and satisfied the senior mendicants with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^sn41-3-8-1

When the senior mendicants had eaten and washed their hands and bowls, they rose from their seats and left. ^sn41-3-8-2

Then the senior venerable said to Venerable Isidatta, ^sn41-3-8-3

“Isidatta, it’s good that you felt inspired to answer that question, because I didn’t. ^sn41-3-8-4

So when a similar question comes up, you should also answer it as you feel inspired.” ^sn41-3-8-6

But Isidatta set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, left Macchikāsaṇḍa, ^sn41-3-8-7

never to return. ^sn41-3-8-8

### Mahaka’s Demonstration (Linked Discourses 41.4) ^sn41-4-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-4-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to them, ^sn41-4-1-2

“Sirs, would the senior monks please accept tomorrow’s meal from me in my milkshed?” ^sn41-4-1-3

They consented with silence. ^sn41-4-1-4

Then, knowing that the senior mendicants had consented, Citta rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled them, keeping them on his right, before leaving. ^sn41-4-1-5

Then when the night had passed, the senior mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, went to Citta’s milkshed, and sat down on the seats spread out. ^sn41-4-1-6

Then Citta served and satisfied the senior mendicants with his own hands with delicious milk-rice made with ghee. ^sn41-4-2-1

When the senior mendicants had eaten and washed their hands and bowls, they rose from their seats and left. ^sn41-4-2-2

Citta instructed that the leftovers of the food be distributed, then followed behind the senior mendicants. ^sn41-4-2-3

Now at that time it was boiling hot. ^sn41-4-2-4

And those senior mendicants walked along as if their bodies were melting, as happens after a meal. ^sn41-4-2-5

Now at that time Venerable Mahaka was the most junior mendicant in that Saṅgha. ^sn41-4-3-1

Then Venerable Mahaka said to the senior venerable, ^sn41-4-3-2

“Wouldn’t it be nice, Honorable Senior, if a cool wind blew, a cloud canopy formed, and a gentle rain drizzled down?” ^sn41-4-3-3

“It would indeed be nice, Reverend Mahaka.” ^sn41-4-4-1

Then Mahaka used his psychic power to will that a cool wind would blow, a cloud canopy would form, and a gentle rain would drizzle down. ^sn41-4-4-2

Then Citta thought, ^sn41-4-4-3

“The most junior mendicant in this Saṅgha has such psychic power!” ^sn41-4-4-4

When they reached the monastery, Mahaka said to the senior venerable, ^sn41-4-4-5

“Honorable Senior, is that sufficient?” ^sn41-4-4-6

“That’s sufficient, Reverend Mahaka, ^sn41-4-4-7

you’ve done enough ^sn41-4-4-8

and offered enough.” ^sn41-4-4-9

Then the senior mendicants entered their dwellings, ^sn41-4-4-10

and Mahaka entered his own dwelling. ^sn41-4-4-11

Then Citta went up to Mahaka, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn41-4-5-1

“Sir, please show me a superhuman demonstration of psychic power.” ^sn41-4-5-2

“Well, then, householder, place your upper robe on the porch and spread a handful of grass on it.” ^sn41-4-5-3

“Yes, sir,” replied Citta, and did as he was asked. ^sn41-4-5-4

Mahaka entered his dwelling and latched the door. Then he used his psychic power to will that a flame shoot out through the keyhole and the chink in the door-panel, and it burned up the grass but not the upper robe. ^sn41-4-5-5

Then Citta shook out his upper robe and stood to one side, shocked and awestruck. ^sn41-4-5-6

Mahaka left his dwelling and said to Citta, ^sn41-4-5-7

“Is that sufficient, householder?” ^sn41-4-5-8

“That’s sufficient, sir, ^sn41-4-6-1

you’ve done enough ^sn41-4-6-2

and offered enough. ^sn41-4-6-3

I hope Master Mahaka is happy here in Macchikāsaṇḍa, ^sn41-4-6-4

for the Wild Mango Grove is lovely. ^sn41-4-6-5

I’ll make sure that Master Mahaka is provided with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.” ^sn41-4-6-6

“That’s nice of you to say, householder.” ^sn41-4-6-7

But Mahaka set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, left Macchikāsaṇḍa, ^sn41-4-6-8

never to return. ^sn41-4-6-9

### With Kāmabhū (1st) (Linked Discourses 41.5) ^sn41-5-0

At one time Venerable Kāmabhū was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-5-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to Venerable Kāmabhū, bowed, and sat down to one side. Kāmabhū said to him, ^sn41-5-1-2

“Householder, there is this saying: ^sn41-5-2-1

‘With faultless parts and white canopy, ^sn41-5-3-1

the one-spoked chariot rolls on. ^sn41-5-3-2

See it come, untroubled, ^sn41-5-3-3

with stream cut, unbound.’ ^sn41-5-3-4

How should we see the detailed meaning of this brief statement?” ^sn41-5-4-1

“Sir, was this spoken by the Buddha?” ^sn41-5-4-2

“Yes, householder.” ^sn41-5-4-3

“Well then, sir, please wait a while as I consider the meaning of this.” ^sn41-5-4-4

Then after staying silent for a while, Citta said to Kāmabhū: ^sn41-5-4-5

“Sir, ‘faultless parts’ is a term for ethics. ^sn41-5-5-1

‘White canopy’ is a term for freedom. ^sn41-5-5-2

‘One spoke’ is a term for mindfulness. ^sn41-5-5-3

‘Rolls on’ is a term for going forward and coming back. ^sn41-5-5-4

‘Chariot’ is a term for this body made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^sn41-5-5-5

Greed, hate, and delusion are troubles. ^sn41-5-5-6

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-5-5-7

That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is called ‘untroubled’. ^sn41-5-5-8

‘The one who comes’ is a term for the perfected one. ^sn41-5-5-9

‘Stream’ is a term for craving. ^sn41-5-5-10

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given this up, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, and obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-5-5-11

That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is said to have ‘cut the stream’. ^sn41-5-5-12

Greed, hate, and delusion are bonds. ^sn41-5-5-13

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-5-5-14

That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is called ‘unbound’. ^sn41-5-5-15

So, sir, that’s how I understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief: ^sn41-5-5-16

‘With faultless parts and white canopy, ^sn41-5-6-1

the one-spoked chariot rolls on. ^sn41-5-6-2

See it come, untroubled, ^sn41-5-6-3

with stream cut, unbound.’” ^sn41-5-6-4

“You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate, ^sn41-5-7-2

to traverse the Buddha’s deep teachings with the eye of wisdom.” ^sn41-5-7-3

### With Kāmabhū (2nd) (Linked Discourses 41.6) ^sn41-6-0

At one time Venerable Kāmabhū was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-6-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to Venerable Kāmabhū, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn41-6-1-2

“Sir, how many processes are there?” ^sn41-6-1-3

“Householder, there are three processes. ^sn41-6-1-4

The physical process, the verbal process, and the mental process.” ^sn41-6-1-5

Saying “Good, sir,” Citta approved and agreed with what Kāmabhū said. Then he asked another question: ^sn41-6-1-6

“But sir, what is the physical process? What’s the verbal process? What’s the mental process?” ^sn41-6-1-7

“Breathing is a physical process. Placing the mind and keeping it connected are a verbal process. Perception and feeling are a mental process.” ^sn41-6-1-8

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-2-1

“But sir, why is breathing a physical process? Why are placing the mind and keeping it connected a verbal process? Why are perception and feeling a mental process?” ^sn41-6-2-2

“Breathing is physical. It’s tied up with the body, that’s why breathing is a physical process. ^sn41-6-2-3

First you place the mind and keep it connected, then you break into speech. That’s why placing the mind and keeping it connected are a verbal process. ^sn41-6-2-4

Perception and feeling are mental. They’re tied up with the mind, that’s why perception and feeling are a mental process.” ^sn41-6-2-5

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-3-1

“But sir, how does someone attain the cessation of perception and feeling?” ^sn41-6-3-2

“A mendicant who is entering such an attainment does not think: ^sn41-6-3-3

‘I will enter the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I am entering the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I have entered the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^sn41-6-3-4

Rather, their mind has been previously developed so as to lead to such a state.” ^sn41-6-3-5

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-4-1

“But sir, which cease first for a mendicant who is entering the cessation of perception and feeling: the physical, verbal, or mental process?” ^sn41-6-4-2

“The verbal process ceases first, then physical, then mental.” ^sn41-6-4-3

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-5-1

“What’s the difference between someone who has passed away and a mendicant who has attained the cessation of perception and feeling?” ^sn41-6-5-2

“When someone dies, their physical, verbal, and mental processes have ceased and stilled; their vitality is spent; their warmth is dissipated; and their faculties have disintegrated. ^sn41-6-5-3

When a mendicant has attained the cessation of perception and feeling, their physical, verbal, and mental processes have ceased and stilled. But their vitality is not spent; their warmth is not dissipated; and their faculties are very clear. ^sn41-6-5-4

That’s the difference between someone who has passed away and a mendicant who has attained the cessation of perception and feeling.” ^sn41-6-5-5

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-6-1

“But sir, how does someone emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling?” ^sn41-6-6-2

“A mendicant who is emerging from such an attainment does not think: ^sn41-6-6-3

‘I will emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I am emerging from the cessation of perception and feeling’ or ‘I have emerged from the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^sn41-6-6-4

Rather, their mind has been previously developed so as to lead to such a state.” ^sn41-6-6-5

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-7-1

“But sir, which arise first for a mendicant who is emerging from the cessation of perception and feeling: the physical, verbal, or mental process?” ^sn41-6-7-2

“The mental process arises first, then physical, then verbal.” ^sn41-6-7-3

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-8-1

“But sir, when a mendicant has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of perception and feeling, how many kinds of contact do they experience?” ^sn41-6-8-2

“They experience three kinds of contact: ^sn41-6-8-3

emptiness, signless, and undirected contacts.” ^sn41-6-8-4

Saying “Good, sir,” he asked another question: ^sn41-6-9-1

“But sir, when a mendicant has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of perception and feeling, what does their mind slant, slope, and incline to?” ^sn41-6-9-2

“Their mind slants, slopes, and inclines to seclusion.” ^sn41-6-9-3

Saying “Good, sir,” Citta approved and agreed with what Kāmabhū said. Then he asked another question: ^sn41-6-10-1

“But sir, how many things are helpful for attaining the cessation of perception and feeling?” ^sn41-6-10-2

“Well, householder, you’ve finally asked what you should have asked first! ^sn41-6-10-3

Nevertheless, I will answer you. ^sn41-6-10-4

Two things are helpful for attaining the cessation of perception and feeling: ^sn41-6-10-5

serenity and discernment.” ^sn41-6-10-6

### With Godatta (Linked Discourses 41.7) ^sn41-7-0

At one time Venerable Godatta was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove. ^sn41-7-1-1

Then Citta the householder went up to Venerable Godatta, bowed, and sat down to one side. Godatta said to him: ^sn41-7-1-2

“Householder, the limitless release of the heart, and the release of the heart through nothingness, and the release of the heart through emptiness, and the signless release of the heart: do these things differ in both meaning and phrasing? Or do they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing?” ^sn41-7-1-3

“Sir, there is a way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing. ^sn41-7-1-4

But there’s also a way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing. ^sn41-7-1-5

And what’s the way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing? ^sn41-7-2-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn41-7-2-2

They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion … ^sn41-7-2-3

They meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing … ^sn41-7-2-4

They meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn41-7-2-5

This is called the limitless release of the heart. ^sn41-7-2-6

And what is the release of the heart through nothingness? ^sn41-7-3-1

It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn41-7-3-2

This is called the release of the heart through nothingness. ^sn41-7-3-3

And what is the release of the heart through emptiness? ^sn41-7-4-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^sn41-7-4-2

‘This is empty of a self or what belongs to a self.’ ^sn41-7-4-3

This is called the release of the heart through emptiness. ^sn41-7-4-4

And what is the signless heart’s release? ^sn41-7-5-1

It’s when a mendicant, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart. ^sn41-7-5-2

This is called the signless release of the heart. ^sn41-7-5-3

This is the way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing. ^sn41-7-5-4

And what’s the way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing? ^sn41-7-6-1

Greed, hate, and delusion are makers of limits. ^sn41-7-6-2

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-7-6-3

The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of limitless release of the heart. ^sn41-7-6-4

That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn41-7-6-5

Greed is something, hate is something, and delusion is something. ^sn41-7-6-6

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-7-6-7

The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of release of the heart through nothingness. ^sn41-7-6-8

That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn41-7-6-9

Greed, hate, and delusion are makers of signs. ^sn41-7-6-10

A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future. ^sn41-7-6-11

The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of signless release of the heart. ^sn41-7-6-12

That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn41-7-6-13

This is the way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing.” ^sn41-7-6-14

“You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate, ^sn41-7-6-15

to traverse the Buddha’s deep teachings with the eye of wisdom.” ^sn41-7-6-16

### The Jain Ascetic of the Ñātika Clan (Linked Discourses 41.8) ^sn41-8-0

Now at that time the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan had arrived at Macchikāsaṇḍa together with a large assembly of Jain ascetics. ^sn41-8-1-1

Citta the householder heard that they had arrived. ^sn41-8-1-2

Together with several lay followers, he went up to the Jain Ñātika and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn41-8-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The Jain Ñātika said to him, ^sn41-8-1-4

“Householder, do you have faith in the ascetic Gotama’s claim that ^sn41-8-1-5

there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected?” ^sn41-8-1-6

“Sir, in this case I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha’s claim that ^sn41-8-2-1

there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected.” ^sn41-8-2-2

When he said this, the Jain Ñātika looked up at his assembly and said, ^sn41-8-2-3

“See, good fellows, how straightforward this householder Citta is! He’s not devious or deceitful at all. To imagine that you can stop placing the mind and keeping it connected would be like imagining that you can catch the wind in a net, or dam the Ganges river with your own hand.” ^sn41-8-2-4

“What do you think, sir? ^sn41-8-3-1

Which is better—knowledge or faith?” ^sn41-8-3-2

“Knowledge is definitely better than faith, householder.” ^sn41-8-3-3

“Well sir, whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn41-8-3-4

And whenever I want, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … I enter and remain in the second absorption. ^sn41-8-3-5

And whenever I want, with the fading away of rapture … I enter and remain in the third absorption. ^sn41-8-3-6

And whenever I want, giving up pleasure and pain … I enter and remain in the fourth absorption. ^sn41-8-3-7

And so, sir, since I know and see like this, why should I rely on faith in another ascetic or brahmin who claims that ^sn41-8-3-8

there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected?” ^sn41-8-3-9

When he said this, the Jain Ñātika looked askance at his own assembly and said, ^sn41-8-4-1

“See, good fellows, how crooked this householder Citta is! He’s so devious and deceitful!” ^sn41-8-4-2

“Sir, just now I understood you to say: ^sn41-8-5-1

‘See, good fellows, how straightforward this householder Citta is! He’s not devious or deceitful at all.’ ^sn41-8-5-2

But then I understood you to say: ^sn41-8-5-3

‘See, good fellows, how crooked this householder Citta is! He’s so devious and deceitful!’ ^sn41-8-5-4

If your first statement is true, the second is wrong. ^sn41-8-5-5

If your first statement is wrong, the second is true. ^sn41-8-5-6

And also, sir, these ten legitimate questions are relevant. ^sn41-8-5-7

When you understand what they mean, then, together with your assembly of Jain ascetics, you can rebut me. ^sn41-8-5-8

‘One thing: question, summary, and answer. ^sn41-8-5-9

Two … ^sn41-8-5-10

Three … ^sn41-8-5-11

Four … ^sn41-8-5-12

Five … ^sn41-8-5-13

Six … ^sn41-8-5-14

Seven … ^sn41-8-5-15

Eight … ^sn41-8-5-16

Nine … ^sn41-8-5-17

Ten things: question, summary, and answer.’” ^sn41-8-5-18

Then Citta rose from his seat and left without asking the Jain Ñātika these ten legitimate questions. ^sn41-8-5-19

### With Kassapa, the Naked Ascetic (Linked Discourses 41.9) ^sn41-9-0

Now at that time the naked ascetic Kassapa, an old friend of Citta in the lay life, had arrived at Macchikāsaṇḍa. ^sn41-9-1-1

Citta the householder heard that he had arrived. ^sn41-9-1-2

So he went up to him, and they exchanged greetings. ^sn41-9-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the naked ascetic Kassapa, ^sn41-9-1-4

“Honorable Kassapa, how long has it been since you went forth?” ^sn41-9-1-5

“It’s been thirty years, householder.” ^sn41-9-1-6

“But sir, in these thirty years have you achieved any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones, a comfortable meditation?” ^sn41-9-1-7

“I have no such achievement, householder, only nudity, baldness, and pokes in the buttocks.” ^sn41-9-1-8

Citta said to him, ^sn41-9-1-9

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing, ^sn41-9-1-10

how well explained the teaching is. For in thirty years you have achieved no superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones, no comfortable meditation, only nudity, baldness, and pokes in the buttocks.” ^sn41-9-1-11

“But householder, how long have you been a lay follower?” ^sn41-9-2-1

“It’s been thirty years, sir.” ^sn41-9-2-2

“But householder, in these thirty years have you achieved any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones, a comfortable meditation?” ^sn41-9-2-3

“How, sir, could I not? ^sn41-9-2-4

For whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn41-9-2-5

And whenever I want, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … I enter and remain in the second absorption. ^sn41-9-2-6

And whenever I want, with the fading away of rapture … I enter and remain in the third absorption. ^sn41-9-2-7

And whenever I want, giving up pleasure and pain … I enter and remain in the fourth absorption. ^sn41-9-2-8

If I pass away before the Buddha, it would be no wonder if the Buddha declares of me: ^sn41-9-2-9

‘The householder Citta is bound by no fetter that might return him to this world.’” ^sn41-9-2-10

When this was said, Kassapa said to Citta, ^sn41-9-2-11

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing, ^sn41-9-2-12

how well explained the teaching is. For a white-clothed layperson can achieve such a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones, a comfortable meditation. ^sn41-9-2-13

Householder, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in this teaching and training?” ^sn41-9-2-14

Then Citta the householder took the naked ascetic Kassapa to see the senior mendicants, and said to them: ^sn41-9-3-1

“Sirs, this is the naked ascetic Kassapa, who in lay life was an old friend of mine. ^sn41-9-3-2

May the senior monks give him the going forth, the ordination. ^sn41-9-3-3

I’ll make sure that he’s provided with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.” ^sn41-9-3-4

And the naked ascetic Kassapa received the going forth, the ordination in this teaching and training. ^sn41-9-3-5

Not long after his ordination, Venerable Kassapa, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn41-9-3-6

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^sn41-9-3-7

And Venerable Kassapa became one of the perfected. ^sn41-9-3-8

### Seeing the Sick (Linked Discourses 41.10) ^sn41-10-0

Now at that time the householder Citta was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn41-10-1-1

Then several deities of the parks, forests, trees, and those who haunt the herbs, grass, and big trees came together and said to Citta, ^sn41-10-1-2

“Householder, make a wish to become a wheel-turning monarch in the future!” ^sn41-10-1-3

When they said this, Citta said to them, ^sn41-10-2-1

“That too is impermanent! That too will pass! That too will be left behind!” ^sn41-10-2-2

When he said this, his friends and colleagues, relatives and kin said, ^sn41-10-2-3

“Be mindful, master! Don’t babble.” ^sn41-10-2-4

“What have I said that makes you say that?” ^sn41-10-2-5

“It’s because you said: ^sn41-10-2-7

‘That too is impermanent! That too will pass! That too will be left behind!’” ^sn41-10-2-8

“Oh, well, that’s because the deities of the parks, forests, trees, and those who haunt the herbs, grass, and big trees said to me: ^sn41-10-2-9

‘Householder, make a wish to become a wheel-turning monarch in the future!’ ^sn41-10-2-10

So I said to them: ^sn41-10-2-11

‘That too is impermanent! That too will pass! That too will be left behind!’” ^sn41-10-2-12

“But what reason do they see for saying that to you?” ^sn41-10-2-13

“They think: ^sn41-10-2-15

‘This householder Citta is ethical, of good character. ^sn41-10-2-16

If he makes a wish to ^sn41-10-2-17

become a wheel-turning monarch in the future, his heart’s wish will succeed because of the purity of his ethics. And then as a proper, principled king he will provide proper spirit-offerings.’ ^sn41-10-2-18

That’s the reason they see for saying to me: ^sn41-10-2-19

‘Householder, make a wish to become a wheel-turning monarch in the future!’ ^sn41-10-2-20

So I said to them: ^sn41-10-2-21

‘That too is impermanent! That too will pass! That too will be left behind!’” ^sn41-10-2-22

“Then, master, advise us!” ^sn41-10-3-1

“So you should train like this: ^sn41-10-3-2

We will have experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn41-10-3-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn41-10-3-4

We will have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^sn41-10-3-5

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn41-10-3-6

We will have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^sn41-10-3-7

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn41-10-3-8

And we will share without reservation all the gifts available to give in our family with those who are ethical and of good character.” ^sn41-10-3-9

Then, after Citta had encouraged his friends and colleagues, relatives and kin in the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, and generosity, he passed away. ^sn41-10-3-10

The Linked Discourses on Citta the Householder are complete. ^sn41-10-4-5

## Saṁyutta 42 ^sn42-0

### Fury (Linked Discourses 42.1) ^sn42-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn42-1-1-1

Then Fury the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-1-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some people are regarded as furious, ^sn42-1-1-3

while others are regarded as gentle?” ^sn42-1-1-4

“Take someone who hasn’t given up greed. ^sn42-1-1-5

So they get annoyed by others, and they show it. ^sn42-1-1-6

They’re regarded as furious. ^sn42-1-1-7

They haven’t given up hate. ^sn42-1-1-8

So they get annoyed by others, and they show it. ^sn42-1-1-9

They’re regarded as furious. ^sn42-1-1-10

They haven’t given up delusion. ^sn42-1-1-11

So they get annoyed by others, and they show it. ^sn42-1-1-12

They’re regarded as furious. ^sn42-1-1-13

This is the cause, this is the reason why some people are regarded as furious. ^sn42-1-1-14

But take someone who has given up greed. ^sn42-1-2-1

So they don’t get annoyed by others, and don’t show it. ^sn42-1-2-2

They’re regarded as gentle. ^sn42-1-2-3

They’ve given up hate. ^sn42-1-2-4

So they don’t get annoyed by others, and don’t show it. ^sn42-1-2-5

They’re regarded as gentle. ^sn42-1-2-6

They’ve given up delusion. ^sn42-1-2-7

So they don’t get annoyed by others, and don’t show it. ^sn42-1-2-8

They’re regarded as gentle. ^sn42-1-2-9

This is the cause, this is the reason why some people are regarded as gentle.” ^sn42-1-2-10

When he said this, Fury the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-1-3-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^sn42-1-3-2

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn42-1-3-3

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn42-1-3-4

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-1-3-5

### With Tālapuṭa (Linked Discourses 42.2) ^sn42-2-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn42-2-1-1

Then Tālapuṭa the dancing chief came up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^sn42-2-1-2

“Sir, I have heard that the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors said: ^sn42-2-1-3

‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’ ^sn42-2-1-4

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn42-2-1-5

“Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.” ^sn42-2-1-6

For a second time … ^sn42-2-1-7

And for a third time Tālapuṭa said to the Buddha: ^sn42-2-1-12

“Sir, I have heard that the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors said: ^sn42-2-1-13

‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’ ^sn42-2-1-14

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn42-2-1-15

“Clearly, chief, I’m not getting through to you when I say: ^sn42-2-2-1

‘Enough, chief, let it be. ^sn42-2-2-2

Don’t ask me that.’ ^sn42-2-2-3

Nevertheless, I will answer you. ^sn42-2-2-4

When sentient beings are still not free of greed, and are still bound by greed, ^sn42-2-2-5

a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more arousing things. ^sn42-2-2-6

When sentient beings are still not free of hate, and are still bound by hate, ^sn42-2-2-7

a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more hateful things. ^sn42-2-2-8

When sentient beings are still not free of delusion, and are still bound by delusion, ^sn42-2-2-9

a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more delusory things. ^sn42-2-2-10

And so, being heedless and negligent themselves, they’ve encouraged others to be heedless and negligent. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the hell called ‘Laughter’. ^sn42-2-2-11

But if you have such a view: ^sn42-2-2-12

‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’ This is your wrong view. ^sn42-2-2-13

An individual with wrong view is reborn in one of two places, I say: ^sn42-2-2-14

hell or the animal realm.” ^sn42-2-2-15

When he said this, Tālapuṭa cried and burst out in tears. ^sn42-2-3-1

“This is what I didn’t get through to you when I said: ^sn42-2-3-2

‘Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.’” ^sn42-2-3-3

“Sir, I’m not crying because of what the Buddha said. ^sn42-2-3-4

But sir, for a long time I’ve been cheated, tricked, and deceived by the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors, who said: ^sn42-2-3-5

‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’ ^sn42-2-3-6

Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^sn42-2-3-7

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn42-2-3-8

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn42-2-3-9

Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?” ^sn42-2-3-10

And Tālapuṭa the dancing chief received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^sn42-2-3-11

Not long after his ordination, Venerable Tālapuṭa ^sn42-2-3-12

became one of the perfected. ^sn42-2-3-13

### A Warrior (Linked Discourses 42.3) ^sn42-3-0

Then Dustin the warrior chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-3-1-1

“Sir, I have heard that the warriors of the past who were tutors of tutors said: ^sn42-3-1-2

‘Suppose a warrior, while striving and struggling in battle, is killed and finished off by his foes. When his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in the company of the gods of the fallen.’ ^sn42-3-1-3

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn42-3-1-4

“Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.” ^sn42-3-1-5

For a second time … ^sn42-3-1-6

And for a third time Dustin the warrior chief said to the Buddha: ^sn42-3-1-7

“Sir, I have heard that the warriors of the past who were tutors of tutors said: ^sn42-3-1-8

‘Suppose a warrior, while striving and struggling in battle, is killed and finished off by his foes. When his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in the company of the gods of the fallen.’ ^sn42-3-1-9

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn42-3-1-10

“Clearly, chief, I’m not getting through to you when I say: ^sn42-3-2-1

‘Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.’ ^sn42-3-2-2

Nevertheless, I will answer you. ^sn42-3-2-3

When a warrior strives and struggles in battle, their mind is already low, degraded, and misdirected as they think: ^sn42-3-2-4

‘May these sentient beings be killed, slaughtered, slain, destroyed, or annihilated!’ ^sn42-3-2-5

His foes kill him and finish him off, and ^sn42-3-2-6

when his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in the hell called ‘The Fallen’. ^sn42-3-2-7

But if you have such a view: ^sn42-3-2-8

‘Suppose a warrior, while striving and struggling in battle, is killed and finished off by his foes. When his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in the company of the gods of the fallen.’ This is your wrong view. ^sn42-3-2-9

An individual with wrong view is reborn in one of two places, I say: ^sn42-3-2-10

hell or the animal realm.” ^sn42-3-2-11

When he said this, Dustin the warrior chief cried and burst out in tears. ^sn42-3-3-1

“This is what I didn’t get through to you when I said: ^sn42-3-3-2

‘Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.’” ^sn42-3-3-3

“Sir, I’m not crying because of what the Buddha said. ^sn42-3-3-4

But sir, for a long time I’ve been cheated, tricked, and deceived by the warriors of the past who were tutors of tutors, who said: ^sn42-3-3-5

‘Suppose a warrior, while striving and struggling in battle, is killed and finished off by his foes. When his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in the company of the gods of the fallen.’ ^sn42-3-3-6

Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-3-3-7

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-3-3-8

### An Elephant Marshal (Linked Discourses 42.4) ^sn42-4-0

Then an elephant marshal chief went up to the Buddha … ^sn42-4-1-1

“From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-4-1-2

### A Cavalryman (Linked Discourses 42.5) ^sn42-5-0

Then a cavalry chief went up to the Buddha … ^sn42-5-1-1

“From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-5-4-8

### With Asibandhaka’s Son (Linked Discourses 42.6) ^sn42-6-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Nāḷandā in Pāvārika’s mango grove. ^sn42-6-1-1

Then Asibandhaka’s son the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-6-1-2

“Sir, there are western brahmins garlanded with aquatic flowers who carry pitchers, immerse themselves in water, and serve the sacred flame. ^sn42-6-2-1

When someone has passed away, oh! they lift them up, send them well, and convey them to heaven. ^sn42-6-2-2

But what about the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha: is he able to ensure that the whole world will be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm when their body breaks up, after death?” ^sn42-6-2-3

“Well then, chief, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^sn42-6-3-1

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-4-1

Take an individual who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re covetous, malicious, and have wrong view. ^sn42-6-4-2

A large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty, circumambulating them with cupped palms and saying: ^sn42-6-4-3

‘When this person’s body breaks up, after death, may they be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm!’ ^sn42-6-4-4

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-4-5

Would that person be reborn in heaven because of their supplications?” ^sn42-6-4-6

“No, sir.” ^sn42-6-4-7

“Chief, suppose a person were to throw a broad rock into a deep lake. ^sn42-6-5-1

And a large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty, circumambulating it with cupped palms, and saying: ^sn42-6-5-2

‘Rise, good rock! Float, good rock! Float to shore, good rock!’ ^sn42-6-5-3

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-5-4

Would that broad rock rise up or float because of their supplications?” ^sn42-6-5-5

“No, sir.” ^sn42-6-5-6

“In the same way, take a person who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re covetous, malicious, and have wrong view. ^sn42-6-5-7

Even though a large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty … ^sn42-6-5-8

when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn42-6-5-9

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-6-1

Take an individual who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re contented, kind-hearted, and have right view. ^sn42-6-6-2

And a large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty, circumambulating them with cupped palms and saying: ^sn42-6-6-3

‘When this person’s body breaks up, after death, may they be reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell!’ ^sn42-6-6-4

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-6-5

Would that person be reborn in hell because of their supplications?” ^sn42-6-6-6

“No, sir.” ^sn42-6-6-7

“Chief, suppose a person were to sink a pot of ghee or oil into a deep lake and break it open. Its shards and chips would sink down, while the ghee or oil in it would rise up. ^sn42-6-7-1

And a large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty, circumambulating it with cupped palms and saying: ^sn42-6-7-2

‘Sink, good ghee or oil! Descend, good ghee or oil! Go down, good ghee or oil!’ ^sn42-6-7-3

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-6-7-4

Would that ghee or oil sink and descend because of their supplications?” ^sn42-6-7-5

“No, sir.” ^sn42-6-7-6

“In the same way, take an individual who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re contented, kind-hearted, and have right view. ^sn42-6-8-1

Even though a large crowd were to come together to offer up supplication and entreaty … ^sn42-6-8-2

when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn42-6-8-3

When he said this, Asibandhaka’s son the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-6-8-4

“Excellent, sir! … ^sn42-6-8-5

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-6-8-6

### The Simile of the Field (Linked Discourses 42.7) ^sn42-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Nāḷandā in Pāvārika’s mango grove. ^sn42-7-1-1

Then Asibandhaka’s son the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-7-1-2

“Sir, doesn’t the Buddha live full of sympathy for all living beings?” ^sn42-7-1-3

“Yes, chief.” ^sn42-7-1-4

“So why then, sir, do you teach some people thoroughly and others less thoroughly?” ^sn42-7-1-5

“Well then, chief, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^sn42-7-1-6

What do you think? Suppose a farmer has three fields: one is good, one is average, and one is poor—bad ground of sand and salt. ^sn42-7-1-7

What do you think? When that farmer wants to plant seeds, where would he plant them first: the good field, the average one, or the poor one?” ^sn42-7-1-8

“Sir, he’d plant them first in the good field, then the average, then he may or may not plant seed in the poor field. ^sn42-7-1-9

Why is that? ^sn42-7-1-10

Because at least it can be fodder for the cattle.” ^sn42-7-1-11

“To me, the monks and nuns are like the good field. ^sn42-7-2-1

I teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-2-2

Why is that? ^sn42-7-2-3

Because they live with me as their island, asylum, shelter, and refuge. ^sn42-7-2-4

To me, the laymen and laywomen are like the average field. ^sn42-7-2-5

I also teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-2-6

Why is that? ^sn42-7-2-7

Because they live with me as their island, asylum, shelter, and refuge. ^sn42-7-2-8

To me, the ascetics, brahmins, and wanderers of other religions are like the poor field, the bad ground of sand and salt. ^sn42-7-2-9

I also teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-2-10

Why is that? ^sn42-7-2-11

Hopefully they might understand even a single sentence, which would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^sn42-7-2-12

Suppose a person had three water jars: one that’s uncracked and nonporous; one that’s uncracked but porous; and one that’s cracked and porous. ^sn42-7-3-1

What do you think? When that person wants to store water, where would they store it first: in the jar that’s uncracked and nonporous, the one that’s uncracked but porous, or the one that’s cracked and porous?” ^sn42-7-3-2

“Sir, they’d store water first in the jar that’s uncracked and nonporous, then the one that’s uncracked but porous, then they may or may not store water in the one that’s cracked and porous. ^sn42-7-3-3

Why is that? ^sn42-7-3-4

Because at least it can be used for washing the dishes.” ^sn42-7-3-5

“To me, the monks and nuns are like the water jar that’s uncracked and nonporous. ^sn42-7-4-1

I teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-4-2

Why is that? ^sn42-7-4-3

Because they live with me as their island, asylum, shelter, and refuge. ^sn42-7-4-4

To me, the laymen and laywomen are like the water jar that’s uncracked but porous. ^sn42-7-4-5

I also teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-4-6

Why is that? ^sn42-7-4-7

Because they live with me as their island, asylum, shelter, and refuge. ^sn42-7-4-8

To me, the ascetics, brahmins, and wanderers of other religions are like the water jar that’s cracked and porous. ^sn42-7-4-9

I also teach them the Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And I reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn42-7-4-10

Why is that? ^sn42-7-4-11

Hopefully they might understand even a single sentence, which would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.” ^sn42-7-4-12

When he said this, Asibandhaka’s son the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-7-5-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-7-5-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-7-5-3

### A Horn Blower (Linked Discourses 42.8) ^sn42-8-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Nāḷandā in Pāvārika’s mango grove. ^sn42-8-1-1

Then Asibandhaka’s son the chief, who was a disciple of the Jains, went up to the Buddha, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^sn42-8-1-2

“Chief, how does the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan teach his disciples?” ^sn42-8-1-3

“Sir, this is how the Jain Ñātika teaches his disciples: ^sn42-8-1-4

‘Everyone who kills a living creature, steals, commits sexual misconduct, or lies goes to a place of loss, to hell. ^sn42-8-1-5

You’re led on by what you frequently live by.’ ^sn42-8-1-6

This is how the Jain Ñātika teaches his disciples.” ^sn42-8-1-7

“‘You’re led on by what you usually live by’: if this were true, then, according to what the Jain Ñātika says, no-one would go to a place of loss, to hell. ^sn42-8-1-8

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-8-2-1

Take an individual who kills living creatures. If we compare periods of time during the day and night, which is more frequent: the occasions when they’re killing or when they’re not killing?” ^sn42-8-2-2

“The occasions when they’re killing are less frequent, while the occasions when they’re not killing are more frequent.” ^sn42-8-2-3

“‘You’re led on by what you usually live by’: if this were true, then, according to what the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan says, no-one would go to a place of loss, to hell. ^sn42-8-2-4

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-8-3-1
