Take an individual who steals … ^sn42-8-3-2

Take an individual who commits sexual misconduct … ^sn42-8-4-1

Take an individual who lies. If we compare periods of time during the day and night, which is more frequent: the occasions when they’re lying or when they’re not lying?” ^sn42-8-5-1

“The occasions when they’re lying are less frequent, while the occasions when they’re not lying are more frequent.” ^sn42-8-5-2

“‘You’re led on by what you usually live by’: if this were true, then, according to what the Jain Ñātika says, no-one would go to a place of loss, to hell. ^sn42-8-5-3

Take some teacher who has this doctrine and view: ^sn42-8-6-1

‘Everyone who kills a living creature, steals, commits sexual misconduct, or lies goes to a place of loss, to hell.’ ^sn42-8-6-2

And there’s a disciple who is devoted to that teacher. ^sn42-8-6-3

They think: ^sn42-8-6-4

‘My teacher has this doctrine and view: ^sn42-8-6-5

“Everyone who kills a living creature, steals, commits sexual misconduct, or lies goes to a place of loss, to hell.” ^sn42-8-6-6

But I’ve killed living creatures … ^sn42-8-6-7

stolen … ^sn42-8-6-11

committed sexual misconduct … ^sn42-8-6-15

or lied.’ ^sn42-8-6-20

They get the view: ‘I too am going to a place of loss, to hell.’ ^sn42-8-6-21

Unless they give up that speech and that thought, and let go of that view, they will be placed in hell as if delivered there. ^sn42-8-6-22

But consider when a Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^sn42-8-7-1

In many ways he criticizes and denounces killing living creatures, saying: ‘Stop killing living creatures!’ ^sn42-8-7-2

He criticizes and denounces stealing … ^sn42-8-7-3

sexual misconduct … ^sn42-8-7-4

lying, saying: ‘Stop lying!’ ^sn42-8-7-5

And there’s a disciple who is devoted to that teacher. ^sn42-8-7-6

Then they reflect: ^sn42-8-7-7

‘In many ways the Buddha criticizes and denounces killing living creatures, saying: “Stop killing living creatures!” ^sn42-8-7-8

But I have killed living creatures to a certain extent. ^sn42-8-7-9

That’s not right, it’s not good, ^sn42-8-7-10

and I feel remorseful because of it. ^sn42-8-7-11

But I can’t undo what I have done.’ ^sn42-8-7-12

Reflecting like this, they give up killing living creatures, ^sn42-8-7-13

and in future they don’t kill living creatures. ^sn42-8-7-14

That’s how to give up this bad deed ^sn42-8-7-15

and get past it. ^sn42-8-7-16

‘In many ways the Buddha criticizes and denounces stealing …’ ^sn42-8-8-1

‘In many ways the Buddha criticizes and denounces sexual misconduct …’ ^sn42-8-9-1

‘In many ways the Buddha criticizes and denounces lying, saying: “Stop lying!” ^sn42-8-10-1

But I have lied to a certain extent. ^sn42-8-10-2

That’s not right, it’s not good, and I feel remorseful because of it. But I can’t undo what I have done.’ ^sn42-8-10-3

Reflecting like this, they give up lying, and in future they refrain from lying. ^sn42-8-10-4

That’s how to give up this bad deed and get past it. ^sn42-8-10-5

They give up killing living creatures. ^sn42-8-11-1

They give up stealing. ^sn42-8-11-2

They give up sexual misconduct. ^sn42-8-11-3

They give up lying. ^sn42-8-11-4

They give up backbiting. ^sn42-8-11-5

They give up harsh speech. ^sn42-8-11-6

They give up talking nonsense. ^sn42-8-11-7

They give up covetousness. ^sn42-8-11-8

They give up ill will and malevolence. ^sn42-8-11-9

They give up wrong view and have right view. ^sn42-8-11-10

That noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn42-8-12-1

Suppose there was a powerful horn blower. They’d easily make themselves heard in the four quarters. ^sn42-8-12-2

In the same way, when the heart’s release by love has been developed and cultivated like this, any limited deeds they’ve done don’t remain or persist there. ^sn42-8-12-3

Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion … ^sn42-8-13-1

They meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing … ^sn42-8-13-2

They meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn42-8-13-3

Suppose there was a powerful horn blower. They’d easily make themselves heard in the four quarters. ^sn42-8-13-4

In the same way, when the heart’s release by equanimity has been developed and cultivated like this, any limited deeds they’ve done don’t remain or persist there.” ^sn42-8-13-5

When he said this, Asibandhaka’s son the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-8-14-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-8-14-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-8-14-3

### Families (Linked Discourses 42.9) ^sn42-9-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at Nāḷandā. ^sn42-9-1-1

There he stayed near Nāḷandā in Pāvārika’s mango grove. ^sn42-9-1-2

Now at that time Nāḷandā was undergoing famine and scarcity, with crops blighted and turned to straw. ^sn42-9-2-1

At that time the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan was residing at Nāḷandā together with a large assembly of Jain ascetics. ^sn42-9-2-2

Then Asibandhaka’s son the chief, who was a disciple of the Jains, went up to the Jain Ñātika, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Jain Ñātika said to him: ^sn42-9-2-3

“Come, chief, refute the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine. ^sn42-9-2-4

Then you will get a good reputation: ^sn42-9-2-5

‘Asibandhaka’s son the chief refuted the doctrine of the ascetic Gotama, so mighty and powerful!’” ^sn42-9-2-6

“But sir, how am I to do this?” ^sn42-9-3-1

“Here, brahmin, go to the ascetic Gotama and say to him: ^sn42-9-3-2

‘Sir, don’t you in many ways praise kindness, protection, and sympathy for families?’ ^sn42-9-3-3

When he’s asked this, if he answers: ^sn42-9-3-4

‘Indeed I do, chief,’ say this to him: ^sn42-9-3-5

‘So what then are you doing, wandering together with this large Saṅgha of mendicants during a time of famine and scarcity, with crops blighted and turned to straw? ^sn42-9-3-6

The Buddha is practicing to annihilate, collapse, and ruin families!’ ^sn42-9-3-7

When you put this dilemma to him, the Buddha won’t be able to either spit it out or swallow it down.” ^sn42-9-3-8

“Yes, sir,” replied Asibandhaka’s son. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Jain Ñātika, keeping him on his right. Then he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-9-3-9

“Sir, don’t you in many ways praise kindness, protection, and sympathy for families?” ^sn42-9-4-1

“Indeed I do, chief.” ^sn42-9-4-2

“So what then are you doing, wandering together with this large Saṅgha of mendicants during a time of famine and scarcity, with crops blighted and turned to straw? ^sn42-9-4-3

The Buddha is practicing to annihilate, collapse, and ruin families!” ^sn42-9-4-4

“Well, chief, I recollect ninety-one eons back but I’m not aware of any family that’s been ruined merely by offering some cooked almsfood. ^sn42-9-4-5

Rather, rich, affluent, and wealthy families—with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain—all acquired their wealth because of giving, truth, and restraint. ^sn42-9-4-6

Chief, there are eight causes and reasons for the ruin of families. ^sn42-9-4-7

Their ruin stems from rulers, bandits, fire, or flood. Or they lose their savings. Or their business fails due to not applying themselves to work. Or a wastrel is born into the family who squanders and fritters away their wealth. And impermanence is the eighth. ^sn42-9-4-8

These are the eight causes and reasons for the ruin of families. ^sn42-9-4-9

Given that these eight reasons are found, suppose someone says this: ^sn42-9-4-10

‘The Buddha is practicing to annihilate, collapse, and ruin families!’ ^sn42-9-4-11

Unless they give up that speech and that thought, and let go of that view, they will be placed in hell as if delivered there.” ^sn42-9-4-12

When he said this, Asibandhaka’s son the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-9-5-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-9-5-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-9-5-3

### With Maṇicūḷaka (Linked Discourses 42.10) ^sn42-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn42-10-1-1

Now at that time while the king’s retinue was sitting together in the royal compound this discussion came up among them, ^sn42-10-1-2

“Gold and currency are proper for ascetics who follow the Sakyan. They accept and receive gold and currency.” ^sn42-10-1-3

Now at that time the chief Maṇicūḷaka was sitting in that assembly. ^sn42-10-2-1

He said to that retinue, ^sn42-10-2-2

“Good masters, don’t say that. ^sn42-10-2-3

Gold and currency are not proper for ascetics who follow the Sakyan. They neither accept nor receive gold and currency. They have set aside gems and gold, and quit using gold and currency.” ^sn42-10-2-4

He was able to persuade that assembly. ^sn42-10-2-5

Then Maṇicūḷaka went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. He then said, ^sn42-10-2-6

“Answering this way, I trust that I repeat what the Buddha has said, and don’t misrepresent him with an untruth. I trust my explanation is in line with the teaching, and that there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal or criticism.” ^sn42-10-2-13

“Indeed, in answering this way you repeat what I’ve said, and don’t misrepresent me with an untruth. Your explanation is in line with the teaching, and there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal or criticism. ^sn42-10-3-1

Gold and currency are not proper for ascetics who follow the Sakyan. They neither accept nor receive gold and currency. They have set aside gems and gold, and quit using gold and currency. ^sn42-10-3-2

If gold and currency were proper for them, then the five kinds of sensual stimulation would also be proper. ^sn42-10-3-3

And if the five kinds of sensual stimulation are proper for them, you should categorically regard them as not having the qualities of an ascetic or a follower of the Sakyan. ^sn42-10-3-4

Rather, chief, I say this: ^sn42-10-3-5

Straw may be looked for by one needing straw; wood may be looked for by one needing wood; a cart may be looked for by one needing a cart; a workman may be looked for by one needing a workman. ^sn42-10-3-6

But in any case, there is no way they can accept or look for gold and currency, I say .” ^sn42-10-3-7

### With Bhadraka (Linked Discourses 42.11) ^sn42-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Mallas, near the Mallian town named Uruvelakappa. ^sn42-11-1-1

Then Bhadraka the village chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-11-1-2

“Please, sir, let the Blessed One teach me the origin and disappearance of suffering.” ^sn42-11-1-3

“Chief, if I were to teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering in connection with the past, saying ^sn42-11-1-4

‘this is how it was in the past,’ you might have doubts or uncertainties about that. ^sn42-11-1-5

If I were to teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering in connection with the future, saying ^sn42-11-1-6

‘this is how it will be in the future,’ you might have doubts or uncertainties about that. ^sn42-11-1-7

Rather, chief, I will teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering as I am sitting right here and you are sitting right there. ^sn42-11-1-8

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn42-11-1-9

“Yes, sir,” Bhadraka replied. ^sn42-11-1-10

The Buddha said this: ^sn42-11-1-11

“What do you think, chief? ^sn42-11-2-1

Are there any people here in Uruvelakappa who, if they were executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, it would cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn42-11-2-2

“There are, sir.” ^sn42-11-2-3

“But are there any people here in Uruvelakappa who, if they were executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, it would not cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn42-11-2-4

“There are, sir.” ^sn42-11-2-5

“What’s the cause, chief, what’s the reason why, if this was to happen to some people it could cause you sorrow, while if it happens to others it does not?” ^sn42-11-2-6

“The people regarding whom this would give rise to sorrow are those I have desire and passion. ^sn42-11-2-7

The people regarding whom this would not give rise to sorrow are those I don’t have desire and passion.” ^sn42-11-2-8

“With this present phenomenon that you have seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, you can apply the same method to the past and future: ^sn42-11-2-9

‘All the suffering that arose in the past was rooted and sourced in desire. ^sn42-11-2-10

For desire is the root of suffering. ^sn42-11-2-11

All the suffering that will arise in the future will be rooted and sourced in desire. ^sn42-11-2-12

For desire is the root of suffering.’” ^sn42-11-2-13

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^sn42-11-2-14

How well said this was by the Buddha! ^sn42-11-2-15

‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire. ^sn42-11-2-16

For desire is the root of suffering.’ ^sn42-11-2-17

I have a boy called Ciravāsi, who resides in a house away from here. ^sn42-11-2-18

I rise early and dispatch someone, saying: ^sn42-11-2-19

‘Go, lad, and check on my boy Ciravāsi.’ ^sn42-11-2-20

Until they get back I worry: ^sn42-11-2-21

‘I hope nothing’s wrong with Ciravāsi!’” ^sn42-11-2-22

“What do you think, chief? ^sn42-11-3-1

If Ciravāsi was executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, would it cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn42-11-3-2

“How could it not, sir?” ^sn42-11-3-3

“This too is a way to understand: ^sn42-11-3-4

‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire. ^sn42-11-3-5

For desire is the root of suffering.’ ^sn42-11-3-6

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-11-4-1

Before you’d seen or heard of Ciravāsi’s mother, did you have any desire or passion or fondness for her?” ^sn42-11-4-2

“No, sir.” ^sn42-11-4-3

“Then was it because you saw or heard of her that you had ^sn42-11-4-4

desire or passion or fondness for her?” ^sn42-11-4-5

“Yes, sir.” ^sn42-11-4-6

“What do you think, chief? ^sn42-11-5-1

If Ciravāsi’s mother was executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, would it cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?” ^sn42-11-5-2

“How could it not, sir?” ^sn42-11-5-3

“This too is a way to understand: ^sn42-11-5-4

‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire. ^sn42-11-5-5

For desire is the root of suffering.’” ^sn42-11-5-6

### With Rāsiya (Linked Discourses 42.12) ^sn42-12-0

Then Rāsiya the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-12-1-1

“Sir, I have heard this: ‘The ascetic Gotama criticizes all forms of mortification. He categorically condemns and denounces those fervent mortifiers who live rough.’ ^sn42-12-1-2

Do those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn42-12-1-3

“Chief, those who say this do not repeat what I have said. They misrepresent me with what is false, hollow, and untrue. ^sn42-12-1-4

These two extremes should not be cultivated by one gone forth. ^sn42-12-2-1

Indulgence in sensual pleasures, which is low, crude, ordinary, ignoble, and pointless. And indulgence in self-mortification, which is painful, ignoble, and pointless. ^sn42-12-2-2

Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One understood the middle way of practice, which gives vision and knowledge, and leads to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn42-12-2-3

And what is that middle way of practice? ^sn42-12-2-4

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn42-12-2-5

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn42-12-2-6

This, chief, is the middle way of practice that was understood by the Realized One, which gives vision and knowledge, and leads to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn42-12-2-7

There are these three pleasure seekers in the world. ^sn42-12-3-1

What three? ^sn42-12-3-2

Take a pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-3-3

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. ^sn42-12-3-4

They make themselves happy and pleased, but don’t share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-3-5

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. ^sn42-12-3-6

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-3-7

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^sn42-12-4-1

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-4-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^sn42-12-4-3

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-4-4

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^sn42-12-4-5

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-4-6

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^sn42-12-5-1

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-5-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^sn42-12-5-3

They make themselves happy and pleased, but don’t share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-5-4

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^sn42-12-5-5

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-5-6

They enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. ^sn42-12-5-7

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^sn42-12-5-8

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-5-9

And they enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. ^sn42-12-5-10

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-6-1

They may be criticized on three grounds. ^sn42-12-6-2

What three? ^sn42-12-6-3

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-6-4

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-6-5

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the third ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-6-6

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on these three grounds. ^sn42-12-6-7

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-7-1

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on two grounds, and praised on one. ^sn42-12-7-2

What are the two grounds for criticism? ^sn42-12-7-3

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-7-4

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-7-5

What is the one ground for praise? ^sn42-12-7-6

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the one ground for praise. ^sn42-12-7-7

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on these two grounds, and praised on this one. ^sn42-12-7-8

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. ^sn42-12-8-1

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on one ground, and praised on two. ^sn42-12-8-2

What is the one ground for criticism? ^sn42-12-8-3

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the one ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-8-4

What are the two grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-8-5

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-8-6

They share it and make merit. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-8-7

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on this one ground, and praised on these two. ^sn42-12-8-8

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-9-1

They may be praised on one ground, and criticized on three. ^sn42-12-9-2

What is the one ground for praise? ^sn42-12-9-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the one ground for praise. ^sn42-12-9-4

What are the three grounds for criticism? ^sn42-12-9-5

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-9-6

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-9-7

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the third ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-9-8

This pleasure seeker may be praised on this one ground, and criticized on these three. ^sn42-12-9-9

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-10-1

They may be praised on two grounds, and criticized on two. ^sn42-12-10-2

What are the two grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-10-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-10-4

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-10-5

What are the two grounds for criticism? ^sn42-12-10-6

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-10-7

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-10-8

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these two grounds, and criticized on these two. ^sn42-12-10-9

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. ^sn42-12-11-1

They may be praised on three grounds, and criticized on one. ^sn42-12-11-2

What are the three grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-11-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-11-4

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-11-5

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^sn42-12-11-6

What is the one ground for criticism? ^sn42-12-11-7

They seek wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the one ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-11-8

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these three grounds, and criticized on this one. ^sn42-12-11-9

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, but who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, or share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-12-1

They may be praised on one ground, and criticized on two. ^sn42-12-12-2

What is the one ground for praise? ^sn42-12-12-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the one ground for praise. ^sn42-12-12-4

What are the two grounds for criticism? ^sn42-12-12-5

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-12-6

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-12-7

This pleasure seeker may be praised on this one ground, and criticized on these two. ^sn42-12-12-8

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. ^sn42-12-13-1

This pleasure seeker may be praised on two grounds, and criticized on one. ^sn42-12-13-2

What are the two grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-13-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-13-4

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-13-5

What is the one ground for criticism? ^sn42-12-13-6

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the one ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-13-7

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these two grounds, and criticized on this one. ^sn42-12-13-8

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. But they enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. ^sn42-12-14-1

They may be praised on three grounds and criticized on one. ^sn42-12-14-2

What are the three grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-14-3

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-14-4

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-14-5

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^sn42-12-14-6

What is the one ground for criticism? ^sn42-12-14-7

They enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. This is the one ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-14-8

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these three grounds, and criticized on this one. ^sn42-12-14-9

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. ^sn42-12-15-1

And they enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. ^sn42-12-15-2

This pleasure seeker may be praised on four grounds. ^sn42-12-15-3

What are the four grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-15-4

They seek wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-15-5

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-15-6

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^sn42-12-15-7

They enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. This is the fourth ground for praise. ^sn42-12-15-8

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these four grounds. ^sn42-12-15-9

These three fervent mortifiers who live rough are found in the world. ^sn42-12-16-1

What three? ^sn42-12-16-2

Take a fervent mortifier who has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, thinking: ^sn42-12-16-3

‘Hopefully I will achieve a skillful quality! Hopefully I will realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones!’ ^sn42-12-16-4

They mortify and torment themselves. But they don’t achieve any skillful quality, or realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^sn42-12-16-5

Take another fervent mortifier who has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, thinking: ^sn42-12-17-1

‘Hopefully I will achieve a skillful quality! Hopefully I will realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones!’ ^sn42-12-17-2

They mortify and torment themselves. And they achieve a skillful quality, but don’t realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^sn42-12-17-3

Take another fervent mortifier who has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, thinking: ^sn42-12-18-1

‘Hopefully I will achieve a skillful quality! Hopefully I will realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones!’ ^sn42-12-18-2

They mortify and torment themselves. And they achieve a skillful quality, and they realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^sn42-12-18-3

In this case, the first fervent mortifier may be criticized on three grounds. ^sn42-12-19-1

What three? ^sn42-12-19-2

They mortify and torment themselves. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-19-3

They don’t achieve a skillful quality. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-19-4

They don’t realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. This is the third ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-19-5

This fervent mortifier may be criticized on these three grounds. ^sn42-12-19-6

In this case, the second fervent mortifier ^sn42-12-20-1

may be criticized on two grounds, and praised on one. ^sn42-12-20-2

What are the two grounds for criticism? ^sn42-12-20-3

They mortify and torment themselves. This is the first ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-20-4

They don’t realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. This is the second ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-20-5

What is the one ground for praise? ^sn42-12-20-6

They achieve a skillful quality. This is the one ground for praise. ^sn42-12-20-7

This fervent mortifier may be criticized on these two grounds, and praised on one. ^sn42-12-20-8

In this case, the third fervent mortifier may be criticized on one ground, and praised on two. ^sn42-12-21-1

What is the one ground for criticism? ^sn42-12-21-2

They mortify and torment themselves. This is the one ground for criticism. ^sn42-12-21-3

What are the two grounds for praise? ^sn42-12-21-4

They achieve a skillful quality. This is the first ground for praise. ^sn42-12-21-5

They realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. This is the second ground for praise. ^sn42-12-21-6

This fervent mortifier may be criticized on this one ground, and praised on two. ^sn42-12-21-7

There are these three kinds of wearing away that are apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know them for themselves. ^sn42-12-22-1

What three? ^sn42-12-22-2

A greedy person, because of greed, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. ^sn42-12-22-3

When they’ve given up greed they don’t have such intentions. ^sn42-12-22-4

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn42-12-22-5

A hateful person, because of hate, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. ^sn42-12-22-6

When they’ve given up hate they don’t have such intentions. ^sn42-12-22-7

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn42-12-22-8

A deluded person, because of delusion, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. ^sn42-12-22-9

When they’ve given up delusion they don’t have such intentions. ^sn42-12-22-10

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^sn42-12-22-11

These are the three kinds of wearing away that are apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know them for themselves.” ^sn42-12-22-12

When he said this, Rāsiya the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-12-23-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-12-23-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-12-23-3

### With Pāṭaliya (Linked Discourses 42.13) ^sn42-13-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Uttarakā. ^sn42-13-1-1

Then Pāṭaliya the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn42-13-1-2

“Sir, I have heard this: ^sn42-13-1-3

‘The ascetic Gotama knows deceit.’ ^sn42-13-1-4

Do those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism? ^sn42-13-2-1

For we don’t want to misrepresent the Blessed One.” ^sn42-13-2-2

“Chief, those who say this repeat what I have said, and don’t misrepresent me with an untruth. Their explanation is in line with the teaching, and there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.” ^sn42-13-2-3

“Sir, we didn’t believe that what those ascetics and brahmins said was really true. ^sn42-13-2-4

But it seems the ascetic Gotama is deceitful!” ^sn42-13-2-5

“Chief, does someone who says ‘I know deceit’ also say ‘I am deceitful’?” ^sn42-13-2-6

“That’s right, Blessed One! That’s right, Holy One!” ^sn42-13-2-7

“Well then, chief, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^sn42-13-2-8

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-3-1

Do you know the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses?” ^sn42-13-3-2

“I know them, sir.” ^sn42-13-3-3

“And what’s their job?” ^sn42-13-3-4

“To put a stop to bandits and to deliver messages for the Koliyans.” ^sn42-13-3-5

“What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-3-6

Are the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses moral or immoral?” ^sn42-13-3-7

“I know that they’re immoral, of bad character, sir. They are among those in the world who are immoral and of bad character.” ^sn42-13-3-8

“Would it be right to say that ^sn42-13-3-9

Pāṭaliya knows the Kolyian officers with drooping headdresses who are immoral, of bad character, so he too must be immoral and of bad character?” ^sn42-13-3-10

“No, sir. ^sn42-13-3-11

I’m quite different from the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses; we have quite different characters.” ^sn42-13-3-12

“So if you can know those officers of bad character while you are not of bad character, why can’t the Realized One know deceit, without being deceitful? ^sn42-13-3-13

I understand deceit and its result. And I understand how the deceitful practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn42-13-3-14

I understand killing living creatures and its result. And I understand how those who kill living creatures practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn42-13-4-1

I understand stealing … ^sn42-13-4-2

sexual misconduct … ^sn42-13-4-3

lying … ^sn42-13-4-4

backbiting … ^sn42-13-4-5

harsh speech … ^sn42-13-4-6

talking nonsense … ^sn42-13-4-7

covetousness … ^sn42-13-4-8

ill will … ^sn42-13-4-9

wrong view and its result. And I understand how those who have wrong view practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn42-13-4-10

There are some ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-5-1

‘Everyone who kills living creatures experiences pain and sadness in this very life. ^sn42-13-5-2

Everyone who steals … ^sn42-13-5-3

commits sexual misconduct … ^sn42-13-5-4

lies experiences pain and sadness in this very life.’ ^sn42-13-5-5

But you can see someone, garlanded and adorned, nicely bathed and anointed, hair and beard dressed, taking his pleasure with women as if he were a king. ^sn42-13-6-1

You might ask someone: ^sn42-13-6-2

‘My man, what did that man do?’ ^sn42-13-6-3

And they’d reply: ^sn42-13-6-4

‘My man, that man attacked the king’s enemy and killed them. ^sn42-13-6-5

The king was delighted and gave him this reward. ^sn42-13-6-6

That’s why he’s garlanded and adorned, nicely bathed and anointed, hair and beard dressed, taking his pleasure with women as if he were a king.’ ^sn42-13-6-7

And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back with a strong rope. His head is shaven and he’s marched from street to street and from square to square to the beating of a harsh drum. Then he’s taken out the south gate and there, to the south of the city, they chop off his head. ^sn42-13-7-1

You might ask someone: ^sn42-13-7-2

‘My man, what did that man do?’ ^sn42-13-7-3

And they’d reply: ^sn42-13-7-4

‘My man, that man is an enemy of the king, and he has murdered a man or a woman. ^sn42-13-7-5

That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’ ^sn42-13-7-6

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-8-1

Have you seen or heard of such a thing?” ^sn42-13-8-2

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^sn42-13-8-3

“Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^sn42-13-8-4

everyone who kills living creatures experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong?” ^sn42-13-8-5

“They’re wrong, sir.” ^sn42-13-8-6

“But those who speak hollow, false nonsense: are they moral or immoral?” ^sn42-13-8-7

“Immoral, sir.” ^sn42-13-8-8

“And are those who are immoral, of bad character practicing wrongly or rightly?” ^sn42-13-8-9

“They’re practicing wrongly, sir.” ^sn42-13-8-10

“And do those who are practicing wrongly have wrong view or right view?” ^sn42-13-8-11

“They have wrong view, sir.” ^sn42-13-8-12

“But is it appropriate to have confidence in those of wrong view?” ^sn42-13-8-13

“No, sir.” ^sn42-13-8-14

“You can see someone, garlanded and adorned … ^sn42-13-9-1

‘My man, that man attacked the king’s enemy and took their valuables. ^sn42-13-9-5

The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ … ^sn42-13-9-6

And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back … ^sn42-13-10-1

‘My man, that man took something from a village or wilderness, with the intention to commit theft. ^sn42-13-10-4

That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’ ^sn42-13-10-5

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-10-6

Have you seen or heard of such a thing?” ^sn42-13-10-7

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^sn42-13-10-8

“Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^sn42-13-10-9

everyone who steals experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong? … ^sn42-13-10-10

Is it appropriate to have confidence in them?” ^sn42-13-10-11

“No, sir.” ^sn42-13-10-12

“You can see someone, garlanded and adorned … ^sn42-13-11-1

‘My man, that man had sexual relations with the wives of an enemy king. ^sn42-13-11-5

The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ … ^sn42-13-11-6

And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back … ^sn42-13-12-1

‘My man, that man had sexual relations with the women and maidens of good families. ^sn42-13-12-5

That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’ ^sn42-13-12-6

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-12-7

Have you seen or heard of such a thing?” ^sn42-13-12-8

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^sn42-13-12-9

“Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^sn42-13-12-10

everyone who commits sexual misconduct experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong? … ^sn42-13-12-11

Is it appropriate to have confidence in them?” ^sn42-13-12-12

“No, sir.” ^sn42-13-12-13

“And you can see someone, garlanded and adorned … ^sn42-13-13-1

‘My man, that man amused the king with lies. ^sn42-13-13-5

The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ … ^sn42-13-13-6

And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back … ^sn42-13-14-1

‘My man, that man has ruined a householder or householder’s child by lying. ^sn42-13-14-5

That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’ ^sn42-13-14-6

What do you think, chief? ^sn42-13-14-7

Have you seen or heard of such a thing?” ^sn42-13-14-8

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^sn42-13-14-9

“Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^sn42-13-14-10

everyone who lies experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong?” ^sn42-13-14-11

“They’re wrong, sir.” ^sn42-13-14-12

“But those who speak hollow, false nonsense: are they moral or immoral?” ^sn42-13-14-13

“Immoral, sir.” ^sn42-13-14-14

“And are those who are immoral, of bad character practicing wrongly or rightly?” ^sn42-13-14-15

“They’re practicing wrongly, sir.” ^sn42-13-14-16

“And do those who are practicing wrongly have wrong view or right view?” ^sn42-13-14-17

“They have wrong view, sir.” ^sn42-13-14-18

“But is it appropriate to have confidence in those of wrong view?” ^sn42-13-14-19

“No, sir. ^sn42-13-14-20

It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^sn42-13-15-1

I have a guest house, ^sn42-13-15-2

where there are cots, seats, water jars, and oil lamps. ^sn42-13-15-3

Whenever an ascetic or brahmin comes to stay, I share what I have as best I can. ^sn42-13-15-4

Once it so happened, sir, that four teachers of different views, beliefs, and opinions came to stay at my guest house. ^sn42-13-15-5

One teacher had this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-16-1

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^sn42-13-16-2

One teacher had this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-17-1

‘There is meaning in giving, sacrifice, and offerings. There are fruits and results of good and bad deeds. There is an afterlife. There are such things as mother and father, and beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^sn42-13-17-2

One teacher had this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-18-1

‘The one who acts does nothing wrong when they punish, mutilate, torture, aggrieve, oppress, intimidate, or when they encourage others to do the same. Nothing bad is done when they kill, steal, break into houses, plunder wealth, steal from isolated buildings, commit highway robbery, commit adultery, and lie. ^sn42-13-18-2

If you were to reduce all the living creatures of this earth to one heap and mass of flesh with a razor-edged chakram, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil. ^sn42-13-18-3

If you were to go along the south bank of the Ganges killing, mutilating, and torturing, and encouraging others to do the same, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil. ^sn42-13-18-4

If you were to go along the north bank of the Ganges giving and sacrificing and encouraging others to do the same, no merit comes of that, and no outcome of merit. ^sn42-13-18-5

In giving, self-control, restraint, and truthfulness there is no merit or outcome of merit.’ ^sn42-13-18-6

One teacher had this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-19-1

‘The one who acts does a bad deed when they punish, mutilate, torture, aggrieve, oppress, intimidate, or when they encourage others to do the same. A bad deed is done when they kill, steal, break into houses, plunder wealth, steal from isolated buildings, commit highway robbery, commit adultery, and lie. ^sn42-13-19-2

If you were to reduce all the living creatures of this earth to one heap and mass of flesh with a razor-edged chakram, evil comes of that, and an outcome of evil. ^sn42-13-19-3

If you were to go along the south bank of the Ganges killing, mutilating, and torturing, and encouraging others to do the same, evil comes of that, and an outcome of evil. ^sn42-13-19-4

If you were to go along the north bank of the Ganges giving and sacrificing and encouraging others to do the same, merit comes of that, and an outcome of merit. ^sn42-13-19-5

In giving, self-control, restraint, and truthfulness there is merit and outcome of merit.’ ^sn42-13-19-6

I had doubt and uncertainty about that: ^sn42-13-20-1

‘I wonder who of these respected ascetics and brahmins speaks the truth, and who speaks falsehood?’” ^sn42-13-20-2

“Chief, no wonder you’re doubting and uncertain. ^sn42-13-21-1

Doubt has come up in you about an uncertain matter.” ^sn42-13-21-2

“I am quite confident that the Buddha is capable of teaching me so that I can give up this state of uncertainty.” ^sn42-13-21-3

“Chief, there is immersion based on understanding of principle. ^sn42-13-22-1

If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty. ^sn42-13-22-2

And what is immersion based on understanding of principle? ^sn42-13-22-3

It’s when a noble disciple has given up killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, backbiting, harsh speech, talking nonsense, covetousness, ill will, and wrong view. ^sn42-13-22-4

Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn42-13-23-1

They reflect thus: ^sn42-13-23-2

‘That teacher who had this doctrine and view: ^sn42-13-23-3

“There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.” ^sn42-13-23-4

If what this good teacher says is true, it’s a sure bet for me to not hurt any creature firm or frail. ^sn42-13-23-5

I hold a winning hand on both counts, since I’m restrained in body, speech, and mind, and when my body breaks up, after death, I’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ ^sn42-13-23-6

Joy springs up in them. ^sn42-13-23-7

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^sn42-13-23-8

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^sn42-13-23-9

When the body is tranquil, they feel bliss. ^sn42-13-23-10

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn42-13-23-11

This is that immersion based on understanding of principle. ^sn42-13-23-12

If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty. ^sn42-13-23-13

Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn42-13-24-1

And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.” ^sn42-13-24-4

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn42-13-24-11

This is that immersion based on understanding of principle. ^sn42-13-24-12

If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty. ^sn42-13-24-13

rejoicing … ^sn42-13-27-2

equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn42-13-28-1

They reflect thus: ^sn42-13-31-2

‘If what this good teacher says is true, it’s a sure bet for me to not hurt any creature firm or frail. ^sn42-13-31-9

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^sn42-13-31-13

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn42-13-31-15

This is that immersion based on understanding of principle. ^sn42-13-31-16

If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty.” ^sn42-13-31-17

When he said this, Pāṭaliya the chief said to the Buddha, ^sn42-13-32-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^sn42-13-32-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn42-13-32-3

The Linked Discourses on chiefs are complete. ^sn42-13-33-5

## Saṁyutta 43 ^sn43-0

### Mindfulness of the Body (Linked Discourses 43.1) ^sn43-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn43-1-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-1-1-2

Listen … ^sn43-1-1-3

And what is the unconditioned? ^sn43-1-1-4

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn43-1-1-5

This is called the unconditioned. ^sn43-1-1-6

And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-1-1-7

Mindfulness of the body. ^sn43-1-1-8

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-1-1-9

So, mendicants, I’ve taught you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-1-2-1

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^sn43-1-2-2

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^sn43-1-2-3

### Serenity and Discernment (Linked Discourses 43.2) ^sn43-2-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-2-1-1

Listen … ^sn43-2-1-2

And what is the unconditioned? ^sn43-2-1-3

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn43-2-1-4

This is called the unconditioned. ^sn43-2-1-5

And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-2-1-6

Serenity and discernment. ^sn43-2-1-7

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. …” ^sn43-2-1-8

### Placing the Mind and Keeping it Connected (Linked Discourses 43.3) ^sn43-3-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-3-1-1

Immersion with placing the mind and keeping it connected. Immersion without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. Immersion without placing the mind or keeping it connected. …” ^sn43-3-1-2

### Emptiness Immersion (Linked Discourses 43.4) ^sn43-4-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-4-1-1

Emptiness immersion; signless immersion; undirected immersion. …” ^sn43-4-1-2

### Mindfulness Meditation (Linked Discourses 43.5) ^sn43-5-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-5-1-1

The four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^sn43-5-1-2

### Right Efforts (Linked Discourses 43.6) ^sn43-6-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-6-1-1

The four right efforts. …” ^sn43-6-1-2

### Bases of Psychic Power (Linked Discourses 43.7) ^sn43-7-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-7-1-1

The four bases of psychic power. …” ^sn43-7-1-2

### Faculties (Linked Discourses 43.8) ^sn43-8-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-8-1-1

The five faculties. …” ^sn43-8-1-2

### Powers (Linked Discourses 43.9) ^sn43-9-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-9-1-1

The five powers. …” ^sn43-9-1-2

### Awakening Factors (Linked Discourses 43.10) ^sn43-10-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-10-1-1

The seven awakening factors. …” ^sn43-10-1-2

### The Path (Linked Discourses 43.11) ^sn43-11-0

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-11-1-1

The noble eightfold path. ^sn43-11-1-2

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-11-1-3

So, mendicants, I’ve taught you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-11-1-4

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^sn43-11-1-5

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^sn43-11-1-6

### The Unconditioned (Linked Discourses 43.12) ^sn43-12-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-1-1

Listen … ^sn43-12-1-2

And what is the unconditioned? ^sn43-12-1-3

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn43-12-1-4

This is called the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-1-5

And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-12-1-6

Serenity. ^sn43-12-1-7

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-1-8

So, mendicants, I’ve taught you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-1-9

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^sn43-12-1-10

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^sn43-12-1-11

“Mendicants, I will teach you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-2-1

Listen … ^sn43-12-2-2

And what is the unconditioned? ^sn43-12-2-3

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn43-12-2-4

This is called the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-2-5

And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-12-2-6

Discernment. ^sn43-12-2-7

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. …” ^sn43-12-2-8

“And what is the path that leads to the unconditioned? ^sn43-12-3-1

Immersion with placing the mind and keeping it connected. … ^sn43-12-3-2

Immersion without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. … ^sn43-12-3-5

Immersion without placing the mind or keeping it connected. … ^sn43-12-3-8

Emptiness immersion. … ^sn43-12-4-2

Signless immersion. … ^sn43-12-4-5

Undirected immersion. … ^sn43-12-4-8

A mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. … ^sn43-12-5-2

A mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn43-12-5-5

A mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the mind … ^sn43-12-5-8

A mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of principles … ^sn43-12-5-11

A mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. … ^sn43-12-6-2

A mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities are given up. … ^sn43-12-6-5

A mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that skillful qualities arise. … ^sn43-12-6-8

A mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are fulfilled by development. … ^sn43-12-6-11

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. … ^sn43-12-7-2

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy … ^sn43-12-7-5

immersion due to mental development … ^sn43-12-7-8

immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. … ^sn43-12-7-11

A mendicant develops the faculty of faith, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. … ^sn43-12-8-2

A mendicant develops the faculty of energy … ^sn43-12-8-5

mindfulness … ^sn43-12-8-8

immersion … ^sn43-12-8-11

wisdom, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. … ^sn43-12-8-14

A mendicant develops the power of faith … ^sn43-12-9-2

energy … ^sn43-12-9-5

mindfulness … ^sn43-12-9-8

immersion … ^sn43-12-9-11

wisdom, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. … ^sn43-12-9-14

A mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness … ^sn43-12-10-2

investigation of principles … ^sn43-12-10-5

energy … ^sn43-12-10-6

rapture … ^sn43-12-10-7

tranquility … ^sn43-12-10-8

immersion … ^sn43-12-10-9

equanimity, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. … ^sn43-12-10-10

A mendicant develops right view … ^sn43-12-11-2

right purpose … ^sn43-12-11-5

right speech … ^sn43-12-11-6

right action … ^sn43-12-11-7

right livelihood … ^sn43-12-11-8

right effort … ^sn43-12-11-9

right mindfulness … ^sn43-12-11-10

right immersion, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. ^sn43-12-11-15

This is called the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-11-16

So, mendicants, I’ve taught you the unconditioned and the path that leads to the unconditioned. ^sn43-12-11-17

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^sn43-12-11-18

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^sn43-12-11-19

### The Final Haven (Linked Discourses 43.44) ^sn43-44-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the final haven and the path that leads to the final haven. ^sn43-44-1-1

Listen … ^sn43-44-1-2

And what is the final haven? ^sn43-44-1-3

The ending of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn43-44-1-4

And what is the path that leads to the final haven? ^sn43-44-1-6

Mindfulness of the body. ^sn43-44-1-7

The Linked Discourses on the unconditioned are complete. ^sn43-44-4-5

## Saṁyutta 44 ^sn44-0

### With Khemā (Linked Discourses 44.1) ^sn44-1-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn44-1-1-1

Now at that time the nun Khemā was wandering in the land of the Kosalans between Sāvatthī and Sāketa when she took up residence in Toraṇavatthu. ^sn44-1-1-2

Then King Pasenadi was traveling from Sāketa to Sāvatthī, and he too stayed in Toraṇavatthu for a single night. ^sn44-1-1-3

Then King Pasenadi addressed a man, ^sn44-1-1-4

“Please, my man, check if there’s a suitable (male) ascetic or brahmin in Toraṇavatthu to whom I can pay homage.” ^sn44-1-1-5

“Yes, Your Majesty,” replied that man. He searched all over Toraṇavatthu, but he couldn’t see a suitable ascetic or brahmin for the king to pay homage to. ^sn44-1-2-1

But he saw that the nun Khemā was staying there, ^sn44-1-2-2

so he went to the king and said to him, ^sn44-1-2-3

“Your Majesty, there’s no (male) ascetic or brahmin in Toraṇavatthu for the king to pay homage to. ^sn44-1-3-1

But there is the nun Khemā, who’s a disciple of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^sn44-1-3-2

She has a good reputation as being ^sn44-1-3-3

astute, competent, clever, learned, a brilliant speaker, and eloquent. ^sn44-1-3-4

Your Majesty may pay homage to her.” ^sn44-1-3-5

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the nun Khemā, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to her: ^sn44-1-4-1

“Ma’am, does a realized one still exist after death?” ^sn44-1-4-2

“Great king, this has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-1-4-3

“Well then, does a realized one no longer exist after death?” ^sn44-1-4-5

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-1-4-6

“Well then, does a realized one both exist and not exist after death?” ^sn44-1-4-8

“This has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-1-4-9

“Well then, does a realized one neither exist nor not exist after death?” ^sn44-1-4-11

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-1-4-12

“Ma’am, when asked these questions, you say ^sn44-1-5-1

that this has not been declared by the Buddha. ^sn44-1-5-2

What’s the cause, what’s the reason why this has not been declared by the Buddha?” ^sn44-1-5-13

“Well then, great king, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^sn44-1-6-1

What do you think, great king? ^sn44-1-6-2

Do you have any accountant or finger-tallier or reckoner who can count the grains of sand in the Ganges, that is, ^sn44-1-6-3

how many grains of sand there are, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of grains of sand?” ^sn44-1-6-4

“No, ma’am.” ^sn44-1-6-5

“Do you have any accountant or finger-tallier or reckoner who can count the water in the ocean, that is, ^sn44-1-6-6

how many gallons of water there are, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of gallons of water?” ^sn44-1-6-7

“No, ma’am. ^sn44-1-6-8

Why is that? ^sn44-1-6-9

Because the ocean is deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom.” ^sn44-1-6-10

“In the same way, great king, any form by which a realized one might be described has been cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn44-1-6-11

A realized one is freed from reckoning in terms of form. They’re deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom, ^sn44-1-6-12

like the ocean. ^sn44-1-6-13

To say that after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists: none of these apply. ^sn44-1-6-14

Any feeling … ^sn44-1-7-1

perception … ^sn44-1-8-1

choices … ^sn44-1-8-2

consciousness by which a realized one might be described has been cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn44-1-9-1

A realized one is freed from reckoning in terms of consciousness. They’re deep, immeasurable, and hard to fathom, ^sn44-1-9-2

like the ocean. ^sn44-1-9-3

To say that after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists: none of these apply.” ^sn44-1-9-4

Then King Pasenadi approved and agreed with what the nun Khemā said. Then he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled her, keeping her on his right, before leaving. ^sn44-1-9-5

Then on a later occasion King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. He asked the Buddha exactly the same questions he had asked the nun Khemā, and received the same answers. ^sn44-1-10-1

He said, “It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^sn44-1-13-1

How the meaning and the phrasing of the teacher and the disciple fit together and agree without contradiction when it comes to the chief matter! ^sn44-1-13-2

This one time I went to the nun Khemā and asked her about this matter. ^sn44-1-13-3

And she explained it to me with these words and phrases, just like the Buddha. ^sn44-1-13-4

It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^sn44-1-13-5

How the meaning and the phrasing of the teacher and the disciple fit together and agree without contradiction when it comes to the chief matter! ^sn44-1-13-6

Well, now, sir, I must go. ^sn44-1-13-7

I have many duties, and much to do.” ^sn44-1-13-8

“Please, great king, go at your convenience.” ^sn44-1-13-9

Then King Pasenadi approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled him, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^sn44-1-13-10

### With Anurādha (Linked Discourses 44.2) ^sn44-2-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn44-2-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Anurādha was staying not far from the Buddha in a wilderness hut. ^sn44-2-1-2

Then several wanderers of other religions went up to Venerable Anurādha and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn44-2-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him: ^sn44-2-1-4

“Reverend Anurādha, when a realized one is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them in these four ways: ^sn44-2-1-5

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn44-2-1-6

“Reverends, when a realized one is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them other than these four ways: ^sn44-2-1-7

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.” ^sn44-2-1-8

When he said this, the wanderers said to him, ^sn44-2-1-9

“This mendicant must be junior, recently gone forth, or else a foolish, incompetent senior mendicant.” ^sn44-2-1-10

Then, after rebuking Venerable Anurādha by calling him “junior” and “foolish”, the wanderers rose from their seat and left. ^sn44-2-1-11

Soon after they had left, Anurādha thought, ^sn44-2-2-1

“If those wanderers were to inquire further, ^sn44-2-2-2

how should I answer them so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should I explain in line with his teaching, so that there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^sn44-2-2-3

Then Venerable Anurādha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn44-2-2-4

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn44-2-3-1

Is form permanent or impermanent?” ^sn44-2-3-2

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn44-2-4-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn44-2-5-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn44-2-6-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn44-2-7-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn44-2-7-2

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-8-1

“Is feeling … ^sn44-2-9-1

perception … ^sn44-2-9-3

choices … ^sn44-2-9-4

consciousness permanent or impermanent?” ^sn44-2-9-5

“Impermanent, sir.” ^sn44-2-10-1

“But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?” ^sn44-2-11-1

“Suffering, sir.” ^sn44-2-12-1

“But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus: ^sn44-2-13-1

‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?” ^sn44-2-13-2

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-14-1

“So, Anurādha, you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn44-2-15-1

Any kind of feeling … ^sn44-2-15-2

perception … ^sn44-2-15-3

choices … ^sn44-2-15-4

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^sn44-2-15-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn44-2-15-6

Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn44-2-15-7

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn44-2-15-8

What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn44-2-16-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to form?” ^sn44-2-16-2

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-16-3

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to feeling?” … ^sn44-2-16-4

“… perception?” … ^sn44-2-16-6

“… choices?” … ^sn44-2-16-8

“… consciousness?” ^sn44-2-16-10

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-16-11

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn44-2-16-12

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to form?” ^sn44-2-16-13

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-16-14

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies apart from form?” ^sn44-2-16-15

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-16-16

“Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies in relation to feeling?” ^sn44-2-16-17

“… apart from feeling?” … ^sn44-2-16-18

“… in relation to perception?” … ^sn44-2-16-19

“… apart from perception?” … ^sn44-2-16-20

“… in relation to choices?” … ^sn44-2-16-21

“… apart from choices?” … ^sn44-2-16-22

“… in relation to consciousness?” … ^sn44-2-16-23

“… apart from consciousness?” ^sn44-2-16-25

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-16-26

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn44-2-17-1

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to one who has form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn44-2-17-2

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-17-3

“What do you think, Anurādha? ^sn44-2-17-4

Do you consider that the label ‘realized one’ applies to that one who is without form?” … “… feeling?” … “… perception?” … “… choices?” … “… consciousness?” ^sn44-2-17-5

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-17-6

“In that case, Anurādha, since a realized one is not found by you as a genuine fact in this very life, is it cogent for you to declare: ^sn44-2-17-7

‘Reverends, when a realized one is describing a realized one—a supreme person, highest of people, who has reached the highest point—they describe them other than these four ways: ^sn44-2-17-8

After death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists’?” ^sn44-2-17-9

“No, sir.” ^sn44-2-17-11

“Good, good, Anurādha! ^sn44-2-17-12

In the past, as today, what I describe is suffering and the cessation of suffering.” ^sn44-2-17-13

### With Sāriputta and Koṭṭhita (1st) (Linked Discourses 44.3) ^sn44-3-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^sn44-3-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn44-3-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^sn44-3-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, does a realized one still exist after death?” ^sn44-3-2-1

“Reverend, this has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-3-2-2

“Well then, does a realized one no longer exist after death? … ^sn44-3-2-4

Does a realized one both exist and not exist after death? … ^sn44-3-2-7

Does a realized one neither exist nor not exist after death?” ^sn44-3-2-10

“This too has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-3-2-11

“Reverend, when asked these questions, you say that they have not been declared by the Buddha. ^sn44-3-3-1

What’s the cause, what’s the reason why they have not been declared by the Buddha?” ^sn44-3-3-6

“Reverend, ‘does a realized one still exist after death?’ is included in form. ^sn44-3-4-1

‘Does a realized one no longer exist after death?’ is included in form. ^sn44-3-4-2

‘Does a realized one both still exist and no longer exist after death?’ is included in form. ^sn44-3-4-3

‘Does a realized one neither still exist nor no longer exist after death?’ is included in form. ^sn44-3-4-4

‘does a realized one still exist after death?’ is included in feeling … ^sn44-3-4-5

perception … ^sn44-3-4-9

choices … ^sn44-3-4-13

consciousness. ^sn44-3-4-17

‘Does a realized one no longer exist after death?’ is included in consciousness. ^sn44-3-4-18

‘Does a realized one both still exist and no longer exist after death?’ is included in consciousness. ^sn44-3-4-19

‘Does a realized one neither still exist nor no longer exist after death?’ is included in consciousness. ^sn44-3-4-20

This is the cause, this is the reason why this has not been declared by the Buddha.” ^sn44-3-4-21

### With Sāriputta and Koṭṭhita (2nd) (Linked Discourses 44.4) ^sn44-4-0
