“Mendicants, all the hard work that gets done depends on the earth and is grounded on the earth. ^sn45-149-1-2

In the same way, a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics. ^sn45-149-1-3

And how does a mendicant grounded on ethics develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-149-1-4

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-149-1-5

That’s how a mendicant grounded on ethics develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.” ^sn45-149-1-6

“… which culminate in the removal of greed, hate, and delusion …” ^sn45-149-3-4

“… which have freedom from death as their objective, destination, and culmination …” ^sn45-149-4-4

“… slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment …” ^sn45-149-5-4

### Seeds (Linked Discourses 45.150) ^sn45-150-0

“All the plants and seeds that achieve growth, increase, and maturity do so depending on the earth and grounded on the earth. ^sn45-150-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, achieving growth, increase, and maturity in good qualities. ^sn45-150-1-2

And how does a mendicant develop the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, achieving growth, increase, and maturity in good qualities? ^sn45-150-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-150-1-4

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, achieving growth, increase, and maturity in good qualities.” ^sn45-150-1-5

### Dragons (Linked Discourses 45.151) ^sn45-151-0

“Mendicants, dragons grow and wax strong supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^sn45-151-1-1

When they’re strong they dive into the pools. Then they dive into the lakes, the streams, the rivers, and finally the ocean. There they acquire a great and abundant body. ^sn45-151-1-2

In the same way, a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities. ^sn45-151-1-3

And how does a mendicant develop the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities? ^sn45-151-1-4

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-151-1-5

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities.” ^sn45-151-1-6

### Trees (Linked Discourses 45.152) ^sn45-152-0

“Mendicants, suppose a tree slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn45-152-1-1

If it was cut off at the root, where would it fall?” ^sn45-152-1-2

“Sir, it would fall in the direction that it slants, slopes, and inclines.” ^sn45-152-1-3

“In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn45-152-1-4

And how does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment? ^sn45-152-1-5

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-152-1-6

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.” ^sn45-152-1-7

### Pots (Linked Discourses 45.153) ^sn45-153-0

“Mendicants, suppose a pot full of water is tipped over, so the water drains out and doesn’t go back in. ^sn45-153-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path expels bad, unskillful qualities and doesn’t let them back in. ^sn45-153-1-2

And how does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path expel bad, unskillful qualities and not let them back in? ^sn45-153-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-153-1-4

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path expels bad, unskillful qualities and doesn’t let them back in.” ^sn45-153-1-5

### A Spike (Linked Discourses 45.154) ^sn45-154-0

“Mendicants, suppose a spike of rice or barley was pointing the right way. If you trod on it with hand or foot, it’s possible it may break the skin and produce blood. ^sn45-154-1-1

Why is that? ^sn45-154-1-2

Because the spike is pointing the right way. ^sn45-154-1-3

In the same way, a mendicant whose view and development of the path is pointing the right way may well break ignorance, produce knowledge, and realize extinguishment. ^sn45-154-1-4

Why is that? ^sn45-154-1-5

Because their view is pointing the right way. ^sn45-154-1-6

And how does a mendicant whose view and development of the path is pointing the right way break ignorance, give rise to knowledge, and realize extinguishment? ^sn45-154-1-7

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-154-1-8

That’s how a mendicant whose view and development of the path is pointing the right way breaks ignorance, gives rise to knowledge, and realizes extinguishment.” ^sn45-154-1-9

### The Sky (Linked Discourses 45.155) ^sn45-155-0

“Mendicants, various winds blow in the sky. ^sn45-155-1-1

Winds blow from the east, the west, the north, and the south. There are winds that are dusty and dustless, cool and warm, weak and strong. ^sn45-155-1-2

In the same way, when the noble eightfold path is developed and cultivated the following are fully developed: the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, and the seven awakening factors. ^sn45-155-1-3

And how are they fully developed? ^sn45-155-1-4

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-155-1-5

That’s how they’re fully developed.” ^sn45-155-1-6

### Storms (1st) (Linked Discourses 45.156) ^sn45-156-0

“Mendicants, in the last month of summer, when the dust and dirt is stirred up, a large storm out of season disperses and settles it on the spot. ^sn45-156-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path disperses and stills bad, unskillful qualities on the spot. ^sn45-156-1-2

How does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path disperse and still bad, unskillful qualities on the spot? ^sn45-156-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-156-1-4

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path disperses and stills bad, unskillful qualities on the spot.” ^sn45-156-1-5

### Storms (2nd) (Linked Discourses 45.157) ^sn45-157-0

“Mendicants, when a large storm has blown up, a strong wind disperses and settles it as it proceeds. ^sn45-157-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path disperses and stills bad, unskillful qualities as they proceed. ^sn45-157-1-2

And how does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path disperse and still bad, unskillful qualities as they proceed? ^sn45-157-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-157-1-4

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path disperses and stills bad, unskillful qualities as they proceed.” ^sn45-157-1-5

### A Ship (Linked Discourses 45.158) ^sn45-158-0

“Mendicants, suppose there was a sea-faring ship bound together with ropes. For six months they deteriorated in the water. Then in the cold season it was hauled up on dry land, where the ropes were weathered by wind and sun. When the clouds soaked it with rain, the ropes would readily collapse and rot away. ^sn45-158-1-1

In the same way, when a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path their fetters readily collapse and rot away. ^sn45-158-1-2

And how do they develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path so that their fetters readily collapse and rot away? ^sn45-158-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-158-1-4

That’s how they develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path so that their fetters readily collapse and rot away.” ^sn45-158-1-5

### A Guest House (Linked Discourses 45.159) ^sn45-159-0

“Mendicants, suppose there was a guest house. Lodgers come from the east, west, north, and south. Aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials all stay there. ^sn45-159-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path completely understands by direct knowledge the things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-1-2

They give up by direct knowledge the things that should be given up by direct knowledge. They realize by direct knowledge the things that should be realized by direct knowledge. They develop by direct knowledge the things that should be developed by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-1-3

And what are the things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge? ^sn45-159-2-1

It should be said: the five grasping aggregates. ^sn45-159-2-2

What five? ^sn45-159-2-3

That is: form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn45-159-2-4

These are the things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-2-5

And what are the things that should be given up by direct knowledge? ^sn45-159-2-6

Ignorance and craving for continued existence. ^sn45-159-2-7

These are the things that should be given up by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-2-8

And what are the things that should be realized by direct knowledge? ^sn45-159-2-9

Knowledge and freedom. ^sn45-159-2-10

These are the things that should be realized by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-2-11

And what are the things that should be developed by direct knowledge? ^sn45-159-2-12

Serenity and discernment. ^sn45-159-2-13

These are the things that should be developed by direct knowledge. ^sn45-159-2-14

And how does a mendicant develop the noble eightfold path in this way? ^sn45-159-2-15

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-159-2-16

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the eightfold path in this way.” ^sn45-159-2-17

### A River (Linked Discourses 45.160) ^sn45-160-0

“Mendicants, suppose that, although the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east, ^sn45-160-1-1

a large crowd were to come along with a spade and basket, saying: ^sn45-160-1-2

‘We’ll make this Ganges river slant, slope, and incline to the west!’ ^sn45-160-1-3

What do you think, mendicants? ^sn45-160-1-4

Would they succeed?” ^sn45-160-1-5

“No, sir. ^sn45-160-1-6

Why is that? ^sn45-160-1-7

The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn45-160-1-8

It’s not easy to make it slant, slope, and incline to the west. ^sn45-160-1-9

That large crowd will eventually get weary and frustrated.” ^sn45-160-1-10

“In the same way, while a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path, if rulers or their chief ministers, friends or colleagues, relatives or kin should invite them to accept wealth, saying: ^sn45-160-1-11

‘Please, my man, why let these ocher robes torment you? ^sn45-160-1-12

Why follow the practice of shaving your head and carrying an alms bowl? ^sn45-160-1-13

Come, return to a lesser life, enjoy wealth, and make merit!’ ^sn45-160-1-14

It is quite impossible for a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path to resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^sn45-160-1-15

Why is that? ^sn45-160-1-16

Because for a long time that mendicant’s mind has slanted, sloped, and inclined to seclusion. So it’s impossible for them to return to a lesser life. ^sn45-160-1-17

And how does a mendicant develop the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-160-1-18

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-160-1-19

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.” ^sn45-160-1-20

### Searches (Linked Discourses 45.161) ^sn45-161-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn45-161-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-1-2

What three? ^sn45-161-1-3

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-1-4

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-1-5

The noble eightfold path should be developed to directly know these three searches. ^sn45-161-1-6

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-161-1-7

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-161-1-8

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed to directly know these three searches.” ^sn45-161-1-9

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-2-1

What three? ^sn45-161-2-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-2-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-2-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to directly know these three searches. ^sn45-161-2-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-161-2-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which culminate in the removal of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn45-161-2-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed to directly know these three searches.” ^sn45-161-2-8

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-3-1

What three? ^sn45-161-3-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-3-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-3-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to directly know these three searches. ^sn45-161-3-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-161-3-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, whose objective, destination, and culmination is freedom from death. ^sn45-161-3-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed to directly know these three searches.” ^sn45-161-3-8

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-4-1

What three? ^sn45-161-4-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-4-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-4-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to directly know these three searches. ^sn45-161-4-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-161-4-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn45-161-4-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed to directly know these three searches.” ^sn45-161-4-8

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-5-1

What three? ^sn45-161-5-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-5-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-5-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to completely understand …” ^sn45-161-5-5

(Tell in full with “completely understand” instead of “directly know”.) ^sn45-161-5-6

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-6-1

What three? ^sn45-161-6-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-6-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-6-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to finish …” ^sn45-161-6-5

(Tell in full with “finish” instead of “directly know”.) ^sn45-161-6-6

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn45-161-7-1

What three? ^sn45-161-7-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. ^sn45-161-7-3

These are the three searches. ^sn45-161-7-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed to give up …” ^sn45-161-7-5

(Tell in full with “give up” instead of “directly know”.) ^sn45-161-7-9

### Discriminations (Linked Discourses 45.162) ^sn45-162-0

“Mendicants, there are three kinds of discrimination. ^sn45-162-1-1

What three? ^sn45-162-1-2

One discriminates, thinking that ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. ^sn45-162-1-3

These are the three kinds of discrimination. ^sn45-162-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three kinds of discrimination. ^sn45-162-1-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-162-1-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-162-1-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three kinds of discrimination.” ^sn45-162-1-8

(Tell in full as in the section on searches.) ^sn45-162-1-9

### Defilements (Linked Discourses 45.163) ^sn45-163-0

“Mendicants, there are these three defilements. ^sn45-163-1-1

What three? ^sn45-163-1-2

The defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^sn45-163-1-3

These are the three defilements. ^sn45-163-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three defilements.” ^sn45-163-1-5

### States of Existence (Linked Discourses 45.164) ^sn45-164-0

“There are these three states of existence. ^sn45-164-1-1

What three? ^sn45-164-1-2

Existence in the sensual realm, the realm of luminous form, and the formless realm. ^sn45-164-1-3

These are the three states of existence. ^sn45-164-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three states of existence.” ^sn45-164-1-5

### Forms of Suffering (Linked Discourses 45.165) ^sn45-165-0

“Mendicants, there are these three forms of suffering. ^sn45-165-1-1

What three? ^sn45-165-1-2

The suffering inherent in painful feeling; the suffering inherent in conditions; and the suffering inherent in perishing. ^sn45-165-1-3

These are the three forms of suffering. ^sn45-165-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three forms of suffering.” ^sn45-165-1-5

### Kinds of Barrenness (Linked Discourses 45.166) ^sn45-166-0

“Mendicants, there are these three kinds of barrenness. ^sn45-166-1-1

What three? ^sn45-166-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn45-166-1-3

These are the three kinds of barrenness. ^sn45-166-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three kinds of barrenness.” ^sn45-166-1-5

### Stains (Linked Discourses 45.167) ^sn45-167-0

“Mendicants, there are these three stains. ^sn45-167-1-1

What three? ^sn45-167-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn45-167-1-3

These are the three stains. ^sn45-167-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three stains.” ^sn45-167-1-5

### Troubles (Linked Discourses 45.168) ^sn45-168-0

“Mendicants, there are these three troubles. ^sn45-168-1-1

What three? ^sn45-168-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn45-168-1-3

These are the three troubles. ^sn45-168-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three troubles.” ^sn45-168-1-5

### Feelings (Linked Discourses 45.169) ^sn45-169-0

“Mendicants, there are these three feelings: ^sn45-169-1-1

What three? ^sn45-169-1-2

Pleasant, painful, and neutral feeling. ^sn45-169-1-3

These are the three feelings. ^sn45-169-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three feelings.” ^sn45-169-1-5

### Craving (Linked Discourses 45.170) ^sn45-170-0

“Mendicants, there are these three cravings. ^sn45-170-1-1

What three? ^sn45-170-1-2

Craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^sn45-170-1-3

These are the three cravings. ^sn45-170-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three cravings. ^sn45-170-1-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-170-1-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-170-1-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three cravings.” ^sn45-170-1-8

Thirst ^sn45-170-2-0

“Mendicants, there are these three thirsts. ^sn45-170-2-1

What three? ^sn45-170-2-2

Thirst for sensual pleasures, thirst to continue existence, and thirst to end existence. ^sn45-170-2-3

For the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three thirsts … ^sn45-170-2-4

… which culminates in the removal of greed, hate, and delusion. ^sn45-170-2-5

… whose objective, destination, and culmination is freedom from death. ^sn45-170-2-6

… which slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn45-170-2-7

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these three thirsts.” ^sn45-170-2-8

### Floods (Linked Discourses 45.171) ^sn45-171-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn45-171-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four floods. ^sn45-171-1-2

What four? ^sn45-171-1-3

The floods of sensuality, desire to be reborn, views, and ignorance. ^sn45-171-1-4

These are the four floods. ^sn45-171-1-5

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these four floods.” ^sn45-171-1-6

(Tell in full as in the section on searches.) ^sn45-171-1-7

### Yokes (Linked Discourses 45.172) ^sn45-172-0

“Mendicants, there are these four yokes. ^sn45-172-1-1

What four? ^sn45-172-1-2

The yokes of sensual pleasures, future lives, views, and ignorance. ^sn45-172-1-3

These are the four yokes. ^sn45-172-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these four yokes.” ^sn45-172-1-5

### Grasping (Linked Discourses 45.173) ^sn45-173-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of grasping. ^sn45-173-1-1

What four? ^sn45-173-1-2

Grasping at sensual pleasures, views, precepts and observances, and theories of a self. ^sn45-173-1-3

These are the four kinds of grasping. ^sn45-173-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these four kinds of grasping.” ^sn45-173-1-5

### Personal Ties (Linked Discourses 45.174) ^sn45-174-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ties. ^sn45-174-1-1

What four? ^sn45-174-1-2

The personal ties to covetousness, ill will, misapprehension of precepts and observances, and the insistence that this is the only truth. ^sn45-174-1-3

These are the four ties. ^sn45-174-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these four ties.” ^sn45-174-1-5

### Tendencies (Linked Discourses 45.175) ^sn45-175-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven underlying tendencies. ^sn45-175-1-1

What seven? ^sn45-175-1-2

The underlying tendencies of sensual desire, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^sn45-175-1-3

These are the seven underlying tendencies. ^sn45-175-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these seven underlying tendencies.” ^sn45-175-1-5

### Kinds of Sensual Stimulation (Linked Discourses 45.176) ^sn45-176-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^sn45-176-1-1

What five? ^sn45-176-1-2

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn45-176-1-3

Sounds known by the ear … Smells known by the nose … ^sn45-176-1-4

Tastes known by the tongue … ^sn45-176-1-5

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^sn45-176-1-6

These are the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^sn45-176-1-7

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five kinds of sensual stimulation.” ^sn45-176-1-8

### Hindrances (Linked Discourses 45.177) ^sn45-177-0

“Mendicants, there are these five hindrances. ^sn45-177-1-1

What five? ^sn45-177-1-2

The hindrances of sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^sn45-177-1-3

These are the five hindrances. ^sn45-177-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five hindrances.” ^sn45-177-1-5

### Grasping Aggregates (Linked Discourses 45.178) ^sn45-178-0

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^sn45-178-1-1

What five? ^sn45-178-1-2

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^sn45-178-1-3

These are the five grasping aggregates. ^sn45-178-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five grasping aggregates.” ^sn45-178-1-5

### Lower Fetters (Linked Discourses 45.179) ^sn45-179-0

“Mendicants, there are five lower fetters. ^sn45-179-1-1

What five? ^sn45-179-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, sensual desire, and ill will. ^sn45-179-1-3

These are the five lower fetters. ^sn45-179-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five lowers fetters.” ^sn45-179-1-5

### Higher Fetters (Linked Discourses 45.180) ^sn45-180-0

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-1-1

What five? ^sn45-180-1-2

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^sn45-180-1-3

These are the five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-1-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-1-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-180-1-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn45-180-1-7

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters.” ^sn45-180-1-8

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-2-1

What five? ^sn45-180-2-2

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^sn45-180-2-3

These are the five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-2-4

The noble eightfold path should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters. ^sn45-180-2-5

What is the noble eightfold path? ^sn45-180-2-6

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which culminate in the removal of greed, hate, and delusion …” ^sn45-180-2-7

“… whose objective, destination, and culmination is freedom from death …” ^sn45-180-2-8

“… which slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment. ^sn45-180-2-9

This is the noble eightfold path that should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters.” ^sn45-180-2-10

The Linked Discourses on the Path, the first section. ^sn45-180-6-6

## Saṁyutta 46 ^sn46-0

### The Himalaya (Linked Discourses 46.1) ^sn46-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-1-1-1

“Mendicants, dragons grow and wax strong supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^sn46-1-1-2

When they’re strong they dive into the pools. Then they dive into the lakes, the streams, the rivers, and finally the ocean. There they acquire a great and abundant body. ^sn46-1-1-3

In the same way, a mendicant develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities. ^sn46-1-1-4

And how does a mendicant develop the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities? ^sn46-1-1-5

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. ^sn46-1-1-6

They develop the awakening factor of investigation of principles … ^sn46-1-1-7

They develop the awakening factor of energy … ^sn46-1-1-8

They develop the awakening factor of rapture … ^sn46-1-1-9

They develop the awakening factor of tranquility … ^sn46-1-1-10

They develop the awakening factor of immersion … ^sn46-1-1-11

They develop the awakening factor of equanimity, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. ^sn46-1-1-12

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities.” ^sn46-1-1-13

### The Body (Linked Discourses 46.2) ^sn46-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-2-1-1

“Mendicants, this body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue. ^sn46-2-1-2

In the same way, the five hindrances are sustained by fuel. They depend on fuel to continue, and without fuel they don’t continue. ^sn46-2-1-3

And what fuels the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-2-2-1

There is the feature of beauty. ^sn46-2-2-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-2-2-3

fuels the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-2-2-4

And what fuels the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-2-3-1

There is the feature of aversion. ^sn46-2-3-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-2-3-3

fuels the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-2-3-4

And what fuels the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow? ^sn46-2-4-1

There is discontent, sloth, yawning, sleepiness after eating, and mental sluggishness. ^sn46-2-4-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-4-3

fuels the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow. ^sn46-2-4-4

And what fuels the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow? ^sn46-2-5-1

There is the unsettled mind. ^sn46-2-5-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-2-5-3

fuels the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow. ^sn46-2-5-4

And what fuels the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-2-6-1

There are things that are grounds for doubt. ^sn46-2-6-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-6-3

fuels the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-2-6-4

This body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue. ^sn46-2-7-1

In the same way, the five hindrances are sustained by fuel. They depend on fuel to continue, and without fuel they don’t continue. ^sn46-2-7-2

This body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue. ^sn46-2-8-1

In the same way, the seven awakening factors are sustained by fuel. They depend on fuel to continue, and without fuel they don’t continue. ^sn46-2-8-2

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-9-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of mindfulness. ^sn46-2-9-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-9-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-9-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-10-1

There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^sn46-2-10-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-10-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-10-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-11-1

There are the elements of initiative, persistence, and exertion. ^sn46-2-11-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-11-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-11-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-12-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of rapture. ^sn46-2-12-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-12-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-12-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-13-1

There is tranquility of the body and of the mind. ^sn46-2-13-2

Frequent rational application of mind to that ^sn46-2-13-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-13-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-14-1

There are things that are the basis of serenity and freedom from distraction. ^sn46-2-14-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-14-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-14-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-2-15-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of equanimity. ^sn46-2-15-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-2-15-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-2-15-4

This body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue. ^sn46-2-16-1

In the same way, the seven awakening factors are sustained by fuel. They depend on fuel to continue, and without fuel they don’t continue.” ^sn46-2-16-2

### Ethics (Linked Discourses 46.3) ^sn46-3-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant is accomplished in ethics, immersion, knowledge, freedom, or the knowledge and vision of freedom, even the sight of them is very helpful, I say. ^sn46-3-1-1

Even to hear them, ^sn46-3-1-2

approach them, ^sn46-3-1-3

pay homage to them, ^sn46-3-1-4

recollect them, ^sn46-3-1-5

or go forth following them is very helpful, I say. ^sn46-3-1-6

Why is that? ^sn46-3-1-7

Because after hearing the teaching of such mendicants, a mendicant will live withdrawn in both body and mind, ^sn46-3-1-8

as they recollect and think about that teaching. ^sn46-3-1-9

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of mindfulness; ^sn46-3-2-1

they develop it ^sn46-3-2-2

and perfect it. ^sn46-3-2-3

As they live mindfully in this way they investigate, explore, and inquire into that teaching with wisdom. ^sn46-3-2-4

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of investigation of principles; ^sn46-3-3-1

they develop it ^sn46-3-3-2

and perfect it. ^sn46-3-3-3

As they investigate principles with wisdom in this way their energy is roused up and unflagging. ^sn46-3-3-4

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of energy; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn46-3-4-1

When they’re energetic, rapture not of the flesh arises. ^sn46-3-4-2

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of rapture; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn46-3-5-1

When the mind is full of rapture, the body and mind become tranquil. ^sn46-3-5-2

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of tranquility; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn46-3-6-1

When the body is tranquil and one feels bliss, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn46-3-6-2

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of immersion; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn46-3-7-1

They closely watch over that mind immersed in samādhi. ^sn46-3-7-2

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of equanimity; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn46-3-8-1

When the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated in this way they can expect seven fruits and benefits. ^sn46-3-9-1

What seven? ^sn46-3-9-2

They attain enlightenment early on in this very life. ^sn46-3-9-3

If not, they attain enlightenment at the time of death. ^sn46-3-9-4

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters, they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^sn46-3-9-5

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. ^sn46-3-9-6

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^sn46-3-9-7

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^sn46-3-9-8

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^sn46-3-9-9

When the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated in this way these are the seven fruits and benefits they can expect.” ^sn46-3-9-10

### Clothes (Linked Discourses 46.4) ^sn46-4-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn46-4-1-1

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^sn46-4-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn46-4-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn46-4-1-4

Sāriputta said this: ^sn46-4-1-5

“There are these seven awakening factors. ^sn46-4-2-1

What seven? ^sn46-4-2-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-4-2-3

These are the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-4-2-4

In the morning, I meditate on whichever of these seven awakening factors I want. ^sn46-4-2-5

At midday, ^sn46-4-2-6

and in the evening, I meditate on whichever of these seven awakening factors I want. ^sn46-4-2-7

If it’s the awakening factor of mindfulness, I know that it’s limitless and that it’s properly implemented. While it remains I understand that it remains. ^sn46-4-2-8

And if it subsides in me I understand the specific reason it subsides. … ^sn46-4-2-9

If it’s the awakening factor of equanimity, I know that it’s limitless and that it’s properly implemented. While it remains I understand that it remains. ^sn46-4-2-10

And if it subsides I understand the specific reason it subsides. ^sn46-4-2-11

Suppose that a ruler or their chief minister had a chest full of garments of different colors. ^sn46-4-3-1

In the morning, they’d don whatever pair of garments they wanted. ^sn46-4-3-2

At midday, ^sn46-4-3-3

and in the evening, they’d don whatever pair of garments they wanted. ^sn46-4-3-4

In the same way, in the morning, ^sn46-4-3-5

at midday, ^sn46-4-3-6

and in the evening, I meditate on whichever of these seven awakening factors I want. ^sn46-4-3-7

If it’s the awakening factor of mindfulness, I know that it’s limitless and that it’s properly implemented. While it remains I understand that it remains. ^sn46-4-3-8

And if it subsides I understand the specific reason it subsides. … ^sn46-4-3-9

If it’s the awakening factor of equanimity, I know that it’s limitless and that it’s properly implemented. While it remains I understand that it remains. ^sn46-4-3-10

And if it subsides I understand the specific reason it subsides.” ^sn46-4-3-11

### A Monk (Linked Discourses 46.5) ^sn46-5-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-5-1-1

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn46-5-1-2

“Sir, they speak of the ‘awakening factors’. ^sn46-5-1-3

How are the awakening factors defined?” ^sn46-5-1-4

“Mendicant, they’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening. ^sn46-5-1-5

A mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-5-1-6

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-5-1-7

As they develop the seven awakening factors, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^sn46-5-1-8

When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^sn46-5-1-9

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn46-5-1-10

They’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.” ^sn46-5-1-11

### Kuṇḍaliya (Linked Discourses 46.6) ^sn46-6-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāketa in the deer park at the Añjana Wood. ^sn46-6-1-1

Then the wanderer Kuṇḍaliya went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn46-6-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^sn46-6-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, I like to hang around the monasteries and visit the assemblies. ^sn46-6-1-4

When I’ve finished breakfast, it’s my habit to ^sn46-6-1-5

wander from monastery to monastery, from park to park. ^sn46-6-1-6

There I see some ascetics and brahmins speaking for the sake of winning debates and finding fault. ^sn46-6-1-7

But what benefit does the worthy Gotama live for?” ^sn46-6-1-8

“The benefit the Realized One lives for, Kuṇḍaliya, is the fruit of knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-6-1-9

“But what things must be developed and cultivated in order to fulfill knowledge and freedom?” ^sn46-6-2-1

“The seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-6-2-2

“But what things must be developed and cultivated in order to fulfill the seven awakening factors?” ^sn46-6-2-3

“The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^sn46-6-2-4

“But what things must be developed and cultivated in order to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation?” ^sn46-6-2-5

“The three kinds of good conduct.” ^sn46-6-2-6

“But what things must be developed and cultivated in order to fulfill the three kinds of good conduct?” ^sn46-6-2-7

“Sense restraint. ^sn46-6-2-8

And Kuṇḍaliya, how is sense restraint developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the three kinds of good conduct? ^sn46-6-3-1

A mendicant sees an agreeable sight with their eye. They don’t desire it or enjoy it, and they don’t give rise to greed. ^sn46-6-3-2

Their mind and body are steady internally, well settled and well freed. ^sn46-6-3-3

But if they see a disagreeable sight they’re undismayed, with mind unirritated, spirit undaunted, and heart unangered. ^sn46-6-3-4

Their mind and body are steady internally, well settled and well freed. ^sn46-6-3-5

Furthermore, a mendicant hears an agreeable sound with the ear … ^sn46-6-4-1

smells an agreeable odor with the nose … ^sn46-6-4-2

tastes an agreeable flavor with the tongue … ^sn46-6-4-3

encounters an agreeable touch with the body … ^sn46-6-4-4

knows an agreeable idea with their mind. They don’t desire it or enjoy it, and they don’t give rise to greed. ^sn46-6-4-5

Their mind and body are steady internally, well settled and well freed. ^sn46-6-4-6

But if they know a disagreeable idea they’re undismayed, with mind unirritated, spirit undaunted, and heart unangered. ^sn46-6-4-7

Their mind and body are steady internally, well settled and well freed. ^sn46-6-4-8

When a mendicant’s mind and body are steady internally, they’re well settled and well freed when it comes to both agreeable and disagreeable sights, ^sn46-6-5-1

sounds, ^sn46-6-5-2

smells, ^sn46-6-5-3

tastes, ^sn46-6-5-4

touches, ^sn46-6-5-5

and ideas. ^sn46-6-5-6

That’s how sense restraint is developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the three kinds of good conduct. ^sn46-6-5-7

And how are the three kinds of good conduct developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^sn46-6-6-1

A mendicant gives up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develops good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^sn46-6-6-2

That’s how the three kinds of good conduct are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn46-6-6-3

And how are the four kinds of mindfulness meditation developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-6-7-1

A mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn46-6-7-2

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn46-6-7-3

mind … ^sn46-6-7-4

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn46-6-7-5

That’s how the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-6-7-6

And how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom? ^sn46-6-8-1

A mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-6-8-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-6-8-3

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-6-8-4

When he said this, the wanderer Kuṇḍaliya said to the Buddha, ^sn46-6-9-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^sn46-6-9-2

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^sn46-6-9-3

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn46-6-9-4

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn46-6-9-5

### A Bungalow (Linked Discourses 46.7) ^sn46-7-0

“Mendicants, the rafters of a bungalow all slant, slope, and incline to the peak. ^sn46-7-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn46-7-1-2

And how does a mendicant who develops the seven awakening factors slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment? ^sn46-7-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-7-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-7-2-3

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.” ^sn46-7-2-4

### With Upavāṇa (Linked Discourses 46.8) ^sn46-8-0

At one time the venerables Upavāṇa and Sāriputta were staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^sn46-8-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Upavāṇa and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn46-8-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Upavāṇa: ^sn46-8-1-3

“Reverend Upavāṇa, can a mendicant know that by investigating inside themselves, the seven awakening factors have been properly implemented so that they lead to living at ease?” ^sn46-8-2-1

“They can, Reverend Sāriputta. ^sn46-8-2-2

As a mendicant rouses up the awakening factor of mindfulness, they understand: ‘My mind is well freed. I’ve eradicated dullness and drowsiness, and eliminated restlessness and remorse. My energy is roused up, and my mind is sharply focused, not sluggish.’ … ^sn46-8-3-1

As they rouse up the awakening factor of equanimity, they understand: ‘My mind is well freed. I’ve eradicated dullness and drowsiness, and eliminated restlessness and remorse. My energy is roused up, and my mind is sharply focused, not sluggish.’ ^sn46-8-3-2

That’s how a mendicant can know that by investigating inside themselves, the seven awakening factors are properly implemented so that they lead to living at ease.” ^sn46-8-3-3

### Arisen (1st) (Linked Discourses 46.9) ^sn46-9-0

“Mendicants, these seven awakening factors don’t arise to be developed and cultivated except when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has appeared. ^sn46-9-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-9-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-9-1-3

These seven awakening factors don’t arise to be developed and cultivated except when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has appeared.” ^sn46-9-1-4

### Arisen (2nd) (Linked Discourses 46.10) ^sn46-10-0

“Mendicants, these seven awakening factors don’t arise to be developed and cultivated apart from the Holy One’s training. ^sn46-10-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-10-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-10-1-3

These seven awakening factors don’t arise to be developed and cultivated apart from the Holy One’s training.” ^sn46-10-1-4

### Living Creatures (Linked Discourses 46.11) ^sn46-11-0

“Mendicants, whatever living creatures engage in the four postures— ^sn46-11-1-1

sometimes walking, sometimes standing, sometimes sitting, sometimes lying down—all do so depending on the earth and grounded on the earth. ^sn46-11-1-2

In the same way, a mendicant develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics. ^sn46-11-1-3

And how does a mendicant develop the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics? ^sn46-11-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-11-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-11-2-3

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics.” ^sn46-11-2-4

### The Simile of the Sun (1st) (Linked Discourses 46.12) ^sn46-12-0

“Mendicants, the dawn is the forerunner and harbinger of the sunrise. ^sn46-12-1-1

In the same way, for a mendicant good friendship is the forerunner and harbinger of the arising of the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-12-1-2

A mendicant with good friends can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-12-1-3

And how does a mendicant with good friends develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-12-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-12-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-12-2-3

That’s how a mendicant with good friends develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-12-2-4

### The Simile of the Sun (2nd) (Linked Discourses 46.13) ^sn46-13-0

“Mendicants, the dawn is the forerunner and harbinger of the sunrise. ^sn46-13-1-1

In the same way, for a mendicant rational application of mind is the forerunner and harbinger of the arising of the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-13-1-2

A mendicant accomplished in rational application of mind can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-13-1-3

And how does a mendicant with rational application of mind develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-13-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-13-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-13-2-3

That’s how a mendicant with rational application of mind develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-13-2-4

### Sick (1st) (Linked Discourses 46.14) ^sn46-14-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn46-14-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Mahākassapa was staying in the Pipphali cave, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn46-14-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat, went to Venerable Mahākassapa, sat down on the seat spread out, and said to him: ^sn46-14-1-3

“I hope you’re keeping well, Kassapa; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn46-14-2-1

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn46-14-2-2

“Kassapa, I’ve rightly explained these seven awakening factors. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment. ^sn46-14-3-1

What seven? ^sn46-14-3-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-14-3-3

These are the seven awakening factors that I’ve rightly explained. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment.” ^sn46-14-3-4

“Indeed, Blessed One, these are awakening factors! ^sn46-14-3-5

Indeed, Holy One, these are awakening factors!” ^sn46-14-3-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn46-14-4-1

Satisfied, Venerable Mahākassapa approved what the Buddha said. ^sn46-14-4-2

And that’s how he recovered from that illness. ^sn46-14-4-3

### Sick (2nd) (Linked Discourses 46.15) ^sn46-15-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn46-15-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was staying on the Vulture’s Peak mountain, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn46-15-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat, went to Venerable Moggallāna, sat down on the seat spread out, and said to him: ^sn46-15-1-3

“I hope you’re keeping well, Moggallāna; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn46-15-2-1

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn46-15-2-2

“Moggallāna, I’ve rightly explained these seven awakening factors. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment. ^sn46-15-3-1

What seven? ^sn46-15-3-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-15-3-3

These are the seven awakening factors that I’ve rightly explained. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment.” ^sn46-15-3-4

“Indeed, Blessed One, these are awakening factors! ^sn46-15-3-5

Indeed, Holy One, these are awakening factors!” ^sn46-15-3-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn46-15-4-1

Satisfied, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna approved what the Buddha said. ^sn46-15-4-2

And that’s how he recovered from that illness. ^sn46-15-4-3

### Sick (3rd) (Linked Discourses 46.16) ^sn46-16-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn46-16-1-1

Now at that time he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn46-16-1-2

Then Venerable Mahācunda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn46-16-1-3

“Cunda, express your understanding of the awakening factors.” ^sn46-16-1-4

“Sir, the Buddha has rightly explained these seven awakening factors. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment. ^sn46-16-2-1

What seven? ^sn46-16-2-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-16-2-3

These are the seven awakening factors that the Buddha has rightly explained. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment.” ^sn46-16-2-4

“Indeed, Cunda, these are awakening factors! ^sn46-16-2-5

Indeed, Cunda, these are awakening factors!” ^sn46-16-2-6

This is what Cunda said, ^sn46-16-3-1

and the teacher approved. ^sn46-16-3-2

And that’s how the Buddha recovered from that illness. ^sn46-16-3-3

### Going to the Far Shore (Linked Discourses 46.17) ^sn46-17-0

“Mendicants, when these seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they lead to going from the near shore to the far shore. ^sn46-17-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-17-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-17-1-3

These seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, lead to going from the near shore to the far shore. ^sn46-17-1-4

Few are those among humans ^sn46-17-2-1

who cross to the far shore. ^sn46-17-2-2

The rest just run ^sn46-17-2-3

around on the near shore. ^sn46-17-2-4

When the teaching is well explained, ^sn46-17-3-1

those who practice accordingly ^sn46-17-3-2

are the ones who will cross over ^sn46-17-3-3

Death’s dominion so hard to pass. ^sn46-17-3-4

Rid of dark qualities, ^sn46-17-4-1

an astute person should develop the bright. ^sn46-17-4-2

Leaving home behind ^sn46-17-4-3

for the seclusion so hard to enjoy, ^sn46-17-4-4

try to find satisfaction there, ^sn46-17-5-1

having left behind sensual pleasures. ^sn46-17-5-2

Owning nothing, an astute person ^sn46-17-5-3

should cleanse themselves of mental corruptions. ^sn46-17-5-4

And those whose minds are rightly developed ^sn46-17-6-1

in the awakening factors; ^sn46-17-6-2

letting go of attachments, ^sn46-17-6-3

they delight in not grasping. ^sn46-17-6-4

With defilements ended, brilliant, ^sn46-17-6-5

they are quenched in this world.” ^sn46-17-6-6

### Missed Out (Linked Discourses 46.18) ^sn46-18-0

“Mendicants, whoever has missed out on the seven awakening factors has missed out on the noble path to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn46-18-1-1

Whoever has undertaken the seven awakening factors has undertaken the noble path to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn46-18-1-2

What seven? ^sn46-18-1-3

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-18-1-4

Whoever has missed out on these seven awakening factors has missed out on the noble path to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn46-18-1-5

Whoever has undertaken these seven awakening factors has undertaken the noble path to the complete ending of suffering.” ^sn46-18-1-6

### Noble (Linked Discourses 46.19) ^sn46-19-0

“Mendicants, when these seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they are noble and emancipating, and bring one who practices them to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn46-19-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-19-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-19-1-3

When these seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they are noble and emancipating, and bring one who practices them to the complete ending of suffering.” ^sn46-19-1-4

### Disillusionment (Linked Discourses 46.20) ^sn46-20-0

“Mendicants, the seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn46-20-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-20-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-20-1-3

These seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment.” ^sn46-20-1-4

### To Awakening (Linked Discourses 46.21) ^sn46-21-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and said to him: ^sn46-21-1-1

“Sir, they speak of the ‘awakening factors’. ^sn46-21-2-1

How are the awakening factors defined?” ^sn46-21-2-2

“Mendicant, they’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening. ^sn46-21-2-3

A mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-21-2-4

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-21-2-5

They’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.” ^sn46-21-2-6

### A Teaching on the Awakening Factors (Linked Discourses 46.22) ^sn46-22-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-22-1-1

Listen … ^sn46-22-1-2

And what are the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-22-1-3

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-22-1-4

These are the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-22-1-5

### Grounds (Linked Discourses 46.23) ^sn46-23-0

“Mendicants, when you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for sensual greed, sensual desire arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^sn46-23-1-1

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for ill will, ill will arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^sn46-23-1-2

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for dullness and drowsiness, dullness and drowsiness arise, and once arisen they increase and grow. ^sn46-23-1-3

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for restlessness and remorse, restlessness and remorse arise, and once arisen they increase and grow. ^sn46-23-1-4

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for doubt, doubt arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^sn46-23-1-5

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for the awakening factor of mindfulness, the awakening factor of mindfulness arises, and once arisen it’s fully developed. … ^sn46-23-2-1

When you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for the awakening factor of equanimity, the awakening factor of equanimity arises, and once arisen it’s fully developed.” ^sn46-23-2-2

### Irrational Application of Mind (Linked Discourses 46.24) ^sn46-24-0

“Mendicants, when you apply the mind irrationally, sensual desire, ^sn46-24-1-1

ill will, ^sn46-24-1-2

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn46-24-1-3

restlessness and remorse, ^sn46-24-1-4

and doubt arise, and once arisen they increase and grow. ^sn46-24-1-5

And the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity don’t arise, or if they’ve already arisen, they cease. ^sn46-24-1-6

When you apply the mind rationally, sensual desire, ^sn46-24-2-1

ill will, ^sn46-24-2-2

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn46-24-2-3

restlessness and remorse, ^sn46-24-2-4

and doubt don’t arise, or if they’ve already arisen they’re given up. ^sn46-24-2-5

And the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity arise, and once they’ve arisen, they’re fully developed.” ^sn46-24-3-1

### Non-decline (Linked Discourses 46.25) ^sn46-25-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline. ^sn46-25-1-1

Listen … ^sn46-25-1-2

And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^sn46-25-1-3

They are the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-25-1-5

What seven? ^sn46-25-1-6

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-25-1-7

These are the seven principles that prevent decline.” ^sn46-25-1-8

### The Ending of Craving (Linked Discourses 46.26) ^sn46-26-0

“Mendicants, you should develop the path and the practice that leads to the ending of craving. ^sn46-26-1-1

And what is the path and the practice that leads to the ending of craving? ^sn46-26-1-2

It is the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-26-1-4

What seven? ^sn46-26-1-5

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.” ^sn46-26-1-6

When he said this, Udāyī said to him: ^sn46-26-1-7

“Sir, how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to lead to the ending of craving?” ^sn46-26-1-8

“Udāyī, it’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. And it is abundant, expansive, limitless, and free of ill will. ^sn46-26-2-1

As they do so, craving is given up. ^sn46-26-2-2

When craving is given up, deeds are given up. ^sn46-26-2-3

When deeds are given up, suffering is given up. … ^sn46-26-2-4

A mendicant develops the awakening factor of equanimity, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. And it is abundant, expansive, limitless, and free of ill will. ^sn46-26-2-5

As they do so, craving is given up. ^sn46-26-2-6

When craving is given up, deeds are given up. ^sn46-26-2-7

When deeds are given up, suffering is given up. ^sn46-26-2-8

And so, Udāyī, when craving ends, deeds end; when deeds end suffering ends.” ^sn46-26-2-9

### The Cessation of Craving (Linked Discourses 46.27) ^sn46-27-0

“Mendicants, you should develop the path and the practice that leads to the cessation of craving. ^sn46-27-1-1

And what is the path and the practice that leads to the cessation of craving? ^sn46-27-1-2

It is the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-27-1-4

What seven? ^sn46-27-1-5

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-27-1-6

And how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to lead to the cessation of craving? ^sn46-27-1-7

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-27-2-1

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-27-2-2

This is how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to lead to the cessation of craving.” ^sn46-27-2-3

### Helping Penetration (Linked Discourses 46.28) ^sn46-28-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a path that helps penetration. ^sn46-28-1-1

Listen … ^sn46-28-1-2

And what is the path that helps penetration? ^sn46-28-1-3

It is the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-28-1-5

What seven? ^sn46-28-1-6

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.” ^sn46-28-1-7

When he said this, Udāyī said to him: ^sn46-28-1-8

“Sir, how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to lead to penetration?” ^sn46-28-1-9

“Udāyī, it’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. And it is abundant, expansive, limitless, and free of ill will. ^sn46-28-2-1

With a mind that has developed the awakening factor of mindfulness, they penetrate and shatter the mass of greed, ^sn46-28-2-2

the mass of hate, ^sn46-28-2-3

and the mass of delusion for the first time. … ^sn46-28-2-4

A mendicant develops the awakening factor of equanimity, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. And it is abundant, expansive, limitless, and free of ill will. ^sn46-28-2-5

With a mind that has developed the awakening factor of equanimity, they penetrate and shatter the mass of greed, ^sn46-28-2-6

the mass of hate, ^sn46-28-2-7

and the mass of delusion for the first time. ^sn46-28-2-8

This is how are the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to lead to penetration.” ^sn46-28-2-9

### One Thing (Linked Discourses 46.29) ^sn46-29-0

“Mendicants, I do not see a single thing that, when it is developed and cultivated like this, leads to giving up the things that tighten the fetters like the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-29-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-29-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-29-1-3

And how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to lead to giving up the things that tighten the fetters? ^sn46-29-1-4

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-29-2-1

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-29-2-2

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to lead to giving up the things that tighten the fetters. ^sn46-29-2-3

And what are the things that tighten the fetters? ^sn46-29-3-1

The eye is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn46-29-3-2

This is where these fetters, shackles, and attachments arise. ^sn46-29-3-3

The ear … nose … tongue … body … ^sn46-29-3-4

mind is something that tightens the fetters. ^sn46-29-3-6

This is where these fetters, shackles, and attachments arise. ^sn46-29-3-7

These are called the things that tighten the fetters.” ^sn46-29-3-8

### With Udāyī (Linked Discourses 46.30) ^sn46-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sumbhas, near the town of the Sumbhas named Sedaka. ^sn46-30-1-1

Then Venerable Udāyī went up to the Buddha … and said to him: ^sn46-30-1-2

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^sn46-30-2-1

How helpful my fondness and respect for the Buddha have been, and my sense of conscience and prudence. ^sn46-30-2-2

For when I was still a layman, I wasn’t helped much by the teaching or the Saṅgha. ^sn46-30-2-3

But when I considered my fondness and respect for the Buddha, and my sense of conscience and prudence, I went forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^sn46-30-2-4

The Buddha taught me the Dhamma: ^sn46-30-2-5

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the disappearance of form. ^sn46-30-2-6

Such is feeling … ^sn46-30-2-7

Such is perception … ^sn46-30-2-8

Such are choices … ^sn46-30-2-9

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.’ ^sn46-30-2-10

Then, while staying in an empty hut, I followed the churning of the five grasping aggregates. I truly understood: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^sn46-30-3-1

I comprehended the teaching, sir, I acquired the path. ^sn46-30-3-2

This, when developed and cultivated by me meditating in just such a way, will bring me to such a state that I will understand: ^sn46-30-3-3

‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn46-30-3-4

I acquired the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-30-4-1

Each of these, when developed and cultivated by me meditating in just such a way, will bring me to such a state that I will understand: ^sn46-30-4-3

‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^sn46-30-4-4

This is the path that I acquired which, when developed and cultivated by me meditating in just such a way, will bring me to such a state that I will understand: ^sn46-30-4-5

‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn46-30-4-6

“Good, good, Udāyī! ^sn46-30-5-1

For that is indeed the path that you acquired which, when developed and cultivated by you meditating in just such a way, will bring you to such a state that you will understand: ^sn46-30-5-2

‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’” ^sn46-30-5-3

### Skillful (1st) (Linked Discourses 46.31) ^sn46-31-0

“Mendicants, whatever qualities are skillful, part of the skillful, on the side of the skillful, all of them are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them. ^sn46-31-1-1

A mendicant who is diligent can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-31-1-2

And how does a diligent mendicant develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-31-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-31-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-31-2-3

That’s how a diligent mendicant develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-31-2-4

### Skillful (2nd) (Linked Discourses 46.32) ^sn46-32-0

“Mendicants, whatever qualities are skillful, part of the skillful, on the side of the skillful, all of them are rooted in rational application of mind and meet at rational application of mind, and rational application of mind is said to be the best of them. ^sn46-32-1-1

A mendicant accomplished in rational application of mind can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-32-1-2

And how does a mendicant with rational application of mind develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-32-2-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-32-2-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-32-2-3

That’s how a mendicant with rational application of mind develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-32-2-4

### Corruptions (Linked Discourses 46.33) ^sn46-33-0

“Mendicants, there are these five corruptions of native gold. When native gold is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working. ^sn46-33-1-1

What five? ^sn46-33-1-2

Iron, ^sn46-33-1-3

copper, ^sn46-33-1-4

tin, ^sn46-33-1-5

lead, ^sn46-33-1-6

and silver. ^sn46-33-1-7

When native gold is corrupted by these five corruptions it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working. ^sn46-33-1-8

In the same way, there are these five corruptions of the mind. When the mind is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant. It’s brittle, and not rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^sn46-33-2-1

What five? ^sn46-33-2-2

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^sn46-33-2-3

These are the five corruptions of the mind. When the mind is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant. It’s brittle, and not rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements.” ^sn46-33-2-4

### Not Corruptions (Linked Discourses 46.34) ^sn46-34-0

“Mendicants, these seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom. ^sn46-34-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-34-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-34-1-3

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-34-1-4

### Irrational Application of Mind (Linked Discourses 46.35) ^sn46-35-0

“Mendicants, when you apply the mind irrationally, sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt arise, and once arisen they increase and grow.” ^sn46-35-1-1

“Mendicants, when you apply the mind rationally, the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity arise, and once they’ve arisen, they’re fully developed.” ^sn46-35-2-1

### Growth (Linked Discourses 46.36) ^sn46-36-0

“Mendicants, when the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they lead to growth and progress. ^sn46-36-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-36-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-36-1-3
