When these seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they lead to growth and progress.” ^sn46-36-1-4

### Obstacles (Linked Discourses 46.37) ^sn46-37-0

“Mendicants, there are these five obstacles and hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^sn46-37-1-1

What five? ^sn46-37-1-2

Sensual desire, ^sn46-37-1-3

ill will, ^sn46-37-1-4

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn46-37-1-5

restlessness and remorse, ^sn46-37-1-6

and doubt. ^sn46-37-1-7

These are the five obstacles and hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^sn46-37-1-8

There are these seven awakening factors that are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom. ^sn46-37-2-1

What seven? ^sn46-37-2-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-37-2-3

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-37-2-4

### Without Obstacles (Linked Discourses 46.38) ^sn46-38-0

“Mendicants, sometimes a noble disciple pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens to the teaching. At such a time the five hindrances are absent, and the seven awakening factors are fully developed. ^sn46-38-1-1

What are the five hindrances that are absent? ^sn46-38-2-1

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^sn46-38-2-2

These are the five hindrances that are absent. ^sn46-38-2-3

And what are the seven awakening factors that are fully developed? ^sn46-38-3-1

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-38-3-2

These are the seven awakening factors that are fully developed. ^sn46-38-3-3

Sometimes a noble disciple pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens to the teaching. At such a time the five hindrances are absent, and the seven awakening factors are fully developed.” ^sn46-38-3-4

### Trees (Linked Discourses 46.39) ^sn46-39-0

“Mendicants, there are large trees with tiny seeds and big bodies, which grow up and around other trees as parasites. The trees they engulf break apart, collapse, and fall. ^sn46-39-1-1

And what are those large trees with tiny seeds and big bodies? ^sn46-39-1-2

The peepal, banyan, wavy leaf fig, cluster fig, Moreton Bay fig, and white fig. ^sn46-39-1-3

These are the large trees with tiny seeds and big bodies, which grow up and around other trees as parasites. The trees they engulf break apart, collapse, and fall. ^sn46-39-1-4

In the same way, take a certain gentleman who has gone forth from the lay life to homelessness, abandoning sensual pleasures. But beset by sensual pleasures that are similar, or even worse, he breaks apart, collapses, and falls. ^sn46-39-1-5

There are these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. ^sn46-39-2-1

What five? ^sn46-39-2-2

Sensual desire, ^sn46-39-2-3

ill will, ^sn46-39-2-4

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn46-39-2-5

restlessness and remorse, ^sn46-39-2-6

and doubt. ^sn46-39-2-7

These are the five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. ^sn46-39-2-8

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or parasites of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom. ^sn46-39-3-1

What seven? ^sn46-39-3-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-39-3-3

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or parasites of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-39-3-4

### Hindrances (Linked Discourses 46.40) ^sn46-40-0

“Mendicants, these five hindrances are destroyers of sight, vision, and knowledge. They block wisdom, they’re on the side of distress, and they don’t lead to extinguishment. ^sn46-40-1-1

What five? ^sn46-40-1-2

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^sn46-40-1-3

These five hindrances are destroyers of sight, vision, and knowledge. They block wisdom, they’re on the side of distress, and they don’t lead to extinguishment. ^sn46-40-1-8

These seven awakening factors are creators of vision and knowledge. They grow wisdom, they’re on the side of solace, and they lead to extinguishment. ^sn46-40-2-1

What seven? ^sn46-40-2-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-40-2-3

These seven awakening factors are creators of vision and knowledge. They grow wisdom, they’re on the side of solace, and they lead to extinguishment.” ^sn46-40-2-4

### Discriminations (Linked Discourses 46.41) ^sn46-41-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-41-1-1

“Mendicants, all the ascetics and brahmins in the past who have given up the three discriminations have done so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-41-1-2

All the ascetics and brahmins in the future who will give up the three discriminations will do so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-41-1-3

All the ascetics and brahmins in the present who are giving up the three discriminations do so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-41-1-4

What seven? ^sn46-41-1-5

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-41-1-6

All the ascetics and brahmins in the past … ^sn46-41-1-7

future … ^sn46-41-1-8

and present who give up the three discriminations do so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-41-1-9

### A Wheel-Turning Monarch (Linked Discourses 46.42) ^sn46-42-0

“Mendicants, when a Wheel-Turning Monarch appears seven treasures appear. ^sn46-42-1-1

What seven? ^sn46-42-1-2

The wheel, the elephant, the horse, the jewel, the woman, the householder, and the commander. ^sn46-42-1-3

When a Wheel-Turning Monarch appears these seven treasures appear. ^sn46-42-1-4

When a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha appears the seven treasures of the awakening factors appear. ^sn46-42-2-1

What seven? ^sn46-42-2-2

The treasures of the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-42-2-3

When a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha appears these seven treasures of the awakening factors appear.” ^sn46-42-2-4

### About Māra (Linked Discourses 46.43) ^sn46-43-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a path for crushing Māra’s army. ^sn46-43-1-1

Listen … ^sn46-43-1-2

And what is that path? ^sn46-43-1-3

It is the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-43-1-5

What seven? ^sn46-43-1-6

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-43-1-7

This is the path for crushing Māra’s army.” ^sn46-43-1-8

### Witless (Linked Discourses 46.44) ^sn46-44-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him, ^sn46-44-1-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘a witless idiot’. ^sn46-44-1-2

How is a witless idiot defined?” ^sn46-44-1-3

“Mendicant, they’re called a witless idiot because they haven’t developed and cultivated the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-44-1-4

What seven? ^sn46-44-1-5

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-44-1-6

They’re called a witless idiot because they haven’t developed and cultivated these seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-44-1-7

### Wise (Linked Discourses 46.45) ^sn46-45-0

“Sir, they speak of a one ‘wise and clever’. ^sn46-45-1-1

How is a person who is wise and clever defined?” ^sn46-45-1-2

“Mendicant, they’re called wise and clever because they’ve developed and cultivated the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-45-1-3

What seven? ^sn46-45-1-4

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-45-1-5

They’re called wise and clever because they’ve developed and cultivated these seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-45-1-6

### Poor (Linked Discourses 46.46) ^sn46-46-0

“Sir, they speak of someone who is ‘poor’. ^sn46-46-1-1

How is a poor person defined?” ^sn46-46-1-2

“Mendicant, they’re called poor because they haven’t developed and cultivated the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-46-1-3

What seven? ^sn46-46-1-4

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-46-1-5

They’re called poor because they haven’t developed and cultivated these seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-46-1-6

### Prosperous (Linked Discourses 46.47) ^sn46-47-0

“Sir, they speak of someone who is ‘prosperous’. ^sn46-47-1-1

How is a prosperous person defined?” ^sn46-47-1-2

“Mendicant, they’re called prosperous because they’ve developed and cultivated the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-47-1-3

What seven? ^sn46-47-1-4

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-47-1-5

They’re called prosperous because they’ve developed and cultivated these seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-47-1-6

### The Sun (Linked Discourses 46.48) ^sn46-48-0

“Mendicants, the dawn is the forerunner and harbinger of the sunrise. ^sn46-48-1-1

In the same way, for a mendicant good friendship is the forerunner and harbinger of the arising of the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-48-1-2

A mendicant with good friends can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-48-1-3

And how does a mendicant with good friends develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors? ^sn46-48-1-4

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-48-1-5

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-48-1-6

That’s how a mendicant with good friends develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors.” ^sn46-48-1-7

### Interior (Linked Discourses 46.49) ^sn46-49-0

“Taking into account interior factors, mendicants, I do not see a single one that gives rise to the seven awakening factors like rational application of mind. …” ^sn46-49-1-1

### Exterior (Linked Discourses 46.50) ^sn46-50-0

“Taking into account exterior factors, mendicants, I do not see a single one that gives rise to the seven awakening factors like good friendship. …” ^sn46-50-1-1

### Nourishing (Linked Discourses 46.51) ^sn46-51-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-51-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you what fuels and what starves the five hindrances and the seven awakening factors. ^sn46-51-1-2

Listen … ^sn46-51-1-3

And what fuels the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-51-1-4

There is the feature of beauty. ^sn46-51-1-5

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-1-6

fuels the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-51-1-7

And what fuels the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-51-2-1

There is the feature of aversion. ^sn46-51-2-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-2-3

fuels the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-51-2-4

And what fuels the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow? ^sn46-51-3-1

There is discontent, sloth, yawning, sleepiness after eating, and mental sluggishness. ^sn46-51-3-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-3-3

fuels the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow. ^sn46-51-3-4

And what fuels the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow? ^sn46-51-4-1

There is the unsettled mind. ^sn46-51-4-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-4-3

fuels the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, makes them increase and grow. ^sn46-51-4-4

And what fuels the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow? ^sn46-51-5-1

There are things that are grounds for doubt. ^sn46-51-5-2

Frequent irrational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-5-3

fuels the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow. ^sn46-51-5-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-6-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of mindfulness. ^sn46-51-6-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-6-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-6-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-7-1

There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^sn46-51-7-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-7-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-7-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-8-1

There are the elements of initiative, persistence, and exertion. ^sn46-51-8-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-8-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-8-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-9-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of rapture. ^sn46-51-9-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-9-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-9-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-10-1

There is tranquility of the body and of the mind. ^sn46-51-10-2

Frequent rational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-10-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-10-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-11-1

There are things that are the basis of serenity and freedom from distraction. ^sn46-51-11-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-11-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-11-4

And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it? ^sn46-51-12-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of equanimity. ^sn46-51-12-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-12-3

fuels the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it. ^sn46-51-12-4

And what starves the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth? ^sn46-51-13-1

There is the feature of ugliness. ^sn46-51-13-2

Frequent rational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-13-3

starves the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth. ^sn46-51-13-4

And what starves the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth? ^sn46-51-14-1

There is the heart’s release by love. ^sn46-51-14-2

Frequent rational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-14-3

starves the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth. ^sn46-51-14-4

And what starves the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, starves their increase and growth? ^sn46-51-15-1

There are the elements of initiative, persistence, and exertion. ^sn46-51-15-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-15-3

starves the arising of dullness and drowsiness, or, when they have arisen, starves their increase and growth. ^sn46-51-15-4

And what starves the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, starves their increase and growth? ^sn46-51-16-1

There is the settled mind. ^sn46-51-16-2

Frequent rational application of mind to that ^sn46-51-16-3

starves the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, starves their increase and growth. ^sn46-51-16-4

And what starves the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth? ^sn46-51-17-1

There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^sn46-51-17-2

Frequent rational application of mind to them ^sn46-51-17-3

starves the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, starves its increase and growth. ^sn46-51-17-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-18-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of mindfulness. ^sn46-51-18-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-18-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of mindfulness, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-18-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-19-1

There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^sn46-51-19-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-19-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of investigation of principles, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-19-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-20-1

There are the elements of initiative, persistence, and exertion. ^sn46-51-20-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-20-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-20-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-21-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of rapture. ^sn46-51-21-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-21-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-21-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-22-1

There is tranquility of the body and of the mind. ^sn46-51-22-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-22-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of tranquility, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-22-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-23-1

There are things that are the basis of serenity and freedom from distraction. ^sn46-51-23-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-23-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of immersion, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development. ^sn46-51-23-4

And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development? ^sn46-51-24-1

There are things that are grounds for the awakening factor of equanimity. ^sn46-51-24-2

Not frequently applying the mind to that ^sn46-51-24-3

starves the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development.” ^sn46-51-24-4

### Is There a Way? (Linked Discourses 46.52) ^sn46-52-0

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn46-52-1-1

Then it occurred to him, ^sn46-52-1-2

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī. ^sn46-52-1-3

Why don’t we visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^sn46-52-1-4

Then they went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^sn46-52-2-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. The wanderers said to them: ^sn46-52-2-2

“Reverends, the ascetic Gotama teaches his disciples like this: ^sn46-52-3-1

‘Mendicants, please give up the five hindrances—corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom—and truly develop the seven awakening factors.’ ^sn46-52-3-2

We too teach our disciples: ^sn46-52-3-3

‘Reverends, please give up the five hindrances—corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom—and truly develop the seven awakening factors.’ ^sn46-52-3-4

What, then, is the difference between the ascetic Gotama’s teaching and instruction and ours?” ^sn46-52-3-5

Those mendicants neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^sn46-52-4-1

They rose from their seat, thinking: ^sn46-52-4-2

“We will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^sn46-52-4-3

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn46-52-4-4

“Mendicants, when wanderers of other religions say this, you should say to them: ^sn46-52-8-1

‘But reverends, is there a way in which the five hindrances become ten and the seven awakening factors become fourteen?’ ^sn46-52-8-2

Questioned like this, the wanderers of other religions would be stumped, and, in addition, would get frustrated. ^sn46-52-8-3

Why is that? ^sn46-52-8-4

Because they’re out of their element. ^sn46-52-8-5

I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who could provide a satisfying answer to these questions except for the Realized One or his disciple or someone who has heard it from them. ^sn46-52-8-6

And what is the way in which the five hindrances become ten? ^sn46-52-9-1

Sensual desire for what is internal is a hindrance; and sensual desire for what is external is also a hindrance. ^sn46-52-9-2

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of sensual desire’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-9-3

Ill will for what is internal is a hindrance; and ill will for what is external is also a hindrance. ^sn46-52-9-4

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of ill will’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-9-5

Dullness is a hindrance; and drowsiness is also a hindrance. ^sn46-52-9-6

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of dullness and drowsiness’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-9-7

Restlessness is a hindrance; and remorse is also a hindrance. ^sn46-52-9-8

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of restlessness and remorse’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-9-9

Doubt about internal things is a hindrance; and doubt about external things is also a hindrance. ^sn46-52-9-10

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of doubt’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-9-11

This is the way in which the five hindrances become ten. ^sn46-52-9-12

And what is the way in which the seven awakening factors become fourteen? ^sn46-52-10-1

Mindfulness of internal things is the awakening factor of mindfulness; and mindfulness of external things is also the awakening factor of mindfulness. ^sn46-52-10-2

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of mindfulness’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-10-3

Investigating, exploring, and inquiring into internal things with wisdom is the awakening factor of investigation of principles; and investigating, exploring, and inquiring into external things with wisdom is also the awakening factor of investigation of principles. ^sn46-52-11-1

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of investigation of principles’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-11-2

Physical energy is the awakening factor of energy; and mental energy is also the awakening factor of energy. ^sn46-52-12-1

That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of energy’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-12-2

Rapture while placing the mind and keeping it connected is the awakening factor of rapture; and rapture without placing the mind and keeping it connected is also the awakening factor of rapture. ^sn46-52-13-1

In this way what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of rapture’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-13-2

Physical tranquility is the awakening factor of tranquility; and mental tranquility is also the awakening factor of tranquility. ^sn46-52-14-1

In this way what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of tranquility’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-14-2

Immersion while placing the mind and keeping it connected is the awakening factor of immersion; and immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected is also the awakening factor of immersion. ^sn46-52-15-1

In this way what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of immersion’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-15-2

Equanimity for internal things is the awakening factor of equanimity; and equanimity for external things is also the awakening factor of equanimity. ^sn46-52-16-1

In this way what is concisely referred to as ‘the awakening factor of equanimity’ becomes twofold. ^sn46-52-16-2

This is the way in which the seven awakening factors become fourteen.” ^sn46-52-16-3

### Fire (Linked Discourses 46.53) ^sn46-53-0

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^sn46-53-1-1

(The same as the previous discourse.) ^sn46-53-1-2

“Mendicants, when wanderers of other religions say this, you should say to them: ^sn46-53-2-1

‘Reverends, which awakening factors should not be developed when the mind is sluggish? And which awakening factors should be developed at that time? ^sn46-53-2-2

Which awakening factors should not be developed when the mind is restless? And which awakening factors should be developed at that time?’ ^sn46-53-2-3

Questioned like this, the wanderers of other religions would be stumped, and, in addition, would get frustrated. ^sn46-53-2-4

Why is that? ^sn46-53-2-5

Because they’re out of their element. ^sn46-53-2-6

I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who could provide a satisfying answer to these questions except for the Realized One or his disciple or someone who has heard it from them. ^sn46-53-3-1

When the mind is sluggish, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-53-4-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-4-2

Because it’s hard to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things. ^sn46-53-4-3

Suppose someone wanted to make a small fire flare up. ^sn46-53-5-1

If they toss wet grass, cow-dung, and timber on it, spray it with water, and scatter dirt on it, ^sn46-53-5-2

could they make it flare up?” ^sn46-53-5-3

“No, sir.” ^sn46-53-5-4

“In the same way, when the mind is sluggish, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-53-6-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-6-2

Because it’s hard to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things. ^sn46-53-6-3

When the mind is sluggish, it’s the right time to develop the awakening factors of investigation of principles, energy, and rapture. ^sn46-53-7-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-7-2

Because it’s easy to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things. ^sn46-53-7-3

Suppose someone wanted to make a small fire flare up. ^sn46-53-8-1

If they toss dry grass, cow-dung, and timber on it, blow on it, and don’t scatter dirt on it, ^sn46-53-8-2

could they make it flare up?” ^sn46-53-8-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn46-53-8-4

“In the same way, when the mind is sluggish, it’s the right time to develop the awakening factors of investigation of principles, energy, and rapture. ^sn46-53-9-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-9-2

Because it’s easy to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things. ^sn46-53-9-3

When the mind is restless, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of investigation of principles, energy, and rapture. ^sn46-53-10-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-10-2

Because it’s hard to settle a restless mind with these things. ^sn46-53-10-3

Suppose someone wanted to extinguish a great mass of fire. ^sn46-53-11-1

If they toss dry grass, cow-dung, and timber on it, blow on it, and don’t scatter dirt on it, ^sn46-53-11-2

could they extinguish it?” ^sn46-53-11-3

“No, sir.” ^sn46-53-11-4

“In the same way, when the mind is restless, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of investigation of principles, energy, and rapture. ^sn46-53-12-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-12-2

Because it’s hard to settle a restless mind with these things. ^sn46-53-12-3

When the mind is restless, it’s the right time to develop the awakening factors of tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-53-13-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-13-2

Because it’s easy to settle a restless mind with these things. ^sn46-53-13-3

Suppose someone wanted to extinguish a great mass of fire. ^sn46-53-14-1

If they toss wet grass, cow-dung, and timber on it, spray it with water, and scatter dirt on it, ^sn46-53-14-2

could they extinguish it?” ^sn46-53-14-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn46-53-14-4

“In the same way, when the mind is restless, it’s the right time to develop the awakening factors of tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-53-15-1

Why is that? ^sn46-53-15-2

Because it’s easy to settle a restless mind with these things. ^sn46-53-15-3

But mindfulness is always useful, I say.” ^sn46-53-15-4

### Full of Love (Linked Discourses 46.54) ^sn46-54-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Haliddavasana. ^sn46-54-1-1

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Haliddavasana for alms. ^sn46-54-1-2

Then it occurred to them, ^sn46-54-1-3

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Haliddavasana. ^sn46-54-1-4

Why don’t we visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^sn46-54-1-5

Then they went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^sn46-54-2-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. The wanderers said to them: ^sn46-54-2-2

“Reverends, the ascetic Gotama teaches his disciples like this: ^sn46-54-3-1

‘Come, mendicants, give up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom, and meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn46-54-3-2

Meditate spreading a heart full of compassion to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, spread a heart full of compassion to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn46-54-3-3

Meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, spread a heart full of rejoicing to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^sn46-54-3-4

Meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.’ ^sn46-54-3-5

We too teach our disciples in just the same way. ^sn46-54-4-1

What, then, is the difference between the ascetic Gotama’s teaching and instruction and ours?” ^sn46-54-4-7

Those mendicants neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^sn46-54-5-1

They rose from their seat, thinking: ^sn46-54-5-2

“We will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^sn46-54-5-3

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^sn46-54-5-4

“Mendicants, when wanderers of other religions say this, you should say to them: ^sn46-54-11-1

‘But reverends, how is the heart’s release by love developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and culmination? ^sn46-54-11-2

How is the heart’s release by compassion developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-11-3

How is the heart’s release by rejoicing developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-11-4

How is the heart’s release by equanimity developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end?’ ^sn46-54-11-5

Questioned like this, the wanderers of other religions would be stumped, and, in addition, would get frustrated. ^sn46-54-11-6

Why is that? ^sn46-54-11-7

Because they’re out of their element. ^sn46-54-11-8

I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who could provide a satisfying answer to these questions except for the Realized One or his disciple or someone who has heard it from them. ^sn46-54-11-9

And how is the heart’s release by love developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-12-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the heart’s release by love together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-54-12-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-54-12-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-12-4

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-12-5

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive and the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-12-6

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-12-7

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-12-8

The apex of the heart’s release by love is the beautiful, I say, for a wise mendicant here who has not penetrated to a higher freedom. ^sn46-54-12-9

And how is the heart’s release by compassion developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-13-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the heart’s release by compassion together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-54-13-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-54-13-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. … ^sn46-54-13-4

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-13-5

Or else, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space. ^sn46-54-13-6

The apex of the heart’s release by compassion is the dimension of infinite space, I say, for a wise mendicant here who has not penetrated to a higher freedom. ^sn46-54-13-7

And how is the heart’s release by rejoicing developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-14-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the heart’s release by rejoicing together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-54-14-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-54-14-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. … ^sn46-54-14-4

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-14-5

Or else, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^sn46-54-14-6

The apex of the heart’s release by rejoicing is the dimension of infinite consciousness, I say, for a wise mendicant here who has not penetrated to a higher freedom. ^sn46-54-14-7

And how is the heart’s release by equanimity developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end? ^sn46-54-15-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the heart’s release by equanimity together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-54-15-2

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-54-15-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-15-4

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-15-5

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive and the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-15-6

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-15-7

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn46-54-15-8

Or else, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. ^sn46-54-15-9

The apex of the heart’s release by equanimity is the dimension of nothingness, I say, for a wise mendicant here who has not penetrated to a higher freedom.” ^sn46-54-15-10

### With Saṅgārava (Linked Discourses 46.55) ^sn46-55-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-55-1-1

Then Saṅgārava the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn46-55-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^sn46-55-1-3

“What is the cause, Mister Gotama, what is the reason why sometimes even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed? ^sn46-55-2-1

And why is it that sometimes even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed?” ^sn46-55-2-2

“Brahmin, there’s a time when your heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire and you don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. At that time you don’t truly know or see what is good for yourself, good for another, or good for both. Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-3-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was mixed with dye such as red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. ^sn46-55-4-1

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^sn46-55-4-2

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-4-3

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-5-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was heated by fire, boiling and bubbling. ^sn46-55-6-1

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^sn46-55-6-2

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-6-3

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-7-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water enveloped with moss and aquatic plants. ^sn46-55-8-1

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^sn46-55-8-2

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-8-3

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-9-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water stirred by the wind, churning, swirling, and rippling. ^sn46-55-10-1

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^sn46-55-10-2

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-10-3

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in doubt … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-11-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was cloudy, murky, and muddy, hidden in the darkness. ^sn46-55-12-1

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^sn46-55-12-2

In the same way, there’s a time when your heart is overcome and mired in doubt and you don’t truly understand the escape from doubt that has arisen. At that time you don’t truly know or see what is good for yourself, good for another, or good for both. Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-12-3

This is the cause, brahmin, this is the reason why sometimes even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^sn46-55-12-4

There’s a time when your heart is not overcome and mired in sensual desire and you truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. At that time you truly know and see what is good for yourself, good for another, and good for both. Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-13-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was not mixed with dye such as red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. ^sn46-55-14-1

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^sn46-55-14-2

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in sensual desire … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^sn46-55-14-3

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-15-1

Suppose there is a bowl of water that is not heated by a fire, boiling and bubbling. ^sn46-55-16-1

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^sn46-55-16-2

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-16-3

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-17-1

Suppose there is a bowl of water that is not enveloped with moss and aquatic plants. ^sn46-55-18-1

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^sn46-55-18-2

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-18-3

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-19-1

Suppose there is a bowl of water that is not stirred by the wind, churning, swirling, and rippling. ^sn46-55-20-1

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^sn46-55-20-2

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-20-3

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in doubt … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-21-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was transparent, clear, and unclouded, brought into the light. ^sn46-55-22-1

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^sn46-55-22-2

In the same way, there’s a time when your heart is not overcome and mired in doubt and you truly understand the escape from doubt that has arisen. At that time you truly know and see what is good for yourself, good for another, and good for both. Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-22-3

This is the cause, brahmin, this is the reason why sometimes even hymns that are long-unrehearsed do spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^sn46-55-22-4

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom. ^sn46-55-23-1

What seven? ^sn46-55-23-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^sn46-55-23-3

These seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom.” ^sn46-55-23-4

When he said this, Saṅgārava said to the Buddha, ^sn46-55-24-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! … ^sn46-55-24-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^sn46-55-24-3

### With Prince Abhaya (Linked Discourses 46.56) ^sn46-56-0

So I have heard. ^sn46-56-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^sn46-56-1-2

Then Prince Abhaya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn46-56-1-3

“Sir, Pūraṇa Kassapa says this: ^sn46-56-1-4

‘There is no cause or reason for not knowing and not seeing. ^sn46-56-1-5

Not knowing and not seeing have no cause or reason. ^sn46-56-1-6

There is no cause or reason for knowing and seeing. ^sn46-56-1-7

Knowing and seeing have no cause or reason.’ ^sn46-56-1-8

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^sn46-56-1-9

“Prince, there are causes and reasons for not knowing and not seeing. ^sn46-56-1-10

Not knowing and not seeing have causes and reasons. ^sn46-56-1-11

There are causes and reasons for knowing and seeing. ^sn46-56-1-12

Knowing and seeing have causes and reasons.” ^sn46-56-1-13

“But sir, what is the cause and reason for not knowing and not seeing? ^sn46-56-2-1

How do not knowing and not seeing have causes and reasons?” ^sn46-56-2-2

“There’s a time when the heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, without truly knowing and seeing the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. ^sn46-56-2-3

This is a cause and reason for not knowing and not seeing. ^sn46-56-2-4

And this is how not knowing and not seeing have causes and reasons. ^sn46-56-2-5

Furthermore, there’s a time when the heart is overcome and mired in ill will … ^sn46-56-3-1

dullness and drowsiness … ^sn46-56-3-2

restlessness and remorse … ^sn46-56-3-3

doubt, without truly knowing and seeing the escape from doubt that has arisen. ^sn46-56-3-4

This is a cause and reason for not knowing and not seeing. ^sn46-56-3-5

And this is how not knowing and not seeing have causes and reasons.” ^sn46-56-3-6

“Sir, what is the name of this exposition of the teaching?” ^sn46-56-4-1

“These are called the ‘hindrances’, prince.” ^sn46-56-4-2

“Indeed, Blessed One, these are hindrances! ^sn46-56-4-3

Indeed, Holy One, these are hindrances! ^sn46-56-4-4

Overcome by even a single hindrance you wouldn’t truly know or see, let alone all five hindrances. ^sn46-56-4-5

But sir, what is the cause and reason for knowing and seeing? ^sn46-56-5-1

How do knowing and seeing have causes and reasons?” ^sn46-56-5-2

“It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go. ^sn46-56-5-3

They truly know and see with a mind that has developed the awakening factor of mindfulness. ^sn46-56-5-4

This is a cause and reason for knowing and seeing. ^sn46-56-5-5

And this is how knowing and seeing have causes and reasons. ^sn46-56-5-6

Furthermore, a mendicant develops the awakening factor of investigation of principles … energy … rapture … tranquility … immersion … ^sn46-56-6-1

equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-56-6-2

They truly know and see with a mind that has developed the awakening factor of equanimity. ^sn46-56-6-3

This is a cause and reason for knowing and seeing. ^sn46-56-6-4

And this is how knowing and seeing have causes and reasons.” ^sn46-56-6-5

“Sir, what is the name of this exposition of the teaching?” ^sn46-56-7-1

“These are called the ‘awakening factors’, prince.” ^sn46-56-7-2

“Indeed, Blessed One, these are awakening factors! ^sn46-56-7-3

Indeed, Holy One, these are awakening factors! ^sn46-56-7-4

Endowed with even a single awakening factor you would truly know and see, let alone all seven awakening factors. ^sn46-56-7-5

When climbing Mount Vulture’s Peak I became fatigued in body and mind. But this has now faded away. And I’ve comprehended the teaching.” ^sn46-56-7-6

### A Skeleton (Linked Discourses 46.57) ^sn46-57-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-57-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn46-57-1-2

How so? ^sn46-57-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of a skeleton together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-57-1-4

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-57-1-5

That’s how the perception of a skeleton, when developed and cultivated, is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^sn46-57-1-6

“When the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated you can expect one of two results: enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return. ^sn46-57-2-1

How so?…” ^sn46-57-2-2

“Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to great benefit. ^sn46-57-3-1

How so?…” ^sn46-57-3-2

“Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to great sanctuary from the yoke. ^sn46-57-4-1

How so?…” ^sn46-57-4-2

“Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to great inspiration. ^sn46-57-5-1

How so?…” ^sn46-57-5-2

“Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to dwelling in great ease. ^sn46-57-6-1

How so?…” ^sn46-57-6-2

“Mendicants, when love is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-62-1-1

“Mendicants, when equanimity is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-65-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-71-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of suffering in impermanence is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-72-1-1

### Not-Self in Suffering (Linked Discourses 46.73) ^sn46-73-0

“Mendicants, when the perception of not-self in suffering is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-73-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of giving up is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-74-1-1

“Mendicants, when the perception of dispassion is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …” ^sn46-75-1-1

### Cessation (Linked Discourses 46.76) ^sn46-76-0

“Mendicants, when the perception of cessation is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn46-76-1-1

How so? ^sn46-76-1-2

It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of cessation together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-76-1-3

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-76-1-4

That’s how, when the perception of cessation is developed and cultivated, it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn46-76-1-5

When the perception of cessation is developed and cultivated you can expect one of two results: ^sn46-76-2-1

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return. ^sn46-76-2-2

How so? ^sn46-76-2-3

It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of cessation together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-76-2-5

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-76-2-6

When the perception of cessation is developed and cultivated in this way you can expect one of two results: ^sn46-76-2-7

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return.” ^sn46-76-2-8

“The perception of cessation, when developed and cultivated, leads to great benefit … great sanctuary from the yoke … great inspiration … great ease. ^sn46-76-3-1

How so? ^sn46-76-3-2

It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of cessation together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-76-3-3

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-76-3-4

That’s how the perception of cessation, when developed and cultivated, leads to great benefit … great sanctuary from the yoke … great inspiration … great ease.” ^sn46-76-3-5

### The Ganges River, Etc. (Linked Discourses 46.77–88) ^sn46-77-88-0

“Mendicants, the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn46-77-88-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn46-77-88-1-2

And how does a mendicant who develops the seven awakening factors slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment? ^sn46-77-88-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-77-88-1-4

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn46-77-88-1-5

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the seven awakening factors slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.” ^sn46-77-88-1-6

(Tell in full for each of the different rivers as in SN 45.91–102.) ^sn46-77-88-1-7

### A Realized One, Etc. (Linked Discourses 46.89–98) ^sn46-89-98-0

“Mendicants, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, is said to be the best of all sentient beings—be they footless, with two feet, four feet, or many feet …” ^sn46-89-98-1-1

(Tell in full as in SN 45.139–148.) ^sn46-89-98-3-1

### Hard Work, Etc. (Linked Discourses 46.99–110) ^sn46-99-110-0

“Mendicants, all the hard work that gets done depends on the earth and is grounded on the earth. …” ^sn46-99-110-1-1

(Tell in full as in SN 45.149–160.) ^sn46-99-110-3-1

### Searches, Etc. (Linked Discourses 46.111–120) ^sn46-111-120-0

“Mendicants, there are these three searches. ^sn46-111-120-1-1

What three? ^sn46-111-120-1-2

The search for sensual pleasures, the search for continued existence, and the search for a spiritual path. …” ^sn46-111-120-1-3

(Tell in full as in SN 45.161–170.) ^sn46-111-120-3-1

### Floods (Linked Discourses 46.121–129) ^sn46-121-129-0

“Mendicants, there are these four floods. ^sn46-121-129-1-1

What four? ^sn46-121-129-1-2

The floods of sensuality, desire to be reborn, views, and ignorance.” ^sn46-121-129-1-3

(Tell in full as in SN 45.171–179.) ^sn46-121-129-1-4

### Higher Fetters (Linked Discourses 46.130) ^sn46-130-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn46-130-1-1

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^sn46-130-1-2

What five? ^sn46-130-1-3

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^sn46-130-1-4

These are the five higher fetters. ^sn46-130-1-5

The seven awakening factors should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters. ^sn46-130-1-6

What seven? ^sn46-130-1-7

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn46-130-1-8

and equanimity, which culminate in the removal of greed, hate, and delusion. …” ^sn46-130-1-9

“… which have freedom from death as their objective, destination, and culmination …” ^sn46-130-1-10

“… which slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment … ^sn46-130-1-11

The seven awakening factors should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters.” ^sn46-130-1-12

### More on the Ganges River, Etc. (Linked Discourses 46.131–142) ^sn46-131-142-0

(Tell in full as in SN 45.103–114, removal of greed version.) ^sn46-131-142-3-1
