right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn51-30-5-3

This is called the practice that leads to the development of the bases of psychic power.” ^sn51-30-5-4

### About Moggallāna (Linked Discourses 51.31) ^sn51-31-0

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn51-31-1-1

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn51-31-1-2

What things has the mendicant Moggallāna developed and cultivated so as to have such power and might?” ^sn51-31-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. …” ^sn51-31-2-1

“The mendicant Moggallāna has become so powerful and mighty by developing and cultivating the four bases of psychic power. ^sn51-31-2-2

What four? ^sn51-31-3-1

Moggallāna develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^sn51-31-3-2

He thinks: ‘My enthusiasm won’t be too lax or too tense. And it’ll be neither constricted internally nor scattered externally.’ ^sn51-31-3-3

And he meditates perceiving before and behind: ^sn51-31-3-4

as before, so behind; as behind, so before; ^sn51-31-3-5

as below, so above; as above, so below; ^sn51-31-3-6

as by day, so by night; as by night, so by day. ^sn51-31-3-7

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, he develops a mind that’s full of radiance. ^sn51-31-3-8

He develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy … ^sn51-31-3-9

mental development … ^sn51-31-3-10

inquiry, and active effort. ^sn51-31-3-11

He thinks: ‘My inquiry won’t be too lax or too tense. And it’ll be neither constricted internally nor scattered externally.’ … ^sn51-31-3-12

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, he develops a mind that’s full of radiance. ^sn51-31-3-13

The mendicant Moggallāna has become so powerful and mighty by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power. ^sn51-31-3-14

And by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power, the mendicant Moggallāna wields the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying himself and becoming one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^sn51-31-4-1

And by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power, the mendicant Moggallāna realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And he lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^sn51-31-4-2

### The Realized One (Linked Discourses 51.32) ^sn51-32-0

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn51-32-1-1

“What do you think, mendicants? ^sn51-32-1-2

What things has the Realized One developed and cultivated so as to have such power and might?” ^sn51-32-1-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn51-32-2-1

“The Realized One has become so powerful and mighty by developing and cultivating the four bases of psychic power. ^sn51-32-2-2

What four? ^sn51-32-3-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^sn51-32-3-2

He thinks: ‘My enthusiasm won’t be too lax or too tense. And it’ll be neither constricted internally nor scattered externally.’ ^sn51-32-3-3

And he meditates perceiving before and behind: ^sn51-32-3-4

as before, so behind; as behind, so before; ^sn51-32-3-5

as below, so above; as above, so below; ^sn51-32-3-6

as by day, so by night; as by night, so by day. ^sn51-32-3-7

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, he develops a mind that’s full of radiance. ^sn51-32-3-8

He develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy … ^sn51-32-3-9

mental development … ^sn51-32-3-10

inquiry, and active effort. ^sn51-32-3-11

He thinks: ‘My inquiry won’t be too lax or too tense. And it’ll be neither constricted internally nor scattered externally.’ ^sn51-32-3-12

And he meditates perceiving before and behind: ^sn51-32-3-13

as before, so behind; as behind, so before; ^sn51-32-3-14

as below, so above; as above, so below; ^sn51-32-3-15

as by day, so by night; as by night, so by day. ^sn51-32-3-16

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, he develops a mind that’s full of radiance. ^sn51-32-3-17

The Realized One has become so powerful and mighty by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power. ^sn51-32-3-18

And by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power, the Realized One wields the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying himself and becoming one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^sn51-32-4-1

And by developing and cultivating these four bases of psychic power, the Realized One realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And he lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^sn51-32-4-2

(The six direct knowledges should be told in full.) ^sn51-32-4-4

### The Ganges River, Etc. (Linked Discourses 51.33–44) ^sn51-33-44-0

“Mendicants, the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn51-33-44-1-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the four bases of psychic power slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn51-33-44-1-2

And how does a mendicant who develops the four bases of psychic power slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment? ^sn51-33-44-1-3

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm … ^sn51-33-44-1-4

energy … ^sn51-33-44-1-5

mental development … ^sn51-33-44-1-6

inquiry, and active effort. ^sn51-33-44-1-7

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the four bases of psychic power slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.” ^sn51-33-44-2-1

Six on slanting to the east, ^sn51-33-44-3-1

and six on slanting to the ocean; ^sn51-33-44-3-2

these two sixes make twelve, ^sn51-33-44-3-3

and that’s how this chapter is recited. ^sn51-33-44-3-4

### Floods, Etc. (Linked Discourses 51.77–86) ^sn51-77-86-0

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^sn51-77-86-1-1

What five? ^sn51-77-86-1-2

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^sn51-77-86-1-3

These are the five higher fetters. ^sn51-77-86-1-4

The four bases of psychic power should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters. ^sn51-77-86-1-5

What four? ^sn51-77-86-1-6

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm … ^sn51-77-86-1-7

energy … ^sn51-77-86-1-8

mental development … ^sn51-77-86-1-9

inquiry, and active effort. ^sn51-77-86-1-10

These four bases of psychic power should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters.” ^sn51-77-86-1-11

(Tell in full as in the Linked Discourses on the Path at SN 45.171–180.) ^sn51-77-86-1-12

Floods, yokes, grasping, ^sn51-77-86-2-1

ties, and underlying tendencies, ^sn51-77-86-2-2

kinds of sensual stimulation, hindrances, ^sn51-77-86-2-3

aggregates, and fetters high and low. ^sn51-77-86-2-4

The Linked Discourses on the Bases of Psychic Power, the seventh section. ^sn51-77-86-2-5

## Saṁyutta 52 ^sn52-0

### In Private (1st) (Linked Discourses 52.1) ^sn52-1-0

So I have heard. ^sn52-1-1-1

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn52-1-1-2

Then as Anuruddha was in private retreat this thought came to his mind: ^sn52-1-1-3

“Whoever has missed out on these four kinds of mindfulness meditation has missed out on the noble path to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn52-1-1-4

Whoever has undertaken these four kinds of mindfulness meditation has undertaken the noble path to the complete ending of suffering.” ^sn52-1-1-5

Then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna knew what Venerable Anuruddha was thinking. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he reappeared in front of Anuruddha, ^sn52-1-2-1

and said to him: ^sn52-1-2-2

“Reverend Anuruddha, how do you define the undertaking of the four kinds of mindfulness meditation by a mendicant?” ^sn52-1-2-3

“Reverend, it’s when a mendicant, with respect to the body internally, meditates observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-1-3-1

With respect to the body externally, they meditate observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-1-3-2

With respect to the body internally and externally, they meditate observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-1-3-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-4-1

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-4-2

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive and the repulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-4-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-4-4

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-4-5

With respect to feelings internally … ^sn52-1-5-1

externally … ^sn52-1-5-2

internally and externally, they meditate observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish … ^sn52-1-5-3

With respect to the mind internally … ^sn52-1-7-1

externally … ^sn52-1-7-2

internally and externally, they meditate observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish … ^sn52-1-7-3

With respect to principles internally … ^sn52-1-9-1

externally … ^sn52-1-9-2

internally and externally, they meditate observing the liability to originate, to vanish, and to originate and vanish … ^sn52-1-9-3

If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. … ^sn52-1-10-1

If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, ignoring both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. ^sn52-1-10-2

That’s how to define the undertaking of the four kinds of mindfulness meditation by a mendicant.” ^sn52-1-10-3

### In Private (2nd) (Linked Discourses 52.2) ^sn52-2-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn52-2-1-1

Then as Anuruddha was in private retreat this thought came to his mind: ^sn52-2-1-2

“Whoever has missed out on these four kinds of mindfulness meditation has missed out on the noble path to the complete ending of suffering. ^sn52-2-1-3

Whoever has undertaken these four kinds of mindfulness meditation has undertaken the noble path to the complete ending of suffering.” ^sn52-2-1-4

Then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna knew what Venerable Anuruddha was thinking. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he reappeared in front of Anuruddha and said to him: ^sn52-2-2-1

“Reverend Anuruddha, how do you define the undertaking of the four kinds of mindfulness meditation by a mendicant?” ^sn52-2-3-1

“Reverend, it’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body internally—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-2-4-1

They meditate observing an aspect of the body externally … ^sn52-2-4-2

internally and externally—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-2-4-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings internally … ^sn52-2-5-1

externally … ^sn52-2-5-2

internally and externally … ^sn52-2-5-3

They meditate observing an aspect of the mind internally … ^sn52-2-6-1

externally … ^sn52-2-6-2

internally and externally … ^sn52-2-6-3

They meditate observing an aspect of principles internally … ^sn52-2-7-1

externally … ^sn52-2-7-2

internally and externally—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-2-7-3

That’s how to define the undertaking of the four kinds of mindfulness meditation by a mendicant.” ^sn52-2-7-4

### On the Bank of the Sutanu (Linked Discourses 52.3) ^sn52-3-0

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī on the bank of the Sutanu. ^sn52-3-1-1

Then several mendicants went up to Venerable Anuruddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn52-3-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn52-3-1-3

“What things has Venerable Anuruddha developed and cultivated to attain great direct knowledge?” ^sn52-3-1-4

“Reverends, I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-3-2-1

What four? ^sn52-3-2-2

I meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-3-2-3

I meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn52-3-2-4

mind … ^sn52-3-2-5

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-3-2-6

I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-3-2-7

And it was by developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I directly knew the lower principle as lower, the middle principle as middle, and the higher principle as higher.” ^sn52-3-2-8

### At Thorny Wood (1st) (Linked Discourses 52.4) ^sn52-4-0

At one time the venerables Anuruddha, Sāriputta, and Mahāmoggallāna were staying near Sāketa, in the Thorny Wood. ^sn52-4-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Sāriputta and Mahāmoggallāna came out of retreat, went to Anuruddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn52-4-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. Sāriputta said to Anuruddha: ^sn52-4-1-3

“Reverend Anuruddha, what things should a trainee mendicant enter and remain in?” ^sn52-4-1-4

“Reverend Sāriputta, a trainee mendicant should enter and remain in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-4-2-1

What four? ^sn52-4-2-2

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-4-2-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn52-4-2-4

mind … ^sn52-4-2-5

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-4-2-6

A trainee mendicant should enter and remain in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^sn52-4-2-7

### At Thorny Wood (2nd) (Linked Discourses 52.5) ^sn52-5-0

At Sāketa. ^sn52-5-1-1

Sāriputta said to Anuruddha: ^sn52-5-1-2

“Reverend Anuruddha, what things should a mendicant who is an adept enter and remain in?” ^sn52-5-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, a mendicant who is an adept should enter and remain in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-5-1-4

What four? ^sn52-5-1-5

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-5-1-6

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn52-5-1-7

mind … ^sn52-5-1-8

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-5-1-9

A mendicant who is an adept should enter and remain in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^sn52-5-1-10

### At Thorny Wood (3rd) (Linked Discourses 52.6) ^sn52-6-0

At Sāketa. ^sn52-6-1-1

Sāriputta said to Anuruddha: ^sn52-6-1-2

“What things has Venerable Anuruddha developed and cultivated to attain great direct knowledge?” ^sn52-6-1-3

“Reverend, I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-6-1-4

What four? ^sn52-6-1-5

I meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-6-1-6

I meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn52-6-1-7

mind … ^sn52-6-1-8

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-6-1-9

I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-6-1-10

And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I directly know the thousandfold galaxy.” ^sn52-6-1-11

### The Ending of Craving (Linked Discourses 52.7) ^sn52-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn52-7-1-1

There Venerable Anuruddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn52-7-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^sn52-7-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^sn52-7-1-4

Anuruddha said this: ^sn52-7-1-5

“Reverends, when these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to the ending of craving. ^sn52-7-2-1

What four? ^sn52-7-2-2

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body … ^sn52-7-2-3

feelings … ^sn52-7-2-4

mind … ^sn52-7-2-5

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-7-2-6

When these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to the ending of craving.” ^sn52-7-2-7

### The Salaḷa Tree Hut (Linked Discourses 52.8) ^sn52-8-0

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the <i lang='pi' translate='no'>salaḷa</i> tree hut. ^sn52-8-1-1

There Venerable Anuruddha addressed the mendicants: ^sn52-8-1-2

“Reverends, suppose that, although the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east, ^sn52-8-1-3

a large crowd were to come along with a spade and basket, saying: ^sn52-8-1-4

‘We’ll make this Ganges river slant, slope, and incline to the west!’ ^sn52-8-1-5

What do you think, reverends? Would they succeed?” ^sn52-8-1-6

“No, reverend. ^sn52-8-1-7

Why is that? ^sn52-8-1-8

The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn52-8-1-9

It’s not easy to make it slant, slope, and incline to the west. ^sn52-8-1-10

That large crowd will eventually get weary and frustrated.” ^sn52-8-1-11

“In the same way, while a mendicant develops and cultivates the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, if rulers or their chief ministers, friends or colleagues, relatives or family should invite them to accept wealth, saying: ^sn52-8-2-1

‘Please, my man, why let these ocher robes torment you? ^sn52-8-2-2

Why follow the practice of shaving your head and carrying an alms bowl? ^sn52-8-2-3

Come, return to a lesser life, enjoy wealth, and make merit!’ ^sn52-8-2-4

It is quite impossible for a mendicant who is developing and cultivating the four kinds of mindfulness meditation to resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^sn52-8-3-1

Why is that? ^sn52-8-3-2

Because for a long time that mendicant’s mind has slanted, sloped, and inclined to seclusion. So it’s impossible for them to return to a lesser life. ^sn52-8-3-3

And how does a mendicant develop and cultivate the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^sn52-8-3-4

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body … ^sn52-8-3-5

feelings … ^sn52-8-3-6

mind … ^sn52-8-3-7

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-8-3-8

That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^sn52-8-3-9

### In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove (Linked Discourses 52.9) ^sn52-9-0

At one time the venerables Anuruddha and Sāriputta were staying near Vesālī, in Ambapālī’s Mango Grove. ^sn52-9-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Anuruddha, and said to him: ^sn52-9-1-2

“Reverend Anuruddha, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright. ^sn52-9-2-1

What kind of meditation are you usually practicing these days?” ^sn52-9-2-2

“These days, reverend, I usually meditate with my mind firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-9-2-3

What four? ^sn52-9-2-4

I meditate observing an aspect of the body … ^sn52-9-2-5

feelings … ^sn52-9-2-6

mind … ^sn52-9-2-7

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-9-2-8

These days I usually meditate with my mind firmly established in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-9-2-9

A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—usually meditates with their mind firmly established in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^sn52-9-2-10

“We’re so fortunate, reverend, so very fortunate, ^sn52-9-3-1

to have heard such a dramatic statement in the presence of Venerable Anuruddha.” ^sn52-9-3-2

### Gravely Ill (Linked Discourses 52.10) ^sn52-10-0

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the Dark Forest. And he was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn52-10-1-1

Then several mendicants went up to Venerable Anuruddha, and said to him: ^sn52-10-1-2

“What meditation does Venerable Anuruddha practice so that physical pain doesn’t occupy his mind?” ^sn52-10-2-1

“Reverends, I meditate with my mind firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation so that physical pain doesn’t occupy my mind. ^sn52-10-2-2

What four? ^sn52-10-2-3

I meditate observing an aspect of the body … ^sn52-10-2-4

feelings … ^sn52-10-2-5

mind … ^sn52-10-2-6

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-10-2-7

I meditate with my mind firmly established in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation so that physical pain doesn’t occupy my mind.” ^sn52-10-2-8

### A Thousand Eons (Linked Discourses 52.11) ^sn52-11-0

At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn52-11-1-1

Then several mendicants went up to Venerable Anuruddha, exchanged greetings with him … and said: ^sn52-11-1-2

“What things has Venerable Anuruddha developed and cultivated to attain great direct knowledge?” ^sn52-11-2-1

“Reverends, I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-11-2-2

What four? ^sn52-11-2-3

I meditate observing an aspect of the body … ^sn52-11-2-4

feelings … ^sn52-11-2-5

mind … ^sn52-11-2-6

principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn52-11-2-7

I attained great direct knowledge by developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn52-11-2-8

And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I recollect a thousand eons.” ^sn52-11-2-9

### Psychic Powers (Linked Discourses 52.12) ^sn52-12-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.” ^sn52-12-1-1

### Clairaudience (Linked Discourses 52.13) ^sn52-13-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that, with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.” ^sn52-13-1-1

### Encompassing the Mind (Linked Discourses 52.14) ^sn52-14-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^sn52-14-1-1

I understand mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’ … ^sn52-14-1-2

I understand unfreed mind as ‘unfreed mind’.” ^sn52-14-1-3

### Possible (Linked Discourses 52.15) ^sn52-15-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand the possible as possible and the impossible as impossible.” ^sn52-15-1-1

### The Results of Deeds Undertaken (Linked Discourses 52.16) ^sn52-16-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes.” ^sn52-16-1-1

### Where All Paths of Practice Lead (Linked Discourses 52.17) ^sn52-17-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand where all paths of practice lead.” ^sn52-17-1-1

### Diverse Elements (Linked Discourses 52.18) ^sn52-18-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand the world with its many and diverse elements.” ^sn52-18-1-1

### Diverse Beliefs (Linked Discourses 52.19) ^sn52-19-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand the diverse convictions of sentient beings.” ^sn52-19-1-1

### Comprehending the Faculties of Others (Linked Discourses 52.20) ^sn52-20-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand the faculties of other sentient beings and other individuals after encompassing them with my mind.” ^sn52-20-1-1

### Absorptions, Etc. (Linked Discourses 52.21) ^sn52-21-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I truly understand corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments.” ^sn52-21-1-1

### Past Lives (Linked Discourses 52.22) ^sn52-22-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I recollect my many kinds of past lives, with features and details.” ^sn52-22-1-1

### Clairvoyance (Linked Discourses 52.23) ^sn52-23-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, I understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds.” ^sn52-23-1-1

### The Ending of Defilements (Linked Discourses 52.24) ^sn52-24-0

“… And it’s because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation that I realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^sn52-24-1-1

The Linked Discourses with Anuruddha, the eighth section. ^sn52-24-2-5

## Saṁyutta 53 ^sn53-0

### Absorptions, Etc. (Linked Discourses 53.1–12) ^sn53-1-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn53-1-12-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four absorptions. ^sn53-1-12-1-3

What four? ^sn53-1-12-1-4

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn53-1-12-1-5

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn53-1-12-1-6

And with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^sn53-1-12-1-7

With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^sn53-1-12-1-8

These are the four absorptions. ^sn53-1-12-1-9

The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. ^sn53-1-12-2-1

In the same way, a mendicant who develops and cultivates the four absorptions slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment. ^sn53-1-12-2-2

And how does a mendicant who develops and cultivates the four absorptions slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment? ^sn53-1-12-2-3

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn53-1-12-2-4

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption … ^sn53-1-12-2-5

third absorption … ^sn53-1-12-2-6

fourth absorption. ^sn53-1-12-2-7

That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the four absorptions slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.” ^sn53-1-12-2-8

Six on slanting to the east, ^sn53-1-12-3-1

and six on slanting to the ocean; ^sn53-1-12-3-2

these two sixes make twelve, ^sn53-1-12-3-3

and that’s how this chapter is recited. ^sn53-1-12-3-4

### Diligence (Linked Discourses 53.13–22) ^sn53-13-22-0

(Tell in full as in the chapter on diligence at SN 45.139–148.) ^sn53-13-22-1-1

The Realized One, footprint, roof peak, ^sn53-13-22-2-1

roots, heartwood, jasmine, ^sn53-13-22-2-2

monarch, sun and moon, ^sn53-13-22-2-3

and cloth is the tenth. ^sn53-13-22-2-4

### Hard Work (Linked Discourses 53.23–34) ^sn53-23-34-0

(Tell in full as in the chapter on hard work at SN 45.149–160.) ^sn53-23-34-1-1

Hard work, seeds, and dragons, ^sn53-23-34-2-1

a tree, a pot, and a spike, ^sn53-23-34-2-2

the atmosphere, and two on clouds, ^sn53-23-34-2-3

a ship, a guest house, and a river. ^sn53-23-34-2-4

### Searches (Linked Discourses 53.35–44) ^sn53-35-44-0

(Tell in full as in the chapter on searches at SN 45.161–170.) ^sn53-35-44-1-1

Searches, discriminations, defilements, ^sn53-35-44-2-1

states of existence, three kinds of suffering, ^sn53-35-44-2-2

barrenness, stains, and troubles, ^sn53-35-44-2-3

feelings, craving, and thirst. ^sn53-35-44-2-4

### Floods, etc. (Linked Discourses 53.45–54) ^sn53-45-54-0

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^sn53-45-54-1-1

What five? ^sn53-45-54-1-2

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^sn53-45-54-1-3

These are the five higher fetters. ^sn53-45-54-1-4

The four absorptions should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters. ^sn53-45-54-1-5

What four? ^sn53-45-54-1-6

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^sn53-45-54-1-7

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption … ^sn53-45-54-1-8

third absorption … ^sn53-45-54-1-9

fourth absorption. ^sn53-45-54-1-10

These four absorptions should be developed for the direct knowledge, complete understanding, finishing, and giving up of these five higher fetters.” ^sn53-45-54-1-11

(Tell in full as in the Linked Discourses on the Path at SN 45.171–180.) ^sn53-45-54-1-12

Floods, yokes, grasping, ^sn53-45-54-2-1

ties, and underlying tendencies, ^sn53-45-54-2-2

kinds of sensual stimulation, hindrances, ^sn53-45-54-2-3

aggregates, and fetters high and low. ^sn53-45-54-2-4

The Linked Discourses on Absorption, the ninth section. ^sn53-45-54-2-5

## Saṁyutta 54 ^sn54-0

### One Thing (Linked Discourses 54.1) ^sn54-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn54-1-1-1

“Mendicants, when one thing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-1-1-3

What one thing? ^sn54-1-1-4

Mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-1-1-5

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-1-1-6

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-1-1-7

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. ^sn54-1-1-8

Breathing in heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ ^sn54-1-2-1

When breathing in lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing in lightly.’ Breathing out lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing out lightly.’ ^sn54-1-2-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the whole body.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the whole body.’ ^sn54-1-2-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the physical process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the physical process.’ ^sn54-1-2-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing rapture.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing rapture.’ ^sn54-1-3-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing bliss.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing bliss.’ ^sn54-1-3-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mental process.’ ^sn54-1-3-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the mental process.’ ^sn54-1-3-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mind.’ ^sn54-1-4-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in gladdening the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out gladdening the mind.’ ^sn54-1-4-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in immersing the mind in samādhi.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out immersing the mind in samādhi.’ ^sn54-1-4-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in freeing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out freeing the mind.’ ^sn54-1-4-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing impermanence.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing impermanence.’ ^sn54-1-4-5

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing fading away.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing fading away.’ ^sn54-1-5-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing cessation.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing cessation.’ ^sn54-1-5-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-1-5-4

Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^sn54-1-6-1

### Awakening Factors (Linked Discourses 54.2) ^sn54-2-0

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-2-1-1

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-2-1-2

It’s when a mendicant develops mindfulness of breathing together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, ^sn54-2-1-3

investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn54-2-1-4

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn54-2-1-5

Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^sn54-2-1-6

### Plain Version (Linked Discourses 54.3) ^sn54-3-0

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-3-1-1

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-3-1-2

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-3-1-3

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-3-1-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-3-1-5

Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^sn54-3-1-6

### Fruits (1st) (Linked Discourses 54.4) ^sn54-4-0

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-4-1-1

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-4-1-2

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-4-1-3

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-4-1-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-4-1-5

Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-4-1-6

When mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated in this way you can expect one of two results: ^sn54-4-1-7

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return.” ^sn54-4-1-8

### Fruits (2nd) (Linked Discourses 54.5) ^sn54-5-0

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-5-1-1

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-5-1-2

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-5-1-3

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-5-1-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-5-1-5

Mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-5-1-6

When mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated in this way you can expect seven fruits and benefits. ^sn54-5-2-1

What seven? ^sn54-5-2-2

You attain enlightenment early on in this very life. ^sn54-5-2-3

If not, you attain enlightenment at the time of death. ^sn54-5-2-5

If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters you’re extinguished in between one life and the next … ^sn54-5-2-7

you’re extinguished upon landing … ^sn54-5-2-8

you’re extinguished without extra effort … ^sn54-5-2-9

you’re extinguished with extra effort … ^sn54-5-2-10

you head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm … ^sn54-5-2-11

When mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated in this way you can expect these seven fruits and benefits.” ^sn54-5-2-12

### With Ariṭṭha (Linked Discourses 54.6) ^sn54-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn54-6-1-1

There the Buddha … said: ^sn54-6-1-2

“Mendicants, do you develop mindfulness of breathing?” ^sn54-6-1-3

When he said this, Venerable Ariṭṭha said to him: ^sn54-6-1-4

“Sir, I develop mindfulness of breathing.” ^sn54-6-1-5

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^sn54-6-1-6

“Sir, I’ve given up desire for sensual pleasures of the past. I’m rid of desire for sensual pleasures of the future. And I have eliminated perception of aversion regarding phenomena internally and externally. ^sn54-6-1-7

Just mindful, I will breathe in. Mindful, I will breathe out. ^sn54-6-1-8

That’s how I develop mindfulness of breathing.” ^sn54-6-1-9

“That is mindfulness of breathing, Ariṭṭha; I don’t deny it. ^sn54-6-2-1

But as to how mindfulness of breathing is fulfilled in detail, ^sn54-6-2-2

listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn54-6-2-3

“Yes, sir,” Ariṭṭha replied. ^sn54-6-2-4

The Buddha said this: ^sn54-6-2-5

“And how is mindfulness of breathing fulfilled in detail? ^sn54-6-3-1

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-6-3-2

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. ^sn54-6-3-3

Breathing in heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ … ^sn54-6-3-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-6-3-5

This is how mindfulness of breathing is fulfilled in detail.” ^sn54-6-3-6

### About Mahākappina (Linked Discourses 54.7) ^sn54-7-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn54-7-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Mahākappina was sitting not far from the Buddha, cross-legged, his body set straight, and mindfulness brought to the present. ^sn54-7-1-2

The Buddha saw him, ^sn54-7-1-3

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn54-7-1-4

“Mendicants, do you see any disturbance or trembling in that mendicant’s body?” ^sn54-7-2-1

“Sir, whenever we see that mendicant meditating—whether in the middle of the Saṅgha or alone in private—we never see any disturbance or trembling in his body.” ^sn54-7-2-2

“Mendicants, when an immersion has been developed and cultivated there’s no disturbance or trembling of the body or mind. That mendicant gets such immersion when he wants, without trouble or difficulty. ^sn54-7-3-1

And what is that immersion? ^sn54-7-3-2

When immersion due to mindfulness of breathing has been developed and cultivated there’s no disturbance or trembling of the body or mind. ^sn54-7-4-1

And how is immersion due to mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated in such a way? ^sn54-7-4-2

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-7-5-1

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-7-5-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-7-5-3

That’s how immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated so that there’s no disturbance or trembling of the body or mind.” ^sn54-7-5-4

### The Simile of the Lamp (Linked Discourses 54.8) ^sn54-8-0

“Mendicants, when immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-8-1-1

How so? ^sn54-8-1-2

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-8-2-1

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. ^sn54-8-2-2

Breathing in heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ … ^sn54-8-2-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-8-2-4

That’s how immersion due to mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated, is very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-8-2-5

Before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I too usually practiced this kind of meditation. ^sn54-8-3-1

And while I was usually practicing this kind of meditation neither my body nor my eyes became fatigued. ^sn54-8-3-2

And my mind was freed from defilements by not grasping. ^sn54-8-3-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-4-1

‘May neither my body nor my eyes became fatigued. And may my mind be freed from the defilements by not grasping.’ ^sn54-8-4-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-4-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-5-1

‘May I give up memories and thoughts tied to domestic life.’ ^sn54-8-5-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-5-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-6-1

‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive.’ ^sn54-8-6-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-6-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-7-1

‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive.’ ^sn54-8-7-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-7-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-8-1

‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive and the repulsive.’ ^sn54-8-8-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-8-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-9-1

‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive and the unrepulsive.’ ^sn54-8-9-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-9-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-10-1

‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive.’ ^sn54-8-10-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-10-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-11-1

‘Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, may I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.’ ^sn54-8-11-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-11-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-12-1

‘As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, may I enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.’ ^sn54-8-12-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-12-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-13-1

‘With the fading away of rapture, may I enter and remain in the third absorption, where I will meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.”’ ^sn54-8-13-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-13-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-14-1

‘With the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, may I enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.’ ^sn54-8-14-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-14-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-15-1

‘Going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that “space is infinite”, may I enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space.’ ^sn54-8-15-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-15-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-16-1

‘Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, may I enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness.’ ^sn54-8-16-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-16-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-17-1

‘Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, may I enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness.’ ^sn54-8-17-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-17-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-18-1

‘Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, may I enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’ ^sn54-8-18-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-18-3

Now, a mendicant might wish: ^sn54-8-19-1

‘Going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, may I enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^sn54-8-19-2

So let them closely focus on this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-8-19-3

When immersion due to mindfulness of breathing has been developed and cultivated in this way, if they feel a pleasant feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn54-8-20-1

If they feel a painful feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn54-8-20-2

If they feel a neutral feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it. ^sn54-8-20-3

If they feel a pleasant feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn54-8-21-1

If they feel a painful feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn54-8-21-2

If they feel a neutral feeling, they feel it detached. ^sn54-8-21-3

Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn54-8-21-4

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here.’ ^sn54-8-21-5

Suppose an oil lamp depended on oil and a wick to burn. ^sn54-8-22-1

As the oil and the wick are used up, it would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^sn54-8-22-2

In the same way, feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^sn54-8-22-3

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here.’” ^sn54-8-22-4

### At Vesālī (Linked Discourses 54.9) ^sn54-9-0

So I have heard. ^sn54-9-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn54-9-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha spoke in many ways to the mendicants about the meditation on ugliness. He praised the meditation on ugliness and its development. ^sn54-9-1-3

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^sn54-9-2-1

“Mendicants, I wish to go on retreat for a fortnight. ^sn54-9-2-2

No-one should approach me, except for the one who brings my almsfood.” ^sn54-9-2-3

“Yes, sir,” replied those mendicants. And no-one approached him, except for the one who brought the almsfood. ^sn54-9-2-4

Then those mendicants thought, ^sn54-9-3-1

“The Buddha spoke in many ways about the meditation on ugliness. He praised the meditation on ugliness and its development.” They committed themselves to developing the many different facets of the meditation on ugliness. ^sn54-9-3-2

Becoming horrified, repelled, and disgusted with this body, they looked for a suicide weapon. ^sn54-9-3-3

Each day ten, twenty, or thirty mendicants committed suicide. ^sn54-9-3-4

Then after a fortnight had passed, the Buddha came out of retreat and addressed Ānanda, ^sn54-9-4-1

“Ānanda, why does the mendicant Saṅgha seem so diminished?” ^sn54-9-4-2

Ānanda told the Buddha all that had happened, and said, ^sn54-9-4-3

“Sir, please explain another way the mendicant Saṅgha might be settled in enlightenment.” ^sn54-9-4-6

“Well then, Ānanda, gather all the mendicants staying in the vicinity of Vesālī together in the assembly hall.” ^sn54-9-5-1

“Yes, sir,” replied Ānanda. He did what the Buddha asked, went up to him, and said, ^sn54-9-5-2

“Sir, the mendicant Saṅgha has assembled. ^sn54-9-5-3

Please, sir, come at your convenience.” ^sn54-9-5-4

Then the Buddha went to the assembly hall, sat down on the seat spread out, ^sn54-9-6-1

and addressed the mendicants: ^sn54-9-6-2

“Mendicants, when this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s peaceful and sublime, a delectable and blissful meditation. And it disperses and settles unskillful qualities on the spot whenever they arise. ^sn54-9-6-3

In the last month of summer, when the dust and dirt is stirred up, a large storm out of season disperses and settles it on the spot. ^sn54-9-7-1

In the same way, when this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it’s peaceful and sublime, a delectable and blissful meditation. And it disperses and settles unskillful qualities on the spot whenever they arise. ^sn54-9-7-2

And how is it so developed and cultivated? ^sn54-9-7-3

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-9-8-1

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-9-8-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-9-8-3

That’s how this immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated so that it’s peaceful and sublime, a delectable and blissful  meditation. And it disperses and settles unskillful qualities on the spot whenever they arise.” ^sn54-9-8-4

### With Kimbila (Linked Discourses 54.10) ^sn54-10-0

So I have heard. ^sn54-10-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kimbilā in the Freshwater Mangrove Wood. ^sn54-10-1-2

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Kimbila, ^sn54-10-1-3

“Kimbila, how is immersion due to mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so that it is very fruitful and beneficial?” ^sn54-10-1-4

When he said this, Kimbila kept silent. ^sn54-10-2-1

For a second time … ^sn54-10-2-2

And for a third time, the Buddha said to him, ^sn54-10-2-3

“How is immersion due to mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so that it is very fruitful and beneficial?” ^sn54-10-2-4

And a second time and a third time Kimbila kept silent. ^sn54-10-2-5

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^sn54-10-3-1

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^sn54-10-3-2

Let the Buddha speak on immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^sn54-10-3-3

“Well then, Ānanda, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn54-10-4-1

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^sn54-10-4-2

The Buddha said this: ^sn54-10-4-3

“Ānanda, how is immersion due to mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so that it is very fruitful and beneficial? ^sn54-10-4-4

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-10-4-5

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. … ^sn54-10-4-6

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-10-4-7

That’s how immersion due to mindfulness of breathing, when developed and cultivated, is very fruitful and beneficial. ^sn54-10-4-8

There’s a time when a mendicant is breathing in heavily, and they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ ^sn54-10-5-1

When breathing in lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing in lightly.’ Breathing out lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing out lightly.’ ^sn54-10-5-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the whole body.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the whole body.’ ^sn54-10-5-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the physical process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the physical process.’ ^sn54-10-5-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-5-5

Why is that? ^sn54-10-5-6

Because the breath is a certain aspect of the body, I say. ^sn54-10-5-7

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-5-8

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing rapture.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing rapture.’ ^sn54-10-6-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing bliss.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing bliss.’ ^sn54-10-6-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the the mental process.’ ^sn54-10-6-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the mental process.’ ^sn54-10-6-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of feelings—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-6-5

Why is that? ^sn54-10-6-6

Because careful application of mind to the in-breaths and out-breaths is a certain aspect of feelings, I say. ^sn54-10-6-7

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of feelings—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-6-8

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mind.’ ^sn54-10-7-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in gladdening the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out gladdening the mind.’ ^sn54-10-7-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in immersing the mind in samādhi.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out immersing the mind in samādhi.’ ^sn54-10-7-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in freeing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out freeing the mind.’ ^sn54-10-7-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the mind—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-7-5

Why is that? ^sn54-10-7-6

Because there is no development of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing for someone who is unmindful and lacks awareness, I say. ^sn54-10-7-7

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the mind—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-7-8

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing impermanence.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing impermanence.’ ^sn54-10-8-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing fading away.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing fading away.’ ^sn54-10-8-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing cessation.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing cessation.’ ^sn54-10-8-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-10-8-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-8-5

Having seen with wisdom the giving up of covetousness and displeasure, they watch closely over with equanimity. ^sn54-10-8-6

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-10-8-7

Suppose there was a large heap of sand at the crossroads. ^sn54-10-9-1

And a cart or chariot were to come by from the east, ^sn54-10-9-2

west, ^sn54-10-9-3

north, ^sn54-10-9-4

or south and destroy that heap of sand. ^sn54-10-9-5

In the same way, when a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the body, ^sn54-10-9-6

feelings, ^sn54-10-9-7

mind, ^sn54-10-9-8

or principles, they destroy bad, unskillful qualities.” ^sn54-10-9-9

### Icchānaṅgala (Linked Discourses 54.11) ^sn54-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^sn54-11-1-1

There he addressed the mendicants, ^sn54-11-1-2

“Mendicants, I wish to go on retreat for three months. ^sn54-11-1-3

No-one should approach me, except for the one who brings my almsfood.” ^sn54-11-1-4

“Yes, sir,” replied those mendicants. And no-one approached him, except for the one who brought the almsfood. ^sn54-11-1-5

Then after three months had passed, the Buddha came out of retreat and addressed the mendicants: ^sn54-11-2-1

“Mendicants, if wanderers who follow another religion were to ask you: ^sn54-11-2-2

‘Reverends, what was the ascetic Gotama’s usual meditation during the rainy season residence?’ You should answer them like this. ^sn54-11-2-3

‘Reverends, the ascetic Gotama’s usual meditation during the rainy season residence was immersion due to mindfulness of breathing.’ ^sn54-11-2-4

In this regard: mindful, I breathe in. Mindful, I breathe out. ^sn54-11-2-5

Breathing in heavily I know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily I know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ ^sn54-11-2-6

When breathing in lightly I know: ‘I’m breathing in lightly.’ Breathing out lightly I know: ‘I’m breathing out lightly.’ ^sn54-11-2-7

I know: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the whole body.’ … ^sn54-11-2-8

I know: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ I know: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-11-2-9

For if anything should be rightly called ^sn54-11-3-1

‘a noble meditation’, or else ‘a divine meditation’, or else ‘a realized one’s meditation’, ^sn54-11-3-2

it’s immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-11-3-3

For those mendicants who are trainees—who haven’t achieved their heart’s desire, but live aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke—the development and cultivation of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing leads to the ending of defilements. ^sn54-11-3-5

For those mendicants who are perfected—who have ended the defilements, completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and are rightly freed through enlightenment—the development and cultivation of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing leads to blissful meditation in this life, and to mindfulness and awareness. ^sn54-11-3-6

For if anything should be rightly called ^sn54-11-4-1

‘a noble meditation’, or else ‘a divine meditation’, or else ‘a realized one’s meditation’, ^sn54-11-4-2

it’s immersion due to mindfulness of breathing.” ^sn54-11-4-3

### In Doubt (Linked Discourses 54.12) ^sn54-12-0

At one time Venerable Lomasakaṅgiya was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^sn54-12-1-1

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to Venerable Lomasakaṅgiya, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^sn54-12-1-2

“Sir, is the meditation of a trainee just the same as the meditation of a realized one? Or is the meditation of a trainee different from the meditation of a realized one?” ^sn54-12-1-3

“Reverend Mahānāma, the meditation of a trainee and a realized one are not the same; ^sn54-12-2-1

they are different. ^sn54-12-2-2

Those mendicants who are trainees haven’t achieved their heart’s desire, but live aspiring for the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. They meditate after giving up the five hindrances. ^sn54-12-2-3

What five? ^sn54-12-2-4

The hindrances of sensual desire, ill will, ^sn54-12-2-5

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn54-12-2-6

restlessness and remorse, ^sn54-12-2-7

and doubt. ^sn54-12-2-8

Those who are trainee mendicants … meditate after giving up the five hindrances. ^sn54-12-3-1

Those mendicants who are perfected—who have ended the defilements, completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and are rightly freed through enlightenment—for them, the five hindrances are cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^sn54-12-4-1

What five? ^sn54-12-4-2

The hindrances of sensual desire, ^sn54-12-4-3

ill will, ^sn54-12-4-4

dullness and drowsiness, ^sn54-12-4-5

restlessness and remorse, ^sn54-12-4-6

and doubt. ^sn54-12-4-7

Those mendicants who are perfected—who have ended the defilements … for them, the five hindrances are cut off at the root … and unable to arise in the future. ^sn54-12-5-1

And here’s another way to understand how ^sn54-12-5-2

the meditation of a trainee and a realized one are different. ^sn54-12-5-3

At one time the Buddha was staying in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^sn54-12-6-1

There he addressed the mendicants, ^sn54-12-6-2

‘Mendicants, I wish to go on retreat for three months. ^sn54-12-6-3

No-one should approach me, except for the one who brings my almsfood.’ ^sn54-12-6-4

‘Yes, sir,’ replied those mendicants. And no-one approached him, except for the one who brought the almsfood. ^sn54-12-6-5

Then after three months had passed, the Buddha came out of retreat and addressed the mendicants: ^sn54-12-7-1

‘Mendicants, if wanderers who follow another religion were to ask you: ^sn54-12-7-2

“Reverends, what was the ascetic Gotama’s usual meditation during the rainy season residence?” You should answer them like this: ^sn54-12-7-3

“Reverends, the ascetic Gotama’s usual meditation during the rainy season residence was immersion due to mindfulness of breathing.” ^sn54-12-7-4

In this regard: mindful, I breathe in. Mindful, I breathe out. ^sn54-12-7-5

Breathing in heavily I know: “I’m breathing in heavily.” Breathing out heavily I know: “I’m breathing out heavily.” … ^sn54-12-7-6

I know: “I’ll breathe in observing letting go.” I know: “I’ll breathe out observing letting go.” ^sn54-12-7-7

For if anything should be rightly called ^sn54-12-8-1

“noble meditation”, or else “divine meditation”, or else “a realized one’s meditation”, ^sn54-12-8-2

it’s immersion due to mindfulness of breathing. ^sn54-12-8-3

For those mendicants who are trainees—who haven’t achieved their heart’s desire, but live aspiring for the supreme sanctuary from the yoke—the development and cultivation of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing leads to the ending of defilements. ^sn54-12-9-1

For those mendicants who are perfected—who have ended the defilements, completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and are rightly freed through enlightenment—the development and cultivation of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing leads to blissful meditation in this very life, and to mindfulness and awareness. ^sn54-12-10-1

For if anything should be rightly called ^sn54-12-11-1

“noble meditation”, or else “divine meditation”, or else “a realized one’s meditation”, ^sn54-12-11-2

it’s immersion due to mindfulness of breathing.’ ^sn54-12-11-3

This is another way to understand how ^sn54-12-11-5

the meditation of a trainee and a realized one are different.” ^sn54-12-11-6

### With Ānanda (1st) (Linked Discourses 54.13) ^sn54-13-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn54-13-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn54-13-1-2

“Sir, is there one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills four things; and those four things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things; and those seven things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill two things?” ^sn54-13-1-3

“There is, Ānanda.” ^sn54-13-2-1

“Sir, what is that one thing?” ^sn54-13-3-1

“Immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. And the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, when developed and cultivated, fulfill the seven awakening factors. And the seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^sn54-13-3-2

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^sn54-13-4-1

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^sn54-13-4-2

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. ^sn54-13-4-3

Breathing in heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ … ^sn54-13-4-4

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-13-4-5

There’s a time when a mendicant is breathing in heavily, and they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ ^sn54-13-5-1

… ^sn54-13-5-2

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the physical process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the physical process.’ ^sn54-13-5-3

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-5-4

Why is that? ^sn54-13-5-5

Because the breath is a certain aspect of the body, I say. ^sn54-13-5-6

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-5-7

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing rapture … ^sn54-13-6-1

bliss … ^sn54-13-6-2

mind …’ … ^sn54-13-6-3

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the mental process.’ ^sn54-13-6-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of feelings—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-6-5

Why is that? ^sn54-13-6-6

Because careful application of mind to the in-breaths and out-breaths is a certain aspect of feelings, I say. ^sn54-13-6-7

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of feelings—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-6-8

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mind.’ ^sn54-13-7-1

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in gladdening the mind … ^sn54-13-7-2

immersing the mind in samādhi … ^sn54-13-7-3

freeing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out freeing the mind.’ ^sn54-13-7-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the mind—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-7-5

Why is that? ^sn54-13-7-6

Because there is no development of immersion due to mindfulness of breathing for someone who is unmindful and lacks awareness, I say. ^sn54-13-7-7

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of the mind—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-7-8

There’s a time when a mendicant practices like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing impermanence … ^sn54-13-8-1

fading away … ^sn54-13-8-2

cessation … ^sn54-13-8-3

letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-13-8-4

At such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-8-5

Having seen with wisdom the giving up of covetousness and displeasure, they watch closely over with equanimity. ^sn54-13-8-6

Therefore, at such a time a mendicant is meditating by observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^sn54-13-8-7

That’s how immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^sn54-13-9-1

And how are the four kinds of mindfulness meditation developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the seven awakening factors? ^sn54-13-10-1

Whenever a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body, ^sn54-13-10-2

their mindfulness is established and lucid. ^sn54-13-10-3

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of mindfulness; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-10-4

As they live mindfully in this way they investigate, explore, and inquire into that principle with wisdom. ^sn54-13-11-1

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of investigation of principles; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-11-2

As they investigate principles with wisdom in this way their energy is roused up and unflagging. ^sn54-13-12-1

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of energy; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-12-2

When you’re energetic, rapture not of the flesh arises. ^sn54-13-13-1

At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of rapture; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-13-2

When the mind is full of rapture, the body and mind become tranquil. ^sn54-13-14-1

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of tranquility; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-14-2

When the body is tranquil and one feels bliss, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^sn54-13-15-1

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of immersion; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-15-2

They closely watch over that mind immersed in samādhi. ^sn54-13-16-1

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of equanimity; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-16-2

Whenever a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of feelings … ^sn54-13-17-1

mind … ^sn54-13-17-2

principles, ^sn54-13-17-3

their mindfulness is established and lucid. ^sn54-13-17-4

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of mindfulness; they develop it and perfect it. … ^sn54-13-17-5

(Tell in full as for the first kind of mindfulness meditation.) ^sn54-13-17-7

They closely watch over that mind immersed in samādhi. ^sn54-13-18-1

At such a time, a mendicant has activated the awakening factor of equanimity; they develop it and perfect it. ^sn54-13-18-2

That’s how the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the seven awakening factors. ^sn54-13-18-4

And how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom? ^sn54-13-19-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, ^sn54-13-19-2

investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, ^sn54-13-19-3

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^sn54-13-19-4

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.” ^sn54-13-19-5

### With Ānanda (2nd) (Linked Discourses 54.14) ^sn54-14-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn54-14-1-1

“Ānanda, is there one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills four things; and those four things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things; and those seven things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill two things?” ^sn54-14-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn54-14-1-3

“There is, Ānanda. ^sn54-14-1-4

And what is that one thing? ^sn54-14-2-1

Immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. And the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, when developed and cultivated, fulfill the seven awakening factors. And the seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^sn54-14-2-2

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? … ^sn54-14-2-3

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.” ^sn54-14-2-5

### Several Mendicants (1st) (Linked Discourses 54.15) ^sn54-15-0

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn54-15-1-1

“Sir, is there one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills four things; and those four things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things; and those seven things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill two things?” ^sn54-15-1-2

“There is, mendicants.” ^sn54-15-1-3

“Sir, what is that one thing?” ^sn54-15-2-1

“Immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. And the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, when developed and cultivated, fulfill the seven awakening factors. And the seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^sn54-15-2-2

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? … ^sn54-15-3-1

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.” ^sn54-15-3-3

### Several Mendicants (2nd) (Linked Discourses 54.16) ^sn54-16-0

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^sn54-16-1-1

“Mendicants, is there one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills four things; and those four things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things; and those seven things, when developed and cultivated, fulfill two things?” ^sn54-16-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …” ^sn54-16-1-3

“There is, mendicants. ^sn54-16-1-4

And what is that one thing? ^sn54-16-2-1

Immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, fulfills the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. And the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, when developed and cultivated, fulfill the seven awakening factors. And the seven awakening factors, when developed and cultivated, fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^sn54-16-2-2

And how is mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^sn54-16-3-1

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. … ^sn54-16-3-2

That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.” ^sn54-16-18-5

### Giving Up the Fetters (Linked Discourses 54.17) ^sn54-17-0

“Mendicants, when immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it leads to giving up the fetters …” ^sn54-17-1-1

### Uprooting the Tendencies (Linked Discourses 54.18) ^sn54-18-0

“Mendicants, when immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it leads to uprooting the underlying tendencies …” ^sn54-18-1-1

### Completely Understanding the Course of Time (Linked Discourses 54.19) ^sn54-19-0

“Mendicants, when immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it leads to completely understanding the course of time …” ^sn54-19-1-1

### The Ending of Defilements (Linked Discourses 54.20) ^sn54-20-0

“Mendicants, when immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated it leads to the ending of defilements.” … ^sn54-20-1-1

“And how is immersion due to mindfulness of breathing developed and cultivated so as to lead to giving up the fetters, ^sn54-20-1-2

uprooting the underlying tendencies, ^sn54-20-1-3

completely understanding the course of time, ^sn54-20-1-4
