and ending the defilements? ^sn54-20-1-5

It’s when a mendicant—gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut—sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and establishes mindfulness in front of them. … ^sn54-20-1-6

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^sn54-20-1-7

That’s how immersion due to mindfulness of breathing is developed and cultivated so as to lead to giving up the fetters, ^sn54-20-1-8

uprooting the underlying tendencies, ^sn54-20-1-9

completely understanding the course of time, ^sn54-20-1-10

and ending the defilements.” ^sn54-20-1-11

The Linked Discourses on Mindfulness of Breathing, the tenth section. ^sn54-20-2-5

## Saṁyutta 55 ^sn55-0

### A Wheel-Turning Monarch (Linked Discourses 55.1) ^sn55-1-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-1-1-1

There the Buddha … said: ^sn55-1-1-2

“Mendicants, suppose a wheel-turning monarch were to rule as sovereign lord over these four continents. And when his body breaks up, after death, he’s reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm, in the company of the gods of the thirty-three. There he entertains himself in the Garden of Delight, escorted by a band of nymphs, and supplied and provided with the five kinds of heavenly sensual stimulation. Still, as he’s lacking four things, he’s not exempt from hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. He’s not exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-1-1-3

Now suppose a noble disciple wears rags and feeds on scraps of almsfood. Still, as they have four things, they’re exempt from hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-1-1-4

What four? ^sn55-1-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn55-1-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn55-1-2-3

They have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^sn55-1-2-4

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn55-1-2-5

They have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^sn55-1-2-6

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn55-1-2-7

Furthermore, a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn55-1-2-8

These are the four qualities that they possess. ^sn55-1-2-9

And, mendicants, gaining these four continents is not worth a sixteenth part of gaining these four things.” ^sn55-1-2-10

### The Objective of the Spiritual Life (Linked Discourses 55.2) ^sn55-2-0

“Mendicants, a noble disciple who has four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-2-1-1

What four? ^sn55-2-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-2-2-2

the teaching … ^sn55-2-2-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-2-2-5

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-2-2-6

A noble disciple who has these four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-2-2-7

That is what the Buddha said. ^sn55-2-3-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^sn55-2-3-2

“Those who have faith and ethics, ^sn55-2-4-1

confidence, and vision of the truth, ^sn55-2-4-2

in time arrive at happiness, ^sn55-2-4-3

the objective of the spiritual life.” ^sn55-2-4-4

### With Dīghāvu (Linked Discourses 55.3) ^sn55-3-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^sn55-3-1-1

Now at that time the lay follower Dīghāvu was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-3-1-2

Then he addressed his father, the householder Jotika, ^sn55-3-1-3

“Please, householder, go to the Buddha, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^sn55-3-1-4

‘Sir, the lay follower Dīghāvu is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-3-1-5

He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn55-3-1-6

And then say: ^sn55-3-1-7

‘Sir, please visit him at his home out of sympathy.’” ^sn55-3-1-8

“Yes, dear,” replied Jotika. He did as Dīghāvu asked. ^sn55-3-1-9

The Buddha consented with silence. ^sn55-3-1-14

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the lay follower Dīghāvu, sat down on the seat spread out, and said to him, ^sn55-3-2-1

“I hope you’re keeping well, Dīghāvu; I hope you’re all right. I hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn55-3-2-2

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn55-3-2-3

“So, Dīghāvu, you should train like this: ^sn55-3-2-4

‘I will have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-3-2-5

the teaching … ^sn55-3-2-6

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-3-2-7

And I will have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion.’ ^sn55-3-2-8

That’s how you should train.” ^sn55-3-2-9

“Sir, these four factors of stream-entry that were taught by the Buddha are found in me, and I exhibit them. ^sn55-3-3-1

For I have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-3-3-2

the teaching … ^sn55-3-3-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-3-3-4

And I have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion.” ^sn55-3-3-5

“In that case, Dīghāvu, grounded on these four factors of stream-entry you should further develop these six things that play a part in realization. ^sn55-3-3-6

You should meditate observing the impermanence of all conditions, perceiving suffering in impermanence, perceiving not-self in suffering, perceiving giving up, perceiving fading away, and perceiving cessation. ^sn55-3-3-7

That’s how you should train.” ^sn55-3-3-8

“These six things that play a part in realization that were taught by the Buddha are found in me, and I exhibit them. ^sn55-3-4-1

For I meditate observing the impermanence of all conditions, perceiving suffering in impermanence, perceiving not-self in suffering, perceiving giving up, perceiving fading away, and perceiving cessation. ^sn55-3-4-2

But still, sir, I think, ^sn55-3-4-3

‘I hope Jotika doesn’t suffer distress when I’ve gone.’” ^sn55-3-4-4

Jotika said, “Dear Dīghāvu, don’t focus on that. ^sn55-3-4-5

Come on, dear Dīghāvu, you should closely focus on what the Buddha is saying.” ^sn55-3-4-6

When the Buddha had given this advice he rose from his seat and left. ^sn55-3-5-1

Not long after the Buddha left, Dīghāvu passed away. ^sn55-3-5-2

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn55-3-5-3

“Sir, the lay follower named Dīghāvu, who was advised in brief by the Buddha, has passed away. ^sn55-3-5-4

Where has he been reborn in his next life?” ^sn55-3-5-5

“Mendicants, the lay follower Dīghāvu was astute. He practiced in line with the teaching, and did not trouble me about the teachings. ^sn55-3-5-6

With the ending of the five lower fetters, he’s been reborn spontaneously, and will become extinguished there, not liable to return from that world.” ^sn55-3-5-7

### With Sāriputta (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.4) ^sn55-4-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn55-4-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Ānanda came out of retreat … and said to Sāriputta: ^sn55-4-1-2

“Reverend, how many things do people have to possess in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^sn55-4-1-3

“Reverend, people have to possess four things in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-4-1-4

What four? ^sn55-4-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-4-2-2

the teaching … ^sn55-4-2-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-4-2-5

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-4-2-6

People have to possess these four things in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-4-2-7

### With Sāriputta (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.5) ^sn55-5-0

Then Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn55-5-1-1

“Sāriputta, they speak of a ‘factor for stream-entry’. ^sn55-5-1-2

What is a factor for stream-entry?” ^sn55-5-1-3

“Sir, the factors for stream-entry are associating with true persons, listening to the true teaching, rational application of mind, and practicing in line with the teaching.” ^sn55-5-1-4

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^sn55-5-1-5

For the factors for stream-entry are associating with true persons, listening to the true teaching, rational application of mind, and practicing in line with the teaching. ^sn55-5-1-6

Sāriputta, they speak of ‘the stream’. ^sn55-5-2-1

What is the stream?” ^sn55-5-2-2

“Sir, the stream is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn55-5-2-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.” ^sn55-5-2-4

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^sn55-5-2-5

For the stream is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^sn55-5-2-6

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^sn55-5-2-7

Sāriputta, they speak of ‘a stream-enterer’. ^sn55-5-3-1

What is a stream-enterer?” ^sn55-5-3-2

“Sir, anyone who possesses this noble eightfold path is called a stream-enterer, the venerable of such and such name and clan.” ^sn55-5-3-3

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^sn55-5-3-4

For anyone who possesses this noble eightfold path is called a stream-enterer, the venerable of such and such name and clan.” ^sn55-5-3-5

### The Chamberlains (Linked Discourses 55.6) ^sn55-6-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-6-1-1

At that time several mendicants were making a robe for the Buddha, thinking that ^sn55-6-1-2

when his robe was finished and the three months of the rains residence had passed the Buddha would set out wandering. ^sn55-6-1-3

Now at that time the chamberlains Isidatta and Purāṇa were residing in Sādhuka on some business. ^sn55-6-1-4

They heard about this. ^sn55-6-1-5

So they posted someone on the road, saying: ^sn55-6-2-1

“My man, let us know when you see the Blessed One coming, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.” ^sn55-6-2-2

And that person stood there for two or three days before they saw the Buddha coming off in the distance. ^sn55-6-2-3

When they saw him, they went to the chamberlains and said: ^sn55-6-2-4

“Sirs, the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha is coming. ^sn55-6-2-5

Please come at your convenience.” ^sn55-6-2-6

Then the chamberlains went up to the Buddha, bowed, and followed behind him. ^sn55-6-3-1

And then the Buddha left the road, went to the root of a certain tree, and sat down on the seat spread out. ^sn55-6-3-2

The chamberlains Isidatta and Purāṇa bowed, sat down to one side, ^sn55-6-3-3

and said to the Buddha: ^sn55-6-3-4

“Sir, when we hear that ^sn55-6-4-1

you will be setting out from Sāvatthī to wander in the Kosalan lands, we’re sad and upset, thinking that ^sn55-6-4-2

you will be far from us. ^sn55-6-4-3

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-4-4

you are setting out from Sāvatthī to wander in the Kosalan lands, we’re sad and upset, thinking that ^sn55-6-4-5

you are far from us. ^sn55-6-4-6

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-5-1

you will be setting out from the Kosalan lands to wander in the Mallian lands, we’re sad and upset, thinking that ^sn55-6-5-2

you will be far from us. ^sn55-6-5-3

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-5-4

you are setting out from the Kosalan lands to wander in the Mallian lands, we’re sad and upset, thinking that ^sn55-6-5-5

you are far from us. ^sn55-6-5-6

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-6-1

you will be setting out from the Mallian lands to wander in the Vajjian lands … ^sn55-6-6-2

you will be setting out from the Vajjian lands to wander in the Kāsian lands … ^sn55-6-7-2

you will be setting out from the Kāsian lands to wander in the Magadhan lands … ^sn55-6-8-2

you are setting out from the Kāsian lands to wander in the Magadhan lands, we’re sad and upset, thinking that ^sn55-6-8-5

you are far from us. ^sn55-6-8-6

But when we hear that ^sn55-6-9-1

you will be setting out from the Magadhan lands to wander in the Kāsian lands, we’re happy and joyful, thinking that ^sn55-6-9-2

you will be near to us. ^sn55-6-9-3

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-9-4

you are setting out from the Magadhan lands to wander in the Kāsian lands … ^sn55-6-9-5

you will be setting out from the Kāsian lands to wander in the Vajjian lands … ^sn55-6-10-2

you will be setting out from the Vajjian lands to wander in the Mallian lands … ^sn55-6-11-2

you will be setting out from the Mallian lands to wander in the Kosalan lands … ^sn55-6-12-2

you will be setting out in the Kosalan lands to wander to Sāvatthī, we’re happy and joyful, thinking that ^sn55-6-13-2

you will be near to us. ^sn55-6-13-3

And when we hear that ^sn55-6-13-4

you are staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery we have no little happiness and joy, thinking that ^sn55-6-13-5

you are near to us.” ^sn55-6-13-6

“Well then, chamberlains, life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open. ^sn55-6-14-1

Just this much is enough for diligence.” ^sn55-6-14-2

“Sir, for us there is something that’s even more cramped than that, and is considered as such.” ^sn55-6-14-3

“What is that?” ^sn55-6-14-4

“Sir, it’s when King Pasenadi of Kosala wants to go and visit a park. We have to harness and prepare his royal elephants. Then we have to seat his dear and beloved wives on the elephants, one in front of us, and one behind. ^sn55-6-15-1

Those sisters smell like a freshly opened perfume box; that’s how the royal ladies smell with makeup on. ^sn55-6-15-2

The touch of those sisters is like a tuft of cotton-wool or kapok; that’s how pampered the royal ladies are. ^sn55-6-15-3

Now at that time we must look after the elephants, the sisters, and ourselves. ^sn55-6-15-4

But we don’t recall having a bad thought regarding those sisters. ^sn55-6-15-5

This is that thing that’s even more cramped than that, and is considered as such.” ^sn55-6-15-6

“Well then, chamberlains, life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open. ^sn55-6-16-1

Just this much is enough for diligence. ^sn55-6-16-2

A noble disciple who has four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-6-16-3

What four? ^sn55-6-17-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-6-17-2

the teaching … ^sn55-6-17-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-6-17-5

They live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^sn55-6-17-6

A noble disciple who has these four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-6-17-7

And you have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-6-18-1

the teaching … ^sn55-6-18-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-6-18-4

And whatever there is in your family that’s available to give, you share it all with those who are ethical, of good character. ^sn55-6-18-5

What do you think, chamberlains? ^sn55-6-18-6

How many people among the Kosalans are your equal ^sn55-6-18-7

when it comes to giving and sharing?” ^sn55-6-18-8

“We’re fortunate, sir, so very fortunate, ^sn55-6-18-9

in that the Buddha understands us like this.” ^sn55-6-18-10

### The People of Bamboo Gate (Linked Discourses 55.7) ^sn55-7-0

So I have heard. ^sn55-7-1-1

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a village of the Kosalan brahmins named Bamboo Gate. ^sn55-7-1-2

The brahmins and householders of Bamboo Gate heard: ^sn55-7-1-3

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Bamboo Gate, together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^sn55-7-1-4

He has this good reputation: ^sn55-7-1-5

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn55-7-1-6

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. ^sn55-7-1-7

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^sn55-7-1-8

It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^sn55-7-1-9

Then the brahmins and householders of Bamboo Gate went up to the Buddha. Before sitting down to one side, some bowed, some exchanged greetings and polite conversation, some held up their cupped palms toward the Buddha, some announced their name and clan, while some kept silent. Seated to one side they said to the Buddha: ^sn55-7-2-1

“Worthy Gotama, these are our wishes, desires, and hopes. ^sn55-7-2-2

We wish to live at home with our children; to use sandalwood imported from Kāsi; to wear garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and to accept gold and currency. And when our body breaks up, after death, we wish to be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-7-2-3

Given that we have such wishes, may the Buddha teach us the Dhamma so that we may achieve them.” ^sn55-7-2-4

“Householders, I will teach you an explanation of the Dhamma that is relevant for oneself. ^sn55-7-3-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^sn55-7-3-2

“Yes, worthy sir,” they replied. ^sn55-7-3-3

The Buddha said this: ^sn55-7-3-4

“And what is the explanation of the Dhamma that applies to oneself? ^sn55-7-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-4-2

‘I want to live and don’t want to die; I want to be happy and recoil from pain. ^sn55-7-4-3

Since this is so, if someone were to take my life, I wouldn’t like that. ^sn55-7-4-4

But others also want to live and don’t want to die; they want to be happy and recoil from pain. So if I were to take the life of someone else, they wouldn’t like that either. ^sn55-7-4-5

The thing that is disliked by me is also disliked by others. ^sn55-7-4-6

Since I dislike this thing, how can I inflict it on someone else?’ ^sn55-7-4-7

Reflecting in this way, they give up killing living creatures themselves. And they encourage others to give up killing living creatures, praising the giving up of killing living creatures. ^sn55-7-4-8

So their bodily behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-4-9

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-5-1

‘If someone were to steal from me, I wouldn’t like that. ^sn55-7-5-2

But if I were to steal from someone else, they wouldn’t like that either. ^sn55-7-5-3

The thing that is disliked by me is also disliked by others. ^sn55-7-5-4

Since I dislike this thing, how can I inflict it on someone else?’ ^sn55-7-5-5

Reflecting in this way, they give up stealing themselves. And they encourage others to give up stealing, praising the giving up of stealing. ^sn55-7-5-6

So their bodily behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-5-7

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-6-1

‘If someone were to have sexual relations with my wives, I wouldn’t like it. ^sn55-7-6-2

But if I were to have sexual relations with someone else’s wives, he wouldn’t like that either. ^sn55-7-6-3

The thing that is disliked by me is also disliked by others. ^sn55-7-6-4

Since I dislike this thing, how can I inflict it on others?’ ^sn55-7-6-5

Reflecting in this way, they give up sexual misconduct themselves. And they encourage others to give up sexual misconduct, praising the giving up of sexual misconduct. ^sn55-7-6-6

So their bodily behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-6-7

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-7-1

‘If someone were to distort my meaning by lying, I wouldn’t like it. ^sn55-7-7-2

But if I were to distort someone else’s meaning by lying, they wouldn’t like it either. ^sn55-7-7-3

The thing that is disliked by me is also disliked by someone else. ^sn55-7-7-4

Since I dislike this thing, how can I inflict it on others?’ ^sn55-7-7-5

Reflecting in this way, they give up lying themselves. And they encourage others to give up lying, praising the giving up of lying. ^sn55-7-7-6

So their verbal behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-7-7

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-8-1

‘If someone were to break me up from my friends by backbiting, I wouldn’t like it. ^sn55-7-8-2

But if I were to break someone else from their friends by backbiting, they wouldn’t like it either. …’ ^sn55-7-8-3

So their verbal behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-8-4

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-9-1

‘If someone were to attack me with harsh speech, I wouldn’t like it. ^sn55-7-9-2

But if I were to attack someone else with harsh speech, they wouldn’t like it either. …’ ^sn55-7-9-3

So their verbal behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-9-5

Furthermore, a noble disciple reflects: ^sn55-7-10-1

‘If someone were to annoy me by talking silliness and nonsense, I wouldn’t like it. ^sn55-7-10-2

But if I were to annoy someone else by talking silliness and nonsense, they wouldn’t like it either.’ ^sn55-7-10-3

The thing that is disliked by me is also disliked by another. ^sn55-7-10-4

Since I dislike this thing, how can I inflict it on another?’ ^sn55-7-10-5

Reflecting in this way, they give up talking nonsense themselves. And they encourage others to give up talking nonsense, praising the giving up of talking nonsense. ^sn55-7-10-6

So their verbal behavior is purified in three points. ^sn55-7-10-7

And they have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-7-11-1

the teaching … ^sn55-7-11-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-7-11-4

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-7-11-5

When a noble disciple possesses these seven good qualities and these four desirable states they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn55-7-11-6

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-7-11-7

When he had spoken, the brahmins and householders of Bamboo Gate said to the Buddha, ^sn55-7-12-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! … ^sn55-7-12-2

We go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^sn55-7-12-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge for life.” ^sn55-7-12-4

### In the Brick Hall (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.8) ^sn55-8-0

So I have heard. ^sn55-8-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^sn55-8-1-2

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn55-8-1-3

“Sir, the monk named Sāḷha has passed away. ^sn55-8-2-1

Where has he been reborn in his next life? ^sn55-8-2-2

The nun named Nandā, ^sn55-8-2-3

the layman named Sudatta, ^sn55-8-2-5

and the laywoman named Sujātā have passed away. ^sn55-8-2-7

Where have they been reborn in the next life?” ^sn55-8-2-8

“Ānanda, the monk Sāḷha passed away having realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn55-8-3-1

The nun Nandā passed away having ended the five lower fetters. She’s been reborn spontaneously, and will be extinguished there, not liable to return from that world. ^sn55-8-3-2

The layman Sudatta passed away having ended three fetters, and weakened greed, hate, and delusion. He’s a once-returner; he will come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^sn55-8-3-3

The laywoman Sujātā passed away having ended three fetters. She’s a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-8-3-4

It’s no wonder that a human being should pass away. ^sn55-8-4-1

But if you should come and ask me about it each and every time someone dies that would be a bother for me. ^sn55-8-4-2

So Ānanda, I will teach you the explanation of the Dhamma named ‘the mirror of the teaching’. A noble disciple who has this may declare of themselves: ^sn55-8-4-3

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^sn55-8-4-4

And what is that mirror of the teaching? ^sn55-8-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-8-6-1

the teaching … ^sn55-8-6-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-8-6-3

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-8-6-4

This is that mirror of the teaching. ^sn55-8-6-5

A noble disciple who has this may declare of themselves: ^sn55-8-6-6

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-8-6-7

(The following two discourses have the same setting.) ^sn55-8-6-9

### At the Brick Hall (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.9) ^sn55-9-0

Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^sn55-9-1-1

“Sir, the monk named Asoka has passed away. ^sn55-9-1-2

Where has he been reborn in his next life? ^sn55-9-1-3

The nun named Asokā, ^sn55-9-1-4

the layman named Asoka, ^sn55-9-1-5

and the laywoman named Asokā have passed away. ^sn55-9-1-6

Where have they been reborn in the next life?” ^sn55-9-1-7

“Ānanda, the monk Asoka passed away having realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life … ^sn55-9-2-1

(And all is explained as in SN 55.8.) ^sn55-9-2-2

This is that mirror of the teaching. ^sn55-9-3-1

A noble disciple who has this may declare of themselves: ^sn55-9-3-2

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-9-3-3

### At the Brick Hall (3rd) (Linked Discourses 55.10) ^sn55-10-0

Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^sn55-10-1-1

“Sir, the layman named Kakkaṭa has passed away in Ñātika. ^sn55-10-1-2

Where has he been reborn in his next life? ^sn55-10-1-3

The laymen named Kaḷibha, ^sn55-10-1-4

Nikata, ^sn55-10-1-5

Kaṭissaha, ^sn55-10-1-6

Tuṭṭha, ^sn55-10-1-7

Santuṭṭha, ^sn55-10-1-8

Bhadda, and ^sn55-10-1-9

Subhadda have passed away in Ñātika. ^sn55-10-1-10

Where have they been reborn in the next life?” ^sn55-10-1-11

“Ānanda, the laymen Kakkaṭa, ^sn55-10-2-1

Kaḷibha, ^sn55-10-2-2

Nikata, ^sn55-10-2-3

Kaṭissaha, ^sn55-10-2-4

Tuṭṭha, ^sn55-10-2-5

Santuṭṭha, ^sn55-10-2-6

Bhadda, ^sn55-10-2-7

and Subhadda passed away having ended the five lower fetters. They’ve been reborn spontaneously, and will be extinguished there, not liable to return from that world. ^sn55-10-2-8

Over fifty laymen in Ñātika have passed away having ended the five lower fetters. They’ve been reborn spontaneously, and will be extinguished there, not liable to return from that world. ^sn55-10-3-1

More than ninety laymen in Ñātika have passed away having ended three fetters, and weakened greed, hate, and delusion. They’re once-returners, who will come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^sn55-10-3-2

More than five hundred laymen in Ñātika have passed away having ended three fetters. They’re stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-10-3-3

It’s no wonder that a human being should pass away. ^sn55-10-4-1

But if you should come and ask me about it each and every time someone passes away, that would be a bother for me. ^sn55-10-4-2

So Ānanda, I will teach you the explanation of the Dhamma named ‘the mirror of the teaching’. A noble disciple who has this may declare of themselves: ^sn55-10-4-3

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^sn55-10-4-4

And what is that mirror of the teaching? ^sn55-10-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-10-6-1

the teaching … ^sn55-10-6-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-10-6-3

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-10-6-4

This is that mirror of the teaching. ^sn55-10-6-5

A noble disciple who has this may declare of themselves: ^sn55-10-6-6

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-10-6-7

### A Saṅgha of a Thousand Nuns (Linked Discourses 55.11) ^sn55-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the Royal Monastery. ^sn55-11-1-1

Then a Saṅgha of a thousand nuns went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^sn55-11-1-2

“Nuns, a noble disciple who has four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-11-2-1

What four? ^sn55-11-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-11-2-3

the teaching … ^sn55-11-2-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-11-2-6

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-11-2-7

A noble disciple who has these four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-11-2-8

### The Brahmins (Linked Discourses 55.12) ^sn55-12-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-12-1-1

“Mendicants, the brahmins advocate a practice called ‘get up and go’. ^sn55-12-1-2

They encourage their disciple: ^sn55-12-1-3

‘Please, my man, rising early you should face east and walk. ^sn55-12-1-4

Do not avoid a pit, a cliff, a stump, thorny ground, a swamp, or a sewer. ^sn55-12-1-5

You should await death in the place that you fall. ^sn55-12-1-6

And when your body breaks up, after death, you’ll be reborn in a good place, a heaven realm.’ ^sn55-12-1-7

But this practice of the brahmins is a foolish procedure, a stupid procedure. It doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, or extinguishment. ^sn55-12-2-1

But in the training of the Noble One I advocate a ‘get up and go’ practice ^sn55-12-2-2

which does lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^sn55-12-2-3

And what is that ‘get up and go’ practice? ^sn55-12-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-12-3-3

the teaching … ^sn55-12-3-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-12-3-6

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-12-3-7

This is that ‘get up and go’ practice ^sn55-12-3-8

which does lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment.” ^sn55-12-3-9

### With the Senior Monk Ānanda (Linked Discourses 55.13) ^sn55-13-0

At one time the venerables Ānanda and Sāriputta were staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^sn55-13-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Sāriputta came out of retreat, went to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^sn55-13-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him: ^sn55-13-1-3

“Reverend, how many things do people have to give up and how many do they have to possess in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^sn55-13-1-4

“Reverend, people have to give up four things and possess four things in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-13-1-5

What four? ^sn55-13-2-1

They don’t have the distrust in the Buddha that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-13-2-2

And they do have the experiential confidence in the Buddha that causes a learned noble disciple to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-13-2-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn55-13-2-4

They don’t have the distrust in the teaching that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-13-3-1

And they do have the experiential confidence in the teaching that causes a learned noble disciple to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-13-3-2

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn55-13-3-3

They don’t have the distrust in the Saṅgha that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-13-4-1

And they do have the experiential confidence in the Saṅgha that causes a learned noble disciple to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-13-4-2

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, and worthy of veneration with cupped palms.’ ^sn55-13-4-3

They don’t have the unethical conduct that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-13-5-1

And they do have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones that causes a learned noble disciple to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-13-5-2

Their ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn55-13-5-3

People have to give up these four things and possess these four things in order for the Buddha to declare that they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-13-5-4

### Fear of the Bad Place (Linked Discourses 55.14) ^sn55-14-0

“Mendicants, a noble disciple who has four things has gone beyond all fear of being reborn in a bad place. ^sn55-14-1-1

What four? ^sn55-14-1-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-14-1-3

the teaching … ^sn55-14-1-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-14-1-6

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-14-1-7

A noble disciple who has these four things has gone beyond all fear of being reborn in a bad place.” ^sn55-14-1-8

### Fear of the Bad Place, the Underworld (Linked Discourses 55.15) ^sn55-15-0

“Mendicants, a noble disciple who has four things has gone beyond all fear of being reborn in a bad place, the underworld. ^sn55-15-1-1

What four? ^sn55-15-1-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-15-1-3

the teaching … ^sn55-15-1-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-15-1-6

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-15-1-7

A noble disciple who has these four things has gone beyond all fear of being reborn in a bad place, the underworld.” ^sn55-15-1-8

### Friends and Colleagues (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.16) ^sn55-16-0

“Mendicants, those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in the four factors of stream-entry. ^sn55-16-1-1

What four? ^sn55-16-1-2

Experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-16-1-3

the teaching … ^sn55-16-1-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-16-1-6

And the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-16-1-7

Those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in these four factors of stream-entry.” ^sn55-16-1-8

### Friends and Colleagues (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.17) ^sn55-17-0

“Mendicants, those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in the four factors of stream-entry. ^sn55-17-1-1

What four? ^sn55-17-1-2

Experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-17-1-3

There might be change in the four principal states—the elements of earth, water, fire, and air—but a noble disciple with experiential confidence in the Buddha would never change. ^sn55-17-2-1

In this context, ‘change’ means that such a noble disciple will be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm: this is quite impossible. ^sn55-17-2-2

Experiential confidence in the teaching … ^sn55-17-2-3

Experiential confidence in the Saṅgha … ^sn55-17-2-4

The ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-17-2-5

There might be change in the four principal states—the elements of earth, water, fire, and air—but a noble disciple with the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones would never change. ^sn55-17-2-6

In this context, ‘change’ means that such a noble disciple will be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm: this is quite impossible. ^sn55-17-2-7

Those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in these four factors of stream-entry.” ^sn55-17-2-8

### A Visit to the Gods (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.18) ^sn55-18-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-18-1-1

And then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared among the gods of the thirty-three. ^sn55-18-1-2

Then several deities of the company of the thirty-three went up to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and stood to one side. Moggallāna said to them: ^sn55-18-1-3

“Reverends, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha. … ^sn55-18-2-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-18-2-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-18-2-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-18-2-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-18-2-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn55-18-2-7

“My good fellow Moggallāna, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-18-3-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-18-3-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-18-3-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-18-3-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-18-3-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn55-18-3-7

### A Visit to the Gods (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.19) ^sn55-19-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-19-1-1

And then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared among the gods of the thirty-three. ^sn55-19-1-2

Then several deities of the company of the thirty-three went up to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and stood to one side. Moggallāna said to them: ^sn55-19-1-3

“Reverends, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha. … ^sn55-19-2-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, have been reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-19-2-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-19-2-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-19-2-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-19-2-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, have been reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn55-19-2-7

“My good Moggallāna, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-19-3-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, have been reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^sn55-19-3-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-19-3-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-19-3-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-19-3-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, have been reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^sn55-19-3-7

### A Visit to the Gods (3rd) (Linked Discourses 55.20) ^sn55-20-0

Then the Buddha, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared among the gods of the thirty-three. ^sn55-20-1-1

Then several deities of the company of the thirty-three went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^sn55-20-1-2

“Reverends, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha. … ^sn55-20-2-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-20-2-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-20-2-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-20-2-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-20-2-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-20-2-7

“Good fellow, it’s good to have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-20-3-1

It’s the reason why some sentient beings are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-20-3-3

It’s good to have experiential confidence in the teaching. … ^sn55-20-3-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-20-3-5

and to have the ethical conduct that’s loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-20-3-6

It’s the reason why some sentient beings are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^sn55-20-3-7

### With Mahānāma (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.21) ^sn55-21-0

So I have heard. ^sn55-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^sn55-21-1-2

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn55-21-1-3

“Sir, this Kapilavatthu is successful and prosperous, populous, full of people, with cramped cul-de-sacs. ^sn55-21-1-4

In the late afternoon, after paying homage to the Buddha or an esteemed mendicant, I enter Kapilavatthu. ^sn55-21-1-5

I encounter a stray elephant, ^sn55-21-1-6

horse, ^sn55-21-1-7

chariot, ^sn55-21-1-8

cart, ^sn55-21-1-9

or person. ^sn55-21-1-10

At that time I lose mindfulness regarding the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^sn55-21-1-11

I think: ^sn55-21-1-12

‘If I were to die at this time, where would I be reborn in my next life?’” ^sn55-21-1-13

“Do not fear, Mahānāma, do not fear! ^sn55-21-2-1

Your death will not be a bad one; your passing will not be a bad one. ^sn55-21-2-2

Take someone whose mind has for a long time been imbued with faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. Their body is formed, made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^sn55-21-2-3

Right here the crows, vultures, hawks, hounds, jackals, and many kinds of little creatures devour it. ^sn55-21-2-4

But their mind rises up, headed for a higher place. ^sn55-21-2-5

Suppose a person was to sink a pot of ghee or oil into a deep lake and break it open. ^sn55-21-3-1

Its shards and chips would sink down, while the ghee or oil in it would rise up, headed for a higher place. ^sn55-21-3-2

In the same way, take someone whose mind has for a long time been imbued with faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. Their body consists of form, made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^sn55-21-3-3

Right here the crows, vultures, hawks, hounds, jackals, and many kinds of little creatures devour it. ^sn55-21-3-4

But their mind rises up, headed for a higher place. ^sn55-21-3-5

Your mind, Mahānāma, has for a long time been imbued with faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^sn55-21-3-6

Do not fear, Mahānāma, do not fear! ^sn55-21-3-7

Your death will not be a bad one; your passing will not be a bad one.” ^sn55-21-3-8

### With Mahānāma (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.22) ^sn55-22-0

So I have heard. ^sn55-22-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^sn55-22-1-2

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^sn55-22-1-3

“Sir, this Kapilavatthu is successful and prosperous, populous, full of people, with cramped cul-de-sacs. ^sn55-22-1-4

In the late afternoon, after paying homage to the Buddha or an esteemed mendicant, I enter Kapilavatthu. ^sn55-22-1-5

I encounter a stray elephant, ^sn55-22-1-6

horse, ^sn55-22-1-7

chariot, ^sn55-22-1-8

cart, ^sn55-22-1-9

or person. ^sn55-22-1-10

At that time I lose mindfulness regarding the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^sn55-22-1-11

I think: ^sn55-22-1-12

‘If I were to die at this time, where would I be reborn in my next life?’” ^sn55-22-1-13

“Do not fear, Mahānāma, do not fear! ^sn55-22-2-1

Your death will not be a bad one; your passing will not be a bad one. ^sn55-22-2-2

A noble disciple who has four things slants, slopes, and inclines towards extinguishment. ^sn55-22-2-3

What four? ^sn55-22-2-4

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-22-2-5

the teaching … ^sn55-22-2-7

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-22-2-8

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-22-2-9

Suppose there was a tree that slants, slopes, and inclines to the east. If it was cut off at the root where would it fall?” ^sn55-22-3-1

“Sir, it would fall in the direction that it slants, slopes, and inclines.” ^sn55-22-3-2

“In the same way, a noble disciple who has four things slants, slopes, and inclines towards extinguishment.” ^sn55-22-3-3

### With Godhā the Sakyan (Linked Discourses 55.23) ^sn55-23-0

At Kapilavatthu. ^sn55-23-1-1

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to Godhā the Sakyan, and said to her, ^sn55-23-1-2

“Godhā, how many things must an individual have for you to recognize them as a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^sn55-23-1-3

“Mahānāma, an individual must have three things for me to recognize them as a stream-enterer. ^sn55-23-2-1

What three? ^sn55-23-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-23-2-3

the teaching … ^sn55-23-2-5

and the Saṅgha … ^sn55-23-2-6

When an individual has these three things I recognize them as a stream-enterer. ^sn55-23-2-8

But Mahānāma, how many things must an individual have for <em>you</em> to recognize them as a stream-enterer?” ^sn55-23-3-1

“Godhā, an individual must have four things for me to recognize them as a stream-enterer. ^sn55-23-3-2

What four? ^sn55-23-3-3

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-23-3-4

the teaching … ^sn55-23-3-6

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-23-3-7

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-23-3-8

When an individual has these four things I recognize them as a stream-enterer.” ^sn55-23-3-9

“Hold on, Mahānāma, hold on! ^sn55-23-4-1

Only the Buddha would know whether or not they have these things.” ^sn55-23-4-2

“Come, Godhā, let’s go to the Buddha and inform him about this.” ^sn55-23-4-3

Then Mahānāma and Godhā went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Mahānāma told the Buddha all that had happened, and then said: ^sn55-23-4-4

“Sir, some instigation of a legal proceeding might come up. The Buddha might take one side, and the Saṅgha of monks the other. ^sn55-23-8-4

I’d side with the Buddha. ^sn55-23-8-5

May the Buddha remember me as having such confidence. ^sn55-23-8-6

Some instigation of a legal proceeding might come up. The Buddha might take one side, and the Saṅgha of monks and the Saṅgha of nuns the other. … ^sn55-23-8-7

The Buddha might take one side, and the Saṅgha of monks and the Saṅgha of nuns and the laymen the other. … ^sn55-23-8-10

The Buddha might take one side, and the Saṅgha of monks and the Saṅgha of nuns and the laymen and the laywomen the other. … ^sn55-23-8-13

The Buddha might take one side, and the Saṅgha of monks and the Saṅgha of nuns and the laymen and the laywomen and the world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—the other. ^sn55-23-8-16

I’d side with the Buddha. ^sn55-23-8-17

May the Buddha remember me as having such confidence.” ^sn55-23-8-18

“Godhā, what do you have to say to Mahānāma when he speaks like this?” ^sn55-23-8-19

“Sir, I have nothing to say to Mahānāma when he speaks like this, except what is good and wholesome.” ^sn55-23-8-20

### About Sarakāni (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.24) ^sn55-24-0

At Kapilavatthu. ^sn55-24-1-1

Now at that time Sarakāni the Sakyan had passed away. ^sn55-24-1-2

The Buddha declared that he was ^sn55-24-1-3

a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-24-1-4

At that, several Sakyans came together complaining, grumbling, and objecting, ^sn55-24-1-5

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^sn55-24-1-6

Who can’t become a stream-enterer these days? ^sn55-24-1-7

For the Buddha even declared Sarakāni to be a stream-enterer after he passed away. ^sn55-24-1-8

Sarakāni was too weak for the training; he used to drink liquor.” ^sn55-24-1-11

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said: ^sn55-24-2-1

“Mahānāma, when a lay follower has for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, how could they go to the underworld? ^sn55-24-3-1

And if anyone should rightly be said to ^sn55-24-3-2

have for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, it’s Sarakāni the Sakyan. ^sn55-24-3-3

Sarakāni the Sakyan has for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^sn55-24-3-4

How could he go to the underworld? ^sn55-24-3-5

Take a certain individual who has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-4-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-4-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-4-4

They have laughing wisdom and swift wisdom, and are endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-4-5

They’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn55-24-4-6

This individual is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-4-7

Take another individual who has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-5-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-5-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-5-4

They have laughing wisdom and swift wisdom, but are not endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-5-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^sn55-24-5-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-5-7

Take another individual who has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-6-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-6-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-6-4

But they don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-6-5

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^sn55-24-6-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-6-7

Take another individual who has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-7-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-7-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-7-4

But they don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-7-5

With the ending of three fetters they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-24-7-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-7-7

Take another individual who doesn’t have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-8-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-8-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-8-3

They don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-8-4

Still, they have these qualities: ^sn55-24-8-5

the faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^sn55-24-8-6

And they accept the teachings proclaimed by the Realized One after deliberating them with a degree of wisdom. ^sn55-24-8-7

This individual, too, doesn’t go to hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They don’t go to places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-8-8

Take another individual who doesn’t have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-24-9-1

the teaching … ^sn55-24-9-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-24-9-3

They don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-24-9-4

Still, they have these qualities: ^sn55-24-9-5

the faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^sn55-24-9-6

And they have a degree of faith and fondness for the Buddha. ^sn55-24-9-7

This individual, too, doesn’t go to hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They don’t go to places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-24-9-8

If these great sal trees could understand what was well said and poorly said, I’d declare them to be stream-enterers. ^sn55-24-9-9

Why can’t this apply to Sarakāni? ^sn55-24-9-10

Mahānāma, Sarakāni the Sakyan undertook the training at the time of his death.” ^sn55-24-9-11

### About Sarakāni the Sakyan (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.25) ^sn55-25-0

At Kapilavatthu. ^sn55-25-1-1

Now at that time Sarakāni the Sakyan had passed away. ^sn55-25-1-2

The Buddha declared that he was ^sn55-25-1-3

a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-25-1-4

At that, several Sakyans came together complaining, grumbling, and objecting, ^sn55-25-1-5

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^sn55-25-1-6

Who can’t become a stream-enterer these days? ^sn55-25-1-7

For the Buddha even declared Sarakāni to be a stream-enterer after he passed away. ^sn55-25-1-8

Sarakāni didn’t fulfill the training.” ^sn55-25-1-11

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said: ^sn55-25-1-12

“Mahānāma, when a lay follower has for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, how could they go to the underworld? ^sn55-25-3-1

And if anyone should rightly be said to ^sn55-25-3-2

have for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, it’s Sarakāni the Sakyan. ^sn55-25-3-3

Sarakāni the Sakyan has for a long time gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^sn55-25-3-4

Take a certain individual who is sure and devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-4-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-4-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-4-4

They have laughing wisdom and swift wisdom, and are endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-4-5

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^sn55-25-4-6

This individual is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-4-7

Take another individual who is sure and devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-5-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-5-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-5-4

They have laughing wisdom and swift wisdom, but are not endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-5-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters, they’re extinguished between one life and the next … they’re extinguished upon landing … they’re extinguished without extra effort … they’re extinguished with extra effort … they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^sn55-25-5-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-5-7

Take another individual who is sure and devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-6-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-6-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-6-4

But they don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-6-5

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^sn55-25-6-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-6-7

Take another individual who is sure and devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-7-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-7-3

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-7-4

But they don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-7-5

With the ending of three fetters they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-25-7-6

This individual, too, is exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-7-7

Take another individual who isn’t sure or devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-8-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-8-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-8-3

They don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-8-4

Still, they have these qualities: ^sn55-25-8-5

the faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^sn55-25-8-6

And they accept the teachings proclaimed by the Realized One after deliberating them with a degree of wisdom. ^sn55-25-8-7

This individual, too, doesn’t go to hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They don’t go to places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-8-8

Take another individual who isn’t sure or devoted to the Buddha … ^sn55-25-9-1

the teaching … ^sn55-25-9-2

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-25-9-3

They don’t have laughing wisdom or swift wisdom, nor are they endowed with freedom. ^sn55-25-9-4

Still, they have these qualities: ^sn55-25-9-5

the faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^sn55-25-9-6

And they have a degree of faith and love for the Buddha. ^sn55-25-9-7

This individual, too, doesn’t go to hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They don’t go to places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^sn55-25-9-8

Suppose there was a barren field, a barren ground, with uncleared stumps. And you had seeds that were broken, spoiled, weather-damaged, infertile, and ill kept. And the heavens didn’t provide enough rain. ^sn55-25-10-1

Would those seeds grow, increase, and mature?” ^sn55-25-10-2

“No, sir.” ^sn55-25-10-3

“In the same way, take a teaching that’s badly explained and badly propounded, not emancipating, not leading to peace, proclaimed by someone who is not a fully awakened Buddha. ^sn55-25-10-4

This is what I call a barren field. ^sn55-25-10-5

A disciple remains in such a teaching, practicing in line with that teaching, practicing it properly, living in line with that teaching. ^sn55-25-10-6

This is what I call a bad seed. ^sn55-25-10-7

Suppose there was a fertile field, a fertile ground, well-cleared of stumps. And you had seeds that were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. And there’s plenty of rainfall. ^sn55-25-11-1

Would those seeds grow, increase, and mature?” ^sn55-25-11-3

“Yes, sir.” ^sn55-25-11-4

“In the same way, take a teaching that’s well explained and well propounded, emancipating, leading to peace, proclaimed by someone who is a fully awakened Buddha. ^sn55-25-11-5

This is what I call a fertile field. ^sn55-25-11-6

A disciple remains in such a teaching, practicing in line with that teaching, practicing it properly, living in line with that teaching. ^sn55-25-11-7

This is what I call a good seed. ^sn55-25-11-8

Why can’t this apply to Sarakāni? ^sn55-25-11-9

Mahānāma, Sarakāni the Sakyan fulfilled the training at the time of his death.” ^sn55-25-11-10

### Anāthapiṇḍika (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.26) ^sn55-26-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-26-1-1

Now at that time the householder Anāthapiṇḍika was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-26-1-2

Then he addressed a man, ^sn55-26-1-3

“Please, my man, go to Venerable Sāriputta, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^sn55-26-1-4

‘Sir, the householder Anāthapiṇḍika is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-26-1-5

He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn55-26-1-6

And then say: ^sn55-26-1-7

‘Sir, please visit him at his home out of sympathy.’” ^sn55-26-1-8

“Yes, sir,” that man replied. He did as Anāthapiṇḍika asked. ^sn55-26-2-1

Sāriputta consented with silence. ^sn55-26-3-5

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went with Venerable Ānanda as his second monk to Anāthapiṇḍika’s home. He sat down on the seat spread out, and said to Anāthapiṇḍika, ^sn55-26-4-1

“I hope you’re keeping well, householder; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^sn55-26-4-2

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn55-26-4-3

“Householder, you don’t have the distrust in the Buddha that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-26-5-1

And you have experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^sn55-26-5-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^sn55-26-5-3

Seeing in yourself that experiential confidence in the Buddha, that pain may die down on the spot. ^sn55-26-5-4

You don’t have the distrust in the teaching that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-26-6-1

And you have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^sn55-26-6-2

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^sn55-26-6-3

Seeing in yourself that experiential confidence in the teaching, that pain may die down on the spot. ^sn55-26-6-4

You don’t have the distrust in the Saṅgha that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-26-7-1

And you have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^sn55-26-7-2

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, and worthy of veneration with cupped palms. It is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^sn55-26-7-3

Seeing in yourself that experiential confidence in the Saṅgha, that pain may die down on the spot. ^sn55-26-7-4

You don’t have the unethical conduct that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-26-8-1

Your ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn55-26-8-2

Seeing in yourself that ethical conduct loved by the noble ones, that pain may die down on the spot. ^sn55-26-8-3

You don’t have the wrong view that causes an unlearned ordinary person to be reborn—when their body breaks up, after death—in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^sn55-26-9-1

You have right view. ^sn55-26-9-2

Seeing in yourself that right view, that pain may die down on the spot. ^sn55-26-9-3

You don’t have the wrong purpose … ^sn55-26-10-1

wrong speech … ^sn55-26-11-1

wrong action … ^sn55-26-12-1

wrong livelihood … ^sn55-26-13-1

wrong effort … ^sn55-26-14-1

wrong mindfulness … ^sn55-26-15-1

wrong immersion … ^sn55-26-16-1

wrong knowledge … ^sn55-26-17-1

wrong freedom … ^sn55-26-18-1

You have right freedom. ^sn55-26-18-2

Seeing in yourself that right freedom, that pain may die down on the spot.” ^sn55-26-18-3

And then Anāthapiṇḍika’s pain died down on the spot. ^sn55-26-19-1

Then he served Sāriputta and Ānanda from his own dish. ^sn55-26-19-2

When Sāriputta had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, Anāthapiṇḍika took a low seat and sat to one side. ^sn55-26-19-3

Venerable Sāriputta expressed his appreciation to him with these verses. ^sn55-26-19-4

“Whoever has faith in the Realized One, ^sn55-26-20-1

unwavering and well grounded; ^sn55-26-20-2

whose ethical conduct is good, ^sn55-26-20-3

praised and loved by the noble ones; ^sn55-26-20-4

who has confidence in the Saṅgha, ^sn55-26-21-1

and correct view: ^sn55-26-21-2

they’re said to be prosperous, ^sn55-26-21-3

their life is not in vain. ^sn55-26-21-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^sn55-26-22-1

to faith, ethical behaviour, ^sn55-26-22-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^sn55-26-22-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^sn55-26-22-4

After expressing his appreciation to Anāthapiṇḍika with these verses, Sāriputta rose from his seat and left. ^sn55-26-23-1

Then Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^sn55-26-24-1

“So, Ānanda, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^sn55-26-24-2

“Sir, Venerable Sāriputta advised the householder Anāthapiṇḍika in this way and that.” ^sn55-26-24-3

“Sāriputta is astute, Ānanda. ^sn55-26-24-4

He has great wisdom, since he can analyze the four factors of stream-entry in ten respects.” ^sn55-26-24-5

### With Anāthapiṇḍika (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.27) ^sn55-27-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-27-1-1

Now at that time the householder Anāthapiṇḍika was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-27-1-2

Then he addressed a man, ^sn55-27-1-3

“Please, my man, go to Venerable Ānanda, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^sn55-27-1-4

‘Sir, the householder Anāthapiṇḍika is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^sn55-27-1-5

He bows with his head to your feet.’ ^sn55-27-1-6

And then say: ^sn55-27-1-7

‘Sir, please visit him at his home out of sympathy.’” ^sn55-27-1-8

“Yes, sir,” that man replied. He did as Anāthapiṇḍika asked. ^sn55-27-2-1

Ānanda consented with silence. ^sn55-27-2-6

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Anāthapiṇḍika. He sat down on the seat spread out and said to Anāthapiṇḍika, ^sn55-27-3-1

“I hope you’re keeping well, householder; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing; that its fading, not its growing, is apparent.” ^sn55-27-3-2

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^sn55-27-3-3

“Householder, when an unlearned ordinary person has four things, they’re frightened and terrified, and fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-4-1

What four? ^sn55-27-4-2

Firstly, an unlearned ordinary person distrusts the Buddha. ^sn55-27-4-3

Seeing in themselves that distrust of the Buddha, they’re frightened and terrified, and fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-4-4

Furthermore, an unlearned ordinary person distrusts the teaching … ^sn55-27-5-1

Furthermore, an unlearned ordinary person distrusts the Saṅgha … ^sn55-27-6-1

Furthermore, an unlearned ordinary person has unethical conduct. ^sn55-27-7-1

Seeing in themselves that unethical conduct, they’re frightened and terrified, and fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-7-2

When an unlearned ordinary person has these four things, they’re frightened and terrified, and fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-7-3

When a learned noble disciple has four things, they’re not frightened or terrified, and don’t fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-8-1

What four? ^sn55-27-8-2

Firstly, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-27-8-3

Seeing in themselves that experiential confidence in the Buddha, they’re not frightened or terrified, and don’t fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-8-5

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the teaching … ^sn55-27-9-1

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Saṅgha … ^sn55-27-10-1

Furthermore, a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^sn55-27-11-1

Seeing in themselves that ethical conduct loved by the noble ones, they’re not frightened or terrified, and don’t fear what awaits them after death. ^sn55-27-11-2

When a learned noble disciple has these four things, they’re not frightened or terrified, and don’t fear what awaits them after death.” ^sn55-27-11-3

“Honorable Ānanda, I am not afraid. ^sn55-27-12-1

What have I to fear? ^sn55-27-12-2

For I have experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-27-12-3

the teaching … ^sn55-27-12-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-27-12-6

And of the training rules appropriate for laypeople taught by the Buddha, I don’t see any that I have broken.” ^sn55-27-12-8

“You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate! ^sn55-27-12-9

You have declared the fruit of stream-entry.” ^sn55-27-12-10

### Fears and Enmities (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.28) ^sn55-28-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-28-1-1

Seated to one side, the Buddha said to the householder Anāthapiṇḍika: ^sn55-28-1-2

“Householder, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn55-28-1-3

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^sn55-28-1-4

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^sn55-28-2-1

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. ^sn55-28-2-2

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^sn55-28-2-3

Anyone who steals … ^sn55-28-2-4

Anyone who commits sexual misconduct … ^sn55-28-2-5

Anyone who lies … ^sn55-28-2-6

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. ^sn55-28-2-7

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^sn55-28-2-8

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^sn55-28-2-9

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^sn55-28-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-28-3-2

the teaching … ^sn55-28-3-4

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-28-3-5

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-28-3-6

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^sn55-28-3-7

And what is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom? ^sn55-28-4-1

A noble disciple rationally applies the mind to dependent origination itself: ^sn55-28-4-2

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^sn55-28-4-3

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases.' That is: ^sn55-28-4-4

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. Choices are a requirement for consciousness. Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. The six sense fields are requirements for contact. Contact is a requirement for feeling. Feeling is a requirement for craving. Craving is a requirement for grasping. Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^sn55-28-4-5

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^sn55-28-4-6

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. When choices cease, consciousness ceases. When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. When contact ceases, feeling ceases. When feeling ceases, craving ceases. When craving ceases, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^sn55-28-4-7

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^sn55-28-4-8

This is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom. ^sn55-28-4-9

When a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn55-28-5-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-28-5-2

### Fears and Enmities (2nd) (Linked Discourses 55.29) ^sn55-29-0

At Sāvatthī. … ^sn55-29-1-1

“Mendicants, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^sn55-29-1-2

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^sn55-29-1-3

### With Nandaka the Licchavi (Linked Discourses 55.30) ^sn55-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^sn55-30-1-1

Then Nandaka the Licchavi chief minister went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^sn55-30-1-2

“Nandaka, a noble disciple who has four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-30-2-1

What four? ^sn55-30-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-30-2-3

the teaching … ^sn55-30-2-5

the Saṅgha … ^sn55-30-2-6

And they have the ethical conduct loved by the noble ones … leading to immersion. ^sn55-30-2-7

A noble disciple who has these four things is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^sn55-30-2-8

A noble disciple who has these four things is endowed with long life, both human and heavenly … ^sn55-30-3-1

beauty … ^sn55-30-3-2

happiness … ^sn55-30-3-3

fame … ^sn55-30-3-4

and sovereignty, both human and heavenly. ^sn55-30-3-5

Now, I don’t say this because I’ve heard it from some other ascetic or brahmin. I only say it because I’ve known, seen, and realized it for myself.” ^sn55-30-3-6

When he had spoken, a certain person said to Nandaka: ^sn55-30-4-1

“Sir, it is time to bathe.” ^sn55-30-4-2

“Enough now, lad, with that exterior bath. ^sn55-30-4-3

This interior bathing will do for me, that is, ^sn55-30-4-4

confidence in the Buddha.” ^sn55-30-4-5

### Overflowing Merit (1st) (Linked Discourses 55.31) ^sn55-31-0

At Sāvatthī. ^sn55-31-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness that nurture happiness. ^sn55-31-1-2

What four? ^sn55-31-1-3

Firstly, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^sn55-31-1-4
